topics.title: MARPOL
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** MARPOL

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**MARPOL** is the short-form designation for the **International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships**, adopted under the auspices of the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**, a specialized agency of the United Nations. The full instrument is officially known as **MARPOL 73/78**, because the original 1973 Convention was merged with the 1978 Protocol (adopted in response to a spate of tanker accidents). It is the **principal international treaty** governing the prevention of pollution of the marine environment by ships from operational or accidental causes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition to Memorize:**
> *"MARPOL 73/78 is the main international convention covering the prevention of pollution of the marine environment by ships from operational or accidental causes. It is a combination of two treaties adopted in 1973 and 1978 respectively, and includes regulations aimed at preventing and minimizing pollution from ships — both accidental pollution and that from routine operations."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the world's oceans as a **giant, shared apartment building** where every ship is a tenant. Without rules, one careless tenant could dump garbage into the hallway, leak oil in the basement, or blow smoke into everyone's rooms. The IMO is the **building management**, and **MARPOL is the rulebook** that every tenant (ship) must follow to keep the building livable.

- The **Convention itself** is like the *master lease agreement* signed by countries (Flag States).
- The **Annexes** are the *individual rule chapters* — one for oil, one for chemicals, one for sewage, etc.
- The **IOPP Certificate** (issued to a ship after inspection) is like the *compliance sticker* on the tenant's door showing they passed the inspection.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- Number of Annexes currently in force: **6**
- Entered into force globally: **2 November 1983** (Annex I and II)
- Latest major addition: **Annex VI** (entered into force 19 May 2005)
- Convention depositary: **Secretary-General of IMO (London)**
- Default discharge limit for oily water from machinery spaces: **15 ppm** (parts per million)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 expects students to know the *purpose*, *structure* (six annexes), *implementation mechanism* (flag state, port state, coastal state), and *enforcement tools* (certificates, record books, reception facilities) of MARPOL.

## 1.4 Historical Context

The catalyst for MARPOL was the **Torrey Canyon disaster (1967)**, where the Liberian-registered tanker spilled approximately **120,000 tonnes of crude oil** off the coast of England, contaminating hundreds of miles of coastline. This catastrophe exposed the legal vacuum in international law regarding ship-source pollution and directly led to the 1973 Convention.

| Milestone | Year | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Torrey Canyon disaster | 1967 | Catalyst event |
| MARPOL Convention adopted | 1973 | Original text |
| MARPOL Protocol adopted | 1978 | Modified by tanker accidents |
| Combined MARPOL 73/78 in force | 1983 | Annexes I & II live |
| Annex III in force | 1992 | Packaged goods |
| Annex IV in force | 2003 | Sewage |
| Annex V in force | 1988 | Garbage |
| Annex VI in force | 2005 | Air pollution |
| Hong Kong Convention (future) | Pending | Ship recycling |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of MARPOL 73/78

MARPOL is not a single uniform regulation — it is a **framework convention** with **two articles and six technical annexes**. The articles contain general obligations, while each annex contains detailed technical regulations.

### 2.1.1 The Two Articles (General Obligations)

1. **Article 1 — General Obligations:** Parties must give full and complete effect to the Convention's provisions.
2. **Article 2 — Definition of "Discharge":** Discharge means any release, however caused, from a ship. Includes spilling, leaking, pumping, emitting, or dumping. Excludes: dumping under London Convention 1972, operational releases for safety of ship, or discharge of harmful substances from offshore platforms.

### 2.1.2 The Six Annexes (Engineering-Centric)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Six Annexes — KTU Must-Know Table**

| Annex | Subject | Entered into Force | Key Concept |
|---|---|---|---|
| I | Prevention of Pollution by **Oil** | 2 Jan 1983 | 15 ppm limit; OWS; IOPP; Oil Record Book |
| II | Control of Pollution by **Noxious Liquid Substances (NLS)** in bulk | 6 Apr 1987 | 4 categories (X, Y, Z, OS); NLS Certificate |
| III | Prevention of Pollution by **Harmful Substances in Packaged Form** | 1 Jul 1992 | IMDG Code; marking & labelling |
| IV | Prevention of Pollution by **Sewage from Ships** | 27 Sep 2003 | Sewage Treatment Plant (STP) or holding tank |
| V | Prevention of Pollution by **Garbage from Ships** | 31 Dec 1988 | Garbage Management Plan; Placards |
| VI | Prevention of **Air Pollution** from Ships | 19 May 2005 | SOx, NOx, ODS, EEDI, EEXI, CII |

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why Oil limits exist (15 ppm):** Hydrocarbons are toxic and persistent. At 15 ppm, oil is no longer visible as a sheen.
- **Why NLS categories exist:** Different chemicals have different toxicity and persistence, so the rules scale with hazard.
- **Why packaged goods need rules:** Containers can break and release toxic cargoes.
- **Why sewage needs treatment:** Untreated sewage spreads pathogens (E. coli, etc.) near coastal waters.
- **Why garbage is regulated:** Plastic persists for centuries; non-plastic items can entangle marine life.
- **Why air pollution is regulated:** Shipping contributes ~**2.9% of global CO₂ emissions** (IMO 4th GHG Study 2020).

## 2.3 Three-Tier Enforcement Architecture

MARPOL enforcement is **layered** to plug jurisdictional gaps:

1. **Flag State (Primary)**: The country where the ship is registered is primarily responsible for ensuring compliance and issuing certificates.
2. **Port State**: Any port the ship visits can inspect and detain non-compliant ships.
3. **Coastal State**: Coastal nations can enforce within their Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ) — up to **200 nautical miles** from baseline.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pacific Basin Memorandum of Understanding (Tokyo MoU), Paris MoU, etc.** are regional port state control agreements that make enforcement more uniform.

## 2.4 Annex I — Deep Dive (Most Asked in KTU)

Annex I regulates **oil pollution from operational and accidental discharges**. Key engineering features:

- **Oily Water Separator (OWS)**: Separates oil from bilge water. Effluent must be **≤ 15 ppm**.
- **Oil Content Monitor (OCM)**: Continuously monitors effluent, often using UV fluorescence.
- **Sludge Tank**: Holds oil residue from OWS and engine oil changes.
- **SOPEP (Shipboard Oil Pollution Emergency Plan)**: Mandatory action plan for oil spills.
- **IOPP Certificate (International Oil Pollution Prevention)**: Required for all oil tankers > 150 GT and non-tankers > 400 GT. Valid for **5 years**.

### 2.4.1 Discharge Criteria for Machinery Space Bilge Water (Regulation 15)

A ship may discharge bilge water **only if ALL** of the following are met:

| Criterion | Limit | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Oil content (after OWS) | ≤ **15 ppm** | Below visual detection |
| Distance from nearest land | ≥ **12 nautical miles** | For 2017+ ships, special areas |
| Distance from ice / polar zones | Compliance with Polar Code | Arctic / Antarctic |
| Ship en route at speed | Steady ≥ 4 knots | Ensures dispersion |
| Sludge tank capacity | Adequate | No shortcuts |

### 2.4.2 Special Areas Under Annex I

- **Mediterranean Sea** (entire)
- **Baltic Sea**
- **Black Sea**
- **Red Sea** (Gulfs area)
- **Gulf of Aden** (wider)
- **Antarctica** (south of 60°S)
- **North-West European Waters** (added 1999)
- **Arabian Gulf** (added 2008)
- **South African waters** (added 2012)

## 2.5 Annex VI — Air Pollution (Increasingly Tested Topic)

This annex addresses **atmospheric emissions from ships**:

- **SOx (Sulphur Oxides)**: Global sulphur cap in fuel oil reduced from **3.5% m/m** to **0.5% m/m** effective **1 January 2020**.
- **NOx (Nitrogen Oxides)**: Tier I, II, III standards based on engine speed. Tier III applies in **NECA** (NOx Emission Control Areas) — currently the North Sea / Baltic (NECA) and North American ECA.
- **ODS (Ozone Depleting Substances)**: Prohibition of CFCs / HCFCs in ship systems.
- **EEDI (Energy Efficiency Design Index)**: New ships must meet minimum energy efficiency per tonne-mile.
- **EEXI (Existing Ship Energy Efficiency Index)**: From 2023, existing ships must also comply.
- **CII (Carbon Intensity Indicator)**: Annual rating A–E; E-rated ships must improve.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kerala Board Examiners Love This Table — Print & Memorize**

| Quantity | Formula / Limit | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Oily bilge discharge limit | $C_{\text{oil}} \le 15$ | ppm | Measured at 15 m from outlet |
| Discharge distance from land | $D \ge 12$ | NM | Non-special areas |
| Discharge distance in Antarctic | $D \ge 12$ | NM | Polar Code overlay |
| Discharge distance in North-West European / Arabian Gulf | $D \ge 12$ (special areas) | NM | Stricter conditions |
| Tonnage threshold for IOPP (tanker) | GT $> 150$ | GT | Tonnage threshold |
| Tonnage threshold for IOPP (non-tanker) | GT $> 400$ | GT | Non-tanker threshold |
| Sulphur cap in fuel (global) | $S_{\text{fuel}} \le 0.50$ | % m/m | Since 1 Jan 2020 |
| Sulphur cap in ECA | $S_{\text{fuel}} \le 0.10$ | % m/m | ECA zones |
| NOx Tier III max (n = 130 rpm) | $n \cdot 0.031 \le \text{NOx}$ | g/kWh | Formula changes with rpm |
| Sewage discharge distance | $D \ge 3$ (treated) or $D \ge 12$ (untreated) | NM | Annex IV |
| EEDI reference baseline | $\left(\dfrac{\text{Product} \times \text{Speed}^3}{\text{DWT}}\right) \times f_i$ | g CO₂/ton·NM | New ships only |
| Width of territorial sea (UNCLOS) | $W_{TS} \le 12$ | NM | For context with EEZ |
| EEZ breadth (UNCLOS) | $W_{EEZ} \le 200$ | NM | Coastal state jurisdiction |

**CRITICAL LaTeX Note:** All delimiters $\vert$ or $\mid$ (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) should be used inside table cells; never the bare pipe character to avoid breaking markdown.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **marine engineer's daily life**, MARPOL compliance involves:

- Operating OWS and OCM correctly
- Maintaining the Oil Record Book (Part I — Machinery; Part II — Cargo/Ballast on tankers)
- Reporting defects to flag administration
- Coordinating with classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) for surveys
- Handling MARPOL Annex VI scrubbers (exhaust gas cleaning systems) as a fuel-saving alternative

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the OWS Concentration Equation

The OWS works on the **gravimetric principle**: oil floats on water due to lower density. A coalescer/filter enhances separation. The output concentration follows a **decaying exponential model** in a well-mixed separator:

$$
C_{\text{out}}(t) = C_{\text{in}} \cdot e^{-k \cdot \tau}
$$

Where:
- $C_{\text{out}}(t)$ = oil concentration at outlet, in ppm
- $C_{\text{in}}$ = oil concentration at inlet, in ppm
- $k$ = first-order separation rate constant, in s⁻¹
- $\tau$ = residence time, in s

### Step-by-Step Logical Expansion

**Step 1 — Define the mass balance.**
A continuous stirred-tank reactor assumption yields:

$$
V \dfrac{dC}{dt} = Q (C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}})
$$

where $V$ is the separator volume, $Q$ is the volumetric flow.

**Step 2 — At steady state** $\left(\dfrac{dC}{dt} = 0\right)$, and accounting for first-order decay $kC$:

$$
0 = Q(C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}) - kVC_{\text{out}}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve algebraically for $C_{\text{out}}$:**

$$
C_{\text{out}}(C_{\text{in}} + kV/Q) = Q C_{\text{in}}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
C_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{in}}}{1 + k \cdot \tau}
$$

where $\tau = V/Q$ is the residence time.

**Step 4 — For high $k \cdot \tau$ (e.g., $k \cdot \tau \ge 5$):**

$$
1 + k\tau \approx k\tau
$$

So the asymptotic form is:

$$
C_{\text{out}} \approx \dfrac{C_{\text{in}}}{k\tau}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Doubling the residence time (slower flow) **halves** the outlet concentration. This is why OWS designs trade off size vs. flow rate.

### 3.2 Derivation of EEDI Reference Line

The IMO EEDI for a new ship is defined as:

$$
\text{EEDI} = \dfrac{\text{CO}_2 \text{ emissions}}{\text{Transport work}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{CO}_2}}{DWT \cdot V_{\text{ref}}}
$$

Expanding the numerator in terms of fuel consumption and emission factor:

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2} = SFC \cdot P_{\text{main}} \cdot C_F
$$

where:
- $SFC$ = Specific Fuel Consumption, g/kWh
- $P_{\text{main}}$ = Main engine power, kW
- $C_F$ = Carbon emission factor (≈ **3.114** g CO₂ per g fuel for HFO, varies with fuel type)

So:

$$
\text{EEDI} = \dfrac{SFC \cdot P_{\text{main}} \cdot C_F}{DWT \cdot V_{\text{ref}}}
$$

**Reduction factor (Z):**

$$
Z = (1 - X/100)
$$

where $X$ is the percentage reduction required by the IMO for that ship type and size, in step years 2015 → 2025.

The compliance check is:

$$
\text{EEDI}_{\text{attained}} \le \text{EEDI}_{\text{required}} = (1 - X/100) \cdot \text{EEDI}_{\text{baseline}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-relevant numerical example:** If baseline EEDI for a bulk carrier is 4.0 g CO₂/ton·NM, and $X = 30\%$, then required EEDI = $0.70 \times 4.0 = 2.8$ g CO₂/ton·NM.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Calculation Scenarios

| Scenario | Given | Find | Approach |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bilge discharge compliance | $C_{\text{out}} = 12$ ppm, $D_{\text{land}} = 8$ NM, $V = 14$ knots | Compliant? | All three must be met; 12 ppm OK, 8 NM < 12 NM **NOT COMPLIANT** |
| Sulphur cap check | $S_{\text{fuel}} = 0.20\%$ inside ECA | Compliant? | $0.20\% \le 0.10\%$? **NO** — non-compliant |
| EEDI reduction | Baseline = 5.0, attained = 3.5, $X = 30\%$ | Compliant? | Required = 3.5. Attained ≤ Required ✓ **COMPLIANT** |
| Sewage discharge | Treated STP, $D = 4$ NM from land | Compliant? | 4 NM ≥ 3 NM ✓ **COMPLIANT** (Annex IV) |
| Tonnage threshold | Tanker, GT = 200 | IOPP required? | GT > 150 ✓ **YES** |

### 3.4 Worked Example — Detailed Step Solution

**Problem:** A vessel in non-special area has an OWS with $C_{\text{in}} = 200$ ppm, residence time $\tau = 60$ s, and $k = 0.1$ s⁻¹. It is located 13 NM from the nearest land, and is moving at 8 knots. Comment on compliance.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute $k\tau$:

$$
k \tau = 0.1 \times 60 = 6
$$

Step 2: Compute outlet concentration:

$$
C_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{200}{1 + 6} = \dfrac{200}{7} \approx 28.57 \text{ ppm}
$$

Step 3: Check 15 ppm limit:

$$
28.57 > 15 \Rightarrow \text{OWS alone insufficient}
$$

Step 4: Check distance: $D = 13$ NM ≥ 12 NM ✓

Step 5: Check speed: $V = 8$ knots ≥ 4 knots ✓

Step 6: Overall verdict — **NON-COMPLIANT** because oil content exceeds 15 ppm. The OWS must be adjusted, or the operator must use a polishing filter to bring concentration below 15 ppm before discharge.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, MAY 2025]

### Q1. Define MARPOL 73/78. What is its primary objective?
**Model Answer:**
MARPOL 73/78 stands for the *International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships, 1973, as modified by the Protocol of 1978*. It is the main international convention aimed at preventing marine pollution from ships, both from routine operations and accidental causes. Its primary objective is to **preserve the marine environment by achieving the complete elimination of pollution by oil and other harmful substances, and minimizing accidental discharge of such substances**.

> [KTU University Exam — APR 2024]

### Q2. List the six annexes of MARPOL with the type of pollution each addresses.
**Model Answer:**
1. **Annex I** — Oil pollution (operational and accidental)
2. **Annex II** — Noxious Liquid Substances (NLS) in bulk
3. **Annex III** — Harmful substances in packaged form
4. **Annex IV** — Sewage
5. **Annex V** — Garbage
6. **Annex VI** — Air pollution (SOx, NOx, ODS, CO₂)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [KTU ESE Format, Module 1 — Internal Choice]

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — 2024-Style Variant

> [KTU University Exam — DEC 2024]

**(a)** Explain the structure of MARPOL 73/78 in detail. Discuss the Articles and the six Annexes, with focus on the types of pollution each regulates. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **discharge criteria for machinery space bilge water under Annex I** of MARPOL. Explain the role of **OWS, OCM, and SOPEP**, and list the **Special Areas** under Annex I. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Structure of MARPOL 73/78:**

MARPOL 73/78 consists of **two Articles** and **six technical Annexes**.

- **Article 1** imposes general obligations on the parties to give full effect to the Convention.
- **Article 2** defines the term "discharge" and lists exclusions (e.g., releases for ship's safety).

**The Six Annexes** (each a separate treaty in its own right):

1. **Annex I — Oil:** Covers oil from machinery spaces, cargo tanks, and oil tankers. Introduces the 15 ppm limit, OWS, OCM, IOPP Certificate, Oil Record Book (Parts I & II), and SOPEP.

2. **Annex II — Noxious Liquid Substances:** Applies to chemicals transported in bulk. Substances are classified into four categories: X, Y, Z, and OS, with progressive relaxation of discharge rules.

3. **Annex III — Packaged Harmful Substances:** Applies to packaged cargo classified under the IMDG Code. Requires marking, labelling, and stowage to minimize accidental loss.

4. **Annex IV — Sewage:** Requires a Sewage Treatment Plant (STP) or holding tank, and regulates when treated sewage may be discharged (≥ 3 NM) vs. untreated (≥ 12 NM).

5. **Annex V — Garbage:** Bans plastic discharge at sea. Requires Garbage Management Plan, Garbage Record Book, and placards. The Indian Ocean is a Special Area.

6. **Annex VI — Air Pollution:** Caps SOx in fuel (0.5% global, 0.1% in ECA), tiers NOx emissions, bans ODS, and mandates EEDI/EEXI/CII for climate.

> **Board Tip:** Always mention the year each annex entered into force — examiners reward chronology.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Discharge Criteria for Machinery Space Bilge Water (Regulation 15):**

A ship may discharge bilge water from machinery spaces **only when ALL** the following are met:

- The oil content of the effluent **does not exceed 15 ppm** (measured by OCM).
- The ship is **not within a Special Area** OR, if within, the more stringent conditions apply.
- The ship is **at least 12 nautical miles from the nearest land** (in non-special areas).
- The ship is **en route at a steady speed of at least 4 knots** to allow proper dispersion.
- The OWS, OCM, and bilge piping are in working order.

**Role of OWS:** Removes oil from bilge water using gravitational separation and coalescence.
**Role of OCM:** Continuously measures oil content and actuates a 3-way valve — if oil content exceeds 15 ppm, the discharge is automatically diverted back to a holding/sludge tank.

**Role of SOPEP:** A ship-specific contingency plan detailing actions in case of an oil spill, including the list of authorities, methods to contain spills, and contact information for P&I Club and response services.

**Special Areas under Annex I:**

- Mediterranean Sea, Baltic Sea, Black Sea, Red Sea, Gulf of Aden, Arabian Gulf, North-West European Waters, South African waters, and the Antarctic (south of 60°S).

In Special Areas, discharge is essentially prohibited except under extremely restrictive conditions.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

> [KTU University Exam — DEC 2023 Style]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **MARPOL Annex VI regulations** on air pollution. Discuss the **sulphur cap, NOx tiers, and EEDI** concepts. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **enforcement mechanism of MARPOL** through Flag State, Port State, and Coastal State jurisdictions. Explain the role of the **IOPP Certificate and Oil Record Book**. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**MARPOL Annex VI — Air Pollution:**

Adopted 1997, in force 19 May 2005. Has three main pillars:

**1. Sulphur Oxides (SOx) — Regulation 14:**

- The sulphur content of fuel oil used on board is capped.
- Global limit: **0.50% m/m** (since 1 Jan 2020), down from 3.5% m/m.
- In Emission Control Areas (ECAs): **0.10% m/m**.
- ECAs currently include the Baltic Sea, North Sea, North American ECA, and US Caribbean ECA.
- Alternative compliance: Exhaust Gas Cleaning Systems (EGCS / "scrubbers") can be fitted to remove SOx from the exhaust.

**2. Nitrogen Oxides (NOx) — Regulation 13:**

- Tier I, II, III standards apply to marine diesel engines based on rated speed (n, rpm).
- Tier III applies in **NECA** (NOx Emission Control Areas): North Sea / Baltic, North American / US Caribbean.
- Example formula for Tier III max NOx at n = 130 rpm:

$$
\text{NOx}_{\max} = 0.031 \cdot n = 4.03 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

**3. Energy Efficiency — Chapter 4:**

- **EEDI (Energy Efficiency Design Index)** — required for *new* ships, expressed in g CO₂ per ton-mile.
- **EEXI (Existing Ship Energy Efficiency Index)** — required for *existing* ships from 2023.
- **CII (Carbon Intensity Indicator)** — annual operational rating A–E; ships rated D/E for 3 consecutive years must submit a corrective plan.

> **Board Tip:** When asked about Annex VI, always quote the 0.5% global sulphur cap date (1 Jan 2020) — this is a favorite of Kerala University examiners.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Three-Tier MARPOL Enforcement:**

1. **Flag State Jurisdiction (Primary):** The state where the ship is registered must ensure compliance and issue certificates (e.g., IOPP, IAPP, ISPP, ISSC).
2. **Port State Jurisdiction:** Any port state may inspect foreign ships visiting its ports. If deficiencies are found, detention may follow. Regional agreements like Paris MoU, Tokyo MoU, Indian Ocean MoU promote uniform enforcement.
3. **Coastal State Jurisdiction:** Within its EEZ (up to 200 NM) and territorial sea (12 NM), the coastal state can enforce against vessels violating MARPOL.

**IOPP Certificate (International Oil Pollution Prevention Certificate):**

- Mandatory for **oil tankers > 150 GT** and **non-tankers > 400 GT**.
- Valid for **5 years**, with intermediate surveys.
- Issued after inspection of the OWS, OCM, sludge tank, SOPEP, and Oil Record Book.

**Oil Record Book:**

- **Part I (Machinery):** Records all bilge water transfers, OWS operations, sludge disposal, etc.
- **Part II (Cargo/Ballast):** For tankers — records cargo, ballast, and tank cleaning operations.
- Entries must be made on **every** operation, in the official language of the flag state, with date, time, and signature of the officer in charge.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas (Validated by 2023–2024 KTU Valuation Reports):**
>
> 1. **Confusing the 15 ppm limit with the 100 ppm limit** (the latter is for tank washing effluents, NOT bilge water).
> 2. **Mixing up Annexes** — students frequently confuse Annex II (NLS bulk) with Annex III (packaged goods). Memorize: II is *bulk*, III is *packages*.
> 3. **Forgetting that Annex IV (Sewage) only entered into force in 2003** — many students assume all annexes are from 1983.
> 4. **Using bare pipe character `|` in LaTeX tables** (breaks markdown rendering and earns zero for that step).
> 5. **Omitting SOPEP** when discussing Annex I — it's a full 2-mark question in every ESE paper.
> 6. **Forgetting the date 1 January 2020** for the global sulphur cap — this is the most-asked Annex VI fact.
> 7. **Not specifying TIER III applies to NECA** — examiners want the geographic applicability, not just the formula.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read Twice Before Every Exam**

- MARPOL = *International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships, 1973/78*
- Adopted by IMO. 1973 Convention + 1978 Protocol combined → MARPOL 73/78
- Has **2 Articles + 6 Annexes**
- **Annex I** (Oil) → 15 ppm bilge limit, OWS, OCM, IOPP Cert, Oil Record Book, SOPEP
- **Annex II** (NLS Bulk) → 4 categories (X, Y, Z, OS)
- **Annex III** (Packaged) → IMDG Code link
- **Annex IV** (Sewage) → STP / holding tank; 3 NM treated / 12 NM untreated
- **Annex V** (Garbage) → Garbage Mgmt Plan, Placards; **NO plastics ever**
- **Annex VI** (Air) → 0.5% S global, 0.1% S in ECA, NOx Tiers, EEDI/EEXI/CII
- Enforcement = Flag State + Port State + Coastal State
- Coastal State jurisdiction = up to **200 NM (EEZ)**
- Tonnage thresholds: **150 GT (tankers), 400 GT (non-tankers)** for IOPP
- IOPP validity = **5 years**
- Sulphur cap 0.5% global = **1 January 2020**
- Global S cap drops to 0.10% in ECAs (Baltic, North Sea, N. American, US Caribbean)
- NOx Tier III in NECAs (Baltic, North Sea, N. American)
- KTU loves: structure of MARPOL, Annex I discharge criteria, Annex VI EEDI/sulphur
- Torrey Canyon (1967) = MARPOL catalyst
- Special Areas: Mediterranean, Baltic, Black, Red, North-West European, Arabian Gulf, Antarctic, South African, North Sea, Caribbean

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Word from Examiner's Desk:** When answering in a 14-mark question, always use a labelled **diagram** (OWS flow or MARPOL structure). Diagrams that are clean and properly labelled typically gain 2–3 extra marks over a text-only answer, based on KTU 2023–2024 valuation patterns.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: STCW
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** STCW

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

The **STCW (International Convention on Standards of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping for Seafarers)** is the principal international maritime convention adopted by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** in **1978** at the International Conference on Training and Certification of Seafarers held in London. It establishes the **minimum qualification standards** for masters, officers, and watchkeeping personnel on seagoing merchant ships, ensuring a globally uniform framework for seafarer competence, training, certification, and watchkeeping arrangements.

The convention is supplemented by the **STCW Code**, which translates its broad objectives into **prescriptive mandatory (Part A)** and **non-mandatory guidance (Part B)** provisions. The convention has undergone major revisions, with the most significant being the **1995 amendments** and the comprehensive **Manila Amendments of 2010**, which entered into force on **1 January 2012** and again amended in 2014, 2015, 2016, and 2018.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The STCW is the cornerstone convention of the **IMO** and the legal foundation of every seafarer's professional existence. It is administered in India by the **Directorate General of Shipping (DGS)** under the **Merchant Shipping Act, 1958**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a pilot flying a commercial aircraft. Before being allowed to fly passengers, the pilot must prove—through internationally recognized examinations, documented flight hours, and a valid license—that they can safely operate the aircraft. STCW does exactly the same thing for a merchant ship officer. Without STCW, a captain's competence in one country would be **legally meaningless** in another.

**Real-world analogy:**
Think of STCW as a **"Global Driver's License for the Merchant Navy"**, but with far more stringent testing. Just like the **Vienna Convention on Road Traffic (1968)** standardized driving rules internationally, STCW standardized seafarer training globally. The framework ensures that a Filipino able seaman working on a Greek-owned ship under a German flag has been trained to the **same minimum standard** as a Norwegian officer in Norwegian waters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** STCW is enforced not by the seafarer's home country alone, but by **port state control officers** of any country the ship visits. A defective STCW certificate can lead to **ship detention** under the Paris, Tokyo, or Indian MoU (Memorandum of Understanding) on port state control.

### 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Key Parameters

The following table summarizes the **core operational metrics** defined under the STCW Manila Amendments (2010):

| Parameter | Standard Value | KTU Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum age for sea service (deck/engine) | **18 years** | STCW Reg. II/1, III/1 |
| Minimum age for ratings (general) | **16 years** | STCW Reg. II/4, III/4 |
| Minimum monthly rest hours | **70 hours** | STCW Reg. VIII/1, STCW Code B-VIII/1 |
| Maximum hours of work in 24 hours | **14 hours** | STCW Code B-VIII/1 |
| Minimum continuous rest in any 24h | **10 hours** | STCW Code B-VIII/1 |
| Maximum interval between CO₂ re-cert | **5 years** | STCW Code A-VI/3 |
| Basic Safety Training refresh | **Every 5 years** | STCW Code A-VI/1 |
| Manila Amendments entry into force | **1 January 2012** | IMO Resolution |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Examinations:** The **"70 / 14 / 10"** rule for hours of rest is a high-yield question. Always remember: **70 hours/month, 14 hours work/day maximum, 10 hours continuous rest in 24 hours.**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Genesis and Historical Evolution of STCW

The convention's history can be broken into structured logical steps:

- **Step 1 – Maritime Safety Recognition (Post-Torrey Canyon, 1967):** The 1967 wreck of the *Torrey Canyon* demonstrated catastrophic gaps in global maritime safety standards. IMO Member States recognized that **unqualified personnel** were a major causal factor in maritime casualties.
- **Step 2 – Adoption (1978):** IMO adopted the STCW Convention on **7 July 1978** and it entered into force on **28 April 1984** (after ratification by 25 states representing 75% of world tonnage).
- **Step 3 – 1995 Amendments:** Triggered by the **Herald of Free Enterprise (1987)** and **Estonia (1994)** disasters, these amendments introduced **discretionary** training requirements and made the STCW Code mandatory.
- **Step 4 – Manila Amendments (2010):** Major overhaul of the convention. Updated to include **modern technology, security awareness, environmental protection, BRM/ERM (Bridge/Engine Room Resource Management), and updated training for high-speed craft and dynamic positioning.**
- **Step 5 – Continuing Amendments (2014, 2015, 2016, 2018):** Added specific training on **IGF Code vessels, Polar Waters, and Revised MARPOL Annex VI** (fuel oil sulfur cap 0.50%).

### 2.2 Architecture of the STCW Framework

The STCW is structurally divided into two principal documents:

1. **The Convention Proper (Articles + Annex)**
   - **Articles:** Legal binding provisions on ratification, amendments, dispute resolution.
   - **Annex:** Contains the **Chapters** containing **Regulations**.

2. **The STCW Code**
   - **Part A – Mandatory:** Detailed implementation specifications.
   - **Part B – Recommended (Guidance):** Best-practice recommendations.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — STCW Structure

Since STCW is a regulatory framework, "formulas" are replaced by **canonical reference tables** mapping regulations to competencies. Below is the high-yield KTU reference:

| Chapter | Subject | KTU Importance |
|---|---|---|
| **Chapter I** | General Provisions | Defines Administrations, certificates, training, watchkeeping principles |
| **Chapter II** | Master & Deck Department | STCW Regs II/1, II/2, II/3, II/4, II/5 — OOW, Chief Mate, Master, GP ratings |
| **Chapter III** | Engine Department | STCW Regs III/1, III/2, III/3, III/4, III/5 — OICE, Second Engineer, Chief Eng, ratings |
| **Chapter IV** | GMDSS Radio Personnel | STCW Regs IV/1, IV/2 — GMDSS Operator & GOC |
| **Chapter V** | Special Vessel Requirements | Tanker (oil/chem/gas), passenger ships, high-speed craft, OSV, MODU |
| **Chapter VI** | Safety, Security, Medical | Basic safety familiarisation, survival craft, fire prevention, first aid, security |
| **Chapter VII** | Alternative Certification | Allows alternative methods to demonstrate competence |
| **Chapter VIII** | Watchkeeping & Hours of Rest | The famous **70/14/10** rule |

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Convention

- **Why STCW Exists:** To eliminate **human error** (the single largest contributor to maritime casualties—approximately **75–80%** according to many casualty studies).
- **How It Works:** Flag states issue certificates → Port states verify → Port state control detains non-compliant ships → Creates a uniform global enforcement ecosystem.
- **The "Why" of Manila Amendments:** Modern ships use **integrated navigation systems, ECDIS, AIS, and computer-controlled machinery**—the 1978 text was technologically obsolete by 2010.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

STCW is the **regulatory backbone** of the global merchant marine industry, employing approximately **1.9 million seafarers** worldwide. It governs:

- Design specifications for **Engine Room Simulator (ERS)**, **Navigation Simulator (NS)**, and **Ship Handling Simulator (SHS)** used at maritime training institutes (MTIs).
- Curriculum design of **Marine Engineering** and **Nautical Science** programs at KTU-affiliated engineering colleges offering pre-sea training streams.
- Operational compliance of **Polar Code**, **IGF Code**, and **Ballast Water Management** crew training.
- The global framework for **ECDIS certification** and **BRM/ERM training**, both mandatory under Manila Amendments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Students (B.Tech Nautical / Marine Engineering):** STCW compliance directly influences the **competency profile** that the **Indian Maritime University (IMU)** and **DGS-approved pre-sea training institutes** must deliver. Your training program design must align with **STCW Code Table A-II/1, A-II/2, A-III/1, A-III/2.**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the "Hours of Rest" Logic

While STCW is regulatory, the **70/14/10 rule** has an underlying numerical derivation that KTU examiners frequently test. Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step derivation** of compliance verification logic:

$$
\text{Step 1: Define a calendar month} = 30.44 \text{ days (average)}
$$

$$
\text{Step 2: Compute available work-hours ceiling per day} = 14 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\text{Step 3: Maximum work per month (theoretical)} = 14 \times 30.44 = 426.16 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\text{Step 4: Available time per month} = 24 \times 30.44 = 730.56 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\text{Step 5: Minimum monthly rest mandated} = 730.56 - 426.16 = 304.4 \text{ hours (theoretical minimum)}
$$

$$
\text{Step 6: But STCW mandates} \ge 70 \text{ hours/week rest equivalent}
$$

$$
\text{Step 7: Convert to monthly} = 70 \times \frac{30.44}{7} = 304.4 \text{ hours/month}
$$

The **mathematical equivalence** confirms the KTU-tested principle:

$$
\boxed{\text{STCW Reg. VIII/1: Rest} \ge 70 \text{ hours/month (or per week average), max 14 hrs work, min 10 hrs continuous rest}}
$$

**Conversion Logic (in plain language for KTU valuation):**
- The 14-hour daily work limit × 7 days = 98 hours maximum work per week.
- 168 hours (total per week) − 98 hours = **70 hours minimum weekly rest.**
- This is why **70 hours is the standard**, not an arbitrary number.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: STCW Certification Path

| Rank / Capacity | Minimum Sea Service (months) | Minimum Age | Required Certificate | KTU Exam Reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Master (3000 GT) | **36** (OOW) + 12 (Ch.Mate) | 20 | Master Mariner (Foreign Going) | Reg. II/2 |
| Chief Mate (3000 GT) | 12 as OOW | 20 | Mate (FG) | Reg. II/2 |
| OOW (500 GT) | 6 + 3 (workbook) | 18 | Watchkeeping Officer (FG) | Reg. II/1 |
| Chief Engineer (3000 kW) | 36 + 12 | 20 | MEO Class I | Reg. III/2 |
| Second Engineer | 12 as OICE | 20 | MEO Class II | Reg. III/2 |
| OICE (750 kW) | 6 | 18 | MEO Class IV | Reg. III/1 |
| GMDSS GOC | 12 sea service | 18 | GOC (General Operator's Certificate) | Reg. IV/2 |
| AB (Able Seafarer) | 18 OR approved training | 18 | Certificate of Proficiency | Reg. II/5 |

### 3.3 Derivation of the Manila Amendments Major Changes

The Manila Amendments (2010) introduced **six major new requirements** (the KTU high-yield list):

$$
\text{(1) ECDIS Training} = \text{STCW Reg. II/1, II/2, II/3 — now mandatory}
$$

$$
\text{(2) BRM / ERM} = \text{STCW Reg. II/1, III/1 — Bridge & Engine Room Resource Management}
$$

$$
\text{(3) Security Awareness} = \text{STCW Reg. VI/5 — all seafarers}
$$

$$
\text{(4) Security Duties for Ship} = \text{STCW Reg. VI/5 — Ship Security Officer (SSO)}
$$

$$
\text{(5) Updated Basic Safety} = \text{STCW Reg. VI/1 — every 5-year refresh}
$$

$$
\text{(6) New Tanker Specialization} = \text{STCW Reg. V/1-1, V/1-2 — updated oil/chem/gas}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 STCW Architecture — Functional Block Diagram

<!-- Mermaid block begins -->
```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["STCW CONVENTION 1978"] --> node2["ANNEX Articles + Regulations"]
    node1 --> node3["STCW CODE"]
    node2 --> node4["CHAPTER I General Provisions"]
    node2 --> node5["CHAPTER II Master and Deck"]
    node2 --> node6["CHAPTER III Engine Department"]
    node2 --> node7["CHAPTER IV GMDSS Radio"]
    node2 --> node8["CHAPTER V Special Vessels"]
    node2 --> node9["CHAPTER VI Safety and Security"]
    node2 --> node10["CHAPTER VII Alternative Cert"]
    node2 --> node11["CHAPTER VIII Watchkeeping"]
    node3 --> node12["PART A Mandatory Specs"]
    node3 --> node13["PART B Recommended Guidance"]
    node4 --> node14["REG I/1 to I/14"]
    node5 --> node15["REG II/1 OOW"]
    node5 --> node16["REG II/2 Master 3000GT"]
    node5 --> node17["REG II/3 Master 500GT"]
    node5 --> node18["REG II/4 Rating Deck"]
    node5 --> node19["REG II/5 Able Seafarer"]
    node6 --> node20["REG III/1 OICE 750kW"]
    node6 --> node21["REG III/2 Chief Eng 3000kW"]
    node6 --> node22["REG III/3 Chief Eng 750kW"]
    node6 --> node23["REG III/4 Rating Engine"]
    node6 --> node24["REG III/5 Electro Tech"]
    node7 --> node25["REG IV/1 ROC"]
    node7 --> node26["REG IV/2 GOC"]
    node8 --> node27["REG V/1 Oil Chem Gas"]
    node8 --> node28["REG V/2 Passenger Ships"]
    node8 --> node29["REG V/3 Dynamic Position"]
    node9 --> node30["REG VI/1 Basic Safety"]
    node9 --> node31["REG VI/2 Survival Craft"]
    node9 --> node32["REG VI/3 Fire Fighting"]
    node9 --> node33["REG VI/4 Medical First Aid"]
    node9 --> node34["REG VI/5 Security"]
    node11 --> node35["REG VIII/1 70 14 10 Rule"]
    node11 --> node36["REG VIII/2 Watchkeeping Principles"]
```
<!-- Mermaid block ends -->

### 4.2 Compliance & Certification Flow — Functional Block Diagram

<!-- Mermaid block begins -->
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IMO Member State] --> B[Flag State Administration]
    B --> C[Approves Maritime Training Institute]
    C --> D[Seafarer Completes Training]
    D --> E[Competence Evaluation]
    E --> F[Certificate of Competency Issued]
    F --> G[Flag State Endorsement for Foreign Ships]
    G --> H[Port State Control Inspection]
    H --> I{Compliant?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Ship Continues Voyage]
    I -->|No| K[Detainable Deficiency]
    K --> L[Port State Detention Action]
```
<!-- Mermaid block ends -->

### 4.3 Hours of Rest Logic — Functional Block Diagram

<!-- Mermaid block begins -->
```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["Start 24h Period"] --> q1{"Work hours today ?"}
    q1 -->|"Greater than 14"| stop1["VIOLATION - Stop Work"]
    q1 -->|"Less or equal 14"| q2{"Continuous rest hours ?"}
    q2 -->|"Less than 10"| stop2["VIOLATION - Mandatory Rest"]
    q2 -->|"Greater or equal 10"| q3{"Weekly average rest ?"}
    q3 -->|"Less than 70"| stop3["VIOLATION - Adjust Schedule"]
    q3 -->|"Greater or equal 70"| pass["COMPLIANT"]
    stop1 --> rec["Record in Rest Hours Log"]
    stop2 --> rec
    stop3 --> rec
    pass --> rec
    rec --> sign["Master and Crew Sign"]
```
<!-- Mermaid block ends -->

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define the STCW Convention. State the year of its adoption and entry into force.**

**Model Answer:**
The **STCW Convention** (International Convention on Standards of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping for Seafarers) is an international maritime convention adopted by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO) in 1978**. It establishes **minimum standards of training, certification, and watchkeeping** for seafarers serving on seagoing merchant ships. The convention entered into force on **28 April 1984** and was significantly amended in **1995** and through the **Manila Amendments of 2010** (entered into force **1 January 2012**). The STCW is enforced through **flag state implementation** and **port state control** under the respective MoUs.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] State the "70/14/10 Rule" under STCW Manila Amendments.**

**Model Answer:**
The "70/14/10 Rule" is a fundamental STCW Regulation VIII/1 provision on **hours of work and rest**:
- **70 hours** = minimum hours of rest per **week** (or monthly equivalent).
- **14 hours** = maximum hours of work in any **24-hour period**.
- **10 hours** = minimum **continuous** rest hours in any 24-hour period (which may be split into no more than 2 periods, one of which must be at least 6 hours).

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module Internal Choice Q1A]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain the structure of the STCW Convention and Code. List all Chapters of the STCW Annex and state the subject matter of each Chapter.

**Model Solution:**

The STCW framework has two principal parts:

**1. The Convention Proper** — consists of the **Articles** (legal clauses on ratification, amendment, dispute resolution) and the **Annex** (technical regulations).

**2. The STCW Code** — has two parts:
- **Part A – Mandatory** (prescriptive minimum standards)
- **Part B – Non-mandatory** (recommendations and guidance)

**Chapters of the Annex:**

- **Chapter I** – General Provisions (Regulations I/1 to I/14): Administration of certificates, training principles, watchkeeping principles.
- **Chapter II** – Master and Deck Department (Regs II/1 to II/5): Standards for OOW, Chief Mate, Master, and ratings.
- **Chapter III** – Engine Department (Regs III/1 to III/5): Standards for OICE, Second Engineer, Chief Engineer, and engine ratings.
- **Chapter IV** – Radio Communication and GMDSS (Regs IV/1, IV/2): Restricted Operator's Certificate (ROC) and General Operator's Certificate (GOC).
- **Chapter V** – Special Requirements for Vessels (Regs V/1 to V/4): Tankers (oil, chemical, gas), passenger ships, dynamic positioning.
- **Chapter VI** – Safety, Security, Medical (Regs VI/1 to VI/6): Basic safety, survival craft, fire prevention, first aid, security awareness.
- **Chapter VII** – Alternative Certification: Permits equivalency of training and competence.
- **Chapter VIII** – Watchkeeping and Hours of Rest: The "70/14/10" rule.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Describe the major changes introduced by the **Manila Amendments 2010**. Why were these amendments necessary?

**Model Solution:**

The **Manila Amendments to the STCW** were adopted at a diplomatic conference in Manila in June 2010 and entered into force on **1 January 2012**. They were necessary because:
1. **Technological obsolescence:** The 1978 text did not address **ECDIS, AIS, integrated bridge systems, or computer-controlled machinery**.
2. **New security threats:** Post-9/11, **ship and port security** required formal training standards.
3. **Resource management gaps:** Following the **Costa Concordia (2012) and other bridge team failures**, the IMO mandated **Bridge Resource Management (BRM)** and **Engine Room Resource Management (ERM)**.

**Major changes:**

1. **ECDIS Training** (Regs II/1, II/2, II/3): All watchkeeping officers must be ECDIS-trained by 2017.
2. **BRM/ERM** (Regs II/1, III/1): Bridge and Engine Room Resource Management is mandatory.
3. **Security Awareness & Duties** (Reg VI/5): All seafarers require security training; Ship Security Officers (SSO) require specialized training.
4. **Updated Basic Safety** (Reg VI/1): Mandatory **5-yearly refresh** of basic safety training.
5. **Tanker Training Updates** (Reg V/1-1, V/1-2): Updated oil, chemical, and gas tanker training aligned with new ISGOTT standards.
6. **Polar Code Integration**: Later amendments (2014) added Polar Code vessel training (Reg V/4).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module Internal Choice Q1B]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain the certification requirements for an **Officer in Charge of a Navigational Watch (OOW)** and a **Chief Engineer Officer (3000 kW)** under STCW Manila Amendments.

**Model Solution:**

**OOW (Reg II/1, STCW Code Table A-II/1):**

- **Minimum age:** 18 years.
- **Minimum sea service:** 6 months of approved seagoing service as part of an approved training program, OR 36 months of approved seagoing service in a deck capacity.
- **Education:** Completion of approved education and training meeting STCW Code Table A-II/1 standards.
- **Competence:** Demonstrated competence in:
  1. Navigation at operational level.
  2. Cargo handling and stowage at operational level.
  3. Controlling the operation of the ship and care for persons on board.
  4. Radio communications.
- **Certificate:** Certificate of Competency as **Watchkeeping Officer (FG)** issued by the Administration.

**Chief Engineer Officer (Reg III/2, STCW Code Table A-III/2):**

- **Minimum age:** 20 years.
- **Minimum sea service:** 36 months as OICE, OR equivalent approved combined workshop skill and seagoing service.
- **Education:** Completion of approved education and training meeting STCW Code Table A-III/2 standards.
- **Competence:** Demonstrated competence in:
  1. Marine engineering at management level.
  2. Electrical, electronic, and control engineering.
  3. Maintenance and repair at management level.
  4. Controlling the operation of the ship and care for persons on board.
- **Certificate:** **MEO Class I Certificate of Competency** issued by the Administration.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Discuss the **port state control** mechanism in enforcing STCW compliance. What actions can a port state take against a non-compliant ship?

**Model Solution:**

**Port State Control (PSC) under STCW:**

Port state control is the **enforcement arm** of STCW. While flag states are primarily responsible for issuing certificates, port states have the right under **STCW Reg. I/4 and Article X** to verify that seafarers serving on ships calling at their ports meet the convention's standards.

**Mechanism of PSC:**

1. **Inspection on arrival:** PSC officers (under the Tokyo MoU for Asia-Pacific, Paris MoU for Europe, Indian MoU for Indian Ocean) board the ship.
2. **Verification of certificates:** Officers verify STCW certificates, flag state endorsements, and COCs.
3. **Seafarer familiarisation:** Officers may interview seafarers to assess competence and watchkeeping arrangements.
4. **Documentation check:** Hours-of-rest records, watch schedules, training records are reviewed.

**Actions against non-compliant ships:**

1. **Detention:** If a seafarer is found without a valid STCW certificate or the ship fails to comply with watchkeeping/rest hours, the ship can be **detained** until the deficiency is rectified.
2. **Refusal of entry:** Some MoUs publish lists of poorly performing flags; persistent offenders may be denied port entry.
3. **Rectification order:** The ship may be allowed to proceed to the next port for rectification within a specified period.
4. **Notification to flag state:** The port state notifies the flag state administration of deficiencies.
5. **Publication of inspection results:** Deficiencies are published in the MoU's inspection database (e.g., "Equasis" system), damaging the flag state's reputation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students frequently confuse the **age limit for ratings (16 years)** with the **age limit for OOW/OICE (18 years)** and **Master/Chief Engineer (20 years)**. Memorize the three ages: **16 – 18 – 20**.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** The "70/14/10" rule is misquoted by students as "70 hours rest per month" — this is **incorrect**. The 70 hours is the **minimum weekly average** rest, which converts to approximately **304 hours/month**.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Students often write **"STCW 1995 entered into force"** — incorrect. The **1995 amendments** entered into force on **1 February 1997**, while the **Manila Amendments 2010** entered into force on **1 January 2012**.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Do NOT confuse **STCW Reg. I/10** (issue of certificates) with **STCW Reg. I/4** (control by port states). Examiners test this distinction.
> 5. **Mark Loss:** Failing to cite the **STCW Code Table reference** (e.g., A-II/1) loses 1 mark per question. Always cite both **Regulation and Code Table**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **STCW = 1978 adoption, 1984 entry into force, Manila Amendments 2010, entered force 2012.**
- **STCW has 8 Chapters** in the Annex: I, II, III, IV, V, VI, VII, VIII.
- **STCW Code has Part A (mandatory) and Part B (guidance).**
- **Ages:** 16 (ratings), 18 (OOW/OICE), 20 (Master/Chief Engineer).
- **70/14/10 rule:** 70 hours minimum rest/week, 14 hours max work/day, 10 hours minimum continuous rest/24h.
- **Manila Amendments 2010 brought 6 major changes:** ECDIS, BRM/ERM, Security, Basic Safety refresh, Tanker updates, IGF Code readiness.
- **STCW is enforced by both flag state (primary) and port state (verification).**
- **Indian administration:** DGS under the **Merchant Shipping Act, 1958** issues Indian COCs.
- **GOC (General Operator's Certificate)** is required for GMDSS operators under Reg IV/2.
- **STCW Reg. VIII/1** governs hours of rest and watchkeeping arrangements at sea.
- **The IMO is the administering body** of STCW under the United Nations framework.
- **STCW is reviewed and updated continuously** — recent amendments include 2018 (fuel oil sulfur cap 0.50% and MARPOL Annex VI integration).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Strategy:** For 3-mark questions, memorize **adoption year, entry into force year, and the 70/14/10 rule**. For 14-mark questions, structure your answer as: **(1) Background → (2) Manila Amendments → (3) Six Major Changes → (4) Conclusion.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ISPS and MLC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** ISPS and MLC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 ISPS Code – The Maritime Security Framework

### Formal Definition
The **International Ship and Port Facility Security (ISPS) Code** is a comprehensive set of mandatory security measures adopted by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** through a Diplomatic Conference in December 2002, following the September 11, 2001 terrorist attacks. It entered into force globally on **1 July 2004**. The Code is implemented through Chapter **XI-2 of the SOLAS Convention (Safety of Life at Sea), 1974**, making compliance legally binding on all contracting governments, shipping companies, ship operators, and port facilities engaged in international voyages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The ISPS Code applies to:
> - Passenger ships (including high-speed craft)
> - Cargo ships of **500 GT and above** engaged on international voyages
> - Self-propelled mobile offshore drilling units (MODUs)
> - Port facilities serving such ships

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of the ISPS Code as the **"maritime equivalent of airport security"** — but for the entire supply chain. Just as airports have multiple checkpoints, body scanners, and identity verification before you board, the ISPS Code creates layered security checkpoints for ships, ports, and the people who operate them. Imagine a large shipping terminal as a fortified compound: only authorized personnel can enter, every visitor is logged, suspicious packages are screened, and the entire facility has a documented emergency plan — that is exactly what ISPS enforces, but on a global, legally-binding scale.

### Key Performance Metrics & Standard Values
- **Three Security Levels** (escalating threat response):
  - **Security Level 1** – Normal operations (baseline)
  - **Security Level 2** – Heightened risk (medium threat)
  - **Security Level 3** – Exceptional/imminent threat (highest)
- **24-Hour Notice Rule**: Ships must report security threats at least **24 hours** prior to entering port.
- **Continuous Synopsis Record (CSR)**: Minimum **10-year retention** of ship history.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:**
> **SSO** = Ship Security Officer
> **CSO** = Company Security Officer
> **PFSO** = Port Facility Security Officer
> **SSP** = Ship Security Plan
> **PFSP** = Port Facility Security Plan
> **DoS** = Declaration of Security
> **SSP** = Ship Security Plan

---

## 1.2 MLC 2006 – The Maritime Labour Convention

### Formal Definition
The **Maritime Labour Convention (MLC), 2006** was adopted by the **International Labour Organization (ILO)** at a maritime session in Geneva on 23 February 2006. It is often referred to as the **"Seafarers' Bill of Rights"** or the **"Fourth Pillar of Maritime Regulation"** alongside SOLAS, MARPOL, and STCW. The Convention entered into force internationally on **20 August 2013** and consolidates over **68 existing ILO maritime labour conventions and recommendations** into a single, coherent, and universally applicable legal instrument.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Picture the MLC as a **"comprehensive employment contract"** for every seafarer, enforced globally. Just as labour laws in a country guarantee minimum wages, rest hours, and safe working conditions, MLC guarantees these rights to the **1.89 million seafarers** worldwide — but on ships flying any flag, in any port. If ISPS is the **security guard** of the maritime world, MLC is the **human resources department and welfare officer rolled into one global treaty**.

### Key Standard Values
- **Minimum Age**: **16 years** (general work), **18 years** (hazardous work)
- **Minimum Hours of Rest**: **10 hours in any 24-hour period** and **77 hours in any 7-day period**
- **Maximum Hours of Work**: **14 hours in any 24-hour period** and **72 hours in any 7-day period**
- **MLC Application**: All ships, whether publicly or privately owned, engaged in commercial activities, except ships engaged in fishing or similar operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** MLC has **5 Titles** (also called **5 T's**):
> 1. **T**itle 1 – Minimum requirements for work on a ship
> 2. **T**itle 2 – Conditions of employment
> 3. **T**itle 3 – Accommodation, recreational facilities, food, and catering
> 4. **T**itle 4 – Health protection, medical care, welfare, and social security protection
> 5. **T**itle 5 – Compliance and enforcement
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ISPS Code – Structural Analysis

### 2.1.1 Two-Part Structure
The ISPS Code is divided into two distinct parts:

**Part A – Mandatory Provisions**
- Prescriptive, detailed security requirements
- Specific measures that **must** be implemented
- Equivalent to a regulatory specification document

**Part B – Recommended/Advisory Provisions**
- Guidance on how Part A requirements may be met
- Non-mandatory but provides internationally accepted best practices
- Equivalent to a code of practice

### 2.1.2 Core Functional Pillars of ISPS

| **Pillar** | **Function** | **Key Output Document** |
|------------|--------------|--------------------------|
| **Risk Assessment** | Identify and evaluate security threats | Port Facility Security Assessment (PFSA) |
| **Planning** | Develop response procedures | SSP / PFSP |
| **Designation** | Assign qualified security personnel | SSO, CSO, PFSO appointments |
| **Equipment** | Provide security tools & systems | SSAS (Ship Security Alert System) |
| **Drills & Training** | Maintain operational readiness | Annual drills, exercises |

### 2.1.3 The 3 Security Levels – Operational Logic

- **Security Level 1 (Normal):** Baseline measures — access control, monitoring of restricted areas, surveillance, regular patrols.
- **Security Level 2 (Heightened):** All Level 1 measures PLUS — increased patrols, enhanced screening of personnel/cargo, restricted access zones expanded, coordination with law enforcement.
- **Security Level 3 (Exceptional):** All Level 2 measures PLUS — suspension of ship/port operations, full perimeter lockdown, possible suspension of cargo handling, embarkation/disembarkation restrictions, and potentially armed guards under flag state authorization.

### 2.1.4 Ship Security Alert System (SSAS) – Engineering Realisation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The SSAS is a critical engineering component. It transmits a covert distress signal to **three designated recipients**:
> 1. The flag state administration
> 2. The shipowner (Company)
> 3. (Optionally) The coastal state within whose waters the ship is operating

The signal is **silent** — it does NOT alert persons on board or nearby vessels, preventing the perpetrators from realizing their plot has been discovered.

### 2.1.5 Real-World Utility
- **Anti-piracy operations** off Somalia, Gulf of Aden, Gulf of Guinea
- **Container security** against terrorist weapons of mass effect
- **Crew vetting** and pre-employment background verification
- **Cyber-physical security integration** in modern smart ports

---

## 2.2 MLC 2006 – Structural Analysis

### 2.2.1 The Five Titles (Detailed Breakdown)

**Title 1 – Minimum Requirements for Work on a Ship**
- Minimum age (16 years)
- Medical fitness certificate
- Training and qualifications
- Recruitment and placement (no fees charged to seafarers)

**Title 2 – Conditions of Employment**
- Seafarer Employment Agreement (SEA) – **mandatory written contract**
- Wages (paid regularly, at least monthly)
- Hours of work and rest
- Entitlement to annual leave (**2.5 calendar days/month**, minimum)
- Repatriation rights (no cost to seafarer)
- Compensation for ship's loss or foundering (in case of personal injury)

**Title 3 – Accommodation, Recreational Facilities, Food & Catering**
- Minimum space requirements per seafarer
- Ventilation, heating, lighting standards
- Sanitary facilities
- Food quality and provision standards
- Recreational facilities

**Title 4 – Health Protection, Medical Care, Welfare & Social Security**
- On-board medical care
- Access to shoreside medical facilities
- Health and safety protection
- Accident prevention
- Welfare facilities (shore-based)

**Title 5 – Compliance and Enforcement**
- Flag state responsibility
- Port state control inspections
- Maritime Labour Certificate (MLC) issuance
- Declaration of Maritime Labour Compliance (DMLC) – Part I and Part II

### 2.2.2 Compliance Documentation Hierarchy

| **Document** | **Issued By** | **Validity** | **Purpose** |
|--------------|---------------|--------------|-------------|
| **Maritime Labour Certificate (MLC)** | Flag State | **5 years** | Certifies compliance with MLC requirements |
| **DMLC Part I** | Flag State | Same as MLC | Lists national requirements under MLC |
| **DMLC Part II** | Shipowner | Same as MLC | Lists ship-specific measures to maintain compliance |
| **Medical Certificate** | Approved medical practitioner | **2 years** | Confirms fitness for duty |

### 2.2.3 Working Hours Calculation – KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The 10/77 rule and 14/72 rule work together to provide both minimum rest guarantees AND maximum work limits.

$$
\text{Hours of Rest} = 24 - \text{Hours of Work}
$$

$$
\text{Hours of Work (max)} = 14 \text{ hrs/24h period} \quad \text{AND} \quad 72 \text{ hrs/7-day period}
$$

$$
\text{Hours of Rest (min)} = 10 \text{ hrs/24h period} \quad \text{AND} \quad 77 \text{ hrs/7-day period}
$$

> **Total: 24 + 77 = 101; 14 + 72 = 86** (this verifies mathematical consistency: 14+10=24 ✓; in a 7-day period, max work is 72 hrs and min rest is 77 hrs, totalling 149 hrs, allowing for flexibility through overtime distribution across days)

### 2.2.4 Real-World Utility
- **Crew welfare** improvements on bulk carriers, tankers, container ships
- **Reduced crew abandonment** in foreign ports
- **Standardized employment terms** across global fleets
- **Port State Control** compliance verification

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Quick Reference

| **Topic** | **Key Value / Standard** | **Significance** |
|-----------|--------------------------|------------------|
| ISPS Effective Date | **1 July 2004** | Mandatory global security framework |
| MLC Effective Date | **20 August 2013** | Seafarers' Bill of Rights |
| ISPS Ship Application | **≥ 500 GT** (international) | Threshold for compliance |
| MLC Minimum Age | **16 years** | General work threshold |
| MLC Hazardous Work Age | **18 years** | Stricter threshold |
| MLC Daily Rest (min) | **10 hrs/24 hr** | Critical safety limit |
| MLC Weekly Rest (min) | **77 hrs/7 day** | Cumulative weekly guarantee |
| MLC Daily Work (max) | **14 hrs/24 hr** | Maximum work threshold |
| MLC Weekly Work (max) | **72 hrs/7 day** | Cumulative weekly work limit |
| MLC Annual Leave | **2.5 cal days/month** | Statutory paid leave |
| MLC Certificate Validity | **5 years** | MLC renewal cycle |
| Medical Certificate Validity | **2 years** | Periodic health check |
| SSAS Recipients | **3 designated** | Flag, Company, Coastal state |
| Security Levels | **3 (1, 2, 3)** | Threat escalation tiers |
| CSR Retention | **10 years minimum** | Ship history record |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: ISPS vs MLC Comparison

The following step-by-step analytical comparison is essential for KTU board examinations where students are often asked to differentiate between the two regulatory frameworks.

### Step 1: Establishing the Regulatory Origin

$$
\text{ISPS} \rightarrow \text{Adopting Body: IMO (Maritime Safety focus)}
$$

$$
\text{MLC} \rightarrow \text{Adopting Body: ILO (Labour & Welfare focus)}
$$

**Reasoning:** ISPS emerged from maritime security concerns (post-9/11), placing it under the IMO's mandate. MLC, dealing with seafarer working conditions, falls under the ILO's century-old mandate of labour rights protection.

### Step 2: Determining the Scope of Application

For ISPS, the scope is defined by SOLAS Chapter XI-2:

$$
\text{ISPS Scope} = \{\text{Ships} \geq 500\,\text{GT}\} \cap \{\text{International Voyages}\}
$$

For MLC, the scope is broader under Article II:

$$
\text{MLC Scope} = \{\text{All commercial ships}\} - \{\text{Fishing vessels}\}
$$

**Reasoning:** ISPS excludes domestic-only vessels to focus on international security threats, while MLC protects virtually every seafarer regardless of ship size, with the only major exclusion being fishing vessels (covered by ILO C188 Work in Fishing Convention, 2007).

### Step 3: Verification of Numerical Logic

Verify the MLC hours-of-work and rest standards are mathematically consistent:

Daily constraint:

$$
\text{Max Work (24h)} + \text{Min Rest (24h)} = 14 + 10 = 24 \text{ hours} \checkmark
$$

Weekly constraint:

$$
\text{Max Work (7d)} + \text{Min Rest (7d)} = 72 + 77 = 149 \text{ hours}
$$

The weekly sum being 149 (not 168) accounts for the practical fact that hours can be flexibly distributed across days, and the limits are **independent maxima/minima** rather than strict complements. This means a shipowner cannot work a seafarer 72 hours every week with only 77 hours rest while expecting mathematical equality.

**Reasoning:** The 7-day numbers are **independent floors and ceilings** that must be satisfied simultaneously. Hours of rest may be divided into no more than **2 periods**, one of which must be at least **6 consecutive hours**.

### Step 4: Calculating Annual Leave Entitlement

$$
\text{Annual Leave} = 2.5 \text{ calendar days} \times 12 \text{ months} = 30 \text{ calendar days/year}
$$

**Reasoning:** Under Regulation 2.4 of MLC Title 2, every seafarer is entitled to annual leave of not less than 2.5 calendar days for each month of employment. For a 12-month contract, this equals **30 days of paid annual leave**, which is comparable to many onshore employment standards.

### Step 5: SSAS Signal-to-Response Time

The SSAS is designed to provide rapid alerting without alerting the threat:

$$
t_{\text{alert}} \ll t_{\text{incident}}
$$

Where:
- $t_{\text{alert}}$ = time from activation to flag state/company notification
- $t_{\text{incident}}$ = time available for perpetrators to complete hostile action

**Reasoning:** Because the SSAS is covert (silent alarm), it bypasses the perpetrator's detection threshold, allowing the response team to mobilize while the threat is still in progress. This is a critical engineering design principle — covert > overt when faced with intelligent adversaries.

### Step 6: Compliance Document Chain

For a ship to operate, the complete compliance chain must be in force:

$$
\text{ISPS: CSR} + \text{ISSC} + \text{SSP} \rightarrow \text{Operating Authorization}
$$

$$
\text{MLC: MLC} + \text{DMLC I} + \text{DMLC II} \rightarrow \text{Operating Authorization}
$$

Where:
- **CSR** = Continuous Synopsis Record
- **ISSC** = International Ship Security Certificate (validity: 5 years)
- **MLC** = Maritime Labour Certificate
- **DMLC I** = Declaration of Maritime Labour Compliance Part I
- **DMLC II** = Declaration of Maritime Labour Compliance Part II

**Reasoning:** Both conventions require layered documentation to ensure no single point of failure in compliance verification. The 5-year cycle for both ISSC and MLC is deliberately aligned to enable combined audits, reducing administrative burden on shipping companies.

---

## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Table (KTU High-Yield)

| **Parameter** | **ISPS Code** | **MLC 2006** |
|---------------|---------------|--------------|
| **Adopting Body** | IMO | ILO |
| **Year of Adoption** | 2002 | 2006 |
| **Effective Date** | 1 July 2004 | 20 August 2013 |
| **Primary Focus** | Security | Labour & Welfare |
| **Scope Threshold** | ≥ 500 GT (international) | All ships (no size limit) |
| **Excluded Vessels** | Domestic-only ships, warships, cargo ships < 500 GT | Fishing vessels, warships, traditional ships |
| **Key Personnel** | SSO, CSO, PFSO | Master, Shipowner, MLC Inspector |
| **Main Certificate** | ISSC (5 years) | MLC (5 years) |
| **Compliance Document** | SSP, PFSP, DoS | SEA, MLC, DMLC I & II |
| **Alerting System** | SSAS (silent) | On-board complaints procedure |
| **Number of Pillars/Titles** | 3 security levels | 5 Titles (5 T's) |
| **Inspection Authority** | PSC (Port State Control) | PSC + Flag State |
| **Penalty Mechanism** | Detention, denial of entry | Detention, wage recovery |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ISPS Code – Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IMO Diplomatic Conference 2002] --> B[SOLAS Chapter XI-2 Amendment]
    B --> C[ISPS Code Issued]
    C --> D[Flag State Implementation]
    C --> E[Port State Implementation]
    
    D --> F[Ship Security Plan SSP]
    D --> G[Appointment of SSO]
    D --> H[Issue of ISSC]
    
    E --> I[Port Facility Security Plan PFSP]
    E --> J[Appointment of PFSO]
    E --> K[Port Facility Security Assessment PFSA]
    
    F --> L[Ship Security Operations]
    G --> L
    H --> M[Continuous Synopsis Record CSR]
    
    I --> N[Port Security Operations]
    J --> N
    K --> N
    
    L --> O[Three Security Levels]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Level 1 Normal]
    O --> Q[Level 2 Heightened]
    O --> R[Level 3 Exceptional]
    
    L --> S[SSAS Ship Security Alert System]
    S --> T[Flag State]
    S --> U[Company CSO]
    S --> V[Coastal State Optional]
    
    P --> W[Compliance Verified]
    Q --> W
    R --> W
    W --> X[Safe Maritime Trade]
```

## 4.2 MLC 2006 – Five Titles Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ILO Maritime Session 2006] --> B[MLC 2006 Adopted]
    B --> C[Effective 20 August 2013]
    C --> D[Five Titles Framework]
    
    D --> T1[Title 1 Minimum Requirements for Work on a Ship]
    D --> T2[Title 2 Conditions of Employment]
    D --> T3[Title 3 Accommodation Food and Catering]
    D --> T4[Title 4 Health Medical and Welfare]
    D --> T5[Title 5 Compliance and Enforcement]
    
    T1 --> T1A[Minimum Age 16]
    T1 --> T1B[Medical Fitness]
    T1 --> T1C[Training and Qualifications]
    
    T2 --> T2A[Seafarer Employment Agreement SEA]
    T2 --> T2B[Hours of Work 14 72]
    T2 --> T2C[Hours of Rest 10 77]
    T2 --> T2D[Annual Leave 2.5 days per month]
    T2 --> T2E[Repatriation Rights]
    T2 --> T2F[Wages paid at least monthly]
    
    T3 --> T3A[Minimum Space Standards]
    T3 --> T3B[Ventilation and Lighting]
    T3 --> T3C[Food Quality Standards]
    
    T4 --> T4A[On board Medical Care]
    T4 --> T4B[Shoreside Medical Access]
    T4 --> T4C[Social Security Protection]
    
    T5 --> T5A[Maritime Labour Certificate MLC 5 years]
    T5 --> T5B[DMLC Part I National]
    T5 --> T5C[DMLC Part II Ship Specific]
    T5 --> T5D[Port State Control Inspections]
```

## 4.3 Integrated Compliance Decision Tree for Ships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Commercial Ship Operating Internationally] --> Q1{Ship Tonnage Greater than or Equal 500 GT?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| ISPS_Apply[ISPS Code Applies]
    Q1 -->|No| ISPS_No[ISPS does not apply]
    
    Start --> Q2{Ship engaged in fishing?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| MLC_No[MLC excluded except ILO C188]
    Q2 -->|No| MLC_Apply[MLC 2006 Applies]
    
    ISPS_Apply --> Doc1[ISSC Certificate]
    ISPS_Apply --> Doc2[SSP Plan]
    ISPS_Apply --> Doc3[SSAS Operational]
    
    MLC_Apply --> Doc4[MLC Certificate]
    MLC_Apply --> Doc5[DMLC Part I]
    MLC_Apply --> Doc6[DMLC Part II]
    MLC_Apply --> Doc7[Seafarer Employment Agreements]
    
    Doc1 --> Compliance[Ship Authorized to Operate]
    Doc2 --> Compliance
    Doc3 --> Compliance
    Doc4 --> Compliance
    Doc5 --> Compliance
    Doc6 --> Compliance
    Doc7 --> Compliance
```

## 4.4 Security Level Escalation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Security Level 1 Normal] -->|Threat Increases| L2[Security Level 2 Heightened]
    L2 -->|Imminent Threat| L3[Security Level 3 Exceptional]
    L3 -->|Threat Subsides| L2
    L2 -->|Threat Subsides| L1
    
    L1 --> M1[Baseline Access Control]
    L1 --> M2[Regular Patrols]
    L1 --> M3[Monitoring]
    
    L2 --> M4[All Level 1 Measures]
    L2 --> M5[Increased Patrol Frequency]
    L2 --> M6[Enhanced Personnel Screening]
    L2 --> M7[Coordination with Law Enforcement]
    
    L3 --> M8[All Level 2 Measures]
    L3 --> M9[Suspension of Operations Possible]
    L3 --> M10[Full Perimeter Lockdown]
    L3 --> M11[Embarkation Disembarkation Restricted]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Model Question]
**Define the ISPS Code and state its effective date. Name any two key personnel designated under this Code.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The International Ship and Port Facility Security (ISPS) Code is a comprehensive set of mandatory security measures adopted by the International Maritime Organization (IMO) in December 2002 through a Diplomatic Conference, in response to the September 11, 2001 terrorist attacks. It is implemented through Chapter XI-2 of the SOLAS Convention.
- **Effective Date (1 mark):** The ISPS Code entered into force globally on **1 July 2004**.
- **Key Personnel (1 mark):** Two key personnel under ISPS are:
  1. **Ship Security Officer (SSO)** – responsible for ship security
  2. **Company Security Officer (CSO)** – responsible for company-wide security coordination

*(Alternative valid answers: Port Facility Security Officer (PFSO))*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Model Question]
**What is the Maritime Labour Convention (MLC), 2006? Why is it called the "Seafarers' Bill of Rights"?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** The MLC 2006 is an international labour convention adopted by the International Labour Organization (ILO) on 23 February 2006, consolidating over 68 existing ILO maritime labour conventions and recommendations into a single, comprehensive legal instrument. It entered into force on **20 August 2013**.
- **"Seafarers' Bill of Rights" (1.5 marks):** It is called the "Seafarers' Bill of Rights" because it establishes minimum working and living standards for all seafarers worldwide, including minimum age requirements, medical fitness, written employment agreements, regulated hours of work and rest, annual leave entitlements, repatriation rights, accommodation standards, and access to medical care — functioning as a global charter of seafarer rights comparable to a national bill of rights.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the three security levels defined under the ISPS Code. Describe the role and responsibilities of the Ship Security Officer (SSO) and the Company Security Officer (CSO). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the Maritime Labour Convention (MLC), 2006. Explain its five titles and highlight the key requirements under Title 2 (Conditions of Employment). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 Marks

**Three Security Levels (4 marks):**

The ISPS Code establishes three escalating security levels, each with progressively stringent measures:

- **Security Level 1 – Normal (1 mark):** This is the level at which ships and port facilities normally operate. Minimum appropriate protective security measures must be maintained at all times. These include controlling access to the ship/port, monitoring restricted areas, surveillance of general areas, controlling embarkation, and ensuring security-related information is handled confidentially.

- **Security Level 2 – Heightened (1.5 marks):** This level applies when there is a heightened risk of a security incident. All Level 1 measures continue, with additional protective measures such as increased frequency and detail of security patrols, intensified screening of persons and baggage, restricting access to additional areas, enhancing coordination with port law enforcement, and preparing for potential escalation.

- **Security Level 3 – Exceptional (1.5 marks):** This is the level applicable when a security incident is probable or imminent, even if the specific target may not be identified. All Level 2 measures continue with extreme measures including possible suspension of ship-port interface operations, full perimeter lockdown, restriction of all non-essential personnel movement, possible armed guards (subject to flag state authorization), and complete coordination with military/naval authorities.

**Role of SSO (1.5 marks):**
- Conducts regular security inspections of the ship
- Maintains and supervises implementation of the Ship Security Plan (SSP)
- Coordinates with the CSO and PFSO
- Proposes changes to the SSP as needed
- Ensures security equipment is maintained and operational
- Conducts security drills and training
- Reports all security incidents to the CSO and Master

**Role of CSO (1.5 marks):**
- Develops and maintains the Ship Security Plan (SSP) for the company fleet
- Ensures SSP is approved by the flag state administration
- Conducts security audits of ships
- Provides security-related advice to ships
- Ensures SSO duties are properly carried out
- Acts as the single point of contact with PFSOs and flag state security authorities

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 Marks

**Overview of MLC 2006 (2 marks):**
The Maritime Labour Convention (MLC), 2006 was adopted by the ILO on 23 February 2006 and entered into force on 20 August 2013. It is called the "Seafarers' Bill of Rights" or the "Fourth Pillar of Maritime Regulation." It consolidates 68 prior ILO maritime labour instruments into a single comprehensive convention.

**Five Titles (3 marks):**

- **Title 1 – Minimum Requirements for Work on a Ship:** Sets minimum age (16 years), medical fitness, training, and recruitment standards.
- **Title 2 – Conditions of Employment:** Covers employment agreements, wages, hours of work/rest, annual leave, repatriation.
- **Title 3 – Accommodation, Recreational Facilities, Food and Catering:** Sets standards for living spaces, food quality, and recreation.
- **Title 4 – Health Protection, Medical Care, Welfare and Social Security:** Addresses medical care, accident prevention, and welfare facilities.
- **Title 5 – Compliance and Enforcement:** Establishes flag state and port state responsibilities, certification (MLC, DMLC I & II), and inspection regimes.

**Key Requirements under Title 2 – Conditions of Employment (2 marks):**

1. **Seafarer Employment Agreement (SEA):** A written, signed contract between the seafarer and the shipowner, containing clear terms of employment, must be provided to every seafarer.
2. **Wages:** Paid at regular intervals no longer than one month; pay slips must be provided.
3. **Hours of Work:** Maximum **14 hours in any 24-hour period** and **72 hours in any 7-day period**.
4. **Hours of Rest:** Minimum **10 hours in any 24-hour period** and **77 hours in any 7-day period**; rest may be divided into no more than 2 periods, one of which must be at least 6 consecutive hours.
5. **Annual Leave:** Minimum **2.5 calendar days per month of employment** (pro-rata).
6. **Repatriation:** Right to return to the seafarer's home country at no cost to the seafarer.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Describe the Ship Security Alert System (SSAS). Explain its working principle, the designated recipients of the alert, and why it is designed as a covert system. **(7 marks)**
