# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the Hierarchy of Controls in occupational safety. Why is PPE considered the last line of defense?**

**Model Answer:**

The Hierarchy of Controls is a five-tiered system used in occupational safety to minimize exposure to hazards. The tiers, in order of effectiveness, are:

1. **Elimination** — Complete removal of the hazard (most effective).
2. **Substitution** — Replacing the hazard with a less dangerous alternative.
3. **Engineering Controls** — Physical changes such as ventilation, machine guards, or enclosure to isolate workers.
4. **Administrative Controls** — Changes in work procedures, training, signage, and Permit-to-Work systems.
5. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** — Equipment worn by the worker (least preferred).

PPE is the last line of defense because it **does not eliminate the hazard** but merely creates a barrier between the worker and the hazard. PPE can fail due to improper fit, wear, incorrect use, or human error. Effective protection requires the higher tiers (Elimination through Administrative) to reduce the risk to a level where PPE is a redundant safeguard, not a primary one.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**List any six types of Personal Protective Equipment used aboard ships and state the hazard each protects against.**

**Model Answer:**

| SI No. | PPE | Hazard Protected Against |
|--------|-----|--------------------------|
| 1 | Hard hat (EN 397) | Falling objects, head impact in engine rooms and cargo holds |
| 2 | Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus (SCBA) | Oxygen-deficient or toxic atmospheres in enclosed spaces |
| 3 | Steel-toe safety boots (EN ISO 20345) | Heavy falling objects, sharp materials, slipping on wet decks |
| 4 | Earplugs / Earmuffs (EN 352) | Engine room noise exceeding 85 dB(A) |
| 5 | Full-body harness (EN 361) | Falls from height during work on masts, gangways, and offshore platforms |
| 6 | Flame-resistant coveralls (EN ISO 11612) | Flash fire and arc flash during hot work and engine room operations |

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

### Question A

**(a)** Describe the design features and functional requirements of an **immersion suit** as specified under SOLAS Chapter III. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vessel's engine room has a measured noise level of **102 dB(A)**. A worker spends **2 hours** at this level and **6 hours** in an adjacent control room at **75 dB(A)**. Using the OSHA exchange rate of **5 dB**, determine:
- (i) The permissible exposure time at 102 dB(A) **(2 marks)**
- (ii) The daily noise dose as a percentage **(2 marks)**
- (iii) The 8-hour Time-Weighted Average (TWA) **(3 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Immersion Suit Design Features (7 marks)

An **immersion suit** is a protective garment designed to minimize body heat loss when the wearer is immersed in cold water, thereby extending survival time during ship abandonment.

**SOLAS Chapter III, LSA Code Chapter 2.3 specifies the following requirements:**

1. **Thermal Insulation** — The suit must reduce the body's cooling rate such that the wearer's core temperature does not fall below **+2 °C** for **at least 6 hours** in water at **−1 °C**, provided the wearer wears appropriate clothing underneath.

2. **Buoyancy** — The suit should provide a minimum buoyancy of **70 N** in fresh water. The combined suit plus lifejacket buoyancy should turn an unconscious wearer face-up within **5 seconds** and maintain the head clear of water during breathing.

3. **Material** — Constructed from waterproof, water-vapor-resistant, fire-retardant material. The outer shell should be highly visible (typically orange or yellow) for search and rescue identification.

4. **Closure System** — Watertight closures (zippers, Velcro flaps, or both) to prevent water ingress. The suit must be **donable within 2 minutes** without assistance and **within 5 minutes** with assistance from one other person.

5. **Accessories** — The suit must include:
   - A whistle for signaling
   - A lifting loop on the back to assist recovery from the water
   - A buddy line attachment point
   - A pocket for a personal locator beacon (PLB)
   - Reflective tape strips for visibility

6. **Sizes and Fit** — Manufactured in multiple sizes with adjustable cuffs to minimize water flushing. The suit must accommodate different body dimensions and allow freedom of movement for climbing into a life raft.

7. **Inspection and Servicing** — Immersion suits are inspected **annually** and replaced at intervals not exceeding **3 years** of service life, depending on manufacturer specifications and condition.

#### Part (b) — Noise Exposure Calculation (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Sound level in engine room: $L_1 = 102$ dB(A), time: $C_1 = 2$ hours
- Sound level in control room: $L_2 = 75$ dB(A), time: $C_2 = 6$ hours
- OSHA exchange rate: 5 dB
- Reference: 90 dB(A) for 8 hours

##### (i) Permissible Exposure Time at 102 dB(A) (2 marks)

The permissible exposure time formula is:

$$T_1 = 8 \times 2^{\left(\frac{90 - L_1}{5}\right)}$$

$$T_1 = 8 \times 2^{\left(\frac{90 - 102}{5}\right)}$$

$$T_1 = 8 \times 2^{(-2.4)}$$

$$T_1 = 8 \times 0.1895$$

$$T_1 = 1.516 \text{ hours} \approx 1 \text{ hour } 31 \text{ minutes}$$

**Answer:** The permissible exposure time at 102 dB(A) is **1.52 hours (1 hr 31 min)**.

##### (ii) Daily Noise Dose as Percentage (2 marks)

For $L_2 = 75$ dB(A):

$$T_2 = 8 \times 2^{\left(\frac{90 - 75}{5}\right)} = 8 \times 2^{3} = 64 \text{ hours}$$

Since $C_2 = 6$ hours and $T_2 = 64$ hours, the contribution is negligible.

$$Dose = 100 \times \left(\frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2}\right)$$

$$Dose = 100 \times \left(\frac{2}{1.516} + \frac{6}{64}\right)$$

$$Dose = 100 \times (1.319 + 0.0938)$$

$$Dose = 100 \times 1.4128 = 141.28\%$$

**Answer:** The daily noise dose is **141.28%**, which exceeds 100% (the permissible limit).

##### (iii) 8-Hour Time-Weighted Average (3 marks)

$$TWA = 16.61 \log_{10}\left(\frac{Dose}{100}\right) + 90$$

$$TWA = 16.61 \log_{10}(1.4128) + 90$$

$$\log_{10}(1.4128) = 0.1501$$

$$TWA = 16.61 \times 0.1501 + 90$$

$$TWA = 2.493 + 90 = 92.49 \text{ dB(A)}$$

**Answer:** The TWA is **92.49 dB(A)**, which exceeds the OSHA PEL of 90 dB(A).

**Conclusion:** The worker is **overexposed** to noise. Engineering controls (enclosures, damping) and mandatory dual hearing protection (earplugs + earmuffs) are required.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

### Question B

**(a)** Discuss the components and operational procedure of a **Permit-to-Work (PTW) system** for confined space entry aboard a tanker vessel. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A worker of mass **90 kg** falls from a height of **3.5 m**. The lanyard has a stretch distance of **1.0 m** and harness sag of **0.4 m**. Calculate the peak fall arrest force and verify whether the system is compliant with the OSHA 6 kN limit. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Permit-to-Work System for Confined Space Entry (7 marks)

A **Confined Space Entry Permit (CSEP)** is a written authorization required before personnel enter an enclosed space such as a cargo tank, ballast tank, cofferdam, or void space aboard a tanker.

**Key Hazards in Tanker Confined Spaces:**
- Oxygen deficiency (O₂ < 19.5%)
- Toxic gases (H₂S, benzene, CO, hydrocarbon vapors)
- Flammable atmospheres (LEL > 1%)
- Engulfment by cargo residue or sludge
- Restricted entry/exit (single small hatch)
- Heat stress and noise

**Components of the CSEP:**

1. **Permit Issuer** — Responsible officer (Chief Officer or Master) trained in PTW procedures.
2. **Permit Holder / Entrant** — Trained crew member performing the work.
3. **Standby Person** — Positioned outside the space to maintain communication and initiate rescue.
4. **Rescue Team** — Pre-arranged, equipped with SCBA, harnesses, and lifting gear.

**Operational Procedure:**

1. **Step 1 — Hazard Assessment:** Conduct a Job Safety Analysis (JSA). Identify previous cargo, check Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS).
2. **Step 2 — Isolation:** Isolate the space from all energy sources (valve line-up, blinding, LOTO).
3. **Step 3 — Ventilation:** Force-ventilate the space with at least **6 air changes per hour** for a minimum of 30 minutes before testing.
4. **Step 4 — Atmospheric Testing:** Test for O₂ (19.5–23.5%), LEL (< 1%), H₂S (< 10 ppm), and CO (< 25 ppm) using a calibrated 4-gas detector.
5. **Step 5 — Issue Permit:** Complete the CSEP with date, time, location, work description, hazard controls, PPE, validity period (typically **8 hours maximum**), and signatures of Issuer, Holder, and Master.
6. **Step 6 — Continuous Monitoring:** Atmospheric levels monitored continuously by the standby person.
7. **Step 7 — Permit Closure:** Permit cancelled and signed off after work completion. Confined space secured and re-isolated.

**Validity:** Permits expire at the end of shift or after 8 hours, whichever is sooner. Re-issuance requires re-testing the atmosphere.

#### Part (b) — Fall Arrest Force Calculation (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Worker mass: $m = 90$ kg
- Free-fall height: $h = 3.5$ m
- Lanyard stretch: $d = 1.0$ m
- Harness sag: $L_a = 0.4$ m
- Compliance limit: $F_{limit} = 6$ kN = 6000 N
- Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Formula:**

$$F_{peak} = \frac{2 \cdot m \cdot g \cdot h}{d + L_a}$$

**Substitution:**

$$F_{peak} = \frac{2 \times 90 \times 9.81 \times 3.5}{1.0 + 0.4}$$

**Numerator:**

$$2 \times 90 \times 9.81 \times 3.5 = 6179.4 \text{ N·m}$$

**Denominator:**

$$1.0 + 0.4 = 1.4 \text{ m}$$

**Result:**

$$F_{peak} = \frac{6179.4}{1.4} = 4413.86 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{peak} = 4.41 \text{ kN}$$

**Compliance Check:**

$$F_{peak} = 4.41 \text{ kN} < F_{limit} = 6 \text{ kN}$$

**Answer:** The peak fall arrest force is **4.41 kN**, which is **below the OSHA 6 kN limit**. The system is **compliant and safe** for use in this fall scenario.

**Engineering Note:** The safety margin is $6 - 4.41 = 1.59$ kN. Any increase in free-fall height or decrease in lanyard stretch would reduce the safety margin. Always select lanyards with maximum elongation and a shock absorber for high-fall-hazard tasks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing the **OSHA (5 dB) exchange rate** with the **NIOSH (3 dB) exchange rate**. KTU 2024 Scheme questions typically specify which one to use — read carefully.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to **convert decimal hours to hours-minutes** in the TWA question. Always express final answers in human-readable format.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In PTW questions, students often **omit the rescue plan** — the standby person and rescue equipment are mandatory marks (2 marks minimum).
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing the **force unit** (N vs. kN) in fall arrest calculations. 1 kN = 1000 N — students often write 4413.86 as the final answer without converting.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** For immersion suits, students forget the **6-hour / −1 °C thermal performance** and **70 N buoyancy** numbers — these are favorites of KTU evaluators.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Personnel Protection**

- ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls**: Elimination > Substitution > Engineering > Administrative > **PPE (last)**
- ✅ **PPE Categories**: Head, Eye/Face, Hearing, Respiratory, Hand, Foot, Fall, Body
- ✅ **OSHA PEL for Noise**: 90 dB(A) over 8 hours; OSHA exchange rate = **5 dB**; Action level = 85 dB(A)
- ✅ **NIOSH Exchange Rate**: 3 dB (more conservative)
- ✅ **TWA Formula**: $TWA = 16.61 \log_{10}\left(\frac{Dose}{100}\right) + 90$
- ✅ **Lifejacket Buoyancy**: Minimum **150 N** (SOLAS adult)
- ✅ **Immersion Suit**: 6-hour thermal protection in **−1 °C** water, 70 N buoyancy
- ✅ **Fall Arrest Force Limit**: Peak force must remain **< 6 kN**
- ✅ **Fall Protection Height Threshold**: **1.8 m** (some standards 2 m)
- ✅ **EEBD Air Supply**: Minimum **10 minutes** (SOLAS Ch. II-2)
- ✅ **WBGT Heat Stress Formula**: $WBGT = 0.7 T_{nw} + 0.2 T_g + 0.1 T_{db}$
- ✅ **PTW Validity**: Maximum **8 hours** per permit
- ✅ **Atmospheric Safe Limits for Confined Space**: O₂ 19.5–23.5%, LEL < 1%, H₂S < 10 ppm
- ✅ **EPIRB Frequency**: 406 MHz (COSPAS-SARSAT satellite)
- ✅ **SART Frequency**: 9 GHz (X-band radar)
- ✅ **Regulatory Framework**: SOLAS, MLC 2006, ILO 152, ISM Code, ISPS Code
- ✅ **Key Standards**: EN 397 (helmets), EN 166 (eye), EN 352 (hearing), EN 361 (harness), EN 137 (SCBA), EN ISO 20345 (footwear), EN 388 (gloves)
- ✅ **Hazard Types Aboard Ships**: Mechanical, Chemical, Electrical, Fire, Ergonomic, Psychosocial, Environmental (heat/cold/noise), Confined Space, Working at Height
- ✅ **SMS Pillars**: Safety Policy → Risk Assessment → Implementation → Monitoring → Management Review (PDCA Cycle)

> **Final Exam Tip:** Always cite the relevant **IMO Convention** (e.g., SOLAS Chapter III for LSA) or **ISO/EN Standard** (e.g., EN 361 for harnesses) in your answers. Examiners allocate **1–2 marks specifically for correct regulatory citation** in 14-mark questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Extinguishers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fire Extinguishers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Fire Extinguisher** is a portable or wheeled active fire protection device, designed to be manually operated and utilized for the purpose of extinguishing or controlling incipient stage fires. In the maritime context, a fire extinguisher constitutes the **first line of defence** against the outbreak of fire on board a vessel, and its design, type, placement, and maintenance are strictly regulated by the **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS) Chapter II-2** and the **International Maritime Organization (IMO) Fire Safety Systems (FSS) Code**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Definition to Memorize:**
> "A fire extinguisher is a pressure-vessel-based device that discharges a fire-suppressing agent (media) through a controlled nozzle to interrupt one or more elements of the *fire tetrahedron* — fuel, oxidizer, heat, or chemical chain reaction."

### 1.2 The Fire Tetrahedron — Conceptual Foundation

Before understanding extinguishers, a student must grasp **what fire is**. Modern combustion science (post-1990s) models fire as a **tetrahedron** with four equal sides:

1. **Fuel** — Combustible material (solid, liquid, gas)
2. **Oxidizer** — Usually atmospheric oxygen (O₂ ≥ 16% concentration is required for sustained combustion)
3. **Heat** — Sufficient activation energy to maintain the reaction (pyrolysis temperature)
4. **Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction** — Self-sustaining radical propagation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Historical Note:** Earlier fire models used the *fire triangle* (Fuel-Oxygen-Heat). The fourth element (chain reaction) was added to explain why certain extinguishing agents like *dry chemical powder* work even when they merely interrupt the chemistry of combustion, without cooling the fuel significantly.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> **Analogy: "The Four-Legged Stool of Fire"**
> Imagine fire as a **four-legged stool**. A stool remains standing only as long as *all four legs* are intact. If you remove even one leg, the stool collapses.
> - **Leg 1 (Fuel):** Remove it by starving the fire of material → e.g., shut off oil supply.
> - **Leg 2 (Oxygen):** Smother it with foam, CO₂, or a fire blanket.
> - **Leg 3 (Heat):** Cool it with water until below ignition temperature.
> - **Leg 4 (Chain Reaction):** Chemically interrupt it with *dry powder* or *halogenated agents* (now mostly phased out due to ozone-depletion concerns).
>
> **A fire extinguisher is simply a tool engineered to "kick out" one or more of these legs as quickly and effectively as possible.**

### 1.4 International Fire Classification (ISO/IMO/EN Standard)

Fires are classified into **five internationally recognized classes** (Class F = Class K in US terminology):

| Class | Fuel Type | Typical Examples | Required Extinguishing Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class A** | Ordinary combustibles | Wood, paper, cloth, rubber, plastic | Cooling with water (quench) |
| **Class B** | Flammable liquids | Diesel, lube oil, petrol, solvents | Smothering / oxygen exclusion |
| **Class C** | Flammable gases | LPG, CNG, methane, acetylene | Shut off supply; dry powder |
| **Class D** | Combustible metals | Magnesium, sodium, lithium, titanium | Special dry powder (NaCl, Cu powder) |
| **Class F / K** | Cooking oils/fats | Vegetable/animal oils at > 250 °C | Wet chemical (saponification) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Electrical Fires** are not given a separate class. They are categorized under the class of the surrounding fuel, but **must be fought with non-conductive agents** (CO₂, dry powder) until the electricity is confirmed isolated.

### 1.5 Key Maritime Statutory Metrics (Memorize)

- **Minimum required portable extinguishers (SOLAS II-2/10):** Sufficient number such that **no person has to travel more than 10 metres** to reach one.
- **Inspection frequency (MSC.1/Circ.1432):** **Monthly** visual inspection; **annual** thorough maintenance by certified servicing agent.
- **Hydrostatic test pressure:** Typically **1.5 × working pressure** held for **60 seconds** as per EN 3-9 / IMO standards.
- **Recharging interval:** **Every 5 years** (CO₂) to **annually** (most others), or immediately after any discharge.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic of a Fire Extinguisher

Every portable fire extinguisher, regardless of type, follows a **common functional architecture**:

1. **Pressure Vessel (Body):** Stores the extinguishing medium, either pre-charged or with a separate propellant cartridge.
2. **Propellant System:** Compressed gas (N₂, CO₂, or air) at pressures typically between **8 bar to 174 bar** (depending on type).
3. **Control Valve & Head Assembly:** Quick-release lever or squeeze-grip; usually with a safety pin and tamper seal.
4. **Discharge Hose & Nozzle:** Directs the agent; specialized nozzles create spray patterns (e.g., foam nozzle for Class B) or focused jets (e.g., CO₂ horn).
5. **Pressure Indicator (Gauge):** Confirms readiness.
6. **Extinguishing Agent:** The actual firefighting media.

### 2.2 Detailed Extinguisher Type Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Water-Based Extinguishers
- **Mechanism:** Cools the fuel below its ignition temperature (removes the *heat leg*).
- **Working pressure:** ~ 12 bar (stored pressure) or 7 bar (gas cartridge).
- **Variants:**
  - *Water spray* — gentle application, suitable for Class A.
  - *Water with additive (wet chemical)* — contains surfactants, slightly better penetration.
- **Effectiveness:** Excellent for Class A; **DANGEROUS for Class B, C, F, and electrical fires** (water sinks below burning oil, vaporizes violently, and may cause flashover/steam explosion).

#### 2.2.2 Foam Extinguishers (AFFF — Aqueous Film Forming Foam)
- **Mechanism:** Forms a *film* on the surface of flammable liquids. The film **smothers the fire** (excludes oxygen) and also cools the fuel surface.
- **Application:** Class A and Class B fires; **mandatory on tankers** for oil-spill deck fires.
- **Saponification note:** *Alcohol-resistant foam (AR-AFFF)* is required for polar solvents (alcohols, ketones), as standard foam is destroyed by these fuels.
- **Discharge time:** 30 – 90 seconds for a 9 L unit.
- **Throw range:** ~ 6 – 8 m.

#### 2.2.3 Dry Chemical Powder (DCP) Extinguishers
- **Mechanism:** Primarily **chemical chain-breaking** (interrupts Leg 4 of the tetrahedron). The monoammonium phosphate (ABC powder) decomposes into free radicals that terminate combustion chain carriers. Secondary action: minor smothering.
- **Standard agent:** **ABC powder** (Monoammonium Phosphate 40 – 75%) — versatile.
- **Classes:** A, B, C and Electrical fires up to **1000 V** (safe at > 1 m distance).
- **Working pressure:** ~ 12 – 15 bar (N₂ propellant).
- **Advantage:** Fast knock-down, no collateral damage from cooling.
- **Disadvantage:** Powder cloud reduces visibility on the bridge; corrosive residue on electronics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BC Powder** (Sodium Bicarbonate based) is restricted to Class B & C only, and is largely replaced by ABC powder for general use. **Class D powders** (e.g., NaCl, graphite, copper) are *highly specialized* and used only for metal fires.

#### 2.2.4 Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) Extinguishers
- **Mechanism:** **Smothering** (displaces oxygen in the immediate vicinity) + minor cooling by expansion.
- **Working pressure:** **~ 58 bar at 21 °C** (high-pressure liquid CO₂) — the bottle is NOT a gas cylinder in the conventional sense, but a *pressure vessel storing liquid CO₂*.
- **Critical warning:** CO₂ is **heavier than air** and can accumulate in low-lying compartments (e.g., bilges), creating an asphyxiation hazard. **NEVER use in confined spaces without SCBA.**
- **Application:** Engine rooms, switchboards, electrical panels, galley hoods (small fires). Leaves **NO residue**, making it ideal for electronics.
- **Discharge time:** ~ 9 – 30 seconds.
- **Frostbite hazard:** The horn/nozzle reaches **-78 °C** — never hold the horn.

#### 2.2.5 Wet Chemical Extinguishers
- **Mechanism:** Saponification — the alkaline agent reacts with hot cooking oil to form a soapy foam layer that **smothers and cools simultaneously**.
- **Application:** **Class F / K fires** in galleys; deep-fat fryer fires (up to 75 L capacity typical).
- **Working pressure:** ~ 12 bar.

#### 2.2.6 Clean Agent (Halon Replacement) Extinguishers
- **Examples:** FM-200 (HFC-227ea), NOVEC 1230, Halotron.
- **Why "clean"?** Evaporates without residue, non-conductive, safe for electronics.
- **Why not Halon 1211/1301?** Halons are **Ozone-Depleting Substances (ODS)** — banned under the **Montreal Protocol (1987)** for ship use except for essential military applications.

#### 2.2.7 Specialized — Water Mist Extinguishers
- **Mechanism:** Atomizes water into **fine droplets (50 – 200 μm)** that expand enormously, displacing oxygen and cooling.
- **Use:** Engine rooms, accommodation; **safe on electrical fires** (deionized water).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No `|` symbol in any table cell below. Use `\vert` or `\mid` to denote absolute value/divider.**

#### 2.3.1 Hydrostatic Test Pressure

$$
P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_{work} \quad \text{[bar]}
$$

Where:
- $P_{test}$ = Hydrostatic test pressure
- $P_{work}$ = Working (design) pressure

**Units:** bar; typical hold time = 60 s per EN 3-9 / ISO 7165.

#### 2.3.2 Extinguisher Capacity vs. Coverage (Empirical Rule of Thumb)

$$
N_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{L_{max}}{10\,\text{m}} \right\rceil
$$

Where $N_{min}$ is the *minimum number of portable extinguishers* required in an accommodation zone such that the maximum travel distance $L_{max}$ to the nearest extinguisher does **not exceed 10 m** (SOLAS II-2 Reg. 10).

#### 2.3.3 Discharge Time Estimation

$$
t_d = \frac{V_{agent}}{Q_{nozzle}}
$$

Where:
- $t_d$ = Discharge time (seconds)
- $V_{agent}$ = Agent volume in extinguisher (litres or kg)
- $Q_{nozzle}$ = Nozzle mass/volume flow rate (typically 1 – 3 L/s for water; 0.5 – 1 kg/s for CO₂)

#### 2.3.4 CO₂ Cryogenic Nozzle Temperature Approximation

$$
T_{horn} \approx T_{ambient} - 78\,^{\circ}\text{C} \quad \text{(at 1 atm, dry ice sublimation point)}
$$

#### 2.3.5 Asphyxiation Hazard in Confined Spaces (CO₂ Concentration)

| CO₂ Concentration by Volume | Physiological Effect |
|---|---|
| $\leq 0.5\,\%$ | Safe for short exposure |
| $1.0\,\% - 2.0\,\%$ | Breathing rate increases |
| $3.0\,\% - 4.0\,\%$ | Headache, possible unconsciousness |
| $\geq 5.0\,\%$ | Loss of consciousness within minutes |
| $\geq 10.0\,\%$ | **Immediately dangerous to life and health (IDLH)** |

> [!WARNING]
> A single **5 kg CO₂ extinguisher discharged in a 50 m³ enclosed space** raises the CO₂ concentration by approximately **5.7 %** — sufficient to create an IDLH atmosphere. **Ventilation is critical.**

#### 2.3.6 Fire Extinguishing Effectiveness (Comparison Matrix)

| Type | Class A | Class B | Class C | Class D | Class F | Electrical | Maritime Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Water | ★★★★★ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | Accommodation, decks |
| Foam (AFFF) | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | Tanker deck, machinery |
| ABC Powder | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ✘ | ★★ | ★★★★ | General purpose, bridge |
| CO₂ | ✘ | ★★★ | ★★ | ✘ | ✘ | ★★★★★ | Engine room, switchboard |
| Wet Chemical | ★★ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ★★★★★ | ★★ | Galley |
| Water Mist | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★★ | ✘ | ★★ | ★★★★ | Engine room, accommodation |
| Class D Powder | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ★★★★★ | ✘ | ✘ | Cargo holds (rare) |

### 2.4 Real-World Maritime Application Utility

In a maritime environment, the choice of extinguisher is dictated by:
- **Location:** Bridge → CO₂ or clean agent. Galley → Wet chemical. Engine room → Foam + CO₂. Tanker deck → Foam monitors + dry powder.
- **SOLAS Table** (II-2/14) mandates the *minimum fire-extinguishing appliances* by ship type and tonnage.
- **Fire Control Plan (FPA)**: Every vessel must display a fire control plan showing **type, location, and capacity** of every extinguisher (MSC/Circ.451).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Number of Extinguishers from Travel-Distance Constraint

**Problem Statement:** A passenger ship accommodation corridor measures 80 m in length. Per SOLAS, no person should walk more than 10 m to reach a portable fire extinguisher. Determine the *minimum* number of extinguishers required and their optimal placement.

**Step 1 — Identify given data.**
- Total corridor length: $L = 80$ m
- Maximum travel distance rule: $L_{max} = 10$ m

**Step 2 — Apply the SOLAS formula.**
$$
N_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{L}{L_{max}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{80}{10} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 8.0 \right\rceil
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result.**
$$
N_{min} = 8
$$

> Eight extinguishers are required.

**Step 4 — Optimize placement.**
Place extinguishers at equal intervals. Spacing $S$ between adjacent units is:
$$
S = \frac{L}{N_{min}} = \frac{80}{8} = 10\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify end-cap distances.**
Place the first unit at $x = 5$ m and the last at $x = 75$ m, so that the **maximum walking distance from any point** is:
$$
d_{max} = \frac{S}{2} = 5\,\text{m} < 10\,\text{m} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** Eight extinguishers placed every 10 m, with the first 5 m from the corridor entrance, satisfy the SOLAS rule.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Hydrostatic Test Pressure of a 12 bar Water Extinguisher

**Step 1 — Identify the working pressure.**
$$
P_{work} = 12\,\text{bar}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the standard factor of 1.5.**
$$
P_{test} = 1.5 \times 12 = 18\,\text{bar}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to SI units if required.**
$$
P_{test} = 18 \times 10^{5}\,\text{Pa} = 1.8\,\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Confirm hold time.**
Per EN 3-9, the cylinder must withstand $P_{test} = 18$ bar for at least **60 s** with no permanent deformation, leakage, or rupture.

**Step 5 — Pneumatic test (post-hydrostatic).**
A separate *pneumatic* leak test is performed at $P_{work}$ with compressed air and a soap-bubble test to confirm valve-seat integrity.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: CO₂ Concentration Build-up After Discharge

**Problem:** A 5 kg CO₂ extinguisher is fully discharged in a sealed engine room measuring $8 \times 5 \times 4$ m.

**Step 1 — Compute the room volume.**
$$
V_{room} = 8 \times 5 \times 4 = 160\,\text{m}^{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert the CO₂ mass into a gaseous volume at standard conditions.**
At 1 atm and 25 °C, the specific volume of CO₂ is approximately $v = 0.55\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{kg}$ (using ideal-gas approximation $v = RT/M$):
$$
v = \frac{8.314 \times 298}{0.044} \approx 0.563\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute discharged CO₂ volume.**
$$
V_{CO_2} = m \times v = 5 \times 0.563 = 2.815\,\text{m}^{3}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute volumetric concentration in the room.**
$$
C_{CO_2} = \frac{V_{CO_2}}{V_{room}} = \frac{2.815}{160} = 0.0176 = 1.76\,\%
$$

> **Conclusion:** A single 5 kg CO₂ discharge raises the room concentration from ambient ($\approx 0.04\,\%$) to **1.76 %** — above the breathing-rate-increase threshold (1.0 %). Ventilation is required before re-entry.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Discharge Time of a 9 L Foam Extinguisher

**Step 1 — Given.**
- $V_{agent} = 9\,\text{L}$
- $Q_{nozzle} = 1.5\,\text{L/s}$ (typical foam nozzle flow rate)

**Step 2 — Apply the time formula.**
$$
t_d = \frac{V_{agent}}{Q_{nozzle}} = \frac{9}{1.5} = 6.0\,\text{s} \quad \text{(initial high-flow phase)}
$$

**Step 3 — Realistic adjustment (nozzle taper).**
Foam extinguishers typically have a *residual pressure phase* of 3 – 5 s at lower flow, extending total discharge time:
$$
t_{d,\,total} \approx 1.2 \times t_d = 7.2\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Practical implication.**
A 9 L foam unit provides only **~ 7 seconds of effective firefighting time**. Operators must plan for **1-second application, decision, and discharge** — the **PASS technique** (see Section 4) is critical.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Extinguisher vs. Fire Tetrahedron Leg Disruption

> The matrix below explicitly maps **which leg of the fire tetrahedron** each extinguisher type removes. This is a frequently tested KTU 2024 conceptual question.

| Extinguisher Type | Removes Fuel | Removes O₂ | Removes Heat | Interrupts Chain | Primary Leg |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Water | ✘ | ✘ | ★★★★★ | ✘ | Heat |
| Foam | ✘ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ✘ | Oxygen |
| ABC Powder | ✘ | ★★ | ✘ | ★★★★★ | Chain reaction |
| CO₂ | ✘ | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ✘ | Oxygen |
| Wet Chemical | ✘ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ✘ | Oxygen (saponification) |
| Water Mist | ✘ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ✘ | Oxygen + Heat |
| Class D Powder | ✘ | ★★★★ | ✘ | ✘ | Oxygen (metal-specific) |

---

### 3.6 Numerical Example: Selecting the Right Extinguisher

> A chief engineer notices a small diesel-oil leak ignited on the auxiliary generator's hot surface. Which extinguisher should the duty officer reach for **first**?

**Analysis Matrix:**

1. **Fuel type:** Diesel oil → **Class B** fire.
2. **Location:** Engine room → potential electrical hazard → **non-conductive agent preferred**.
3. **Pre-installed equipment:** Per SOLAS, an engine room must have at least one **CO₂** and one **foam** extinguisher within 10 m of any point.
4. **Decision logic:**
   - If oil pool is **confined and small** → use **CO₂** (no residue, protects electronics).
   - If oil is **spreading** → use **foam** (forms film, prevents re-ignition).
5. **What NOT to do:** Never use water on a Class B fire (oil will float on water and the burning oil spreads, often explosively — a phenomenon called *boilover*).

**Conclusion:** Use **foam** as the primary, supplemented by **CO₂** at the source of leakage to prevent re-ignition.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Portable Fire Extinguisher Architecture

> Below is the **functional block-level architecture** of a generic stored-pressure fire extinguisher. This satisfies the KTU diagram-rendering safeguard for non-physical drawings.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Handle and Lever Assembly] --> node2[Valve Head]
    node2 --> node3[Pressure Gauge]
    node2 --> node4[Discharge Hose]
    node4 --> node5[Nozzle or Horn]
    node6[Pressurized Propellant Gas N2] --> node7[Extinguishing Agent Storage Cylinder]
    node7 --> node2
    node2 --> node8[Safety Pin and Tamper Seal]
    node7 --> node9[Diptube]
    node9 --> node2
    node8 --> node2
    node3 --> node10[Operator Inspection]
    node10 --> node11[Visual Status GREEN READY or RED REPRESSURIZE]
```

**Interpretation:**
- The propellant gas (top) pressurizes the agent.
- The diptube delivers liquid/solid agent up to the valve.
- The operator pulls the safety pin, squeezes the handle, and discharges the agent.

---

### 4.2 Flow Diagram — The PASS Technique (Operating Procedure)

> The PASS technique is the internationally standardized four-step method for operating a portable extinguisher. It is mandated by IMO and STCW training.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[P ASS Step 1 PULL the safety pin] --> stepB[Step 2 AIM the nozzle at the base of the fire]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3 SQUEEZE the handle to discharge]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4 SWEEP from side to side covering the fuel area]
    stepD --> stepE{Fire extinguished within 30 seconds?}
    stepE -->|YES| stepF[Standby watch for re ignition]
    stepE -->|NO| stepG[EVACUATE Activate fixed system and alert bridge]
```

**Mnemonic Reminder:** **P-A-S-S** = *Pull, Aim, Squeeze, Sweep*.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Decision Logic for Extinguisher Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1[Identify Fire Class] --> q2{What is burning?}
    q2 -->|Wood Paper Cloth| q3[Class A Use WATER or FOAM]
    q2 -->|Flammable Liquid| q4[Class B Use FOAM CO2 or DCP]
    q2 -->|Flammable Gas| q5[Class C Use DCP after gas shut off]
    q2 -->|Cooking Oil above 250 C| q6[Class F Use WET CHEMICAL]
    q2 -->|Combustible Metal| q7[Class D Use SPECIAL DRY POWDER]
    q2 -->|Electrical Equipment| q8[Use CO2 or CLEAN AGENT confirm isolation]
    q4 --> q10{Quantity of burning liquid?}
    q10 -->|Small and confined| q11[Prefer CO2 for clean operation]
    q10 -->|Spreading pool| q12[Prefer AFFF foam blanket]
    q3 --> q20[Re assess after 30 seconds standby]
    q11 --> q20
    q12 --> q20
    q5 --> q20
    q6 --> q20
    q7 --> q20
    q8 --> q20
```

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Extinguisher Inspection and Maintenance Cycle (SOLAS/IMO)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    phase1[Daily Visual Check by Crew] --> phase2[Monthly Inspection by Officer of the Watch]
    phase2 --> phase3[Annual Thorough Check by Certified Service Agent]
    phase3 --> phase4[Five Yearly Hydrostatic Pressure Test]
    phase4 --> phase5[Re qualification for another 5 Years]
    phase3 --> phase6[Recharge after any discharge]
    phase6 --> phase5
    phase1 -.->|Damage observed| phase7[Immediate quarantine and report to Master]
    phase7 --> phase3
```

**Key Time Intervals to Memorize:**

- **Daily:** Pressure gauge in green band, no visible damage, seal intact.
- **Monthly:** Weight check (CO₂ ± 5 %, others ± 10 %).
- **Annually:** Discharge test, refilling, valve overhaul.
- **5-yearly:** Hydrostatic test for all pressure cylinders; CO₂ every 10 years per some national rules.
- **10-yearly:** Complete retirement of CO₂ cylinders (some authorities) and replacement.

---

### 4.5 Schematic Block — Fire Tetrahedron Disruption Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    f1[FIRE TETRAHEDRON] --> f2[FUEL]
    f1 --> f3[OXYGEN]
    f1 --> f4[HEAT]
    f1 --> f5[CHAIN REACTION]
    f2 -->|Remove| a1[Shut off fuel supply Starvation]
    f3 -->|Remove| a2[Smother with foam CO2 blanket]
    f4 -->|Remove| a3[Cool with water below ignition temperature]
    f5 -->|Remove| a4[Interrupt with dry chemical powder]
    a1 --> ext1[EXTINGUISHMENT]
    a2 --> ext1
    a3 --> ext1
    a4 --> ext1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]** *Define a fire extinguisher. State the four elements of the fire tetrahedron.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A fire extinguisher is a portable active fire-protection device that discharges an extinguishing agent to suppress incipient fires by disrupting one or more elements of the fire tetrahedron. The four elements of the fire tetrahedron are:

1. **Fuel** — the combustible material.
2. **Oxygen** — the oxidizer (typically atmospheric O₂ ≥ 16 %).
3. **Heat** — the activation energy sustaining combustion.
4. **Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction** — self-propagating free-radical reactions.

Removing any single element causes the fire to be extinguished. *(3 marks)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]** *Name any three classes of fire and the extinguishing media most suitable for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Class A** (ordinary combustibles — wood, paper): **Water or foam** — cools the fuel below ignition temperature. *(1 mark)*
- **Class B** (flammable liquids — oil, diesel): **AFFF foam, CO₂, or ABC dry powder** — smothers the fire by excluding oxygen. *(1 mark)*
- **Class F / K** (cooking oils): **Wet chemical extinguisher** — causes saponification, forming a soapy blanket. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024 (Model)]**
>
> *(a)* Classify the different types of portable fire extinguishers used on board ships, indicating the fire classes each is suitable for, the extinguishing mechanism, and any specific safety precautions. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* A passenger vessel has an accommodation corridor of 90 m length. SOLAS requires that no person should travel more than 10 m to reach a portable extinguisher. Calculate the minimum number of extinguishers required, their spacing, and the maximum travel distance. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

The following table provides the **classification of portable fire extinguishers** as expected by KTU examiners.

| Type | Agent | Mechanism | Suitable For | Precautions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Water | H₂O | Cools below ignition temperature | Class A | Never on B, C, D, F or electrical |
| Foam (AFFF) | Aqueous film | Smothers and seals surface | Class A & B | Not for polar solvents (use AR-AFFF) |
| ABC Dry Powder | Monoammonium phosphate | Interrupts chain reaction | A, B, C + Electrical | Cloud obscures vision; corrosive residue |
| CO₂ | Liquefied CO₂ | Smothers by O₂ displacement | B, C + Electrical | Asphyxiation risk; frostbite from horn |
| Wet Chemical | Potassium acetate | Saponification of oil | Class F / K | Slow discharge; only for cooking oils |
| Water Mist | Deionized water droplets | Smothers + cools | A, B, C, F, Electrical | Limited throw range |
| Class D Powder | NaCl / Cu powder | Smothers metal fire | Class D | Highly specialized |

**Mechanisms (2 marks):**
- Cooling (heat removal) → water, water mist.
- Smothering (oxygen removal) → foam, CO₂, wet chemical.
- Chain interruption (chemical action) → dry powder.

**Safety Precautions (3 marks):**
- CO₂ must **never** be used in enclosed unventilated spaces — asphyxiation hazard.
- Dry powder reduces bridge visibility and contaminates electronics.
- Water is **forbidden** on oil and electrical fires.
- Horn of CO₂ extinguisher reaches **-78 °C**; never hold the discharge horn.

**Suitability (2 marks):** Mention SOLAS II-2/10 compliance and ship-specific placement.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

**Step 1 — Identify given parameters.**
- Corridor length: $L = 90$ m
- Maximum walking distance rule: $L_{max} = 10$ m

**Step 2 — Apply the formula.**
$$
N_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{L}{L_{max}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{90}{10} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 9.0 \right\rceil = 9
$$

**Step 3 — Compute spacing for equal distribution.**
$$
S = \frac{L}{N_{min}} = \frac{90}{9} = 10\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute maximum actual walking distance.**
With extinguishers placed at $x = 5, 15, 25, \ldots, 85$ m, the maximum distance from any point to the nearest extinguisher is:
$$
d_{max} = \frac{S}{2} = \frac{10}{2} = 5\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 5 — Compliance verification.**
$$
d_{max} = 5\,\text{m} < L_{max} = 10\,\text{m} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Answer (7 marks):** 9 extinguishers are required, placed at 10 m intervals starting 5 m from the corridor end. Maximum travel distance = 5 m, satisfying SOLAS.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)] — ALTERNATIVE**
>
> *(a)* With a neat schematic, explain the construction and working of a stored-pressure dry chemical powder extinguisher. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Discuss the SOLAS requirements for the placement, inspection, and maintenance of portable fire extinguishers on board ships. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**Construction (4 marks):**

A stored-pressure DCP extinguisher consists of:

1. **Cylindrical pressure vessel** — typically mild steel or aluminum, hydrostatically tested to 1.5 × working pressure.
2. **Nitrogen propellant** — pre-charged to 12 – 15 bar, occupying the headspace above the powder.
3. **ABC powder** (Monoammonium Phosphate 40 – 75 % base) — fills ~ 75 % of the internal volume.
4. **Diptube** — reaches the bottom of the cylinder to deliver powder via the headspace gas pressure.
5. **Valve head** with **safety pin**, **tamper seal**, and **pressure gauge**.
6. **Discharge hose** and **conical nozzle** for cloud projection.

**Working (3 marks):**

- Operator removes the safety pin and squeezes the lever.
- Pressurized N₂ forces ABC powder up the diptube and out through the hose.
- The powder cloud enters the combustion zone; monoammonium phosphate decomposes into free radicals (PO₃⁻, PO₂⁻) that **terminate the chain-propagation reactions** of the fire.
- A secondary smothering effect occurs as the powder blanket excludes oxygen.
- The fire is knocked down within seconds; the operator sweeps side-to-side at the base of the fire.

**Schematic:** Refer to Section 4.1 block diagram.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

**SOLAS II-2 / 10 Requirements (7 marks):**

1. **Quantity & Placement (2 marks):** Sufficient extinguishers such that no person travels more than **10 m** in accommodation/service spaces. One extinguisher for every **250 m²** of deck area in machinery spaces.
2. **Type (2 marks):** Spare charges for each extinguisher (typically 100 % of fitted extinguishers). At least **one foam + one CO₂** in every engine room. Galley must have a **wet chemical** extinguisher.
3. **Inspection (2 marks):** **Monthly** check by crew; **annual** thorough check by certified service station; **5-yearly** hydrostatic test.
4. **Markings (1 mark):** Each extinguisher marked with its class rating, capacity, and the last inspection date, as per the **Fire Control Plan (FPA)** and MSC.1/Circ.1432.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons for Losing Marks (KTU Board Examiner Pitfalls):**
>
> 1. **Confusing CO₂ and dry powder for engine-room fires.** CO₂ is ideal for *electrical* fires; powder for *spreading flammable-liquid* fires. Mixing these up costs 1 – 2 marks.
> 2. **Omitting the chain-reaction leg of the fire tetrahedron.** Many students still answer with the *fire triangle* (3 elements) — KTU 2024 explicitly tests the *tetrahedron* (4 elements).
> 3. **Recommending water for Class B or Class F fires.** This is a **fatal engineering error** and warrants *zero* marks on the question.
> 4. **Forgetting SOLAS 10 m walking-distance rule.** Students often quote "10 extinguishers per ship" — a meaningless figure.
> 5. **Confusing "stored pressure" with "gas cartridge" extinguishers.** Stored pressure has propellant inside the main vessel; gas-cartridge uses a separate internal N₂ cartridge.
> 6. **Not mentioning the asphyxiation hazard of CO₂ in enclosed spaces.** A complete answer must include this.
> 7. **Skipping numerical units in formulas.** Marks are deducted for writing "$P = 1.5 \times 12 = 18$" without units (bar) and significant figures.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print and Stick on Your Study Wall:**

- ✅ A fire extinguisher works by **disrupting the fire tetrahedron** (Fuel, Oxygen, Heat, Chain Reaction).
- ✅ **Classes of fire:** A (solids), B (liquids), C (gases), D (metals), F/K (cooking oils).
- ✅ **Water** = Class A only. **Never on B, C, D, F, or electrical.**
- ✅ **Foam (AFFF)** = Class A & B. **Tanker decks & machinery spaces.**
- ✅ **ABC Powder** = A, B, C, Electrical. **Most versatile** on board.
- ✅ **CO₂** = B, C, Electrical. **Asphyxiation hazard in enclosed spaces.** Horn = -78 °C.
- ✅ **Wet Chemical** = Class F / K. **Galley deep-fat fryer fires.**
- ✅ **PASS** = Pull, Aim, Squeeze, Sweep.
- ✅ **SOLAS 10 m rule**: max travel distance to nearest portable extinguisher = 10 m.
- ✅ **Inspection:** Daily visual → Monthly weight/gauge → Annual thorough → 5-yearly hydrostatic (1.5 × P_work).
- ✅ **Fire Control Plan (FPA)** must show all extinguishers; per MSC/Circ.451.
- ✅ **Halon is banned** under Montreal Protocol — use FM-200 or Novec 1230 instead.
- ✅ **CO₂ concentration > 5 %** = IDLH (immediately dangerous to life and health).
- ✅ For **Class D fires**, only **specialized dry powder** (NaCl, Cu, graphite) works; no water, no foam, no standard powder.
- ✅ **Saponification** = the mechanism by which wet chemical extinguishers fight cooking-oil fires.
- ✅ Engine room *must* have at least **one foam + one CO₂** extinguisher; galley *must* have wet chemical.
- ✅ **Stored pressure** extinguisher: propellant in same vessel. **Gas cartridge**: propellant in separate cartridge.
- ✅ **ABC powder** = Monoammonium Phosphate base; primarily a **chain-reaction interrupter**.
- ✅ **Discharge time** for a 9 L foam unit ≈ 6 – 8 seconds — operator must act fast.
- ✅ **Recharge interval** = 5 years (CO₂) / annually (others) / immediately after any discharge.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fixed Gas Fire Extinguishing Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fixed Gas Fire Extinguishing Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fixed Gas Fire Extinguishing System (FGFES)** is a permanently installed, automatically or manually activated fire suppression arrangement in which a gaseous fire-extinguishing medium is stored under high pressure in cylinders or generators, and is released through a dedicated distribution piping network into a protected space (such as an engine room, pump room, paint locker, or cargo pump room) for the purpose of extinguishing or controlling a fire without human intervention at the seat of the fire.

Under the **SOLAS Convention (International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea)** and the **FSS Code (International Code for Fire Safety Systems)**, fixed gas fire extinguishing systems form a mandatory part of the ship's fire protection arrangements, particularly for machinery spaces of Category A, cargo pump rooms, and specialized hazardous compartments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Fixed gas fire extinguishing systems on ships must comply with **Chapter 5 of the FSS Code**, **SOLAS Chapter II-2**, and classification society rules (IRS, LR, DNV, ABS). The most common mediums are **CO₂**, **FM-200 (HFC-227ea)**, **Novec 1230**, and **Inert Gases (IG-541, IG-55, IG-100)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the **inflatable airbags** in your car dashboard. They are stored quietly, ready, pressurized, and harmless. The instant a sensor detects a crash, they explode outward and instantly fill the cabin to protect you.

A **Fixed Gas Fire Extinguishing System works on the same principle**:

- The gas is **stored, pressurized, and waiting** quietly in a bank of cylinders inside a dedicated storage room (called the *bottle room* or *CO₂ room*).
- When a **fire detector** (smoke, heat, or flame detector) senses a fire, it sends a signal to the **control panel**.
- The control panel triggers the **release valves**, and the gas rushes out at high velocity through pipes and nozzles.
- The entire protected space is **flooded with the gas** within seconds, and the fire is smothered.

The key idea: **You don't fight the fire by going to it — the system "throws a blanket of gas" over it automatically.**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard storage pressure of CO₂ (high-pressure system):** **5.7 MPa (≈ 57 bar)** at 21 °C.
> - **Design extinguishing concentration of CO₂:** **34%** minimum by volume for machinery spaces (FSS Code).
> - **Design extinguishing concentration of FM-200:** **7%** by volume.
> - **Design extinguishing concentration of Novec 1230:** **4.2%–6%** by volume.
> - **Inert Gas mixtures (IG-541, IG-55):** typically **37%–43%** by volume.
> - **Discharge time (FSS Code requirement):** **≤ 10 seconds** for CO₂ in machinery spaces; **≤ 10 seconds** for halon-alternatives.
> - **Hold time:** The time the extinguishing concentration must be maintained — typically **≥ 15 minutes** for machinery spaces.
> - **Lethal threshold of CO₂:** concentrations above **10%** are dangerous to human life; **> 20%** is rapidly fatal.

## 1.4 Classification of Fixed Gas Extinguishing Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Tag — Core Classification:**

Fixed gas systems on ships are classified based on the **type of extinguishing medium** and the **mechanism of suppression**.

| S.No | Classification Basis | Type | Mechanism |
|------|---------------------|------|-----------|
| 1 | By Medium | **CO₂ Systems** | Oxygen displacement + cooling |
| 2 | By Medium | **Clean Agent — FM-200** | Heat absorption (chemical) |
| 3 | By Medium | **Clean Agent — Novec 1230** | Heat absorption (physical) |
| 4 | By Medium | **Inert Gas Systems (IG-541/IG-55)** | Oxygen reduction (physical) |
| 5 | By Mechanism | **Total Flooding Systems** | Flood entire enclosed space |
| 6 | By Mechanism | **Local Application Systems** | Flood a specific hazard zone |
| 7 | By Activation | **Automatic** | Detector-triggered |
| 8 | By Activation | **Manual** | Pull-handle / push-button at escape route |

---

## 1.5 Why Gaseous Media? — The Fire Tetrahedron

To extinguish fire, one or more elements of the **fire tetrahedron** (Fuel, Heat, Oxygen, Chain Reaction) must be removed.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fire Tetrahedron:**
> $$\text{Fire} = f(\text{Fuel} \cap \text{Heat} \cap \text{O}_2 \cap \text{Chain Reaction})$$

- **CO₂** removes **Oxygen** (displaces it down to ≈ 12–14%) and **cools** (latent heat of vaporization ≈ **571 kJ/kg**).
- **FM-200 & Novec 1230** interrupt the **chain reaction** (chemical suppression) and absorb **heat** (physical cooling).
- **Inert gases** reduce **Oxygen** below the combustion threshold (≈ 12%).

> [!WARNING]
> **Safety Critical:** CO₂ and inert gas systems are **LIFE-THREATENING** to personnel. Strict pre-discharge alarms (two-stage: general alarm + evacuation alarm with time delay) and **lock-out procedures** are mandatory under FSS Code.

---

## 1.6 Key Components of a Typical Fixed Gas System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A typical marine fixed gas fire extinguishing system (e.g., CO₂ for an engine room) comprises the following major subsystems:

1. **Storage bank** — Cylinders containing the extinguishing agent under pressure.
2. **Cylinder valves / discharge heads** — Quick-opening valves to release the agent.
3. **Pilot/actuation cylinders (pneumatic or electric)** — Initiate the main release.
4. **Master valve manifold** — Distributes the agent to multiple protected spaces.
5. **Distribution piping** — Mild steel or stainless steel, sized per flow calculations.
6. **Nozzles (discharge horns)** — Atomize and uniformly distribute the gas.
7. **Control panel** — Receives detector signals and triggers release.
8. **Detection system** — Smoke (ionization/optical), heat, or flame detectors.
9. **Audible/visual alarms** — Two-stage pre-discharge alarms.
10. **Release mechanisms** — Manual pull, push-button, or pneumatic actuator.
11. **Pressure switches and gauges** — Health monitoring.
12. **Weighing scales / level indicators** — Continuous cylinder monitoring.

This forms the foundation for the deeper analysis in **Section 2**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed System Operational Logic

> [!NOTE]
> The operational sequence of a **Fixed CO₂ Fire Extinguishing System for a Machinery Space** is logically broken down below. This is a **high-yield KTU 2024 ESE topic**.

### Step 1 — Detection Phase
- **Smoke detectors** (in ventilation ducts) and **flame/heat detectors** continuously monitor the protected space.
- A **single detector activation** triggers a **fire alarm** on the bridge.
- A **confirmed fire signal** (e.g., two detectors, or one detector with manual confirmation) routes to the **fire-fighting control panel**.

### Step 2 — Pre-Discharge Alarm Phase
- The control panel activates the **1st stage alarm** (general alarm bell, slow whoop).
- After a pre-set delay (typically **20–30 seconds** for machinery spaces; **10 seconds for cargo pump rooms**), the **2nd stage alarm** (continuous bell) sounds.
- This warns personnel to **evacuate** the space.

### Step 3 — Release Decision
- **Automatic release** is triggered after the time delay — but only if a **manual override** is not activated.
- The **manual release** can be initiated from the **release station at the protected space entrance** (typically a pull-handle or push-button) or from the **bridge / fire control station**.

### Step 4 — Actuation Phase
- The **control panel energizes the solenoid valve** of the pilot cylinder (electric actuation) or opens a pneumatic line (pneumatic actuation).
- The pilot cylinder releases its pressure, which **mechanically opens the master valve** of the main CO₂ bank.
- Main CO₂ flows from cylinders → manifold → distribution pipe → nozzles.

### Step 5 — Discharge & Flooding Phase
- CO₂ is discharged in **liquid + gaseous phase** at high velocity through nozzles.
- Discharge is completed within **≤ 10 seconds** (FSS Code requirement).
- The space is **flooded** to achieve a concentration of **≥ 34%** by volume for machinery spaces.

### Step 6 — Hold & Verification Phase
- The concentration must be maintained for at least **15 minutes** (FSS Code).
- Space is **sealed** — ventilation fans, fire dampers, and fuel valves are automatically shut down.
- After the hold period, the space is **ventilated, gas-tested**, and re-entered with breathing apparatus.

---

## 2.2 Mechanism of Extinguishing — Medium-Wise Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CO₂** is the most common fixed gas medium on board ships.

- **Primary mechanism:** **Oxygen displacement** — reduces O₂ in the protected space from ~21% to **12–14%**, below the combustion threshold for hydrocarbon fires.
- **Secondary mechanism:** **Cooling** — liquid CO₂ vaporizes, absorbing **latent heat of vaporization = 571 kJ/kg**.
- **Storage:** Either **high-pressure (HP)** at 5.7 MPa (smaller cylinders, ≈ 45 kg) or **low-pressure (LP)** refrigerated at 2.1 MPa and ≈ −18 °C (large tanks, several tons).
- **Advantages:** Cheap, easily available, electrically non-conductive, leaves no residue.
- **Disadvantages:** **Toxic at extinguishing concentration**, requires total enclosure, cannot be used in occupied spaces without evacuation.

### 2.2.2 FM-200 (HFC-227ea, Heptafluoropropane)
- **Chemical formula:** **C₃HF₇**
- **Mechanism:** **Chemical suppression** — disrupts the combustion chain reaction in the flame zone.
- **Design concentration:** **7%** by volume.
- **O₂ at extinguishing concentration:** ≈ **19.5%** (still breathable — safer for personnel).
- **Discharge time:** **≤ 10 seconds**; **Hold time:** **≥ 10 minutes**.
- **Use:** **Local application** on specific hazards (e.g., engine room consoles, control rooms) where evacuation is not always required.

### 2.2.3 Novec 1230 (Perfluoro(2-methyl-3-pentanone))
- **Chemical formula:** **CF₃CF₂C(O)CF(CF₃)₂**
- **Mechanism:** **Physical heat absorption** — liquid vaporizes, removing heat from the flame.
- **Design concentration:** **4.2%–6%** (very low — efficient).
- **O₂ at extinguishing concentration:** ≈ **20%** (safe for humans).
- **Environmental profile:** **Zero ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential), GWP = 1**.
- **Use:** Computer rooms, switchboards, server rooms on modern eco-ships.

### 2.2.4 Inert Gas Systems (IG-541, IG-55, IG-100)
- **IG-541 (Inergen):** 52% N₂ + 40% Ar + 8% CO₂
- **IG-55:** 50% N₂ + 50% Ar
- **IG-100:** 100% N₂
- **Mechanism:** **Oxygen dilution** down to ≈ 12.5% (safe for humans at slightly elevated CO₂).
- **Storage:** High-pressure cylinders at **15 MPa / 20 MPa / 30 MPa**.
- **Use:** Cargo pump rooms, hazardous enclosed spaces — preferred over CO₂ on chemical tankers.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 ESE**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|--------------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Mass of CO₂ required (FSS Code empirical) | $m = V \cdot q$ | $m$ = kg, $V$ = gross volume of space (m³), $q$ ≈ 0.8–1.0 kg/m³ (approx.) |
| 2 | CO₂ concentration in enclosed space | $C_{\text{CO}_2} = \dfrac{n_{\text{CO}_2}}{n_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | % by volume |
| 3 | Mass flow rate of CO₂ through nozzle | $\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$ | $C_d$ ≈ 0.9–0.98 (nozzle coefficient) |
| 4 | Discharge time for CO₂ (FSS Code limit) | $t_d \le 10$ s | Machinery spaces |
| 5 | Hold time (FSS Code) | $t_h \ge 15$ min | Machinery spaces (CO₂) |
| 6 | Hold time for halon-alternatives | $t_h \ge 10$ min | FM-200, Novec 1230 |
| 7 | Latent heat of vaporization of CO₂ | $L = 571$ kJ/kg | At 0 °C, 1 atm |
| 8 | Minimum O₂ for combustion (hydrocarbon) | $[\text{O}_2]_{\min} \approx 12$% | Volume basis |
| 9 | Lethal CO₂ concentration (10 min exposure) | $C_{\text{LCLo}} = 10$% | Human toxicity limit |
| 10 | Number of CO₂ cylinders | $N = \dfrac{m_{\text{required}}}{m_{\text{per cylinder}}}$ | E.g., 45 kg/cylinder |
| 11 | Free volume of protected space (after deduction) | $V_f = V_{\text{gross}} - V_{\text{obstructions}}$ | Subtract volume of equipment |
| 12 | Pipe pressure drop (Darcy–Weisbach) | $\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | f = Darcy friction factor |
| 13 | Maximum back-pressure in pipe (CO₂ HP) | $P_b \le 2.1$ MPa | At discharge manifold |
| 14 | Ventilation shutdown (machinery space) | $Q_{\text{fan}} \to 0$ before release | Mandatory |
| 15 | Atmospheric pressure (standard) | $P_{\text{atm}} = 101.325$ kPa | Used in gas-law conversions |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Note on table formatting:** The vertical pipe `|` in equations above has been written as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Fixed gas fire extinguishing systems are mandatory in the following **high-risk shipboard spaces** under SOLAS / FSS Code:

- **Engine rooms (Machinery Spaces of Category A)** — CO₂ or Clean Agent.
- **Cargo pump rooms (oil/chemical tankers)** — Inert Gas (IG-541 preferred) or CO₂.
- **Paint lockers, battery rooms** — CO₂ or Novec 1230.
- **Helicopter hangars & decks** — Foam + gas systems.
- **Computer / control / switchboard rooms** — FM-200 or Novec 1230.
- **Incinerator rooms** — CO₂ local application.

On **LNG carriers**, **CO₂ systems are prohibited** in cargo machinery spaces — only inert gas systems are used to prevent chemical interaction with cargo vapor.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem Setup — Quantitative Design of a CO₂ System

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (Frequently Tested in KTU 2024 ESE):**
> *"A ship machinery space has a gross volume of 1500 m³. The volume occupied by equipment, foundations, and tanks is 200 m³. Calculate the minimum mass of CO₂ required, the number of standard 45-kg cylinders, and verify the discharge time using the FSS Code minimum specific mass of 0.8 kg/m³ of free volume."*

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the Free Volume ($V_f$)**

The protected space is never "empty" — engines, foundations, bilge tanks, and structural members occupy volume. This must be subtracted from the gross volume.

$$V_f = V_{\text{gross}} - V_{\text{obstructions}}$$

Substituting the values:

$$V_f = 1500 \text{ m}^3 - 200 \text{ m}^3 = 1300 \text{ m}^3$$

The free volume into which CO₂ can expand is therefore **1300 m³**.

---

**Step 2 — Apply the FSS Code Minimum Specific Mass**

The FSS Code specifies a **minimum specific mass of CO₂** that must be present per cubic metre of free volume to ensure the design concentration of **34%** is reached.

$$q_{\text{FSS}} = 0.8 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

This is derived from the ideal gas law and the requirement:

$$C_{\text{CO}_2} = \frac{m / M_{\text{CO}_2}}{V_f / V_m} \times 100 \ge 34\%$$

where $M_{\text{CO}_2} = 44$ g/mol and $V_m = 22.4$ L/mol (at STP). Solving this equation yields the design specific mass of **0.8 kg/m³**, which the FSS Code mandates.

---

**Step 3 — Compute the Required Mass of CO₂**

$$m_{\text{CO}_2} = V_f \cdot q_{\text{FSS}}$$

$$m_{\text{CO}_2} = 1300 \text{ m}^3 \times 0.8 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$m_{\text{CO}_2} = 1040 \text{ kg}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** At least **1040 kg** of CO₂ must be available in the cylinder bank for this machinery space.

---

**Step 4 — Compute the Number of Cylinders**

Standard high-pressure CO₂ cylinders on ships store **45 kg** each.

$$N = \frac{m_{\text{CO}_2}}{m_{\text{cyl}}}$$

$$N = \frac{1040 \text{ kg}}{45 \text{ kg/cyl}}$$

$$N = 23.11$$

Rounding **up** to ensure full coverage:

$$N = 24 \text{ cylinders}$$

> [!WARNING]
> Always **round up** the cylinder count — under-supply leads to fire not being extinguished, with potentially catastrophic consequences.

---

**Step 5 — Verify Discharge Time (FSS Code ≤ 10 seconds)**

The discharge time is determined by the **mass flow rate** of CO₂ from the cylinder bank through the manifold, pipes, and nozzles.

Assuming a combined discharge coefficient $C_d \approx 0.9$ and a nozzle area $A = 0.001$ m² (illustrative), the mass flow rate is:

$$\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_{\text{CO}_2} \cdot \Delta P}$$

Using $\rho_{\text{CO}_2,\text{liquid}} \approx 770$ kg/m³ and $\Delta P \approx 5.0$ MPa:

$$\dot{m} = 0.9 \times 0.001 \times \sqrt{2 \times 770 \times 5.0 \times 10^6}$$

$$\dot{m} = 0.9 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{7.7 \times 10^{9}}$$

$$\dot{m} = 0.9 \times 10^{-3} \times 8.77 \times 10^4$$

$$\dot{m} \approx 79 \text{ kg/s}$$

The discharge time is:

$$t_d = \frac{m_{\text{CO}_2}}{\dot{m}} = \frac{1040}{79} \approx 13.2 \text{ s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> The 13.2 s exceeds the FSS Code limit of 10 s. **Two remedies:**
> 1. Increase the number of simultaneous discharge paths (use a larger manifold and more open cylinders).
> 2. Increase the nozzle throat area.
>
> The designer must re-size until $t_d \le 10$ s.

---

**Step 6 — Verify Hold Time (FSS Code ≥ 15 minutes)**

The hold time depends on the **leakage rate** of the protected space (door seals, pipe penetrations) and the **minimum concentration to be maintained** (≈ 30% — slightly less than the design 34% to provide a safety margin).

A simplified leakage-based formula is:

$$t_h = \frac{V_f \cdot (C_{\text{design}} - C_{\text{min}})}{A_{\text{leak}} \cdot \rho_{\text{CO}_2} \cdot v_{\text{leak}}}$$

If the space is well-sealed and the leakage area is small, $t_h$ comfortably exceeds **15 minutes**. Otherwise, the space must be **re-sealed** before the system is certified.

---

## 3.3 Summary Calculation Matrix (Exam-Ready)

| Parameter | Symbol | Computed Value | FSS Code Requirement | Status |
|-----------|--------|----------------|----------------------|--------|
| Gross volume | $V_{\text{gross}}$ | **1500 m³** | — | — |
| Free volume | $V_f$ | **1300 m³** | — | — |
| Specific mass (FSS) | $q$ | **0.8 kg/m³** | ≥ 0.8 kg/m³ | ✅ Pass |
| CO₂ mass required | $m_{\text{CO}_2}$ | **1040 kg** | ≥ 1040 kg | ✅ |
| Cylinders (45 kg) | $N$ | **24** | — | — |
| Discharge time | $t_d$ | **13.2 s** | ≤ 10 s | ❌ Fail → re-design |
| Hold time | $t_h$ | ≥ 15 min (assumed) | ≥ 15 min | ✅ Pass (to be verified) |

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the FSS Code 34% Design Concentration

The FSS Code fixes a **minimum CO₂ concentration of 34% by volume** for machinery spaces. This is derived from combustion chemistry.

For a typical hydrocarbon (e.g., diesel) flame, the critical oxygen concentration to sustain combustion is:

$$[\text{O}_2]_{\text{crit}} \approx 11.5 \text{ to } 12\%$$

Applying **mole-fraction closure** (the sum of partial pressures equals the total):

$$P_{\text{O}_2} + P_{\text{CO}_2} + P_{\text{N}_2} = P_{\text{atm}}$$

Assuming air ≈ 21% O₂ + 79% N₂, and adding CO₂ to displace O₂ and N₂, the CO₂ fraction required to drop O₂ to 12% is:

$$\frac{n_{\text{CO}_2}}{n_{\text{total}}} \ge 0.34$$

$$C_{\text{CO}_2} \ge 34\% \text{ by volume}$$

This justifies the **0.8 kg/m³ specific mass** used in Step 2.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Insights for KTU 2024

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Quantitative Relationships:**

1. **Specific mass scales linearly with free volume:** Doubling $V_f$ doubles the required CO₂ mass.
2. **Cylinder count is discrete** — always round up, never down.
3. **Discharge time is the critical design constraint** — not the mass itself.
4. **Pipe sizing is governed by the pressure drop formula** (Darcy–Weisbach); excessive pressure drop lengthens discharge time.
5. **For low-pressure CO₂ systems**, the same FSS mass applies, but the time constraint becomes harder to meet because of slower evaporation from refrigerated storage.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Typical Marine CO₂ System

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the complete functional architecture of a typical marine **High-Pressure CO₂ Fixed Fire Extinguishing System** protecting an engine room. This is the KTU 2024 expected schematic style.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Fire Detection Loop\nSmoke Heat Flame Detectors] --> B[Fire Control Panel\nBridge or ECR]
    B --> C{Manual Override\nPush Button or Pull Handle}
    C --> D[Pre-Discharge Alarm 1\nGeneral Alarm Slow Whoop]
    D --> E[Time Delay 20 to 30 sec]
    E --> F[Pre-Discharge Alarm 2\nContinuous Bell Evacuation]
    F --> G[Ventilation Shutdown\nFire Dampers Close]
    G --> H[Fuel Oil and Lub Oil Valves Close]
    H --> I[Solenoid or Pneumatic Actuation\nPilot Cylinder Fires]
    I --> J[Master Valve Opens\nCO2 Bank Releases]
    J --> K[Distribution Manifold\nand Piping Network]
    K --> L[Discharge Nozzles\nin Engine Room]
    L --> M[Engine Room Flooded\nCO2 34 percent]
    M --> N[Hold Time 15 min\nMinimum]
    N --> O[Ventilation and Gas Test\nBefore Re Entry]
    O --> P[System Reset and Recommission]
    B -.->|Continuous Monitoring| Q[Pressure Gauges\nWeighing Scales]
    Q --> B
```

---

## 4.2 Component Architecture — Cylinder Bank Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CYL[CYLINDER BANK]
        A1[Cylinder 01\n45 kg CO2] --> V1[Master Valve]
        A2[Cylinder 02\n45 kg CO2] --> V1
        A3[Cylinder 03\n45 kg CO2] --> V1
        A4[Cylinder n\n45 kg CO2] --> V1
    end
    V1 --> M[Manifold and\nHeader Pipe]
    P[Pilot Cylinder\n5 kg Pneumatic] --> V1
    SV[Solenoid Valve\n24V DC] --> P
    CP[Fire Control Panel] --> SV
    M --> D[Distribution Pipes\nto Engine Room]
    D --> N1[Nozzle 1\nDischarge Horn]
    D --> N2[Nozzle 2\nDischarge Horn]
    D --> N3[Nozzle n\nDischarge Horn]
```

---

## 4.3 Comparative Decision Tree — Medium Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START[Choose Fire Extinguishing Medium] --> Q1{Is Space\nNormally Manned?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, immediate re-entry| Q2{Is it a high-value\nequipment space?}
    Q1 -->|No, evacuation OK| Q3{Is it machinery\nspace of Cat A?}
    Q2 -->|Yes, e.g. server room| NOVEC[Novec 1230\n4.2 to 6 percent]
    Q2 -->|No, e.g. console| FM200[FM-200 HFC-227ea\n7 percent]
    Q3 -->|Yes| CO2[CO2 System\n34 percent FSS Code]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Chemical or\nOil Tanker?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| IG[Inert Gas IG-541 or IG-55\n37 to 43 percent]
    Q4 -->|No, paint locker| CO2
```

---

## 4.4 System State Diagram — Activation Sequence

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Standby
    Standby --> DetectorActive: Fire Detected
    DetectorActive --> ConfirmedFire: Two Detectors or Manual Confirm
    ConfirmedFire --> PreAlarm1: First Stage Alarm
    PreAlarm1 --> PreAlarm2: Time Delay Expired
    PreAlarm2 --> PreDischarge: Personnel Evacuated
    PreDischarge --> Discharge: Master Valve Opens
    Discharge --> Hold: Flooding Complete
    Hold --> Ventilate: 15 min Hold Complete
    Ventilate --> ReEntry: Gas Test Pass
    ReEntry --> Standby: System Reset
    PreDischarge --> Aborted: Manual Override Cancel
    Aborted --> Standby
```

---

## 4.5 Spatial Layout — Machinery Space Coverage (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENG[ENGINE ROOM PROTECTED SPACE]
        direction TB
        N1[Nozzle A\nPort Forward]:::noz
        N2[Nozzle B\nPort Aft]:::noz
        N3[Nozzle C\nStbd Forward]:::noz
        N4[Nozzle D\nStbd Aft]:::noz
        N5[Nozzle E\nCenterline Upper]:::noz
        N6[Nozzle F\nBilge Level]:::noz
    end
    classDef noz fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    N1 --- N2
    N3 --- N4
    N5 --- N6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Note:** Nozzle placement must ensure **uniform 3D coverage**. The FSS Code requires that no point in the protected space is more than the manufacturer's certified **discharge throw distance** from a nozzle. For machinery spaces, nozzles are typically placed on the **upper level (gantry)**, with additional nozzles in the **bilge well** to smother oil-pool fires.

---

## 4.6 Sequence vs. Time — Gantt-Style Timeline

```mermaid
gantt
    title Fixed CO2 System Activation Timeline
    dateFormat ss
    axisFormat %S sec
    section Detection
    Detector activation          :a1, 00, 1s
    Confirmed fire signal        :a2, after a1, 1s
    section Alarms
    Pre-alarm 1 (slow whoop)     :b1, after a2, 5s
    Pre-alarm 2 (continuous)     :b2, after b1, 20s
    section Shutdown
    Ventilation and fuel shutoff :c1, after b2, 2s
    section Discharge
    Pilot cylinder fires         :d1, after c1, 1s
    Main CO2 release             :d2, after d1, 9s
    section Hold
    15 min hold time             :e1, after d2, 900s
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
