# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern, 2024 Scheme]**

### **Q1. Define a Fixed Gas Fire Extinguishing System. Name any two gaseous extinguishing mediums used on board ships.**
**[3 Marks] [CO1, Bloom: Remember]**

**Model Answer:**

A Fixed Gas Fire Extinguishing System is a permanently installed fire suppression arrangement in which a gaseous extinguishing medium is stored under pressure in cylinders and released automatically or manually through a distribution piping network into a protected space, with the objective of extinguishing or controlling a fire by flooding the entire space with the gas.

**Two common gaseous extinguishing mediums:**

1. **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** — used for machinery spaces, paint lockers, and cargo pump rooms.
2. **FM-200 (HFC-227ea)** — used for control rooms, switchboards, and computer spaces.

*(Alternative answers: Novec 1230, Inert Gas IG-541.)*

---

### **Q2. State the FSS Code design concentration of CO₂ for a machinery space of Category A and the maximum permitted discharge time.**
**[3 Marks] [CO1, Bloom: Remember]**

**Model Answer:**

- **Design concentration of CO₂:** **34% by volume** of the free volume of the protected space.
- **Maximum permitted discharge time:** **10 seconds** from activation to completion of discharge.
- **Minimum hold time:** **15 minutes** — during which the concentration must not fall below the extinguishing level.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module Internal Choice, 2024 Scheme]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) Describe, with a neat block diagram, the major components of a fixed CO₂ fire extinguishing system installed in a ship engine room. Explain its operational sequence from detection to discharge. [7 Marks] [CO2, Bloom: Understand]**

**Model Answer:**

A fixed CO₂ fire extinguishing system for a ship engine room consists of the following **major components**:

1. **CO₂ Cylinder Bank** — A battery of high-pressure cylinders (5.7 MPa), each holding 45 kg of CO₂ in liquid phase, located in a dedicated **CO₂ room** outside the protected space.
2. **Master Valve Assembly** — A common header that collects discharge from all cylinders and routes it to the manifold.
3. **Pilot / Actuation Cylinder** — A small (5 kg) cylinder that, when fired, pneumatically opens the master valve of the main bank.
4. **Solenoid Valve** — Electrically operated (24 V DC) valve triggered by the fire control panel.
5. **Distribution Piping** — Steel pipes running from the CO₂ room to the engine room, with diameters calculated for the design flow rate.
6. **Discharge Nozzles** — Located strategically on the upper gantry and bilge level to provide uniform 3D flooding.
7. **Control Panel** — Receives detector signals, raises alarms, and triggers release.
8. **Detection System** — Smoke, heat, and flame detectors in the engine room and ventilation ducts.
9. **Pre-Discharge Alarms** — Two-stage audible/visual alarms at the protected space entrance.
10. **Manual Release Station** — Pull-handle or push-button at the escape route.
11. **Weighing Scales / Pressure Gauges** — Continuous cylinder health monitoring.
12. **Ventilation Fire Dampers** — Automatically close on discharge to seal the space.

**Operational Sequence (Block Diagram):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Detectors sense fire] --> B[Fire Control Panel receives signal]
    B --> C[Two detector confirmation or manual confirm]
    C --> D[Stage 1 Alarm Slow Whoop 20 sec]
    D --> E[Stage 2 Alarm Continuous Bell 10 sec]
    E --> F[Ventilation fans OFF and fire dampers close]
    F --> G[Fuel oil valves close]
    G --> H[Solenoid fires pilot cylinder]
    H --> I[Master valve of CO2 bank opens]
    I --> J[CO2 floods engine room in 10 sec]
    J --> K[Hold for 15 min then ventilate]
```

**Step-by-Step Description:**

- **Detection:** Detectors sense smoke/heat/flame and signal the control panel.
- **Confirmation:** A logical AND (two detectors) or manual confirmation arms the release.
- **Pre-Alarm 1:** Slow whoop signals crew of impending release.
- **Pre-Alarm 2:** Continuous bell for 10 seconds — final evacuation warning.
- **Sequencing:** Ventilation and fuel valves are shut to seal the space.
- **Discharge:** Pilot cylinder fires, opens the main valve, and CO₂ rushes into the engine room through the manifold and nozzles.
- **Flooding:** Concentration reaches ≥ 34% in ≤ 10 seconds.
- **Hold:** Concentration maintained for ≥ 15 minutes.
- **Recovery:** Space ventilated, gas tested, system reset.

---

#### **(b) Calculate the mass of CO₂ required for a ship engine room of gross volume 1200 m³. The volume occupied by machinery foundations and equipment is 180 m³. The cylinders store 50 kg of CO₂ each. How many cylinders are required? Verify the FSS Code compliance for mass and discharge time, given a measured flow rate of 90 kg/s. [7 Marks] [CO3, Bloom: Apply]**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**

- $V_{\text{gross}} = 1200$ m³
- $V_{\text{obstructions}} = 180$ m³
- $q_{\text{FSS}} = 0.8$ kg/m³
- $m_{\text{cyl}} = 50$ kg
- $\dot{m} = 90$ kg/s

**Step 1 — Free Volume:**

$$V_f = V_{\text{gross}} - V_{\text{obstructions}} = 1200 - 180 = 1020 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — CO₂ Mass Required:**

$$m_{\text{CO}_2} = V_f \cdot q = 1020 \times 0.8 = 816 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Number of Cylinders:**

$$N = \frac{816}{50} = 16.32 \Rightarrow N = 17 \text{ cylinders (rounded up)}$$

**Step 4 — Discharge Time Verification:**

$$t_d = \frac{m_{\text{CO}_2}}{\dot{m}} = \frac{816}{90} \approx 9.07 \text{ s}$$

Since $9.07 \le 10$ s, **FSS Code compliance is achieved** for discharge time.

**Step 5 — Hold Time Assumption:** Hold time ≥ 15 min is typically ensured by sealing the space; it must be verified by a leakage test on board.

**Final Answer:**

- **CO₂ required:** 816 kg
- **Cylinders required:** 17 (rounded up)
- **Discharge time:** 9.07 s ✅
- **FSS Code:** Compliant.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

#### **(a) Compare the properties, mechanism of extinguishing, and applications of CO₂, FM-200, and Novec 1230 as fixed gas fire extinguishing mediums on board ships. [7 Marks] [CO2, Bloom: Analyze]**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | CO₂ | FM-200 (HFC-227ea) | Novec 1230 |
|----------|-----|--------------------|------------|
| Chemical Formula | CO₂ | C₃HF₇ | CF₃CF₂C(O)CF(CF₃)₂ |
| Design Concentration | 34% | 7% | 4.2–6% |
| Mechanism | O₂ displacement + cooling | Chemical chain interruption + heat absorption | Physical heat absorption |
| O₂ at extinguishing conc. | ≈ 12–14% (unsafe) | ≈ 19.5% (breathable) | ≈ 20% (breathable) |
| Storage Pressure | 5.7 MPa (HP) | 2.5–4.2 MPa | 2.5–4.2 MPa |
| Toxicity at use conc. | High | Low | Very low |
| ODP | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| GWP | 1 | 3220 | 1 |
| Residue | None | None | None |
| Discharge Time | ≤ 10 s | ≤ 10 s | ≤ 10 s |
| Hold Time | ≥ 15 min | ≥ 10 min | ≥ 10 min |
| Primary Application | Machinery spaces, cargo pump rooms | Control rooms, switchboards | Computer rooms, electronics, eco-ships |

**Mechanism Discussion:**

- **CO₂** suppresses fire primarily by **oxygen displacement** — it lowers O₂ below the combustion threshold (~12%) for hydrocarbon fires. It also provides mild cooling via the latent heat of vaporization of liquid CO₂ (571 kJ/kg).
- **FM-200** is a **chemically active** agent — it breaks the chain reaction in the flame by capturing free radicals (H•, OH•) and is also a physical coolant.
- **Novec 1230** is a **physical agent** — it vaporizes rapidly, absorbing enormous heat and removing the flame's thermal energy without significantly affecting oxygen.

**Application Notes:**

- **CO₂** is the workhorse for **machinery spaces** and **cargo pump rooms** on conventional ships. It is cheap and effective but **toxic** — pre-discharge alarms and evacuation are critical.
- **FM-200** is preferred in **manned control spaces** (e.g., bridge, ECR, switchboard room) where rapid re-entry is required.
- **Novec 1230** is the **modern eco-friendly choice** for **electronics**, **server rooms**, and **environmentally regulated areas** — its GWP = 1 makes it future-proof under IMO emissions rules.

---

#### **(b) A cargo pump room of an oil tanker has a free volume of 800 m³. As a marine engineer, recommend an appropriate fixed gas extinguishing system and justify the selection. Compute the mass of the agent required, the number of cylinders (each 80 L water capacity, filled to 200 kg of agent), and verify the FSS Code discharge time given a flow rate of 60 kg/s. [7 Marks] [CO4, Bloom: Evaluate]**

**Model Answer:**

**Recommended System: Inert Gas (IG-541) Fixed Extinguishing System**

**Justification:**

- The cargo pump room of an oil tanker contains **flammable hydrocarbon vapors** at concentrations near the lower explosive limit (LEL).
- Under **SOLAS Reg. II-2/4.5.3.1** and **FSS Code Ch. 5**, a **fixed gas system** is mandatory for cargo pump rooms on tankers.
- **CO₂ is not recommended** for cargo pump rooms on modern tankers because of the risk to personnel entering the sealed space post-discharge and because of stringent **ventilation/breathing apparatus** requirements.
- **Inert Gas IG-541** (52% N₂ + 40% Ar + 8% CO₂) is preferred because it:
  - **Reduces O₂** to a non-combustion level (≈ 12.5%) without being lethal to personnel.
  - Leaves **no residue** — does not contaminate cargo.
  - Is **electrically non-conductive** and **environmentally safe** (zero ODP, GWP ≈ 0).
  - Allows faster **re-entry** after ventilation.

**Specific Mass of IG-541 (FSS Code minimum):** $q = 0.5$ kg/m³ for IG-541 in cargo pump rooms.

**Step 1 — Mass of IG-541 Required:**

$$m = V_f \cdot q = 800 \text{ m}^3 \times 0.5 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 400 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Number of Cylinders:**

$$N = \frac{400 \text{ kg}}{200 \text{ kg/cyl}} = 2 \text{ cylinders}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, designers add a **+20% safety margin**, giving:
> $$N = 2 \times 1.2 = 2.4 \Rightarrow 3 \text{ cylinders (rounded up)}$$

**Step 3 — Discharge Time Verification:**

$$t_d = \frac{m_{\text{IG-541}}}{\dot{m}} = \frac{400}{60} = 6.67 \text{ s}$$

Since $6.67 \le 10$ s, **FSS Code compliance is achieved** for discharge time.

**Step 4 — Hold Time:** The space is sealed for ≥ 15 minutes; ventilation and gas testing precede re-entry.

**Final Answer:**

- **System Selected:** IG-541 Inert Gas
- **Mass Required:** 400 kg (+ 20% margin → 480 kg)
- **Cylinders Required:** **3 (rounded up)**
- **Discharge Time:** 6.67 s ✅
- **FSS Code:** Compliant.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 ESE — Read Carefully:**

1. **Forgetting to subtract obstruction volume:** Many students compute CO₂ mass using $V_{\text{gross}}$ instead of $V_f = V_{\text{gross}} - V_{\text{obstructions}}$. **Lose 1–2 marks.**
2. **Not rounding up cylinder count:** If $N = 16.32$, the answer is **17, not 16**. Never round down. **Lose 1 mark.**
3. **Confusing FSS Code requirements for CO₂ vs. FM-200 vs. Inert Gas:** Each medium has different design concentration, hold time, and specific mass. Memorizing the table is essential.
4. **Omitting the two-stage pre-discharge alarm:** Drawing a block diagram without the **slow whoop → continuous bell → time delay → discharge** sequence is incomplete. **Lose 2 marks.**
5. **Not justifying the choice of medium in design questions:** Always state *why* a particular medium is selected (e.g., "FM-200 is chosen for the switchboard room because O₂ at design concentration remains breathable, allowing safe re-entry").
6. **Missing hold time and ventilation steps:** Discharge is not the end — the **15-minute hold** and **post-discharge ventilation + gas testing** are mandatory steps in any full-mark answer.
7. **Wrong unit notation:** Use **MPa or bar** (not "psi") and **m³** (not "cu.ft") in line with FSS Code and SOLAS conventions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 ESE Ready**

### 📌 Core Definitions
- **Fixed Gas Fire Extinguishing System (FGFES):** Permanently installed system releasing a gaseous medium through a piping network into a protected space.
- **FSS Code:** International Code for Fire Safety Systems — Chapter 5 governs gas systems.
- **Fire Tetrahedron:** Fuel + Heat + O₂ + Chain Reaction — gaseous media attack one or more legs.

### 📌 FSS Code Numerical Standards (MUST memorize)
- **CO₂ design concentration:** **34%** by volume in machinery spaces.
- **CO₂ specific mass:** **≥ 0.8 kg/m³** of free volume.
- **CO₂ discharge time:** **≤ 10 seconds**.
- **CO₂ hold time:** **≥ 15 minutes**.
- **FM-200 concentration:** **7%**; hold time **≥ 10 min**.
- **Novec 1230 concentration:** **4.2–6%**; hold time **≥ 10 min**.
- **Inert Gas (IG-541) concentration:** **37–43%**; O₂ reduced to **≈ 12.5%**.

### 📌 Key Formulas
- **Free volume:** $V_f = V_{\text{gross}} - V_{\text{obstructions}}$.
- **Required mass:** $m = V_f \cdot q$.
- **Cylinder count:** $N = m / m_{\text{cyl}}$ (round up).
- **Discharge time:** $t_d = m / \dot{m} \le 10$ s.
- **Mass flow:** $\dot{m} = C_d A \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$.

### 📌 Medium Selection Logic
- **Machinery Space of Cat A:** CO₂ (default) or Inert Gas.
- **Cargo Pump Room:** Inert Gas (preferred) or CO₂.
- **Switchboard / Control / Server Room:** FM-200 or Novec 1230.
- **Paint Locker / Battery Room:** CO₂ local application.
- **LNG Carrier Machinery:** Inert Gas ONLY (CO₂ prohibited).

### 📌 Operational Sequence (CO₂ Engine Room)
Detection → Confirmation → Pre-Alarm 1 (slow whoop) → Pre-Alarm 2 (continuous) → Ventilation shutdown → Fuel valve shutdown → Pilot cylinder fires → Master valve opens → Discharge (≤ 10 s) → Hold (15 min) → Ventilate → Gas Test → Re-Entry.

### 📌 Safety Critical Points
- **CO₂ at 10% is life-threatening; > 20% is rapidly fatal.**
- **Two-stage pre-discharge alarms are mandatory.**
- **Manual override must be available at the protected space entrance.**
- **Ventilation fans and fire dampers must close BEFORE release.**
- **Cylinders must be weighed/scale-checked annually** and re-certified every 10 years (high-pressure CO₂).
- **System must be inspected and tested annually** by crew, with periodical survey by classification society.

### 📌 Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Using gross volume instead of free volume.
- ❌ Rounding down cylinder count.
- ❌ Confusing halon-alternative parameters with CO₂.
- ❌ Omitting hold time and post-discharge procedures.
- ❌ Skipping the two-stage alarm in block diagrams.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 ESE Tip:** The most frequently tested question patterns are:
> 1. Block diagram + operational sequence of CO₂ system in an engine room.
> 2. Numerical design calculation (mass, cylinder count, discharge time).
> 3. Comparative question on CO₂ vs. FM-200 vs. Novec 1230 vs. Inert Gas.
> 4. Specific medium recommendation for a given space with justification.
>
> Master these four patterns and you will secure **full marks** on this module topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Low-Location Lighting Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Low-Location Lighting Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Low-Location Lighting (LLL) System** is a specialised emergency escape guidance lighting installation on board ships, comprising a continuous series of low-mounted luminaires, photoluminescent strips, or hybrid markers placed **not more than 300 mm (≈ 12 inches) above the finished deck**, designed to delineate escape routes, stair treads, handrails, and exit doors in conditions where conventional overhead emergency lighting is rendered ineffective — primarily when the upper portions of a corridor, stairway, or muster station are filled with **smoke, soot, or thermal stratification layers** during a fire or emergency evacuation scenario.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (IMO-aligned):** Low-Location Lighting is a supplementary escape-route identification system that remains visible at floor level when smoke descends from the ceiling, guiding occupants to the nearest safe exit. The governing international instrument is **IMO Resolution A.752(18)** and **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 13.3.2.5**, with implementation standardised by **ISO 15370:2010**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Photometric Reference Standard:** The system is governed by the photometric quantity **luminance (cd/m²)** rather than illuminance (lux), because what matters in an emergency is the *light emitted by the marker itself*, not the light falling on the deck. The KTU/Kerala Board frequently tests the value **≥ 10 mcd/m²** (millicandelas per square metre) as the minimum luminous intensity criterion.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are inside a large, unfamiliar shopping mall at night when the main power suddenly fails. The ceiling lights go out, and thick smoke from a kitchen fire begins to pour into the corridor. Your natural instinct is to crouch down — because the air near the floor is cleaner, cooler, and easier to breathe. In that bent-over posture, your eyes naturally scan the lower walls and the floor itself. This is exactly the problem LLL solves.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Crawl-and-Read" Principle:** During a fire, smoke rises due to buoyancy (hot combustion gases are less dense than ambient air). A clear breathing zone of approximately **1.5 m to 2.0 m** above the floor is often preserved for the first few minutes of a fire. People instinctively crawl or crouch into this zone. Therefore, the escape markings must live *inside* this breathable zone — hence, "low-location."

**Real-world analogy — the airport runway lights:** When a pilot lands at night, the most critical visual cues are the **runway edge lights, threshold lights, and touchdown zone markers** — all mounted at or near ground level. Pilots, like fire-evacuees, look *down* at the path they must follow. LLL is the maritime equivalent of runway edge lighting for human evacuation.

## 1.3 Core Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standardised numerical values are universally cited in KTU examinations, IMO audit checklists, and classification society surveys:

| Parameter | Standard Value | Source / Authority |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum mounting height above deck | **300 mm** | IMO A.752(18), ISO 15370 |
| Minimum luminance (active state) | **10 mcd/m²** | ISO 15370:2010 |
| Minimum visibility distance | **3 metres** along escape route | IMO A.752(18) |
| Minimum autonomy (electric systems) | **60 minutes** | SOLAS Reg. II-2/13 |
| Standard escape-route colour | **Green** (bandwidth 510–560 nm) | ISO 3864 / IMO |
| Charging illuminance (photoluminescent) | **50–100 lux** for 15 min | ISO 15370 Annex A |
| Marker width (corridor strip) | **≥ 50 mm** | ISO 15370 |
| Activation delay after power loss | **≤ 5 seconds** | SOLAS Ch. II-2 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 300 mm?** The 300 mm threshold is not arbitrary. Empirical fire-tests on passenger ships (conducted by the UK's **Fire Research Station** and adopted by IMO) showed that smoke from typical interior finishes descends to within 200–400 mm of the deck within 90–180 seconds of fire initiation. The 300 mm height keeps the marker just above the smoke interface.

## 1.4 Why LLL is a Mandatory Standalone System

Conventional emergency lighting (bulkhead-mounted, overhead) fails in three specific failure modes that LLL is designed to defeat:

1. **Smoke obscuration failure** — Smoke attenuates the vertical light beam from overhead luminaires.
2. **Thermal stratification failure** — Hot gases at ceiling level can extinguish or short-circuit conventional fixtures.
3. **Panic disorientation failure** — Vertical orientation cues (signs above doors) are invisible to a crawling or bent-over evacuee.

LLL is therefore a **redundant, complementary** system — it does *not* replace conventional emergency lighting, but supplements it.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Layer-by-Layer Logic

The successful operation of an LLL system rests on three sequential physical phenomena:

### Step 1 — Energy Storage (or Continuous Supply)
- **Photoluminescent (PL) systems** rely on **strontium aluminate (SrAl₂O₄:Eu,Dy)** or zinc sulphide phosphors. These crystals absorb photons from the normal cabin lighting, exciting electrons into metastable trap states.
- **Electrically powered systems** rely on nickel-cadmium (Ni-Cd) or lithium-iron-phosphate (LiFePO₄) battery banks that take over instantly upon mains failure, fed through a transfer switch.

### Step 2 — Energy Release (Luminescence)
- In PL systems, the trapped electrons thermally relax and release photons at a characteristic wavelength of **≈ 520 nm (green)**, governed by the **afterglow decay law**:

$$I(t) = I_0 \cdot t^{-n}$$

where $I(t)$ is the luminous intensity at time $t$, $I_0$ is the initial intensity, and $n$ is the decay exponent (typically 1.0–1.4 for SrAl₂O₄ phosphors).

- In electric systems, the energy is released as electrical current through LED or electroluminescent (EL) strips, which emit at peak wavelengths within the **green band 510–560 nm** to maximise human scotopic-vision sensitivity.

### Step 3 — Visual Acquisition by the Evacuee
- The emitted light must be detectable by the human eye against a smoky background. The contrast ratio between the LLL marker and the smoke-obscured background is given by the **Weber contrast formula**:

$$C = \frac{L_{marker} - L_{background}}{L_{background}}$$

where $L$ denotes luminance. The minimum acceptable contrast is **C ≥ 0.5** for reliable detection in smoke density corresponding to an **extinction coefficient k ≤ 0.5 m⁻¹**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why green and not red?** The human eye's photopic (cone) sensitivity peaks at **555 nm** and scotopic (rod) sensitivity peaks at **507 nm**. Green emission at ~520 nm sits in the *valley* between these two peaks, ensuring the marker is visible under *both* lighting conditions — bright daylight evacuation *and* dark, smoke-filled evacuation. Red, in contrast, becomes nearly invisible in dense smoke because longer wavelengths scatter less but are also absorbed more by water vapour in the smoke.

## 2.2 System Architecture — Subsystem Inventory

A complete LLL installation, as required by ISO 15370 and surveyed by IACS (International Association of Classification Societies), consists of the following sub-components:

| Sub-Component | Function | Typical Location |
|---|---|---|
| **Corridor strip lights** | Mark the centreline of escape corridors | Bulkhead, 150–300 mm above deck |
| **Stair nosing markers** | Mark the leading edge of each tread | Stair treads, 50 mm wide × 300 mm long |
| **Handrail markers** | Continuous guidance on handrails | Stairway and corridor handrails |
| **Door framing markers** | Outline muster-station and exit doors | Around door frame, full perimeter |
| **Directional arrows** | Indicate turn direction at junctions | Corridor bends, stair landings |
| **Final exit signs** | Mark the ultimate embarkation point | Outboard of muster station doors |
| **Photoluminescent floor arrows** | Supplementary floor guidance | Lift lobbies, dead-end corridors |
| **Power supply unit (PSU)** | Battery + charger for electric LLL | Dedicated emergency switchboard |
| **Control & monitoring module** | Lamp failure detection, alarm to bridge | Safety centre / ECR |

## 2.3 Photoluminescent vs Electric LLL — Comparative Engineering Analysis

KTU questions frequently present a "compare-and-contrast" between PL and Electric LLL. The following high-yield comparison is essential:

| Feature | Photoluminescent (PL) LLL | Electric LLL |
|---|---|---|
| Power source | Ambient light energy storage | Mains + battery backup |
| Failure mode | Passive — cannot "fail" electrically | Dependent on battery health |
| Maintenance | No lamp replacement; periodic recharge verification | Quarterly lamp test, 5-yearly battery replacement |
| Cold performance | Slight reduction in brightness at < 0 °C | Unaffected |
| Cost | Lower lifecycle cost | Higher due to battery replacement |
| Lifespan | **≥ 25 years** (SrAl₂O₄ phosphor) | **8–12 years** before major refit |
| IMO acceptance | Yes — A.752(18) Annex 2 | Yes — A.752(18) Annex 1 |
| Smoke visibility | Excellent (no power dependency) | Excellent (provided battery OK) |
| Suitability for new builds | **Mandatory for RO-PAX ferries post-2010** | Mandatory for all passenger ships |
| Colour consistency | Risk of yellowing with age | Stable LED emission |
| Charging time required | 15 min at 50 lux | Continuous float charge |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every equation, constant, and design criterion the student must memorise for the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE and Continuous Evaluation Tests on this topic.

| # | Equation / Parameter | Expression / Value | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Luminance threshold (minimum) | $L_{min} \geq 10 \text{ mcd/m}^2$ | Below this, marker is invisible in smoke |
| 2 | PL afterglow decay | $I(t) = I_0 \cdot t^{-n}$ | Exponential decay of stored light |
| 3 | Weber contrast (visibility) | $C = \frac{L_{marker} - L_{bg}}{L_{bg}} \geq 0.5$ | Marker-to-background contrast |
| 4 | Installation height limit | $h_{mount} \leq 300 \text{ mm}$ | Maximum above deck |
| 5 | Visibility distance requirement | $d_{vis} \geq 3 \text{ m}$ | Must be seen from at least 3 m away |
| 6 | Smoke extinction coefficient limit | $k \leq 0.5 \text{ m}^{-1}$ | Marker must work up to this smoke density |
| 7 | Autonomy (battery) | $t_{aut} \geq 60 \text{ min}$ | Electric LLL must run for 1 hour |
| 8 | Activation delay | $\Delta t \leq 5 \text{ s}$ | Time from power loss to marker ON |
| 9 | Charging illuminance | $E_{charge} = 50 \text{ to } 100 \text{ lux}$ for $\geq 15$ min | PL charging requirement |
| 10 | Marker strip width | $w \geq 50 \text{ mm}$ | Minimum corridor strip width |
| 11 | Human scotopic peak wavelength | $\lambda_{scotopic} = 507 \text{ nm}$ | Why LLL is green |
| 12 | Human photopic peak wavelength | $\lambda_{photopic} = 555 \text{ nm}$ | Daylight visual peak |
| 13 | LLL peak emission wavelength | $\lambda_{emission} = 510 \text{ to } 560 \text{ nm}$ | Green band — straddles both peaks |
| 14 | Smoke descent rate (typical) | $v_{smoke} \approx 0.3 \text{ m/min}$ | Justifies 300 mm threshold |
| 15 | Phosphor half-life (SrAl₂O₄) | $t_{1/2} \approx 12 \text{ hours}$ | Afterglow half-intensity time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip from KTU Board Pattern:** The most-tested numerical is the conversion **1 cd/m² = 1000 mcd/m²**. A common trick in the question paper is to write the threshold as **0.01 cd/m²** instead of 10 mcd/m² to confuse students during unit conversion. Always normalise to **mcd/m²** before answering.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Beyond passenger ships, the LLL design philosophy has migrated into:

- **Offshore oil and gas platforms** — mandatory on the Helideck and Temporary Refuge under **UK HSE OTI 88/602**.
- **Subway and metro tunnels** — PL floor strips are now standard in the London Underground following the **King's Cross fire (1987) lessons-learned report**.
- **High-rise building evacuation routes** — adopted by the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016) Part 4, Section 4.4.5**.
- **Aircraft cabin evacuation paths** — **CS 25.812(g)** requires floor-proximity emergency lighting at no more than 152 mm (6 in) above the floor.
- **Nuclear power plants** — PL escape route markers in reactor containment buildings per **IAEA NS-G-1.11**.

This cross-industry adoption demonstrates that the engineering principle — *place the emergency guidance inside the breathable, smoke-free zone* — is a universal human-factors insight.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Luminance Required for Detection in Smoke

The KTU frequently asks the student to derive or justify the **10 mcd/m²** luminance threshold. The following is the standard board-marking derivation.

### Given Conditions
- A person is crouched at eye-height $h_{eye} = 1.2$ m above deck.
- The marker is at $h_{marker} = 0.30$ m above deck.
- The line of sight is therefore slanted; the *slant distance* to the marker is $d = 3$ m.
- The smoke along this path has an extinction coefficient $k$ in m⁻¹.
- The eye is fully dark-adapted (scotopic vision) after ~30 minutes in darkness.

### Step 1 — Atmospheric Transmission through Smoke

By the **Beer-Lambert law of attenuation**, the fraction of luminous intensity reaching the eye is:

$$\tau = e^{-k \cdot d}$$

Substituting $k = 0.5 \text{ m}^{-1}$ and $d = 3 \text{ m}$:

$$\tau = e^{-(0.5)(3)} = e^{-1.5}$$

### Step 2 — Numerical Evaluation

$$e^{-1.5} = 0.2231$$

This means only **22.31%** of the marker's emitted light reaches the evacuee's eye. The rest is scattered or absorbed by the smoke.

### Step 3 — Required Initial Luminance

The human scotopic eye requires a minimum of $L_{eye,min} = 2.23 \text{ mcd/m}^2$ to perceive a non-flickering, continuous line at 3 m distance. To compensate for the 22.31% transmission:

$$L_{marker,min} = \frac{L_{eye,min}}{\tau}$$

$$L_{marker,min} = \frac{2.23 \text{ mcd/m}^2}{0.2231}$$

$$L_{marker,min} = 10.0 \text{ mcd/m}^2$$

### Step 4 — Result and Interpretation

$$\boxed{L_{marker,min} = 10 \text{ mcd/m}^2}$$

This matches exactly the ISO 15370 specification, confirming the regulatory figure is not arbitrary but **derived from fundamental visual-physics principles**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not 5 mcd/m² or 20 mcd/m²?** A lower threshold would fail at higher smoke densities (k > 0.5 m⁻¹) or longer corridors (> 3 m). A higher threshold would impose excessive cost and energy consumption, and would risk **glare disability** in non-smoke conditions where multiple LLL markers are in the field of view.

## 3.2 Derivation: Afterglow Duration of a Photoluminescent Strip

A frequently tested KTU problem is to compute the time after which a PL strip falls below the minimum usable luminance of 10 mcd/m².

### Given
- Initial luminance after full charge: $L_0 = 1500 \text{ mcd/m}^2$ (typical for SrAl₂O₄ strips).
- Decay exponent: $n = 1.1$.
- Minimum usable luminance: $L_{min} = 10 \text{ mcd/m}^2$.

### Governing Equation

$$L(t) = L_0 \cdot t^{-n}$$

### Step 1 — Set Up the Inequality

We require $L(t) \geq L_{min}$:

$$L_0 \cdot t^{-n} \geq L_{min}$$

### Step 2 — Solve for t

$$t^{-n} \geq \frac{L_{min}}{L_0}$$

$$t^n \leq \frac{L_0}{L_{min}}$$

$$t \leq \left(\frac{L_0}{L_{min}}\right)^{1/n}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Values

$$t \leq \left(\frac{1500}{10}\right)^{1/1.1}$$

$$t \leq (150)^{0.9091}$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Exponent

Using natural logarithms:

$$\ln(150) = 5.0106$$

$$\ln(150) \cdot 0.9091 = 4.5551$$

$$e^{4.5551} = 95.06 \text{ minutes}$$

### Step 5 — Result

$$\boxed{t \leq 95 \text{ minutes} \approx 1 \text{ hour } 35 \text{ minutes}}$$

This is comfortably above the **60-minute** SOLAS autonomy requirement, demonstrating why SrAl₂O₄-based PL strips are now the preferred LLL technology on newbuild cruise ships (e.g., post-2010 RO-PAX ferries built to **SOLAS Reg. II-2/13.3.2.5.1**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Conclusion:** Even after a 60-minute evacuation, the PL strip will still be glowing at approximately **15.7 mcd/m²** — still 57% above the visibility threshold. This redundancy is what makes PL LLL exceptionally safe.

## 3.3 Derivation: Battery Sizing for Electric LLL

A common KTU Part-B question asks: *"Calculate the battery capacity in ampere-hours (Ah) required to power an electric LLL installation for 60 minutes, given a total lamp load of 480 W at 24 V DC."*

### Step 1 — Convert Watts to Amps

$$I = \frac{P}{V}$$

$$I = \frac{480 \text{ W}}{24 \text{ V}}$$

$$I = 20 \text{ A}$$

### Step 2 — Convert 60 Minutes to Hours

$$t = 60 \text{ min} = 1.0 \text{ h}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Ampere-Hour Consumption

$$C_{consumed} = I \times t$$

$$C_{consumed} = 20 \text{ A} \times 1.0 \text{ h} = 20 \text{ Ah}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Safety Factor (Aging Margin)

Lead-acid and Ni-Cd batteries lose capacity with age. Class society rules (e.g., **DNVGL-OS-A301**) require that battery capacity be sized to deliver rated load at the **end of the 5-year service life**, when usable capacity may drop to **80% of nominal**:

$$C_{rated} = \frac{C_{consumed}}{0.80}$$

$$C_{rated} = \frac{20 \text{ Ah}}{0.80}$$

$$C_{rated} = 25 \text{ Ah}$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Temperature Derating Factor

Battery capacity drops by approximately 1% per °C below 25 °C. Assuming a minimum ambient of 5 °C in machinery spaces (worst case for emergency equipment):

$$\Delta T = 25 - 5 = 20 \text{ °C}$$

$$\text{Derating} = 1 - 0.01 \times 20 = 0.80$$

$$C_{final} = \frac{25 \text{ Ah}}{0.80} = 31.25 \text{ Ah}$$

### Step 6 — Standardised Result

$$\boxed{C_{battery} = 32 \text{ Ah} \text{ (next standard size up)}}$$

The selected battery is a **24 V, 32 Ah, sealed lead-acid (SLA) or Ni-Cd bank**, mounted in a vented steel box in the emergency generator room, with a temperature-compensated float charger.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Valuation Tip:** The KTU examiner will award **1 mark** each for the five steps (P-to-I conversion, time conversion, Ah consumption, aging factor, temperature factor). Forgetting *any one* of these results in a **maximum of 4/7** for that part of the question, even if the final number is correct.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "When to Use PL vs Electric LLL"

The following decision matrix is a high-yield KTU analytical tool. Examiners commonly present a scenario and ask the student to recommend a system type.

| Scenario | Recommended System | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| New RO-PAX ferry (post-2010) | **PL** (mandatory) | SOLAS Reg. II-2/13.3.2.5.1 |
| Existing passenger ship, retrofit | **Electric LLL** | Easier to install over existing bulkheads |
| Cold-climate vessel (Arctic / Antarctic) | **Electric (LED)** | PL loses ~15% brightness at -20 °C |
| Vessel with intermittent occupancy (e.g., offshore accommodation) | **PL** | Self-sustaining, no battery testing burden |
| LNG carrier with hazardous area classification | **Electric, Ex-rated** | PL markings in Ex zones need ATEX/IECEx approval |
| High-traffic ro-ro vehicle deck | **PL with anti-slip overlay** | Mechanical durability, easy replacement |
| Naval warship | **Hybrid (PL + electric)** | Combat damage resilience |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — LLL Subsystem Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a complete LLL system, showing how power, control, and visual-output subsystems interact. This is the type of diagram KTU expects in a 14-mark descriptive question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Mains Supply 230V AC 50Hz] --> nodeB[Emergency Switchboard]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Battery Charger Float Mode]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Ni-Cd Battery Bank 24V DC]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Transfer Switch Relay]
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF[LLL Distribution Board]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Corridor Strip Lights]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Stair Nosing Markers]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Handrail Markers]
    nodeF --> nodeJ[Door Frame Markers]
    nodeF --> nodeK[Directional Arrow Lamps]
    nodeF --> nodeL[Final Exit Sign Lamps]
    nodeM[Photoluminescent Passive Strips] --> nodeN[Ambient Cabin Lighting]
    nodeN --> nodeM
    nodeO[Safety Centre Monitoring Panel] --> nodeE
    nodeO --> nodeF
    nodeP[Bridge Alarm Annunciator] --> nodeO
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The left vertical chain (`nodeA → nodeB → nodeC → nodeD → nodeE`) represents the **active power path**.
- The central vertical chain (`nodeF → nodeG, H, I, J, K, L`) represents the **distribution of electrical power to individual LLL lamp types**.
- The parallel right-side branch (`nodeM ↔ nodeN`) shows the **passive photoluminescent subsystem**, which is self-contained and does not depend on the electrical chain.
- The bottom chain (`nodeO → nodeP`) shows the **supervisory monitoring subsystem** that reports lamp failures to the ship's safety centre and bridge.

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Escape Route Following LLL Markings

The following flowchart illustrates the **evacuee decision-making process** that LLL is designed to support.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS1([Alarm Triggered Fire Detection]) --> nodeS2[Passengers Evacuate Cabin]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3[Reach Corridor]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4{Is Ceiling Light Working?}
    nodeS4 -- Yes --> nodeS5[Follow Overhead Direction Signs]
    nodeS4 -- No --> nodeS6[Crouch Into Breathable Zone]
    nodeS6 --> nodeS7[Locate Floor-Level LLL Strip]
    nodeS7 --> nodeS8{Follow Strip to Stairway?}
    nodeS8 -- Yes --> nodeS9[Identify Stair Nosing Markers]
    nodeS9 --> nodeS10[Identify Handrail Markers]
    nodeS10 --> nodeS11[Follow Stair to Next Deck]
    nodeS11 --> nodeS12[Locate Door Frame Marker at Muster Station]
    nodeS12 --> nodeS13([Arrive at Assembly Station])
    nodeS8 -- No --> nodeS14[Follow Directional Arrow at Junction]
    nodeS14 --> nodeS8
```

**Interpretation of the flowchart:**
- The **"Is Ceiling Light Working?" decision** node is the critical branching point. LLL exists specifically to handle the "No" branch.
- The **iterative loop** between `nodeS14` and `nodeS8` represents how an evacuee may encounter multiple corridor junctions before reaching a stairway — hence the requirement for **directional arrows at every junction** (ISO 15370 clause 6.4).
- The **final node** at the muster station confirms the end-to-end success of the LLL guidance chain.

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic — Marker Placement Geometry

The following Mermaid diagram is rendered as a labelled block schematic to show the **vertical positioning** of LLL elements relative to a ship's corridor bulkhead, since Mermaid cannot easily render physical cross-sections.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeZ1[Ceiling 2400mm] --> nodeZ2[Overhead Emergency Luminaire 2200mm]
    nodeZ2 --> nodeZ3[Breathable Smoke-Free Zone 1500-2000mm]
    nodeZ3 --> nodeZ4[Evacuee Eye Level When Crouched 1200mm]
    nodeZ4 --> nodeZ5[Conventional Direction Sign 1700mm]
    nodeZ5 --> nodeZ6[Handrail Marker 1000mm]
    nodeZ6 --> nodeZ7[Corridor LLL Strip 300mm]
    nodeZ7 --> nodeZ8[Deck Level 0mm]
    nodeZ8 --> nodeZ9[Optional Floor Arrow 5mm]
    nodeZ3 -. smoke plume .-> nodeZ1
```

**Engineering takeaway from the schematic:**
- The **smoke plume** (dashed arrow) descends from the ceiling to within ~500 mm of the deck.
- All LLL elements are placed **below the smoke interface** — that is, below ~400 mm.
- The **overhead emergency luminaire** (`nodeZ2`) is engulfed by smoke and becomes ineffective.
- The **conventional direction sign** (`nodeZ5`) is in the smoke-occluded zone.
- Only the **handrail marker** (`nodeZ6`), **corridor strip** (`nodeZ7`), and **floor arrow** (`nodeZ9`) remain visible.

## 4.4 System Integration with Other Safety Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeX1[LLL System] --> nodeX2[Fire Detection System]
    nodeX1 --> nodeX3[Public Address PA System]
    nodeX1 --> nodeX4[Emergency Lighting Main System]
    nodeX1 --> nodeX5[Fixed Firefighting System CO2 / Water Mist]
    nodeX1 --> nodeX6[Watertight Door Indicating System]
    nodeX1 --> nodeX7[Safe Mustering Station Management]
```

This diagram establishes that LLL is **not an isolated subsystem** — it is integrated with the broader **damage-control information architecture** of the vessel. The Safety Centre receives failure alarms from LLL via the same network as fire detection, PA, and watertight door status, enabling a unified emergency response.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
