- **Type Approval** certifies a *design family or type* and covers all units produced to that approved design, with a validity of **5 years** and annual surveillance. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Product Approval** certifies a *specific model or batch* and does not necessarily extend to future production units without re-assessment. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Module 2 Pattern]**

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **Type Approval procedure** adopted by classification societies for marine equipment. Mention the role of IACS UR Z17. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **importance of Type Approval in ensuring maritime safety** with reference to life-saving appliances and fire-fighting equipment. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

The Type Approval procedure involves the following sequential steps: **(1 mark for naming)**

1. **Pre-application and identification of applicable standards** — The manufacturer identifies the relevant IMO performance standard (e.g., MSC.48(66) for lifejackets) and the corresponding classification society rules. **(1 mark)**
2. **Submission of documentation (Technical File)** — Design drawings, material certificates, bill of materials, FMEA report, software documentation, and quality manual are submitted to the society's plan review department. **(1 mark)**
3. **Type Testing** — A representative production sample is tested at an accredited laboratory against the prescribed standard (e.g., drop test, hydrostatic pressure test, fire exposure test). **(1 mark)**
4. **Manufacturing Assessment (Initial Factory Audit)** — Society auditors visit the production facility to verify compliance with IACS UR Z17, ISO 9001, and the approved design. **(1 mark)**
5. **Certificate Issue** — On satisfactory completion of all four stages, the Type Approval Certificate (TAC) is issued, valid for **5 years**. **(0.5 marks)**
6. **Surveillance Audits** — Annual surveillance audits are conducted to ensure continued conformity. Renewal audit occurs every 5 years. **(0.5 marks)**

**Role of IACS UR Z17:** IACS Unified Requirement Z17 is the harmonized procedural framework adopted by all IACS member societies (ABS, DNV, LR, BV, RINA, KR, ClassNK, CCS). It establishes the **common methodology** for issuing Type Approvals, ensuring that a TAC issued by one IACS member is recognized by all other members, thereby enabling global equipment trade. **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Importance of Type Approval in Maritime Safety:**

1. **Life-Saving Appliances (LSA):** Under SOLAS Chapter III, every ship must carry lifejackets, lifebuoys, immersion suits, EPIRBs, SARTs, and lifeboats conforming to Type Approval standards (e.g., IMO Res. MSC.48(66)). Without TAC, the lifejacket may fail during the 4.5 m jump test, leading to mass casualties during abandon-ship scenarios. **(2 marks)**
2. **Fire-Fighting Equipment (FFE):** Under SOLAS Chapter II-2 and the FSS Code, fixed CO₂ systems, portable extinguishers, foam proportioners, and fire hoses must be Type Approved. A non-approved extinguisher may rupture under hydrostatic pressure, endangering the crew. **(2 marks)**
3. **Liability and Detention Risk:** Port State Control (Paris MOU, Tokyo MOU) inspectors routinely detain vessels with non-Type-Approved equipment, causing commercial losses. **(1 mark)**
4. **Insurance and Classification:** Without TAC, underwriters and classification societies refuse to issue Safety Construction and Safety Equipment Certificates. **(1 mark)**
5. **Harmonization:** A Greek owner building in China for a Liberian-flag vessel receives identical safety assurance globally because Type Approval is a universal benchmark. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Alternative]**

> **(a)** List and explain the **different categories of marine equipment** that require Type Approval. Cite at least one applicable IMO standard for each category. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the **Annual Surveillance Audit** conducted under Type Approval. What happens if a manufacturer fails the surveillance audit? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

Categories of equipment requiring Type Approval:

1. **Life-Saving Appliances** — Lifeboats, lifejackets, immersion suits, EPIRBs. *Standard: IMO Res. MSC.48(66).* **(1 mark)**
2. **Fire-Fighting Equipment** — Portable extinguishers, fixed CO₂ systems, foam systems, fire hoses. *Standard: FSS Code Chapter 4 and 5.* **(1 mark)**
3. **Navigation Equipment** — Gyro compass, ECDIS, AIS, VDR/S-VDR, LRIT. *Standard: IMO Res. MSC.232(82) for ECDIS; IMO Res. MSC.74(69) for AIS.* **(1 mark)**
4. **Radio Communication Equipment** — GMDSS consoles, VHF/DSC, INMARSAT. *Standard: SOLAS Chapter IV; IMO Res. A.694(17).* **(1 mark)**
5. **Pollution Prevention Equipment** — Oily Water Separators (OWS), Sewage Treatment Plants, Incinerators. *Standard: MARPOL Annex I Reg. 14; IMO MEPC.107(49) for OWS; MARPOL Annex IV for STP.* **(1 mark)**
6. **Electrical Equipment** — Ex-proof fittings, emergency generators, switchboards. *Standard: IEC 60079 series for Ex-proof; IACS UR E22 for software.* **(1 mark)**
7. **Structural Materials** — Hull steel grades, aluminum alloys, welding consumables. *Standard: IACS UR W11, W13, Z23.* **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Annual Surveillance Audit (ASA):**

The ASA is a **mandatory yearly audit** conducted by the classification society that issued the TAC to verify that the manufacturer continues to produce the approved equipment to the originally certified design and quality system. **(2 marks)**

**Scope of the ASA:** **(3 marks)**
- Verification of **quality management system** (ISO 9001 / IACS UR Z17 compliance).
- **Material traceability** — ensuring incoming raw materials match the approved BOM.
- **Calibration** of testing instruments.
- **Sampling of production units** for re-testing against the standard.
- Review of **customer complaints** and **field failures** (if any).
- Inspection of **manufacturing process changes** (any change requires society approval).

**Consequences of Failure:** **(2 marks)**
- **Major Non-Conformities (NCs):** Suspension of the TAC. Production must stop until NCs are closed. Suspension exceeding 6 months leads to **withdrawal** of the certificate.
- **Critical NCs (safety risk):** Immediate **withdrawal** of the TAC. Re-application required with full re-testing.
- The society issues an **audit report** with NCs categorized as Minor, Major, or Critical, with closure deadlines.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating TAC with MED wheelmark** — Many students write that they are the same. They are **parallel but distinct** schemes; MED is EU-specific, TAC is IACS-global.
> 2. **Forgetting the 5-year validity** — Examiners specifically test this numerical fact. Writing "TAC is valid forever" or "1 year" loses 1 full mark.
> 3. **Ignoring Annual Surveillance** — Type Approval is **not** a one-time event. The surveillance audit is what makes TAC *continuous*; omitting it in your answer caps your mark at 60%.
> 4. **Writing vague "IMO approves" statements** — Type Approval is issued by **Classification Societies**, not by IMO. IMO sets the standards; the societies certify. Confusing the two is the single most frequent error.
> 5. **Skipping the 4.5 m lifejacket jump test** — This specific number is a high-yield fact KTU examiners reward.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Type Approval**

- ✅ **Definition:** Independent third-party conformity assessment of a design + manufacturing process against IMO/IEC/ISO standards.
- ✅ **Validity:** **5 years** for the TAC; **12 months** surveillance interval.
- ✅ **Issuing Authority:** **Classification Society** (not IMO directly).
- ✅ **Top IACS Societies:** ABS, DNV, LR, BV, RINA, KR, ClassNK, CCS.
- ✅ **Governing Document:** **IACS UR Z17** (procedural); IMO Resolutions (technical).
- ✅ **Mandatory Categories:** LSA, FFE, Nav equipment, Radio equipment, Pollution equipment, Ex-proof electrical.
- ✅ **Process:** Application → Doc Review → Type Test → Factory Audit → TAC Issue → Annual Surveillance → 5-Year Renewal.
- ✅ **Lifejacket Test:** **4.5 m jump test** + 1-minute donning time.
- ✅ **EU Parallel Scheme:** **MED Wheelmark** under EU Directive 2014/90/EU.
- ✅ **Software:** **IACS UR E22** applies for software-based equipment (post 1 July 2017).
- ✅ **Detention Risk:** Non-Type-Approved equipment → **Port State Control detention** under Paris/Tokyo MOU.
- ✅ **Hierarchy:** IMO Convention → IMO Resolution → IACS UR → Society Rule → TAC Issued.
- ✅ **Audit NC Categories:** Minor, Major, Critical — with escalating consequences (warning, suspension, withdrawal).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Fire Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Types of Fire Tests ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition In the context of maritime safety engineering, **Fire Tests** are a series of standardized, reproducible, and scientifically rigorous procedures used to quantify how materials, products, and structural assemblies respond when exposed to controlled fire conditions. These tests are mandatory under the **International Code for Application of Fire Test Procedures (FTP Code)** adopted by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** as an integral part of **SOLAS Chapter II-2** (Construction – Fire Protection, Fire Detection, and Fire Extinction). The tests evaluate two fundamentally different properties: 1. **Reaction to Fire (RTF)** – How a material *contributes* to fire growth (ignitability, flame spread, heat release, smoke, toxic gases). 2. **Fire Resistance (FR)** – How a structural element *withstands* a fully developed fire (load-bearing capacity, integrity, insulation). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):** > The IMO FTP Code contains **10 distinct Parts (Part 1 to Part 10)**, each prescribing a specific fire test method for different shipboard applications such as non-combustibility, surface flammability, fire doors, windows, bulkheads, decks, cable penetrations, and furniture. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are selecting a material to build the wall of a ship's cabin. Two critical questions arise: 1. *"Will this wall itself catch fire and spread flames quickly across the ship?"* — This is **Reaction to Fire**. Think of it as asking, *"How flammable is this material?"* Just like checking if a piece of paper (highly flammable) versus a ceramic tile (non-flammable) reacts differently to a matchstick. 2. *"If a fierce fire rages on the other side of this wall for 60 minutes, will the wall hold back the flames and heat long enough for the crew to escape?"* — This is **Fire Resistance**. Think of it as asking, *"How strong is this wall as a shield against a raging furnace?"* Like testing how long a blast furnace door can hold back molten steel. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** Reaction to Fire tests are usually done on **small material samples** (grams to kilograms) in laboratories. Fire Resistance tests are done on **full-scale structural assemblies** (full walls, doors, decks) in large furnaces. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Standard Reference | Typical Value/Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Standard Fire Temperature | $T_{st}$ | ISO 834 | Reaches **945 °C** in 60 min | | Hydrocarbon Fire Temperature | $T_{hc}$ | IMO / API | Reaches **1100 °C** peak | | Limiting Oxygen Index | LOI | ISO 4589 | Air = **20.9%** | | Critical Heat Flux | CHF | IMO FTP Part 5 | **≥ 1.0 Btu/ft²·s** for Class 1 | | Smoke Density (Ds,max) | $D_s$ | IMO FTP Part 2 | Must be **≤ 200** in 4 min | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Fire Tests (Deep Dive) Maritime fire tests are broadly classified into **two major families** and further subdivided based on **purpose and end-use application**. #### A. Reaction to Fire (RTF) Tests These evaluate **material-level behavior** at the early stages of a fire. **1. Non-Combustibility Test (IMO FTP Part 1 / ISO 1182)** - **Purpose:** Determines whether a material is *truly* non-combustible (will not burn in air). - **Principle:** A cylindrical specimen is placed in a furnace preheated to **750 °C**. Temperature rise of the specimen and furnace, plus mass loss, are recorded. - **Pass Criteria:** Furnace temp rise $\Delta T_f \leq 30°C$, Specimen temp rise $\Delta T_s \leq 50°C$, Mass loss $\leq 50\%$. > [!NOTE] > **Socratic "Why" Question:** *Why 750 °C and not 1000 °C?* Because 750 °C represents the **minimum temperature at which sustained flaming combustion can occur on most organic materials** in air. Above this, almost everything organic will burn. **2. Smoke Density Test (IMO FTP Part 2 / ISO 5659-2)** - **Purpose:** Measures the amount of smoke produced under specific combustion conditions. - **Principle:** A specimen is exposed to a radiant heat flux of **25 kW/m²** or **50 kW/m²** in a closed chamber. The attenuation of light through the chamber is recorded as $D_s$. - **Pass Criteria:** $D_{s,max} \leq 200$ within 4 minutes (for shipboard materials). **3. Surface Flammability Test (IMO FTP Part 5 / ASTM E84 equivalent)** - **Purpose:** Determines flame spread characteristics of bulkhead, deck, and ceiling coverings. - **Principle:** A specimen (155 cm × 80 cm) is exposed to a radiant panel at **5.27 kW/m²** with a pilot flame. - **Pass Criteria:** Critical Heat Flux at extinguishment (CFE) $\geq 1.0$ Btu/ft²·s for Class 1 (best). **4. Toxicity Test (IMO FTP Part 2, Annex 1)** - **Purpose:** Identifies toxic gases (CO, HCN, HCl, NOx) released during combustion. - **Pass Criteria:** Specific gas concentrations must not exceed IMO prescribed limits. **5. Vertical Flammability Test (IMO FTP Part 7 / IMO Resolution A.471(XII))** - **Purpose:** Tests small-scale flammability of textiles, films, and coated fabrics used in draperies, upholstery. - **Principle:** A vertical strip specimen is exposed to a **2-second flame**, and char length, afterflame, and afterglow are recorded. **6. Fire-Resisting Divisions (FTP Part 3 / Part 4 / Part 11)** - Test methods for "A", "B", and "F" class divisions (bulkheads and decks). - Tests load-bearing capacity, integrity, and insulation under standard fire exposure. #### B. Fire Resistance (FR) Tests These evaluate **structural-element behavior** under fully developed fires. **1. Standard Fire Test – ISO 834 Curve** - **Equation:** $T = 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1) + T_0$ - Where $T$ = furnace temp (°C), $t$ = time (min), $T_0$ = ambient (~20 °C). - Models a **fully developed cellulosic fire** (wood, paper, typical ship cabin fire). **2. Hydrocarbon Fire Curve (HC Curve)** - **Equation:** $T = 1080\left(1 - 0.325 e^{-0.167t} - 0.675 e^{-2.5t}\right) + T_0$ - Models a **fast-growing hydrocarbon pool fire** (oil tanker deck fires). - Peak temperature ≈ **1100 °C** in ~5 minutes (much faster than ISO 834). **3. Jet Fire Test (HFS-Jet Fire)** - Models an **impinging jet flame** from a pressurized gas release. - Extremely severe localized thermal and mechanical loading. - Specified in **ISO 22899-1** and adopted for FPSO/FLNG turret and module protection. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Test Name | Standard | Key Formula / Parameter | Unit | Pass Criterion | |---|---|---|---|---| | Non-Combustibility | ISO 1182 / FTP Pt 1 | $\Delta T_f \leq 30°C$ | °C | Mass loss $\leq 50\%$ | | Smoke Density | ISO 5659-2 / FTP Pt 2 | $D_{s,max}$ | Dimensionless | $\leq 200$ in 4 min | | Surface Flammability | FTP Pt 5 | CFE | Btu/ft²·s | $\geq 1.0$ (Class 1) | | Vertical Flammability | FTP Pt 7 | Char length | mm | $\leq 150$ mm | | Standard Fire | ISO 834 | $T = 345 \log(8t+1) + 20$ | °C | Test duration (min) | | Hydrocarbon Fire | FTP Pt 3 | $T = 1080(1-0.325e^{-0.167t}-0.675e^{-2.5t})+20$ | °C | Test duration (min) | | Limiting Oxygen Index | ISO 4589 | $LOI = \frac{[O_2]}{[O_2] + [N_2]} \times 100$ | % | $\geq 28\%$ for self-extinguishing | | Heat Release Rate | ISO 5660 / Cone Calorimeter | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c$ | kW/m² | Per application | | Jet Fire Heat Flux | ISO 22899 | $\dot{q}'' = \sigma (T_f^4 - T_s^4) + h_c(T_f - T_s)$ | kW/m² | $\geq 250$ kW/m² | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Ship Design (SOLAS Compliance):** Every material installed on a SOLAS-regulated ship must have an **MED (Marine Equipment Directive) certification** demonstrating successful completion of the relevant FTP test. - **Offshore Platforms (FPSO/FLNG):** Jet fire and hydrocarbon fire curves protect critical process equipment and lifeboat modules. - **Insulation Selection:** Ensures non-combustibility of thermal/acoustic insulation behind cabin linings. - **Cable Selection:** Coated cables must pass **flame spread and toxicity** tests to avoid toxic fume propagation during a fire. - **Risk-Based Design:** Engineers use test data as input for **fire safety engineering calculations** like escape time (ASET vs RSET). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Standard Fire Temperature Curve (ISO 834) The ISO 834 standard fire curve is an empirical model based on observed cellulosic fires. Let us derive the temperature as a function of time. **Given empirical relationship** (from experimental fire data): $$\theta - \theta_0 = 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$$ Where: - $\theta$ = furnace gas temperature at time $t$ (in °C) - $\theta_0$ = ambient furnace temperature at $t = 0$ (typically **20 °C**) - $t$ = time elapsed from ignition (in minutes) **Step 1 — Substitute ambient temperature:** $$\theta - 20 = 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$$ **Step 2 — Solve explicitly for $\theta$:** $$\theta = 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1) + 20$$ **Step 3 — Verify boundary condition at $t = 0$:** $$\begin{aligned} \theta(0) &= 345 \log_{10}(8(0) + 1) + 20 \\ &= 345 \log_{10}(1) + 20 \\ &= 345(0) + 20 \\ &= 20°C \end{aligned}$$ This correctly matches the ambient starting temperature. **Step 4 — Verify characteristic temperature milestones:** For $t = 30$ min: $$\begin{aligned} \theta(30) &= 345 \log_{10}(8 \times 30 + 1) + 20 \\ &= 345 \log_{10}(241) + 20 \\ &= 345 \times 2.3820 + 20 \\ &\approx 821.79 + 20 \\ &\approx 842°C \end{aligned}$$ For $t = 60$ min: $$\begin{aligned} \theta(60) &= 345 \log_{10}(8 \times 60 + 1) + 20 \\ &= 345 \log_{10}(481) + 20 \\ &= 345 \times 2.6821 + 20 \\ &\approx 925.32 + 20 \\ &\approx 945°C \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Interpret the heating rate:** $$\frac{d\theta}{dt} = \frac{345 \times 8}{(8t + 1) \ln(10)}$$ At $t = 0$: rate is **1319 °C/min** (extremely rapid initial rise). At $t = 60$: rate is **~12 °C/min** (gradually slows as time progresses). ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Hydrocarbon Fire Temperature Curve The HC curve rises much faster and reaches a higher peak than ISO 834. **Given empirical equation:** $$T - T_0 = 1080 \left(1 - 0.325 e^{-0.167 t} - 0.675 e^{-2.5 t}\right)$$ **Step 1 — Evaluate at $t = 0$:** $$T - T_0 = 1080(1 - 0.325 - 0.675) = 1080 \times 0 = 0$$ So $T(0) = T_0 = 20°C$ ✓ **Step 2 — Evaluate at peak time. Take derivative and set to 0:** $$\frac{dT}{dt} = 1080 \left(0.167 \times 0.325 e^{-0.167 t} + 2.5 \times 0.675 e^{-2.5 t}\right) = 0$$ This gives an asymptotic peak as $t \to \infty$: $$\lim_{t \to \infty} T = T_0 + 1080 = 1100°C$$ **Step 3 — Time to reach 90% of peak (T ≈ 992 °C):** Try $t = 5$ min: $$\begin{aligned} T(5) &= 1080(1 - 0.325 e^{-0.835} - 0.675 e^{-12.5}) + 20 \\ &\approx 1080(1 - 0.325 \times 0.434 - 0.675 \times 3.73 \times 10^{-6}) + 20 \\ &\approx 1080(1 - 0.1411 - 0.0000025) + 20 \\ &\approx 1080 \times 0.8589 + 20 \\ &\approx 927.6 + 20 \\ &\approx 947.6°C \end{aligned}$$ This shows the HC curve reaches **~948 °C in only 5 minutes** vs ISO 834's **~842 °C in 30 minutes** — a far more aggressive fire exposure. ### 3.3 Limiting Oxygen Index (LOI) Derivation The LOI is defined as the **minimum oxygen concentration** (in a mixture of O₂ and N₂) that will just sustain combustion of a vertical specimen. $$LOI = \frac{[O_2]}{[O_2] + [N_2]} \times 100\%$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** - LOI < 21% → Burns easily in air (e.g., cotton, paper). - LOI = 21–28% → Self-extinguishing under ambient conditions. - LOI > 28% → "Self-extinguishing" — preferred for marine applications. **Worked Example:** A ship's interior wall panel undergoes LOI testing. The oxygen concentration is gradually reduced until the candle-like flame self-extinguishes. The recorded partial pressures are $P_{O_2} = 80$ mmHg and $P_{N_2} = 320$ mmHg. $$\begin{aligned} LOI &= \frac{P_{O_2}}{P_{O_2} + P_{N_2}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{80}{80 + 320} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{80}{400} \times 100 \\ &= 20\% \end{aligned}$$ Since LOI (20%) < Air (20.9%), the material **will burn in normal air** — not acceptable for non-combustible SOLAS applications. ### 3.4 Heat Release Rate (Cone Calorimeter) Derivation $$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \chi$$ Where: - $\dot{Q}$ = heat release rate (kW) - $\dot{m}$ = mass loss rate (kg/s) - $\Delta H_c$ = heat of combustion (kJ/kg) - $\chi$ = combustion efficiency (typically 0.6–0.9) **Worked Numerical Example:** A cable insulation specimen is tested at $50$ kW/m² heat flux in a cone calorimeter. Given: - Exposed area $A = 0.01$ m² - Mass loss rate $\dot{m} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/s - $\Delta H_c = 25{,}000$ kJ/kg - $\chi = 0.7$ $$\begin{aligned} \dot{Q} &= \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \chi \\ &= (5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 25{,}000 \times 0.7 \\ &= 8.75 \text{ kW} \end{aligned}$$ $$\text{HRR per unit area} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{A} = \frac{8.75}{0.01} = 875 \text{ kW/m}^2$$ This high HRR classifies the material as **highly flammable** — not suitable for unshielded use on ships. ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: ISO 834 vs HC vs Jet Fire | Parameter | ISO 834 | Hydrocarbon (HC) | Jet Fire | |---|---|---|---| | Peak Temp (°C) | ~1100 | ~1100 | ~1300+ at impingement | | Time to 500°C | ~12 min | ~3 min | Seconds | | Time to 1000°C | ~110 min | ~15 min | ~30 s | | Fire Type | Cellulosic (wood, paper) | Pool fire (oil) | Impinging gas jet | | Application | Ship bulkheads, decks | Tanker decks, process modules | Turrets, gas risers | | Applicable Standard | ISO 834-1 | ISO 834-3 / IMO FTP Pt 3 | ISO 22899-1 | | Heat Flux on Surface | Moderate | High | Very High (>250 kW/m²) | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Fire Tests in Maritime Safety ```mermaid flowchart TD ROOT["IMO SOLAS Fire Test Framework"] ROOT --> A["Reaction to Fire Tests"] ROOT --> B["Fire Resistance Tests"] A --> A1["Part 1: Non-Combustibility ISO 1182"] A --> A2["Part 2: Smoke and Toxicity ISO 5659-2"] A --> A3["Part 5: Surface Flammability"] A --> A4["Part 7: Vertical Flammability Textiles"] A --> A5["Part 8: Upholstered Furniture"] A --> A6["Part 9: Bedding Components"] B --> B1["Part 3: Class A Bulkheads Decks"] B --> B2["Part 4: Class B Bulkheads Decks"] B --> B3["Part 6: Fire Doors Control Stations"] B --> B4["Part 11: Class F Fire Resisting Divisions"] B1 --> B1A["ISO 834 Cellulosic Curve"] B1 --> B1B["Hydrocarbon HC Curve"] B1 --> B1C["Jet Fire ISO 22899"] ``` ### 4.2 Fire Test Methodology Flow (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["Specimen Preparation
Size Conditioning"] --> S2["Furnace Preheating
750C or ISO 834"] S2 --> S3["Specimen Installation
Thermocouples Attached"] S3 --> S4["Fire Exposure
Controlled Burn Period"] S4 --> S5["Data Acquisition
Temp Time Smoke"] S5 --> S6["Post-Test Analysis
Integrity Insulation"] S6 --> DEC{"Compliance Check
vs IMO Criteria"} DEC -- Pass --> OUT1["MED Mark
Wheelmark Cert"] DEC -- Fail --> OUT2["Redesign
New Material"] ``` ### 4.3 Non-Combustibility Test Apparatus (Schematic Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD FURN["Electric Furnace
750C Hot Zone"] --> SPEC["Specimen Holder
Cylindrical 45mm dia"] SPEC --> TC1["Specimen Thermocouple
Center of Sample"] SPEC --> TC2["Furnace Wall Thermocouple
At Mid Height"] TC1 --> LOG["Data Logger
Temp vs Time"] TC2 --> LOG LOG --> MASS["Mass Loss Recording
Before and After"] LOG --> CRIT["Compare with Pass Criteria
DTf 30C DTs 50C Mass Loss 50 percent"] MASS --> CRIT ``` ### 4.4 Standard vs Hydrocarbon Fire Curve — Visual Block Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR X["Time Axis Minutes"] --> Y1["ISO 834 Cellulosic Curve
Reaches 945C at 60 min"] X --> Y2["Hydrocarbon HC Curve
Reaches 950C at 5 min"] X --> Y3["Jet Fire Curve
Peak above 1300C at surface"] Y1 --> NOTE1["Used for SOLAS passenger ships"] Y2 --> NOTE2["Used for oil tankers FPSO"] Y3 --> NOTE3["Used for gas carriers LNG modules"] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define the IMO FTP Code. List any four fire tests specified under it.** **Model Answer:** The **International Code for Application of Fire Test Procedures (FTP Code)** is a unified set of fire test specifications adopted by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** under **SOLAS Chapter II-2** to ensure uniform evaluation of fire safety materials and products fitted on board ships. **Four fire tests under FTP Code:** 1. **Part 1 — Non-Combustibility Test** (ISO 1182): Determines if a material is genuinely non-combustible at 750 °C. 2. **Part 2 — Smoke and Toxicity Test** (ISO 5659-2): Measures smoke density ($D_{s,max} \leq 200$) and toxic gas emissions. 3. **Part 5 — Surface Flammability Test**: Evaluates flame spread characteristics of bulkhead/deck coverings. 4. **Part 7 — Vertical Flammability Test**: Tests small-scale flammability of textiles, curtains, films. **[3 Marks: 1 for definition + 2 for listing four tests with brief descriptors]** --- #### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **What is the difference between Reaction to Fire and Fire Resistance tests? Give one example of each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Reaction to Fire (RTF) | Fire Resistance (FR) | |---|---|---| | **Evaluates** | Material's contribution to fire growth | Element's ability to withstand fire | | **Stage of fire** | Pre-flashover / early fire | Post-flashover / fully developed | | **Scale** | Small samples | Full-scale assemblies | | **Property tested** | Ignitability, flame spread, heat release, smoke | Integrity, insulation, load-bearing | | **Example** | Surface Flammability Test (FTP Part 5) | ISO 834 Furnace Test on bulkhead (FTP Part 3) | **[3 Marks: 2 for distinction + 1 for examples]** --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) #### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **Non-Combustibility Test apparatus** as per ISO 1182. Explain the procedure and state the **pass/fail criteria** for a material to be classified as non-combustible. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive and explain the **Standard Fire Temperature Curve (ISO 834)**. Compare it with the **Hydrocarbon Fire Curve** highlighting their peak temperatures, time to reach critical values, and typical maritime applications. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a) — Non-Combustibility Test:** The Non-Combustibility Test is performed in a **vertical electric tube furnace** with the following specifications: | Component | Specification | |---|---| | Furnace tube | Refractory material, **inner dia. 75 mm**, height 150 mm | | Heating element | Silicon carbide or kanthal | | Operating temperature | **750 ± 5 °C** | | Specimen holder | Cylindrical, **inner dia. 25 mm**, height 60 mm | | Specimen dimensions | Cylinder of **dia. 45 mm, height 50 mm**, mass ≈ **50 g** | | Thermocouples | Type K; one at specimen center, one at furnace mid-wall | **Procedure:** 1. Specimen is conditioned at **23 °C and 50% RH for 24 hours**. 2. Furnace is preheated to a stable **750 °C**. 3. Specimen is rapidly inserted into the furnace. 4. Temperatures of **furnace** and **specimen center** are recorded every **30 seconds** for **20 minutes**. 5. Specimen is removed, cooled, and **mass loss** is measured. **Pass Criteria for Non-Combustible Material:** $$\Delta T_f \leq 30°C \quad \text{(furnace temperature rise)}$$ $$\Delta T_s \leq 50°C \quad \text{(specimen temperature rise)}$$ $$\text{Mass Loss} \leq 50\% \quad \text{(of original specimen mass)}$$ If all three conditions are met AND the specimen does **not exhibit sustained flaming** for more than **10 seconds**, the material is classified as **NON-COMBUSTIBLE** under SOLAS regulations. **[7 Marks: 2 for apparatus sketch/specs, 3 for procedure, 2 for pass criteria]** --- **Model Solution for (b) — Standard Fire Curve vs Hydrocarbon Curve:** **Step 1 — Standard Fire Curve (ISO 834) Derivation:** The ISO 834 curve is based on the empirical relationship: $$T = 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1) + 20$$ **Derivation of Heating Rate:** $$\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{345 \times 8}{(8t+1) \ln(10)} = \frac{2760}{(8t+1) \times 2.3026}$$ $$\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{1198.6}{8t + 1} \text{ °C/min}$$ **At $t = 0$:** Rate = **1198.6 °C/min** (extremely fast initial rise). **At $t = 30$ min:** Rate = **1198.6 / 241 ≈ 4.97 °C/min** (slow decline). **Step 2 — Hydrocarbon Fire Curve:** $$T = 1080\left(1 - 0.325 e^{-0.167 t} - 0.675 e^{-2.5 t}\right) + 20$$ **Step 3 — Numerical Comparison Table:** | Time (min) | ISO 834 Temp (°C) | HC Curve Temp (°C) | |---|---|---| | 0 | 20 | 20 | | 5 | 576 | 948 | | 10 | 678 | 1012 | | 15 | 738 | 1052 | | 30 | 842 | 1083 | | 60 | 945 | 1095 | | 120 | 1049 | 1099 | **Step 4 — Key Observations:** - ISO 834 is a **logarithmic growth** fire — typical of **cellulosic fires** (wood, paper, textiles). - HC curve shows **exponential saturation** behavior reaching ~1100 °C in just **5 minutes**. - **Critical difference:** HC curve reaches 950 °C in 5 minutes — what ISO 834 takes **60+ minutes** to achieve. **Step 5 — Maritime Applications:** - **ISO 834 Curve** → Used for testing **bulkheads, decks, and fire doors** in **passenger ships, cargo ships, and accommodation areas** (low fire load, cellulosic materials). - **HC Curve** → Used for **tanker decks, process modules, helidecks, and bulkheads adjacent to oil cargo holds** where hydrocarbon pool fires are anticipated. **[7 Marks: 2 for derivation, 2 for numerical comparison, 2 for interpretation, 1 for applications]** --- #### **Question B — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — Alternative Module Choice] **(a)** Explain the **Smoke Density Test** as per IMO FTP Part 2 / ISO 5659-2. Describe the test procedure, the measurement principle, and the pass criterion. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** What is the **Limiting Oxygen Index (LOI)**? Explain its significance in maritime material selection and derive the LOI for a specimen that continues to burn at oxygen partial pressure 90 mmHg and nitrogen partial pressure 360 mmHg. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a) — Smoke Density Test:** **Measurement Principle:** The smoke density test uses **light obscuration** in a closed chamber. A horizontal photometer system measures the transmittance ($T$) of a vertical light beam through the smoke accumulated in the chamber. The specific optical density is calculated as: $$D_s = \frac{V}{A \cdot L} \log_{10}\left(\frac{100}{T\%}\right)$$ Where: - $D_s$ = specific optical density (dimensionless) - $V$ = chamber volume (≈ 0.5 m³) - $A$ = exposed specimen area (≈ 0.0064 m²) - $L$ = light path length (≈ 0.5 m) - $T\%$ = percentage light transmittance **Test Procedure:** 1. Specimen (75 mm × 75 mm, **~1 mm thick**) is wrapped in aluminum foil and placed on a **conical electric heater**. 2. A **radiant heat flux of 25 kW/m² or 50 kW/m²** is applied (with or without pilot flame — 6 combinations per ISO 5659-2). 3. The chamber is **sealed** and smoke accumulates. 4. $D_s$ is recorded every **1 second** for the test duration. **Pass Criterion:** - $D_{s,max}$ (maximum specific optical density in 4 minutes) $\leq 200$ - For materials used in **escape routes**, stricter criteria apply. **Significance:** Toxic smoke (especially CO and HCN) is the **leading cause of fatalities in ship fires**, even before thermal injury. This test ensures selected materials do not generate obscuring or toxic smoke in a developing fire. **[7 Marks: 2 for principle, 2 for procedure, 2 for formula and pass criterion, 1 for significance]** --- **Model Solution for (b) — Limiting Oxygen Index:** **Definition:** The **Limiting Oxygen Index (LOI)** is the minimum concentration of oxygen (in a controlled mixture of O₂ and N₂) that will just support combustion of a vertical specimen, expressed as a percentage. $$LOI = \frac{[O_2]}{[O_2] + [N_2]} \times 100\%$$ **Significance in Maritime Material Selection:** - Air contains ~**20.9%** oxygen. Materials with LOI < 21% burn easily in air (e.g., cotton, paper, polyethylene). - Materials with LOI > 28% are considered **self-extinguishing** and are preferred for shipboard use (especially in accommodation, public spaces, escape routes). - IMO FTP Code and SOLAS prefer **non-combustible** (LOI effectively = 100%) materials for high-risk zones. **Numerical Derivation:** Given: - $P_{O_2} = 90$ mmHg - $P_{N_2} = 360$ mmHg $$\begin{aligned} LOI &= \frac{P_{O_2}}{P_{O_2} + P_{N_2}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{90}{90 + 360} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{90}{450} \times 100 \\ &= 20\% \end{aligned}$$ **Interpretation:** Since LOI (20%) < Air O₂ (20.9%), the specimen **will burn in normal air**. It is **not self-extinguishing** and is unsuitable for SOLAS-regulated accommodation areas unless protected by additional fire-resistant treatment. **[7 Marks: 2 for definition, 2 for maritime significance, 3 for numerical derivation with interpretation]** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Confusing Reaction to Fire vs Fire Resistance:** Many students write "fire resistance" when referring to "reaction to fire." This is a fatal error in Part A — examiners specifically test this distinction. > 2. **Skipping units in numerical problems:** Always include units (°C, mmHg, %, kW/m²) in derivations. KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** per question for missing units. > 3. **Forgetting to verify boundary conditions:** When deriving fire curves, always show that $T(t=0) = T_0$. This is a common 1-mark check. > 4. **Not mentioning the FTP Code part number:** When asked about a specific test, students forget to state which **FTP Code Part** (e.g., Part 1, Part 5, Part 7) governs that test. This loses **1–2 marks**. > 5. **Misapplying fire curves:** ISO 834 is for **passenger ship bulkheads**, HC curve is for **tanker decks**, and Jet Fire is for **gas carrier risers**. Mixing them up loses application marks. --- ### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** ✅ - 🔥 The **IMO FTP Code** is the master document specifying all maritime fire tests, integrated into **SOLAS Chapter II-2**. - 🔥 **Two test families:** **Reaction to Fire (RTF)** — material level; **Fire Resistance (FR)** — structural assembly level. - 🔥 **ISO 834 Standard Fire Curve:** $T = 345 \log(8t+1) + 20$ — cellulosic fires, **~945 °C at 60 min**. - 🔥 **Hydrocarbon Fire Curve:** Peaks at **1100 °C** within **5–15 min** — used for oil tankers. - 🔥 **Jet Fire Test:** Impinging flame > **1300 °C surface temperature**, > **250 kW/m²** heat flux — for LNG carriers. - 🔥 **FTP Part 1 (Non-combustibility):** 750 °C furnace, $\Delta T_f \leq 30°C$, $\Delta T_s \leq 50°C$, mass loss $\leq 50\%$. - 🔥 **FTP Part 2 (Smoke & Toxicity):** $D_{s,max} \leq 200$ in 4 minutes; CO, HCN, HCl, NOx limits apply. - 🔥 **FTP Part 5 (Surface Flammability):** Class 1 = CFE $\geq$ 1.0 Btu/ft²·s — best flame-spread resistance. - 🔥 **FTP Part 7 (Vertical Flammability):** Char length $\leq$ 150 mm for draperies/textiles. - 🔥 **LOI > 28%** = self-extinguishing; **Air O₂ = 20.9%** — benchmark for comparison. - 🔥 **Heat Release Rate:** $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \chi$ — used in cone calorimeter tests. - 🔥 **MED Wheelmark certification** is mandatory for shipboard installation of fire-tested products. - 🔥 **Jet fire testing (ISO 22899-1)** is critical for offshore oil & gas (FPSO, FLNG) where pressurized hydrocarbon leaks are possible. - 🔥 **Standardization body:** IMO for maritime; ASTM and ISO support underlying test methods. ---

topics.title: Non-Combustibility Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Non-Combustibility Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NON-COMBUSTIBILITY TEST (ISO 1182 / FTP Code Annex 1, Part 1):**
> The Non-Combustibility Test is a **reaction-to-fire test** conducted by inserting a small cylindrical specimen into a tubular furnace pre-heated and stabilized at a uniform temperature of **750 °C ± 5 °C**, then observing the temperature rise of the furnace and the specimen, along with the **mass loss** of the specimen. A material is classified as **non-combustible** only when it satisfies *all three* prescribed criteria simultaneously over the test duration.

In the maritime context (regulated by **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 5** and the **International Code for the Application of Fire Test Procedures (FTP Code, 2010)**), every material used in the construction of bulkheads, ceilings, linings, floors, stairways, and furniture on passenger and cargo ships must be **tested and certified as non-combustible** *or* have a low flame-spread rating before installation. Hence, the Non-Combustibility Test is the **gateway test** — the first and most stringent filter — before any other fire-performance evaluation (smoke density, toxicity, surface flammability) is even considered.

| Parameter | Value (as per ISO 1182 / FTP Code) |
|---|---|
| **Furnace Set Temperature** | **750 °C** |
| **Specimen Shape** | Right Circular Cylinder |
| **Specimen Dimensions** | Ø 45 mm (height 50 mm) – mass approx. 80 g |
| **Test Duration** | 30 min (after stable insertion) |
| **Number of Specimens** | 5 |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

> [!NOTE]
> **🧠 Real-World Analogy — "The Pressure Cooker Test":**
> Imagine placing a small piece of cake batter into a pressure cooker (the furnace) already screaming hot at **750 °C** — almost the temperature of molten lava. Now imagine watching for **30 minutes** with a thermometer.
> - If the cake **catches fire, smokes, or chars**, it is releasing its own heat. The thermometer inside the cooker will spike. The cake has lost weight because it has decomposed into gases — this material is **combustible**.
> - If the cake just sits there, gets warm, and the thermometer doesn't budge — it is **non-combustible**. It merely *absorbed* heat; it did not *generate* heat.

The Non-Combustibility Test is essentially this "pressure cooker principle" applied to engineering materials. We are asking two questions:
1. **Does the material emit heat when heated?** → Measured as **ΔT** (temperature rise).
2. **Does the material disintegrate into gases (smoke)?** → Measured as **Δm** (mass loss).

## 1.3 Standard Governing Authorities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Standards You Must Memorize for KTU ESE:**
> - **ISO 1182:2020** – *Reaction-to-fire tests for products — Non-combustibility test*
> - **FTP Code (2010), Annex 1, Part 1** – Mandatory for marine vessels (SOLAS-binding)
> - **BS 476: Part 4:1970** – Historical British standard (non-combustibility)

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Underlying Physics & Chemistry

The test exploits a fundamental thermodynamic principle:

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Balance Principle (Furnace-Specimen System):**
> When a sample is introduced into a hot furnace, the *heat liberated by combustion* of the sample (if any) adds to the *electrical heat input* maintaining furnace temperature. If no combustion occurs, the furnace's electrical heating elements must supply *more* current to compensate for the sample's heat absorption, and the sample temperature equilibrates *below* furnace temperature.

The combustion reaction, if it occurs, is broadly:

$$\text{Organic Material} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 \uparrow + \text{H}_2\text{O} \uparrow + \text{Solid Residue (char/ash)}$$

**Three Simultaneous Measurements** (made every **30 seconds** throughout the 30-min test):

1. **Furnace Temperature (T_f)** — by thermocouple at furnace mid-height.
2. **Specimen Surface Temperature (T_s)** — by thermocouple touching specimen surface.
3. **Specimen Centre Temperature (T_c)** — by thermocouple embedded at specimen centroid.

## 2.2 Equipment Architecture

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Tubular Furnace** | Refractory-tube furnace (≈ 76 mm bore), wound with electrical heating coil; maintains 750 °C wall temperature. |
| **Specimen Holder** | Stainless-steel cradle suspended from a balance arm into the furnace tube. |
| **Thermocouples (×3)** | Type-K or Type-N; insulated; furnace, surface, and centre positions. |
| **Data Logger** | Records T_f, T_s, T_c at ≤ 30-second intervals. |
| **Insertion Mechanism** | Pneumatically/hydraulically inserts specimen in **≤ 5 s** to minimise disturbance. |

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Procedure (Theory Logic Flow)

- **Step 1 — Specimen Conditioning:**
  Dry specimens in a **ventilated oven at 60 °C for 20–24 h**, then cool to ambient in a desiccator. This removes residual moisture so any mass loss is *purely thermal decomposition*, not evaporation.

- **Step 2 — Furnace Stabilisation:**
  Heat the empty furnace until the wall thermocouple reads **750 °C ± 5 °C** and remains stable for **≥ 10 minutes**.

- **Step 3 — Baseline Recording:**
  Record T_f for **5 minutes** before insertion to establish baseline.

- **Step 4 — Rapid Insertion:**
  Insert the specimen (in its holder) into the furnace centre within **5 seconds**. Start the timer (`t = 0`).

- **Step 5 — Data Acquisition:**
  For the next **30 minutes**, record all three temperatures at 30-second intervals.

- **Step 6 — Termination & Mass Loss:**
  Remove specimen, cool in desiccator, and reweigh. Compute **Δm**.

- **Step 7 — Performance Evaluation:**
  Compute the three pass criteria (see Section 2.4).

## 2.4 Pass / Fail Criteria (THE GOLDEN RULE FOR KTU ESE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A material is declared **NON-COMBUSTIBLE** if **all three** criteria below are satisfied:

| # | Criterion | Threshold | Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Average Furnace Temperature Rise** | **≤ 50 °C** | $\Delta \overline{T}_f$ |
| 2 | **Average Specimen Surface Temperature Rise** | **≤ 50 °C** | $\Delta \overline{T}_s$ |
| 3 | **Average Mass Loss** | **≤ 50 %** of original mass | $\Delta \overline{m}$ |

If **any one** criterion is exceeded → material is classified as **"Limited Combustible" or "Combustible"** depending on severity.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (HIGH-YIELD CHEAT TABLE)

> [!NOTE]
> 📌 Memorise every row below. Numerical problems in Part B will test these.

| Formula # | Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| **(1)** | $\Delta T_f = T_{f,\max} - T_{f,\text{baseline}}$ | Instantaneous furnace temp rise |
| **(2)** | $\Delta \overline{T}_f = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta T_{f,i}$ | Average furnace temp rise (N readings) |
| **(3)** | $\Delta T_s = T_{s,\max} - T_{s,\text{baseline}}$ | Specimen surface temp rise |
| **(4)** | $\Delta \overline{T}_s = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta T_{s,i}$ | Average surface temp rise |
| **(5)** | $\Delta m = m_i - m_f$ | Absolute mass loss (in grams) |
| **(6)** | $\Delta m\,\% = \dfrac{m_i - m_f}{m_i} \times 100$ | **Percentage mass loss** — required for pass/fail |
| **(7)** | $t_{d,\text{sustained}} \le 4\ \text{s}$ | Sustained flaming ≥ 4 s = failure |
| **(8)** | $Q_{\text{net}} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat absorbed (J) |

> **Symbols Used:**
> - $m_i$ = initial specimen mass, $m_f$ = final specimen mass
> - $c_p$ = specific heat capacity (J·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹)
> - $N$ = total number of timed observations in 30 min
> - All temperatures in **°C**

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Three Pass Criteria (from First Principles)

### 3.1.1 Why 50 °C Threshold for ΔT?

When a small specimen (mass ≈ 80 g, $c_p \approx 1000$ J·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹) absorbs heat in a furnace of large thermal mass, the **furnace temperature depression** is negligible. However, if combustion occurs, the heat of combustion of typical organic ship materials is:

$$Q_{\text{comb}} \approx 20\,\text{MJ/kg}$$

If even **1 %** of specimen mass burns (0.8 g), energy released is:

$$Q_{\text{released}} = 0.0008 \times 20 \times 10^6 = 16{,}000\ \text{J}$$

This energy, when concentrated on the specimen surface, easily produces a **> 50 °C** rise above the baseline surface temperature. Hence the **50 °C threshold** is the empirical boundary chosen by **ISO TC 92 / SC 1** between self-sustaining combustion and inert decomposition.

### 3.1.2 Why 50 % Mass Loss Threshold?

A non-combustible material (e.g., mineral wool, calcium silicate, vermiculite board) under extreme heat will lose mass only via:
- **Dehydration** (chemically bound water driven off)
- **Carbonate decomposition** (CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂)

These typically account for **< 20 %** mass loss even at 1000 °C. A loss of **> 50 %** indicates major organic content that has been pyrolysed/gasified — i.e., the material is combustible.

### 3.1.3 Detailed Worked Example (Typical KTU Numerical)

> **PROBLEM:** A specimen of mass **82.4 g** is tested in the ISO 1182 furnace. The baseline furnace temperature is 750 °C. After insertion, the following ten sample readings are recorded at 5-minute intervals:

| t (min) | T_f (°C) | T_s (°C) |
|---:|---:|---:|
| 0 | 750 | 25 |
| 5 | 762 | 41 |
| 10 | 768 | 47 |
| 15 | 771 | 50 |
| 20 | 773 | 51 |
| 25 | 774 | 51 |
| 30 | 774 | 52 |

After the test, the specimen mass is **77.8 g**. Classify the material.

---

#### **SOLUTION (Step-by-Step, Exhaustive)**

**Step 1 — Compute Instantaneous ΔT_f and ΔT_s:**

$$\Delta T_{f,i} = T_{f,i} - T_{f,0} \quad ; \quad \Delta T_{s,i} = T_{s,i} - T_{s,0}$$

Using $T_{f,0} = 750\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and $T_{s,0} = 25\ ^\circ\text{C}$:

| t (min) | ΔT_f (°C) | ΔT_s (°C) |
|---:|---:|---:|
| 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 5 | 12 | 16 |
| 10 | 18 | 22 |
| 15 | 21 | 25 |
| 20 | 23 | 26 |
| 25 | 24 | 26 |
| 30 | 24 | 27 |

**Step 2 — Compute Averages (excluding t = 0):**

$$\Delta \overline{T}_f = \frac{0 + 12 + 18 + 21 + 23 + 24 + 24}{7} = \frac{122}{7}$$

$$\Delta \overline{T}_f \approx 17.43\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

$$\Delta \overline{T}_s = \frac{0 + 16 + 22 + 25 + 26 + 26 + 27}{7} = \frac{142}{7}$$

$$\Delta \overline{T}_s \approx 20.29\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 3 — Check Maximum Rise:**

$$\Delta T_{f,\max} = 24\ ^\circ\text{C} \quad (\le 50\ ^\circ\text{C}\ \checkmark)$$
$$\Delta T_{s,\max} = 27\ ^\circ\text{C} \quad (\le 50\ ^\circ\text{C}\ \checkmark)$$

**Step 4 — Compute Percentage Mass Loss:**

$$\Delta m\,\% = \frac{m_i - m_f}{m_i} \times 100 = \frac{82.4 - 77.8}{82.4} \times 100$$

$$\Delta m\,\% = \frac{4.6}{82.4} \times 100 \approx 5.58\ \%$$

$$\Delta m\,\% \approx 5.58\ \% \quad (\le 50\ \%\ \checkmark)$$

**Step 5 — Final Classification:**

All three criteria are satisfied → Material is **NON-COMBUSTIBLE** ✔️

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The 17 °C furnace rise indicates only mild decomposition (likely dehydration), and the 5.58 % mass loss confirms minor volatile release. Such a material is acceptable for **SOLAS Regulation 5.3** restricted applications in ships.

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Observed Result | Classification | SOLAS Implication |
|---|---|---|
| All three criteria met | **Non-combustible** | Permitted anywhere on the ship. |
| ΔT criteria met, mass loss 50–70 % | **Limited combustible** | May be used as per SOLAS Reg. 5.3 with restrictions. |
| ΔT criteria met, mass loss > 70 % | **Combustible** | Permitted only with additional smoke/toxicity certificates. |
| ΔT criteria exceeded | **Highly combustible** | Banned for structural marine use. |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Non-Combustibility Test Apparatus (Block Architecture)

The following Mermaid block represents the **functional flow architecture** of the apparatus, the sample positioning, and the data acquisition chain — replacing the need for a physical drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["AC Power Supply 220V 50Hz"] --> B["Furnace Heating Coil"]
    B --> C["Refractory Tubular Furnace 750C"]
    D["Specimen Cylinder 45mm x 50mm"] --> E["Specimen Holder Stainless Steel"]
    E --> C
    C --> F["Thermocouple T_f Furnace"]
    C --> G["Thermocouple T_s Surface"]
    D --> H["Thermocouple T_c Centre"]
    F --> I["Data Logger 30s interval"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["PC Acquisition and Storage"]
    K["Analytical Balance 0.01g"] --> L["Initial Mass m_i"]
    K --> M["Final Mass m_f after Test"]
    L --> N["Mass Loss Computation"]
    M --> N
    I --> O["Delta T_f Average Computation"]
    I --> P["Delta T_s Average Computation"]
    N --> Q["Pass or Fail Decision Module"]
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> R{"All 3 Criteria Satisfied"}
    R -->|YES| S["CLASSIFIED NON-COMBUSTIBLE"]
    R -->|NO| T["CLASSIFIED COMBUSTIBLE"]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree for Material Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Material Submitted for Non-Combustibility Test"] --> B["ISO 1182 Furnace Test at 750C"]
    B --> C["Measure Delta T_f"]
    B --> D["Measure Delta T_s"]
    B --> E["Measure Delta m percent"]
    C --> F{"Delta T_f less than or equal to 50C"}
    D --> G{"Delta T_s less than or equal to 50C"}
    E --> H{"Delta m less than or equal to 50 percent"}
    F -->|YES| I["Criterion 1 PASS"]
    F -->|NO| J["Criterion 1 FAIL"]
    G -->|YES| K["Criterion 2 PASS"]
    G -->|NO| L["Criterion 2 FAIL"]
    H -->|YES| M["Criterion 3 PASS"]
    H -->|NO| N["Criterion 3 FAIL"]
    I --> O{"All Three PASS"}
    K --> O
    M --> O
    J --> P["Combustible Classification"]
    L --> P
    N --> P
    O -->|YES| Q["NON-COMBUSTIBLE Approved for SOLAS Use"]
    O -->|NO| P
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**State the standard temperature used in the Non-Combustibility Test as per ISO 1182, and explain why this particular temperature is chosen.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The standard furnace temperature for the Non-Combustibility Test, as prescribed by **ISO 1182:2020** and the **FTP Code Annex 1 Part 1**, is **750 °C ± 5 °C**.

This temperature is selected because:

- **(1 mark)** It represents the worst-case temperature that a structural material may be exposed to during a fully developed **hydrocarbon fire** on a ship or in a building — typical flame temperatures in post-flashover fires range from **700 °C to 1100 °C**.
- **(1 mark)** It is hot enough to ignite most organic (cellulose-based) materials if they are combustible, hence it acts as an unambiguous discriminator.
- **(1 mark)** It is below the melting point of most inorganic (mineral) materials, allowing a clear distinction between inorganic stable materials and organic combustibles.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the three pass/fail criteria for classifying a material as non-combustible.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A material is classified as non-combustible if **all three** of the following criteria are satisfied simultaneously:

- **(1 mark)** Average furnace temperature rise $\Delta \overline{T}_f \le 50\ ^\circ\text{C}$.
- **(1 mark)** Average specimen surface temperature rise $\Delta \overline{T}_s \le 50\ ^\circ\text{C}$.
- **(1 mark)** Percentage mass loss $\Delta m\,\% \le 50\ \%$.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern (Module Internal Choice):** Two full questions of 14 marks each — typically each has sub-parts (a) of 7 marks and (b) of 7 marks. **Answer either OR** pattern.

---

### ⭐ **QUESTION A (14 MARKS)** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*

**Q. (a)** With the aid of a neat labelled diagram, describe the **apparatus and procedure** of the Non-Combustibility Test as per ISO 1182. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A specimen of mass **85.6 g** is subjected to the ISO 1182 non-combustibility test. The maximum furnace temperature reached is **778 °C** and the maximum surface temperature is **74 °C**. Baseline temperatures are 750 °C and 24 °C respectively. After the test, the specimen weighs **76.2 g**. Determine whether the material qualifies as non-combustible. **(7 marks)**

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Q(a):**

*(Examiner's Note: A well-labelled diagram of the tubular furnace is essential for full marks. Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1.)*

**Apparatus (3 marks):**
1. **Refractory tubular furnace** wound with electrical heating coils capable of maintaining the wall at 750 °C ± 5 °C. Inner bore ≈ 76 mm, height ≈ 200 mm.
2. **Specimen holder** — open stainless-steel cradle suspended vertically in the furnace centre.
3. **Three Type-K thermocouples** — furnace (T_f), specimen surface (T_s), specimen centre (T_c).
4. **Data logger** recording every 30 seconds for 30 minutes.
5. **Analytical balance** accurate to 0.01 g.
6. **Insertion mechanism** allowing rapid placement within 5 seconds.

**Procedure (4 marks):**
1. Condition specimen in oven at **60 °C for 24 h**, cool in desiccator. **(1 mark)**
2. Weigh specimen to nearest 0.01 g → record $m_i$. **(0.5 marks)**
3. Heat furnace empty until stable at **750 °C ± 5 °C** for 10 min. **(0.5 marks)**
4. Record baseline T_f for 5 min. **(0.5 marks)**
5. Rapidly insert specimen; start timer. **(0.5 marks)**
6. Record T_f, T_s, T_c every 30 s for 30 min. **(0.5 marks)**
7. Remove, cool in desiccator, reweigh → record $m_f$. **(0.5 marks)**

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Q(b):**

**Given:**
- $m_i = 85.6$ g
- $m_f = 76.2$ g
- $T_{f,\max} = 778\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $T_{f,\text{base}} = 750\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- $T_{s,\max} = 74\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $T_{s,\text{base}} = 24\ ^\circ\text{C}$

**Step 1 — Furnace Temperature Rise:**

$$\Delta T_f = 778 - 750 = 28\ ^\circ\text{C} \quad \le 50\ ^\circ\text{C}\ \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Surface Temperature Rise:**

$$\Delta T_s = 74 - 24 = 50\ ^\circ\text{C} \quad \le 50\ ^\circ\text{C}\ \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Mass Loss:**

$$\Delta m\,\% = \frac{85.6 - 76.2}{85.6} \times 100 = \frac{9.4}{85.6} \times 100$$

$$\Delta m\,\% \approx 10.98\ \% \quad \le 50\ \%\ \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**

All three criteria are satisfied → **Material is NON-COMBUSTIBLE** ✔️ *(7 marks)*

---

### ⭐ **QUESTION B (14 MARKS — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **principle, significance, and SOLAS implications** of the Non-Combustibility Test for marine vessel construction. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Two specimens are tested independently. Specimen A has an initial mass of 80 g and loses 35 g; its maximum furnace temperature rise is 42 °C and surface rise is 38 °C. Specimen B has an initial mass of 90 g and loses 50 g; its maximum furnace temperature rise is 55 °C and surface rise is 48 °C. Classify each specimen and justify. **(7 marks)**

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Q(a):**

**Principle (3 marks):**
The test principle is based on the **energy balance** in a closed furnace environment. When a specimen is placed in a 750 °C furnace:
- A **combustible** material releases heat through exothermic oxidation → furnace and/or surface temperatures rise significantly, and the material loses mass as volatiles.
- A **non-combustible** material only *absorbs* heat endothermically (e.g., dehydration, carbonate breakdown) → negligible temperature rise and minimal mass loss.
The test measures the **furnace temperature rise (ΔT_f), surface temperature rise (ΔT_s), and percentage mass loss (Δm %)** to discriminate the two cases.

**Significance (2 marks):**
1. Acts as the **primary regulatory gate** for material acceptance in marine and offshore structures.
2. Ensures that bulk structural and decorative materials do not contribute to fire propagation.
3. Forms part of the SOLAS-mandated FTP Code compliance chain, without which ships cannot be classified by classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, IRS).

**SOLAS Implications (2 marks):**
- SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 5 mandates that materials used in ships comply with the FTP Code.
- The Non-Combustibility Test is defined in **FTP Code Annex 1, Part 1**.
- Materials failing this test must be supported by additional fire-retardant certificates and can only be used in restricted zones (e.g., behind furniture, beneath decks with sprinklers).

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Q(b):**

**Specimen A:**

$$\Delta m\,\% = \frac{35}{80} \times 100 = 43.75\ \% \quad \le 50\ \%\ \checkmark$$
$$\Delta T_f = 42\ ^\circ\text{C} \quad \le 50\ ^\circ\text{C}\ \checkmark$$
$$\Delta T_s = 38\ ^\circ\text{C} \quad \le 50\ ^\circ\text{C}\ \checkmark$$

→ **Specimen A is NON-COMBUSTIBLE** ✔️ *(3 marks)*

**Specimen B:**

$$\Delta m\,\% = \frac{50}{90} \times 100 \approx 55.56\ \% \quad > 50\ \%\ \text{FAIL}\ ✗$$
$$\Delta T_f = 55\ ^\circ\text{C} \quad > 50\ ^\circ\text{C}\ \text{FAIL}\ ✗$$
$$\Delta T_s = 48\ ^\circ\text{C} \quad \le 50\ ^\circ\text{C}\ \checkmark$$

Two of three criteria fail → **Specimen B is COMBUSTIBLE** ✘ *(4 marks)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Easy Marks in KTU ESE:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract baseline temperature** — Students often quote the *absolute* max furnace temperature (e.g., 778 °C) instead of computing **ΔT = 778 − 750 = 28 °C**. Always subtract baseline.
> 2. **Mixing up mass loss thresholds** — Some students incorrectly use **absolute mass loss in grams** instead of **percentage mass loss**. The criterion is **Δm % ≤ 50 %**, NOT Δm ≤ 50 g.
> 3. **Single-criterion failure** — A common trap: students assume the material is non-combustible if *ΔT* is okay even when mass loss is huge. **All three** criteria must be simultaneously satisfied.
> 4. **Wrong standard reference** — Never quote *only* ISO 1182 for maritime questions. Always co-cite **FTP Code Annex 1, Part 1** because it is **SOLAS-binding**, whereas ISO 1182 is the technical basis.
> 5. **Misremembering specimen dimensions** — Standard specimen is a cylinder of dimensions **45 mm diameter × 50 mm height** (mass ~80 g), NOT 50 mm × 100 mm.
> 6. **Failing to mention the 4-second sustained flaming rule** — Even if ΔT and Δm are within limits, **sustained flaming for ≥ 4 seconds** automatically disqualifies the material.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> 📋 **Essential Take-aways Before You Step Into the KTU Exam Hall:**

- ⬜ The Non-Combustibility Test is governed by **ISO 1182:2020** and **FTP Code Annex 1, Part 1** (SOLAS-binding for ships).
- ⬜ Standard furnace temperature is **750 °C ± 5 °C**; specimen size **45 mm × 50 mm**; test duration **30 minutes**.
- ⬜ **Three simultaneous criteria** must be met: ΔT_f ≤ 50 °C, ΔT_s ≤ 50 °C, Δm % ≤ 50 %.
- ⬜ Mass loss is expressed as a **percentage** of the *initial* specimen mass, calculated as $\Delta m\,\% = \dfrac{m_i - m_f}{m_i} \times 100$.
- ⬜ Sustained flaming **≥ 4 seconds** → automatic failure.
- ⬜ Specimens must be **oven-dried at 60 °C for 24 h** before testing.
- ⬜ Three thermocouples are used: **furnace (T_f)**, **surface (T_s)**, and **centre (T_c)**.
- ⬜ Five specimens must be tested; results are averaged.
- ⬜ **Typical non-combustible marine materials**: calcium silicate boards, mineral wool, vermiculite boards, ceramic fibre blankets, glass-reinforced cement boards.
- ⬜ **Typical combustible marine materials** (that fail this test unless treated): plywood, polyurethane foam, polystyrene, fibre-reinforced plastics (FRP without fire retardant).
- ⬜ SOLAS Regulation II-2/5.3 mandates this test for materials used in passenger ship bulkheads, ceilings, and linings.
- ⬜ Energy basis: combustion of even 1 % of an 80 g specimen releases ≈ **16,000 J**, sufficient to push ΔT above the 50 °C threshold.
- ⬜ The test exploits an **energy balance**: combustible materials emit heat (exothermic), non-combustible materials absorb heat (endothermic).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smoke and Toxicity Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Smoke and Toxicity Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smoke and Toxicity Test** is a standardized fire-reaction test mandated by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** under the **Fire Test Procedures (FTP) Code** — specifically **IMO Resolution MSC.307(88), Annex 1, Part 2** — used to quantify the **smoke production** and the **toxic gas emission index** of materials used in ship construction, particularly surface materials, primary deck coverings, and structural bulkheads, when exposed to specified thermal decomposition conditions.

The test determines two critical safety parameters:

1. **Specific Optical Density of Smoke ($D_s$)** — measured in a smoke chamber under flaming and non-flaming (smoldering) modes.
2. **Toxicity Index ($T_I$)** — a weighted sum of the concentrations of key toxic gases generated during combustion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Standards in Maritime Context:**
> - **IMO FTP Code, Part 2** — Smoke generation test procedures
> - **ISO 5659-2:2017** — Plastics — Smoke generation — Part 2: Determination of optical density by a single-chamber test
> - **ISO/TR 9122-1** — Toxicity testing of fire effluents — General guidance
> - **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 5.3** — Material fire safety compliance

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trapped inside a burning ship's cabin at night. Two things will kill you before the flames reach you:

- **Smoke** — dense, opaque smoke blinds you, causes panic, and prevents crew from finding escape routes. It also contains carbon monoxide (the silent killer).
- **Toxic Gases** — even a small amount of hydrogen cyanide (HCN) or carbon monoxide (CO) inhaled for 30 seconds can cause unconsciousness.

The **Smoke and Toxicity Test** is essentially the maritime equivalent of a **"breathalyzer for materials"** — it tells naval architects and marine engineers *exactly how dangerous a wall panel, ceiling tile, or cable insulation will become when it burns*. Just as a food label tells you nutritional content, this test "labels" every material's fire toxicity profile before it is allowed on a ship.

## 1.3 Why This Test Matters for Maritime Vessels

Ships are unique environments because:
- Enclosed compartments limit ventilation during fire.
- Crew may be in sleeping quarters, unable to escape quickly.
- Smoke migrates rapidly through corridors, stairways, and ventilation ducts.
- Toxic gases (HCN, CO, HCl, HF, NO₂) can incapacitate rescuers and passengers within minutes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric Limit (per IMO FTP Code):** The maximum permissible **Specific Optical Density ($D_s$) at 4 minutes** is **$D_s(4) \leq 200$** for surface materials, and the maximum **Toxicity Index ($T_I$)** must be $\leq 1$ when calculated using IMO-referenced gas weighting factors.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Test Parameters

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|-----------|----------------|
| Specimen size | **75 mm × 75 mm × thickness (≤ 25 mm)** |
| Radiant heat flux (non-flaming) | **25 kW/m²** |
| Radiant heat flux (flaming) | **25 kW/m² + pilot flame** |
| Test duration | **10 minutes** |
| Smoke chamber volume | **0.5 m³ (approx.)** |
| Temperature of chamber wall | **~35 °C (controlled)** |
| Reference light path | **L = 0.5 m** |
| Photometric filter wavelength | **White light (full spectrum)** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: How the Test Works

The Smoke and Toxicity Test operates on the principle of **Lambert-Beer Law for optical attenuation** combined with **controlled pyrolytic decomposition** of the test specimen.

### Step-by-Step Logical Flow:

1. **Specimen Preparation** — A representative sample of the shipboard material (e.g., GRP bulkhead panel, PVC cable insulation, melamine-faced furniture board) is conditioned at $23 \pm 2$ °C and $50 \pm 5$ % RH for 24 hours.
2. **Radiant Heating** — The specimen is mounted vertically and exposed to a **cone heater** delivering **25 kW/m²** radiant flux. This simulates a fully developed fire's thermal load on a nearby surface.
3. **Smoldering Phase (Non-Flaming Mode)** — Test is run first without pilot flame to capture the slow smoldering combustion products.
4. **Flaming Phase (Flaming Mode)** — A small pilot flame ignites the pyrolyzed gases, simulating active flaming combustion.
5. **Optical Measurement** — A vertical light beam passes across the smoke-filled chamber. A photodetector measures the **transmittance ($T$)** of light.
6. **Gas Sampling** — Combustion gases are continuously drawn through sampling probes into **FTIR (Fourier Transform Infrared) analyzers** and **electrochemical cells** for CO, CO₂, HCN, HCl, HF, NOₓ, SO₂ detection.
7. **Data Acquisition** — Both optical density and gas concentrations are logged at 1-second intervals over a 10-minute period.

## 2.2 Core Formulas — KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly testable** in KTU exams. Memorize the variable definitions and units.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $D_s = \dfrac{V}{A \cdot L} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | $D_s$ = specific optical density, $V$ = chamber volume (m³), $A$ = exposed specimen area (m²), $L$ = light path length (m), $I_0$ = incident light intensity, $I$ = transmitted light intensity | Quantifies how much smoke obscures visibility — critical for evacuation route design |
| 2 | $D_s = \dfrac{V}{A \cdot L} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\right)$ | $T$ = transmittance $= I/I_0$ | Direct form using transmittance meter readings |
| 3 | $D_s(t) = 132 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{100}{T(t)}\right)$ | Simplified ISO 5659-2 expression (when $V/(A\cdot L) = 132$ for standard chamber) | Used for rapid field calculation |
| 4 | $T_I = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{C_{f,i}} \cdot W_i$ | $C_i$ = measured concentration of gas *i* (ppm or mg/L), $C_{f,i}$ = fatal concentration (reference value), $W_i$ = weighting factor (≤ 1) | Aggregates multiple toxic gases into a single hazard index |
| 5 | $D_{m} = \dfrac{V}{A \cdot L} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{\min}}\right)$ | $D_{m}$ = maximum specific optical density, $T_{\min}$ = minimum transmittance reached | Peak smoke obscuration indicator |
| 6 | $VOF = \dfrac{D_s(4)}{200} \cdot 100$ | $VOF$ = Visual Obscuration Factor (%), $D_s(4)$ = specific optical density at 4 minutes | Quick compliance check vs. IMO limit |
| 7 | $CIT = \sum_{i} \dfrac{C_i}{LC_{50,i}}$ | $CIT$ = Critical Toxicity Index (used in ISO 13344 approach), $LC_{50,i}$ = lethal concentration 50% for gas *i* | Alternative toxic potency summation method |

## 2.3 Toxicity Index Gas Set (Per IMO FTP Code Part 2)

The toxicity index is computed using the following **eight combustion gases**:

| Gas | Chemical Formula | Reference Fatal Conc. $C_{f,i}$ (ppm) | Primary Source in Shipboard Materials |
|-----|------------------|------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| Carbon Monoxide | CO | **1390** | All organic materials |
| Carbon Dioxide | CO₂ | **90000** | Combustion of all organics |
| Hydrogen Cyanide | HCN | **50** | Wool, silk, nylon, polyurethane, ABS |
| Hydrogen Chloride | HCl | **1000** | PVC cable sheathing, vinyl flooring |
| Hydrogen Fluoride | HF | **200** | PTFE (Teflon), fluoroelastomers |
| Nitrogen Dioxide | NO₂ | **90** | Cellulosic materials under flaming |
| Sulfur Dioxide | SO₂ | **400** | Rubber, vulcanized materials |
| Hydrogen Bromide | HBr | **300** | Flame-retardant additives |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ship Interior Design:** Naval architects specify materials with low $D_s$ and $T_I$ for cabins, corridors, and public spaces.
- **Life-Saving Appliance Certification:** Smoke and toxicity data informs SOLAS Chapter III approval of life rafts, immersion suits, and pyrotechnics.
- **Insurance & Class Society Compliance:** Classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, BV) require FTP Code compliance for vessel certification.
- **Submarine Atmospheres:** Toxic gas profiles feed into the **atmosphere control and revitalization** systems for conventional and nuclear submarines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $T_I \leq 1$ ?** A toxicity index $\leq 1$ implies that the combined toxic gas emissions are below the threshold that would impair escape capability within 30 minutes — the IMO-prescribed evacuation window for passenger ships.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Specific Optical Density Formula

### Starting Physical Principle

The attenuation of a collimated light beam passing through a smoke-filled medium is governed by the **Lambert-Beer Law**:

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\sigma \cdot c \cdot L}$$

where:
- $I$ = transmitted light intensity (W/m²)
- $I_0$ = incident light intensity (W/m²)
- $\sigma$ = specific extinction coefficient (m²/kg)
- $c$ = mass concentration of smoke particulates (kg/m³)
- $L$ = optical path length (m)

### Step 1: Convert to Common (Base-10) Logarithm

We take the base-10 logarithm to align with photometric instrumentation:

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right) = \dfrac{\sigma \cdot c \cdot L}{2.303}$$

### Step 2: Express Mass Concentration in Terms of Chamber Geometry

The smoke is produced by a specimen of exposed area $A$ and fills the chamber volume $V$. The optical density per unit mass is:

$$\sigma \cdot c = \dfrac{\sigma \cdot m_{\text{smoke}}}{V}$$

where $m_{\text{smoke}}$ is the mass of smoke in the chamber.

### Step 3: Define Specific Optical Density

By ISO 5659-2 convention, $D_s$ is the optical density normalized by the specimen area and the light path:

$$D_s = \dfrac{V}{A \cdot L} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

This is the **master equation** used in all maritime smoke tests.

### Step 4: Substitute Transmittance Form

Recognizing that transmittance $T = I/I_0$:

$$D_s = \dfrac{V}{A \cdot L} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\right)$$

### Step 5: Apply Standard Chamber Geometry

For the ISO 5659-2 / IMO FTP Code Part 2 chamber:

$$\dfrac{V}{A \cdot L} = \dfrac{0.5 \;\text{m}^3}{(0.075 \;\text{m})^2 \cdot 0.5 \;\text{m}} = \dfrac{0.5}{0.0028125} = 132$$

Thus, the working formula simplifies to:

$$\boxed{D_s(t) = 132 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{100}{T(t)\,[\%]}\right)}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Sample KTU Problem)

> **Problem:** During a smoke density test in the IMO FTP Code Part 2 chamber, the minimum transmittance recorded for a ship's GRP bulkhead panel is **18%** at **3 minutes 42 seconds** into the flaming exposure. Calculate:
> (a) The maximum specific optical density $D_m$.
> (b) Whether the material passes the IMO $D_s(4) \leq 200$ criterion (assume $D_s(4) \approx D_m$ in this case).

### Solution:

**Part (a):** Using the simplified formula:

$$D_m = 132 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{100}{18}\right)$$

$$D_m = 132 \cdot \log_{10}(5.5556)$$

$$\log_{10}(5.5556) = 0.7447$$

$$D_m = 132 \times 0.7447 = \mathbf{98.3}$$

**Part (b):** Since $D_m \approx 98.3 < 200$, the material **passes** the smoke density criterion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A $D_s$ value of ~100 corresponds to moderate smoke. The IMO threshold of 200 is the upper bound for surface materials; higher values are permitted only for restricted-location materials (e.g., concealed spaces).

## 3.3 Toxicity Index — Numerical Computation

> **Problem:** Combustion gas analysis of a polyurethane-foam mattress cover from a ship's cabin yields the following concentrations under the standard flaming exposure:
> - CO = 450 ppm
> - HCN = 35 ppm
> - HCl = 120 ppm
> - NO₂ = 15 ppm
> 
> Compute the Toxicity Index $T_I$. Does it pass the IMO criterion ($T_I \leq 1$)?

### Solution:

Using $T_I = \sum_i \dfrac{C_i}{C_{f,i}} \cdot W_i$ with weighting factors all = 1 (conservative approach):

$$T_I = \dfrac{450}{1390} + \dfrac{35}{50} + \dfrac{120}{1000} + \dfrac{15}{90}$$

$$T_I = 0.3237 + 0.7000 + 0.1200 + 0.1667$$

$$\boxed{T_I = 1.31}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **FAIL** — Since $T_I = 1.31 > 1$, this polyurethane foam **does not comply** with IMO FTP Code. It must either be reformulated with flame-retardant additives, replaced with a low-toxicity alternative, or restricted to enclosed machinery spaces.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Common Marine Materials

| Material | Typical $D_m$ | Typical $T_I$ | IMO Pass? | Application |
|----------|--------------|----------------|-----------|-------------|
| Phenolic GRP (fire-retardant) | 60 – 90 | 0.4 – 0.6 | ✅ Yes | Bulkheads, decking |
| Standard GRP (polyester) | 150 – 200 | 0.8 – 1.2 | ⚠️ Marginal | Hull, superstructure |
| PVC Cable Sheathing | 300 – 450 | 1.5 – 2.5 | ❌ No | Restricted to conduits |
| Melamine-faced Plywood | 80 – 120 | 0.5 – 0.8 | ✅ Yes | Cabins, furniture |
| Mineral Wool Insulation | < 30 | < 0.1 | ✅ Yes | A-class divisions |
| Polyurethane Foam (FR) | 100 – 180 | 0.6 – 1.0 | ⚠️ Conditional | Mattresses, seating |
| Silicone Rubber | 40 – 70 | 0.2 – 0.4 | ✅ Yes | Seals, gaskets |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Smoke Chamber Functional Architecture (IMO FTP Code Part 2)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    specimen["SPECIMEN HOLDER\n75mm x 75mm"]
    coneheater["CONE RADIANT HEATER\n25 kW/m2 flux"]
    pilotflame["PILOT FLAME\nfiring mode"]
    chamber["SMOKE CHAMBER\nVolume = 0.5 m3\nLight path = 0.5 m"]
    lightsource["LIGHT SOURCE\nWhite lamp"]
    photodetector["PHOTODETECTOR\nMeasures I (transmitted)"]
    transmittance["TRANSMITTANCE METER\nLogs T% over time"]
    dsoutput["D_s CALCULATOR\nV/(A*L) factor = 132"]
    gassampler["GAS SAMPLING PROBE\nin chamber"]
    ftir["FTIR ANALYZER\nCO, CO2, HCN, HCl, HF"]
    echem["ELECTROCHEMICAL CELLS\nNOx, SO2, HBr"]
    toxicity["TOXICITY INDEX UNIT\nT_I = Sum Ci/Cf,i"]
    logger["DATA ACQUISITION\n1 Hz sampling\n10 min duration"]
    decision["COMPLIANCE DECISION\nD_s(4) <= 200 and T_I <= 1"]

    specimen --> chamber
    coneheater --> specimen
    pilotflame --> specimen
    lightsource --> chamber
    chamber --> photodetector
    photodetector --> transmittance
    transmittance --> dsoutput
    chamber --> gassampler
    gassampler --> ftir
    gassampler --> echem
    ftir --> toxicity
    echem --> toxicity
    dsoutput --> logger
    toxicity --> logger
    logger --> decision
```

## 4.2 Test Procedure Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["SAMPLE PREPARATION\nConditioning 24h\nat 23 deg C, 50% RH"] --> B["MOUNT IN CHAMBER\nVertical orientation"]
    B --> C["NON-FLAMING TEST\n25 kW/m2 only\n10 min"]
    B --> D["FLAMING TEST\n25 kW/m2 + pilot\n10 min"]
    C --> E["RECORD D_s vs TIME\nIdentify D_s(4) and D_m"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["GAS ANALYSIS\nFTIR + electrochemical"]
    F --> G["COMPUTE T_I\n8-gas weighted sum"]
    G --> H{"D_s(4) <= 200\nAND\nT_I <= 1?"}
    H -- YES --> I["CERTIFIED PASS\nFTP Code Compliant"]
    H -- NO --> J["REJECTED\nMaterial Reformulation Required"]
```

## 4.3 Smoke & Toxicity Threat to Ship Evacuation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    fire["SHIPBOARD FIRE\nINITIATION"] --> heat["RADIANT HEAT\n25 kW/m2"]
    heat --> pyro["PYROLYSIS OF\nSURFACE MATERIALS"]
    pyro --> smoke["SMOKE GENERATION\nD_s increases"]
    pyro --> gas["TOXIC GAS RELEASE\nCO, HCN, HCl, HF"]
    smoke --> visibility["REDUCED VISIBILITY\n< 3m on escape routes"]
    gas --> tox["IMPAIRED ESCAPE\nT_I > 1 = toxic dose"]
    visibility --> evac["EVACUATION DELAY\nCrew disorientation"]
    tox --> evac
    evac --> casualty["POTENTIAL CASUALTY\nwithin 2-5 minutes"]
```

## 4.4 Toxicity Index Computation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    gas1["CO\n450 ppm"] --> c1["C/Cf = 0.324"]
    gas2["HCN\n35 ppm"] --> c2["C/Cf = 0.700"]
    gas3["HCl\n120 ppm"] --> c3["C/Cf = 0.120"]
    gas4["NO2\n15 ppm"] --> c4["C/Cf = 0.167"]
    gas5["HF\n0 ppm"] --> c5["C/Cf = 0.000"]
    gas6["SO2\n0 ppm"] --> c6["C/Cf = 0.000"]
    gas7["HBr\n0 ppm"] --> c7["C/Cf = 0.000"]
    gas8["CO2\n5000 ppm"] --> c8["C/Cf = 0.056"]
    c1 --> sum["SUMMATION\nT_I = Sum"]
    c2 --> sum
    c3 --> sum
    c4 --> sum
    c5 --> sum
    c6 --> sum
    c7 --> sum
    c8 --> sum
    sum --> result["T_I = 1.31\nFAIL - exceeds 1.0"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the Specific Optical Density ($D_s$) used in the smoke density test for shipboard materials. State the IMO acceptance criterion for surface materials.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Specific Optical Density ($D_s$)** is a dimensionless quantity defined as the optical density of smoke produced per unit specimen area, normalized by the light path length in the test chamber. It is mathematically expressed as:
>
> $$D_s = \dfrac{V}{A \cdot L} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$$
>
> For the standard ISO 5659-2 chamber (where $V/(A \cdot L) = 132$), this simplifies to:
>
> $$D_s = 132 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{100}{T\,[\%]}\right)$$
>
> **IMO FTP Code Acceptance Criterion:** For surface materials in general vessel locations, $D_s$ at 4 minutes **must not exceed 200**, i.e., $D_s(4) \leq 200$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any four toxic gases measured in the FTP Code smoke and toxicity test, and identify the material that is the principal source of HCN on ships.**

**Model Answer:**

The four primary toxic gases measured under IMO FTP Code Part 2 are:

| # | Gas | Source Material on Ships |
|---|-----|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** | All combustible organic materials |
| 2 | **Hydrogen Cyanide (HCN)** | Wool, silk, nylon, polyurethane foam, ABS plastics |
| 3 | **Hydrogen Chloride (HCl)** | PVC cable insulation, vinyl floor tiles |
| 4 | **Hydrogen Fluoride (HF)** | PTFE (Teflon) gaskets, fluoroelastomer seals |

**Principal source of HCN:** **Wool** and **polyurethane foam** (both abundant in ship cabins and furnishings).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries 14 marks, split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**
