**(a)** Describe with a neat schematic the **smoke chamber apparatus** used in the IMO FTP Code smoke and toxicity test. Explain the function of the cone heater, pilot flame, photometric system, and gas sampling system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship's interior wall panel is tested in the standard ISO 5659-2 chamber. The minimum transmittance recorded is **22%**. The gas analysis at peak smoke gives:
- CO = 380 ppm
- HCN = 22 ppm
- HCl = 90 ppm

Determine:
- (i) The maximum specific optical density $D_m$
- (ii) The toxicity index $T_I$
- (iii) Whether the material passes IMO FTP Code Part 2 criteria. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Schematic Description:**

The smoke chamber apparatus consists of the following key subsystems:

1. **Specimen Holder** — A vertical platform holding a 75 mm × 75 mm specimen with the exposed face oriented toward the cone heater.
2. **Cone Radiant Heater** — An electrical heater delivering **25 kW/m²** constant radiant flux, simulating a fully developed compartment fire.
3. **Pilot Flame** — A small gas burner positioned above the specimen, activated only in the **flaming mode** to ignite pyrolyzed gases.
4. **Photometric System** — A horizontal light beam (white spectrum) traverses the chamber; a photodetector records transmitted intensity $I$, from which transmittance $T\%$ is computed.
5. **Gas Sampling System** — A stainless-steel probe draws chamber atmosphere continuously to **FTIR (Fourier Transform Infrared)** and **electrochemical analyzers**, providing real-time concentrations of CO, CO₂, HCN, HCl, HF, NOₓ, SO₂, HBr.

The chamber is sealed, temperature-controlled, and instrumented with 1 Hz data logging over 10 minutes.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Maximum Specific Optical Density:**

$$D_m = 132 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{100}{22}\right) = 132 \cdot \log_{10}(4.545)$$

$$D_m = 132 \times 0.6576 = \mathbf{86.8}$$

**(ii) Toxicity Index:**

$$T_I = \dfrac{380}{1390} + \dfrac{22}{50} + \dfrac{90}{1000}$$

$$T_I = 0.2734 + 0.4400 + 0.0900 = \mathbf{0.803}$$

**(iii) Compliance Check:**
- Smoke criterion: $D_m = 86.8 < 200$ ✅ **PASS**
- Toxicity criterion: $T_I = 0.803 < 1.0$ ✅ **PASS**

**Conclusion:** The material **fully complies** with the IMO FTP Code Part 2 requirements and is suitable for general-location surface application on ships.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of the **Toxicity Index ($T_I$)** as defined by the IMO FTP Code. List the eight combustion gases considered, and discuss why HCN is the most lethal per ppm on ships. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **PVC-insulated cable** is tested. Transmittance at peak smoke = **6%**. Gas analysis yields: CO = 600 ppm, HCl = 850 ppm, HF = 80 ppm, CO₂ = 40000 ppm. Determine:
- (i) $D_m$
- (ii) $T_I$
- (iii) Comment on the toxicity hazard to shipboard occupants, suggesting a safer alternative material. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Toxicity Index Concept:**

The **Toxicity Index ($T_I$)** is a dimensionless number that aggregates the lethal potential of all toxic combustion gases into a single hazard metric. It is computed as:

$$T_I = \sum_{i=1}^{8} \dfrac{C_i}{C_{f,i}} \cdot W_i$$

The **eight gases** considered are: **CO, CO₂, HCN, HCl, HF, NO₂, SO₂, HBr**.

**Why HCN is most lethal per ppm:** HCN has an extremely low reference fatal concentration of **50 ppm** (vs. 1390 ppm for CO). This means HCN is **~28 times more toxic per unit concentration** than CO. It acts by binding to cytochrome c oxidase in mitochondria, halting cellular respiration within seconds. On ships, HCN is particularly dangerous because:
- It is generated from **wool, silk, nylon, and polyurethane** (all common in cabins).
- It is **lighter than air** and rises with smoke, reaching escape routes first.
- Its **bitter-almond odor** is detectable by only ~40% of the population.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Maximum Specific Optical Density:**

$$D_m = 132 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{100}{6}\right) = 132 \cdot \log_{10}(16.67)$$

$$D_m = 132 \times 1.2218 = \mathbf{161.3}$$

**(ii) Toxicity Index:**

$$T_I = \dfrac{600}{1390} + \dfrac{850}{1000} + \dfrac{80}{200} + \dfrac{40000}{90000}$$

$$T_I = 0.4317 + 0.8500 + 0.4000 + 0.4444 = \mathbf{2.126}$$

**(iii) Hazard Assessment:**
- Smoke criterion: $D_m = 161.3 < 200$ ✅ **Passes**
- Toxicity criterion: $T_I = 2.126 \gg 1.0$ ❌ **FAILS**

**Comment:** Although the smoke obscuration is within the IMO limit, the toxicity index of **2.13** indicates that the combined emission of HCl and HF is **~2.1 times the safe threshold**. The acidic halide gases (HCl from PVC, HF from fluoropolymers) will cause severe respiratory damage, and in combination with HCN/CO, will render evacuation routes impassable within minutes.

**Safer Alternatives:**
- **LSZH (Low Smoke Zero Halogen) cables** — use polyethylene or cross-linked polyolefin with mineral fillers (e.g., ATH, MDH).
- **Silicone rubber insulation** — extremely low $T_I$ (≤ 0.3).
- **Mineral-insulated (MI) cables** with copper sheath — zero organic content, used in critical safety circuits.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $V/(A \cdot L) = 132$ factor** — using $\log_{10}(I_0/I)$ alone without the geometry factor results in a $D_s$ value **132 times too low**. Always check whether to include the chamber constant.
> 2. **Mixing up reference fatal concentrations** — CO = 1390, HCN = 50, HCl = 1000. Memorize these — KTU examiners often flip a number to test students.
> 3. **Forgetting the toxicity criterion $T_I \leq 1$** — many students compute $T_I$ but omit the pass/fail verdict. Always end with a clear compliance statement.
> 4. **Wrong logarithm base** — $\log_{10}$ is the standard (not $\ln$). Watch for this in numerical problems.
> 5. **Ignoring weighting factors** — some gas-specific standards use $W_i < 1$ for additive effects. When in doubt, state the assumption (e.g., "all $W_i = 1$").
> 6. **Confusing $D_s(4)$ with $D_m$** — $D_s(4)$ is the value at 4 minutes; $D_m$ is the absolute maximum. The IMO criterion is on $D_s(4)$, not $D_m$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Smoke and Toxicity Test**

- ✅ The test is governed by **IMO FTP Code, Annex 1, Part 2** (Resolution MSC.307(88)).
- ✅ Specimen size: **75 mm × 75 mm**; Heat flux: **25 kW/m²**; Duration: **10 min**.
- ✅ Two test modes: **Non-flaming (smoldering)** and **Flaming (with pilot)**.
- ✅ Master formula: $D_s = \dfrac{V}{A \cdot L} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$
- ✅ For standard chamber: $D_s = 132 \cdot \log_{10}(100/T\%)$
- ✅ IMO smoke limit: $D_s(4) \leq 200$ for surface materials.
- ✅ IMO toxicity limit: $T_I \leq 1$.
- ✅ Toxicity index: $T_I = \sum_i (C_i / C_{f,i})$ — sum over 8 gases.
- ✅ Critical reference values: **CO = 1390 ppm, HCN = 50 ppm, HCl = 1000 ppm, HF = 200 ppm, NO₂ = 90 ppm**.
- ✅ HCN is the most lethal per ppm due to mitochondrial cytochrome inhibition.
- ✅ PVC cables, PU foam, and PTFE are typical **T_I > 1** materials on ships.
- ✅ **LSZH (Low Smoke Zero Halogen) cables** are the modern maritime-safe alternative.
- ✅ Smoke density $D_s$ affects visibility, $T_I$ affects toxic incapacitation — both must pass simultaneously.
- ✅ The test chamber uses **FTIR + electrochemical analyzers** for gas detection.
- ✅ The Lambert-Beer Law is the underlying physical principle of the optical measurement.
- ✅ Aligned with **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 5.3** for material fire safety.

> **Final Exam Tip:** Always quote the standard (IMO FTP Code Part 2), the formula, the chamber constant (132), and the pass/fail verdict in a single coherent sentence. This is exactly how KTU examiners expect a complete answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test for 'A', 'B' and 'F' Class Divisions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Test for 'A', 'B' and 'F' Class Divisions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Class Divisions?

Class divisions are **fire-resisting bulkheads and decks** that subdivide a ship into fire zones, preventing the spread of fire and smoke for a specified duration. They are the **physical firewall system** of a vessel and form the cornerstone of SOLAS Chapter II-2 fire protection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea) Chapter II-2, Regulation 3.2** mandates that ships must be subdivided into main vertical and horizontal zones using class divisions, primarily to:
> - Confine any single fire to a small zone
> - Maintain structural integrity of the ship
> - Allow safe evacuation of passengers and crew
> - Provide protected escape routes

### 1.2 The Three Primary Class Divisions

| Class | Material | Fire Resistance | Typical Application |
|-------|----------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **A Class** | Steel or equivalent non-combustible | 60 minutes (A-60) standard | Main vertical zone bulkheads, main horizontal zone decks |
| **B Class** | Non-combustible (lower spec) | 30 minutes (B-30) standard | Corridor bulkheads, cabin partition walls, stairway enclosures |
| **F Class** | Non-combustible (fire-resisting) | Up to 60 minutes (F-60) | Galley boundaries, vehicle/aircraft carrier spaces, high-risk areas |

### 1.3 The A/B/F Sub-Categorization

Each class has subdivisions based on required integrity and insulation:

**A Class subdivisions (most stringent):**
- **A-60**: 60 min integrity + insulation
- **A-30**: 30 min integrity + insulation
- **A-15**: 15 min integrity + insulation
- **A-0**: 0 min but full integrity (no insulation failure)

**B Class subdivisions:**
- **B-15**: 15 min integrity + insulation
- **B-0**: 0 min but full integrity (smoke-tight)

**F Class subdivisions:**
- **F-60, F-30, F-15, F-0**: Similar time-based categories

> [!NOTE]
> The number after the letter (e.g., A-**60**) indicates the minutes of fire resistance under the **standard fire test**. Higher numbers mean more time before failure.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ship as a High-Rise Apartment Building"

Imagine a ship as a 10-storey apartment building with each floor being a "zone." The A Class divisions are like the **reinforced concrete floors and walls** that prevent a fire in one apartment from spreading to others for at least an hour. B Class divisions are like the **interior drywall partitions** between rooms — they slow the fire for 30 minutes, buying time for evacuation. F Class divisions are like the **fire-rated doors of a commercial kitchen** — protecting specific high-risk zones from the rest of the building. Without these, a single fire could engulf the entire structure in minutes.

### 1.5 The Governing Standard — FTP Code

All fire tests for class divisions are conducted as per the **International Code for Application of Fire Test Procedures (FTP Code)** under **SOLAS 1974, as amended**:

- **FTP Code Part 3** → Test for A Class Divisions
- **FTP Code Part 4** → Test for B Class Divisions
- **FTP Code Part 5** → Test for F Class Divisions (and Part 11 for specific F applications)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The FTP Code is administered by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** and is mandatory under SOLAS Chapter II-2.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Standard Fire Curve (Cellulosic / ISO 834)

All A and B class fire tests use the **standard cellulosic fire curve** defined by ISO 834. This models a fully developed fire with cellulosic fuel (wood, paper, fabric).

$$T_f = T_0 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8t + 1)$$

Where:
- $T_f$ = Average furnace temperature (°C)
- $T_0$ = Ambient temperature (typically **20 °C**)
- $t$ = Time elapsed from start of test (minutes)

**Key data points on the curve:**

| Time (min) | Furnace Temperature (°C) |
|-----------|-------------------------|
| 0 | **20** |
| 5 | **576** |
| 10 | **678** |
| 30 | **842** |
| 60 | **945** |
| 90 | **1006** |
| 120 | **1049** |

### 2.2 The Hydrocarbon Fire Curve (F Class / Helideck Fires)

The F class test, especially for **helidecks and process areas**, uses the more severe **hydrocarbon fire curve**:

$$T_f = 1080 \left(1 - 0.325 \cdot e^{-0.167t} - 0.675 \cdot e^{-2.5t}\right)$$

This curve reaches approximately **1100 °C** in just 5 minutes (vs. 576 °C for cellulosic), reflecting the rapid, high-temperature fires of aviation fuel and petrochemicals.

### 2.3 The Three Acceptance Criteria (Integrity, Insulation, Load-Bearing)

A division must pass **all three** criteria during testing:

1. **Integrity (E)** — Resistance to flame/hot gas penetration
   - Cracks/openings must not allow flames or hot gases through
   - Measured by **cotton pad test** and **gap gauge test**

2. **Insulation (I)** — Resistance to heat transmission
   - Average temperature on the unexposed face must not rise by more than **140 °C** above initial
   - Maximum temperature at any point must not rise by more than **180 °C** above initial

3. **Load-Bearing (R)** — For structural divisions only
   - Must support design load throughout the test duration

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units | Application |
|---------|-------------|-------|-------------|
| $T_f = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t+1)$ | Standard cellulosic fire curve | °C, min | A and B class tests |
| $T_f = 1080(1 - 0.325e^{-0.167t} - 0.675e^{-2.5t})$ | Hydrocarbon fire curve | °C, min | F class / helideck tests |
| $\Delta T_{avg} \leq 140°C$ | Insulation criterion (average) | °C | All class tests |
| $\Delta T_{max} \leq 180°C$ | Insulation criterion (point) | °C | All class tests |
| $HFI = \int_{0}^{t} Q(t) \, dt$ | Heat flux integration | kJ/m² | Energy exposure analysis |
| $t_{fail} = 60 \text{ min (A-60)}$ | A class maximum rating | min | A class only |

> [!NOTE]
> The standard fire test furnace must be **at least 4m × 3m** for specimens, with full temperature control following the curve within ±10% tolerance.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These test standards govern:
- **Cruise ship design** (passenger evacuation safety)
- **Oil tanker construction** (cargo pump room boundaries)
- **Naval vessels** (damage control zones)
- **Offshore platforms** (helideck F class requirements)
- **Cargo ships** (machinery space boundaries)

The classification directly affects:
- Ship tonnage and cost (heavier ratings = more steel)
- Layout flexibility
- Insurance and classification society approval (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, BV)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Fire Curve Behavior at Critical Times

The standard cellulosic fire curve $T_f = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$ can be analyzed at key test milestones:

**At t = 0 minutes (start of test):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_f &= 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8(0) + 1) \\
&= 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(1) \\
&= 20 + 345 \cdot (0) \\
&= 20 \, °C
\end{aligned}$$

This confirms the curve starts at ambient room temperature (20 °C) as expected.

**At t = 30 minutes (midpoint of A-60 test):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_f &= 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8(30) + 1) \\
&= 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(241) \\
&= 20 + 345 \cdot (2.382) \\
&= 20 + 821.8 \\
&= 841.8 \, °C \approx 842 \, °C
\end{aligned}$$

This matches the standard reference table value for 30 minutes.

**At t = 60 minutes (end of A-60 test):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_f &= 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8(60) + 1) \\
&= 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(481) \\
&= 20 + 345 \cdot (2.682) \\
&= 20 + 925.4 \\
&= 945.4 \, °C \approx 945 \, °C
\end{aligned}$$

This matches the standard reference value and confirms the **A-60 test requires the specimen to survive 945 °C for one full hour**.

### 3.2 Comparative Analysis: Cellulosic vs. Hydrocarbon Curve

At **t = 5 minutes**, compare both curves:

**Cellulosic curve:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_f &= 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8(5) + 1) \\
&= 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(41) \\
&= 20 + 345 \cdot (1.613) \\
&= 20 + 556.4 \\
&= 576.4 \, °C
\end{aligned}$$

**Hydrocarbon curve:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_f &= 1080 \left(1 - 0.325 \cdot e^{-0.167(5)} - 0.675 \cdot e^{-2.5(5)}\right) \\
&= 1080 \left(1 - 0.325 \cdot e^{-0.835} - 0.675 \cdot e^{-12.5}\right) \\
&= 1080 \left(1 - 0.325 \cdot (0.434) - 0.675 \cdot (3.73 \times 10^{-6})\right) \\
&= 1080 \left(1 - 0.141 - 0.0000025\right) \\
&= 1080 \times (0.859) \\
&= 927.7 \, °C
\end{aligned}$$

**Analysis:** Within just 5 minutes, the hydrocarbon curve reaches **927.7 °C** — over 350 °C hotter than the cellulosic curve. This is why F class divisions tested for helidecks must use highly specialized materials (e.g., ceramic fiber insulation, intumescent coatings) and why they are tested to a far more severe standard.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Insulation Criterion (Time to Failure)

The insulation criterion requires the unexposed face temperature to not exceed initial + 140 °C (average) or initial + 180 °C (max). To determine the time-to-failure for insulation:

Let $T_{avg}(t)$ = average temperature on unexposed face (°C)
Let $T_0$ = initial average temperature (typically 20 °C)

The failure condition is:

$$T_{avg}(t_{fail}) = T_0 + 140$$

Solving for time:

$$T_{avg}(t_{fail}) - T_0 = 140 \, °C$$

For a typical insulated steel bulkhead with **mineral wool insulation of thickness d (mm)** and thermal conductivity **k = 0.04 W/m·K**, the temperature rise can be modeled using Fourier's heat conduction equation. For a simplified case:

$$\Delta T(t) = \frac{q \cdot d}{k} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

Where $\tau$ is the thermal time constant depending on material properties.

Setting $\Delta T = 140 \, °C$ and solving for t gives the **insulation failure time**, which must exceed the class rating (60 min for A-60, 30 min for A-30, etc.).

### 3.4 Analytical Test Matrix: Failure Mode Identification

| Test Duration | Class A-60 (1 hr) | Class A-30 (30 min) | Class B-15 (15 min) |
|---------------|-------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| 5 min | ~576 °C, pass | ~576 °C, pass | ~576 °C, pass |
| 15 min | ~739 °C, pass | ~739 °C, pass | ~739 °C, **CRITICAL** |
| 30 min | ~842 °C, pass | ~842 °C, **CRITICAL** | Likely failed |
| 60 min | ~945 °C, **CRITICAL** | Likely failed | Failed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The logarithmic nature of the cellulosic curve means that **most temperature rise occurs in the first 10 minutes** (from 20 °C to 678 °C). This is why even short-duration ratings (B-15) provide significant fire protection for escape purposes.

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Test Procedure Logic

**Step 1: Specimen Preparation**
- Build full-scale specimen (minimum 2440 mm × 2440 mm for A class)
- Include all penetrations (cables, pipes, ducts) as designed for ship
- Condition specimen at 23°C ± 2°C, 50% ± 5% RH for 28 days

**Step 2: Furnace Instrumentation**
- Install **9 plate thermometers** per ISO 834
- Install **pressure sensors** (10 ± 2 Pa at top of specimen)
- Install unexposed face thermocouples (minimum 5 for A class, 5 for B class)

**Step 3: Fire Exposure**
- Ignite furnace burners
- Follow the standard curve within ±10% tolerance
- Maintain for the required test duration (e.g., 60 min for A-60)

**Step 4: Monitoring During Test**
- Record furnace temperatures every 30 seconds
- Record unexposed face temperatures every 30 seconds
- Perform **cotton pad test** every 5-10 minutes near gaps
- Perform **gap gauge test** (6 mm and 25 mm gauges)

**Step 5: Acceptance Evaluation**
- Integrity: No flame passage, no sustained flaming on unexposed side, gap gauges don't pass
- Insulation: $\Delta T_{avg} \leq 140°C$ AND $\Delta T_{max} \leq 180°C$
- Load-bearing: Specimen supports design load throughout

**Step 6: Hose Stream Test (A Class only)**
- After the fire test, subject the specimen to a **hose stream test** with 2 minutes of water jet
- Tests the impact damage resistance of the fire-exposed face

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fire Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Furnace Burner System] --> B[Fire Test Furnace Chamber]
    B --> C[Class Division Specimen]
    C --> D[Unexposed Face Side]
    D --> E[Temperature Monitoring Array]
    B --> F[Pressure Control System]
    F --> G[Exhaust Stack]
    E --> H[Data Acquisition System]
    E --> I[Cotton Pad Tester]
    E --> J[Gap Gauge Probe]
    B --> K[Load Application System]
    L[Standard Fire Curve Controller] --> A
    M[Operator Console] --> H
    H --> N[Acceptance Criteria Evaluator]
    N --> O[Pass or Fail Decision]
    P[Specimen Construction] --> C
    Q[Penetrations and Joints] --> C
    R[Insulation Layers] --> C
    S[Steel Framing] --> C
```

### 4.2 Test Methodology Flow Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Specimen Preparation] --> A2[Instrumentation Installation]
    A2 --> A3[Baseline Temperature Recording]
    A3 --> A4[Furnace Ignition]
    A4 --> A5[Fire Curve Application]
    A5 --> A6{Test Duration Reached?}
    A6 -->|No| A7[Continuous Monitoring]
    A7 --> A5
    A6 -->|Yes| A8[Integrity Check]
    A8 --> A9[Insulation Check]
    A9 --> A10[Load Test]
    A10 --> A11[Hose Stream Test]
    A11 --> A12{Final Evaluation}
    A12 -->|Pass| A13[Class Certification]
    A12 -->|Fail| A14[Design Revision]
```

### 4.3 Class Division Hierarchy on a Ship

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Ship Hull] --> B[A-60 Main Vertical Zone Bulkheads]
    A --> C[A-60 Main Horizontal Zone Decks]
    A --> D[B-15 Corridor Bulkheads]
    A --> E[B-15 Stairway Enclosures]
    A --> F[F-60 Galley Boundaries]
    A --> G[F-60 Boiler Room Casings]
    B --> B1[Cruise ships, Tankers]
    C --> C1[Engine room boundaries]
    D --> D1[Passenger accommodation]
    E --> E1[Escape routes]
    F --> F1[High fire risk areas]
    G --> G1[Machinery spaces]
```

### 4.4 Fire Curve Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Fire Test Selection] --> B{Fuel Type?}
    B -->|Cellulosic| C[Standard ISO 834 Curve]
    B -->|Hydrocarbon| D[HC Curve for F Class]
    C --> E[A Class Test 60 min max]
    C --> F[B Class Test 30 min max]
    D --> G[F Class Test 60 min max]
    E --> H[Apply to cellulosic fuel zones]
    F --> I[Apply to accommodation zones]
    G --> J[Apply to helideck or process zones]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The above mermaid diagrams represent the **functional architecture** of the test process and the **logical classification hierarchy**. For physical 3D drawings of the test furnace, refer to ISO 834-1:1999 Annex A which contains standard furnace schematics.

### 4.5 Critical Test Instrumentation Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Fire Test Furnace] --> B[9 Plate Thermometers]
    A --> C[2 Pressure Probes]
    A --> D[Specimen Frame]
    D --> E[Unexposed Face]
    E --> F[5 Thermocouples Min for A Class]
    E --> G[5 Thermocouples Min for B Class]
    D --> H[Penetration Locations]
    H --> I[Cotton Pad Test Points]
    H --> J[Gap Gauge Positions]
    K[Data Logger] --> B
    K --> C
    K --> F
    K --> G
    L[Time Stamp] --> K
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define 'A' Class division as per SOLAS Chapter II-2. State its fire resistance rating and the test standard used.*

**Model Answer:**
An A Class division is a bulkhead or deck constructed of steel or other equivalent non-combustible material, which must withstand the standard fire test for a period of **60 minutes (A-60)** to prevent the passage of flame and smoke. The test standard is **FTP Code Part 3 (Resolution MSC.307(88))**, and the divisions must satisfy both integrity and insulation criteria throughout the test duration. Common applications include main vertical zone bulkheads and main horizontal zone decks in passenger ships and tankers.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between A-60 and B-15 class divisions in terms of material, fire resistance duration, and typical application on a ship.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | A-60 Division | B-15 Division |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| Material | Steel or equivalent non-combustible | Non-combustible but lower spec |
| Fire Resistance | 60 minutes (full fire test) | 15 minutes |
| Application | Main vertical zone bulkheads, main horizontal zone decks | Corridor bulkheads, cabin partitions, stairway enclosures |
| Test Standard | FTP Code Part 3 | FTP Code Part 4 |

The key difference is that A-60 provides structural fire protection for ship-wide subdivision, while B-15 is used for local subdivision within accommodation spaces to provide escape time.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Adapted]

**Q.A(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **standard fire test procedure for A Class divisions** as per FTP Code Part 3, including the fire curve equation, specimen preparation, and acceptance criteria.

**Model Solution:**

The standard fire test for A Class divisions is governed by the **International Code for Application of Fire Test Procedures (FTP Code), Part 3**, adopted by IMO Resolution **MSC.307(88)**.

**Step 1: Fire Curve Specification**
The furnace temperature is controlled to follow the **standard cellulosic (ISO 834) curve**:

$$T_f = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$$

Where $T_f$ is in °C and $t$ is in minutes. The curve is permitted to deviate by **±10%** during the test.

**Step 2: Specimen Preparation**
- Minimum size: 2.44 m × 2.44 m for bulkheads
- Must represent actual ship installation including **all penetrations, joints, and stiffeners**
- Conditioning: 28 days at 23°C ± 2°C and 50% ± 5% relative humidity

**Step 3: Furnace Instrumentation**
- **9 plate thermometers** placed per ISO 834 specification
- Pressure controlled to **10 ± 2 Pa** at the top of the specimen
- Unexposed face monitored by at least **5 thermocouples** (more for larger specimens)

**Step 4: Test Duration**
- For **A-60**: 60 minutes
- For **A-30**: 30 minutes
- For **A-15**: 15 minutes
- For **A-0**: integrity test only, no time requirement

**Step 5: Three Acceptance Criteria**

(i) **Integrity (E):**
- No flames or hot gases pass to unexposed side
- Cotton pad test: No flaming on cotton when held near gaps
- Gap gauges (6 mm and 25 mm) do not pass through

(ii) **Insulation (I):**
- Average temperature rise on unexposed face ≤ **140 °C**
- Maximum temperature rise at any point ≤ **180 °C**

(iii) **Load-Bearing (R):**
- Specimen must support design load throughout the test

**Step 6: Hose Stream Test**
After fire exposure, the specimen is subjected to a water jet from a hose for **2 minutes** to test impact resistance of the fire-exposed face.

**Step 7: Certification**
On passing all criteria, the division is certified for use on SOLAS-regulated ships and is recognized by classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, etc.).

---

**Q.A(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the **furnace temperature** at **t = 15 minutes** and **t = 45 minutes** using the standard cellulosic fire curve, and comment on what these temperatures imply for an **A-30 class bulkhead**.

**Model Solution:**

**At t = 15 minutes:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_f &= 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8(15) + 1) \\
&= 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(121) \\
&= 20 + 345 \cdot (2.0828) \\
&= 20 + 718.6 \\
&= 738.6 \, °C
\end{aligned}$$

**At t = 45 minutes:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_f &= 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8(45) + 1) \\
&= 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(361) \\
&= 20 + 345 \cdot (2.5575) \\
&= 20 + 882.3 \\
&= 902.3 \, °C
\end{aligned}$$

**Comments on A-30 Bulkhead Implications:**

An A-30 bulkhead must survive the standard fire test for **30 minutes**. At the 30-minute mark, the furnace temperature is:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_f &= 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8(30) + 1) \\
&= 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(241) \\
&= 20 + 345 \cdot (2.382) \\
&= 841.8 \, °C
\end{aligned}$$

This means an A-30 bulkhead must:
1. Prevent flame/hot gas passage for 30 minutes at temperatures rising to **~842 °C**
2. Maintain unexposed face average temperature rise ≤ 140 °C and maximum ≤ 180 °C
3. At 15 minutes (mid-test), the bulkhead has already experienced **739 °C** — half the heat exposure in just half the time, due to the logarithmic curve
4. The A-30 bulkhead is suitable for areas with moderate fire risk such as machinery space casings and certain accommodation zone boundaries, but not for main vertical zone bulkheads (which require A-60)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **60-minute** standard with the A-60 rating. Remember: A-60 means **survival for 60 minutes**, not 60% protection. Similarly, B-15 means survival for 15 minutes, not "better than A." Always read the number as **minutes of rated fire resistance**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]

**Q.B(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **test procedure for F Class divisions** used for helideck applications. Explain the **hydrocarbon fire curve** and why it is used instead of the standard cellulosic curve for such applications.

**Model Solution:**

F Class divisions are tested as per **FTP Code Part 11** (Helideck Fire Test) and use the **hydrocarbon (HC) fire curve**, which simulates a fuel-rich, high-temperature fire from aviation kerosene or crude oil.

**The Hydrocarbon Fire Curve Equation:**

$$T_f = 1080 \left(1 - 0.325 \cdot e^{-0.167t} - 0.675 \cdot e^{-2.5t}\right)$$

**Sample Calculation at t = 10 minutes:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_f &= 1080 \left(1 - 0.325 \cdot e^{-0.167(10)} - 0.675 \cdot e^{-2.5(10)}\right) \\
&= 1080 \left(1 - 0.325 \cdot e^{-1.67} - 0.675 \cdot e^{-25}\right) \\
&= 1080 \left(1 - 0.325 \cdot (0.1881) - 0.675 \cdot (1.39 \times 10^{-11})\right) \\
&= 1080 \left(1 - 0.0611 - 0\right) \\
&= 1080 \times (0.9389) \\
&= 1014.0 \, °C
\end{aligned}$$

**Comparison with Cellulosic Curve at t = 10 min:** ~678 °C (cellulosic) vs. ~1014 °C (HC) — the HC curve is **~336 °C hotter** at the same time.

**Why HC Curve for Helidecks:**
1. **Aviation fuel (Jet A-1)** fires reach 1100 °C in minutes, far hotter than cellulosic fires
2. **Rapid flame spread** on the deck requires immediate, high-intensity fire protection
3. **Pool fires from crashed helicopters** create sustained, intense burning
4. The logarithmic ISO 834 curve underestimates the **peak heat flux** and **time-to-peak** for hydrocarbon fires

**Test Procedure for F Class (Helideck):**
1. **Specimen size:** Minimum 2.44 m × 2.44 m of the helideck structure
2. **Furnace:** Hydrocarbon-fueled burner system
3. **Test duration:** 60 minutes for F-60, scaled accordingly for F-30, F-15
4. **Instrumentation:** Radiative heat flux transducers on unexposed side
5. **Acceptance criteria:**
   - Integrity: No flame penetration for the rated duration
   - Insulation: $\Delta T_{avg} \leq 140°C$, $\Delta T_{max} \leq 180°C$
   - Heat flux on unexposed side ≤ **15 kW/m²** (critical for personnel safety)

**Practical Application:**
Helidecks on offshore platforms, FPSOs, and naval vessels (such as aircraft carriers and destroyers with helo decks) must use F-rated divisions because of the catastrophic consequences of a fuel-pool fire spreading to the ship's superstructure or accommodation block.

---

**Q.B(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the **integrity, insulation, and load-bearing criteria** for A Class vs. B Class divisions. Include the test duration, fire curve, and post-test requirements in your answer.

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Matrix: A Class vs. B Class Divisions**

| Parameter | A Class (A-60 max) | B Class (B-15 max) |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Fire Curve** | Standard cellulosic (ISO 834) | Standard cellulosic (ISO 834) |
| **Test Duration** | 60 min (A-60) | 15 min (B-15) |
| **Maximum Rating** | A-60 (60 minutes) | B-15 (15 minutes) |
| **Integrity Criterion** | No flame/gas passage, gap gauge, cotton pad test | No flame/gas passage, gap gauge, cotton pad test |
| **Insulation Criterion** | $\Delta T_{avg} \leq 140°C$, $\Delta T_{max} \leq 180°C$ | $\Delta T_{avg} \leq 140°C$, $\Delta T_{max} \leq 180°C$ |
| **Load-Bearing Criterion** | Must support design load | Not required (B class is non-structural) |
| **Post-Test Hose Stream** | Required (2 min water jet) | Not required |
| **Hose Stream Pressure** | 200-400 kPa depending on fire rating | N/A |
| **Maximum Temperature** | ~945 °C at 60 min | ~739 °C at 15 min |
| **Material** | Steel or equivalent | Any non-combustible |
| **Test Standard** | FTP Code Part 3 | FTP Code Part 4 |
| **Application** | Main vertical/horizontal zones | Corridor bulkheads, stairways |

**Key Similarities:**
- Both use the **same standard cellulosic fire curve** (ISO 834)
- Both have **identical insulation criteria** ($\Delta T_{avg} \leq 140°C$, $\Delta T_{max} \leq 180°C$)
- Both have **integrity criteria** using the cotton pad and gap gauge tests

**Key Differences:**

1. **Test Duration and Severity:**
   - A Class is tested up to **60 minutes** (A-60), reaching **~945 °C**
   - B Class is tested only up to **15 minutes** (B-15), reaching **~739 °C**
   - The A Class test exposes the specimen to **~3.5 times** the heat exposure duration

2. **Load-Bearing Requirement:**
   - A Class must support the design load throughout the test (it's a structural division)
   - B Class is non-load-bearing — it only needs to maintain integrity and insulation
   - In practice, B Class is used as a partition, not a structural element

3. **Hose Stream Test:**
   - A Class requires a **post-fire hose stream test** to ensure mechanical integrity after fire exposure
   - B Class does not require this — it doesn't need to resist mechanical impact post-fire because it serves a different purpose (escape time, not structural)

4. **Material Requirements:**
   - A Class **mandates** steel or equivalent non-combustible material with proven structural fire performance
   - B Class permits any non-combustible material (e.g., mineral wool panels, calcium silicate boards) with adequate fire resistance

5. **Application Hierarchy:**
   - A Class is used for **primary subdivision** — main vertical zones, main horizontal zones, machinery space boundaries
   - B Class is used for **secondary subdivision** — corridors, cabin partitions, escape route enclosures within a zone

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A common mistake is stating that B-15 is "stronger" than A-60 because 15 < 60 in some conceptual sense. **This is completely wrong.** The number denotes **minutes of survival**, not relative strength. A-60 is the **highest** rating for A Class and represents the most stringent fire protection requirement on ships.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist:**

- **A Class**: Steel divisions, 60 min standard (A-60), FTP Code Part 3, includes hose stream test, used for main vertical/horizontal zones
- **B Class**: Non-combustible partitions, 30 min max (B-15), FTP Code Part 4, no load-bearing or hose stream requirement, used for corridors and escape routes
- **F Class**: High-risk area divisions (galleys, helidecks), hydrocarbon curve, FTP Code Part 11
- **Standard Fire Curve (ISO 834):** $T_f = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$ — used for A and B class tests
- **Hydrocarbon Curve:** $T_f = 1080(1 - 0.325e^{-0.167t} - 0.675e^{-2.5t})$ — used for F class/helideck tests
- **Three Acceptance Criteria:** Integrity, Insulation, Load-bearing — ALL must be satisfied
- **Insulation Limits:** $\Delta T_{avg} \leq 140°C$ AND $\Delta T_{max} \leq 180°C$ on the unexposed face
- **Key Reference Temperatures:** 576 °C (5 min), 678 °C (10 min), 842 °C (30 min), 945 °C (60 min) for cellulosic curve
- **Governing Standards:** SOLAS Chapter II-2, FTP Code (MSC.307(88)), IMO Resolutions
- **Classification Societies:** DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, Bureau Veritas (BV) — all use these test standards
- **Furnace Pressure:** Must be controlled at **10 ± 2 Pa** at the top of the specimen
- **Specimen Size:** Minimum 2.44 m × 2.44 m for A and B class tests
- **Hose Stream Test:** Required for A class only, **2 minutes** of water jet, tests post-fire impact resistance
- **Cotton Pad Test:** Used to detect sustained flaming on unexposed face through gaps
- **Gap Gauge Test:** 6 mm and 25 mm gauges used to check integrity failure
- **Logarithmic Nature:** Most temperature rise occurs in the first 10 minutes — even B-15 provides significant protection
- **Penetrations:** All cable, pipe, and duct penetrations must be included in the test specimen as per actual ship installation

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** Think **"A = Armor"** (heavy protection, 60 min, structural), **"B = Barrier"** (lighter protection, 15 min, partition), **"F = Fuel"** (high-fire-risk areas, hydrocarbon curve).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test for Fire Door Control Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Test for Fire Door Control Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU / SOLAS / FSS Code Terminology)

A **Fire Door Control System** on a ship is the integrated arrangement of mechanical hardware, electrical actuators, release mechanisms, control panels, indicators, and alarm circuitry that ensures all designated **fire-rated doors** in main vertical and horizontal zone bulkheads, stairway enclosures, corridors, machinery spaces, and galleys reliably close, latch, and remain closed during a fire emergency. Its **testing regime** is the structured series of inspection, functional, and timed checks mandated by **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 9.4**, the **FSS Code (Chapter 3 — Fire Doors)**, **IMO MSC.1/Circ.1517**, and the rules of classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, BV, IRS) to verify that every fire door, including **power-operated sliding doors, hinged self-closing doors, and remotely released doors**, will perform its containment duty when required.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):** The topic "Test for Fire Door Control Systems" belongs to the broader study of *fire detection, alarm, and compartmentalization systems* and is the most frequently examined area in Maritime Safety ESE for both short-answer and 14-mark questions.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Hotel Corridor Principle"

Think of a modern hotel. The doors to each guest room are *self-closing* and *fire-rated*. If a fire starts in Room 101, you do not want smoke travelling freely down the corridor to Room 250. The fire door is the **automatic barrier** that stops smoke and flame. The **control system** is the hotel's brain that tells every door in the fire-affected zone to "lock down" at once. The **test for the control system** is the manager's weekly drill — he walks the corridor, opens a door, and confirms that it swings shut within the prescribed time, latches firmly, and that the central panel correctly shows the door as "closed." A ship is, in essence, a floating multi-storey building with far more stringent rules, and the **fire door control system test** is the maritime equivalent of that weekly hotel drill, but with far more severe regulatory consequences if it fails.

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Tolerance Values

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Quantitative References (SOLAS / FSS Code / IMO):**
> - **Maximum closing time of a power-operated sliding fire door:** **≤ 40 seconds** (and not less than **10 seconds** from the start of the closing motion) — *SOLAS Reg. II-2/9.4.2*.
> - **Power source for the closing mechanism:** the **ship's main power supply** (with automatic changeover) OR a **dedicated back-up power source** with sufficient capacity for at least **30 minutes of operation** (per FSS Code Ch. 3).
> - **Fire integrity rating of typical A-class fire doors:** **A-60, A-30, A-15, or A-0** based on time (in minutes) of fire resistance.
> - **Smoke-tightness (where fitted):** shall not permit the passage of smoke within the first **3 minutes** of the standard fire test.
> - **Hinged fire door closing speed (self-closing, no power):** typically **0.1 m/s to 0.3 m/s** at the leading edge to prevent injury to crew.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Alert:** Marks are routinely lost when students confuse **A-60** (fire integrity in *minutes*) with **H-60** (a *steel bulkhead* rating). The suffixes A, B, and H denote the **divisions**, not the test method.

## 1.4 Why Fire Door Control Systems Matter — The Engineering Rationale

A modern vessel is divided into **Main Vertical Zones (MVZ)**, each limited to approximately **40 m in length** on passenger ships, to limit fire spread. The fire doors embedded in these MVZ bulkheads are the **physical fire stops** of the ship. If they fail to close:

- Smoke and flame will spread across MVZ boundaries within minutes.
- The **fixed fire-extinguishing system (CO₂, foam, water-mist)** in an adjacent zone may be overwhelmed.
- **Means of escape (escape routes, muster stations)** become unusable.
- The **SOLAS regulatory certificate (Safety Construction Certificate)** becomes invalid.

Hence, the **periodic test** of the fire door control system is the legal and engineering proof that the ship's fire compartmentalization barrier is **operationally alive and not just structurally certified**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Fire Doors on Board

Fire doors are classified by **mode of operation** and **release mechanism**. Understanding this taxonomy is essential before any test can be designed.

### 2.1.1 By Mode of Operation

| Type | Description | Typical Location |
|:-----|:------------|:-----------------|
| **Self-Closing Hinged Door** | Closes under gravity / spring / hydraulic damper | Stairway towers, cabin doors |
| **Power-Operated Sliding Door** | Driven by an electric or hydraulic motor | Main vertical zone (MVZ) bulkheads on passenger ships |
| **Semi-Automatic / Hold-Back Door** | Held open by an electro-magnetic (solenoid) hold-back; released on fire signal | Corridors, lounges, public rooms |
| **Remotely Controlled (from bridge)** | Operated from the fire-control station or navigation bridge | Engine room, fire-station lobby doors |
| **Locally Released (manual release)** | Closes only when a local handle/push-button is activated | Local watertight/ fire doors |

### 2.1.2 By Power Source of Release

| Release Source | Power Origin | SOLAS Reference |
|:---------------|:-------------|:-----------------|
| **Primary (Main) Power** | Ship's main generating plant | FSS Code Ch. 3, Para 3.2 |
| **Emergency Power** | Emergency generator (automatic start) | FSS Code Ch. 3, Para 3.2 |
| **Standby / Buffer Battery** | Dedicated battery with 30-min autonomy | FSS Code Ch. 3, Para 3.2 |
| **Manual / Local Release** | Crew intervention at the door | SOLAS Reg. II-2/9.4.1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Fact:** Each power-operated sliding fire door must be capable of being **closed three times** in sequence — once from the **main power source**, once from the **emergency power source**, and once from the **local manual release point**. This is the **"triple-test"** that examiners love to ask.

## 2.2 The Test Hierarchy — Frequency and Scope

The regulatory framework enforces a **layered testing regime**. Each layer has a specific scope and frequency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Test Frequencies (SOLAS Reg. II-2/14 & FSS Code Ch. 3):**
> - **Daily Check** — visual inspection of all fire doors.
> - **Weekly Test** — operation of all fire doors in **main vertical zone bulkheads**.
> - **Monthly Test** — all **power-operated fire doors** operated from the centralized control position.
> - **Annual / Drydock Survey Test** — full functional test of every fire door, including all release mechanisms, alarms, and indications.

## 2.3 Test Parameters and Acceptance Criteria

The KTU Formula Cheat Sheet for this topic combines **regulatory thresholds** with **physical quantities** (closing time, voltage drop, current draw). Memorize this table.

### 2.3.1 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Test Parameter | Acceptance Criterion | Source / Standard | Engineering Significance |
|:-:|:---------------|:---------------------|:------------------|:------------------------|
| 1 | **Closing time** (power-operated door) | **≤ 40 s**, ≥ **10 s** | SOLAS II-2/9.4.2 | Prevents crew injury + allows orderly evacuation |
| 2 | **Power supply autonomy (backup)** | **≥ 30 min** | FSS Code Ch. 3 | Maintains closure in blackout |
| 3 | **Fire integrity rating** | **A-0, A-15, A-30, A-60** | SOLAS II-2/3.2 / FTP Code | Time (min) of fire resistance |
| 4 | **Smoke leakage** (smoke-tight doors) | **No smoke passage for 3 min** | FTP Code Annex 1 | Limits smoke spread |
| 5 | **Hold-back release current** | Solenoid must release at **≥ 70 % of nominal voltage** | Class rules (DNV/IRS) | Ensures release on degraded power |
| 6 | **Indication lamp** at central panel | **Green = closed, Red = open** | Class rules | Visual status for officer of the watch |
| 7 | **Local audible/visual alarm** at door | Sounds for **≥ 5 s** before closure | FSS Code Ch. 3 | Warns crew in vicinity |
| 8 | **Closing force** at leading edge | Must be **< 150 N** (to avoid injury) | ISO 3008 / Class rules | Ergonomic / safety |
| 9 | **Emergency release handle** force | Operable with **< 60 N** push/pull | Class rules | Accessibility in smoke |
| 10 | **Sequential closure** (multi-door zone) | All doors must close **within 60 s** of fire signal | Class rules | Coordinated lockdown |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The fire door control system is the **silent workhorse** of the ship's fire safety chain. The test is not bureaucratic — it is the **proof that a compartment can be sealed**. In **passenger ships (cruise liners)**, the sliding MVZ doors are gigantic (often **2.5 m × 2.0 m**, weighing over **400 kg**) and must close without crushing crew. In **tankers and bulk carriers**, the same principles apply to machinery-space skylight covers and engine-room fire doors. The test regime is therefore standardized globally by **IACS Unified Requirements (UR F18)** and is recognized by every Port State Control (PSC) inspector.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** Whenever a KTU question says *"Explain the test procedure for fire door control systems,"* always structure your answer in the four layers — *Daily, Weekly, Monthly, Annual* — and finish with the **SOLAS closing-time figure (40 s / 10 s)**. This single sentence usually earns the differentiating mark.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Test Procedure, Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Standardized Step-by-Step Test Procedure (SOLAS-Compliant)

The test is conducted in **seven sequential phases**. Each phase generates an evidence record (logged in the **Fire and Safety Plan Logbook / Fire Doors and Closing Devices Record Book**, mandatory under SOLAS II-2/14.2.4).

### Phase 1 — Pre-Test Visual Inspection (Daily / Weekly)

1. Walk along the door. Verify **no obstructions** are placed against the door (e.g., cabin trolleys, cleaning carts, fire hoses).
2. Confirm the **door frame is undamaged**, gaskets (intumescent strips) are intact, and the door leaf is not warped.
3. Verify that the **local release handle** is intact, the **magnetic hold-back** (if fitted) shows a clean latch face.
4. Check the **central panel indication**: door should be flagged "closed" (green) when latched and "open" (red) when released.

> [!NOTE]
> This is recorded in the **Fire Door Logbook** as a "Daily Visual Check" with the signature of the duty officer.

### Phase 2 — Local Manual Release Test (Weekly / Monthly)

1. With the door in the **CLOSED** position, activate the **local manual release handle** on either side.
2. The door must immediately begin to close (or unlatch).
3. Time the **local release action** with a stopwatch. The result should be **< 3 s** from handle activation to door movement.
4. Repeat from the **opposite side** of the door.

### Phase 3 — Self-Closing Speed Test (Weekly)

For hinged self-closing doors:

1. Open the door to **90°**.
2. Release it.
3. Measure the time from release to full latching.
4. The **kinetic energy at the leading edge** must not exceed the equivalent of a **150 N force**, otherwise the door must be re-set by a service engineer.

For sliding doors, this phase is replaced by **Phase 4**.

### Phase 4 — Power-Operated Closing Test (Weekly / Monthly)

This is the **most important** sub-test and is the heart of every 14-mark answer. It must be executed **three times** in sequence with **three different power sources**.

#### Step-by-Step (with derivation of the closing-time check)

**Step 4.1 — From the Main Power Source (Mains ON):**

- The officer initiates the **FIRE SIGNAL** from the fire detection panel.
- The **Fire Door Control Panel (FDCP)** sends a "CLOSE" command to the actuator.
- The motor drives the door along its track.
- Using a stopwatch, measure the interval $\Delta t_{mains}$ from the **initiation of the close command** to the **door reaching the fully closed and latched position**.

$$
t_{close} \;=\; t_{latched} \;-\; t_{command}
$$

**Acceptance:**

$$
10 \; \mathrm{s} \;\leq\; \Delta t_{mains} \;\leq\; 40 \; \mathrm{s}
$$

**Step 4.2 — From the Emergency Power Source (Mains OFF, Emergency Generator ON):**

- Open the door again.
- Trip the **main circuit breaker** to the door's motor circuit (simulating loss of main power).
- Verify the **automatic changeover** to the emergency power bus.
- Re-trigger the close command and measure $\Delta t_{emergency}$.

$$
10 \; \mathrm{s} \;\leq\; \Delta t_{emergency} \;\leq\; 40 \; \mathrm{s}
$$

**Step 4.3 — From the Local Battery / Manual Release (Both Mains and Emergency OFF):**

- Trip **both** main and emergency breakers.
- Verify that the door can still be closed by:
  - the **dedicated standby battery** (if fitted), OR
  - the **local manual handle** (hand-pulled).
- Measure $\Delta t_{local}$.

$$
\Delta t_{local} \;\leq\; 60 \; \mathrm{s} \quad \text{(or per class rule)}
$$

### Phase 5 — Indication & Alarm Test (Weekly / Monthly)

- The central Fire Door Control Panel (FDCP) must display the **change of status** for every door within **2 s** of the door's actual change of state.
- The **local audible alarm** at the door must sound for **at least 5 seconds before the door begins to close**.
- Verify on the panel that:
  - Red LED = OPEN
  - Green LED = CLOSED
  - Amber LED = FAULT / HOLD-BACK ACTIVE

### Phase 6 — Hold-Back Release Test (Monthly)

1. With door held open by the magnetic hold-back, **simulate a fire signal**.
2. The **electromagnet must de-energize** and the door must close.
3. The release voltage is measured:

$$
V_{release} \;\geq\; 0.7 \cdot V_{nominal}
$$

If the magnet does not release at 70 % of nominal voltage, the hold-back is **defective** and must be replaced.

### Phase 7 — Annual Full Functional & Survey Test (Drydock / Special Survey)

- The above six phases are repeated on **every fire door** in the ship.
- The surveyor's signature is obtained.
- The **Fire Door Logbook** is closed and a **new page is opened** for the next year.
- A **class surveyor** witnesses the test and endorses the records.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (for analytical rigour)

> A sliding fire door is commanded to close at $t = 0 \, \mathrm{s}$. The door begins to move at $t = 2.0 \, \mathrm{s}$ (this 2 s is the **local pre-warning alarm duration**) and is fully latched at $t = 35.0 \, \mathrm{s}$. Determine if the door meets SOLAS acceptance.

**Solution:**

The **closing time** is measured from the **initiation of the close command**, not from the start of motion. So:

$$
\Delta t_{close} \;=\; t_{latched} \;-\; t_{command} \;=\; 35.0 \;-\; 0 \;=\; 35.0 \; \mathrm{s}
$$

Check against the SOLAS envelope:

$$
10 \; \mathrm{s} \;\leq\; 35.0 \; \mathrm{s} \;\leq\; 40 \; \mathrm{s} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Verdict:** The door **passes** the closing-time test. If $\Delta t_{close}$ had been 41 s, the door would **fail** and the hold-back / counterweight / damper must be re-adjusted.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Linking Cause, Test, and Acceptance

| Failure Mode | Symptom | Test That Detects It | Acceptance Threshold |
|:-------------|:--------|:--------------------|:---------------------|
| Door too slow to close | Smothering crew in evacuation | Closing-time test (Phase 4) | ≤ 40 s, ≥ 10 s |
| Door does not release on fire signal | Magnetic hold-back stuck | Hold-back release test (Phase 6) | Release at 70 % V |
| Central panel not updating | Crew misled on zone status | Indication test (Phase 5) | Update within 2 s |
| No audible warning | Crew crushed by door | Local alarm test (Phase 5) | ≥ 5 s warning |
| Backup power failure | Door stuck open in blackout | Emergency-power test (Phase 4.2) | Closes in ≤ 40 s on emergency |
| Local handle jammed | Crew cannot close manually | Local release test (Phase 2) | Releases within 3 s |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fire Door Control System

The diagram below maps the **signal flow** from the fire detection system through the FDCP, the actuators, and back to the panel for status indication. This is the architecture you should draw when a 14-mark question asks for a labelled schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Fire Detection Panel] -- Fire Signal --> B1[Fire Door Control Panel FDCP]
    A2[Manual Call Point MCP] -- Manual Trigger --> B1
    A3[Navigation Bridge / ECR] -- Remote Close Command --> B1
    B1 -- CLOSE Command to Mains Power --> C1[Main Power Bus]
    B1 -- CLOSE Command to Emergency --> C2[Emergency Generator Bus]
    B1 -- CLOSE Command to Battery --> C3[Standby Battery 30 min]
    C1 --> D1[Sliding Door Actuator Motor]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    D1 -- Drives --> E1[Sliding Fire Door Leaf]
    E1 -- Limit Switches Closed --> F1[Status Feedback Loop]
    F1 -- Green Indication --> B1
    E1 -- Local Audible Alarm --> G1[Local Sounder and Beacon]
    B1 -- Status Indication --> H1[Bridge / ECR Indicator Lamp]
    B1 -- Status Indication --> H2[Crew Accommodation Indicator]
    E1 -- Mechanical --> I1[Local Manual Release Handle]
    E1 -- Electromagnetic --> I2[Magnetic Hold Back Solenoid]
    I2 -- Releases on Fire Signal --> E1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The FDCP is the *brain*; the door actuator is the *muscle*; the limit switches are the *nerves*; and the local manual handle is the *manual override* that the crew can pull in an emergency. A failure in any one branch degrades the system.

## 4.2 Test Procedure Flowchart — From Pre-Test to Sign-Off

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Start: Identify Door Location] --> stepB{Visual Inspection Passes}
    stepB -- No --> stepF1[Log Defect and Rectify]
    stepF1 --> stepB
    stepB -- Yes --> stepC[Local Manual Release Test]
    stepC --> stepD[Main Power Close Test]
    stepD --> stepE[Emergency Power Close Test]
    stepE --> stepF[Standby Battery or Manual Close Test]
    stepF --> stepG[Indication and Alarm Test]
    stepG --> stepH[Hold Back Release Test]
    stepH --> stepI{All Tests Pass}
    stepI -- No --> stepF2[Defect Logged - Door Out of Service]
    stepF2 --> stepJ[Rectify and Re-Test]
    stepJ --> stepB
    stepI -- Yes --> stepK[Sign Fire Door Logbook]
    stepK --> stepL[Update Fire and Safety Plan]
    stepL --> stepM[End: Door Returned to Operational Service]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Schematic — Hinged vs Sliding Fire Door

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Hinged
        h1[Door Leaf] --> h2[Spring / Cam Action Closer]
        h2 --> h3[Self-Closing Hinge]
        h3 --> h4[Frame and Latch]
    end
    subgraph Sliding
        s1[Door Leaf] --> s2[Track and Roller Assembly]
        s2 --> s3[Drive Motor and Gearbox]
        s3 --> s4[Limit Switch and Magnetic Hold Back]
        s4 --> s5[Frame and Latch]
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A common KTU 14-mark question asks the student to *compare hinged and sliding fire door control systems*. The block diagrams above are the perfect exam-ready illustration. You can reproduce them in 60 seconds on the answer sheet with a ruler and a pen.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a fire door control system. State SOLAS requirements for the closing time of power-operated sliding fire doors.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A fire door control system is the integrated arrangement of mechanical, electrical, and control components that ensures all designated fire-rated doors close, latch, and remain closed to contain fire and smoke within a compartment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SOLAS Requirement:** *The closing time for a power-operated sliding fire door must be not less than 10 seconds and not more than **40 seconds** from the initiation of the closing sequence (SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 9.4.2).*

*(1 mark: definition; 2 marks: SOLAS 40 s / 10 s envelope with citation.)*

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any four daily checks performed on a fire door during a routine watchkeeping round.**

**Model Answer (4 points × 0.75 mark each = 3 marks):**

1. The door is **not obstructed** by furniture, equipment, or mooring lines.
2. The **intumescent gaskets** around the frame are intact, not cracked or peeled.
3. The **local release handle** is present, undamaged, and free of paint seals.
4. The **central panel indication** (red/green) matches the actual physical state of the door.
5. *(Bonus 0.75 mark)* The **door's signboard** (red diamond) is clearly visible and in position.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark ESE Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question B-A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`
**(a)** Describe the **SOLAS-mandated test procedure for power-operated sliding fire doors** on board a passenger ship, including all three power-source scenarios. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a **block diagram**, explain the **functional architecture of the fire door control system** from the fire detection panel to the door actuator. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Regulatory basis (1 mark):**
SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 9.4 and FSS Code Chapter 3 require every power-operated sliding fire door in a main vertical zone bulkhead to be tested at weekly, monthly, and annual intervals.

**Step 2 — Pre-test (1 mark):**
Visual inspection is performed. Logbook is opened. Door is confirmed clear of obstruction. Local release handle and intumescent seals are inspected.

**Step 3 — Mains-power close test (1.5 marks):**
- With main power available, a fire signal is initiated from the FDCP.
- Door must begin to close within 2 s.
- Closing time is measured: **10 s ≤ $\Delta t \leq$ 40 s**.
- Local audible alarm must sound for **≥ 5 s** before motion.

**Step 4 — Emergency-power close test (1.5 marks):**
- Main circuit breaker is tripped.
- Automatic changeover to emergency generator is verified.
- Close command is re-issued; door must close within the same 40 s envelope.

**Step 5 — Battery / manual close test (1.5 marks):**
- Both main and emergency supplies are isolated.
- Door must close from the standby battery (if fitted) or by **local manual pull** within **60 s** of initiation.

**Step 6 — Logging (0.5 mark):**
All results are entered in the **Fire Doors and Closing Devices Record Book** with signature, date, and time.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Architecture (use the block diagram in Section 4.1):**

- **Fire detection panel → FDCP:** The detection panel sends a fire signal to the Fire Door Control Panel.
- **FDCP → Power buses:** FDCP routes a "close" command to the main bus, emergency bus, and standby battery in priority.
- **Power bus → Actuator → Door:** Motor and gearbox drive the door along its track.
- **Door → Limit switch → FDCP:** Limit switches report "fully closed" status to the FDCP.
- **FDCP → Indicators:** FDCP updates bridge, ECR, and cabin indicators with red/green/amber LEDs.
- **Magnetic hold-back:** Holds door open under normal conditions; releases on fire signal.
- **Local manual handle:** Provides crew override.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for each major block × 6 blocks; 1 mark for interconnection logic.)*

### Question B-B. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**(a)** Compare **hinged self-closing fire doors** and **power-operated sliding fire doors** in terms of operation, power source, test frequency, and typical shipboard location. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sliding fire door is commanded to close at $t = 0 \, \mathrm{s}$ from the main power source. The door begins to move at $t = 2.5 \, \mathrm{s}$ after a local alarm, and is fully latched at $t = 38.0 \, \mathrm{s}$. Determine whether the door **passes** the SOLAS closing-time test. If the door had been latched at $t = 42.0 \, \mathrm{s}$, what action would be required? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

| Parameter | Hinged Self-Closing | Power-Operated Sliding |
|:----------|:-------------------|:-----------------------|
| **Operating principle** | Gravity / spring / damper | Electric or hydraulic motor |
| **Power source** | None (mechanical) | Main + Emergency + Battery |
| **Test frequency** | Weekly visual + monthly functional | Weekly, monthly, annual full test |
| **Closing time** | 2–5 s (manual) | **10 s to 40 s** (SOLAS) |
| **Hold-back** | Not usually fitted | Magnetic hold-back standard |
| **Typical location** | Stairway towers, cabins | MVZ bulkheads (passenger ships) |
| **Indication to bridge** | Optional | Mandatory |

*(2 marks per significant row, 1 mark for summary statement.)*

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Calculation of closing time:**

$$
\Delta t_{close} \;=\; t_{latched} \;-\; t_{command} \;=\; 38.0 \;-\; 0 \;=\; 38.0 \; \mathrm{s}
$$

**Comparison with SOLAS envelope:**

$$
10 \; \mathrm{s} \;\leq\; 38.0 \; \mathrm{s} \;\leq\; 40 \; \mathrm{s} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Verdict:** The door **passes** the SOLAS closing-time test.

**Second scenario** (door latches at $t = 42.0 \, \mathrm{s}$):

$$
\Delta t_{close} \;=\; 42.0 \; \mathrm{s} \;>\; 40 \; \mathrm{s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{FAIL}
$$

**Required actions (3 marks):**
1. The door is **immediately taken out of service**.
2. The **counterweight, damper, or motor speed** is re-adjusted by a service engineer.
3. The **class surveyor** is informed, and the door is **re-tested** within 7 days.
4. An entry is made in the **Fire Doors Logbook** with the defect and the corrective action.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the lower limit of 10 s.** A door that closes in 5 s is *equally non-compliant* as a door that takes 50 s. Both fail the SOLAS envelope.
> 2. **Measuring from the start of motion** instead of from the close command. Always measure from the *initiation of the close signal*, not from the moment the door starts moving.
> 3. **Confusing the A-60 rating** (a door's fire resistance) with the **40 s closing time** (a performance test for power operation). They are different tests.
> 4. **Omitting the triple-test (main / emergency / battery).** Most failures to obtain full marks occur here.
> 5. **Not citing SOLAS Reg. II-2/9.4** or **FSS Code Chapter 3** — examiners reward regulatory citations generously.

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Definition:** Fire Door Control System = integrated mechanical + electrical + control hardware to close fire-rated doors on signal.
- **SOLAS Reg. II-2/9.4** is the master regulation; **FSS Code Chapter 3** is the detailed code.
- **Closing time** for power-operated sliding doors: **10 s ≤ $\Delta t$ ≤ 40 s**.
- **Three test scenarios:** main power, emergency power, local battery/manual.
- **Hold-back release voltage:** ≥ 70 % of nominal $V$.
- **Local audible alarm:** ≥ 5 s before motion.
- **Test frequencies:** Daily (visual), Weekly (operation), Monthly (central control), Annual (class survey).
- **Indication colours:** **Red = Open, Green = Closed, Amber = Fault/Hold-back**.
- **Smoke-tight doors:** no smoke passage for first **3 min** of fire test.
- **Hinged self-closing door closing force:** < 150 N at the leading edge (crew-safety).
- **Logbook:** Mandatory record under **SOLAS II-2/14.2.4** for every test.
- **Failure consequences:** Port State Control detention, loss of Safety Construction Certificate, crew liability in casualty investigation.
- **Cited standards:** SOLAS, FSS Code, FTP Code, MSC.1/Circ.1517, IACS UR F18, ISO 3008.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Whenever a question is asked on *fire door testing*, always structure your answer in the **seven-phase sequence** (Visual → Local Release → Self-Closing → Power-Operated → Indication → Hold-Back → Annual Survey). This is the standard examiners expect, and it is the only way to consistently score full marks in a 14-mark KTU ESE question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test for Surface Flammability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Test for Surface Flammability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Surface Flammability Test (SFT)** is a standardized fire reaction-to-fire test prescribed under the **IMO Fire Test Procedures (FTP) Code, Part 5**, formerly known as **IMO Resolution A.653(16)**. It evaluates the propensity of bulkhead, ceiling, and deck finish materials used in ships to propagate flame across their surface when subjected to a graded radiant heat flux.

The test determines a material's **Critical Heat Flux (CHF)** — the minimum incident radiant heat flux (in **kW/m²**) at which a pilot flame, moved across the specimen, produces **sustained flaming combustion for at least 60 seconds**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Regulatory Anchor:** Under **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 6.1**, surface finish materials in ships must satisfy surface flammability criteria as defined in the FTP Code. The test is mandatory for type approval of any decorative or finish material fitted to a vessel's structure.

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a corridor in a ship, fully lined with wall panels. If a fire breaks out in a cabin at one end, the **heat radiates from the flames** and strikes the corridor walls. The question the surface flammability test answers is simply:

> **"How much heat does the wall need to absorb before it itself starts burning, and how quickly will the flame spread along it?"**

Think of a **matchstick held over a candle**:
- If the matchstick is too far from the flame, it will not ignite (insufficient heat).
- The closer it gets, the more heat it absorbs, until it suddenly ignites.
- The **Critical Heat Flux (CHF)** is exactly the moment that "sudden ignition" occurs on the wall surface.

A material with a **high CHF** is like a **tough, non-igniting matchstick** — it requires enormous heat before it can catch fire. A material with a **low CHF** is like a **dry paper strip** — it ignites easily and propagates the flame rapidly, which is a **catastrophic failure** in a ship.

## 1.3 Key Terminology in Bold

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| **Critical Heat Flux** | **CHF** | Minimum heat flux (kW/m²) needed to sustain flaming for **60 s** |
| **Heat for Sustained Burning** | **Q_sb** | Heat flux just above the threshold of continued burning |
| **Radiant Panel** | — | Gas-fired porous refractory panel acting as the heat source |
| **Pilot Flame** | — | A small flame moved across the specimen to ignite volatiles |
| **Specimen** | — | The material under test, size **155 mm × 800 mm** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever the question mentions "fire reaction test for ship interior finishes" or "spread of flame on bulkheads," the examiner is referring to the **Surface Flammability Test under FTP Code Part 5 / IMO Res. A.653(16)**. Always quote the resolution number if memory allows.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Principle of the Test

The test fundamentally relies on **radiative heat transfer** from a controlled source onto an inclined specimen. A pilot flame, swept laterally, detects ignition of combustible volatiles evolved from the heated specimen.

### Step-by-Step Logic of the Phenomenon

1. **Radiant Panel Activation:** A porous refractory panel is heated by a **premixed air–propane** flame to a temperature of **~890 K (≈ 617 °C)** on its surface.
2. **Heat Transfer to Specimen:** The specimen, mounted at **30°** to the vertical panel, receives a **graded heat flux** that is maximum at the bottom (closest to panel) and decreases with height.
3. **Pyrolysis of the Specimen:** Below CHF, the material releases gases but does not sustain combustion. **Above CHF**, the volatiles ignite readily and burn continuously.
4. **Pilot Flame Sweep:** The pilot flame is moved horizontally across the specimen at a constant speed of **10 mm/s**.
5. **Ignition Detection:** A sustained flame of **≥ 60 s** at any point indicates the **CHF has been crossed** at that location.
6. **Calorimetric Mapping:** The position of flame propagation is correlated with the heat flux measured at that position using a **Schmidt-Boelter type heat flux transducer**.

## 2.2 Heat Transfer Equations Governing the Test

The total incident heat flux on the specimen is the **sum of radiative and convective components**:

$$q'' = q''_{rad} + q''_{conv}$$

### Radiative Component (View-Factor Approach)

The radiation from the panel to the specimen element is given by:

$$q''_{rad} = \varepsilon_p \cdot \sigma \cdot (T_p^4 - T_s^4) \cdot F_{p \to s}$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon_p$ = emissivity of the radiant panel
- $\sigma$ = **Stefan-Boltzmann constant = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴**
- $T_p$ = absolute temperature of panel (K)
- $T_s$ = absolute temperature of specimen (K)
- $F_{p \to s}$ = view factor from panel to specimen element

### Convective Component

The convective heat flux from the hot gases above the panel follows Newton's law of cooling:

$$q''_{conv} = h_c \cdot (T_g - T_s)$$

Where:
- $h_c$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (typically **10–15 W/m²·K** for natural convection in the test rig)
- $T_g$ = temperature of the hot gas layer adjacent to specimen (K)

### Critical Heat Flux Determination

The CHF is determined empirically as the heat flux at the position where the **flame front ceases to advance** (the boundary between sustained and non-sustained flaming):

$$CHF = q''(x_{crit}) = \text{measured heat flux at } x_{crit}$$

Where $x_{crit}$ is the distance from the hottest end where sustained flaming ends.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Variables / Units | Application |
|---|----------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $q'' = \varepsilon \sigma (T_p^4 - T_s^4) F$ | W/m² | Radiative flux from panel to specimen |
| 2 | $q''_{conv} = h_c (T_g - T_s)$ | W/m² | Convective flux from hot gases |
| 3 | $q''_{tot} = q''_{rad} + q''_{conv}$ | W/m² | Total incident flux |
| 4 | $Q = \int q''(x) \, dA$ | W | Integrated heat input to specimen area |
| 5 | $CHF = q''_{60s}$ | kW/m² | Critical Heat Flux (60 s criterion) |
| 6 | $F = \frac{1}{2}[\cos\theta - \cos\phi]$ | dimensionless | View factor for parallel plates |
| 7 | $\dot{m} = \frac{q''_{eff}}{\Delta H_c}$ | kg/s | Pyrolysis mass loss rate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table:** In the cheat sheet, the pipe symbol has been **avoided** in every cell by writing equations in LaTeX. Never use a vertical bar `\vert` symbol in a KTU answer script table cell — it confuses the markdown rendering.

## 2.4 Classification of Surface Materials (SOLAS Compliance)

Based on the CHF, materials are classified as per the FTP Code, Part 5:

| Ship Area | Required Minimum CHF | Class |
|-----------|----------------------|-------|
| **Corridors, stairways, escape routes** | **≥ 7.0 kW/m²** | Highest resistance |
| **Public spaces (cabins, lounges)** | **≥ 4.5 kW/m²** | Medium resistance |
| **Service areas, low-risk spaces** | **≥ 1.5 kW/m²** | Minimum acceptable |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Critical Heat Flux** with **Heat Release Rate (HRR)**. Remember — **CHF** is a *threshold* value, whereas **HRR** is a *rate of energy release during burning*. They are not interchangeable.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Material Selection in Shipyards:** Ship designers use CHF data to choose wall and ceiling panels for passenger vessels, naval ships, and offshore platforms.
- **Life Safety:** Higher CHF materials delay **flashover** in a corridor fire, giving passengers critical **15–30 minutes** more evacuation time.
- **Regulatory Approval:** Every finish material fitted in a SOLAS-regulated ship must be accompanied by a **Type Approval Certificate** with CHF data.
- **Insurance & Classification:** Insurance underwriters (Lloyd's, DNV, BV) reference CHF in fire risk pricing for vessels.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Flux Distribution Along the Specimen

The test specimen is mounted at **30°** to the radiant panel. Let us set up a coordinate system where $x$ is the distance from the bottom (hot end) of the specimen, in metres. The view factor from a small panel element to the specimen strip $dx$ decreases as $x$ increases.

### Step 1: Geometry Definition

Let:
- Panel length: $L_p$
- Specimen length: $L_s = 0.800$ m
- Inclination angle: $\theta = 30°$
- Perpendicular distance from panel top to specimen bottom: $d_0$

The total separation distance between panel surface and specimen strip at position $x$ is:

$$r(x) = \sqrt{d_0^2 + (x \cdot \sin\theta)^2}$$

### Step 2: Differential View Factor

For a small area differential on the panel $dA_p$ and the strip on the specimen $dA_s$ at position $x$:

$$dF_{p \to s} = \frac{\cos\beta_p \cos\beta_s}{\pi r^2} \, dA_p$$

Where $\beta_p$ and $\beta_s$ are the angles from the respective surface normals to the connecting line.

### Step 3: Net Heat Flux at Position x

Integrating over the panel area and applying the Stefan-Boltzmann law:

$$q''(x) = \int_{A_p} \varepsilon_p \sigma (T_p^4 - T_s^4) \frac{\cos\beta_p \cos\beta_s}{\pi r^2} \, dA_p + h_c(T_g - T_s)$$

### Step 4: Empirical Correlation (Simplified)

For the standard test rig, the empirical correlation obtained from calibration is:

$$q''(x) = q''_0 \cdot e^{-k x}$$

Where:
- $q''_0$ = heat flux at the bottom (hot end), typically **~50 kW/m²**
- $k$ = decay constant, typically **~3.2 m⁻¹** for the standard ISO 5658-2 / FTP Part 5 rig
- $x$ = distance in metres from the bottom

### Step 5: Solving for CHF

At the critical position $x_{crit}$ where sustained flaming ends:

$$CHF = q''(x_{crit}) = q''_0 \cdot e^{-k x_{crit}}$$

Solving for $x_{crit}$:

$$x_{crit} = \frac{1}{k} \ln\left(\frac{q''_0}{CHF}\right)$$

## 3.2 Sample Numerical Calculation

> **Problem:** A ship cabin wall panel is being tested. The heat flux at the bottom of the specimen is $q''_0 = 48.0$ kW/m² and the decay constant is $k = 3.2$ m⁻¹. The flame propagated up to a position $x_{crit} = 0.62$ m. Find the Critical Heat Flux (CHF) of the material.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Write the heat flux distribution formula:

$$q''(x) = q''_0 \cdot e^{-k x}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values:

$$q''(x_{crit}) = 48.0 \times e^{-3.2 \times 0.62}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the exponent:

$$-3.2 \times 0.62 = -1.984$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the exponential:

$$e^{-1.984} = 0.1376$$

**Step 5:** Multiply:

$$CHF = 48.0 \times 0.1376 = 6.605 \text{ kW/m}^2$$

**Step 6:** Round to KTU standard precision:

$$\boxed{CHF \approx 6.6 \text{ kW/m}^2}$$

### Step 7: Compliance Check

Compare with SOLAS requirements:
- Corridor requirement: **≥ 7.0 kW/m²**
- Public space requirement: **≥ 4.5 kW/m²**

**Conclusion:** The material is suitable for **public spaces (cabins, lounges)** but **NOT for corridors, stairways, or escape routes** — it falls short by approximately 0.4 kW/m².

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Scenarios

| Scenario | $q''_0$ (kW/m²) | $k$ (m⁻¹) | $x_{crit}$ (m) | CHF (kW/m²) | Suitable for |
|----------|-----------------|-----------|----------------|-------------|--------------|
| Phenolic laminate panel | 50.0 | 3.2 | 0.75 | 7.10 | **Corridors ✓** |
| Standard painted steel | 48.0 | 3.5 | 0.55 | 6.45 | Public spaces only |
| Melamine-faced plywood | 49.0 | 3.3 | 0.40 | 4.85 | Public spaces (marginal) |
| Decorative wallpaper | 47.0 | 3.6 | 0.25 | 1.96 | Service areas only |
| Non-combustible cement board | 50.0 | 3.0 | 0.80 | 9.96 | **All areas ✓** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Insight:** Notice how the **cement board** has a CHF nearly **5×** that of wallpaper. This is why SOLAS regulations are so strict — a single layer of inappropriate finish material can compromise the entire fire safety strategy of a ship.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Surface Flammability Test Rig

> *Note:* Since the actual test apparatus involves complex physical geometry (radiant panel, gas lines, specimen mounting frame, exhaust duct), the following Mermaid diagram presents the **functional architecture** of the test system, mapping component interactions and signal/data flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Gas Supply System (Propane + Air Mixer)"]:::source
    node2["Radiant Panel (Porous Refractory)"]:::heater
    node3["Specimen Holder 30 deg Tilt"]:::holder
    node4["Pilot Flame Assembly (Movable)"]:::igniter
    node5["Heat Flux Transducer (Schmidt-Boelter)"]:::sensor
    node6["Exhaust Hood and Duct"]:::exhaust
    node7["Data Acquisition System (DAS)"]:::data
    node8["Control Unit and Operator Interface"]:::control
    node9["Test Specimen 155x800 mm"]:::sample
    node10["CHF Computation Module"]:::compute
    node11["Classification Report Output"]:::output

    node1 -- Premixed fuel flow --> node2
    node2 -- Radiative heat flux --> node3
    node3 -- Holds at 30 deg --> node9
    node4 -- Sweeps across surface --> node9
    node9 -- Emits volatiles --> node4
    node5 -- Calibrates flux at positions --> node3
    node5 -- Voltage signal --> node7
    node4 -- Ignition detection signal --> node7
    node6 -- Removes combustion products --> node2
    node7 -- Time history data --> node10
    node8 -- Sweep speed control --> node4
    node8 -- Panel temperature setpoint --> node1
    node10 -- CHF value --> node11

    classDef source fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    classDef heater fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
```
