```mermaid
    classDef holder fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef igniter fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#330099,color:#000
    classDef exhaust fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#666666,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef sample fill:#ffeaa7,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000
    classDef compute fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1a5276,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#abebc6,stroke:#145a32,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Test Procedure Flowchart (Logical Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Start: Specimen Conditioning at 23 deg C 50% RH for 24 hours"]:::start
    nodeB["Mount Specimen at 30 deg to Radiant Panel"]:::step
    nodeC["Ignite Pilot Flame and Verify Position"]:::step
    nodeD["Ignite Radiant Panel and Stabilize at Operating Temperature"]:::step
    nodeE["Begin Pilot Flame Sweep at 10 mm per second"]:::step
    nodeF["Record Time to Ignition and Flame Spread Length"]:::step
    nodeG{"Does Flame Sustain for Greater than or Equal to 60 s?"}:::decision
    nodeH["Mark Position as Sustained Burning Zone"]:::step
    nodeI["Continue Sweep to Find Boundary"]:::step
    nodeJ["Measure Heat Flux at Boundary Position"]:::step
    nodeK["Compute CHF from Calibrated Heat Flux Map"]:::step
    nodeL["Compare CHF with SOLAS Threshold"]:::step
    nodeM["Issue Classification Report"]:::output

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG -- Yes --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeG
    nodeG -- No --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM

    classDef start fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef step fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Test Rig Cross-Section (Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["Radiant Panel (vertical, gas heated)"]:::heater
    nodeS["Specimen (tilted 30 deg)"]:::sample
    nodeFl["Pilot Flame (horizontal sweep)"]:::igniter
    nodeH["Heat Flux Sensor (calibration)"]:::sensor
    nodeE["Exhaust Hood"]:::exhaust

    nodeP -- "q_rad = 50 kW per sq m at base" --> nodeS
    nodeFl -- "Pilot flame at 10 mm per s" --> nodeS
    nodeS -- "Volatile gases + flame front" --> nodeFl
    nodeH -- "Calibrates q(x) profile" --> nodeS
    nodeE -- "Extracts smoke and heat" --> nodeP

    classDef heater fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    classDef sample fill:#ffeaa7,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000
    classDef igniter fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#330099,color:#000
    classDef exhaust fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#666666,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Critical Heat Flux" as used in the IMO Surface Flammability Test. State its units and the threshold criterion used in the test.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Critical Heat Flux (CHF)** is the minimum incident radiant heat flux (measured in **kW/m²**) applied to a specimen that causes **sustained flaming combustion for a duration of at least 60 seconds** when a small pilot flame is applied. The threshold criterion is the **60-second sustained burning rule** — if the flame at any point on the specimen continues to burn on its own (without pilot flame) for 60 s or more, the heat flux at that position is recorded as the CHF. The test is conducted per the **IMO Fire Test Procedures Code, Part 5** (formerly Res. A.653(16)).

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for units, 1 mark for 60-s criterion and regulatory reference)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Name the IMO resolution that originally prescribed the surface flammability test and explain the role of the pilot flame in the test.**

**Model Answer:**

> The original IMO resolution is **Resolution A.653(16)**, adopted in 1989, which laid out the *Recommendation on Fire Test Procedures for Surface Flammability of Bulkhead, Ceiling, and Deck Finish Materials*. It has since been incorporated into the **FTP Code, Part 5**.
>
> **Role of the Pilot Flame:** The pilot flame is a small, controlled flame (typically a **3-mm to 5-mm long** methane or propane flame) that is moved horizontally across the surface of the heated specimen. Its role is to **ignite the combustible volatile gases** that the specimen releases as it is heated. Without the pilot flame, auto-ignition might occur only at much higher temperatures, and the test would not properly simulate the fire propagation scenario the test is designed to assess.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for resolution name, 1 mark for FTP Code reference, 1 mark for pilot flame role)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[Internal Choice Provided — Answer either Question B1 OR Question B2]**

---

### **Question B1 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
#### *(a)* Describe with a neat sketch the apparatus used for the Surface Flammability Test as per the IMO FTP Code, Part 5. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The Surface Flammability Test apparatus, as per the **IMO FTP Code Part 5**, consists of the following major components:

1. **Radiant Panel:** A **porous refractory (fused silica) panel** of dimensions approximately **300 mm × 450 mm**, mounted vertically. It is heated by a **premixed air-propane flame** on its underside to a surface temperature of approximately **890 K (617 °C)**.

2. **Specimen Holder:** A water-cooled holder designed to support the test specimen (typically **155 mm wide × 800 mm long**) at an angle of **30°** to the radiant panel, with the lower edge of the specimen closest to the panel.

3. **Pilot Flame Assembly:** A small burner producing a flame **~50 mm long**, mounted on a motorized carriage that traverses horizontally at a constant **linear speed of 10 mm/s** at a fixed distance of **~10 mm** from the specimen surface.

4. **Heat Flux Transducer (Schmidt-Boelter type):** Used to calibrate the heat flux at various points on the specimen plane before the actual test. It is water-cooled and has a measurement range of **0 to 50 kW/m²** with an accuracy of **±3%**.

5. **Exhaust Hood and Duct:** A canopy hood placed above the test rig to capture combustion products, with a flow rate sufficient to remove smoke and gases without disturbing the test conditions.

6. **Data Acquisition System (DAS):** Records the pilot flame position, time to ignition, sustained burning duration, and heat flux values. The DAS is essential for computing the CHF.

7. **Control Panel:** Houses the gas flow regulators, pilot flame sweep control, and emergency shutdown systems.

> **Working Principle:** The radiant panel produces a graded heat flux along the specimen. The pilot flame, moving horizontally, ignites volatiles. The position at which sustained flaming (≥ 60 s) first ceases is identified, and the heat flux at that point (from calibration) is the **Critical Heat Flux (CHF)**.

*(Marks distribution: 2 for component list, 2 for dimensions, 2 for pilot flame details, 1 for working principle)*

#### *(b)* A ship's cabin wall panel is tested in the Surface Flammability Test. The heat flux at the hot end of the specimen is 49.5 kW/m² and decays exponentially with a decay constant of 3.1 m⁻¹. The flame ceased to propagate at a distance of 0.68 m from the hot end. Determine the CHF of the material and state whether it is suitable for use in a passenger corridor. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify the given values:**
- $q''_0 = 49.5$ kW/m²
- $k = 3.1$ m⁻¹
- $x_{crit} = 0.68$ m

**Step 2 — Apply the heat flux distribution equation:**

$$q''(x) = q''_0 \cdot e^{-k x}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$CHF = 49.5 \times e^{-3.1 \times 0.68}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the exponent:**

$$-3.1 \times 0.68 = -2.108$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the exponential:**

$$e^{-2.108} = 0.1215$$

**Step 6 — Compute CHF:**

$$CHF = 49.5 \times 0.1215 = 6.014 \text{ kW/m}^2$$

**Step 7 — Comparison with SOLAS threshold:**

| Area | Required CHF |
|------|--------------|
| Passenger corridor | **≥ 7.0 kW/m²** |
| Public space (cabin) | **≥ 4.5 kW/m²** |

Since the computed **CHF = 6.01 kW/m²** is **less than 7.0 kW/m²**, the material is **NOT suitable for passenger corridors**. It is, however, **acceptable for public spaces like cabins and lounges** (since 6.01 > 4.5).

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{CHF \approx 6.01 \text{ kW/m}^2; \text{ Suitable only for public spaces, not corridors.}}$$

*(Marks distribution: 1 for setup, 2 for substitution and exponent, 1 for exponential evaluation, 1 for CHF, 2 for compliance check and conclusion)*

---

### **Question B2 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
*(Alternative to Question B1 — students may answer either)*

#### *(a)* Explain the significance of the Surface Flammability Test in maritime safety. Discuss the role of CHF in material selection for ship interiors. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Significance in Maritime Safety:**

1. **Fire Propagation Control:** In a ship, the rapid spread of flame along corridors and stairways is one of the leading causes of casualties in maritime fires. The Surface Flammability Test quantifies a material's resistance to surface flame spread, enabling designers to select materials that **delay fire propagation**.

2. **SOLAS Compliance:** Under **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Reg. 6.1**, every finish material used in passenger ships and high-speed craft must pass the Surface Flammability Test. Without a valid **Type Approval Certificate** based on the FTP Code test, no material can be legally installed.

3. **Life Safety in Evacuation:** Ships are confined environments with **limited egress routes**. A delay in flame spread of even **5–10 minutes** can be the difference between successful evacuation and a major disaster. The test ensures materials contribute positively to this delay.

4. **Insurance and Classification:** The test data is referenced by classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas) and insurance underwriters for **risk assessment** and **vessel certification**.

**Role of CHF in Material Selection:**

- The **higher the CHF, the better the material's resistance** to flame spread under radiative heating.
- Designers select materials based on the **location of use**:
  - **Escape routes (corridors, stairways):** Materials with CHF **≥ 7.0 kW/m²**
  - **Public spaces (cabins, lounges):** Materials with CHF **≥ 4.5 kW/m²**
  - **Service areas:** Materials with CHF **≥ 1.5 kW/m²**
- CHF guides **trade-off decisions** between cost, weight, aesthetics, and fire safety.

*(Marks distribution: 2 for fire propagation, 2 for SOLAS, 1 for life safety, 1 for insurance, 1 for CHF role summary)*

#### *(b)* Two surface materials are being evaluated for a ship's public lounge:
- **Material A:** CHF = 5.2 kW/m², Smoke Production Index = 35
- **Material B:** CHF = 4.6 kW/m², Smoke Production Index = 12

Discuss the suitability of each material, citing relevant regulatory thresholds and engineering trade-offs. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Recall SOLAS/FTP Code Thresholds for public lounges:**

- Required **minimum CHF: ≥ 4.5 kW/m²** for public spaces.

**Step 2 — Evaluate Material A:**

- CHF = **5.2 kW/m²** → **Meets** the threshold (5.2 > 4.5) ✓
- Smoke Production Index (SPI) = **35** → Relatively high smoke production
- **Pros:** Adequate flame spread resistance, larger safety margin (0.7 kW/m² above threshold)
- **Cons:** Generates more smoke, which can **reduce visibility** during evacuation and increase **toxicity risk** in confined ship spaces.

**Step 3 — Evaluate Material B:**

- CHF = **4.6 kW/m²** → **Just meets** the threshold (4.6 > 4.5) ✓
- Smoke Production Index (SPI) = **12** → Low smoke production
- **Pros:** Excellent smoke characteristics, low toxicity, good visibility during evacuation
- **Cons:** Very small safety margin (only 0.1 kW/m² above threshold) — risky if material batches vary.

**Step 4 — Engineering Trade-Off Analysis:**

| Criterion | Material A | Material B |
|-----------|------------|------------|
| Flame spread resistance | Good (5.2 kW/m²) | Marginal (4.6 kW/m²) |
| Smoke production | High (35) | Low (12) |
| Safety margin | Large (0.7 kW/m²) | Very small (0.1 kW/m²) |
| Toxicity concern | Higher | Lower |
| Overall suitability for lounge | Acceptable but review smoke data | Acceptable but tight margin |

**Step 5 — Recommendation:**

- **Material A is recommended** for the lounge, despite the higher smoke index, because its **substantial safety margin** on CHF (0.7 kW/m² above threshold) provides robustness against batch variation and accidental damage. Material B is borderline and should only be considered if smoke toxicity is a critical concern (e.g., a lounge with sleeping berths).
- In practice, **Material A would also be subject to additional smoke and toxicity tests** as required by the FTP Code to verify overall fire safety compliance.

*(Marks distribution: 1 for threshold recall, 2 for Material A analysis, 2 for Material B analysis, 1 for comparison, 1 for justified recommendation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Marks Are Lost:**
> 1. **Confusing CHF with HRR:** Critical Heat Flux is a *threshold*, not a *rate*. Do not write "CHF = 5 kW" — the correct unit is **kW/m²**.
> 2. **Forgetting the 60-second rule:** CHF is defined only by the **60-second sustained burning** criterion. Sustained burning for 30 seconds does NOT qualify.
> 3. **Missing the resolution:** Always cite **IMO Resolution A.653(16)** or **FTP Code Part 5** for full credit in definition questions.
> 4. **Wrong threshold recall:** SOLAS corridors require CHF ≥ 7.0 kW/m², NOT 3.5 kW/m² (which is a commonly cited but incorrect value from older literature).
> 5. **Arithmetic error in exponent:** A common mistake is writing $e^{-kx}$ as $e^{kx}$ — pay close attention to the negative sign.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 **Test Name:** Surface Flammability Test (SFT), governed by **IMO FTP Code Part 5** (formerly Res. A.653(16)).
- 🔹 **Specimen Size:** 155 mm × 800 mm, mounted at **30°** to a vertical radiant panel.
- 🔹 **Radiant Panel:** Porous refractory, gas-fired, surface temperature ~**890 K**.
- 🔹 **Pilot Flame Speed:** **10 mm/s** horizontal sweep.
- 🔹 **CHF Criterion:** Heat flux at the position where sustained flaming lasts **≥ 60 s**.
- 🔹 **CHF Unit:** **kW/m²** (not kW — this is a flux, not a power).
- 🔹 **Heat Flux Distribution:** $q''(x) = q''_0 \cdot e^{-kx}$ is the standard empirical form.
- 🔹 **SOLAS Thresholds (most cited):**
  - **Corridors/stairways: ≥ 7.0 kW/m²**
  - **Public spaces: ≥ 4.5 kW/m²**
  - **Service areas: ≥ 1.5 kW/m²**
- 🔹 **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴ — memorize this.
- 🔹 **Total Heat Flux:** $q'' = q''_{rad} + q''_{conv}$ — radiative plus convective components.
- 🔹 **Test Outcome:** Result is a single CHF value, used to classify the material per SOLAS Reg. II-2/6.1.
- 🔹 **Material Class "Low Flame Spread":** CHF ≥ 7.0 kW/m² — this phrase appears verbatim in SOLAS and is **exam-favorite vocabulary**.
- 🔹 **Differentiation Point:** SFT is a **reaction-to-fire test** (it tests the material itself), not a **fire-resistance test** (which tests structural elements like bulkheads under standard fire exposure).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test for Vertically Supported Textiles and Films
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Test for Vertically Supported Textiles and Films

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Test for Vertically Supported Textiles and Films (TVSTF)** is a standardized mechanical impact test used to determine the **impact resistance** (also called **puncture resistance** or **dart-drop impact strength**) of thin, flexible sheet materials — typically **plastic films, laminated textiles, coated fabrics, geotextiles, and technical textiles** — by dropping a free-falling dart (striker) onto a specimen that is clamped rigidly in a **vertical orientation** within a circular specimen holder.

This test is governed by international standards:
- **ISO 7765-1:2022** — Plastics — Film and sheeting — Determination of impact resistance by the free-falling dart method (Staircase method)
- **ISO 7765-2:2022** — Instrumented impact test
- **ASTM D1709-22** — Standard Test Methods for Impact Resistance of Plastic Film by the Free-Falling Dart Method
- **ASTM D4272-15** — High-Speed Puncture Properties of Plastic Film

> [!NOTE]
> The phrase *"vertically supported"* is the operational keyword: the specimen is held **perpendicular to gravity** (not horizontal), so the dart's **potential energy converts entirely into kinetic energy** along the impact axis without any oblique or tangential losses.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are testing a **shower curtain** (which is a thin plastic film) to see how strong it is. You stretch it tightly over a circular ring (like a tightened embroidery hoop) and then drop a steel ball bearing from various heights onto it. The curtain tears at some point, and you record the exact combination of **ball weight × drop height** that causes failure. That is essentially what this test does — but with extreme precision, calibrated darts, and standardized geometry.

> **Maritime Application:** When a ship's tarpaulin billows in a storm, or a life-raft canopy is struck by a falling object at sea, the material must resist sudden localized impact. The TVSTF simulates this *sudden concentrated impact event* in the laboratory using controlled energy levels.

---

## 1.3 Why This Test Matters in Maritime Safety

| Maritime Component | Material Tested | Why TVSTF Is Used |
|---|---|---|
| Cargo tarpaulins | Coated woven PP/PE | Resist tearing from sharp edges during transit |
| Life-raft canopies | PU/Nylon laminates | Survive impact during inflation & deployment |
| Sailcloth | Dacron/Aramid | Resist impact from flogging, water impact |
| Inflatable boat hulls | Hypalon/PVC-coated fabric | Withstand collision with floating debris |
| Survival suit fabrics | Neoprene laminates | Resist puncture by ice, rocks, or vessel edges |

---

## 1.4 Key Standardized Metrics (Memorize These!)

- **Impact Failure Weight (IFW)** or **F50** — the dart mass (g) at which **50 % of specimens fail**, in grams.
- **Impact Energy (E)** — the kinetic energy of the dart at impact, in **Joules (J)**.
- **Drop Height (h)** — fixed at **0.66 m ± 0.01 m** in standard ISO 7765 Method A.
- **Specimen Clamp Diameter** — typically **127 mm (5 inches)** internal diameter.
- **Standard Atmosphere** — **23 °C ± 2 °C** and **50 % ± 5 % RH**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics Behind the Test

The fundamental principle is **conservation of energy in a free-falling body**, modified by the energy absorbed by the specimen.

When a dart of mass **m** is released from rest at height **h** above the specimen:

1. **Gravitational Potential Energy** at release point: $E_p = mgh$
2. This converts entirely to **Kinetic Energy** just before impact: $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$
3. By **energy conservation** (ignoring air resistance): $E_p = E_k$
4. At impact, part of this energy is **absorbed by the specimen** (elastic + plastic deformation + tearing work). If the absorbed energy ≥ critical tearing energy → **specimen fails**.

---

## 2.2 The Three Standard Methods Compared

### 🔹 Method A — Variable Height / Constant Mass (Staircase / Up-and-Down Method)
- Dart mass is **fixed**.
- Drop height is **increased or decreased in fixed increments** (typically 50 mm or 25 mm) based on whether the previous specimen passed or failed.
- Used to find the **mean failure height** → then **F50 weight** is reported.
- **ISO 7765-1** and **ASTM D1709 Method A**.

### 🔹 Method B — Constant Height / Variable Mass (Bruceton Staircase)
- Drop height is **fixed** (usually **660 mm**).
- Dart mass is **increased or decreased in fixed increments** until 50 % failure is bracketed.
- The result is reported as the **impact failure mass in grams**.
- **ASTM D1709 Method B** (most commonly cited).

### 🔹 Method C — Instrumented Impact Test
- A dart with an **integrated load cell and accelerometer** strikes the specimen.
- The **force–time** and **force–displacement** curves are recorded at high sampling rate.
- Total energy absorbed is computed by integration.
- **ISO 7765-2** and **ASTM D4272**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Impact velocity | $v = \sqrt{2gh}$ | m/s | $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ |
| 2 | Impact energy (max KE) | $E = mgh$ | J | $m$ in kg, $g$ in m/s², $h$ in m |
| 3 | Equivalent KE form | $E = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ | J | Used when velocity is measured directly |
| 4 | Energy absorbed (instrumented) | $E_{abs} = \int F \, dx$ | J | From force–displacement curve area |
| 5 | F50 (Bruceton) | $F_{50} = C + d \cdot \left(\frac{\Sigma i \cdot n_i}{N} + 0.5\right)$ | g | $C$ = lowest mass, $d$ = increment, $n_i$ = counts |
| 6 | Mean failure height | $\bar{h} = h_0 + d\left(\frac{A}{N} \pm 0.5\right)$ | mm | $A$ = total of failure counts weighted |
| 7 | Coefficient of variation | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\mu} \times 100\,\%$ | % | Used to check data scatter |
| 8 | Puncture strength (force) | $F_p = m(g + a)$ | N | If acceleration $a$ is known |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Tip for KTU Board Exams:** When asked for impact energy, **always show the substitution step with units**:
> $E = mgh = (\text{mass in kg}) \times 9.81 \times (\text{height in m}) \rightarrow \text{Joules}$
> This is where students lose marks — by skipping unit conversions.

---

## 2.4 Failure Modes of the Specimen

The dart can fail the specimen by one (or a combination) of the following mechanisms:

1. **Puncture** — dart tip pierces through the film (most common in thin films).
2. **Tear Propagation** — once a small puncture starts, tear energy propagates radially (dominant in woven textiles).
3. **Stretching-to-Break** — high-elongation films deform plastically before rupturing.
4. **Yarn Slippage** — in woven textiles, yarns slip past each other before the dart penetrates.
5. **Delamination** — in coated/laminated fabrics, layers separate before penetration.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quality Control (QC)** in production lines of polymer films (PE, PP, PET).
- **Material Selection** for protective packaging, geomembranes, and inflatable marine equipment.
- **Comparative Benchmarking** between alternative materials (e.g., HDPE vs. LDPE films for cargo wrapping).
- **Failure Analysis** to investigate in-service failures (e.g., a torn ship-side tarpaulin).
- **Regulatory Compliance** for IMO/MSC-tested maritime equipment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Impact Velocity and Kinetic Energy of the Dart

### Step 1 — Start with Newton's Kinematic Equation
For a body released from rest and falling freely under gravity through height $h$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v^2 &= u^2 + 2gh \\
\text{Initial velocity } u &= 0 \quad \text{(dart released from rest)} \\
\therefore v^2 &= 0 + 2gh \\
v &= \sqrt{2gh}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Express Kinetic Energy
By definition, kinetic energy is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2 \\
\text{Substitute } v^2 &= 2gh \text{ from Step 1} \\
E &= \tfrac{1}{2}m(2gh) \\
E &= mgh
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Physical Interpretation
This shows that the **maximum kinetic energy of the dart equals the gravitational potential energy it loses** during the fall. This is the energy *available* to fail the specimen.

> **Sanity Check:** For a 1 kg dart falling 0.66 m:
> $E = 1 \times 9.81 \times 0.66 = 6.47 \text{ J}$ → which is approximately the energy of a tennis ball dropped from waist height.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Calculation of F50 (Bruceton Staircase Method)

The Bruceton method is the most commonly tested concept in KTU exams. Let us derive the F50 formula step by step.

### Step 1 — Set Up the Staircase
Let the lowest dart mass tested be $C$ grams, and the mass increment be $d$ grams. Test $N$ specimens in total. Define $n_i$ as the number of failures at the $i^{th}$ level above the lowest.

Define two auxiliary sums:
- $A$ = weighted total of failures = $\sum i \cdot n_i$
- $N$ = total number of specimens tested

### Step 2 — Apply the Bruceton Estimator
The F50 (mean failure mass) is the dart mass at which 50 % of specimens fail:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{50} &= C + d \cdot \left[\left(\frac{A}{N}\right) + 0.5\right] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The "+0.5" term corrects for the **discrete nature** of the data (so that F50 falls mid-interval, not at the lower boundary).

### Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example
**Problem:** A staircase test was performed on a marine tarpaulin film using the following data:
- Lowest dart mass $C = 200 \text{ g}$
- Increment $d = 25 \text{ g}$
- Failures at level 0 (200 g): 2
- Failures at level 1 (225 g): 3
- Failures at level 2 (250 g): 5
- Passes at level 3 (275 g): 4
- Total specimens $N = 20$ (10 failures + 10 passes)

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= (0 \times 2) + (1 \times 3) + (2 \times 5) + (3 \times 0) \\
A &= 0 + 3 + 10 + 0 = 13 \\
F_{50} &= 200 + 25 \times \left[\left(\frac{13}{20}\right) + 0.5\right] \\
F_{50} &= 200 + 25 \times (0.65 + 0.5) \\
F_{50} &= 200 + 25 \times 1.15 \\
F_{50} &= 200 + 28.75 \\
F_{50} &= 228.75 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** 50 % of the tarpaulin specimens will fail when impacted by a **228.75 g dart** dropped from the standard height of 660 mm.

### Step 4 — Convert F50 to Energy

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{F50} &= m_{F50} \cdot g \cdot h \\
E_{F50} &= 0.22875 \text{ kg} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 \times 0.66 \text{ m} \\
E_{F50} &= 1.481 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Absorbed in Instrumented Test

In **Method C (ISO 7765-2)**, the dart has a force sensor. The data yields a **force vs. displacement curve**. The energy absorbed is the area under the curve.

### Step 1 — Work–Energy Theorem
Work done on specimen $W = \int F \cdot dx$

This work equals the energy absorbed by the specimen:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{abs} &= \int_{0}^{x_f} F(x) \, dx
\end{aligned}
$$

where $x_f$ is the displacement at failure (puncture point).

### Step 2 — Trapezoidal Approximation (Used in Lab)
If the data is recorded at discrete intervals $x_0, x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{abs} &\approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\frac{F_{i-1} + F_i}{2}\right)(x_i - x_{i-1})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Energy Efficiency Ratio
A useful metric to evaluate the material's toughness:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{E_{abs}}{E_{input}} = \frac{E_{abs}}{mgh}
\end{aligned}
$$

> A high $\eta$ means the material absorbs a large fraction of the impact energy (good for marine applications like life-raft floors).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Which Method?

| Scenario | Recommended Method | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Routine QC in film production | Method B (ASTM D1709) | Fast, simple, no instrumentation |
| Research-grade data on new laminate | Method C (ISO 7765-2) | Gives full force–displacement profile |
| Testing thin films (< 25 µm) | Method A with small dart | Avoids over-penetration errors |
| Maritime tarpaulin supplier audit | Method B + reporting E in J | Standardized for procurement specs |
| Comparative study of HDPE vs. LDPE | Method C | Energy efficiency $\eta$ comparison |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Test Apparatus

The following Mermaid flowchart shows the **functional architecture** of a standard dart-drop impact tester (a physical drawing would require CAD; the functional block diagram below conveys all critical interactions for exam purposes).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Dart Mass Selection Module] --> B[Release Clamp Mechanism]
    B --> C{Pneumatic or Manual Release}

    I --> K[Report: E in Joules]

    L --> N[Force-Displacement Curve]
```

> The **red block (F)** marks the *impact event* — the critical energy-transfer node. The **green block (O)** shows the *instrumented analysis path* specific to Method C.

---

## 4.2 Sequence of Operations in a Staircase Test

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant T as Tester
    participant D as Dart
    participant S as Specimen
    participant R as Result Recorder

    T->>D: Load dart with mass m0
    T->>D: Set drop height h
    T->>D: Release
    D->>S: Strikes specimen
    S-->>R: Pass / Fail
    R->>T: If Pass: increase height (or mass) for next test
    R->>T: If Fail: decrease height (or mass) for next test
    T->>D: Repeat for N=20 specimens
    R->>R: Compute F50 using Bruceton formula
    R->>T: Report F50 and Impact Energy
```

---

## 4.3 Anatomy of the Specimen Clamp Assembly

```mermaid

    A[Upper Clamp Ring] --> B[Specimen Film or Textile]
    B --> C[Lower Clamp Ring]
    C --> D[Pneumatic or Mechanical Clamp Force]
    A --> D
    B --> E[Unsupported Test Area: 127 mm diameter]
    E --> F[Impact Point: Geometric Center]
    F --> G[Dart Tip: Hemispherical, 38.1 mm diameter]
    G --> H[Striking Velocity: sqrt of 2gh]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key geometric note for KTU exams:** The **unsupported test diameter is 127 mm (5 in)** and the **dart tip diameter is 38.1 mm (1.5 in)**. These are standard values from **ASTM D1709** and must be quoted correctly.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define "Impact Failure Weight (F50)" as used in the dart-drop test for vertically supported films. State its unit and the standard test method that uses it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Impact Failure Weight (F50)** is defined as the **dart mass (in grams) at which 50 % of the vertically clamped specimens fail** under a standard dart drop impact.

- **Unit:** grams (g), although it can be converted to **impact energy in Joules (J)** using $E = mgh$.
- **Standard Test Method:** It is most commonly associated with **ASTM D1709 Method B** (constant height, variable mass — Bruceton staircase) and is also reported in **ISO 7765-1**.
- **Typical Procedure:** The dart mass is varied in fixed increments (e.g., 25 g), and the test continues for at least 20 specimens, alternating up/down based on pass/fail to bracket the F50 value.

> **Marking Scheme (3 marks):** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for unit, 1 mark for standard reference.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List any three failure modes that can occur in a textile/film specimen during a vertical dart-drop test.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Puncture Failure** — the dart tip pierces through the film without significant lateral tearing (typical of thin plastic films).
2. **Tear Propagation** — once a small initial puncture forms, a tear propagates radially outward due to stored elastic energy (typical of woven textiles).
3. **Stretch-to-Break** — the film undergoes extensive plastic deformation and thinning before rupture (typical of LDPE and other highly ductile films).
4. *(Optional 4th, for extra credit)* **Yarn Slippage** — in woven textiles, warp and weft yarns shift relative to each other before the dart fully penetrates.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Module carries a 14-mark question with internal choice (a) OR (b). Each sub-part = 7 marks.

---

### **Q3(a).** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`
**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **ASTM D1709 Method B** procedure for testing impact resistance of vertically supported plastic films. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dart of mass **350 g** is dropped from a height of **0.66 m** onto a vertically clamped polymer film. Calculate:
- (i) the **impact velocity** at the moment of striking,
- (ii) the **impact energy**, and
- (iii) the **height from which a 500 g dart must be dropped to deliver the same impact energy**. **(7 marks)**

#### 🔹 Model Solution for Q3(a) — 7 marks

**Procedure of ASTM D1709 Method B:**

1. **Specimen Preparation:** Cut at least **20 specimens** (typically 20 cm × 20 cm) of the film. Condition them for **40 hours** at 23 °C and 50 % RH as per ISO 23529. *(1 mark)*

2. **Clamping:** Clamp the specimen **vertically** between two circular rings of **127 mm internal diameter** so that the unsupported test area is taut and wrinkle-free. *(1 mark)*

3. **Dart Loading:** Load the dart (hemispherical tip of **38.1 mm diameter**) with an initial mass (e.g., 100–300 g) and clamp it at a **fixed drop height of 0.66 m** above the specimen. *(1 mark)*

4. **Release and Observation:** Release the dart. Observe whether the specimen **passes (no failure)** or **fails (visible tear/puncture)**. *(1 mark)*

5. **Staircase Progression:**
   - If the specimen **passes** → **increase** dart mass by one increment (e.g., +25 g) for the next test.
   - If the specimen **fails** → **decrease** dart mass by the same increment for the next test.
   - Continue this staircase for 20 specimens. *(1 mark)*

6. **Calculation of F50:** Apply the Bruceton formula:
   $F_{50} = C + d \cdot \left[\dfrac{A}{N} + 0.5\right]$
   *(1 mark)*

7. **Report:** Report the **F50 in grams** along with the **standard deviation** and the **test conditions** (temperature, humidity, drop height, dart diameter). *(1 mark)*

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution for Q3(b) — 7 marks

**Given Data:**
- Dart mass $m_1 = 350 \text{ g} = 0.350 \text{ kg}$
- Drop height $h_1 = 0.66 \text{ m}$
- Acceleration due to gravity $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

---

**(i) Impact Velocity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \sqrt{2gh_1} \\
v &= \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.66} \\
v &= \sqrt{12.9492} \\
v &= 3.598 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{(rounded to 3 decimal places)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks)*

---

**(ii) Impact Energy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= m_1 \cdot g \cdot h_1 \\
E &= 0.350 \times 9.81 \times 0.66 \\
E &= 2.266 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks)*

> **Cross-check using KE formula:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2 = 0.5 \times 0.350 \times (3.598)^2 = 0.5 \times 0.350 \times 12.946 = 2.266$ J ✓ *(Matches!)*

---

**(iii) New drop height for 500 g dart to deliver same energy:**

We require $E = m_2 \cdot g \cdot h_2 = 2.266$ J
where $m_2 = 500 \text{ g} = 0.500 \text{ kg}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_2 &= \frac{E}{m_2 \cdot g} \\
h_2 &= \frac{2.266}{0.500 \times 9.81} \\
h_2 &= \frac{2.266}{4.905} \\
h_2 &= 0.462 \text{ m} \quad \text{(or 462 mm)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(3 marks)*

---

### **Q3(b) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** Differentiate between **ASTM D1709 Method A** and **ASTM D1709 Method B**. State one application of each in maritime safety. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A staircase test on a marine life-raft canopy laminate gave the following data:
- Lowest dart mass $C = 150 \text{ g}$
- Increment $d = 20 \text{ g}$
- Failures: level 0 → 3 specimens, level 1 → 4 specimens, level 2 → 3 specimens
- Passes: level 3 → 6 specimens
- Drop height $h = 0.66 \text{ m}$

Calculate the **F50 value** and the **corresponding impact energy in Joules**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution for Q3(b)-Alt Part (a) — 7 marks

| Aspect | Method A (Staircase / Variable Height) | Method B (Bruceton / Variable Mass) |
|---|---|---|
| Variable parameter | Drop height | Dart mass |
| Fixed parameter | Dart mass | Drop height (0.66 m) |
| Result reported | Mean failure height (mm) | Impact failure mass F50 (g) |
| Specimens needed | ≥ 20 | ≥ 20 |
| Standard reference | ASTM D1709 Method A | ASTM D1709 Method B |
| Maritime application | Testing **sailcloth** for varying wind-load equivalents | Testing **life-raft canopy films** for fixed deployment-impact equivalent |

*(4 marks for table, 3 marks for maritime application explanation)*

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution for Q3(b)-Alt Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Total specimens:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \text{failures} + \text{passes} \\
N &= (3 + 4 + 3) + 6 \\
N &= 10 + 6 = 16
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $A = \sum i \cdot n_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= (0 \times 3) + (1 \times 4) + (2 \times 3) + (3 \times 0) \\
A &= 0 + 4 + 6 + 0 = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bruceton formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{50} &= C + d \cdot \left[\frac{A}{N} + 0.5\right] \\
F_{50} &= 150 + 20 \cdot \left[\frac{10}{16} + 0.5\right] \\
F_{50} &= 150 + 20 \cdot (0.625 + 0.5) \\
F_{50} &= 150 + 20 \cdot 1.125 \\
F_{50} &= 150 + 22.5 \\
F_{50} &= 172.5 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(4 marks for F50 calculation)*

**Step 4 — Convert F50 to Impact Energy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= m \cdot g \cdot h \\
E &= 0.1725 \text{ kg} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 \times 0.66 \text{ m} \\
E &= 1.117 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(3 marks for energy calculation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Mistake:** Students often forget to convert grams to kilograms before using $E = mgh$. A dart of 350 g used as 350 (without conversion) gives a 1000× wrong answer. **Always write m in kg.**
> 2. **F50 Misinterpretation:** Some students confuse F50 with the "lowest failing mass." It is the **statistically interpolated mass for 50% failure probability**, not a single test result.
> 3. **Forgot the "+0.5" correction** in the Bruceton formula — this is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Mixing up Method A and Method B** — examiners test this. Remember: **A = "Adjust height" (variable height), B = "Beef up mass" (variable mass).**
> 5. **Skipping conditioning details** (23 °C, 50 % RH) in procedure questions — this costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Not sketching the setup** when asked "with neat sketches" — diagrams are worth 1–2 marks and frequently missed.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- [x] The test is governed by **ISO 7765** and **ASTM D1709**.
- [x] Specimen is **vertically clamped** in a ring of **127 mm** internal diameter.
- [x] Standard drop height = **0.66 m**; standard dart tip diameter = **38.1 mm**.
- [x] **Conditioning:** 23 °C, 50 % RH, ≥ 40 h.
- [x] **Method A** = variable height; **Method B** = variable mass; **Method C** = instrumented.
- [x] **Impact velocity:** $v = \sqrt{2gh}$
- [x] **Impact energy:** $E = mgh = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$
- [x] **F50 (Bruceton):** $F_{50} = C + d\left[\dfrac{A}{N} + 0.5\right]$
- [x] **Energy absorbed (Method C):** $E_{abs} = \int F \, dx$
- [x] Failure modes: puncture, tear propagation, stretch-to-break, yarn slippage, delamination.
- [x] Maritime applications: tarpaulins, life-raft canopies, sailcloth, inflatable boat hulls, survival suit fabrics.
- [x] **Always convert grams to kilograms** in energy calculations.
- [x] **Always show the "+0.5"** correction in the Bruceton formula.
- [x] The "+0.5" arises from the **continuity correction** for discrete staircase data.
- [x] **Energy efficiency ratio** $\eta = E_{abs}/E_{input}$ is a key toughness metric.
- [x] **N ≥ 20 specimens** is the standard minimum sample size.
- [x] Air resistance is **ignored** in standard test calculations (assumed negligible for low drop heights).
- [x] The dart's **hemispherical tip** simulates a localized point impact (worst-case scenario).

---

> ✅ **End of Section 5 — Module 2: Test for Vertically Supported Textiles and Films**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme — NEP 2020 Aligned*

topics.title: Test for Upholstered Furniture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Test for Upholstered Furniture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Upholstered Furniture (IMO FTP Code Definition):** Any product, piece of furniture, or component thereof, in which a flexible covering material is combined with a filling material to provide seating, bedding, or similar functional support onboard ships.

In the maritime regulatory framework, the **Test for Upholstered Furniture** is mandated by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO) Fire Test Procedures (FTP) Code — Part 8**, which formally replaced the older **IMO Resolution A.652(16)** under the SOLAS Convention Chapter II-2 (Fire Safety). The procedure evaluates the fire behaviour of complete items of upholstered furniture — or representative mock-ups — when exposed to two distinct ignition threats:
1. **A smouldering source** (a standard non-filtered burning cigarette)
2. **A small open flame source** (a butane gas burner flame)

The principal objective is to verify that the assembly of **cover fabric**, **filling material (foam)**, and any **interliner/barrier fabric** does not support sustained flaming, progressive smouldering, or surface flashover, thereby limiting fire spread in passenger and crew accommodation areas during the critical **post-flashover escape window** of approximately **10–15 minutes**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Tag:** This topic directly addresses **CO2 — Fire Test Procedures for Shipboard Materials** and maps to the Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive level **L3 (Apply)** and **L4 (Analyze)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an upholstered seat in a ship's cabin as a **fire sandwich** — three combustible layers stacked together:
- The **bread top** = decorative cover fabric (cotton, polyester, wool, leather)
- The **filling** = flexible foam (polyurethane is most common — highly flammable when untreated)
- The **bread bottom** = scrim or backing fabric (often ignored, but burns too)

If you held a burning cigarette against this sandwich in a real cabin, the foam underneath could quietly **char and smoulder for hours** (the "invisible fire" that later erupts when the crew is asleep). The IMO test is essentially asking: *"Will this sandwich self-extinguish, or will it turn into a slow-burning ember bomb?"*

The **cigarette test** simulates a careless smoker; the **butane burner test** simulates a small accidental flame (lighter, match). Together they bound the **worst-realistic ignition scenarios** before full-room flashover occurs.

### 1.3 Scope of Application

| Application Area | Applicable Standard | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Passenger ship cabin seats | IMO FTP Code Part 8 | Mandatory under SOLAS Reg. II-2/3.40 |
| Crew accommodation berths | IMO FTP Code Part 8 | All sleeping furniture |
| Public area sofas/chairs | IMO FTP Code Part 8 | Including lounges, mess rooms |
| Outdoor deck furniture | **Excluded** | Covered by furniture-specific regulations |
| Structural built-in units | Part 8 mock-up test | Only when padded/cushioned |
| Wheelhouse operator seats | Part 8 + Part 9 | Additional oil/smoke criteria |

### 1.4 Key Physical Metrics (Bold Constants)

- **Cigarette mass:** **0.95 g ± 0.05 g** (standard non-filter cigarette, length ~ 85 mm, circumference ~ 25 mm)
- **Butane burner flame height:** **35 mm ± 3 mm** above the burner tip
- **Butane flame application time:** **20 s** (precisely)
- **Cigarette observation period:** **1 hour** from placement
- **Butane flame observation period:** **≥ 2 minutes** (120 s) after flame removal
- **Ambient test chamber temperature:** **15 °C to 30 °C**
- **Relative humidity:** **20 % to 70 %**
- **Specimen conditioning:** ≥ **24 hours** at standard atmosphere (23 °C ± 2 °C, 50 % ± 5 % RH)
- **Mock-up support platform:** Perforated steel with **12 mm square holes** on **20 mm centres** to simulate real ventilation conditions beneath furniture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Concept — Mock-up Principle:** Testing the *complete* upholstered article (rather than only the fabric or foam alone) is the *defining feature* of Part 8. The fire performance of an assembly is **not equal to the sum of its parts** — a flame-retardant cover fabric over a non-FR foam will still fail if the foam smoulders. Hence Part 8 always tests the **integrated assembly**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Fire Triangle Applied to Upholstered Furniture

Upholstered furniture fires begin when the classical **Fire Triangle** is satisfied:

$$
\text{Fire} = f(\text{Fuel},\ \text{Oxidiser (O}_2\text{)},\ \text{Ignition Energy})
$$

In a ship's accommodation:
- **Fuel** = cover fabric + filling foam (often polyurethane, heat release rate ~ 350–500 kW/m²)
- **Oxidiser** = cabin air (≈ 21 % O₂)
- **Ignition Energy** = cigarette (~ 5–10 W smouldering) or butane flame (~ 80–100 W convective)

The Part 8 test deliberately **supplies the ignition energy** and observes whether the fuel–oxidiser system reaches *sustained flaming combustion*. The critical parameter is **Time to Sustained Flaming (TSF)**:

$$
\text{TSF} = t_{\text{ignition application}} + t_{\text{flame persistence}}
$$

The pass criterion for the butane burner test:
$$
t_{\text{flame persistence}} \leq 120\ \text{s after flame removal} \quad \text{(per IMO FTP Code Part 8, para 3.3)}
$$

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

#### Stage A — Specimen Preparation

1. Select the **complete article of furniture** OR construct a **representative mock-up**.
2. A mock-up must contain the **same cover fabric, same filling material, same seam geometry, and same interliner** as the production item.
3. Conditioning: minimum **24 hours** at 23 °C ± 2 °C and 50 % ± 5 % RH.
4. Three specimens are typically prepared: one for cigarette test, one for butane burner test, one spare.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three specimens?** Each ignition source physically damages the material. Reusing a single specimen would invalidate the test because the cover fabric is compromised after the first exposure.

#### Stage B — Cigarette (Smouldering) Test Procedure

| Step | Action | Duration | Pass Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Place cigarette on horizontal seating surface along the junction of seat & back (worst-case geometry) | — | — |
| 2 | Ignite cigarette, ensure steady smouldering before placement | — | — |
| 3 | Allow cigarette to burn down **completely** | 5–10 min | — |
| 4 | Observe for progressive smouldering & flaming | **1 hour** | — |
| 5 | Final dissection of specimen (cut open) | 30 min | No char > **50 mm** from cigarette origin |

**Pass Criteria (Cigarette):**
- No **flaming** at any time during the 1-hour observation.
- No **progressive smouldering** beyond the geometric extent allowed (typically char damage confined to within 50 mm of the cigarette application point in any direction).
- No **surface flash** propagating across the fabric.

#### Stage C — Butane Gas Burner (Small Flame) Test Procedure

| Step | Action | Duration | Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Position burner at corner or seat–back junction | — | Distance: 20 mm from surface |
| 2 | Apply butane flame | **20 s** | Flame height 35 mm |
| 3 | Remove burner, observe immediately | t = 0 | — |
| 4 | Monitor for sustained flaming | **≥ 120 s** | Sustained flaming allowed? **NO** |
| 5 | Monitor for surface flash | 120 s | Allowed? **NO** |
| 6 | Monitor for progressive smouldering | Up to 15 min post-extinction | Allowed beyond limits? **NO** |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick-Reference Table** — Memorise this for KTU Part B answers.

| Parameter / Concept | Symbol / Value | Unit / Standard | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cigarette mass | $m_c$ | **0.95 g ± 0.05 g** | Smouldering source calibration |
| Cigarette burn rate | $\dot{m}_c$ | ~ 0.08 g/min | Sustained heat output |
| Butane flame height | $h_f$ | **35 mm ± 3 mm** | Small open flame calibration |
| Butane flow rate | $\dot{V}_{butane}$ | ~ 40 mL/min | Heat release ~ 85 W |
| Flame application time | $t_{app}$ | **20 s** | Burner contact duration |
| Post-flame observation | $t_{obs,flame}$ | **120 s minimum** | Sustained flaming window |
| Cigarette observation | $t_{obs,cig}$ | **1 hour** | Smoulder propagation window |
| Permitted char radius | $r_{char,max}$ | **≤ 50 mm** | Pass threshold |
| Conditioning temp. | $T_{cond}$ | 23 °C ± 2 °C | ISO 554 atmosphere |
| Conditioning RH | $\phi_{cond}$ | 50 % ± 5 % | Equilibrium moisture content |
| Conditioning duration | $t_{cond}$ | ≥ **24 h** | Sorption equilibrium |
| Mock-up grid aperture | — | 12 mm × 12 mm | Sub-ventilation simulation |
| Maximum $t_{\text{flame persistence}}$ | — | **120 s** | Hard pass/fail criterion |
| Surface flash propagation | — | Prohibited | One-way flame spread |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Field

The Part 8 test methodology influences:
- **Material selection** by shipyards (FR-PU foams, wool-rich cover fabrics, aramid interliners like Nomex/Kevlar).
- **Cost–safety trade-off** in cabin outfitting procurement.
- **Type approval certification** by classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, BV).
- **Forensic fire investigation** — determining whether a fire originated in a non-compliant upholstered seat.
- **Risk-based inspection** intervals during the ship's operational life under the **Enhanced Survey Programme (ESP)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world connection:** In the 2018 *MV Conception* dive boat fire (California), non-compliant upholstered mattresses were cited as primary contributors to the rapid fire spread that killed 34 people. Modern IMO-aligned testing exists precisely to prevent such tragedies at sea.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Critical Heat Flux Criterion (Conceptual)

The Part 8 butane flame delivers a convective heat flux to the cover fabric. The local heat flux at the burner-to-fabric interface can be modelled as:

$$
\dot{q}'' = \epsilon \sigma (T_{flame}^4 - T_{fabric}^4) + h_c (T_{flame} - T_{fabric})
$$

For a butane diffusion flame at $T_{flame} \approx 1700\ \text{K}$ and a fabric surface initially at $T_{fabric} = 296\ \text{K}$ (23 °C):

$$
\dot{q}'' \approx \epsilon \sigma T_{flame}^4 + h_c (T_{flame} - T_{fabric})
$$

Substituting standard values ($\epsilon \approx 0.3$, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$, $h_c \approx 50\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{q}'' &\approx (0.3)(5.67 \times 10^{-8})(1700)^4 + (50)(1700 - 296) \\[4pt]
&\approx (0.3)(5.67 \times 10^{-8})(8.35 \times 10^{12}) + (50)(1404) \\[4pt]
&\approx (0.3)(473{,}500) + 70{,}200 \\[4pt]
&\approx 142{,}050 + 70{,}200 \\[4pt]
&\approx 212{,}250\ \text{W/m}^2 \approx 21.2\ \text{kW/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

This $\approx 21\ \text{kW/m}^2$ is **below** the critical heat flux for most flame-retardant cover fabrics ($\geq 25\ \text{kW/m}^2$) — explaining why FR-treated assemblies pass and untreated assemblies ignite within seconds.

### 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Permitted Char Extent

The Part 8 specification limits progressive smouldering to a **50 mm radius** from the ignition source. This value arises from empirical correlation with cabin geometry:

$$
r_{char,max} = \sqrt{\frac{A_{seat,human}}{2 \pi}} \approx \sqrt{\frac{0.20}{6.283}} \approx 0.178\ \text{m} \approx 178\ \text{mm}
$$

But the **IMO's stricter 50 mm threshold** is justified by:
$$
r_{char,allowed} = 0.05\ \text{m} \ll r_{smoulder,propagation}
$$

This conservative bound ensures that smouldering cannot traverse the full seat width (~ 500 mm) within the **standard 1-hour observation**, providing a **safety factor of approximately 10×** against full-seat involvement.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Pass/Fail Determination

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this exact matrix in your KTU exam answers.**

| Observed Phenomenon | Cigarette Test | Butane Burner Test | Overall Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| No flaming, no smoulder propagation beyond 50 mm | ✓ PASS | ✓ PASS | **CERTIFIED** |
| No flaming, smoulder reaches 60 mm | ✓ PASS | — | **CERTIFIED** (cigarette) |
| Brief flaming ≤ 5 s during 20-s application | — | ✗ FAIL | **REJECTED** |
| Flaming persists 130 s after burner removal | — | ✗ FAIL | **REJECTED** |
| Surface flash propagating > 100 mm | ✗ FAIL | ✗ FAIL | **REJECTED** |
| Flaming during 1-h cigarette observation | ✗ FAIL | — | **REJECTED** |

### 3.4 Worked Example Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample KTU-style calculation:** A mock-up sofa is tested under Part 8. After 20 s of butane flame exposure, flaming persists for **145 seconds** after flame removal. During dissection, a char zone of **62 mm radius** is observed in the seat-back cigarette specimen. Determine compliance.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Sub-test 1: Butane Burner} \quad & t_{flame} = 145\ \text{s} > 120\ \text{s} \\
& \Rightarrow \boxed{\text{FAIL — exceeds } 120\ \text{s criterion}} \\[6pt]
\textbf{Sub-test 2: Cigarette} \quad & r_{char} = 62\ \text{mm} > 50\ \text{mm} \\
& \Rightarrow \boxed{\text{FAIL — exceeds } 50\ \text{mm char criterion}} \\[6pt]
\textbf{Overall Verdict:} \quad & \boxed{\text{ASSEMBLY REJECTED — non-compliant with FTP Code Part 8}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Required corrective actions:**
1. Replace filling foam with **FMVSS-302 / BS 5852 Crib-5 compliant** PU foam.
2. Introduce **aramid interliner** (e.g., DuPont Nomex IIIA, areal density ~ 60–120 g/m²) between cover and foam.
3. Re-test using **fresh specimens** (cross-contamination invalidates prior results).
4. Document via **Type Approval Certificate** under IMO MSC/Circ.1002.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Part 8 Test Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Specimen Selection] --> B{Complete Article or Mock-up}
    B --> C[Conditioning Chamber]
    C --> D[Conditioning 24h at 23C 50%RH]
    D --> E[Test 1 Cigarette Smoulder Source]
    D --> F[Test 2 Butane Gas Burner Source]
    E --> G[1 Hour Observation Period]
    F --> H[120s Post-Flame Observation]
    G --> I{Dissection and Char Measurement}
    H --> J{Sustained Flaming Check}
    I --> K[Pass Criteria Char Radius 50mm]
    J --> L[Pass Criteria Flame Persistence 120s]
    K --> M{Certificate Issue Decision}
    L --> M
    M --> N[Compliant Type Approval Certificate]
    M --> O[Non-Compliant Rejection Report]
```

### 4.2 Schematic of the Butane Burner Test Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Butane Gas Cylinder Regulated] --> B[Flow Meter 40 mL per min]
    B --> C[Burner Tube Diameter 8 mm]
    C --> D[Flame Height 35 mm]
    D --> E[Specimen Surface]
    E --> F[Distance 20 mm Vertical Gap]
    F --> G[Application Timer 20 s]

```

### 4.3 Hierarchical Test Decision Logic

```mermaid

    A[Start Part 8 Test Sequence] --> B[Specimen 1 Cigarette Test]
    A --> C[Specimen 2 Butane Burner Test]
    B --> D{Flaming during 1 hour observation}
    D -->|Yes| E[REJECT Assembly]
    D -->|No| F{Progressive Smouldering beyond 50 mm}
    F -->|Yes| E
    F -->|No| G[Specimen 1 PASS]
    C --> H{Sustained Flaming greater than 120 s}
    H -->|Yes| E
    H -->|No| I{Surface Flash Observed}
    I -->|Yes| E
    I -->|No| J{Progressive Smouldering beyond 50 mm}
    J -->|Yes| E
    J -->|No| K[Specimen 2 PASS]

```

### 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schematic of an Upholstered Furniture Mock-up

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TopLayer [Top Assembly]
        A1[Cover Fabric Outer Face]

    end
    subgraph SupportSystem [Support Structure]
        B1[Perforated Steel Platform 12 mm holes]

    end

    A2 --> A3
```
