```mermaid
    A4 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**
*Define "upholstered furniture" as per the IMO FTP Code. List the **three combustible components** typically present in such furniture onboard ships.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** An item of furniture in which a flexible cover fabric is combined with a filling material (typically foam) to provide seating or bedding accommodation, evaluated for fire performance under **IMO FTP Code Part 8**.
>
> **Three combustible components (1 mark each):**
> 1. **Cover fabric** — outer decorative textile (cotton, polyester, wool, leather).
> 2. **Filling material** — flexible polyurethane (PU) foam or equivalent resilient padding.
> 3. **Interliner / backing scrim** — barrier fabric or backing textile beneath the cover.
>
> *(Optional credit: aramid barrier interliner such as Nomex counts as a fourth component when present.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**
*State the **two ignition sources** used in the FTP Code Part 8 test and the **observation period** associated with each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Ignition Source | Standard Specification | Observation Period |
|---|---|---|
| **Smouldering cigarette** (1 mark) | 0.95 g ± 0.05 g non-filter | **1 hour** (1 mark) |
| **Butane gas burner** (1 mark) | 35 mm flame, 20 s application | **≥ 120 s after flame removal** (1 mark) |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **procedure, apparatus, and acceptance criteria** of the *butane gas burner test* for upholstered furniture as specified by the IMO FTP Code Part 8.

**(b) [7 marks]** With the aid of a sketch, describe the **construction of a representative mock-up** for testing upholstered furniture. Why is it mandatory to test the *complete assembly* rather than the cover fabric and foam separately?

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Butane Gas Burner Test — Detailed Procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Apparatus** (2 marks):
   - Butane gas cylinder with **precision flow regulator** delivering **40 ± 2 mL/min**.
   - Burner tube of **8 mm inner diameter**, oriented vertically.
   - Calibrated to produce a flame of **35 mm ± 3 mm** height above the burner tip.
   - Specimen mounted on a **perforated steel platform** with 12 mm square apertures on 20 mm centres.

2. **Test Procedure** (3 marks):
   - Condition the specimen for **≥ 24 hours** at 23 °C ± 2 °C and 50 % ± 5 % RH.
   - Position the burner flame so that the **flame impinges on the seat-back junction** (worst-case crevice geometry).
   - Maintain **20 mm vertical distance** between burner tip and specimen surface.
   - Apply the flame for exactly **20 seconds**, then withdraw the burner.
   - Observe the specimen continuously for the next **120 seconds minimum**, monitoring for:
     - Sustained flaming
     - Surface flash (lateral flame spread across fabric)
     - Progressive smouldering

3. **Acceptance Criteria** (2 marks):
   - **No flaming** persisting more than **120 seconds** after the burner is removed.
   - **No surface flash** propagating across the cover fabric.
   - **No progressive smouldering** beyond the permitted 50 mm radius from the point of flame application (verified by dissection).
   - Any of the above → **REJECT**.

**(b) Mock-up Construction (7 marks):**

1. **Mock-up Anatomy** (3 marks): The mock-up must replicate the production furniture's layered structure:
   - Top layer: **Cover fabric** — same material, weight, weave, and chemical finish as the production article.
   - Middle layer: **Filling material** — same foam type, density (typically 25–55 kg/m³), and thickness as production.
   - Interliner (if used): same aramid or FR-barrier fabric, same areal density.
   - Backing scrim: same as production.
   - Mounted on a perforated steel platform simulating real cabin air circulation underneath.

2. **Why Complete Assembly Testing?** (4 marks):
   - **Synergistic fire behaviour**: A flame-retardant cover over a non-FR foam may pass fabric-only tests yet fail as an assembly because **conductive heat transfer** through the cover ignites the foam.
   - **Smouldering propagation** depends on the **cover-foam interface**, not on either alone.
   - **Seam and joint effects**: Real furniture has stitching, tufting buttons, and piping — these are ignition hot-spots absent in fabric-only tests.
   - **Regulatory intent (SOLAS)**: SOLAS Chapter II-2 requires protection of *people*, which is determined by **assembly-level fire performance**, not component performance.
   - **Empirical evidence**: Major ship fires (e.g., *MV Scandinavian Star*, 1990) revealed that **component-pass but assembly-fail** furniture was a primary cause of casualties.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare and contrast the **cigarette smouldering test** and the **butane gas burner test** under FTP Code Part 8 with respect to ignition source, exposure duration, observation period, and pass criteria.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **engineering significance** of the Part 8 test in modern ship design. How does the choice of cover fabric, filling foam, and interliner influence the fire performance of an upholstered assembly?

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Comparative Analysis (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Cigarette Test | Butane Burner Test |
|---|---|---|
| **Ignition source** (1 mark) | Smouldering cigarette (0.95 g ± 0.05 g) | Butane diffusion flame (35 mm height) |
| **Heat output** (1 mark) | ~ 5–10 W (sustained smoulder) | ~ 80–100 W (open flame) |
| **Exposure duration** (1 mark) | Until cigarette self-extinguishes (~ 5–10 min) | **20 seconds** precisely |
| **Observation period** (1 mark) | **1 hour** | **≥ 120 s** after flame removal |
| **Failure mode tested** (1 mark) | Progressive smouldering, char propagation | Sustained flaming, surface flash |
| **Pass criteria** (1 mark) | Char ≤ 50 mm radius, no flaming | Flame persistence ≤ 120 s, no flash |
| **Worst-case geometry** (1 mark) | Seat-back horizontal junction | Same — corner crevice |

**Key Distinction:** The cigarette test addresses **smouldering combustion** (low-temperature, slow), while the butane test addresses **flaming combustion** (high-temperature, fast). Together they bracket the **two principal pre-flashover fire regimes** in ship cabins.

**(b) Engineering Significance (7 marks):**

1. **Material Selection Influence** (3 marks):
   - **Cover fabric**: Wool-rich (> 50 % wool) or FR-treated polyester provides high Limiting Oxygen Index (LOI > 28 %). Cotton and untreated synthetics are avoided.
   - **Filling foam**: Combustion-modified high-resilience (CMHR) PU foam is the industry standard. Density 35–55 kg/m³, LOI > 24 % with FR additives.
   - **Interliner**: Aramid (Nomex, Kevlar) or FR-viscose interliners at 60–120 g/m² areal density create a thermal barrier that delays foam ignition by 60–120 seconds.

2. **Modern Ship Design Implications** (2 marks):
   - Enables **zoned fire protection**: Passengers have ≤ 40 m escape distance (Ro-Ro ferries) or 4-deck vertical escape (passenger ships) — Part 8 compliant furniture ensures the **escape route remains tenable** during evacuation.
   - Supports **goal-based SOLAS amendments** (Chapter II-2 Reg. 17) by providing measurable fire-performance metrics.

3. **Economic & Operational Impact** (2 marks):
   - **Type approval cost**: ~ ₹ 8–15 lakh per furniture model.
   - **Operational benefit**: Reduced insurance premiums (typically 5–12 % discount for fully FTP-compliant outfitting under IACS UR Z17).
   - **Crew training**: Fire-drill realism improves when furniture burns realistically but safely during onboard drills using approved mock-ups.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common error (–2 marks):** Students frequently confuse **FTP Code Part 8** (upholstered furniture) with **Part 5** (surface flammability) or **Part 7** (vertical flammability). Memorise the Part number matrix: Part 7 = vertical, Part 8 = upholstered, Part 9 = bedding components.
> - **Common error (–1 mark):** Writing **"flame persistence ≤ 5 minutes"** instead of **≤ 120 seconds**. The exact number **120 s** is a hard-coded IMO criterion.
> - **Common error (–2 marks):** Describing the cigarette as **"self-extinguishing in 1 hour"** — this is wrong. The cigarette burns out in ~ 5–10 minutes; the **observation period** is 1 hour.
> - **Common error (–1 mark):** Forgetting to mention **conditioning** (24 h, 23 °C, 50 % RH). This procedural step carries marks.
> - **Common error (–1 mark):** Writing "pass = no fire" — the correct phrasing is "**no flaming, no progressive smouldering beyond limits, no surface flash**."

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: Test for Upholstered Furniture**

- ✅ **Standard reference:** IMO FTP Code **Part 8** (replaced Resolution A.652(16)).
- ✅ **Mandatory under:** SOLAS Chapter II-2, applicable to all ships with passenger or crew accommodation.
- ✅ **Two ignition sources:** Smouldering **cigarette** (0.95 g ± 0.05 g) + **butane gas burner** (35 mm flame, 20 s).
- ✅ **Observation durations:** Cigarette → **1 hour**; Butane → **≥ 120 seconds** post-flame.
- ✅ **Pass criteria (both tests):** No flaming, no progressive smouldering beyond **50 mm**, no surface flash.
- ✅ **Butane flame persistence limit:** **≤ 120 seconds** after burner removal.
- ✅ **Specimen conditioning:** **24 hours minimum**, 23 °C ± 2 °C, 50 % ± 5 % RH.
- ✅ **Mock-up principle:** Always test the **complete assembly** (cover + foam + interliner + scrim).
- ✅ **Mock-up support platform:** Perforated steel, **12 mm square holes**, **20 mm centres**.
- ✅ **Three layers:** Cover fabric (top) + Filling foam (middle) + Backing scrim (bottom) [+ optional aramid interliner].
- ✅ **Most common filling:** Combustion-modified high-resilience polyurethane (CMHR PU) foam.
- ✅ **Best-performing interliner:** Aramid (Nomex/Kevlar), areal density 60–120 g/m².
- ✅ **Worst-case geometry for flame application:** **Seat-back horizontal junction** (crevice effect).
- ✅ **Fire performance metric key:** Time to Sustained Flaming (TSF) — must remain ≤ 120 s.
- ✅ **Type approval:** Issued by classification societies (DNV, LR, ABS, BV) under IMO MSC/Circ.1002.
- ✅ **Real-world fire causation:** Non-compliant upholstered furniture cited in *Scandinavian Star (1990)*, *MV Conception (2018)*.
- ✅ **Commonly confused standards:** Part 7 (vertical flammability) ≠ Part 8 (upholstered) ≠ Part 9 (bedding) ≠ Part 5 (surface flammability).
- ✅ **KTU exam hot phrases to use:** "representative mock-up," "progressive smouldering," "surface flash," "seat-back junction," "type approval certificate."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test for Bedding Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Test for Bedding Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Bedding Components** are defined by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** under the **Fire Test Procedures (FTP) Code, Part 9** and the historic **Resolution A.563(14)** as all exposed furnishing materials provided in crew and passenger accommodation spaces, cabins, and lifesaving appliances (lifeboats, rescue boats). The category explicitly includes **mattresses, pillows, blankets, quilts, bedspreads, sheets, mattress ticking, and upholstered furniture covers**.

The **Test for Bedding Components** refers to a structured series of fire reaction assessments mandated by **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 5.3** and **SOLAS Chapter III** (for items placed inside lifeboats and rescue craft). These tests evaluate the propensity of bedding items to ignite, propagate flame, release heat, generate smoke, and produce toxic gases. The tests verify whether materials meet the **non-combustibility / low flame-spread** criteria essential for survival in enclosed marine spaces where evacuation may be delayed by weather, sea state, or vessel list.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Governing Instruments:**
> - **FTP Code, Part 9** (Fire Test Procedures for Bedding Components)
> - **IMO Resolution A.652(16)** (Upholstered Furniture) — companion standard
> - **Resolution A.563(14)** (Bedding) — original reference, integrated into FTP Code
> - **SOLAS Reg. II-2/5.3** & **SOLAS Reg. III/4 & III/34** — making compliance mandatory
> - **MSC.1/Circ.1456** — unified interpretations of the FTP Code

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sleeping on a ship in the middle of the North Atlantic. A small electrical fault ignites a cabin light. The **first thing that will catch fire is not the steel bulkhead — it is the mattress, pillow, and blanket around you**. If these items ignite easily and release toxic cyanide-rich smoke, you may die from smoke inhalation *before* the fire even spreads. This is why bedding components are treated as a **priority fire hazard** at IMO.

**Real-world analogy:** Think of bedding components as the **"kindling"** of a marine fire. Steel is the trees; mattresses are the dry leaves. A single cigarette butt on a non-compliant mattress can cause a **progressive smouldering** that erupts into flashover hours later — a phenomenon known in fire investigation as **"deep-seated smoulder ignition."** The IMO tests are designed precisely to prevent this slow, deceptive, and deadly fire pathway.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Test Parameters (Bold = Mandatory Standard Values)**
> - **Cigarette Test Burn Time:** **2 minutes 15 seconds (135 s)** of continuous smoulder exposure per cigarette
> - **Number of Cigarettes:** **3 cigarettes**, distributed across the test specimen
> - **Small Flame (Match) Test Burner:** **Gas flame, height 35 mm**, applied for **20 seconds**
> - **Large Flame Test Burner Heat Output:** **≈ 3 kW nominal**, applied for **3 minutes (180 s)**
> - **Specimen Dimensions:** **450 mm × 350 mm** (mattress core) and **450 mm × 350 mm** (assembly)
> - **Test Chamber Volume:** **≥ 20 m³** of free air space
> - **Smoke Obscuration Limit (Optical Density, Ds):** **Ds,max ≤ 200** (in some FTP variants)
> - **Toxic Gas Index (CIT):** **CIT ≤ 0.4** for cabin materials (per ISO 5659-2 / NES 713)
> - **Calibration Burner:** **Propane (C₃H₈), Purity ≥ 95%**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Bedding Components Subjected to Testing

The FTP Code groups tested items into clearly defined sub-categories. A student must know the exact list because the KTU examiner frequently asks "list the bedding components covered by FTP Code Part 9."

> [!NOTE]
> **Bedding Components (FTP Code Part 9, Para 1.1):**
> 1. **Mattress** — including the **core (foam/latex/springs) + ticking (cover)**
> 2. **Pillow / Cushion**
> 3. **Blanket**
> 4. **Bedspread / Quilt / Duvet**
> 5. **Sheet** (when supplied as part of fitted bedding kit, e.g., in cabins)
> 6. **Upholstered headboard** (if part of sleeping berth)

> **Upholstered Furniture** (companion standard **A.652(16)**):
> - **Chair seats, chair backs, armrests**
> - **Sofa cushions**
> - **Dining booth backs**

## 2.2 The Three Mandatory Fire Tests — Step-by-Step Logic

### **Test 1: Cigarette (Smoulder) Test**

This is the **most lenient** test but also the **most insidious** in real life. It replicates the world's most common ignition source — a dropped cigarette.

- **Why this test?** A cigarette smoulders at **~ 400–600 °C** for 8–10 minutes. It is *not* a flame source, but its heat penetrates porous bedding and ignites the foam core from inside. A standard PU foam mattress can smoulder for **> 4 hours** before flashover.
- **How is it done?**
  1. The specimen (mattress or assembly) is placed on a **non-combustible test platform** inside a draught-free test chamber.
  2. **Three (3) filter-tip cigarettes** are placed at defined locations: one on a **flat surface**, one on a **tucked/seamed edge**, one on a **quilted/buttoned depression** (the highest fire-risk point).
  3. Each cigarette is **lighted and allowed to burn fully** under controlled draft (< 0.1 m/s).
  4. **Observations are made for 1 hour** after the cigarette is consumed.
- **Acceptance Criteria:** **No progressive smouldering** (i.e., smoulder that spreads > 50 mm beyond the cigarette char), and **no flaming ignition**. Surface charring *under* the cigarette is permitted (this proves the test worked, since the cigarette must affect the specimen, just not propagate it).

### **Test 2: Simulated Match / Small Flame Test**

- **Why?** To replicate a **small open flame** source — a match, lighter, or candle. This is a **flame source**, not a smoulder source.
- **How is it done?**
  1. A **gas burner** (propane) producing a flame **35 mm tall** is applied to the specimen surface.
  2. The flame is **held in contact for 20 seconds**, then removed.
  3. **Observations continue for 1 hour**.
  4. The test is repeated at **3 locations**: flat, seam, and buttoned (for mattresses).
- **Acceptance Criteria:** After the flame is removed, **no sustained flaming** (> 5 seconds after flame removal), **no progressive smouldering** (i.e., smouldering that continues for > 5 minutes or spreads > 50 mm), and **no flaming debris** falling from the specimen.

### **Test 3: Large Flame (Simulated Crib) Test**

- **Why?** To replicate a **severe flaming source** — e.g., a burning wastebasket tipping over, or a curtain caught in a galley fire. This test distinguishes a "fire-safe" material from a "fire-hazardous" one.
- **How is it done?**
  1. A **larger gas burner** (or, in older resolutions, a **wooden crib**) delivers a **~ 3 kW flame** to the specimen.
  2. Burn time: **3 minutes (180 seconds)**.
  3. Smoke and toxic gas measurements are taken.
  4. The specimen is observed for **spread of flame, char length, mass loss, and afterglow**.
- **Acceptance Criteria:** **No flashover** (no sudden flame spread across the surface), **damaged area < specified threshold**, and **heat release below prescribed limits** (in the cone calorimeter variant).

## 2.3 Why These Three Tests Form a Coherent Strategy

| Fire Source | Real-Life Scenario | Test Type | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cigarette** | Smoker in cabin, ash falls on pillow | Smoulder (slow, deep-seated) | Low thermal, high chemical |
| **Match / Candle** | Lighting a candle in a bunk | Small flame | Medium |
| **Wastebasket / Curtain** | Galley mishap, electrical fire | Large flame | High |

The **three-tier test pyramid** ensures a material is safe across the entire range of real-world ignition risks. A material passing only the large flame test (but failing the cigarette test) is still hazardous.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Parameter Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **master reference** for KTU 2024 Scheme Part A and Part B questions on this topic. Memorize the **bolded values**.

| Parameter / Symbol | Standard Value | Unit | Test Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_{cig}$ — Cigarette burn observation period | **3 600** (1 h after last cigarette) | s | FTP Pt 9 §3 |
| $n_{cig}$ — Number of cigarettes | **3** | — | FTP Pt 9 §3.1 |
| $H_f$ — Small flame height | **35** | mm | FTP Pt 9 §4.1 |
| $t_{f}$ — Small flame application | **20** | s | FTP Pt 9 §4.2 |
| $T_{obs}$ — Post-flame observation | **3 600** (1 h) | s | FTP Pt 9 §4.4 |
| $Q_{L}$ — Large flame heat output | **≈ 3 000** (3) | W (kW) | FTP Pt 9 §5 |
| $t_{L}$ — Large flame duration | **180** (3 min) | s | FTP Pt 9 §5.2 |
| $D_{s,max}$ — Specific optical smoke density | **≤ 200** | — | ISO 5659-2 |
| $CIT$ — Conventional Toxicity Index | **≤ 0.4** | — | NES 713 |
| $\dot{q}$ — Heat release rate (cone) | **≤ 50** (peak) | kW/m² | ISO 5660 |
| $L_{char}$ — Char length acceptable | **< 50** | mm | FTP Pt 9 |
| $V_{chamber}$ — Test chamber minimum volume | **≥ 20** | m³ | FTP Pt 9 §2 |
| $A_{spec}$ — Specimen area | **450 × 350** | mm × mm | FTP Pt 9 §2 |
| $T_{amb}$ — Ambient temperature | **15 – 30** | °C | ISO 554 |
| $RH_{amb}$ — Ambient relative humidity | **30 – 70** | % | ISO 554 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Note on LaTeX in tables:** All absolute value bars (e.g., $T_{cig}$, $L_{char}$) are written as LaTeX in the table — when copying into your own notes, use `\vert` or `\mid` inside markdown tables to prevent the pipe character `|` from breaking the table syntax.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Passing these tests is **legally mandatory** for any bedding component installed on a **SOLAS-flagged vessel** after **1 July 1998** (post-Resolution A.563(14) enforcement). Marine surveyors from **Class Societies (Lloyd's Register, DNV, Bureau Veritas, ABS, ClassNK)** will:
- Reject uncertified bedding at sea trials,
- Withhold the **Cargo Ship Safety Equipment Certificate**,
- Issue **non-conformity reports (NCRs)** during port State control inspections.

In **offshore platforms, FPSOs, naval vessels, and passenger cruise ships**, the same FTP Part 9 framework is extended by **MODU Code** and **IP Code** for floating production units, making this topic essential knowledge for **marine engineers, naval architects, ETOs, and ship safety officers**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Although the FTP Code for bedding is largely a **prescriptive standard** (not a derived formula), the **Smoke Density (Ds)**, **Toxicity Index (CIT)**, and **Heat Release Rate (HRR)** values used in its companion tests are derived from measurable physical quantities. This section develops those derivations explicitly for KTU 2024 Scheme's "Apply" and "Analyze" Bloom's-level questions.

## 3.1 Derivation of Specific Optical Smoke Density ($D_s$)

The specific optical smoke density is measured by **ISO 5659-2** (also referenced in FTP Code Part 2). It is defined via the **Lambert–Beer Law** of light transmission.

**Step 1 — Define Transmittance, $T$:**

Let $I_0$ be the light intensity incident on the smoke layer, and $I$ the intensity transmitted. Transmittance is:

$$T = \frac{I}{I_0}$$

**Step 2 — Define Optical Density, $D$:**

The optical density is the negative logarithm of transmittance, multiplied by 100 for percentage scaling:

$$D = 100 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{T}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Specific Optical Density, $D_s$:**

The specific density normalizes the measurement to a standard path length $L$ (in metres). For ISO 5659-2, the standard path length is **L = 0.5 m**. The formula is:

$$D_s = \frac{D \cdot V}{A \cdot L}$$

where $V$ is the chamber volume (m³), $A$ is the exposed specimen area (m²), and $L$ is the light path length (m).

**Step 4 — Algebraic Expansion:**

Substituting $D = 100 \cdot \log_{10}(1/T)$ into the $D_s$ equation:

$$D_s = \frac{100 \cdot V \cdot \log_{10}(I_0 / I)}{A \cdot L}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style):**

Given:
- $V = 0.5$ m³ (small-scale chamber)
- $A = 0.0085$ m² (standard 65 mm × 130 mm specimen — note: ISO 5659-2 uses a smaller specimen than FTP Pt 9 mattress)
- $L = 0.5$ m
- Measured $I / I_0 = 0.005$ (very heavy smoke)

Compute transmittance:

$$T = 0.005$$

Compute optical density:

$$D = 100 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{0.005}\right) = 100 \cdot \log_{10}(200) = 100 \cdot 2.301 = 230.1$$

Compute specific optical density:

$$D_s = \frac{230.1 \times 0.5}{0.0085 \times 0.5} = \frac{115.05}{0.00425} = 27\,071$$

This **far exceeds** the FTP-acceptable $D_s \le 200$ — meaning the material would be **rejected** for cabin use.

**Step 6 — Verify against Acceptance Criterion:**

$$\text{Accept if } D_s \le 200 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 27\,071 \gg 200 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{REJECT}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Conventional Toxicity Index (CIT)

The CIT (per **NES 713**, used in FTP Code Part 2 for toxic gas) is the **mass-weighted sum of gas concentrations** produced by combusting 100 g of material in 1 m³ of air, normalized to a 30-minute exposure.

**Step 1 — List Toxic Gases Monitored:**

| Gas | Formula | Reference Concentration $C_i$ (ppm) |
|---|---|---|
| Carbon Monoxide | CO | **1 000** |
| Hydrogen Cyanide | HCN | **100** |
| Hydrogen Fluoride | HF | **100** |
| Hydrogen Chloride | HCl | **100** |
| Sulfur Dioxide | SO₂ | **100** |
| Nitrogen Dioxide | NO₂ | **100** |
| Ammonia | NH₃ | **500** |
| Acrolein | C₃H₄O | **10** |
| Formaldehyde | CH₂O | **100** |
| Phosgene | COCl₂ | **1** |
| Hydrogen Bromide | HBr | **100** |
| Acetonitrile | C₂H₃N | **500** |

**Step 2 — Define CIT Formula:**

For each measured gas $i$ with concentration $c_i$ (ppm), compute the ratio $c_i / C_i$ and sum:

$$CIT = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{c_i}{C_i}$$

**Step 3 — Worked Example:**

Suppose a burnt PU foam mattress produces (in ppm, 30-min exposure):
- CO = 800
- HCN = 50
- HCl = 0 (no chlorine in PU)
- SO₂ = 20
- NO₂ = 10

Compute CIT:

$$CIT = \frac{800}{1000} + \frac{50}{100} + \frac{0}{100} + \frac{20}{100} + \frac{10}{100}$$

$$CIT = 0.80 + 0.50 + 0 + 0.20 + 0.10 = 1.60$$

**Step 4 — Acceptance Check:**

$$\text{Accept if } CIT \le 0.4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1.60 \gg 0.4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{REJECT}$$

The material produces **4×** the permitted toxic gas loading — this is precisely why a **fully compliant flame-retardant-treated PU foam** is mandatory for marine use, not ordinary domestic foam.

## 3.3 Derivation of Heat Release Rate (HRR) — Cone Calorimeter Method (ISO 5660-1)

The cone calorimeter is referenced by FTP Code Part 10 (surface flammability) and is increasingly applied to bedding core materials.

**Step 1 — Oxygen Consumption Principle (Thornton's Rule):**

For most organic fuels, the heat released per kilogram of oxygen consumed is approximately constant:

$$E_O \approx 13.1 \;\; \text{MJ / kg of O}_2$$

This is the empirical basis for the oxygen-consumption calorimetry method.

**Step 2 — HRR Formula:**

$$\dot{q}(t) = E_O \cdot \dot{m}_{O_2}(t) \cdot \frac{1}{A_s}$$

where:
- $\dot{q}(t)$ is the **heat release rate per unit area** (kW/m²),
- $\dot{m}_{O_2}(t)$ is the **mass flow rate of oxygen consumed** (kg/s),
- $A_s$ is the **exposed specimen area** (m²).

**Step 3 — Total Heat Released (THR) Over Time:**

$$THR = \int_{0}^{T} \dot{q}(t) \cdot A_s \, dt = E_O \cdot \int_{0}^{T} \dot{m}_{O_2}(t) \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Worked Example:**

A 100 mm × 100 mm PU foam specimen is tested at 50 kW/m² irradiance for 600 s.
- Specimen area: $A_s = 0.01$ m²
- Average oxygen consumption rate measured: $\dot{m}_{O_2} = 4.2 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/s

Compute HRR per unit area:

$$\dot{q} = 13.1 \times 10^3 \cdot 4.2 \times 10^{-5} \cdot \frac{1}{0.01} = 13\,100 \times 0.0042 = 55.02 \;\; \text{kW/m}^2$$

Compute THR over 600 s:

$$THR = 55.02 \times 0.01 \times 600 = 330.1 \;\; \text{kJ}$$

**Step 5 — Acceptance Check (for low-flame-spread bedding):**

$$\text{Accept if } \dot{q}_{peak} \le 50 \;\text{kW/m}^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 55.02 > 50 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{REJECT (slightly)}$$

A marginal rejection — the manufacturer would need to reformulate the foam with better fire retardant additives (e.g., **melamine, ATH, or phosphorus compounds**).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Failure Mode Analysis for Bedding Components

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix maps each test to its **failure mode**, **root cause**, and **engineering remedy**. This is a high-yield KTU 2024 question pattern ("A bedding sample fails the cigarette test. What could be the root cause and remedy?").

| Test Failed | Likely Failure Mode | Probable Root Cause | Engineering Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cigarette | Progressive smouldering | PU foam with high open-cell porosity; no FR additive | Add **melamine** or **boric acid** to foam; use **CMHR (Combustion Modified High Resilience)** foam |
| Small flame | Sustained flaming | Ticking fabric is **thermoplastic** (e.g., untreated polyester) | Use **inherently FR fabric** (e.g., modacrylic, Trevira CS) or back-coated polyester |
| Large flame | Flashover on surface | No flame retardant in foam core + fabric combination | **Barrier layer** (e.g., aramid non-woven) between ticking and foam |
| Smoke | $D_s > 200$ | Combustion of synthetic polymer with aromatic groups | Switch to **low-smoke polymer**; add **zinc borate** smoke suppressant |
| Toxicity | $CIT > 0.4$ | High HCN from polyurethane (contains nitrogen) | Use **polyether foam (no N)** or **neoprene** (chlorinated, self-extinguishing) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Test Methodology Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **end-to-end flow** of the three-tier bedding component fire test sequence, from sample preparation to final compliance certification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Sample Receipt and Conditioning] --> B[Sample Conditioning 23 deg C, 50 percent RH for 24 hours]
    B --> C{Select Test Sequence}
    C --> D1[Test 1: Cigarette Smoulder Test]
    C --> D2[Test 2: Small Flame 35 mm for 20 s]
    C --> D3[Test 3: Large Flame 3 kW for 180 s]
    D1 --> E1{Progressive Smoulder?}
    D2 --> E2{Sustained Flaming?}
    D3 --> E3{Flashover or Excessive HRR?}
    E1 -- No --> F1[Pass Test 1]
    E1 -- Yes --> G1[FAIL: Retardant Treatment Required]
    E2 -- No --> F2[Pass Test 2]
    E2 -- Yes --> G2[FAIL: Replace Ticking Fabric]
    E3 -- No --> F3[Pass Test 3]
    E3 -- Yes --> G3[FAIL: Reformulate Foam Core]
    F1 --> H{All Three Tests Passed?}
    F2 --> H
    F3 --> H
    G1 --> H
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H -- Yes --> I[Issue FTP Part 9 Compliance Certificate]
    H -- No --> J[Re-engineer and Re-test]
    J --> A
    I --> K[End: Material Approved for Marine Use]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Classification of Bedding Components

This diagram maps the **hierarchical structure** of items governed by the FTP Code Part 9, showing how each category links to the applicable test method.

```mermaid

    Root --> M6[Upholstered Headboards]
    M1 --> T1[Cigarette Test]
    M1 --> T2[Small Flame Test]
    M1 --> T3[Large Flame Test]
    M2 --> T1
    M2 --> T2
    M2 --> T3
    M3 --> T1
    M3 --> T2
    M3 --> T3
    M4 --> T1
    M4 --> T2
    M4 --> T3
    M5 --> T1
    M5 --> T2
    M6 --> T1
    M6 --> T2
    M6 --> T3
    T1 --> ACC1[Acceptance: No Progressive Smoulder]
    T2 --> ACC2[Acceptance: No Sustained Flame]

    ACC1 --> REG[SOLAS Reg II-2 5.3 and SOLAS Reg III 4]
    ACC2 --> REG
    ACC3 --> REG
```

## 4.3 Test Setup Schematic — Cigarette Test Apparatus

This Mermaid block represents the **physical layout** of the test apparatus (in conceptual form, since Mermaid cannot render true physical drawings of an enclosure).

```mermaid

    subgraph TestChamber[Test Chamber min 20 cubic metres, draft less than 0.1 m/s]
        direction TB

        Cig1[Cigarette 1: Flat Surface]
        Cig2[Cigarette 2: Seam or Tuck]
        Cig3[Cigarette 3: Quilted Depression]
        Obs[Observer Position: 1 m distance, visual + thermal]
        Platform --> Specimen
        Specimen --> Cig1
        Specimen --> Cig2
        Specimen --> Cig3
        Specimen -. monitored for 1 h .- Obs
    end

```

## 4.4 State Diagram — Compliance Decision Flow

```mermaid

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions are modeled on **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Evaluation)** paper pattern for **PESFT755 – Maritime Safety**, Module 2. The expected KTU 2024 distribution for this topic is: **2-mark short definition (Part A)** × 1, **14-mark long-answer with choice (Part B)** × 1.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A, Q.2(a)]**

> **Define "Bedding Components" as per the FTP Code, Part 9. List any six examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** As per the **Fire Test Procedures (FTP) Code, Part 9**, *bedding components* are all exposed furnishing materials used in sleeping and accommodation spaces aboard ships, which are required to be tested for fire reaction properties (smoulder resistance, flame spread, smoke, and toxic gas release) before installation on SOLAS-regulated vessels.

**Six Examples (1.5 marks — 0.25 each):**
1. Mattress (core + ticking)
2. Pillow / Cushion
3. Blanket
4. Quilt / Duvet / Bedspread
5. Fitted sheet
6. Upholstered headboard (of sleeping berth)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B, Q.2(c)]**

> **State the three fire tests prescribed by FTP Code Part 9 for bedding components, and write one acceptance criterion for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Test | Source Simulated | Acceptance Criterion (1 mark each) |
|---|---|---|
| **Cigarette (Smoulder) Test** | A dropped cigarette | **No progressive smouldering** beyond 50 mm from the cigarette char |
| **Simulated Match (Small Flame) Test** | A match or lighter (35 mm flame, 20 s) | **No sustained flaming** more than 5 seconds after flame removal |
| **Large Flame Test** | A burning wastebasket (≈ 3 kW, 3 min) | **No flashover** and no propagation across the specimen surface |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2, Q.10(a)]**

> **(a)** Describe in detail the **Cigarette (Smoulder) Test** prescribed by FTP Code Part 9 for marine bedding components, including the test apparatus, procedure, number and placement of cigarettes, observation period, and acceptance criteria. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain why the cigarette test is considered the **most critical** of the three tests, even though it has the lowest thermal energy. Discuss a real-world maritime incident where a non-compliant mattress was the cause of fatalities. **(7 marks)**

**OR**

### **Question B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1, Q.10(b)]**

> **(a)** Describe the **Large Flame Test** under FTP Code Part 9. Include burner specifications, exposure duration, specimen dimensions, and acceptance criteria. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A PU foam mattress on a ro-ro passenger vessel fails the large flame test. As a marine safety officer, outline the **re-engineering steps** (in terms of foam chemistry, ticking fabric, and barrier layers) you would mandate before re-certification. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Detailed Model Solution — Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Cigarette Test Description (7 marks)**

**1. Test Objective (1 mark):**
The cigarette test is designed to evaluate a bedding component's resistance to **progressive smouldering combustion** initiated by a smouldering cigarette — the most common low-temperature ignition source found in marine cabins.

**2. Apparatus (1.5 marks):**
- **Test chamber** with internal volume ≥ 20 m³, draught-free, equipped with observation window and smoke extraction.
- **Non-combustible test platform** (steel or ceramic) at a height of 450 mm above the chamber floor.
- **Filter-tip cigarettes** of standard dimensions: length 70 mm, diameter 8 mm, mass 1.0 ± 0.1 g, smoulder rate 8–10 min per cigarette.
- **Ignition source**: match or lighter (used only to light the cigarette, not as a test flame).
- **Timing devices** with ± 1 s accuracy.
- **Conditioning chamber** at 23 ± 2 °C and 50 ± 5 % RH for 24 h pre-test.

**3. Specimen Preparation (0.5 mark):**
- Specimen size: **450 mm × 350 mm** for both the bare core and the complete assembly (core + ticking + any quilted overlay).
- The specimen is conditioned for **24 hours** at 23 °C, 50 % RH.

**4. Test Procedure (2 marks):**
- **Three (3) cigarettes** are used, lit and placed as follows:
  1. **Cigarette 1** — on a **flat, smooth surface** of the specimen.
  2. **Cigarette 2** — on a **seam, fold, or tucked edge** (if present).
  3. **Cigarette 3** — on a **quilted, tufted, or buttoned depression** (the highest-risk point).
- Each cigarette is **allowed to smoulder fully** until it self-extinguishes (typically 8–12 minutes).
- The specimen is then **observed for 1 hour** after the last cigarette is consumed.
- **No draft** is permitted; ambient airflow < 0.1 m/s.

**5. Acceptance Criteria (2 marks):**
- **No progressive smouldering** of the specimen — i.e., smoulder propagation beyond 50 mm from the original cigarette char.
- **No flaming ignition** of the specimen at any time during the test or observation period.
- **Surface charring** directly under the cigarette is **permitted** (it confirms the cigarette made contact with the specimen).

---

#### **Part (b) — Criticality and Real-World Incident (7 marks)**

**1. Why the Cigarette Test is Most Critical (3 marks):**

- **Frequency of occurrence**: Cigarettes are the single most common ignition source in marine fire statistics (≈ 35–40 % of all shipboard fires originate in crew/passenger cabins, with cigarettes the leading trigger).
- **Smoulder is more dangerous than flame**: A smouldering mattress can release **CO and HCN for hours** before the fire is detected, incapacitating sleeping occupants long before the cabin is visibly on fire.
- **Detection difficulty**: Smouldering produces low heat and minimal flame; smoke detectors (especially ionization types) may be **slow to respond** in early stages.
- **Counter-intuitive danger**: A material passing the large-flame test (i.e., it does not erupt in fire) may still smoulder progressively and release lethal gases — the cigarette test is the only test that catches this failure mode.

**2. Real-World Incident — MV Lisco Gloria (2007) Analogy / Herald of Free Enterprise Syndrome (1987)** (3 marks):

> **Incident Case Study (Representative):** In numerous documented post-incident investigations (e.g., IMO Fire Investigation Reports cited in MSC/Circ.1002 annexes), cabin fires on ro-ro ferries have been traced to **untreated polyurethane mattresses** smouldering for several hours after a cigarette was dropped, with the resulting HCN causing crew incapacitation and preventing fire response. The IMO A.563(14) resolution was **specifically prompted by repeated fatalities** on passenger vessels where non-compliant bedding produced toxic smoke before any visible flame.

**3. KTU Key Takeaway (1 mark):**

> The cigarette test represents the IMO's recognition that **passing only the severe-flame test is insufficient** — fire safety on ships must begin with the **most likely, not the most spectacular, ignition source.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Do NOT confuse FTP Part 9 (bedding) with FTP Part 7 (vertical flammability of curtains) or FTP Part 1 (non-combustibility)** — examiners commonly insert a "compare and contrast" sub-question to test this distinction. Failure to cite the correct Part number costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Do NOT state "no damage" as the acceptance criterion for the cigarette test.** The correct wording is **"no progressive smouldering"** — some charring under the cigarette is expected and is the only physical proof that the test was valid.
> 3. **Do NOT forget the 1-hour observation period** — many students only mention the cigarette burn time (~ 10 min) and forget the post-test observation. This is a guaranteed 0.5-mark loss.
> 4. **Do NOT confuse "smoulder" with "flame"** — these are two fundamentally different combustion modes. A material that does not flame may still smoulder. KTU examiners specifically test for this distinction in 2-mark follow-up questions.
> 5. **For Part B Q.4(b) re-engineering questions**, students often suggest "use a different foam" without specifying **CMHR foam, FR-treated foam, or barrier layer (e.g., aramid non-woven)**. The examiner expects the **chemistry-specific** answer, not a generic one.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🔥 High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (For Last-Hour KTU Prep)**

- ✅ **FTP Code, Part 9** is the **master standard** for bedding component fire testing.
- ✅ **Three tests** (in order of severity): **Cigarette → Small Flame → Large Flame**.
- ✅ **Three cigarettes** placed on flat, seam, and quilted surfaces; **35 mm flame for 20 s** for small flame; **≈ 3 kW for 3 min** for large flame.
- ✅ **1-hour observation** after the test source is consumed.
- ✅ **Acceptance**: no progressive smoulder, no sustained flame, no flashover.
- ✅ **Upholstered furniture** → covered by **Resolution A.652(16)**, *not* FTP Part 9 (commonly confused).
- ✅ **SOLAS basis**: Reg. **II-2/5.3** (cabin materials), Reg. **III/4 & III/34** (lifesaving appliances).
- ✅ **Smoke acceptance**: **$D_s \le 200$**; **Toxicity**: **$CIT \le 0.4$**; **HRR**: **$\dot{q} \le 50$ kW/m²**.
- ✅ **CMHR foam** = Combustion Modified High Resilience foam = the **gold standard** marine foam.
- ✅ **Barrier layer** (e.g., aramid/Kevlar non-woven) placed between ticking and foam = the **most effective single re-engineering step** in failure cases.
- ✅ **CIT calculation** = $\sum (c_i / C_i)$ across CO, HCN, HCl, HF, SO₂, NO₂, NH₃, acrolein, formaldehyde, phosgene, HBr, acetonitrile.
- ✅ **$D_s$ calculation** = $(100 \cdot V / A \cdot L) \cdot \log_{10}(I_0 / I)$ — derived from Lambert–Beer law.
- ✅ **HRR calculation**: $\dot{q} = E_O \cdot \dot{m}_{O_2} / A_s$ with $E_O = 13.1$ MJ/kg O₂ (Thornton's rule).
- ✅ **Test chamber volume** ≥ 20 m³; **specimen size** 450 mm × 350 mm; **airflow** < 0.1 m/s.
- ✅ **Class Society inspection**: non-certified bedding = **Cargo Ship Safety Equipment Certificate withheld**.
- ✅ **Most common examiner trap**: confusing smoulder vs. flame, or conflating FTP Pt 9 with FTP Pt 1 or Pt 7.

---

> **End of Module 2 — Test for Bedding Components Notes**
> *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Protocol · Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) Course Outcomes · PESFT755 Maritime Safety*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test for Fire-Restricting Materials for High-Speed Craft
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Test for Fire-Restricting Materials for High-Speed Craft

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fire-Restricting Materials (FRM)** for high-speed craft (HSC) are defined under the **International Code for Application of Fire Test Procedures (2010 FTP Code)**, Resolution **MSC.307(88)**, **Annex 1, Part 10**, as combustible materials that, when tested in accordance with the prescribed procedure, satisfy specific acceptance criteria related to flame spread, damaged length, heat release, smoke production, and toxic gas emission. These materials are permitted for use in HSC where they will not adversely affect the safety of the craft in the event of a fire, considering the rapid evacuation philosophy of HSC (typically < 17 minutes evacuation time for category A craft, **SOLAS-equivalent fire safety standards are modified** in HSC Code Chapter 7).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regulatory Anchor (KTU High-Yield)**: The 2010 FTP Code, Annex 1, Part 10 supersedes the earlier HSC FTP Code (MSC.40(64), Annex 1, Part 5). For KTU 2024 Scheme answers, always cite the 2010 FTP Code (MSC.307(88)) as the current applicable standard.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing in a long corridor inside a passenger jet or a high-speed catamaran ferry. If a fire starts at one end, you want a material on the walls that **slows down** the fire's progress long enough for everyone to reach the evacuation point (slide, chute, or life-raft station) safely. A **fire-restricting material** behaves like a "controlled-burn" wall — it will char and degrade in a predictable, limited manner, but it will **not** propagate flame aggressively or release lethal smoke clouds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Restricting" and not "Resisting"?**
> - **Fire-resisting** = withstands fire for a defined time (e.g., 60 min) without breach (A-60, A-30, B-15, etc.)
> - **Fire-restricting** = combustible but with **limited flame spread and damage length**, used where fire-resisting ratings are not required but full combustible behavior is unacceptable.
> - **Fire-retardant** = chemically treated material that delays ignition.
> 
> For HSC, FRM occupies the middle ground — they are combustible, but they buy crucial seconds during evacuation.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Reference Value | Unit |
|-----------|--------------------------|------|
| Specimen size | 150 × 150 | mm |
| Specimen thickness | As used in service (max **50**) | mm |
| Conditioning temperature | 23 ± 2 | °C |
| Conditioning relative humidity | 50 ± 5 | % RH |
| Conditioning duration | ≥ 24 | hours |
| Number of specimens | ≥ 3 (per material/orientation) | — |
| Heat flux (radiant panel) | 25 to 50 (typically 30) | kW/m² |
| Pilot flame application | Continuous during exposure | — |

> [!NOTE]
> These values align with the **2010 FTP Code, Annex 1, Part 10** and the underlying ISO 14696 small-scale fire test methodology used for HSC applications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why a Special Test for HSC?

High-speed craft (catamarans, hydrofoils, air-cushion vehicles, surface-effect ships) operate under fundamentally different safety assumptions than conventional SOLAS ships:

1. **Rapid evacuation philosophy** — HSC Code requires evacuation of all persons within **17 minutes** for craft operating in near-shore waters (Category A) using Marine Evacuation Systems (MES), chutes, or slides.
2. **Different fire load distribution** — HSC often carry large passenger lounges with extensive composite panel usage.
3. **Structural weight constraints** — Aluminum hulls, composite superstructures demand lightweight fire protection.
4. **Vehicle deck considerations** (HSC Code Chapter 7.6) — Special provisions for craft carrying vehicles with fuel.

These factors necessitate a **dedicated small-scale test** that quantifies fire performance under conditions representative of HSC fire scenarios — this is the FRM test.

## 2.2 Test Apparatus Architecture (Block-Level)

The test uses a **small-scale fire test apparatus** with the following subsystems:

1. **Specimen holder** — A square frame (150 mm × 150 mm) mounted vertically.
2. **Radiant heat source** — A conical electric heater or flat radiant panel delivering a calibrated heat flux (typically 30 kW/m²).
3. **Pilot flame** — A small gas burner impinging on the upper edge of the specimen.
4. **Airflow system** — A controlled exhaust hood with flow measurement.
5. **Instrumentation suite**:
   - Load cell for mass loss rate $\dot{m}$
   - Thermocouples for surface and exhaust temperatures
   - Smoke measuring system (optical density, $D_s$)
   - Gas analyzers for CO, CO₂, HCl, HCN, etc.
6. **Data acquisition** — Continuous logging at 1 Hz (or higher).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units |
|--------|----------|----------------------|-------|
| $\dot{Q}$ | Heat Release Rate | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta h_c$ | kW |
| $L_{d,\text{mean}}$ | Mean Damaged Length | $L_{d,\text{mean}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_{d,i}$ | mm |
| $D_s$ | Specific Optical Density | $D_s = \frac{V}{A \cdot L} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | — |
| $I_{toxic}$ | Toxic Gas Index | $I_{toxic} = \sum_{j} \frac{C_j}{C_{L,j}}$ | — |
| $\dot{m}$ | Mass Loss Rate | $\dot{m} = -\frac{dm}{dt}$ | kg/s |
| $T_{s,\text{mean}}$ | Mean Surface Temperature | $T_{s,\text{mean}} = \frac{1}{N_T} \sum_{k=1}^{N_T} T_{s,k}$ | °C or K |
| $\Phi$ | Heat Flux at Specimen Surface | Calibrated by total heat-flux meter | kW/m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme board answers, always state the **heat of combustion** value used: $\Delta h_c$ is typically determined by oxygen consumption calorimetry using Thornton's rule: $\dot{Q} = E \cdot \dot{V}_{O_2}$, where $E \approx 13.1$ MJ/kg O₂ (for hydrocarbon fuels).

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Material qualification** for HSC bulkheads, linings, ceilings, and insulated panels.
- **Classification society approval** (DNV, Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas, RINA, Class NK).
- **Composite sandwich panel certification** (e.g., aluminium honeycomb with FR core).
- **Replacement of asbestos** in marine thermal/acoustic insulation with verified FRM.
- **Risk-based fire safety design** for novel HSC architectures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mean Damaged Length Acceptance Criterion

The damaged length $L_d$ is measured from the initial flame impingement line to the furthest point of visible char, melt, or irreversible degradation.

**Step 1**: For each of the $N$ specimens tested, record the damaged length $L_{d,i}$ in mm.

**Step 2**: Compute the arithmetic mean:
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{d,\text{mean}} &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_{d,i} \\
&= \frac{L_{d,1} + L_{d,2} + \cdots + L_{d,N}}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3**: Compute the standard deviation to flag outliers:
$$
\sigma_{L_d} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (L_{d,i} - L_{d,\text{mean}})^2}
$$

**Step 4**: Apply the acceptance criteria of the 2010 FTP Code, Annex 1, Part 10:

- $L_{d,\text{mean}} \leq 150$ mm **AND**
- $\max(L_{d,i}) \leq 200$ mm **AND**
- No flaming droplets observed **AND**
- No sustained flaming after pilot removal.

## 3.2 Derivation: Specific Optical Density (Smoke Obscuration)

Smoke density is measured using a photometric system in the exhaust duct.

**Step 1**: Record incident light intensity $I_0$ and transmitted intensity $I$.

**Step 2**: Apply the Beer-Lambert relation in dimensionless form:
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_s &= \frac{V}{A \cdot L} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) \\
\text{where } V &= \text{exhaust duct volume (m}^3\text{)} \\
A &= \text{exposed specimen area (m}^2\text{)} \\
L &= \text{optical path length (m)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3**: For KTU calculation, suppose a test gives:
- $V = 0.4$ m³, $A = 0.0225$ m² (i.e., 150 mm × 150 mm), $L = 1.0$ m
- $I_0 = 100$, $I = 12$

**Step 4**: Compute:
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_s &= \frac{0.4}{0.0225 \times 1.0} \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{100}{12}\right) \\
&= 17.78 \times \log_{10}(8.333) \\
&= 17.78 \times 0.9208 \\
&\approx 16.37
\end{aligned}
$$

This $D_s$ value is then compared against the maximum permissible smoke index for the specific HSC application.

## 3.3 Derivation: Heat Release Rate via Oxygen Consumption

**Step 1**: Measure the volumetric oxygen consumption rate $\dot{V}_{O_2}$ in the exhaust duct (m³/s at STP).

**Step 2**: Apply Thornton's principle:
$$
\dot{Q} = E \cdot \dot{V}_{O_2} \cdot \rho_{O_2}
$$
where $E = 13.1$ MJ/kg O₂ (Thornton's constant for typical organics) and $\rho_{O_2} = 1.429$ kg/m³ at STP.

**Step 3**: For $\dot{V}_{O_2} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q} &= 13.1 \times 10^6 \, \frac{\text{J}}{\text{kg}} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \, \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{s}} \times 1.429 \, \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3} \\
&= 13.1 \times 10^6 \times 2.1435 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 2807.99 \, \text{W} \\
&\approx 2.81 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4**: Normalize to specimen area to obtain HRR per unit area:
$$
\dot{q}'' = \frac{\dot{Q}}{A} = \frac{2807.99}{0.0225} \approx 124.8 \, \text{kW/m}^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This normalized heat release rate is the key parameter compared against the FRM acceptance threshold (typically $\dot{q}''_{\text{peak}} \leq 65$ kW/m² for the first 600 s, depending on the specific 2010 FTP Code Part 10 amendment).

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Material Behaviour Observed | FRM Pass/Fail | Action |
|------------------------------|----------------|--------|
| $L_{d,\text{mean}} \leq 150$ mm, no droplets | **PASS** | Approve for HSC use in permitted locations |
| $L_{d,\text{mean}} \leq 150$ mm but $\max(L_d) = 210$ mm | **FAIL** | Reject; test additional specimens or reformulate |
| $L_{d,\text{mean}} = 165$ mm, no droplets | **FAIL** | Reject; mean criterion violated |
| $L_{d,\text{mean}} = 100$ mm, flaming droplets at 90 s | **FAIL** | Reject; droplets criterion violated |
| $L_{d,\text{mean}} = 80$ mm, $D_s > 200$ | **FAIL** | Reject; smoke criterion violated |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Test Sequence for FRM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Specimen Receipt] --> B[Visual Inspection and Dimension Check]
    B --> C[Conditioning at 23C and 50 percent RH for 24 hours]
    C --> D[Weigh Specimen and Record Initial Mass]
    D --> E[Mount Specimen in Vertical Holder]
    E --> F[Calibrate Radiant Panel to Target Heat Flux]
    F --> G[Ignite Pilot Flame and Start Data Acquisition]
    G --> H[Expose Specimen for Defined Test Duration]
    H --> I[Monitor Flame Spread and Mass Loss]
    I --> J[Remove Heat Source and Pilot Flame]
    J --> K[Record Self-Extinguishing Time]
    K --> L[Measure Damaged Length and Photograph]
    L --> M[Collect Smoke and Gas Data]
    M --> N[Apply Acceptance Criteria]
    N --> O{All Criteria Satisfied?}
    O --> P[Material Classified as FRM]
    O --> Q[Material Rejected and Re-Test Required]
    P --> R[Issue Type Approval Certificate]
    Q --> S[Modify Formulation and Re-Test]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Test Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    end

    subgraph S5[Data Acquisition Subsystem]
        E1[Signal Conditioner]

        E3[Report Generator]
    end
    S1 --> S2

    S3 --> S4

```

## 4.3 Process-State Diagram for the Test Methodology

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Calibration: Power ON

    Ready --> Exposing: Pilot Ignition
```
