```mermaid
    Rejected --> [*]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
Define the term "fire-restricting material" as applied to high-speed craft and state the regulatory document governing its test procedure.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **fire-restricting material (FRM)** is a combustible material that, when tested in accordance with the procedures specified in the **2010 FTP Code (Resolution MSC.307(88)), Annex 1, Part 10**, meets prescribed limits for damaged length, heat release, smoke, and toxic gas emission, such that it is permitted for use in the construction of high-speed craft (HSC) under **HSC Code Chapter 7** without compromising the safety of the craft in the event of a fire. **(3 marks)**

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
List any **four** acceptance criteria that a material must satisfy to be classified as fire-restricting under the 2010 FTP Code.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four acceptance criteria are:
1. Mean damaged length $\leq$ **150 mm** for the test specimens. **(1 mark)**
2. Maximum damaged length of any single specimen $\leq$ **200 mm**. **(1 mark)**
3. No flaming or burning droplets observed during the test. **(½ mark)**
4. Specific optical density of smoke and toxic gas indices within prescribed limits. **(½ mark)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]`**

**(a)** Describe in detail the test apparatus, specimen preparation, and procedure prescribed by the 2010 FTP Code, Annex 1, Part 10 for fire-restricting materials used in high-speed craft. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fire test on a candidate FRM yields the following damaged lengths for 4 specimens: 105 mm, 118 mm, 112 mm, 130 mm. Determine whether the material passes the FRM acceptance criterion for mean damaged length and maximum damaged length. Justify with calculations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):**

1. **Test apparatus** (3 marks):
   - A **small-scale fire test apparatus** consisting of a vertical specimen holder (150 mm × 150 mm), a **conical radiant electric heater** delivering a calibrated heat flux (typically 30 kW/m²), and a **pilot flame burner** for ignition.
   - **Exhaust hood** with controlled airflow and instrumentation including load cell, thermocouples, smoke photometer, and gas analyzers (CO, CO₂, HCl, HCN).
   - Data acquisition system recording at **≥ 1 Hz** continuously.

2. **Specimen preparation** (2 marks):
   - Specimen size **150 mm × 150 mm**; thickness equal to that used in service but not exceeding **50 mm**.
   - Minimum of **3 specimens** per orientation; conditioned at **23 ± 2 °C** and **50 ± 5 % RH** for a minimum of **24 hours**.

3. **Test procedure** (2 marks):
   - Mount the specimen vertically; ignite the pilot flame and expose to the radiant heat source for the **defined test duration**.
   - Record flame spread, mass loss, surface temperatures, smoke density, and gas emissions continuously.
   - At the end of exposure, remove heat source and pilot; record **self-extinguishing time**.
   - Measure damaged length $L_d$ from the flame impingement line.

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

**Step 1** — Sum of damaged lengths:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum L_{d,i} &= 105 + 118 + 112 + 130 \\
&= 465 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2** — Mean damaged length:
$$
L_{d,\text{mean}} = \frac{465}{4} = 116.25 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 3** — Maximum damaged length:
$$
L_{d,\text{max}} = \max(105, 118, 112, 130) = 130 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 4** — Apply acceptance criteria:
- $L_{d,\text{mean}} = 116.25 \, \text{mm} \leq 150 \, \text{mm}$ ✓ **(3.5 marks — pass on mean criterion)**
- $L_{d,\text{max}} = 130 \, \text{mm} \leq 200 \, \text{mm}$ ✓ **(3.5 marks — pass on maximum criterion)**

**Conclusion**: The material **passes** both acceptance criteria for damaged length. Assuming no burning droplets and acceptable smoke/toxicity indices, the material may be classified as **fire-restricting** under 2010 FTP Code, Annex 1, Part 10.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]`**

**(a)** Explain the philosophy of fire safety in high-speed craft and state how it differs from conventional SOLAS ships. Discuss why a dedicated test for fire-restricting materials is required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A specimen of size 150 mm × 150 mm is tested in a smoke chamber of volume 0.4 m³ and optical path length 1.0 m. The incident light intensity is 100 units and the transmitted intensity is 8 units. Calculate the specific optical density $D_s$ and comment on whether the smoke emission is acceptable for FRM classification, given the prescribed limit is $D_s \leq 200$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):**

1. **HSC evacuation philosophy** (2 marks): HSC Code Chapter 7 is built on the principle of **rapid total evacuation** (≤ 17 minutes for category A craft) using marine evacuation systems (MES), slides, or chutes. This contrasts with SOLAS Chapter II-2 philosophy where evacuation is slower and "defend in place" is partially applicable.

2. **Differences from SOLAS** (2 marks):
   - Different fire load assumptions
   - Different material approval framework (HSC FTP Code / 2010 FTP Code Annex 1)
   - Special provisions for vehicle decks and machinery spaces
   - Stricter requirements on toxic gas emissions due to short evacuation time

3. **Need for dedicated FRM test** (3 marks):
   - HSC use extensive **lightweight composite materials** (aluminium honeycomb, FRP panels).
   - Conventional SOLAS fire tests (e.g., A/B/F class tests) are over-conservative for non-structural FRM applications in HSC.
   - The FRM test provides a **small-scale, performance-based** evaluation of flame spread, heat release, smoke, and toxic gases.
   - The test quantifies the **damage length** and **HRR**, which are key indicators of fire contribution from non-load-bearing HSC components.

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

**Step 1** — Identify inputs:
- $V = 0.4 \, \text{m}^3$
- $A = 0.15 \times 0.15 = 0.0225 \, \text{m}^2$
- $L = 1.0 \, \text{m}$
- $I_0 = 100$, $I = 8$

**Step 2** — Apply the $D_s$ formula:
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_s &= \frac{V}{A \cdot L} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) \\
&= \frac{0.4}{0.0225 \times 1.0} \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{100}{8}\right) \\
&= 17.778 \times \log_{10}(12.5) \\
&= 17.778 \times 1.0969 \\
&\approx 19.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — Compare with acceptance criterion:
- $D_s = 19.50 \leq 200$ ✓ **(3 marks)**
- Hence, the smoke emission is **well within the acceptable limit** for FRM classification. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Pitfall 1**: Many students forget to convert specimen dimensions from mm to m before substitution. This leads to a 1000× error in $D_s$ calculation. **(–2 marks)**
> - **Pitfall 2**: Students often use the common (base 10) log incorrectly; ensure $\log_{10}$ is used, not $\ln$ (natural log). **(–1 mark)**
> - **Pitfall 3**: Do not confuse $D_s$ (specific optical density, dimensionless) with $D_m$ (mass optical density, m²/g). The 2010 FTP Code specifies $D_s$.
> - **Pitfall 4**: In the damaged-length calculation, remember the criterion applies to **mean** AND **maximum** independently — failing either one rejects the material.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Regulatory anchor**: 2010 FTP Code, Resolution **MSC.307(88)**, **Annex 1, Part 10** for FRM test on HSC.
- **Specimen**: 150 mm × 150 mm × actual thickness (max 50 mm), conditioned at 23 °C and 50 % RH for 24 h.
- **Heat source**: Conical radiant heater delivering ~30 kW/m² + pilot flame.
- **Two key acceptance criteria**: $L_{d,\text{mean}} \leq 150$ mm **and** $L_{d,\text{max}} \leq 200$ mm.
- **Other criteria**: No burning droplets, no sustained flaming, $D_s$ and toxic gas indices within limits.
- **Smoke formula**: $D_s = \frac{V}{A \cdot L} \cdot \log_{10}(I_0 / I)$ — remember unit conversions.
- **Heat release formula**: $\dot{Q} = E \cdot \dot{V}_{O_2} \cdot \rho_{O_2}$ with $E \approx 13.1$ MJ/kg O₂.
- **Philosophy**: HSC fire safety is built on **rapid evacuation** (≤ 17 min) using MES, demanding **performance-based** small-scale tests.
- **Difference from A/B class divisions**: FRM test is **not** a structural fire-resistance test; it is a small-scale material qualification test for non-load-bearing applications.
- **Always cite the year and annex/part** in board answers to score full marks in regulatory questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test for Fire-Resisting Divisions of High-Speed Craft
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Test for Fire-Resisting Divisions of High-Speed Craft

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Fire-Resisting Divisions** on a High-Speed Craft (HSC) are structural bulkheads, decks, and linings that are designed and constructed to resist the passage of fire, heat, and smoke for a specified duration. The **Test for Fire-Resisting Divisions of High-Speed Craft** is a standardized fire performance evaluation procedure prescribed under the **International Code of Safety for High-Speed Craft (HSC Code 2000, as amended)** and the **Fire Test Procedures (FTP) Code** — specifically **Resolution MSC.61(67), Annex 1, Part 3** — adopted by the International Maritime Organization (IMO).

The test assesses the ability of a division to:
1. Maintain **structural integrity** (no cracks, openings, or passage of flames/hot gases).
2. Limit **heat transfer** (insulation) to the unexposed face.
3. Resist **smoke and gas penetration** during a controlled standard fire exposure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** The HSC Code applies to craft capable of sustained speed **≥ 30 knots** (or **≥ 20 knots** for craft with special features). It contains dedicated fire-safety chapters (Chapter 4) that supplement, but are distinct from, the SOLAS Convention fire test regime.

### 1.2 Classification of Fire-Resisting Divisions

| Class | Integrity (min) | Insulation (min) | Typical Use in HSC |
|:-----:|:---------------:|:----------------:|:-------------------|
| **A-60** | 60 | 60 | Boundaries of high-risk spaces (machinery, galleys) |
| **A-30** | 30 | 30 | Boundaries of moderate-risk spaces |
| **A-15** | 15 | 15 | Boundaries of low-risk spaces |
| **A-0** | 0 (integrity only) | 0 | Smoke-tight integrity-only boundaries |
| **B-15** | 15 | 15 | Corridors, stairway enclosures |
| **B-0** | 0 (integrity only) | 0 | Lightweight smoke barriers |
| **C** | — | — | No fire rating; smoke/leakage control only |
| **H** | 60 | — | Helicopter landing area (helideck) — special provisions |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Oven Test"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition:** Imagine placing a slice of a ship's wall into a giant industrial pizza oven. One side faces **real fire** at temperatures climbing to over **1000 °C** within an hour. The other side has temperature sensors glued to it. Your job? Determine how long the wall stops the fire from breaking through (**integrity**) AND how hot the cool side gets (**insulation**). If the average temperature on the "safe side" rises by more than **140 °C**, or any single point gets hotter than **180 °C**, the wall **fails**. This is exactly the test conducted on fire-resisting divisions in a dedicated test furnace.

### 1.4 Governing Standards

- **HSC Code 2000** (Resolution MSC.97(73), as amended) — Chapter 4, Regulation 4.4 and 4.5.
- **FTP Code** (Resolution MSC.61(67)) — Annex 1, Part 3.
- **Resolution MSC.91(98)** and **MSC.485(100)** — amendments.
- **SOLAS Chapter II-2** — companion test philosophy (where HSC Code is silent).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For high-speed craft, the test is **substantively similar** to the SOLAS A/B-class test, but with **additional dynamic-load and vibration considerations** relevant to high-speed operation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Standard Fire Time–Temperature Curve

The fire exposure follows a **standardized hydrocarbon-like heating curve**, derived from actual cellulosic fire data. The furnace temperature is controlled to follow:

$$
T(t) \;=\; 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8t+1) \;+\; 20
$$

Where:
- $T(t)$ = average furnace temperature in **°C** at time $t$.
- $t$ = time elapsed since ignition of the test, in **minutes**.

This is the **same curve** used for SOLAS A/B-class tests, the FTP Code, ISO 834, and most marine structural fire tests worldwide.

### 2.2 Sample Reference Temperatures

| Time $t$ (min) | $T(t)$ (°C) | Engineering Significance |
|:--------------:|:-----------:|:------------------------|
| 5 | 576 | Steel begins to lose strength noticeably |
| 10 | 678 | Critical for thin-plate members |
| 15 | 738 | A-15 test endpoint |
| 30 | 842 | A-30 test endpoint |
| 60 | 945 | A-60 test endpoint |
| 90 | 1006 | Hydrocarbon (H) class |
| 120 | 1049 | Extended hydrocarbon |

> [!NOTE]
> The actual furnace tolerance is **± 10 %** deviation from the standard curve, and the **area-under-the-curve** tolerance is also constrained by the FTP Code.

### 2.3 Failure Criteria — The Two Pillars of Fire Resistance

#### (a) Integrity Criterion
The division fails the integrity test if **any one** of the following occurs on the unexposed face:
1. **Sustained flaming** for ≥ 10 seconds.
2. **Ignition** of the cotton-wool pad (when held against suspected cracks/joints).
3. **Through-gap opening** exceeding allowable limits (typically 6 mm width or 25 mm × 25 mm gauge).

#### (b) Insulation Criterion (A-class only)
The division fails insulation if:
- **Average temperature rise** on the unexposed face > **140 °C** above initial temperature.
- **Maximum temperature rise** at any single point (including joints) > **180 °C** above initial temperature.

For **B-class**, the corresponding limits are relaxed: **≤ 225 °C** maximum rise at any point, with no time-averaged criterion required for B-0.

### 2.4 KTU Formula & Constant Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Constant | Expression | Units | Application |
|:-:|:------------------:|:----------:|:-----:|:-----------:|
| 1 | Standard fire curve | $T = 345\log_{10}(8t+1) + 20$ | °C, min | Furnace control |
| 2 | Integrity rise limit (A-class avg.) | $\Delta T_{avg} \le 140$ | °C | Insulation pass mark |
| 3 | Insulation max point (A-class) | $\Delta T_{max} \le 180$ | °C | Hot-spot pass mark |
| 4 | Insulation max point (B-class) | $\Delta T_{max} \le 225$ | °C | B-class pass mark |
| 5 | Test duration for A-60 | $t \ge 60$ | min | Full rating |
| 6 | Test duration for A-30 | $t \ge 30$ | min | Half rating |
| 7 | Test duration for A-15 | $t \ge 15$ | min | Quarter rating |
| 8 | Cotton-wool pad ignition time | $\ge 10$ s sustained | s | Integrity fail trigger |
| 9 | Through-gap limit | $\le 6$ mm width | mm | Integrity fail trigger |
| 10 | Helideck (H-class) duration | $\ge 60$ | min | Helicopter facility |
| 11 | Number of furnace thermocouples (per ISO/FTP) | $\ge 9$ | — | Furnace control |
| 12 | Unexposed thermocouple density | $\ge 1$ per $1.5\ \text{m}^2$ | per m² | Spec. instrumentation |

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Ship design**: Determines bulkhead/deck material selection (steel, aluminium, composite).
- **Approval/certification**: Required by classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, BV) before a division is accepted for use.
- **Fire-zone modelling**: The time–temperature curve underpins CFD fire simulation (FDS, OpenFOAM).
- **Risk-based design**: Forms the basis of "equivalent" or "performance-based" alternative designs under HSC Reg. 4.4.3.
- **Helideck operations**: H-class divisions are critical because **kerosene pool fires** from a helicopter crash can exceed **1100 °C** in minutes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The HSC Code requires that any **non-combustible**, **low-flame-spread** material used in the division be supported by test data from a recognized laboratory (e.g., BRE, SINTEF, NIST, IIT Madras) accredited to ISO/IEC 17025.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Fire Time–Temperature Curve

The curve $T = 345 \log_{10}(8t+1) + 20$ is **empirical**, but it is anchored in first-order heat-transfer physics. Let us derive its functional form.

**Step 1 — Energy balance on a semi-infinite solid**

When a thick wall is exposed to a fire, the surface temperature $T_s$ follows:

$$
\rho \, c_p \, \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} \;=\; k \, \frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}
$$

This is the **1-D transient heat conduction equation** (Fourier), where:
- $\rho$ = density of the wall material,
- $c_p$ = specific heat capacity,
- $k$ = thermal conductivity,
- $x$ = depth coordinate into the wall.

**Step 2 — Boundary condition at the fire face**

The fire imposes a convective–radiative heat flux:

$$
q'' \;=\; h \left( T_f - T_s \right) \;+\; \varepsilon \sigma \left( T_f^{4} - T_s^{4} \right)
$$

Where $T_f$ is the fire (furnace) gas temperature.

**Step 3 — Empirical fit to cellulose fire data**

The IMO originally fitted experimental data from cellulosic crib fires in the 1960s and found that the *effective* gas temperature $T_f$ rises roughly as the **logarithm of time**. Using a least-squares fit on test data, the constants **345** and **8** were obtained, with the offset **20 °C** representing ambient.

**Step 4 — Verify at key time points**

For $t = 0$:

$$
T(0) = 345 \log_{10}(1) + 20 = 0 + 20 = 20 \ \text{°C} \quad \text{(ambient — correct)}
$$

For $t = 60$ min:

$$
T(60) = 345 \log_{10}(8 \times 60 + 1) + 20 = 345 \log_{10}(481) + 20
$$

$$
\log_{10}(481) \approx 2.6821 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 345 \times 2.6821 \approx 925.3
$$

$$
T(60) \approx 925.3 + 20 \approx 945.3 \ \text{°C} \quad \text{(matches IMO target)}
$$

For $t = 30$ min:

$$
T(30) = 345 \log_{10}(241) + 20 = 345 \times 2.382 + 20 \approx 821.8 + 20 = 841.8 \ \text{°C}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The IMO 945 °C at 60 min is often rounded to **945 °C**, but the *strict* FTP target is **945 °C ± 10 %**, i.e., the furnace must be within the band **850.5 °C – 1039.5 °C** at $t = 60$ min.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Sample Numerical Evaluation

**Problem:** Determine the furnace temperature required at the 15-minute, 30-minute, and 60-minute checkpoints for a fire test of an A-60 bulkhead.

**Step 1: Setup**

Use $T(t) = 345 \log_{10}(8t+1) + 20$.

**Step 2: At $t = 15$ min**

$$
T(15) = 345 \log_{10}(8 \cdot 15 + 1) + 20 = 345 \log_{10}(121) + 20
$$

$$
\log_{10}(121) \approx 2.0828 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 345 \times 2.0828 \approx 718.6
$$

$$
T(15) \approx 718.6 + 20 = 738.6 \ \text{°C}
$$

**Step 3: At $t = 30$ min**

Computed earlier:

$$
T(30) \approx 841.8 \ \text{°C}
$$

**Step 4: At $t = 60$ min**

Computed earlier:

$$
T(60) \approx 945.3 \ \text{°C}
$$

**Step 5: Tabulate the results**

| Time $t$ (min) | Furnace $T$ (°C) | Tolerance band (±10 %) (°C) |
|:--------------:|:-----------------:|:---------------------------:|
| 15 | 738.6 | 664.7 – 812.5 |
| 30 | 841.8 | 757.6 – 926.0 |
| 60 | 945.3 | 850.8 – 1039.8 |

### 3.3 The Insulation Analysis — Linearized Back-Calculation

A common examiner-favourite question: *"Given that the unexposed-side average temperature rose from 22 °C to 158 °C during the test, did the division pass the insulation criterion?"*

**Step 1: Compute the temperature rise**

$$
\Delta T_{avg} = 158 - 22 = 136 \ \text{°C}
$$

**Step 2: Compare to A-class limit**

$$
\Delta T_{avg} = 136 \ \text{°C} \;\; \le \;\; 140 \ \text{°C} \quad \text{(PASS — average criterion)}
$$

**Step 3: Check hot-spot rule**

If the maximum recorded at any thermocouple was 199 °C, then:

$$
\Delta T_{max} = 199 - 22 = 177 \ \text{°C} \;\; \le \;\; 180 \ \text{°C} \quad \text{(PASS — point criterion)}
$$

**Step 4: Conclusion**

The division **passes** both integrity (assumed satisfied) and insulation criteria → qualifies as **A-class fire-resisting division**.

> [!NOTE]
> If either $\Delta T_{avg} > 140$ °C or $\Delta T_{max} > 180$ °C, the **first occurrence time** determines the actual **insulation time rating** (e.g., a division failing at 47 minutes is rated **A-30**, not A-60).

### 3.4 The H-Class (Helideck) Distinction

For helicopter landing areas on HSC, the fire is **hydrocarbon-based** (aviation kerosene, JP-1, Jet-A), with much higher heat release rates than cellulosic fires. The FTP Code permits the use of either:

- The **standard cellulosic curve** (A-class equivalent), OR
- The **hydrocarbon curve**: $T = 1080 \left(1 - 0.325 e^{-0.167t} - 0.675 e^{-2.5t}\right)$, with $T_0 = 20$ °C, for a **minimum 60 min** rating.

The hydrocarbon curve reaches **≈ 1100 °C** within **5 minutes** — a far more severe exposure than the standard cellulosic 945 °C in 60 min.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Fire-Resisting Division Test Rig

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TestFurnace["HIGH-TEMPERATURE FURNACE"]
        BurnerUnit["Gas/Oil Burner Array"]
        FurnaceChamber["Furnace Chamber (3m x 3m typical)"]
        FurnaceTC["Furnace Thermocouples (>=9)"]
        PressureCtrl["Pressure Control Damper"]
    end

    subgraph SpecimenZone["SPECIMEN MOUNTING ZONE"]
        SpecimenFrame["Specimen Holding Frame"]
        TestSpecimen["Fire-Resisting Division Specimen (Bulkhead / Deck)"]
        Joints["Joints, Penetrations, Cable Cut-outs"]
    end

    subgraph UnexposedZone["UNEXPOSED (SAFE) SIDE"]
        UnexpTC["Unexposed-Side Thermocouples (>=1 per 1.5 m^2)"]
        CottonPadTest["Cotton-Wool Pad Test Station"]
        DeflectionGauge["Deflection / Movement Gauge"]
        SmokeObs["Smoke & Flaming Observation"]
    end

    subgraph ControlRoom["DATA ACQUISITION & CONTROL"]
        DataLogger["Multi-Channel Data Logger"]
        CurveDisplay["Real-Time T(t) Curve Display"]
        AlarmSystem["Limit-Alarm (140C / 180C / 225C)"]
        OperatorPanel["Operator Console"]
    end

    BurnerUnit --> FurnaceChamber
    FurnaceChamber --> FurnaceTC
    PressureCtrl --> FurnaceChamber
    FurnaceChamber -- "Radiant + Convective Heat" --> TestSpecimen
    TestSpecimen --> SpecimenFrame
    TestSpecimen --- Joints
    TestSpecimen -- "Conducted Heat" --> UnexpTC
    TestSpecimen --> CottonPadTest
    TestSpecimen --> DeflectionGauge
    TestSpecimen --> SmokeObs
    FurnaceTC --> DataLogger
    UnexpTC --> DataLogger
    CottonPadTest --> DataLogger
    DeflectionGauge --> DataLogger
    SmokeObs --> DataLogger
    DataLogger --> CurveDisplay
    DataLogger --> AlarmSystem
    CurveDisplay --> OperatorPanel
    AlarmSystem --> OperatorPanel
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow — Test Acceptance / Rejection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: HSC Fire-Resisting Division Test]) --> SetRating{"Select Target Rating? (A60 / A30 / A15 / B15 / B0 / H)"}
    SetRating --> HeatExposure["Expose Specimen to Standard Fire Curve T = 345 log10(8t+1) + 20"]
    HeatExposure --> IntegrityCheck{"Integrity Check: Sustained Flaming > 10s? OR Cotton Pad Ignition? OR Gap > 6mm?"}
    IntegrityCheck -- "YES" --> Fail1[FAIL - Integrity]
    IntegrityCheck -- "NO" --> InsulationCheck{"Insulation Check: Delta T avg > 140C OR Delta T max > 180C (A-class)?"}
    InsulationCheck -- "YES" --> Fail2[FAIL - Insulation - Record actual time-to-failure"]
    InsulationCheck -- "NO" --> TimeCheck{"Did the spec survive full target duration?"}
    TimeCheck -- "YES" --> Pass[PASS - Certified to Target Rating]
    TimeCheck -- "NO" --> Continue["Continue test to next lower rating threshold"]
    Continue --> HeatExposure
    Fail1 --> Report[Issue Test Report with Failure Mode & Time]
    Fail2 --> Report
    Pass --> Report
    Report --> End([End])
```

### 4.3 Hierarchy of HSC Fire-Resisting Divisions (SOLAS-equivalent Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HSC[High-Speed Craft HSC Code Ch.4] --> FRD[Fire-Resisting Divisions]
    FRD --> AClass[A-Class Divisions]
    FRD --> BClass[B-Class Divisions]
    FRD --> CClass[C-Class Divisions]
    FRD --> HClass[H-Class Helideck]

    AClass --> A60[A-60: 60 min]
    AClass --> A30[A-30: 30 min]
    AClass --> A15[A-15: 15 min]
    AClass --> A0[A-0: integrity only]

    BClass --> B15[B-15: 15 min]
    BClass --> B0[B-0: integrity only]

    HClass --> HCurve1[Cellulosic: 945 C at 60 min]
    HClass --> HCurve2[Hydrocarbon: 1100 C peak, 60 min]

    A60 --> Lim1[Avg dT <= 140 C, Max dT <= 180 C]
    A30 --> Lim1
    A15 --> Lim1
    A0 --> Lim2[Integrity only, no insulation limit]
    B15 --> Lim3[Max dT <= 225 C]
    B0 --> Lim4[Integrity only]
    CClass --> Lim5[No fire rating - smoke / leakage]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*Define "fire-resisting division" as applied to a High-Speed Craft. List the main classes of such divisions recognized by the HSC Code.*

**Model Answer (10–12 lines):**

A **fire-resisting division** on a High-Speed Craft is a bulkhead, deck, or lining constructed of non-combustible or fire-retardant materials that, when subjected to a standard fire test, prevents the passage of flames and hot gases (integrity) and limits temperature rise on the unexposed face (insulation) for a defined period. The HSC Code (Chapter 4) recognizes the following main classes:

1. **A-class** divisions — made of steel or equivalent non-combustible material; rated **A-60, A-30, A-15, A-0** based on test duration.
2. **B-class** divisions — generally lightweight non-combustible partitions; rated **B-15** and **B-0**.
3. **C-class** divisions — non-combustible materials with no fire-rating requirement.
4. **H-class** divisions — for helideck structures; tested against cellulosic or hydrocarbon curves for 60 min.

The tests are governed by the **FTP Code (Res. MSC.61(67), Annex 1, Part 3)**.

> **Key Point:** A "Class" is a *rating*, not a *material*. The same steel bulkhead can be **A-30** or **A-60** depending on thickness and insulation.

---

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
*State the standard time–temperature relationship used in fire-resistance tests of ship divisions and calculate the furnace temperature at 45 minutes.*

**Model Answer:**

The standard time–temperature relationship prescribed by the FTP Code (Annex 1, Part 3) is:

$$
T(t) = 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8t + 1) + 20 \quad \text{(°C, t in min)}
$$

**Calculation at $t = 45$ min:**

$$
T(45) = 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8 \times 45 + 1) + 20 = 345 \cdot \log_{10}(361) + 20
$$

$$
\log_{10}(361) \approx 2.5575 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 345 \times 2.5575 \approx 882.3
$$

$$
T(45) \approx 882.3 + 20 \approx 902.3 \ \text{°C}
$$

**Answer:** Furnace temperature at 45 min ≈ **902 °C**.

> **Engineering interpretation:** At 45 min, the fire exposure is roughly **70 % of the severity** of the 60-min target, but the *temperature* is already at **95 %** of the 60-min value due to the logarithmic nature of the curve.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Path 1: Test Procedure & Pass Criteria

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Model Question]**

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, describe the test apparatus and procedure for determining the fire resistance of an "A-class" division used in a High-Speed Craft. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Explain the integrity and insulation criteria that determine whether the division passes the A-60 rating. A specimen's unexposed-face thermocouples show a maximum temperature of 198 °C and an average of 156 °C, starting from 22 °C. Determine whether the specimen qualifies as A-60, A-30, or fails. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Test apparatus and procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Furnace**: A vertical or horizontal gas-fired furnace of internal dimensions typically **3 m × 3 m**, capable of withstanding temperatures up to **1200 °C** and following the standard curve.
2. **Specimen mounting frame**: Rigid steel frame holding the test specimen (≥ 2.5 m × 2.5 m for a bulkhead) flush with the furnace opening.
3. **Furnace instrumentation**: Minimum **9 thermocouples** distributed in the furnace, with a **plate thermometer** also recommended.
4. **Unexposed-side instrumentation**: Disc or rod thermocouples distributed **one per 1.5 m²** (minimum 5 for typical specimens), plus a **cotton-wool pad test rig** and **gap gauges**.
5. **Procedure**:
   - Mount the specimen with all **representative joints, penetrations, and cable cut-outs** included.
   - Record initial (ambient) temperature of the unexposed face.
   - Ignite the furnace; control the burners to follow $T = 345 \log_{10}(8t+1) + 20$.
   - Continuously log temperatures, observe the unexposed face for flaming, perform **cotton-wool pad tests** at intervals (typically every 5–10 min), and measure gap widths.
   - Maintain the test for the target duration (**60 min for A-60**).
6. **Data acquisition**: Multi-channel data logger with **± 4 °C accuracy**, logging at intervals ≤ 1 min.
7. **Reporting**: Issue a test certificate with the actual **integrity time**, **insulation time**, and the **class assigned**.

**(b) Pass/Fail evaluation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute temperature rises**

$$
\Delta T_{avg} = 156 - 22 = 134 \ \text{°C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{max} = 198 - 22 = 176 \ \text{°C}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare against A-60 criteria**

| Criterion | Limit (A-class) | Observed | Result |
|:---------:|:---------------:|:--------:|:------:|
| Average rise $\Delta T_{avg}$ | $\le 140$ °C | 134 °C | **PASS** |
| Maximum point rise $\Delta T_{max}$ | $\le 180$ °C | 176 °C | **PASS** |
| Integrity (assumed satisfied) | No flaming / pad ignition / gap > 6 mm | — | Assumed **PASS** |

**Step 3 — Conclusion**

Both integrity and insulation criteria are satisfied for the full **60 min** test duration.

**The specimen qualifies as A-60** — the highest fire-resistance class for HSC divisions.

> **Edge case:** If $\Delta T_{max}$ had been, say, 220 °C, the specimen would have **failed insulation at the first thermocouple reading crossing 180 °C** — that time would be the actual **insulation rating**, e.g., 38 min → **A-30**.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Path 2: Standards, Curves & H-Class Distinction

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified Model Question]**

**(a)** *Compare the SOLAS and HSC Code approaches to fire-resisting divisions. Why are HSC-specific test provisions necessary? (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Derive the standard fire temperature at 20, 40, 60, and 120 minutes. Also compute and explain the helideck (H-class) hydrocarbon-curve temperature at 5 min and 30 min, and discuss why it is more severe. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) SOLAS vs HSC Code — Comparison (7 marks):**

| Aspect | SOLAS Ch. II-2 | HSC Code Ch. 4 |
|:-------|:---------------|:---------------|
| **Applicability** | Conventional ships | Craft ≥ 30 knots (or special craft ≥ 20 knots) |
| **Test authority** | FTP Code Annex 1 Part 3 | FTP Code Annex 1 Part 3 (same test) |
| **Vibration load** | Not explicitly considered | **Dynamic-load test** required (vibration of HSC) |
| **Damage scenarios** | Flooding, collision | **Higher survivability standards**, smaller crew |
| **H-class (helideck)** | Optional | More commonly required due to high-speed ferry operations |
| **Evacuation time** | Longer | **Shorter** → higher A-class coverage required |
| **Materials** | Steel dominant | **Aluminium, composites, FRP** allowed with extra testing |

**Why HSC-specific provisions are necessary:**

1. **Speed-induced vibration & dynamic loading** can compromise bonded fire insulation over time — HSC Code mandates additional cyclic-load testing.
2. **Lightweight construction** (aluminium, composites) has **lower melting points** and **faster heat-up** — alternative test methods are required.
3. **Smaller complement and shorter evacuation times** mean any fire-resisting division failure is more catastrophic.
4. **Helidecks on HSC** are more common than on conventional ships, so the H-class (helideck) provisions are critical.

**(b) Numerical derivations (7 marks):**

**Standard cellulosic curve:** $T(t) = 345 \log_{10}(8t+1) + 20$

At $t = 20$ min:

$$
T(20) = 345 \log_{10}(161) + 20 = 345 \times 2.2068 + 20 \approx 781.4 \ \text{°C}
$$

At $t = 40$ min:

$$
T(40) = 345 \log_{10}(321) + 20 = 345 \times 2.5065 + 20 \approx 884.7 \ \text{°C}
$$

At $t = 60$ min:

$$
T(60) \approx 945.3 \ \text{°C} \quad \text{(as derived earlier)}
$$

At $t = 120$ min:

$$
T(120) = 345 \log_{10}(961) + 20 = 345 \times 2.9827 + 20 \approx 1049.0 \ \text{°C}
$$

**Hydrocarbon H-class curve:** $T(t) = 1080 \left( 1 - 0.325 e^{-0.167t} - 0.675 e^{-2.5t} \right) + 20$

At $t = 5$ min:

$$
e^{-0.167 \times 5} = e^{-0.835} \approx 0.4341
$$

$$
e^{-2.5 \times 5} = e^{-12.5} \approx 3.73 \times 10^{-6} \approx 0
$$

$$
T(5) = 1080 (1 - 0.325 \times 0.4341 - 0) + 20 \approx 1080 \times 0.8589 + 20 \approx 947.6 \ \text{°C}
$$

At $t = 30$ min:

$$
e^{-0.167 \times 30} = e^{-5.01} \approx 0.00668
$$

$$
e^{-2.5 \times 30} = e^{-75} \approx 0
$$

$$
T(30) = 1080 (1 - 0.325 \times 0.00668) + 20 \approx 1080 \times 0.99783 + 20 \approx 1097.9 \ \text{°C}
$$

**Summary of results:**

| Time $t$ (min) | Cellulosic $T$ (°C) | Hydrocarbon H-class $T$ (°C) |
|:--------------:|:-------------------:|:----------------------------:|
| 5 | 576 | **948** |
| 20 | 781 | — |
| 30 | 842 | **1098** |
| 40 | 885 | — |
| 60 | **945** | ≈ 1100 |
| 120 | 1049 | — |

**Why hydrocarbon is more severe:** The hydrocarbon fire reaches **~950 °C in just 5 min**, whereas the cellulosic fire takes **60 min** to reach the same temperature. This represents a **12× faster heating rate**, simulating the catastrophic release of aviation fuel in a helideck crash. The steel deck structure must therefore be either much thicker, or be protected by intumescent coatings, ceramic blankets, or active fire-suppression systems.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the +20 °C offset** when computing $T(t)$. Students often quote $T = 345 \log_{10}(8t+1)$ only — losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing the A-60 *rating* with the A-60 *test duration*.** The rating is the *time to failure* in minutes; the test is run for 60 minutes.
> 3. **Mixing up integrity and insulation criteria.** Integrity = no flame/gas; Insulation = temperature rise on the cold side. Many students swap them.
> 4. **Using SOLAS H-class curves** when the question asks for HSC H-class — they are nearly identical, but examiners expect the IMO/FTP terminology, not generic "ISO 834 hydrocarbon curve."
> 5. **Forgetting the cotton-wool pad test** as an integrity criterion — it is a *mandatory* part of the FTP Code procedure.
> 6. **Stating "B-0 means no test"** — B-0 still requires an integrity test (no flames) but no insulation test. Marks are deducted for saying "untested."

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Governing instrument:** HSC Code 2000 (Res. MSC.97(73), as amended), Chapter 4, and the **FTP Code (Res. MSC.61(67), Annex 1, Part 3)**.
- **Standard fire curve:** $T(t) = 345 \log_{10}(8t+1) + 20$ — applies to all A/B/C-class tests.
- **HSC-specific additions:** Dynamic-load (vibration) testing, H-class helideck tests, lightweight-material tests.
- **A-class pass criteria:** Integrity (no sustained flaming, no pad ignition, gap ≤ 6 mm) + Insulation ($\Delta T_{avg} \le 140$ °C, $\Delta T_{max} \le 180$ °C).
- **B-class pass criteria:** Integrity + Insulation ($\Delta T_{max} \le 225$ °C; no average criterion for B-0).
- **C-class:** No fire-rating required; smoke and leakage control only.
- **H-class (helideck):** Either cellulosic or hydrocarbon curve; minimum 60 min; **hydrocarbon curve reaches ~1100 °C** in 30 min.
- **Time ratings:** A-60, A-30, A-15, A-0, B-15, B-0 — derived from test *time to first failure*.
- **Furnace tolerance:** ± 10 % of $T(t)$ at any time; the area-under-curve must also lie within prescribed bands.
- **Specimen size:** Typically **≥ 2.5 m × 2.5 m**, with **all representative joints and penetrations** included.
- **Cotton-wool pad test:** Integrity check; pad held against suspected cracks/joints; **≥ 10 s sustained flaming** on the pad = integrity fail.
- **Thermocouple density:** Minimum **9 furnace TCs** and **1 unexposed TC per 1.5 m²**.
- **Why this matters for HSC:** High-speed craft have shorter evacuation times, lighter materials, and frequent helideck operations → fire-resisting divisions are **first-line-of-defence** in any fire scenario.
- **Key equations to memorize:**
  1. $T(t) = 345 \log_{10}(8t+1) + 20$ (°C, t in min)
  2. $\Delta T_{avg} \le 140$ °C (A-class insulation)
  3. $\Delta T_{max} \le 180$ °C (A-class hot-spot)
  4. $\Delta T_{max} \le 225$ °C (B-class)
  5. $T_{HC}(t) = 1080 (1 - 0.325 e^{-0.167t} - 0.675 e^{-2.5t}) + 20$ (hydrocarbon)
- **Common error to avoid:** Treating "class" as a *material specification* — it is a **performance rating** derived from a standardized test.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Hook:** **"3-4-5-6"** — **3** classes (A, B, C/H), **4** A-class ratings (A-60/30/15/0), **5** key numbers (20 °C ambient, 140 °C avg, 180 °C max, 225 °C B-max, 345 curve constant), **6** mm gap limit. Combine this with the **standard fire curve mnemonic** "**345 log-8-1 plus 20**" and you can answer 90 % of exam questions on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Navigation and Collision Avoidance
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Navigation and Collision Avoidance

# Navigation and Collision Avoidance — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Navigation (KTU 2024 Standard)**
> *Navigation* is the science, art, and practice of determining the position of a vessel and planning, recording, and controlling the movement of a ship from one place to another safely and efficiently across the surface of the water body.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition Box — Collision Avoidance**
> *Collision Avoidance* is the systematic application of the **International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972 (COLREGs)** — adopted by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** — combined with the use of technical aids (Radar, ARPA, AIS, ECDIS) to detect, assess, and execute manoeuvres that prevent two or more vessels from coming into physical contact.

---

## 1.1 The Three Pillars of Maritime Navigation

The IMO and KTU 2024 syllabus classify navigation into three primary domains:

| Domain | Purpose | Key Instruments |
|---|---|---|
| **Celestial Navigation** | Position fixing using celestial bodies (Sun, Moon, Stars, Planets) | Sextant, Chronometer, Nautical Almanac |
| **Terrestrial / Piloting Navigation** | Coastal navigation using fixed landmarks | Compass, Bearings, Charts, GPS |
| **Electronic Navigation** | Modern satellite and radio-based positioning | GPS, DGPS, AIS, ECDIS, Radar |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Highway Analogy**
> Imagine you are driving a **car on a one-way highway** that has no lane markings, no traffic police, and no streetlights — except you are doing it in the **fog**, with a 12-metre wide truck approaching from your opposite side. *Navigation* is knowing where you are on the highway (position fixing), while *Collision Avoidance* is the equivalent of using **indicators, horns, headlights, and the "keep-right" rule** so you don't crash. On the sea, fog is common (visibility often drops below **1 nautical mile**), ships are enormous (**ULCVs exceed 400 m**), and stopping distance can be several kilometres. Therefore, the rules (COLREGs) must be **strict, universal, and unambiguous** — exactly what the IMO drafted in 1972.

---

## 1.3 COLREGs — The Maritime "Highway Code"

The **International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (COLREGs / 72)** are divided into **five parts** containing **38 Rules**:

1. **Part A** — General (Rules 1–3): Application, Responsibility, General Definitions.
2. **Part B** — Steering and Sailing Rules (Rules 4–19): Conduct of vessels in any condition of visibility.
3. **Part C** — Lights and Shapes (Rules 20–31): Lights to be carried and their configuration.
4. **Part D** — Sound and Light Signals (Rules 32–37): Manoeuvring, warning, and distress signals.
5. **Part E** — Exemptions (Rule 38): Exemptions for vessels built before the rules came into force.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy of Precedence (Rule 2 — Responsibility)**
> No Rule of COLREGs can ever relieve a vessel of:
> 1. The responsibility of taking **all necessary precautions**.
> 2. The consequences of any **neglect of precaution**, or of any **default of any kind** which may be required by the ordinary practice of seamen, or by the special circumstances of the case.
>
> *In short: COLREGs tell you the MINIMUM. Seamanship demands the MAXIMUM.*

---

## 1.4 Stand-On vs. Give-Way Vessels (Rule 18 — Responsibility Between Vessels)

The cornerstone of collision avoidance logic is the **Priority Hierarchy**:

$$\text{Rule 18 Priority} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{(i)} \;\;\text{Vessel not under command (NUC)} \\ \text{(ii)} \text{Vessel restricted in ability to manoeuvre (RAM)} \\ \text{(iii)} \text{Vessel constrained by draught} \\ \text{(iv)} \text{Vessel engaged in fishing (when fishing)} \\ \text{(v)} \text{Sailing vessel} \\ \text{(vi)} \text{Power-driven vessel} \end{cases}$$

A vessel **lower** in this list must keep out of the way of any vessel **higher** in the list.

---

## 1.5 Key Terminology Master-List (KTU Examinable)

| Term | Standard Definition |
|---|---|
| **Stand-On Vessel** | Vessel which, by COLREGs, must maintain course and speed. |
| **Give-Way Vessel** | Vessel required to keep out of the way of another. |
| **Bearing (Relative)** | Angle measured clockwise from ship's head (0°–360°). |
| **CPA** | Closest Point of Approach — minimum distance that will occur. |
| **TCPA** | Time to Closest Point of Approach. |
| **Aspect** | The visual angle at which another vessel is seen (Green/Red/White light). |
| **Overtaking** | Approaching from more than **22.5° abaft the beam**. |
| **Head-on** | Meeting end-on or nearly end-on (within **6°** of reciprocal courses). |
| **Crossing** | Vessels on intersecting courses with stand-on/give-way assignment. |

---

## 1.6 Section 1 — Quick Visual Snapshot

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Maritime Safety"] --> B["Navigation"]
    A --> C["Collision Avoidance"]
    B --> B1["Position Fixing"]
    B --> B2["Course & Speed Control"]
    B --> B3["Watchkeeping"]
    C --> C1["COLREGs 1972"]
    C --> C2["Radar + ARPA"]
    C --> C3["AIS + ECDIS"]
    C1 --> C1A["Steering & Sailing Rules"]
    C1 --> C1B["Lights & Shapes"]
    C1 --> C1C["Sound & Light Signals"]
```

---

## 1.7 Why This Module Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The IMO's **Global Integrated Shipping Information System (GISIS)** reports that **75%–96%** of marine casualties involving vessels are caused by **human factors**, with navigational error and collision ranked as top contributors. The economic loss from a single supertanker collision can exceed **USD 1 Billion** (e.g., the *Exxon Valdez*, 1989). Hence, this module is a **direct life-and-property safety discipline** and forms a non-negotiable part of the **STCW Convention** certification under KTU's maritime curriculum.

# Navigation and Collision Avoidance — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 COLREGs Steering and Sailing Rules — Structured Logic

### 2.1.1 Rule 5 — Look-out (Foundation Rule)

> *"Every vessel shall at all times maintain a proper look-out by sight and hearing as well as by all available means appropriate in the prevailing circumstances and conditions so as to make a full appraisal of the situation and of the risk of collision."*

**The "Three Pillars" of Look-out:**
1. **Sight** — Naked eye + binoculars (even at night).
2. **Hearing** — Listening for fog signals, whistles.
3. **All Available Means** — Radar, AIS, ECDIS, VHF.

---

### 2.1.2 Rule 6 — Safe Speed

A vessel must proceed at a **safe speed** so that she can take proper and effective action to avoid collision and be stopped within a distance appropriate to the prevailing circumstances. Factors include:

- Visibility
- Traffic density
- Sea-state, wind, current
- Stopping distance and turning ability
- Draft in relation to available depth of water

$$\boxed{S_{\text{safe}} \le \frac{d_{\text{stop}}}{\text{Reaction Time} + \text{Brake Time}}}$$

---

### 2.1.3 Rule 7 — Risk of Collision

Risk of collision shall be deemed to exist if:
- The compass bearing of an approaching vessel **does not appreciably change**.
- Such bearing change is **insufficient** to exonerate the vessel.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Steady Bearing = Decreasing Range" Rule**
> If the **relative bearing** of a contact does not change appreciably (say < 2°–3° in several minutes), then the **range is closing**, and **collision risk is imminent**, regardless of how far away the contact *appears* to be. This is one of the highest-weightage concepts in KTU exams.

---

### 2.1.4 Rule 13 — Overtaking Situation

A vessel coming up with another from a direction **more than 22.5° abaft her beam** is an **overtaking vessel** and must keep out of the way. The overtaking vessel has **no stand-on rights** under Rule 18 even if it is a power-driven vessel overtaking a sailing vessel.

---

### 2.1.5 Rule 14 — Head-on Situation

When two power-driven vessels are meeting on **reciprocal or nearly reciprocal courses** (within **6°** either side), both shall alter course to **starboard** so that each passes on the **port side** of the other.

---

### 2.1.6 Rule 15 — Crossing Situation

When two power-driven vessels are crossing, the vessel which has the **other on her starboard side** (i.e., the give-way vessel sees the **green light** of the other) shall **keep out of the way**.

---

### 2.1.7 Rule 16 — Action by Give-Way Vessel

The give-way vessel shall, **as early as possible**, take **substantial** alteration of course and/or reduction of speed so as to pass at a safe distance.

---

### 2.1.8 Rule 17 — Action by Stand-on Vessel

The stand-on vessel shall:
- (i) Maintain course and speed.
- (ii) May take action to **avoid collision** by her manoeuvre alone **as soon as it becomes apparent** that the give-way vessel is **not taking appropriate action** (Rule 17(a)(ii)).
- The alteration of course should preferably be **to starboard** so the stand-on vessel does not cross ahead of the give-way vessel.

---

### 2.1.9 Rule 18 — Responsibility Between Vessels (Recap)

- A power-driven vessel underway shall keep out of the way of:
  - A vessel **not under command (NUC)**
  - A vessel **restricted in ability to manoeuvre (RAM)**
  - A vessel **constrained by her draught**
  - A **sailing vessel**
  - A vessel **engaged in fishing**
  - A **seaplane on water**

---

## 2.2 Lights and Shapes — Quick Master Table

| Vessel Type | Masthead Light | Side Lights | Stern Light | Special / Day Shape |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Power-driven ≥ 50 m | **2 white** (forward, aft) | Red (port) / Green (starboard) | **1 white** | — |
| Power-driven < 50 m | **1 white** | Red / Green | **1 white** | — |
| NUC | **2 red all-round** (vertical) | Red / Green | White | **2 black balls** vertical |
| RAM | **3 all-round** (red, white, red) | Red / Green | White | **Ball-Diamond-Ball** |
| Constrained by Draught | **3 red all-round** | Red / Green | White | **Cylinder** |
| Fishing (trawling) | **2 green-white** (vertical) | None | White | **2 cones** apex together |
| At Anchor | **1 white all-round** (forward) | None | None | **1 black ball** at bow |
| Aground | Anchor lights | None | None | **3 black balls** vertical |

## 2.3 Sound Signals — Manoeuvring (Rule 34)

| Signal | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **One short blast** | "I am altering my course to **starboard**." |
| **Two short blasts** | "I am altering my course to **port**." |
| **Three short blasts** | "I am operating **astern** (engines going backwards)." |
| **Five short blasts** | "I am **uncertain of your intentions**" (DANGER signal). |
| **One long blast** (every ≤ 2 min) | Power vessel making way in **restricted visibility**. |
| **Two long blasts** (every ≤ 2 min) | Power vessel **stopped** in restricted visibility. |
| **Bell + Gong + Bell** (in fog) | Vessel **at anchor** ringing for 5 seconds every minute. |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula List for Navigation & Collision Avoidance**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Speed (knots ↔ m/s)** | $V_{\text{m/s}} = V_{\text{kn}} \times 0.5144$ | m/s | 1 knot = 1 NM/h |
| 2 | **Distance travelled** | $D = V \times t$ | NM | $V$ in knots, $t$ in hours |
| 3 | **Position by Bearing & Distance** | $\Delta\varphi = D \cos B$, $\Delta\lambda = D \sin B / \cos\varphi$ | degrees | Mercator sailing |
| 4 | **Compass Error** | $E = T - C$ or $E = C - T$ | degrees | Deviation + Variation |
| 5 | **Range Rate (closing speed)** | $\dot{R} = \dot{R_0} + \dot{V}$ | knots | Used in ARPA tracking |
| 6 | **CPA Distance (vector form)** | $D_{\text{CPA}} = \vert R \sin(\theta_R - \alpha_T) \vert$ | NM | $\alpha_T$ = true course of target, $\theta_R$ = relative bearing |
| 7 | **TCPA (vector form)** | $T_{\text{CPA}} = \dfrac{R \cos(\theta_R - \alpha_T)}{V_R}$ | minutes | $V_R$ = relative speed |
| 8 | **Relative Motion Triangle** | $\vec{V_R} = \vec{V_T} - \vec{V_O}$ | m/s | Vector subtraction |
| 9 | **Stopping Distance (approx.)** | $D_s \approx \dfrac{V^2}{k}$ where $k \approx 30$–$80$ | m or NM | Empirical, depends on displacement |
| 10 | **Rate of Turn (ROT)** | $\text{ROT} = \dfrac{1091 \times \tan(\phi/2)}{V}$ | °/min | $V$ in knots, $\phi$ = rudder angle (heuristic) |
| 11 | **Advance (transfer)** | $A = R \sin\theta$ | NM | $R$ = tactical radius |
| 12 | **Transfer (lateral)** | $T = R(1 - \cos\theta$ | NM | Width of turn |
| 13 | **Time to Turn 90°** | $t_{90} = \dfrac{90}{\text{ROT}}$ | minutes | ROT in °/min |
| 14 | **Day's Run / DR** | $\text{Day's Run} = \sum(V_i \times t_i \cos\phi)$ | NM | Dead-reckoning |
| 15 | **Set & Drift correction** | $\text{Course Made Good} = \text{Heading} + \text{Leeway} + \text{SET}$ | degrees | |

---

## 2.5 ARPA (Automatic Radar Plotting Aids) — Core Concepts

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — ARPA**
> An **ARPA** is a computer-aided radar system that automatically tracks radar contacts (targets), calculates their **course, speed, CPA, TCPA**, and provides **trial manoeuvre** predictions to assist the OOW (Officer of the Watch) in collision avoidance.

### ARPA Capabilities (SOLAS V/19)
- Automatic acquisition of targets (≥ 20 manually + 20 automatically).
- Display of **course, speed, CPA, TCPA** for each target.
- **Trial manoeuvre** — what-if simulation.
- **Predicted area of danger (PAD)** visualisation.
- Audible/visual **collision alarm** when CPA < set limit and TCPA < set limit.

### ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) Settings
Typical OOW settings:
$$\text{CPA}_{\text{limit}} \in [0.5,\, 2.0]\; \text{NM}, \quad \text{TCPA}_{\text{limit}} \in [6,\, 30]\; \text{min}$$

---

## 2.6 AIS (Automatic Identification System)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — AIS**
> A shipborne **broadcast transponder** system operating on **VHF frequencies 161.975 MHz and 162.025 MHz** (channels 87B and 88B), exchanging identification, position, course, speed, and other navigational data **automatically** between ships and shore stations.

| AIS Class | Usage | Reporting Rate |
|---|---|---|
| **Class A** | SOLAS-mandated vessels | 2–10 sec (moving) |
| **Class B** | Non-SOLAS, recreational | 30 sec |
| **AIS-SART** | Survival craft, MOB | Once per minute |
| **AtoN (Aids to Navigation)** | Buoys, beacons | 3 minutes |

### AIS vs Radar vs ARPA — Comparison

| Feature | Radar | ARPA | AIS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Detect non-cooperating target? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (needs AIS transmitter) |
| Detect behind obstacles / in shadow? | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ Yes |
| Identify vessel name? | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ Yes (MMSI) |
| Works in heavy rain? | ✅ (S-band better) | ✅ | ✅ (independent of weather) |
| Vulnerable to spoofing? | Low | Low | High |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

The formulas above are deployed in:
1. **Radar Information Centres (RICs)** worldwide (e.g., UKHO, NAVTEX, MRCCs).
2. **VTS (Vessel Traffic Services)** at major ports (Singapore, Rotterdam, Kochi, Mumbai).
3. **TSS (Traffic Separation Schemes)** — IMO-designated lanes for traffic in busy straits (e.g., English Channel, Malacca Strait).
4. **ECDIS** — Electronic Chart Display and Information System, replacing paper charts.
5. **On-board Integrated Bridge Systems (IBS)** — combining Radar + ECDIS + AIS + Conning.

# Navigation and Collision Avoidance — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation of CPA and TCPA (Core ARPA Mathematics)

This is the **most frequently tested derivation** in KTU Module 3. We will go step-by-step, every algebraic transition shown explicitly.

### 3.1.1 The Geometric Setup

Let:
- **Own Ship (O)** move at velocity $\vec{V_O}$ over ground.
- **Target Ship (T)** move at velocity $\vec{V_T}$ over ground.
- Let $\vec{R}$ be the **relative position vector** from Own Ship to Target.
- The **relative velocity** is:

$$\vec{V_R} = \vec{V_T} - \vec{V_O}$$

We want to find:
- $D_{\text{CPA}}$ — minimum distance between O and T during approach.
- $T_{\text{CPA}}$ — time from "now" until that minimum distance occurs.

### 3.1.2 Expressing $\vec{R}(t)$

The relative position at time $t$ (in future) is:

$$\vec{R}(t) = \vec{R}(0) + \vec{V_R} \cdot t$$

The distance squared is:

$$D^2(t) = \vec{R}(t) \cdot \vec{R}(t) = \vert \vec{R}(0) + \vec{V_R} \cdot t \vert^2$$

Expanding the dot product:

$$D^2(t) = \vert \vec{R}(0) \vert^2 + 2 t \, \vec{R}(0) \cdot \vec{V_R} + t^2 \vert \vec{V_R} \vert^2$$

Let $R_0 = \vert \vec{R}(0) \vert$ (current range), $V_R = \vert \vec{V_R} \vert$ (relative speed). Then:

$$D^2(t) = R_0^2 + 2 t (\vec{R}_0 \cdot \vec{V_R}) + V_R^2 t^2$$

### 3.1.3 Minimizing the Distance

CPA occurs when $\frac{d}{dt}\left[D^2(t)\right] = 0$ (note: minimizing $D^2$ is equivalent to minimizing $D$ since $D \ge 0$).

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left[D^2(t)\right] = 2(\vec{R}_0 \cdot \vec{V_R}) + 2 V_R^2 t = 0$$

Solving for $t$:

$$\boxed{T_{\text{CPA}} = -\dfrac{\vec{R}_0 \cdot \vec{V_R}}{V_R^2}}$$

**Sign convention note:** If $T_{\text{CPA}} > 0$, CPA lies in the **future** (closing situation). If $T_{\text{CPA}} \le 0$, CPA is in the **past** (opening situation, no risk of collision).

### 3.1.4 Substituting Back to Get CPA Distance

Plug $t = T_{\text{CPA}}$ back into $D^2(t)$:

$$D_{\text{CPA}}^2 = R_0^2 - \dfrac{(\vec{R}_0 \cdot \vec{V_R})^2}{V_R^2}$$

Factor out $V_R^2$:

$$D_{\text{CPA}}^2 = \dfrac{R_0^2 V_R^2 - (\vec{R}_0 \cdot \vec{V_R})^2}{V_R^2}$$

Recognize the numerator as the **squared cross-product magnitude** (Lagrange's identity):

$$\vert \vec{R}_0 \times \vec{V_R} \vert^2 = \vert \vec{R}_0 \vert^2 \vert \vec{V_R} \vert^2 - (\vec{R}_0 \cdot \vec{V_R})^2$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{D_{\text{CPA}} = \dfrac{\vert \vec{R}_0 \times \vec{V_R} \vert}{V_R}}$$

Geometrically, $D_{\text{CPA}}$ equals the **perpendicular distance from Own Ship to the relative-motion line** of the target.

### 3.1.5 Magnitude Form (Useful for Numerical Problems)

In scalar form, if $\theta_R$ is the **relative bearing** of the target and $\alpha_T$ is the **true course of the target**:

$$\boxed{D_{\text{CPA}} = \vert R_0 \sin(\theta_R - \alpha_T) \vert}$$

$$\boxed{T_{\text{CPA}} = \dfrac{R_0 \cos(\theta_R - \alpha_T)}{V_R}}$$

(With signs: if numerator is positive, CPA is in the future; if negative, CPA is in the past.)

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (Model KTU Pattern — 7 marks):**

> Two vessels are approaching each other in open sea. Own Ship is at the origin, with true course $\alpha_O = 000°$ and speed $V_O = 12$ knots. Target ship is at position **5 NM on bearing 045°** relative to Own Ship, with true course $\alpha_T = 270°$ and speed $V_T = 8$ knots. Determine CPA and TCPA. Is there a collision risk?

### Step 1: Resolve Position Vector $\vec{R}_0$

$$R_{0x} = 5 \sin(045°) = 5 \times \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = 3.5355 \text{ NM}$$
$$R_{0y} = 5 \cos(045°) = 3.5355 \text{ NM}$$

$$\vec{R}_0 = (3.5355,\, 3.5355)\; \text{NM}$$

### Step 2: Resolve Velocity Vectors

$$V_{Ox} = 12 \sin(000°) = 0,\quad V_{Oy} = 12 \cos(000°) = 12$$

$$V_{Tx} = 8 \sin(270°) = -8,\quad V_{Ty} = 8 \cos(270°) = 0$$

### Step 3: Compute Relative Velocity $\vec{V_R} = \vec{V_T} - \vec{V_O}$

$$V_{Rx} = -8 - 0 = -8 \text{ kn},\quad V_{Ry} = 0 - 12 = -12 \text{ kn}$$

$$\vec{V_R} = (-8,\, -12)\; \text{kn}$$

### Step 4: Relative Speed $V_R$

$$V_R = \sqrt{(-8)^2 + (-12)^2} = \sqrt{64 + 144} = \sqrt{208} = 14.422 \text{ kn}$$

### Step 5: Compute Dot Product $\vec{R}_0 \cdot \vec{V_R}$

$$\vec{R}_0 \cdot \vec{V_R} = (3.5355)(-8) + (3.5355)(-12) = -28.284 - 42.426 = -70.710$$

### Step 6: Compute $T_{\text{CPA}}$

$$T_{\text{CPA}} = -\dfrac{(-70.710)}{(14.422)^2} = \dfrac{70.710}{208} = 0.3399 \text{ hours} = 20.4 \text{ minutes}$$

### Step 7: Compute Cross Product Magnitude $\vert \vec{R}_0 \times \vec{V_R} \vert$

$$\vec{R}_0 \times \vec{V_R} = (3.5355)(-12) - (3.5355)(-8) = -42.426 + 28.284 = -14.142$$

$$\vert \vec{R}_0 \times \vec{V_R} \vert = 14.142$$

### Step 8: Compute $D_{\text{CPA}}$

$$D_{\text{CPA}} = \dfrac{14.142}{14.422} = 0.9806 \text{ NM}$$

### Step 9: Interpretation

- **CPA ≈ 0.98 NM** — below the **safe passing distance of 2 NM** for ocean vessels. ⚠️
- **TCPA ≈ 20.4 minutes** — collision course imminent.
- **Action required:** Own Ship is the **give-way vessel** (target is on her starboard side — crossing situation per Rule 15). She must alter course **to starboard** and/or reduce speed **early and substantially** (Rule 16, Rule 8).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic in Crossing & Overtaking

| Scenario | Own Vessel Aspect (Lights Seen) | Rule Applicable | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Target ahead, fine on port bow (green/white lights) | Target approaching from **port** | Target is the **give-way vessel** | Maintain course & speed (stand-on) |
| Target ahead, fine on starboard bow (red/white) | Target approaching from **starboard** | **Rule 15** — Own vessel is give-way | Alter course to **starboard**, pass astern |
| Target ahead, both seeing **masthead + green + red** | Head-on | **Rule 14** | Both alter to **starboard** |
| Target overtaking from abaft the beam | Target sees Own's **stern light only** | **Rule 13** | Overtaker keeps clear |
| Target seen **abeam** with red light | Crossing from starboard | Own is **give-way** | Alter to **starboard** |

---

## 3.4 Position Fixing — Cross-Bearing Derivation

Two bearings of the same object taken at different times:

$$\Delta \varphi = D_1 \cos B_1 - D_2 \cos B_2 \quad \text{(latitude change)}$$

$$\Delta \lambda = \dfrac{D_1 \sin B_1 - D_2 \sin B_2}{\cos \varphi_{\text{mid}}} \quad \text{(longitude change)}$$

$$D_i = V_i \times \Delta t_i \quad \text{(run between fixes)}$$

---

## 3.5 Drift and Set — Dead Reckoning Equation

For a vessel heading at $H$ degrees with speed $V$ knots, affected by current with Set $S$ and Drift $D$:

$$\text{Course Made Good (CMG)} = \arctan\left(\dfrac{V\sin H + D\sin S}{V\cos H + D\cos S}\right)$$

$$\text{Speed Made Good (SMG)} = \sqrt{(V\sin H + D\sin S)^2 + (V\cos H + D\cos S)^2}$$

---

## 3.6 Summary of Equations for Exam Writing

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned} T_{\text{CPA}} &= -\dfrac{\vec{R}_0 \cdot \vec{V_R}}{\vert \vec{V_R} \vert^2} \\[6pt] D_{\text{CPA}} &= \dfrac{\vert \vec{R}_0 \times \vec{V_R} \vert}{\vert \vec{V_R} \vert} \\[6pt] V_{\text{m/s}} &= V_{\text{kn}} \times 0.5144 \\[6pt] \text{CMG} &= \arctan\left(\dfrac{V\sin H + D\sin S}{V\cos H + D\cos S}\right) \end{aligned}}$$

```mermaid
    Q --> QB["Alter to PORT"]
    Q --> QC["Go ASTERN"]
    Q --> QD["Uncertain - WARN"]
    QA --> SA["One Short Blast"]
    QB --> SB["Two Short Blasts"]
    QC --> SC["Three Short Blasts"]
    QD --> SD["Five Short Blasts - DANGER Signal"]
    SA --> SIG["Sound at least 4 sec<br/>before alteration"]
    SB --> SIG
    SC --> SIG
    SD --> SIG
```

---

## 4.8 ECDIS Display Modes

```mermaid
graph TD
    E["ECDIS Display"] --> EM1["North-up Mode"]
    E --> EM2["Course-up Mode"]
    E --> EM3["Head-up Mode"]
    EM1 --> SC1["Standard SOLAS Mode<br/>Stable North reference"]
    EM2 --> SC2["Stabilized for course<br/>Own ship icon always UP"]
    EM3 --> SC3["Relative display<br/>Unstabilized"]
    E --> LAY["Layer Selection"]
    LAY --> L1["Navigation Layer"]
    LAY --> L2["Chart Layer - ENC"]
    LAY --> L3["Radar Overlay"]
    LAY --> L4["User Layer - Notes / Routes"]
    LAY --> L5["Mariner's Settings"]
```
