# Navigation and Collision Avoidance — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define the following with one illustration each:**
**(a)** Closest Point of Approach (CPA).
**(b)** Time to Closest Point of Approach (TCPA).

**Model Answer:**

**(a)** The **Closest Point of Approach (CPA)** is the **minimum distance** that will occur between an approaching vessel and own ship if both vessels maintain their current course and speed. It is the perpendicular distance from own ship's position to the **relative-motion vector** of the target. If CPA equals **zero**, the vessels will collide.

**(b)** **Time to Closest Point of Approach (TCPA)** is the **time interval** from the present moment until the CPA distance will be reached, assuming no alteration of course or speed by either vessel. A **positive TCPA** indicates that CPA is in the future (vessel closing), while a **negative TCPA** indicates CPA is in the past (vessel opening / moving away).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students confuse *CPA* (a *distance*) with *TCPA* (a *time*). Also, ARPA alarm defaults (CPA < 1 NM, TCPA < 12 min) are **operator-set** values, not IMO-mandated — students should NOT state them as fixed law.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**State Rule 5 of COLREGs. Why is it considered the "foundation rule" of collision avoidance?**

**Model Answer:**

**Rule 5 — Look-out (verbatim):**
> *"Every vessel shall at all times maintain a proper look-out by sight and hearing as well as by all available means appropriate in the prevailing circumstances and conditions so as to make a full appraisal of the situation and of the risk of collision."*

It is the **foundation rule** because:
1. Without proper look-out, **no subsequent rule** can be properly applied.
2. Rule 18 (priority) and Rules 13–17 (manoeuvring) all presuppose that the situation has been detected and assessed.
3. The phrase *"all available means"* incorporates radar, ARPA, AIS, and ECDIS as part of look-out.
4. Violation of Rule 5 is cited in **virtually every major collision case study** (e.g., *Costa Concordia*, 2012 — Captain Schettino's deviation from look-out duties).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **KTU 2024 ESE regulation**, each Module has an internal choice: **either Question A or Question B** must be answered. Each question carries sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Option A. `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 3]`

**Question A. (a)** With neat sketches, explain the **three encounter situations** defined in COLREGs (Rules 13, 14, and 15). State the lights each vessel would show in a head-on situation between two power-driven vessels.

**(b)** Two power-driven vessels are approaching in restricted visibility. Explain with justification the sound signals each vessel shall sound. What additional precautions must be taken as per Rule 19?

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Three Encounter Situations:**

1. **Overtaking (Rule 13):** A vessel approaching another from a direction **more than 22.5° abaft the beam** of the latter. The overtaking vessel has no stand-on status and must keep clear.

2. **Head-on (Rule 14):** Vessels meeting on reciprocal or **nearly reciprocal courses**, i.e., within **6°** of reciprocal. Both must alter course to **starboard** so that each passes on the **port side**.

3. **Crossing (Rule 15):** Vessels on **intersecting courses** that are not head-on or overtaking. The vessel which has the other on her **starboard side** is the give-way vessel.

**Lights shown in head-on situation (two power-driven vessels, ≥ 50 m length):**

| Light | Position | Visibility |
|---|---|---|
| **Two white masthead lights** | One forward, one aft (higher than forward) | 225° each (foreward arc) |
| **Green side light** | Starboard side | 112.5° |
| **Red side light** | Port side | 112.5° |
| **White stern light** | Stern | 135° |

In a head-on, each vessel sees the **masthead lights + both side lights** of the other. *(7 marks)*

**(b) Sound Signals in Restricted Visibility (Rule 35 read with Rule 19):**

For a power-driven vessel **making way through the water**:
$$\boxed{\text{One prolonged blast at intervals of not more than 2 minutes}}$$

For a power-driven vessel **stopped and making no way through the water**:
$$\boxed{\text{Two prolonged blasts with about 2 seconds between, at intervals of not more than 2 minutes}}$$

Additional precautions under **Rule 19 (Conduct of vessels in restricted visibility)**:
1. Proceed at a **safe speed** adapted to the circumstances.
2. Have the **radar set to stand-by** or operating and used.
3. Have **engines ready for immediate manoeuvre**.
4. **Post a proper look-out** by sight and hearing.
5. Be prepared to **alter course substantially** early, avoiding successive alterations.
6. A vessel detecting another by radar alone shall **determine if risk of collision exists** and if so, take avoiding action. If taking avoiding action, alter course such that the other vessel is **clearly visible** (not to be passed with the target abaft the beam — i.e., avoid crossing ahead).

*(7 marks)*

---

### Option B. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**Question B. (a)** Explain the **principle, components, and operational use of ARPA**. Sketch and label a typical PPI (Plan Position Indicator) display showing a target at relative bearing 045°, range 6 NM, with course and speed vector.

**(b)** A target is observed on radar at a range of 8 NM on a relative bearing of 060°. The target's true course is 200° and speed 15 knots. Own ship's course is 350° and speed 12 knots. Determine the **CPA and TCPA** of the target. Comment on the risk and recommended action.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) ARPA — Principle and Components:**

**Principle:** ARPA (Automatic Radar Plotting Aids) is a **computerised radar enhancement** that automatically acquires radar contacts, tracks them over successive scans, and computes **course, speed, CPA, TCPA**, providing visual vectors and **trial manoeuvre** simulation.

**Components:**
1. **Radar transceiver** (X-band or S-band).
2. **Tracking computer** with Kalman filters / α-β trackers.
3. **PPI display** (raster-scan).
4. **Acquisition module** (manual / automatic zone).
5. **Trial manoeuvre generator**.
6. **Display of vector, CPA, TCPA, PAD (Predicted Area of Danger).**

**Operational Use:**
- Acquire target by clicking on PPI or via **automatic acquisition zone (AAZ)**.
- ARPA processes 3–5 scans to estimate course and speed.
- Vector length represents predicted position after selected vector time (commonly 3 or 6 min).
- **Past track** (dotted) shows target history.
- Audible alarm when CPA < set limit AND TCPA < set limit.
- Trial manoeuvre: OOW inputs proposed course/speed change; ARPA simulates new CPA/TCPA.

**PPI Sketch (description):**
- Outer ring labelled "12 NM" range.
- Own ship at centre.
- Heading marker (lubber line) pointing upward.
- Target plotted as filled circle at 045° bearing, 6 NM range.
- Solid arrow = **predicted track** (relative vector) showing direction of motion across PPI.
- Numerals next to target: "200° / 15 kn / CPA 0.6 NM / TCPA 8 min".

*(7 marks)*

**(b) CPA / TCPA Computation:**

**Step 1 — Set up vectors (with North as reference, +x = East, +y = North):**

Position of Target (relative to own ship): 
- East component: $8 \sin(60°) = 6.928$ NM.
- North component: $8 \cos(60°) = 4.000$ NM.

$$\vec{R}_0 = (6.928,\, 4.000)\; \text{NM}$$

**Step 2 — Velocity of Own Ship:**
$$V_{Ox} = 12 \sin(350°) = -2.084\; \text{kn},\quad V_{Oy} = 12 \cos(350°) = 11.818\; \text{kn}$$

**Step 3 — Velocity of Target:**
$$V_{Tx} = 15 \sin(200°) = -5.130\; \text{kn},\quad V_{Ty} = 15 \cos(200°) = -14.095\; \text{kn}$$

**Step 4 — Relative Velocity:**
$$V_{Rx} = -5.130 - (-2.084) = -3.046\; \text{kn}$$
$$V_{Ry} = -14.095 - 11.818 = -25.913\; \text{kn}$$

$$V_R = \sqrt{(-3.046)^2 + (-25.913)^2} = \sqrt{9.279 + 671.484} = \sqrt{680.76} = 26.09\; \text{kn}$$

**Step 5 — Dot Product:**
$$\vec{R}_0 \cdot \vec{V_R} = (6.928)(-3.046) + (4.000)(-25.913) = -21.103 - 103.652 = -124.755$$

**Step 6 — TCPA:**
$$T_{\text{CPA}} = -\dfrac{(-124.755)}{(26.09)^2} = \dfrac{124.755}{680.76} = 0.1833 \text{ hr} = 11.0 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 7 — Cross Product:**
$$\vec{R}_0 \times \vec{V_R} = (6.928)(-25.913) - (4.000)(-3.046) = -179.524 + 12.184 = -167.34$$

**Step 8 — CPA:**
$$D_{\text{CPA}} = \dfrac{\vert -167.34 \vert}{26.09} = 6.41\; \text{NM}$$

**Step 9 — Interpretation:**
- **CPA ≈ 6.41 NM** — **SAFE**. Well above 2 NM safe passing distance.
- **TCPA ≈ 11 minutes** — short, but since CPA is safe, **no immediate collision risk**.
- The target is **opening out** (passing well clear).
- **Recommended Action:** Continue to monitor. No alteration of course required by Rule 8 (no risk). Maintain speed and course.

*(7 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Avoid these mistakes:**
> 1. Students forget to **convert TCPA to minutes** — examiners expect `× 60` from hours.
> 2. Confusing **true bearing vs. relative bearing**. The formula uses **relative bearing** for the trig form. In the vector form, position is **relative to own ship**.
> 3. Forgetting that if the dot product $\vec{R}_0 \cdot \vec{V_R}$ is **positive**, TCPA is **negative**, meaning the target is already opening (no future CPA).
> 4. **Rule application confusion:** A vessel seeing a green light from a target has the target on her **starboard side** but is **NOT automatically the give-way vessel** unless it is a power-vs-power crossing scenario (Rule 15). The vessel type (Rule 18) overrides.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Memory Points — KTU Module 3**

### COLREGs — Must-Know Rules
- [ ] **Rule 5** — Look-out (sight + hearing + all available means).
- [ ] **Rule 6** — Safe speed (depends on visibility, traffic, stopping distance).
- [ ] **Rule 7** — Risk of collision (steady bearing = decreasing range).
- [ ] **Rule 8** — Action to avoid collision (alter course early, no successive small alterations, pass at safe distance).
- [ ] **Rule 9** — Narrow channels (keep to starboard outer limit; no crossing if it impedes).
- [ ] **Rule 10** — Traffic Separation Schemes (TSS).
- [ ] **Rule 13** — Overtaking (> 22.5° abaft the beam).
- [ ] **Rule 14** — Head-on (alter to starboard, pass port-to-port).
- [ ] **Rule 15** — Crossing (target on stbd = give-way).
- [ ] **Rule 17** — Stand-on may alter course if give-way fails to act.
- [ ] **Rule 18** — Priority hierarchy (NUC > RAM > Constrained > Fishing > Sailing > Power).
- [ ] **Rule 19** — Restricted visibility (one prolonged blast / two prolonged blasts).
- [ ] **Rule 34** — Manoeuvring signals (1 short = STBD; 2 short = PORT; 3 short = ASTERN; 5 short = DANGER).
- [ ] **Rule 35** — Fog signals.

### Lights and Shapes — Night Identification
- [ ] Power vessel ≥ 50 m → 2 masthead + side + stern.
- [ ] NUC → 2 red all-round + 2 black balls.
- [ ] RAM → Red-White-Red all-round + Ball-Diamond-Ball.
- [ ] Fishing (trawling) → 2 green-white vertical + 2 cones apex together.
- [ ] At anchor → 1 white all-round forward + 1 black ball.
- [ ] Aground → 3 black balls in vertical line.

### ARPA / Radar / AIS
- [ ] CPA = $\dfrac{\vert \vec{R}_0 \times \vec{V_R} \vert}{\vert \vec{V_R} \vert}$.
- [ ] TCPA = $-\dfrac{\vec{R}_0 \cdot \vec{V_R}}{\vert \vec{V_R} \vert^2}$.
- [ ] AIS channels = 161.975 MHz, 162.025 MHz.
- [ ] Radar range (X-band) ≈ 6 m to 24 NM; (S-band) better in heavy rain.
- [ ] ARPA minimum required tracked targets ≥ 20.

### Quick Conversions
- [ ] 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s.
- [ ] 1 NM = 1852 m.
- [ ] Head-on = within 6° reciprocal.
- [ ] Overtaking = > 22.5° abaft beam.
- [ ] 5 short blasts = danger signal.
- [ ] Fog signals every **≤ 2 minutes**.

### Watchkeeping (STCW)
- [ ] Proper lookout at all times.
- [ ] Hourly position fixes in coastal waters; every 4 hours ocean.
- [ ] Master's call on any doubt.
- [ ] Standing orders and night orders.
- [ ] BRM principles — closed-loop communication, assertiveness, leadership.

### Bridge Equipment (SOLAS V/19)
- [ ] Gyro compass + magnetic compass.
- [ ] Radar ≥ 9 GHz (X-band) + 3 GHz (S-band) for ≥ 3000 GT.
- [ ] ECDIS for ≥ 500 GT on international voyages.
- [ ] AIS for ≥ 300 GT international.
- [ ] VHF with DSC, EPIRB, SART.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy Tip:**
> For 14-mark questions, **always draw a labelled sketch** — Rule 14 / Rule 15 / Rule 19 questions especially. Examiners award marks for diagrams even when the textual description is partial. For numerical ARPA problems, **show every algebraic step**; examiners distribute partial marks generously across vector steps but give **zero** for final numbers without intermediate work.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Celestial Navigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Celestial Navigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Celestial Navigation** is the branch of practical navigation in which the position of a craft on the surface of the Earth is determined by observing the **apparent directions** and **angular altitudes** of celestial bodies — the Sun, the Moon, the four navigational planets (Venus, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn) and selected navigational stars (e.g., Polaris, Sirius, Canopus) — relative to the visible horizon of the observer.

It is fundamentally a problem of **spherical trigonometry** performed on a virtual sphere of infinite radius called the **Celestial Sphere**, centred at the centre of the Earth (geocentric) or at the observer (topocentric).

The complete observed position fix requires three measurable quantities from the celestial sphere, all expressed in **degrees / minutes / seconds of arc**:

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Description |
|---|----------|--------|-------------|
| 1 | True Altitude of body | **Ho** | Angular distance of body above the true (rational) horizon. |
| 2 | True Azimuth | **Zn** | True bearing of body measured from North (000°) clockwise through 360°. |
| 3 | Greenwich Hour Angle | **GHA** | Angular measure westward from the Greenwich celestial meridian. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Frame:** The **WGS-84 World Geodetic System** is the modern reference used in conjunction with the Nautical Almanac (UKHO / USNO editions) and **Pub. No. 229 — Sight Reduction Tables for Marine Navigation**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you are lost in a vast, dark field at night. You see a distant searchlight on top of a tall lighthouse far away. You cannot tell *exactly* how far the lighthouse is, but you can measure **how high** it appears above the ground using a clinometer (an angle-measuring instrument).

Knowing:
- the **exact location** of the lighthouse on a map, and
- the **angle** at which you see it,

you can draw an **arc** on the map around the lighthouse. Your true position lies somewhere along that arc.

Now imagine doing this with **a thousand lighthouses**, each one being a fixed star on the celestial sphere, and each "searchlight" being a precisely calculated direction that changes predictably with time.

That, in essence, is Celestial Navigation:

- The **lighthouse** ↔ a Celestial Body (Sun, Star, Planet, Moon)
- The **map** ↔ The Celestial Sphere / Navigational Chart
- The **clinometer** ↔ A **Sextant**
- The **arc** ↔ A **Circle of Equal Altitude** (a small circle on the Earth's surface — your **Position Line / Line of Position**)

> [!TIP]
> In practice, navigators draw **straight lines of position** (tangent lines) approximating the arc, because over short distances the curvature is negligible.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Anchored to KTU Syllabus

- **GP — Geographical Position:** The point on Earth directly below a celestial body. It is the centre of the circle of equal altitude.
- **LHA — Local Hour Angle:** The angular distance westward of the body's hour circle from the observer's meridian.
- **Declination (δ):** Angular distance North (+) or South (−) of the celestial equator, analogous to latitude.
- **Assumed Position (AP):** A chosen position close to the Dead-Reckoning (DR) position, used so that the calculated azimuth and altitude come out as clean whole-number values for tabular lookup.
- **Intercept:** The difference between **Computed Altitude (Hc)** and **Observed Altitude (Ho)**. Intercept = Hc − Ho.
- **Zenith Distance (z):** z = 90° − Altitude.
- **Co-latitude:** 90° − φ (φ = observer's latitude).
- **Polar Distance (p):** 90° − δ.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Navigational Triangle (PZX Triangle)

The heart of celestial navigation is the **PZX Triangle**, a spherical triangle on the celestial sphere whose three vertices are:

- **P** — the elevated celestial pole (N or S, depending on observer's hemisphere)
- **Z** — the observer's zenith
- **X** — the projected position of the celestial body on the celestial sphere

Its three sides are:

| Side | Name | Length |
|------|------|--------|
| PZ | Co-latitude | 90° − φ |
| PX | Polar Distance (p) | 90° − δ |
| ZX | Zenith Distance (z) | 90° − Ho |

The angle at vertex **P** is the **Local Hour Angle (LHA)** — i.e., the angular separation between the observer's meridian and the body's hour circle.

![Navigational PZX Triangle — Visual Schematic]

```
                P (North Pole)
               /\
              /  \
       p=90-δ/    \ Co-lat=90-φ
            /      \
           /        \
          X (Body)---Z (Zenith)
              z=90-Ho
```

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of a Celestial Sight Reduction

1. **Measure** the **sextant altitude (Hs)** of the celestial body at a noted **time (UT)**.
2. **Apply corrections** to obtain Ho: Ho = Hs + IC − Dip − Refraction (± Parallax for Moon).
3. **Extract** GHA and declination (δ) of the body from the **Nautical Almanac** for the noted UT.
4. **Compute** LHA: LHA = GHA − λWest (or + λEast), normalised to 0°–360°.
5. **Choose an Assumed Position (AP)** near the DR position — usually a whole-degree latitude and a longitude giving a whole-degree LHA.
6. **Enter Sight Reduction Tables (HO 229 / Pub. 229 / NAO tables)** with (LHA, φ, δ) to obtain **Computed Altitude (Hc)** and **Azimuth Angle (Z)**.
7. **Plot** the **Position Line**: draw the azimuth line from AP; mark intercept = Hc − Ho (Toward if +, Away if −); perpendicular to azimuth gives the Line of Position.
8. **Cross** with another sight or a DR / EP line to obtain a **Fix**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **examination-critical**. Memorise the units and the sign conventions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Local Hour Angle | LHA = GHA − λW (or + λE) | Degrees | Normalise 0°–360° |
| 2 | Sidereal Hour Angle | SHA + GHA(ARIES) = GHA(star) | Degrees | Used for stars |
| 3 | Zenith Distance | z = 90° − Ho | Degrees | Always positive |
| 4 | Co-latitude | 90° − φ | Degrees | Pole-to-Zenith arc |
| 5 | Polar Distance | p = 90° − δ | Degrees | Pole-to-body arc |
| 6 | Intercept | a = Hc − Ho | Minutes of arc | +ve = Toward; −ve = Away |
| 7 | Corrected Ho (Sun/Star) | Ho = Hs + IC − Dip − Ref | Minutes of arc | Dip from height-of-eye table |
| 8 | Corrected Ho (Moon) | Ho = Hs + IC − Dip − Ref + HP − Pa | Minutes of arc | HP = Horizontal Parallax; Pa = Parallax in Altitude |
| 9 | Azimuth (true) | Zn = Z (N. lat.) or 360° − Z (S. lat.) | Degrees | Tabulated Z is azimuth angle |
| 10 | Cosine-haversine | hav(z) = hav(co-lat ∓ δ) + cos φ · cos δ · hav(LHA) | — | ∓: subtract lat & decl of same name |
| 11 | Compass error | CE = TC − CC; Dev = CE − Var | Degrees | Apply Easterly −, Westerly + |
| 12 | Altitude correction (Sun) | Ho = Hs + IC − Dip + 3·tan²h (approx.) | — | For non-tabulated; rarely used |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention for Declination (δ):**
> - North declination → + (0° to +90°)
> - South declination → − (0° to −90°)
> - Same name as latitude → use **subtraction** in haversine co-lat ∓ δ term.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Operational Utility

- **Redundancy:** Modern vessels rely on **GNSS (GPS, GLONASS, BeiDou, Galileo)** as the primary position-fixing tool. Celestial Navigation remains a **regulatory backup** under **SOLAS Chapter V, Regulation 19** for vessels above 500 GT and passenger ships — they must be able to perform celestial observation if the electronic system fails.
- **Strategic & Defence:** Submarines, naval vessels, and long-range aircraft routinely train celestial navigators because the system is **jamming-resistant** and **independent of terrestrial infrastructure**.
- **Maritime Examinations:** Competency in sight reduction is mandated for **Chief Mate / Master (Unlimited)** under the **STCW 2010 Manila Amendments**, Code Section A-II/2 and A-II/1.
- **Forensic & Historical Reconstruction:** Shipwrecks (Titanic, Mary Celeste) were historically analysed using celestial observations logged in logbooks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cosine Formula → Haversine Formula

We begin from the **Spherical Law of Cosines for Sides** applied to the PZX triangle, where the side opposite the angle LHA is **z**, and the other two sides are **co-latitude (c = 90° − φ)** and **polar distance (p = 90° − δ)**.

### Step 1 — Spherical Law of Cosines

$$
\cos(z) = \cos(c)\cos(p) + \sin(c)\sin(p)\cos(\text{LHA})
$$

### Step 2 — Substitution

Substitute c = 90° − φ and p = 90° − δ.

Using the identity cos(90° − x) = sin x, and sin(90° − x) = cos x:

$$
\cos(z) = \sin(\phi)\sin(\delta) + \cos(\phi)\cos(\delta)\cos(\text{LHA})
$$

### Step 3 — Introduce the Haversine Identity

The haversine of any angle θ is defined as:

$$
\text{hav}(\theta) = \dfrac{1 - \cos(\theta)}{2}
$$

We now express **both sides** of the cosine equation in haversine form. Starting from the right-hand side, add and subtract 1:

$$
\cos(z) = 1 - 2\,\text{hav}(z)
$$

### Step 4 — Subtract 1 from both sides

$$
\cos(z) - 1 = \sin(\phi)\sin(\delta) + \cos(\phi)\cos(\delta)\cos(\text{LHA}) - 1
$$

Rewrite as:

$$
-2\,\text{hav}(z) = \sin(\phi)\sin(\delta) - 1 + \cos(\phi)\cos(\delta)\cos(\text{LHA})
$$

### Step 5 — Use Product-to-Sum Identities

Note that:

$$
\sin(\phi)\sin(\delta) = \dfrac{\cos(\phi - \delta) - \cos(\phi + \delta)}{2}
$$

and

$$
\cos(\phi)\cos(\delta) = \dfrac{\cos(\phi - \delta) + \cos(\phi + \delta)}{2}
$$

So:

$$
\sin(\phi)\sin(\delta) + \cos(\phi)\cos(\delta)\cos(\text{LHA}) = \dfrac{\cos(\phi - \delta) - \cos(\phi + \delta)}{2} + \dfrac{[\cos(\phi - \delta) + \cos(\phi + \delta)]\cos(\text{LHA})}{2}
$$

### Step 6 — Combine and Factor

$$
= \dfrac{\cos(\phi - \delta)[1 + \cos(\text{LHA})]}{2} + \dfrac{\cos(\phi + \delta)[\cos(\text{LHA}) - 1]}{2}
$$

Now apply the half-angle identity $1 + \cos(\theta) = 2\cos^2(\theta/2)$ and $\cos(\theta) - 1 = -2\sin^2(\theta/2)$:

$$
= \cos(\phi - \delta)\cos^2(\text{LHA}/2) - \cos(\phi + \delta)\sin^2(\text{LHA}/2)
$$

### Step 7 — Apply haversine substitutions

Recall the haversine identity rearranged: $1 - \cos\theta = 2\,\text{hav}(\theta)$.

We note that $\cos(\phi - \delta) = 1 - 2\,\text{hav}(\phi - \delta)$ and similarly for $(\phi + \delta)$.

After algebraic reduction (omitted for brevity here; see Pub. 229 Vol. 1 introduction) we arrive at the **Marcq Saint-Hilaire Haversine Formula**:

$$
\boxed{\text{hav}(z) = \text{hav}(\phi - \delta) + \cos(\phi)\cos(\delta)\,\text{hav}(\text{LHA})}
$$

where $z = 90° - H_c$.

### Step 8 — Practical Implementation

This is the **direct formula** used by all modern digital sight-reduction calculators, electronic sextants, and Pub. 229. The condition `(φ − δ)` is replaced by `(φ + δ)` when latitude and declination are of **opposite names**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Given (typical KTU exam-style data):**

- DR position: φ = 40° N, λ = 50° W
- Body: Sun
- UT of sight: 09 h 24 m 15 s, Date: 21 June
- Sextant altitude (Hs): 38° 42.6′
- Index Correction (IC): −1.8′
- Height of eye: 12 m
- GHA Sun (09h): 165° 12.4′
- GHA Sun (24 m 15 s): 6° 04.2′ (movement = 15°/h × 24.25 m)
- Declination Sun: N 23° 25.6′

**Step 1 — Total GHA:**

$$
\text{GHA} = 165°12.4' + 6°04.2' = 171°16.6'
$$

**Step 2 — LHA:**

$$
\text{LHA} = 171°16.6' - 50°00.0' = 121°16.6'
$$

**Step 3 — Altitude correction:**

Dip (12 m) = −5.9′; Refraction ≈ −1.2′ (approx, 28′ tan correction applied via almanac tables). So:

$$
H_o = 38°42.6' - 1.8' - 5.9' + 15.3' \approx 38°50.2'
$$

(Sun's altitude corrections from Nautical Almanac: Oct–Mar +9.9′; Apr–Sep +15.3′)

**Step 4 — Assumed Position (AP):**

Choose AP at φ = 40° N, λ such that LHA = whole degree. So λA = GHA − 122° (rounded LHA) → recompute λA = 171°16.6′ − 122°00′ = 49°16.6′ W.

**Step 5 — Enter HO 229 (Pub. 229) for LHA = 122°, φ = 40°, δ = 23° (Same Name as lat):**

From tables (illustrative):

$$
H_c = 38°56', \quad Z = 138° \text{ (S. of E. since S. lat, but here N. lat → Zn = Z = 138°)}
$$

**Step 6 — Intercept:**

$$
\text{Intercept} = H_c - H_o = 38°56' - 38°50.2' = +5.8' \quad (\text{Toward})
$$

**Step 7 — Plot:**

From AP, draw azimuth line 138°. Measure 5.8′ toward Sun on that line. Draw perpendicular = **Line of Position (LOP)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Celestial Navigation Process Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Observe Celestial Body with Sextant] --> B[Note Time UT from Chronometer]
    A --> C[Record Sextant Altitude Hs]
    B --> D[Look up GHA and Declination from Nautical Almanac]
    C --> E[Apply Index Correction IC]
    D --> F[Compute LHA from GHA and DR Longitude]
    E --> G[Apply Dip and Refraction Corrections]
    F --> H[Choose Assumed Position AP]
    G --> I[Obtain True Altitude Ho]
    H --> J[Enter Sight Reduction Tables HO 229]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Read Hc and Z Azimuth Angle]
    K --> L[Compute Intercept Hc minus Ho]
    L --> M[Convert Z to True Azimuth Zn]
    M --> N[Plot Line of Position on Chart]
    N --> O[Cross with DR or Second Sight to obtain Fix]
```

## 4.2 PZX Navigational Triangle — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vertex P - Celestial Pole] -->|Side c = 90 minus phi Co-latitude| B[Vertex Z - Observer Zenith]
    B -->|Side z = 90 minus Ho Zenith Distance| C[Vertex X - Celestial Body]
    C -->|Side p = 90 minus delta Polar Distance| A
    B -->|Angle at Z Azimuth| C
    A -->|Angle at P LHA| C
```

## 4.3 The Celestial Sphere — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CS[Celestial Sphere of Infinite Radius]
    CS --> CE[Celestial Equator]
    CS --> NP[North Celestial Pole NCP]
    CS --> SP[South Celestial Pole SCP]
    CS --> Z[Zenith of Observer]
    CS --> N[Nadir]
    CS --> H[Celestial Horizon]
    CE --> EQ[Ecliptic inclined 23.5 deg to Celestial Equator]
    EQ --> SOL[Solstices and Equinoxes]
    NP --> MER[Hour Circles RA or SHA measured eastward]
    Z --> MER
```

## 4.4 Altitude-Correction Decision Block (Sun / Star / Moon)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Raw Sextant Altitude Hs] --> IC[Apply Index Correction IC]
    IC --> BODY{Body Type}
    BODY -->|Sun| SDIP[Dip from Height of Eye Table]
    BODY -->|Star Planet| SDIP2[Dip from Height of Eye Table]
    BODY -->|Moon| SDIP3[Dip from Height of Eye Table]
    SDIP --> SUNALT[Apply Sun Altitude Correction from Almanac]
    SDIP2 --> STARALT[Apply Star Planet Altitude Correction minus 0.6 approx]
    SDIP3 --> MOONALT[Apply HP and Pa from Almanac Moon Correction Table]
    SUNALT --> HO[True Altitude Ho]
    STARALT --> HO
    MOONALT --> HO
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "Ships Make Great Sailors":** Sun → Main correction; Moon → Parallax in Altitude (Pa); Stars/Planets → ~−0.6′ extra dip correction. Always end with refraction (−) at low altitudes.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the terms: (a) Geographical Position (GP) of a celestial body, (b) Assumed Position (AP), and (c) Intercept. State the relationship between GP and Ho.**

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Geographical Position (GP):** The point on the Earth's surface directly beneath a celestial body, i.e., where the body's zenith coincides with the observer's zenith. Its latitude = the body's declination (δ), and its longitude = the body's GHA.

(b) **Assumed Position (AP):** A whole-degree position (whole-degree latitude and whole-degree LHA) selected near the Dead-Reckoning (DR) position to facilitate tabular interpolation in sight-reduction tables such as HO 229.

(c) **Intercept (a):** The difference in minutes of arc between the Computed Altitude (Hc) and the Observed Altitude (Ho):

$$
a = H_c - H_o
$$

If $a > 0$, the intercept is **Toward** the GP; if $a < 0$, it is **Away** from the GP.

**Relationship with Ho:** As Ho increases, the observer is closer to the GP. The line of equal altitude is a small circle on the Earth whose radius is $(90° - H_o)$ in nautical miles, centred on the GP.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the Marcq Saint-Hilaire method of intercept. Why is it the preferred method for sight reduction in modern navigation?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Marcq Saint-Hilaire method** (also called the **intercept and azimuth method**) computes the altitude (Hc) and azimuth (Zn) of a celestial body as it *would* appear from a chosen **Assumed Position (AP)**. The actual observation (Ho) and azimuth are then compared to obtain the **Intercept** and a **Line of Position (LOP)** perpendicular to the azimuth.

**Steps:**

1. Choose an AP near the DR.
2. Compute LHA at the AP.
3. Use HO 229 tables to extract Hc and azimuth angle Z.
4. Compute intercept a = Hc − Ho (in minutes).
5. Plot from AP along Zn line; mark intercept Toward (+) or Away (−); erect perpendicular = LOP.

**Why it is preferred:**

- Requires only one body observation at a time to obtain a usable LOP (unlike the older **longitude-by-chronometer** method which needed simultaneous observations).
- Works for bodies at any altitude (high or low) and at any hour angle.
- Standardised, tabular, and easily programmable for digital calculators.
- Eliminates iterative solutions of the spherical triangle.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Sign errors in LHA** are the #1 cause of lost marks. Always normalise LHA to 0°–360°.
> - **Forgetting to label intercept direction (Toward / Away)** is a 2-mark penalty.
> - **Confusing Azimuth Angle Z with True Azimuth Zn** — students often write the wrong one in the final answer. Remember: N. lat → Zn = Z; S. lat → Zn = 360° − Z.
> - **Rounding errors in declination** — use the *tabulated* declination for the full hour, do not interpolate minute-by-minute for HO 229 (interpolate only LHA).
> - **Plotting mistakes**: do NOT draw the LOP through the AP — it passes through the intercept point.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** A vessel on DR position 32° 10′ N, 65° 30′ W sights the Sun at UT 16 h 12 m 40 s on 15 May. The sextant altitude (Hs) is 24° 18.2′, Index Correction is −2.4′, and height of eye is 9 m. From the Nautical Almanac, GHA Sun (16 h) = 60° 42.8′ and declination = N 18° 52.7′. Increments for 12 m 40 s = 3° 10.0′; Sun altitude correction (Oct–May lower limb) = −1.8′ (refraction) + 13.8′ (SD lower limb in N. hemi.) etc. as in tables. **Determine the True Altitude (Ho), LHA, and the Assumed Position (AP).** (7 Marks)

**(b)** Using Pub. 229 for the AP obtained in (a) with LHA = 64° (nearest whole-degree entry), latitude 32° N (declination 18° same name), obtain Hc, the azimuth angle Z, and hence the intercept. State whether it is Toward or Away. Convert Z to True Azimuth Zn. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Total GHA:**

$$
\text{GHA}_{\text{Sun}} = 60°42.8' + 3°10.0' = 63°52.8'
$$

**Step 2 — LHA at DR longitude:**

$$
\text{LHA} = 63°52.8' - 65°30.0' = -1°37.2'
$$

Since negative, add 360°:

$$
\text{LHA}_{\text{DR}} = 358°22.8'
$$

**Step 3 — Apply altitude corrections:**

Dip (9 m) = −5.3′; Sun correction (lower limb, N. lat, Oct–May, Hs ~24°) ≈ +12.0′; IC = −2.4′.

$$
H_o = 24°18.2' - 2.4' - 5.3' + 12.0' = 24°22.5'
$$

**Step 4 — Assumed Position:**

To make LHA a whole number, choose LHA_A = 359° (or 0°, but 359° is closer). Hence:

$$
\lambda_A = \text{GHA} - \text{LHA}_A = 63°52.8' - 359°00' = -295°07.2'
$$

Add 360° → 64°52.8′ W. So **AP = 32° N, 64°52.8′ W (or equivalently, use whole-deg 65° W with refined entry).**

For simplicity and KTU-exam practicality, the AP is often written as:

$$
\boxed{\text{AP} = 32°00'\,\text{N},\ 65°00'\,\text{W}}
$$

with LHA_A recomputed accordingly (since the GHA itself is the driver for HO 229 — students typically enter HO 229 using the rounded LHA = 64° at the nearest whole degree).

(7 Marks — full credit if all corrections, signs, and AP selection are correctly shown.)

---

**Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Enter Pub. 229** with LHA = 64°, latitude = 32°, declination = N 18° (same name).

**Tabulated (illustrative) result:**

$$
H_c = 24°30.0', \quad Z = 94° \text{ (S. of E.)}
$$

**Step 2 — Intercept:**

$$
a = H_c - H_o = 24°30.0' - 24°22.5' = +7.5' \quad (\text{Toward the GP})
$$

**Step 3 — Convert Z to Zn:**

Since latitude is North, Zn = Z = **094° True**.

**Step 4 — Plotting summary (text description):**

From AP (32° N, 65° W), draw azimuth line 094° T. Measure 7.5 NM toward the Sun. Draw the LOP perpendicular to that azimuth.

**Conclusion:** The vessel lies on the LOP, 7.5 NM toward the Sun's GP from the assumed position.

(7 Marks — full credit if all entries, signs, and azimuth conversion are correct.)

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the aid of a neat diagram, describe the **PZX Navigational Triangle**. State the three sides and the angle at vertex P. Write the **Cosine formula** for solving it. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A ship at DR position 15° S, 30° E sights a star at UT 19 h 45 m. The star's SHA = 270°, GHA (Aries) = 119° 24.6′. Sextant altitude = 45° 12′, IC = +1.5′, height of eye 8 m, star declination = S 10° 22′. **Compute the True Azimuth (Zn) of the star using HO 229 tables given that the tabulated values are Hc = 45° 18′, Z = 130°.** (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — PZX Triangle:**

**Diagram (text-based):**

```
                P (elevated pole)
               /\
              /  \  Side PZ = 90° − φ  (co-latitude)
   Side PX  /    \   (Polar Distance p = 90° − δ)
   =90°−δ  /      \
          X(Star)--Z(Observer Zenith)
           ────────
           Side ZX = 90° − Ho  (Zenith Distance z)
```

- **Angle at P** = Local Hour Angle (LHA).
- **Angle at Z** = Azimuth Angle (Z), from which True Azimuth Zn is derived.
- **Angle at X** = parallactic angle (rarely used in marine navigation).

**Cosine formula:**

$$
\boxed{\cos(z) = \sin(\phi)\sin(\delta) + \cos(\phi)\cos(\delta)\cos(\text{LHA})}
$$

where $z = 90° - H_c$.

**Substitution form:**

$$
\cos(90° - H_c) = \cos(\text{co-lat})\cos(p) + \sin(\text{co-lat})\sin(p)\cos(\text{LHA})
$$

(7 Marks — full credit for correct diagram, identification of sides and angle, and correct formula.)

---

**Part (b) — Azimuth Computation:**

**Step 1 — Compute GHA(star):**

$$
\text{GHA}_{\text{star}} = \text{GHA}_{\text{Aries}} + \text{SHA} = 119°24.6' + 270°00.0' = 389°24.6'
$$

Subtract 360° → **GHA = 29°24.6′**.

**Step 2 — Compute LHA at DR longitude:**

DR λ = 30° E. So:

$$
\text{LHA} = \text{GHA} - \lambda_W = \text{GHA} + \lambda_E = 29°24.6' + 30°00.0' = 59°24.6'
$$

**Step 3 — Azimuth conversion:**

Latitude = 15° S (Southern Hemisphere). The rule for converting azimuth angle Z to true azimuth Zn in the Southern Hemisphere is:

$$
\text{Zn} = 360° - Z
$$

Given Z = 130° (from HO 229):

$$
\text{Zn} = 360° - 130° = \boxed{230°\,T}
$$

**Verification of intercept (bonus):**

$$
a = H_c - H_o
$$

$$
H_o = 45°12' + 1.5' (\text{IC}) - 4.8' (\text{Dip, 8 m}) - 0.6' (\text{star correction}) \approx 45°08.1'
$$

$$
a = 45°18' - 45°08.1' = +9.9' \quad (\text{Toward the GP})
$$

**Plotting:**

From AP, draw line 230° T, measure 9.9 NM along it toward star, erect perpendicular → LOP.

(7 Marks — full credit for correct GHA-SHA arithmetic, sign convention for E longitude, Southern-hemisphere azimuth rule, and final Zn value.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Quick-Reference Memory Aid**

- ☐ **Celestial Navigation = Spherical Trigonometry on the Celestial Sphere.**
- ☐ Three key quantities measured / looked up: **Ho, Zn, GHA**.
- ☐ **Sextant altitude (Hs)** → corrected via **IC + Dip + Refraction (+ SD/P for Sun/Moon)** to get **Ho**.
- ☐ **GHA + SHA(star) → GHA(star)**; LHA = GHA ∓ λ.
- ☐ **Assumed Position (AP)** uses whole-degree latitude and whole-degree LHA for table lookup.
- ☐ **Pub. 229 / HO 229** tables give **Hc** and **azimuth angle Z** (not Zn).
- ☐ **Intercept a = Hc − Ho**: **+ = Toward**, **− = Away**.
- ☐ **Azimuth rule:** N. lat → Zn = Z; S. lat → Zn = 360° − Z.
- ☐ **Haversine formula:** hav(z) = hav(φ − δ) + cos(φ)cos(δ)·hav(LHA).
- ☐ **PZX Triangle** sides: co-lat, polar distance, zenith distance; angle at P = LHA.
- ☐ **Plot LOP perpendicular to azimuth line**, passing through intercept point — NOT through the AP.
- ☐ **Sun's declination maximum** at **June solstice (~23.4° N)**; minimum at **December solstice (~23.4° S)**.
- ☐ **Stars' declination** does not change appreciably during a voyage (effectively fixed for navigational purposes).
- ☐ **Index Correction sign convention:** IC is + when index mirror shows scale above zero, − when below.
- ☐ **Dip correction sign:** always **negative** (reduces Hs).
- ☐ **Refraction correction sign:** always **negative** (except at zenith; negligible above 89°).
- ☐ **STCW reference:** Celestial Navigation competency is mandated for **Officer in Charge of a Navigational Watch** under STCW 2010, Section A-II/1, Table A-II/1.
- ☐ **Time standard for almanac:** **Universal Time (UT / UTC)** — always convert ship's zone time to UT before extraction.
- ☐ **GMT/UT vs Ship's Time:** subtract zone description (W +ve, E −ve) from ship's time to obtain UT.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electronic Navigation Systems (ECDIS, Radar)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electronic Navigation Systems (ECDIS, Radar)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electronic Chart Display and Information System (ECDIS)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (IMO MSC.232(82)):**
> *ECDIS is a navigation information system which, with adequate back-up arrangements, can be accepted as complying with the up-to-date paper chart required by SOLAS Regulation V/19 & V/27, by displaying selected information from a System Electronic Navigational Chart (S-ENC) with positional information from navigation sensors to assist the mariner in route planning and route monitoring, and by displaying additional navigation-related information.*

In simpler terms, ECDIS is the **digital, intelligent replacement for traditional paper nautical charts**. It is a computer-based system that integrates electronic navigational charts (ENCs) with real-time positional data (from GPS, gyrocompass, speed log) and additional sensor inputs (radar overlay, AIS, depth sounder) to give the watchkeeper a unified, continuously updated picture of the vessel's position, surroundings, and intended track.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of ECDIS like **Google Maps for ships, but legally certified for navigation and far more sophisticated**. Just as Google Maps shows your car, the road network, traffic, and speed limits on one screen, ECDIS shows the ship, the waterway, other vessels (via AIS), water depth contours, and navigational hazards — all in real time. However, unlike a phone app, ECDIS must meet strict International Maritime Organization (IMO) performance standards and International Hydrographic Organization (IHO) data standards (S-57, S-63) before it can legally substitute a paper chart on a SOLAS-regulated vessel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Standards & Regulations for ECDIS:**
> - **IMO Resolution A.817(19)** → Performance Standards for ECDIS (original, 1995)
> - **IMO Resolution MSC.232(82)** → Revised Performance Standards for ECDIS (2006, current)
> - **IHO S-57 (IHO Transfer Standard for Digital Hydrographic Data)** → ENC data format
> - **IHO S-63 (IHO Data Protection Scheme)** → ENC encryption and authentication
> - **SOLAS Chapter V, Reg. 19.2.1.4** → Carriage requirement for ECDIS
> - **IEC 61174** → Type-approval testing standard for ECDIS
> - **STCW** → Competency requirements for ECDIS operators

---

## 1.2 Marine Radar (Radio Detection and Ranging)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (IMO Resolution A.477(XII) & MSC.192(79)):**
> *Marine radar is a navigational aid which detects the presence of objects (targets) by transmitting radio frequency (RF) pulses and receiving the reflected echoes from those targets. It determines the range and bearing of targets relative to the ship's position by measuring the round-trip time of the pulse and the angular position of the rotating antenna.*

In simple terms, marine radar is the **ship's electronic "eye in the fog"**. It works on the same principle as a bat's echolocation: it sends out a burst of microwave energy, listens for the echo bouncing back from another ship, a buoy, the coastline, or a rain cloud, and from that echo calculates how far away the object is (using the constant speed of light) and in which direction (using the antenna's heading reference).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine standing in the middle of a dark football stadium and shouting. By listening carefully, you can tell (a) how far the walls are based on how long the echo takes to return, and (b) which wall the echo came from by turning your head. Marine radar does exactly this, but with **microwave pulses** (instead of sound) traveling at the speed of light (**≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s**), and an antenna that rotates 360° at a fixed RPM (typically **24 RPM** for S-band and **42 RPM** for X-band).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Marine Radar Frequency Bands:**
> - **X-Band:** **9.2 – 9.5 GHz** (wavelength ≈ **3 cm**) → Higher resolution, shorter range, affected by rain (sea clutter & rain clutter).
> - **S-Band:** **2.9 – 3.1 GHz** (wavelength ≈ **10 cm**) → Lower resolution, longer range, penetrates rain — preferred for ocean-going vessels.
> - **3 cm (X) and 10 cm (S)** are the two internationally allocated marine radar bands under the **ITU Radio Regulations**.

---

## 1.3 Why These Two Systems Matter Together in Maritime Safety

ECDIS tells the mariner **"where the ship is and what the charted dangers are"**, while radar tells them **"what is actually out there right now, including uncharted hazards, small craft, and floating debris"**. Together they form the **primary real-time situational awareness duo** on the bridge of any modern vessel. Modern integrated bridge systems (IBS) often overlay radar video directly onto the ECDIS chart — a feature called **Radar Overlay** — which gives the navigator charted context for every radar contact.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ECDIS — System Architecture & Operational Logic

An ECDIS is built around **four logical layers**, each with specific responsibilities:

1. **Sensor Layer (Hardware I/O):**
   - GNSS receiver (GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, BeiDou — multi-constellation preferred for redundancy).
   - Gyrocompass / Transmitting Heading Device (THD).
   - Speed log (EM log, Doppler log, or dual-axis GPS speed over ground).
   - Radar video (for overlay), AIS receiver (for target integration), echo sounder (for depth contour verification).

2. **Data Layer (Chart Database):**
   - **SENC (System Electronic Navigational Chart):** The operational chart database used by ECDIS, compiled from the official **ENC** plus user updates.
   - **ENC cells** are vector charts issued by national Hydrographic Offices (e.g., NOAA, UKHO, INHO) under IHO S-57.
   - Updates flow via **S-63 encrypted media** (CD/DVD) or **AVCS** (Admiralty Vector Chart Service) over satellite/IP.
   - **Catalogue:** A small index file (CATALOG.031) listing which ENC cells are installed.

3. **Processing Layer (Software Kernel):**
   - Performs coordinate transformations (WGS-84 datum handling).
   - Conducts continuous **position fixing, course prediction** (vector for look-ahead), and **safety contour checking**.
   - Runs **anti-grounding alarms**, **cross-track error (XTE)** calculations, and **dangerous target** detection.
   - Maintains the **Voyage Plan (Route)** with waypoints, XTD (cross-track distance), and turning radii.

4. **Presentation Layer (Human-Machine Interface):**
   - Renders chart data according to **IHO S-52 Presentation Library** (color, symbols, display categories).
   - Provides **Display Mode**: Base, Standard, All (Other), Custom.
   - **Orientation**: North-up (default, IMO recommended), Head-up, Course-up.
   - Supports day, dusk, and night color palettes (operator-selectable).

### 2.1.1 Why ECDIS Demands a Back-Up (Critical KTU Point)

ECDIS is allowed to **replace** the paper chart only when it has an **adequate back-up arrangement** (SOLAS V/19). The back-up can be:
- A **second, fully independent ECDIS**, OR
- A **folio of up-to-date paper charts** for the intended voyage.

If the primary ECDIS fails and only a back-up is available, the vessel must proceed at a safe speed and arrange repairs; the back-up must be ready for instant use. The transitional period for mandatory carriage of ECDIS under SOLAS ended on **1 July 2018**, after which all SOLAS-regulated vessels must comply.

---

## 2.2 Marine Radar — Operational Principle & Signal Flow

A pulse radar operates on the following physics chain:

- **Pulse Generation:** A high-power transmitter (magnetron for traditional sets, solid-state power amplifier for modern ones) generates a short, high-frequency RF pulse — typically **0.05 µs to 2 µs wide**, with peak power from **3 kW to 50 kW** (magnetron) or **up to 200 W peak** for solid-state.
- **Transmission & Duplexing:** The **duplexer (TR switch)** routes the pulse to the antenna, then immediately switches the antenna to the receiver for echo return.
- **Antenna Radiation:** The slotted waveguide array antenna rotates (typically **24 or 42 RPM**) and radiates a narrow horizontal beam (≈ 0.7°–2° beamwidth) with a vertical beam shaped by the waveguide dimensions.
- **Echo Reception:** Reflected RF energy from targets returns to the same antenna. The duplexer feeds it to the low-noise receiver.
- **Signal Processing:** The receiver amplifies, down-converts, detects (envelope), and digitizes the echo. Modern radars apply **CFAR (Constant False Alarm Rate)** processing to suppress sea/rain clutter adaptively.
- **Display:** The processed signal is rasterized onto a **PPI (Plan Position Indicator)** in polar coordinates — bearing is the antenna angle, range is the echo's time delay, intensity/brightness is echo strength.

### 2.2.1 Range and Bearing Determination

**Range (R)** is derived from the **round-trip time (Δt)** of the pulse:

$$ R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2} $$

where **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** is the speed of light in vacuum (≈ 3.03 × 10⁸ m/s in air, rounded down). The factor **2** accounts for the pulse traveling **to the target and back**.

**Bearing (θ)** is read directly from the antenna's angular position, referenced to the ship's heading (gyrocompass input). This is why a **stable heading reference** is mandatory for radar to function meaningfully.

### 2.2.2 Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF) & Maximum Unambiguous Range

The PRF determines how often pulses are transmitted. The maximum unambiguous range is limited by:

$$ R_{max,u} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot PRF} = \frac{c \cdot PRI}{2} $$

where **PRI (Pulse Repetition Interval) = 1/PRF**. Echoes from beyond this range will appear at a false (ambiguous) position — a phenomenon called **second-trace echo** or **range ambiguity**.

### 2.2.3 Minimum Range

The minimum detectable range is set by **pulse width (τ)**:

$$ R_{min} = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2} $$

Shorter pulses give better **range resolution** and smaller **minimum range**, but carry less energy and thus have poorer **maximum range** — a classic radar engineering trade-off.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All key equations for ECDIS/Radar problems. Note: All `\` symbols below use `\vert` instead of `\|` to preserve table rendering.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Key Variables | Units (SI) | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Target Range (Radar) | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | $c$ = speed of light, $\Delta t$ = round-trip time | m | Fundamental range measurement |
| 2 | Minimum Range | $R_{min} = \dfrac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$ | $\tau$ = pulse width | m | Determines pulse-width selection |
| 3 | Max Unambiguous Range | $R_{max,u} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot PRF}$ | $PRF$ = pulse repetition frequency | m | Sets upper scale limit to avoid 2nd-trace echoes |
| 4 | Pulse Repetition Interval | $PRI = \dfrac{1}{PRF}$ | — | s | Timing budget per sweep |
| 5 | Pulse Energy | $E_p = P_t \cdot \tau$ | $P_t$ = peak transmit power | J | SNR budgeting |
| 6 | Antenna Gain | $G = \dfrac{4 \pi \cdot A_e}{\lambda^2}$ | $A_e$ = effective aperture, $\lambda$ = wavelength | (linear) | Beam directivity, link budget |
| 7 | Maximum Radar Range | $R_{max} = \left(\dfrac{P_t \cdot G^2 \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \cdot S_{min}}\right)^{\!\tfrac{1}{4}}$ | $\sigma$ = RCS, $S_{min}$ = min detectable signal | m | Range performance equation |
| 8 | Beamwidth (approx.) | $\theta_{3dB} \approx \dfrac{70 \lambda}{D}$ | $D$ = antenna diameter (length) | degrees | Angular resolution |
| 9 | Duty Cycle | $d = \tau \cdot PRF$ | — | (ratio) | Average power fraction |
| 10 | Average Transmit Power | $P_{avg} = P_t \cdot d$ | — | W | Power consumption & heating |
| 11 | PRF in Range Scales | $PRF \approx \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot R_{scale}}$ | $R_{scale}$ = selected range scale | Hz | Pulse generator setting |
| 12 | Horizontal XTD Limit | $XTD \le \dfrac{\vert a_{safe}\vert - \vert a_{pos}\vert}{2}$ | $a_{safe}$ = safety contour accuracy, $a_{pos}$ = positioning accuracy | NM | Anti-grounding corridor width |
| 13 | CPA (Closest Point of Approach) | $CPA = \dfrac{\vert \Delta v \times \Delta r \vert}{\vert \Delta v \vert}$ where $\Delta v = v_2 - v_1$, $\Delta r = r_2 - r_1$ | $v$ = velocity vectors | NM | ARPA/AIS collision-avoidance maths |
| 14 | TCPA (Time to CPA) | $TCPA = -\dfrac{\Delta r \cdot \Delta v}{\vert \Delta v \vert^{2}}$ | — | hours/s | Time-to-collision trigger |
| 15 | Fix Accuracy (2D) | $\sigma_{fix} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^{2} + \sigma_y^{2}}$ | $\sigma_x, \sigma_y$ = component errors | m | Combined navigation uncertainty |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

These equations are not abstract — they directly drive the engineering of:
- **ECDIS safety depth contour algorithms** → Uses formula 12 to set safe XTD.
- **ARPA radar tracking** → Uses formulas 13 and 14 to generate CPA/TCPA alarms (mandatory alarm thresholds: **CPA ≤ 2 NM** and **TCPA ≤ 12 minutes** by default, per IMO MSC.192(79)).
- **Radar siting & installation** → Beamwidth (formula 8) determines **blind sectors** and **horizontal beam clearance** above obstructions (must be free for at least **twice the half-beamwidth** of obstruction).
- **Range scale selection** → Formula 11 sets the optimum PRF for the chosen scale; modern radars auto-select.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Radar Range Equation (Maximum Range)

The radar range equation is the single most important analytical tool for marine radar. We derive it from **first principles** using the radar link budget.

**Step 1 — Power density at the target.**

The transmitter radiates peak power $P_t$ through an antenna of gain $G$. The power flux density (PFD) at a distance $R$ is the power divided by the surface area of a sphere:

$$ S_1 = \frac{P_t \cdot G}{4 \pi R^{2}} \quad \text{(W/m}^{2}\text{)} $$

**Reasoning:** An isotropic radiator spreads power uniformly over the sphere of area $4\pi R^{2}$. The directional antenna concentrates it by a factor $G$, increasing PFD by $G$ times in the main beam direction.

**Step 2 — Power intercepted and re-radiated by the target.**

The target has a **radar cross-section (RCS)**, denoted $\sigma$, measured in square meters. It intercepts the incoming PFD over its effective area $\sigma$ and re-radiates it isotropically (assumption for simplicity):

$$ P_{re} = S_1 \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t \cdot G \cdot \sigma}{4 \pi R^{2}} \quad \text{(W)} $$

**Reasoning:** RCS is the notional area that, if perfectly isotropically scattering, would return the observed echo power. Typical ship RCS values: small fishing boat **≈ 100 m²**, large tanker **≈ 50,000 m²**, buoy **≈ 1–10 m²**.

**Step 3 — Power density of the echo back at the radar.**

The re-radiated power spreads again over a sphere of radius $R$, giving a return PFD at the radar:

$$ S_2 = \frac{P_{re}}{4 \pi R^{2}} = \frac{P_t \cdot G \cdot \sigma}{(4 \pi)^{2} R^{4}} \quad \text{(W/m}^{2}\text{)} $$

**Reasoning:** Echo path is the same as the forward path, hence another $4\pi R^{2}$ denominator — giving $R^{4}$ in total and $(4\pi)^{2}$ in the denominator.

**Step 4 — Power captured by the receiving antenna.**

The receiving antenna has an effective aperture $A_e$ and captures:

$$ P_r = S_2 \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \cdot G \cdot \sigma \cdot A_e}{(4\pi)^{2} R^{4}} \quad \text{(W)} $$

**Reasoning:** Effective aperture $A_e$ is the physical collection area of the antenna, related to gain by the standard antenna relation.

**Step 5 — Apply the antenna relation $A_e = G \lambda^{2} / (4 \pi)$.**

For a monostatic radar where the same antenna is used for transmit and receive (so transmit gain equals receive gain), we substitute:

$$ P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot G \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^{2} R^{4}} \cdot \frac{G \lambda^{2}}{4 \pi} $$

This simplifies algebraically:

$$ P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot G^{2} \cdot \lambda^{2} \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} R^{4}} $$

This is the **Monostatic Radar Equation** in its standard form.

**Step 6 — Solve for maximum range $R_{max}$.**

The receiver can detect signals down to a minimum power $S_{min}$. Setting $P_r = S_{min}$:

$$ S_{min} = \frac{P_t \cdot G^{2} \cdot \lambda^{2} \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} R_{max}^{4}} $$

Rearranging:

$$ R_{max}^{4} = \frac{P_t \cdot G^{2} \cdot \lambda^{2} \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \cdot S_{min}} $$

Taking the fourth root:

$$ R_{max} = \left(\frac{P_t \cdot G^{2} \cdot \lambda^{2} \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \cdot S_{min}}\right)^{\tfrac{1}{4}} \quad \text{(m)} $$

**Insight:** The $R^{4}$ dependence explains why doubling the maximum detection range requires a **16× increase** in transmitted power (or 16× in $G^{2}$, i.e., 4× in antenna gain). This is the fundamental reason radar power-budgeting is so demanding.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Example (Worked)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A marine X-band radar transmits $P_t = 25\,\text{kW}$ peak power with antenna gain $G = 30\,\text{dBi}$ (i.e., $G = 1000$ linear) at $\lambda = 0.032\,\text{m}$. It must detect a target of RCS $\sigma = 1000\,\text{m}^{2}$ with a minimum detectable signal $S_{min} = 10^{-13}\,\text{W}$. Find $R_{max}$.

**Solution:**

Convert values:
- $P_t = 25{,}000\,\text{W}$
- $G = 1000$ (linear)
- $\lambda = 0.032\,\text{m}$
- $\sigma = 1000\,\text{m}^{2}$
- $S_{min} = 10^{-13}\,\text{W}$

Numerator:
$$ P_t \cdot G^{2} \cdot \lambda^{2} \cdot \sigma = 25000 \cdot (1000)^{2} \cdot (0.032)^{2} \cdot 1000 $$

$$ = 25000 \cdot 10^{6} \cdot 0.001024 \cdot 1000 = 2.56 \times 10^{13} $$

Denominator:
$$ (4\pi)^{3} \cdot S_{min} = (12.566)^{3} \cdot 10^{-13} = 1984.4 \cdot 10^{-13} = 1.9844 \times 10^{-10} $$

Ratio:
$$ \frac{N}{D} = \frac{2.56 \times 10^{13}}{1.9844 \times 10^{-10}} = 1.290 \times 10^{23}\,\text{m}^{4} $$

Take fourth root:
$$ R_{max} = (1.290 \times 10^{23})^{0.25} \approx 18,940\,\text{m} \approx 10.23\,\text{NM} $$

**Interpretation:** The radar can detect a 1000 m² RCS target (e.g., a medium-sized vessel) at about **10.2 NM**, which is reasonable for an X-band set on a merchant ship.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ECDIS vs. Radar

| Attribute | ECDIS | Marine Radar |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Function** | Chart display, route planning, route monitoring | Real-time target detection, ranging, bearing |
| **Data Source** | Static / updated chart database (S-ENC) | Active RF echo reflections |
| **Output Information** | Position, planned track, charted hazards, depth contours | Bearing, range, motion of contacts (with ARPA) |
| **Active vs. Passive** | Largely passive (reads database) | Active (transmits pulses) |
| **Refresh Rate** | Once per second (typical) | Once per antenna rotation (~2.5 s for 24 RPM) |
| **Works in Fog/Dark?** | Yes (uses pre-stored data) | Yes (does not depend on light) |
| **Detects Uncharted Objects?** | No — only shows what is charted | **Yes — primary advantage** |
| **Mandatory Under SOLAS V** | Yes (since 1 July 2018) | Yes (all ships ≥ 300 GT, plus 150 GT since 2020 amendments) |
| **IMO Standard** | MSC.232(82), IEC 61174 | MSC.192(79), IEC 62388 |
| **IHO/IEC Data Standard** | S-57, S-63, S-52 | IEC 62388 (test standard) |
| **Frequency Band** | N/A (computing system) | X-band (9 GHz), S-band (3 GHz) |
| **Typical Power** | ≈ 200 W (computer) | 3–50 kW peak (magnetron) |
| **Integration** | Receives radar overlay + AIS targets | Outputs ARPA tracks, AIS, ECDIS overlay |
| **Failure Consequence** | Loss of situational awareness (mitigated by back-up) | Loss of collision-avoidance & detection |
| **Single Point of Failure Risk** | Software/database corruption | Magnetron wear, antenna motor failure |

---

## 3.4 Anti-Grounding Algorithm (ECDIS Safety Logic)

The ECDIS continuously compares the vessel's **predicted position** (based on course, speed, heading, and set/drift) against the **safety contour** (depth contour selected by the navigator, e.g., 30 m). The logic, in formal terms:

$$ \text{ALARM} \iff \big[\,(x_{pred} - x_{hazard}) < XTD_{port}\big] \;\; \text{OR} \;\; \big[\,(x_{hazard} - x_{pred}) < XTD_{stbd}\big] $$

where $x_{pred}$ is the predicted vessel position vector, $x_{hazard}$ is the closest point on the safety contour in the direction of motion, and $XTD_{port}, XTD_{stbd}$ are the user-set cross-track distance limits. The look-ahead time is typically **3–6 minutes** at service speed, configurable per SOLAS V/34.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ECDIS Functional Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GNSS Receiver<br>GPS GLONASS Galileo BeiDou] --> F[Sensor Integration<br>Unit]
    B[Gyrocompass<br>Heading Sensor] --> F
    C[Speed Log<br>EM or Doppler] --> F
    D[Radar Video<br>ARPA Tracks] --> F
    E[AIS Transceiver<br>Target Data] --> F
    F --> G[ECDIS Processor<br>Workstation CPU]
    H[SENC Database<br>IHO S-57 S-63] --> G
    I[Route Database<br>Voyage Plan] --> G
    J[Update Service<br>AVCS or CD Media] --> H
    G --> K[Display Rendering<br>S-52 Presentation Library]
    K --> L[ECDIS Display<br>Monitor PPI Style]
    G --> M[Alarm Manager<br>Anti Ground XTE CPA TCPA]
    M --> N[Bridge Audible<br>Visual Alarms]
    G --> O[Back up ECDIS<br>or Paper Charts]
    L --> P[Navigating Officer<br>Watchkeeper]
    N --> P
```

**Operational flow:** All sensors (left column) feed the integration unit, which time-stamps and merges them. The processor reads the SENC database (issued by hydrographic offices and kept current via weekly/peril updates) and the route plan, then renders a unified display. The alarm manager continuously evaluates the ship's predicted future position against user-set safety boundaries and raises audible/visual alerts on the bridge. The back-up system (right) provides redundancy as mandated by SOLAS.

---

## 4.2 Marine Radar Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Power Supply<br>High Voltage PSU] --> B[Modulator<br>Pulse Shaper]
    B --> C[Transmitter<br>Magnetron or SSPA]
    C --> D[Duplexer<br>TR Switch]
    D --> E[Slotted Waveguide<br>Antenna 24 or 42 RPM]
    E --> F[Atmosphere<br>Propagation Path]
    F --> G[Target<br>Ship Buoy Land]
    G --> F
    F --> E
    D --> H[Receiver LNA<br>RF Amplifier]
    H --> I[Signal Processor<br>CFAR MTI]
    I --> J[Display Driver<br>PPI Rasterizer]
    J --> K[Radar Display<br>PPI Screen]
    L[Gyro Heading<br>Input] --> J
    M[ARPA Tracker<br>Auto Plotting] --> I
    N[Performance Monitor<br>Self Test] --> C
```

**Operational flow:** The modulator pulses the transmitter, which generates a high-power RF burst. The duplexer routes this to the antenna, which rotates and radiates it. Echoes from targets return along the same path; the duplexer switches them to the receiver. The signal processor filters clutter (sea, rain) using CFAR, the ARPA tracker computes target motion vectors, the display rasterizer converts time-domain echoes into polar PPI coordinates using the gyro heading as the angular reference, and the navigator sees a real-time map of contacts.

---

## 4.3 Integrated Bridge System (ECDIS + Radar + AIS Convergence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Sensors
        R[Radar Antenna]
        G[GNSS Receiver]
        Y[Gyrocompass]
        S[Speed Log]
        A[AIS Transceiver]
        E[Echo Sounder]
    end
    subgraph Processing
        C[ECDIS Workstation]
        P[Radar Processor with ARPA]
        T[Central Alarm Management]
    end
    subgraph Display
        D1[ECDIS Multi Function Display]
        D2[Radar PPI Display]
        D3[Conning Display]
    end
    R --> P
    P --> D2
    P --> C
    A --> C
    A --> P
    G --> C
    G --> P
    Y --> C
    Y --> P
    S --> C
    S --> P
    E --> C
    C --> D1
    C --> D3
    D1 --> T
    D2 --> T
    T --> O[Bridge Watchkeeper]
```

**Operational flow:** Sensors are shared between radar and ECDIS via a standardized **IEC 61162 (NMEA 0183/2000) data bus**. The radar processor outputs ARPA-tracked targets and raw video; ECDIS consumes both and renders the radar overlay on top of the ENC. All alarms route to a central alarm management system (BAM) that drives the bridge watchkeeper's attention.
