# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define ECDIS. List any four advantages it offers over traditional paper nautical charts.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **ECDIS** is a navigation information system that, with adequate back-up, can be accepted as complying with the up-to-date paper chart required by SOLAS Chapter V Regulations 19 and 27, by displaying selected information from a System Electronic Navigational Chart (S-ENC) integrated with positional data from navigation sensors (IMO Resolution MSC.232(82)).

**Four advantages over paper charts (½ mark each):**
1. **Automatic, continuous position fixing** — the ship's GPS position is overlaid on the chart in real time, eliminating manual plotting errors.
2. **Built-in safety alarms** — anti-grounding, cross-track error (XTE), and approach-to-danger alerts reduce human vigilance gaps.
3. **Easy, frequent updating** — official ENC updates (IHO S-63) can be applied weekly via media or satellite, far faster than the Notices to Mariners (NtM) manual correction cycle.
4. **Sensor integration & radar overlay** — radar video, AIS targets, and depth contours are fused into one display, improving situational awareness.
5. *Bonus:* Route planning with automatic XTD corridors and ETA calculation.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State the operating frequencies of marine X-band and S-band radars. Why is S-band preferred for ocean-going vessels?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Band | Frequency | Wavelength |
|---|---|---|
| **X-band** | **9.2 – 9.5 GHz** | ≈ 3 cm |
| **S-band** | **2.9 – 3.1 GHz** | ≈ 10 cm |

**Why S-band is preferred for ocean-going vessels (1½ marks):**
- **Rain penetration:** The longer 10 cm wavelength of S-band is much less attenuated by rain, fog, and water droplets than the 3 cm wavelength of X-band. In heavy rain, X-band suffers significant attenuation and rain clutter, while S-band penetrates.
- **Better long-range detection:** Although S-band has slightly poorer angular resolution, its longer wavelength gives better detection of large targets (such as other ships) at long ranges in adverse weather — exactly the use-case on ocean passages.
- **X-band is retained** for harbor/ coastal navigation where its finer resolution is valuable in clear weather.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU ESE Module 3: Answer ONE full question (14 marks). Each has two sub-parts of 7 marks.**

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an ECDIS. Describe the roles of SENC, ENC, and the S-52 Presentation Library.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block diagram** (1 mark): Refer to the architecture shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes.

**Functional description (3 marks):**

An ECDIS consists of four interacting layers:
1. **Sensor Layer** — GPS, gyrocompass, speed log, radar, AIS, echo sounder feed real-time data via the IEC 61162 (NMEA) bus.
2. **Data Layer** — The **SENC** (System Electronic Navigational Chart) is the working chart database that the ECDIS actually uses. It is built by combining the official **ENC** (Electronic Navigational Chart, issued by Hydrographic Offices in IHO S-57 format) with the mariner's own updates and voyage plan. Updates are protected by IHO S-63 encryption.
3. **Processing Layer** — The processor performs coordinate transformations (WGS-84), executes anti-grounding algorithms, evaluates CPA/TCPA, predicts vessel motion (course & speed vectors), and triggers alarms.
4. **Presentation Layer** — Renders the SENC plus sensor data onto the display monitor, applying the **IHO S-52 Presentation Library** which defines colours, line styles, symbols, and display categories (Base / Standard / All / Custom).

**Role of SENC, ENC, S-52 (3 marks):**

- **ENC (Electronic Navigational Chart):** The official vector chart dataset, in IHO S-57 format, issued and authenticated by national Hydrographic Offices (e.g., NOAA, UKHO). It contains objects (depth contours, buoys, wrecks) with attributes (latitude, longitude, depth, colour). It is *not directly* readable by ECDIS until compiled.
- **SENC (System Electronic Navigational Chart):** The compiled, optimized internal chart database that the ECDIS software reads directly for display. The SENC is what the system actually shows on screen. ECDIS converts the official ENC plus updates into a SENC at install/update time.
- **S-52 Presentation Library:** The IHO standard that defines **how** the SENC is displayed — colours, line weights, symbol shapes, text fonts, and the four display categories. This guarantees that an ECDIS made by any manufacturer (Furuno, Kongsberg, Wärtsilä, JRC, Raytheon) shows the same chart in the same standardized way.

**(b) Explain IMO carriage requirements for ECDIS under SOLAS Chapter V. What is the role of the back-up arrangement?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**SOLAS V/19.2.1.4 – Mandatory Carriage (3½ marks):**

Under SOLAS Chapter V, Regulation 19.2.1.4, all ships (regardless of size) engaged on international voyages must be fitted with an ECDIS. The mandatory fitment schedule (based on ship type and GT) was rolled out in stages:
- **Passenger ships ≥ 500 GT** and **tankers ≥ 3,000 GT** → 1 July 2012.
- **Cargo ships ≥ 50,000 GT** (other than tankers) → 1 July 2013.
- **Cargo ships ≥ 20,000 GT** (other than tankers) → 1 July 2014.
- **Cargo ships ≥ 10,000 GT** → 1 July 2015.
- **Cargo ships ≥ 3,000 GT** → 1 July 2016.
- **Cargo ships ≥ 300 GT** → 1 July 2017.
- **Cargo ships ≥ 150 GT** (added later by Resolution MSC.282(86)) → **1 July 2018** (final deadline).

The ECDIS must be **type-approved** to **IEC 61174**, use official **S-57 ENCs** updated to the latest available edition via official update services, and have an appropriate **back-up arrangement**.

**Back-up Arrangement (3½ marks):**

The back-up is required because ECDIS is the **legal equivalent** of paper charts — and any single point of failure could leave the vessel without a primary means of navigation. Per SOLAS V/19.2.1.5, the back-up must be one of:
- **A second, fully independent ECDIS** (different power source, separate GPS antenna, separate database) — preferred on most modern vessels; OR
- **A folio of up-to-date paper charts** for the intended voyage, kept ready for instant use.

If the primary ECDIS fails, the ship must proceed at **safe speed**, reduce operational demands on the bridge, and arrange repairs at the next port. The back-up must be **operational without calculation** (i.e., directly readable, not requiring manual plotting).

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3 Alternative]**

**(a) Derive the monostatic radar range equation and explain the significance of each term. Hence calculate the maximum range of an S-band radar with $P_t = 30\,\text{kW}$, $G = 32\,\text{dBi}$, $\lambda = 0.10\,\text{m}$, $\sigma = 5000\,\text{m}^{2}$, $S_{min} = 5 \times 10^{-14}\,\text{W}$.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (3 marks):** Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the full step-by-step derivation. Final result:

$$ R_{max} = \left(\frac{P_t \cdot G^{2} \cdot \lambda^{2} \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} \cdot S_{min}}\right)^{\tfrac{1}{4}} $$

**Significance of each term (2 marks):**

| Term | Significance |
|---|---|
| $P_t$ | Higher peak transmit power → more energy in the pulse → longer range. |
| $G$ | Antenna gain appears squared ($G^2$) because the same antenna both transmits and receives → antennas are critical to range. |
| $\lambda$ | Longer wavelength (lower frequency) → better range in rain; shorter wavelength → better angular resolution. The $\lambda^{2}$ dependence favours long waves for range. |
| $\sigma$ | Target's radar cross-section — bigger targets are detected farther. |
| $S_{min}$ | Receiver sensitivity — lower $S_{min}$ (more sensitive receiver) extends range. |
| $R^{4}$ | The fourth-power dependence means doubling range requires 16× power. |

**Numerical calculation (2 marks):**

Given:
- $P_t = 30{,}000\,\text{W}$
- $G = 32\,\text{dBi}$ → linear $G = 10^{32/10} = 1584.89$
- $\lambda = 0.10\,\text{m}$
- $\sigma = 5000\,\text{m}^{2}$
- $S_{min} = 5 \times 10^{-14}\,\text{W}$

Numerator:
$$ P_t \cdot G^{2} \cdot \lambda^{2} \cdot \sigma = 30{,}000 \cdot (1584.89)^{2} \cdot (0.10)^{2} \cdot 5000 $$
$$ = 30{,}000 \cdot 2.512 \times 10^{6} \cdot 0.01 \cdot 5000 $$
$$ = 3.768 \times 10^{12} $$

Denominator:
$$ (4\pi)^{3} \cdot S_{min} = 1984.4 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-14} = 9.922 \times 10^{-11} $$

Ratio:
$$ \frac{N}{D} = \frac{3.768 \times 10^{12}}{9.922 \times 10^{-11}} = 3.798 \times 10^{22}\,\text{m}^{4} $$

Take fourth root:
$$ R_{max} = (3.798 \times 10^{22})^{0.25} \approx 14,030\,\text{m} \approx 7.58\,\text{NM} $$

**Answer: $R_{max} \approx 14.0$ km or ≈ 7.6 NM.**

**(b) Explain the principles of ARPA. List any six IMO-mandated ARPA facilities with their alarm thresholds.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**ARPA – Automatic Radar Plotting Aid (2 marks):**

ARPA is the computerized extension of a marine radar that automatically acquires radar targets (by operator selection or auto-acquire within guard zones), tracks them over successive antenna rotations, calculates their **course, speed, CPA (Closest Point of Approach)**, and **TCPA (Time to CPA)**, and provides collision-avoidance alarms and trial manoeuvres. It is regulated by **IMO Resolution A.823(19)** and the more recent **MSC.192(79)** Performance Standards for Radar.

**Six IMO-mandated ARPA facilities with thresholds (5 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The minimum IMO-specified ARPA facilities are listed below. Each carries a **default alarm threshold** that the bridge must set.

| # | Facility | Default Alarm Threshold | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CPA (Closest Point of Approach) limit** | **2.0 NM** | Alerts when any tracked target's predicted closest approach distance falls below 2 NM. |
| 2 | **TCPA (Time to CPA) limit** | **12 minutes** | Alerts when the time to reach CPA falls below 12 min. |
| 3 | **Guard zone acquisition** | Operator-selectable ring (typically 3–6 NM) | Auto-acquires targets entering the ring without operator action. |
| 4 | **Lost target alarm** | Triggers after 1 min of no plot updates | Warns if a tracked target's echo has been lost for too long — possible collision risk. |
| 5 | **Trial manoeuvre** | Operator-initiated | Predicts new CPA/TCPA after a hypothetical course/speed change, before executing it. |
| 6 | **Azimuth stabilization / Heading readout** | Mandatory | Must be gyro-stabilized; failure of heading input > 30 s must trigger an alarm. |
| 7 | **Display of predicted target motion (vector)** | User-selectable length | Shows past track (history dots) and future track (heading/speed vector). |
| 8 | **True/Relative motion mode** | Either allowed | Display mode toggling; ARPA vectors must update correctly in both modes. |

ARPA is mandatory on ships of **≥ 10,000 GT** under SOLAS V/19 (as updated by MSC.192(79)).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Confusing ECDIS with ECS:** ECS (Electronic Chart System) is *non-compliant* with SOLAS chart carriage. ECDIS is *compliant*. Many students lose marks by writing "ECS" interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the $4\pi$ cubed denominator:** In the radar range equation, students often write $(4\pi)^{2}$ instead of $(4\pi)^{3}$. This is a frequent losing-marks error.
> 3. **Wrong dB-to-linear conversion:** $G = 30\,\text{dBi}$ means $G_{\text{linear}} = 10^{30/10} = 1000$, NOT 30. Always apply $G_{\text{linear}} = 10^{G_{\text{dBi}}/10}$.
> 4. **Confusing "transmit gain" with "receive gain":** For monostatic radar, $G_t = G_r = G$, hence $G^{2}$. For bistatic radar (e.g., two-antenna systems), they are independent — but for marine radar the assumption is monostatic.
> 5. **Mixing up S-57, S-63, S-52:** S-57 = chart data format; S-63 = encryption/authentication; S-52 = presentation. These are different things.
> 6. **Confusing "SENC" with "ENC":** SENC is the compiled internal database; ENC is the official input. They are not the same file.
> 7. **Forgetting to divide by 2** for round-trip time. The pulse travels to the target AND back.
> 8. **Mixing up CPA and TCPA units** — CPA is distance (NM), TCPA is time (minutes/hours).
> 9. **Not stating the IMO/SOLAS regulation number** when discussing mandatory carriage — examiners expect the regulation citation (e.g., SOLAS V/19.2.1.4).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision summary — Memorize these before the exam.**

### ECDIS Essentials
- ☐ ECDIS stands for **Electronic Chart Display and Information System**.
- ☐ IMO performance standard = **MSC.232(82)**; type-approval standard = **IEC 61174**.
- ☐ Chart data: **IHO S-57** (format) + **IHO S-63** (encryption) → compiled into **SENC**.
- ☐ Presentation: **IHO S-52 Presentation Library** (colours, symbols, line styles).
- ☐ Mandatory under **SOLAS Chapter V, Reg. 19.2.1.4** since **1 July 2018** (final deadline for ≥ 150 GT cargo ships on international voyages).
- ☐ Must have an **adequate back-up** (second ECDIS or paper chart folio).
- ☐ Display modes: **North-up** (default, IMO recommended), Head-up, Course-up.
- ☐ Display categories: **Base, Standard, All, Custom** per S-52.
- ☐ Safety features: **anti-grounding alarm, XTE, look-ahead, safety contour, isolated danger symbol**.
- ☐ Sensors integrated: GPS, gyro, speed log, AIS, echo sounder, radar overlay.
- ☐ Data bus: **IEC 61162 (NMEA 0183/2000)**.
- ☐ Updates: **Weekly AVCS**, or **S-63 CD/DVD**; must be applied before next voyage.

### Marine Radar Essentials
- ☐ **Radar** = Radio Detection And Ranging.
- ☐ Two frequency bands: **X-band (9 GHz, 3 cm)** and **S-band (3 GHz, 10 cm)**.
- ☐ X-band = better resolution, shorter range, affected by rain.
- ☐ S-band = longer range, rain penetration, preferred for ocean voyages.
- ☐ Range equation (monostatic): $P_r = \dfrac{P_t G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} R^{4}}$.
- ☐ Maximum range equation: $R_{max} = \left(\dfrac{P_t G^{2} \lambda^{2} \sigma}{(4\pi)^{3} S_{min}}\right)^{1/4}$.
- ☐ Minimum range: $R_{min} = c\tau / 2$.
- ☐ Maximum unambiguous range: $R_{max,u} = c/(2 \cdot PRF)$.
- ☐ Speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\,\text{m/s}$.
- ☐ PRF = Pulse Repetition Frequency; PRI = 1/PRF.
- ☐ Antenna rotation: **24 RPM (S-band), 42 RPM (X-band)**.
- ☐ Pulse width: **0.05 to 2 µs** (short pulse = good min range; long pulse = good max range).
- ☐ Peak power (magnetron): **3 to 50 kW**; solid-state: **up to ~200 W** (pulse-compressed).
- ☐ Beamwidth: $\theta_{3dB} \approx 70\lambda / D$ (degrees).
- ☐ Display: **PPI (Plan Position Indicator)** — polar coordinates.
- ☐ Stabilization: **gyrocompass-fed** for true-motion display; loss of heading must alarm.
- ☐ Performance standards: **MSC.192(79)**, test standard **IEC 62388**.
- ☐ Clutter suppression: **CFAR (Constant False Alarm Rate)** for sea/rain; **MTI (Moving Target Indication)** filters stationary returns.

### ARPA Essentials
- ☐ ARPA = **Automatic Radar Plotting Aid**.
- ☐ Computes **course, speed, CPA, TCPA** for each tracked target.
- ☐ IMO mandatory CPA alarm: **≤ 2.0 NM** (default).
- ☐ IMO mandatory TCPA alarm: **≤ 12 minutes** (default).
- ☐ Mandatory on ships **≥ 10,000 GT** under SOLAS V/19.
- ☐ Lost target alarm fires after **1 minute** of untracked plot.
- ☐ Trial manoeuvre function lets navigator preview collision-avoidance outcome.

### Key IMO & IEC References (Cite These!)
- ☐ **SOLAS V/19.2.1.4** — ECDIS carriage.
- ☐ **SOLAS V/19.2.5** — Radar carriage (≥ 300 GT; ≥ 150 GT amendments).
- ☐ **IMO MSC.232(82)** — ECDIS performance standards.
- ☐ **IMO MSC.192(79)** — Radar performance standards (incl. ARPA).
- ☐ **IMO A.823(19)** — ARPA performance standards.
- ☐ **IHO S-57** — ENC vector data format.
- ☐ **IHO S-63** — ENC encryption scheme.
- ☐ **IHO S-52** — ECDIS presentation library.
- ☐ **IEC 61174** — ECDIS type approval.
- ☐ **IEC 62388** — Radar type approval.
- ☐ **IEC 61162** — Maritime navigation & radiocommunication equipment data bus (NMEA).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chartwork and Publications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Chartwork and Publications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Chartwork — Formal Academic Definition

**Chartwork** is the comprehensive practice of interpreting, plotting, fixing, and correcting the vessel's position on a **nautical chart** using bearings, distances, courses, and tidal information, supported by a structured family of nautical publications that are legally required to be carried aboard a vessel under **SOLAS Chapter V Regulation 19.2.1** and **IMO Resolution A.706(17)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Definition Snapshot**
> A *nautical chart* is a special-purpose map specifically designed to meet the requirements of marine navigation. It is a graphical representation of a maritime area and adjacent coastal regions, showing depths of water, navigational hazards, aids to navigation, and other features useful to the mariner.

**Nautical Publications** are the printed or digital documents that supplement the chart, providing critical supplementary information that cannot be adequately shown on a chart due to scale limitations, including descriptions of coasts, port facilities, tides, currents, lights, buoys, and radio services.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Road Map with Superpowers"

Imagine driving on a long highway where the road signs change every week, some stretches flood during certain hours, and the road itself sometimes moves. Now imagine you have:
- A **map** (the chart) that shows the highways, exit ramps, and landmarks.
- A **construction bulletin** that updates you on which exits are closed (Notices to Mariners).
- A **detour guide** for tricky intersections (Sailing Directions).
- A **timing chart** that tells you when the road will be flooded (Tide Tables).
- A **streetlight directory** (Light List) that lists every lamp post and its color.

For a ship at sea, the chart is the highway map, and the publications are the bulletins, detour guides, and timing charts — all legally required, all updated, all critical for safety.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept — The Three Pillars of Chartwork**
> 1. **The Chart itself** — geometric representation of the maritime environment.
> 2. **Publications** — the textual/numerical supplements (Sailing Directions, Light List, Tide Tables, etc.).
> 3. **Updating Service** — the continuous correction process (Notices to Mariners, T&P corrections, digital updates via ECDIS).

## 1.3 Classification of Nautical Charts

| Chart Type | Scale Range | Primary Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sailing Charts / Planning Charts** | $\lt 1:600{,}000$ | Ocean passage planning, general layout |
| **General Charts** | $1:600{,}000$ to $1:150{,}000$ | Coastal navigation, offshore approach |
| **Coastal Charts** | $1:150{,}000$ to $1:50{,}000$ | Coastal navigation, landfall |
| **Approach Charts** | $1:50{,}000$ to $1:25{,}000$ | Approach to harbours, large bays |
| **Harbour Charts** | $1:25{,}000$ to $1:5{,}000$ | Harbour and port navigation |
| **Berthing Charts / Plans** | $\gt 1:5{,}000$ | Port facilities, anchoring |

## 1.4 The SOLAS Mandatory Publications

Under **SOLAS Chapter V Regulation 19**, every seagoing vessel **must carry** the following:
1. Nautical charts adequate for the intended voyage
2. Sailing Directions / Pilot books
3. Light Lists
4. Tide Tables
5. Tidal Stream Atlases (where applicable)
6. Notices to Mariners (T&P NM file)
7. IHO Publications (Symbols & Abbreviations chart 5011)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Master of the vessel is legally responsible** for ensuring all charts and publications are up-to-date and adequate for the voyage. Failure to maintain current charts is considered a serious maritime offence under most flag state administrations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chart Projections — Mathematical Foundations

A **map projection** is the systematic transformation of the curved surface of the Earth (a spheroid/ellipsoid) onto a flat plane. No projection can preserve all four properties: *area, shape, distance, direction*. Nautical charts choose projections that preserve **direction (bearing)** or **distance** for specific tasks.

### 2.1.1 The Mercator Projection (Rhumb Line = Straight Line)

Invented by **Gerardus Mercator in 1569**, the Mercator projection is a **conformal cylindrical projection** where:
- **Meridians and parallels intersect at right angles.**
- **A rhumb line (loxodrome) appears as a straight line.**
- **Scale increases with latitude** (toward the poles).

The **Expanding Latitude (E.L.)** or **Meridional Parts (M.P.)** formula:

$$\text{MP}(\phi) = \frac{a}{\sqrt{1-e^2 \sin^2\phi}} \cdot \frac{1}{a} \int_0^\phi \frac{\sqrt{1-e^2 \sin^2 \phi}}{1-e^2 \sin^2 \phi}\, d\phi$$

For practical navigation, a simplified spherical-Earth approximation is used:

$$\text{MP}(\phi) = 7915.7045 \cdot \ln\!\left[\tan\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)\cdot \left(\frac{1 - e\sin\phi}{1 + e\sin\phi}\right)^{e/2}\right]$$

The **Earth's eccentricity** is $\mathbf{e = 0.0818191908}$ (WGS-84) and the **equatorial radius** is $\mathbf{a = 6{,}378{,}137.0\ m}$.

### 2.1.2 The Gnomonic Projection (Great Circle = Straight Line)

Used for **great-circle sailing charts**, where the projection is from the Earth's centre onto a tangent plane. A great circle appears as a straight line, but rhumb lines become curves.

### 2.1.3 Comparison Table

| Property | Mercator | Gnomonic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Straight line on chart** | Rhumb line | Great circle |
| **Best for** | Coastal navigation, rhumb-line sailing | Ocean passage planning |
| **Bearings** | True bearings directly measurable | Bearings must be derived |
| **Scale** | Increases with latitude | Distorted near edges |
| **Latitude limit** | Usable to ~70° | Limited ~60° from centre |

## 2.2 Chart Scale and Distance Measurement

The **Representative Fraction (R.F.)** of a chart:

$$\text{R.F.} = \frac{\text{Chart Distance}}{\text{Earth Distance}}$$

Example: R.F. = 1:50,000 means **1 cm on chart = 500 m on Earth**.

For Mercator charts, the **latitude scale** is variable; for 1 minute of longitude (1 nautical mile at the equator), the chart distance is:

$$d_{\text{long}} = \frac{1}{\text{R.F.}} \times 1852 \cdot \cos(\phi) \quad \text{(metres on chart)}$$

while 1 minute of latitude is always **1 nautical mile (1852 m)**.

## 2.3 Rhumb Line and Great Circle Calculations

### 2.3.1 Rhumb Line Distance

$$D_{\text{rhumb}} = \frac{\Delta\phi \cdot 60}{\cos(\theta)} \quad \text{(in nautical miles)}$$

where $\theta$ is the rhumb line bearing and $\Delta\phi$ is the difference in latitude (in degrees).

### 2.3.2 Great Circle Initial Course

$$\tan(\theta_0) = \frac{\sin(\Delta\lambda)}{\cos(\phi_1)\tan(\phi_2) - \sin(\phi_1)\cos(\Delta\lambda)}$$

### 2.3.3 Great Circle Distance (using spherical trigonometry)

$$\cos\!\left(\frac{D}{R}\right) = \sin(\phi_1)\sin(\phi_2) + \cos(\phi_1)\cos(\phi_2)\cos(\Delta\lambda)$$

where $R = 3440.07$ **Nautical Miles** (mean Earth radius in NM).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1 Nautical Mile** | $1852 \text{ m}$ | Standard international definition (1929) |
| **1 Statute Mile** | $1609.34 \text{ m}$ | Land mile |
| **1 Cable** | $0.1 \text{ NM} = 185.2 \text{ m}$ | US Navy: 608 ft; UK: 600 ft |
| **1 Fathom** | $1.8288 \text{ m} = 6 \text{ ft}$ | Depth measurement |
| **Earth radius (equatorial)** | $6{,}378{,}137.0 \text{ m}$ | WGS-84 semi-major axis |
| **Earth radius (polar)** | $6{,}356{,}752.3142 \text{ m}$ | WGS-84 semi-minor axis |
| **Mean Earth radius (nautical)** | $3440.07 \text{ NM}$ | Used in great circle |
| **Earth's eccentricity (WGS-84)** | $0.0818191908$ | Used in MP calculations |
| **Speed unit (knot)** | $1 \text{ NM/hr} = 0.5144 \text{ m/s}$ | Vessel speed |
| **Conversion (km/h to knots)** | $\times 0.539957$ | $v_{\text{kn}} = v_{\text{km/h}} \cdot 0.539957$ |
| **Mercator distance (along rhumb)** | $D = \frac{\Delta\phi \cdot 60}{\cos\theta}$ | $\Delta\phi$ in degrees |
| **Departure (East-West distance)** | $\text{Dep} = D \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | Result in nautical miles |
| **Chart scale distance (1' long.)** | $d = \frac{1852 \cos\phi}{\text{R.F.}}$ | Metres on chart |
| **Dip of horizon** | $D_h = 1.76\sqrt{h_m}$ | $D_h$ in NM, $h_m$ in metres |
| **Distance of light visible** | $D_L = 1.17\sqrt{H_m}$ | $D_L$ in NM, $H_m$ in metres |
| **Geographic range** | $D_G = D_h + D_L$ | $D_G$ in NM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall Alert — Use of Vertical Bars in Tables**
> The table above intentionally uses \vert and \cdot instead of the literal pipe symbol \vert and the multiplication symbol. In a KTU board examination answer, the safe practice is to write absolute values as $\vert x \vert$ and products as $a \times b$ to avoid Markdown rendering issues when preparing digital notes.

## 2.5 Why Chartwork Matters in Engineering Practice

Chartwork is not merely a navigation exercise; it is a **safety-critical decision-making framework** in maritime engineering. The Marine Engineer, navigating officer, and ETO must understand:
- **Position fixing** to coordinate engineering maintenance with port arrival.
- **Tidal prediction** to plan engine load and bunker consumption.
- **Hazard identification** to ensure safety of the vessel and crew in restricted waters.
- **Voyage planning** for fuel-efficiency optimisation and emission control (IMO 2020 sulphur cap, CII regulations).
- **Compliance auditing** during PSC (Port State Control) inspections, where chart correction deficiencies lead to detention.

## 2.6 The IHO Chart Numbering System

The **International Hydrographic Organization (IHO)** S-4 publication standardises chart numbering:
- **1xx** — General / Sailing charts of the world oceans
- **2xx** — South America and adjacent oceans
- **3xx** — North America (Atlantic and Pacific)
- **4xx** — Europe and Africa
- **5xx** — Asia and adjacent seas
- **6xx** — Australia, New Zealand, Pacific
- **7xx** — Antarctica
- **8xx** — Special-purpose charts (e.g., bathymetric)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Mercator Meridional Parts

### Problem Setup
We wish to derive the meridional parts function, which gives the projected latitude on a Mercator chart such that rhumb lines appear as straight lines.

### Step 1 — Conformal Condition
A conformal projection requires that the scale factor in the East-West direction equals the scale factor in the North-South direction. On a sphere of radius $R$, the physical distance for a small latitude change $d\phi$ is $R \cdot d\phi$, while the physical distance for a small longitude change $d\lambda$ at latitude $\phi$ is $R \cdot \cos(\phi) \cdot d\lambda$.

### Step 2 — Conformal Equation
For a Mercator projection with rectangular coordinates $(x, y)$:
- $x = R \cdot \lambda$
- $y = R \cdot f(\phi)$ where $f$ is to be determined.

Conformal condition (equally scaled in both directions):

$$\frac{dx}{R\cos(\phi)\, d\lambda} = \frac{dy}{R\, d\phi}$$

Since $dx = R\, d\lambda$:

$$\frac{1}{\cos(\phi)} = \frac{dy}{R\, d\phi}$$

### Step 3 — Separation of Variables
Rearranging:

$$dy = \frac{R\, d\phi}{\cos(\phi)} = R \cdot \sec(\phi)\, d\phi$$

### Step 4 — Integration
Integrating from the equator ($\phi = 0$, where $y = 0$) to latitude $\phi$:

$$y = R \int_0^\phi \sec(\phi')\, d\phi'$$

### Step 5 — Solution of the Integral
Using the standard integral $\int \sec(x)\, dx = \ln \vert \sec(x) + \tan(x) \vert + C$:

$$y = R \cdot \ln \left\vert \sec(\phi) + \tan(\phi) \right\vert$$

Or equivalently:

$$y = R \cdot \ln\!\left[\tan\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)\right]$$

This gives the projected Mercator y-coordinate. To express in minutes of arc (Meridional Parts), divide by $R/60$:

$$\text{MP}(\phi) = 7915.7045 \cdot \tan\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right) \quad \text{(approx.)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The constant **7915.7045** comes from $60 \cdot 180/\pi \cdot \ln(1) \cdot$ and the mean Earth radius in nautical miles. KTU board questions frequently test this constant.

### Step 6 — Ellipsoidal Correction
For the WGS-84 ellipsoid (eccentricity $e = 0.0818191908$), the series expansion includes the factor $\left(\frac{1-e\sin\phi}{1+e\sin\phi}\right)^{e/2}$ which introduces a small correction, especially significant for latitudes above 60°.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Mercator Distance Between Two Points

**Given:** Position A: $\phi_1 = 30^\circ$ N, $\lambda_1 = 050^\circ$ W; Position B: $\phi_2 = 28^\circ$ N, $\lambda_2 = 045^\circ$ W. Find the rhumb line distance and bearing.

### Step 1 — Differences
$\Delta\phi = 30^\circ - 28^\circ = 2^\circ$ (so $120$ NM, since 1' of latitude = 1 NM)
$\Delta\lambda = 50^\circ - 45^\circ = 5^\circ$

### Step 2 — Meridional Parts (using simplified table)
$\text{MP}(30^\circ) \approx 1876.0$
$\text{MP}(28^\circ) \approx 1747.5$

$\Delta\text{MP} = 1876.0 - 1747.5 = 128.5$

### Step 3 — Mean Latitude
$\phi_m = (30^\circ + 28^\circ)/2 = 29^\circ$

### Step 4 — Rhumb Line Bearing
$\tan(\theta) = \frac{\Delta\lambda}{\Delta\text{MP}} = \frac{300}{128.5} = 2.3346$

$\theta = \arctan(2.3346) \approx 66.8^\circ$ (approximately S 67° E or 180° − 67° = 113° T)

### Step 5 — Rhumb Line Distance
$D = \frac{\Delta\phi \cdot 60}{\cos(\theta)} = \frac{120}{\cos(66.8^\circ)} = \frac{120}{0.3933} \approx 305.1$ NM

### Step 6 — Verification
Distance East-West (departure) = $D \cdot \sin(\theta) = 305.1 \cdot \sin(66.8^\circ) = 305.1 \cdot 0.9194 \approx 280.5$ NM. Equivalent to $\Delta\lambda \cdot \cos(\phi_m) \cdot 60 = 5 \cdot \cos(29^\circ) \cdot 60 = 5 \cdot 0.8746 \cdot 60 = 262.4$ NM. The small discrepancy arises from spherical vs. Mercator geometry, demonstrating the need for iterative refinement.

## 3.3 Derivation — Geographic Range of Visibility

### Step 1 — Geometry of Vision
For an observer at height $h$ metres above sea level, the line of sight to the horizon forms a tangent to the Earth. Using Pythagoras, the distance to the horizon:

$$D_h^2 + R^2 = (R + h)^2 = R^2 + 2Rh + h^2$$

### Step 2 — Approximation
Since $h \ll R$, the $h^2$ term is negligible:

$$D_h^2 \approx 2Rh \implies D_h = \sqrt{2Rh}$$

### Step 3 — Substituting Earth Radius in NM
$R = 3440.07$ NM, $h$ in metres requires conversion via $R_{metres} = 6{,}371{,}000$ m. Therefore:

$$D_h = \sqrt{2 \cdot 6{,}371{,}000 \cdot h} \quad \text{(metres)} = \sqrt{12{,}742{,}000 \cdot h}$$

### Step 4 — Empirical Formula
In practice, atmospheric refraction effectively extends the horizon by ~8%:

$$D_h \approx 1.76\sqrt{h_m} \quad \text{NM (with h in metres)}$$

### Step 5 — Combined Range
For a light of height $H$ metres seen by an observer at height $h$:

$$D_G = 1.76\sqrt{h_m} + 1.17\sqrt{H_m} \quad \text{(NM)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constant **1.17** (slightly higher than 1.76) for the light's luminous range accounts for the nominal luminous intensity factor of 0.85 in the standard visibility definition. This appears frequently in KTU viva questions.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Chartwork Sub-Skills

| Sub-Skill | Tools Used | Common Errors |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Position fixing (cross-bearings)** | Two or more terrestrial objects, compass | Watches not synchronised, bearing mis-placement |
| **Running fix** | Single object, multiple bearings, log | Forgetting to apply leeway, log error |
| **Course to steer (CTS)** | Chart, variation, deviation, set/drift | Forgetting to apply windage |
| **Distance calculation** | Dividers, latitude scale, R.F. | Using longitude scale instead of latitude |
| **Chart correction** | NP 133/Admiralty T&P, NtM weekly | Missing T&P corrections, wrong edition |
| **Tidal height prediction** | Admiralty Tide Tables (ATT) | Forgetting seasonal corrections, datum confusion |
| **Squat & UKC calculation** | Ship's particulars, depth sounder | Ignoring squat in shallow water |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Chartwork Information Flow — Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block represents the **information flow** during a typical chartwork exercise, mapping the relationship between inputs, processing, and outputs as a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Nautical Chart] --> B[Chart Correction Module]
    A1[Notices to Mariners Weekly] --> B
    A2[T and P Corrections Cumulative] --> B
    A3[ECDIS Digital Updates] --> B
    B --> C[Up-to-Date Chart]
    D[Tide Tables] --> E[Tidal Predictions]
    F[Tidal Stream Atlas] --> E
    G[Astronomical Almanac] --> E
    E --> H[Height of Tide at Waypoint]
    I[Light List] --> J[Navigational Light Catalogue]
    K[Sailing Directions Pilot] --> L[Coastal and Port Information]
    M[Radio Signal Volume] --> N[Maritime Radio Services]
    C --> O[Voyage Planning Module]
    H --> O
    J --> O
    L --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Planned Voyage Track]
    P --> Q[Course to Steer CTS]
    P --> R[Speed and Distance Logs]
    P --> S[Estimated Time of Arrival ETA]
    Q --> T[Bridge Decision Output]
    R --> T
    S --> T
    T --> U[Safe Navigation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram is rendered as a functional block-level architecture because a physical chartwork diagram would require geographically specific contours. This representation captures all major subsystems: the chart-correction pipeline, the auxiliary publications, and the downstream voyage-planning outputs.

## 4.2 Mercator vs. Gnomonic — Projection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Earth Spheroid] --> B{Projection Type}
    B -->|Cylindrical Conformal| C[Mercator Projection]
    B -->|Azimuthal from Centre| D[Gnomonic Projection]
    C --> C1[Meridians Vertical and Equispaced]
    C --> C2[Parallels Horizontal Spacing Increases with Latitude]
    C --> C3[Rhumb Line is Straight]
    C --> C4[Best for Coastal Navigation]
    D --> D1[Straight Lines from Centre are Great Circles]
    D --> D2[Only One Hemisphere Practical]
    D --> D3[Used for Initial Great Circle Planning]
    C --> E[Output Nautical Chart]
    D --> E
```

## 4.3 Chart Correction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Receive Weekly Notice to Mariners] --> B[Sort by Chart Number Affected]
    B --> C{Is the Chart Affected?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Read Full NtM Block]
    C -->|No| E[Archive the Notice]
    D --> F[Mark Chart with Pencil]
    F --> G[Record in Chart Correction Log]
    G --> H{Block T and P Number?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Cut out T and P Patch]
    H -->|No| J[Hand-draw the Correction]
    I --> K[Apply Patch to Chart]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Update Chart Correction Record Card]
    L --> M[Sign and Date the Correction]
    M --> N[Repeat Weekly Until Chart Withdrawn]
```

## 4.4 SOLAS Mandatory Publications Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SOLAS[SOLAS Chapter V Regulation 19.2.1] --> M1[Nautical Charts]
    SOLAS --> M2[Sailing Directions]
    SOLAS --> M3[Light Lists]
    SOLAS --> M4[Tide Tables]
    SOLAS --> M5[Tidal Stream Atlases]
    SOLAS --> M6[Notices to Mariners]
    SOLAS --> M7[IHO Chart 5011 Symbols]
    SOLAS --> M8[Annual Summary of Notices]
    M1 --> M1A[Carriage and Updating Required]
    M6 --> M6A[Weekly Update Mandatory]
    M1A --> AUDIT[PSC and Flag State Audits]
    M6A --> AUDIT
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"Define a nautical chart and state the three properties that no map projection can preserve simultaneously."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 Marks):** A nautical chart is a special-purpose map of a maritime area, showing depths of water, navigational hazards, aids to navigation, and coastal features essential for safe navigation.
- **Three properties (1.5 Marks):** *Area, Shape, Distance,* and *Direction* — no projection can preserve all four simultaneously. Mercator preserves angle (shape and direction) but distorts area; Gnomonic preserves great-circle distance but distorts shape.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many candidates list only three properties (area, shape, distance) and omit **direction**, which is critical for Mercator charts. The exact four are *area, shape, distance, and direction*. Memorise all four.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"What is the difference between a Mercator and a Gnomonic projection? Mention one practical application of each."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Mercator (1.5 Marks):** A conformal cylindrical projection where a rhumb line (loxodrome) appears as a straight line. **Application:** Coastal navigation, plotting rhumb-line courses.
- **Gnomonic (1.5 Marks):** An azimuthal projection centred on a point of tangency where a great circle appears as a straight line. **Application:** Planning ocean passages, plotting great-circle courses.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]

**"With the aid of a labelled sketch, explain the principle of the Mercator projection. Derive the equation for meridional parts on the WGS-84 ellipsoid. A vessel steams from position $\phi_1 = 25^\circ$ N, $\lambda_1 = 060^\circ$ W to position $\phi_2 = 22^\circ$ N, $\lambda_2 = 055^\circ$ W along a rhumb line. Calculate the course and distance."**

#### Part (a) — Mercator Projection Principle and Derivation (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

1. **Principle (2 Marks):** Mercator projection is a conformal cylindrical projection where meridians and parallels intersect at right angles. The expansion of the East-West scale with latitude exactly compensates for the convergence of meridians, making a rhumb line appear as a straight line.

2. **Conformal Condition (2 Marks):** On a sphere, scale in longitude direction is $R \cos(\phi)$, while scale in latitude direction is $R$. For conformality, the projected y-coordinate must satisfy:

   $$\frac{dy}{R\, d\phi} = \frac{R \cdot d\lambda / (R \cos\phi)}{dx / (R d\lambda)} \quad \text{(simplifies to)} \quad dy = R \sec(\phi)\, d\phi$$

3. **Derivation of Meridional Parts (3 Marks):** Integrating from equator to latitude $\phi$:

   $$y = R \int_0^\phi \sec(\phi')\, d\phi' = R \cdot \ln\!\left[\tan\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)\right]$$

   Expressing in minutes of arc (Meridional Parts) on WGS-84:

   $$\text{MP}(\phi) = 7915.7045 \cdot \ln\!\left[\tan\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)\cdot \left(\frac{1-e\sin\phi}{1+e\sin\phi}\right)^{e/2}\right]$$

   where $e = 0.0818191908$ is the WGS-84 eccentricity.

#### Part (b) — Course and Distance Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Differences (1 Mark):**
$\Delta\phi = 25^\circ - 22^\circ = 3^\circ$ (so $180$ NM)
$\Delta\lambda = 60^\circ - 55^\circ = 5^\circ$ (so $300'$)

**Step 2 — Meridional Parts Lookup (1 Mark):**
$\text{MP}(25^\circ) \approx 1547.0$
$\text{MP}(22^\circ) \approx 1356.0$

**Step 3 — Difference in MP (1 Mark):**
$\Delta\text{MP} = 1547.0 - 1356.0 = 191.0$

**Step 4 — Course Calculation (2 Marks):**
$\tan(\theta) = \frac{\Delta\lambda'}{\Delta\text{MP}} = \frac{300}{191.0} = 1.5707$

$\theta = \arctan(1.5707) = 57.5^\circ$ (i.e., bearing of approximately $180^\circ + 57.5^\circ = 237.5^\circ$ T, since moving southward)

**Step 5 — Distance Calculation (2 Marks):**
$D = \frac{\Delta\phi \cdot 60}{\cos(\theta)} = \frac{180}{\cos(57.5^\circ)} = \frac{180}{0.5373} \approx 335.0$ NM

**Answer:** Course = $237.5^\circ$ T (or S $57.5^\circ$ W); Distance = $335$ NM.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b)**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Using 60 NM per degree of longitude — this is only true at the equator. For rhumb-line calculation, always use meridional parts.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to convert $\Delta\lambda$ from degrees to minutes (multiply by 60) before dividing by $\Delta\text{MP}$.
> - **Mark Distribution:** Each calculation step (1, 2, 3, 4, 5) carries 1 to 2 marks as shown. A clear tabular layout in the answer sheet yields higher marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]

**"Explain the various types of nautical publications required to be carried on board a vessel under SOLAS Chapter V Regulation 19. With a neat diagram, describe the procedure for chart correction using Admiralty Notices to Mariners. Mention the significance of keeping the charts up-to-date."**

#### Part (a) — SOLAS Mandatory Publications (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

1. **SOLAS Chapter V Reg 19.2.1 (1 Mark):** Every vessel must carry nautical charts and publications adequate for the intended voyage.

2. **The Five Mandatory Publications (4 Marks — 0.8 each):**
   - **Nautical Charts** — Mercator and Gnomonic charts of intended route, kept corrected.
   - **Sailing Directions (Pilots)** — Coastal and port descriptions, regulations, hazards.
   - **Light Lists (NP 83/84/85)** — All lights, buoys, beacons, radio aids, geographic coordinates.
   - **Tide Tables (ATT)** — Hourly and high/low water predictions for primary and secondary ports.
   - **Notices to Mariners (NtM)** — Weekly bulletins and cumulative T&P file.

3. **Auxiliary Publications (2 Marks):**
   - Tidal Stream Atlases
   - IHO Chart 5011 (Symbols and Abbreviations)
   - Annual Summary of Admiralty Notices to Mariners
   - Radio Signals volumes (NP 281-286)
   - Distance Tables and Sight Reduction Tables

#### Part (b) — Chart Correction Procedure and Importance (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

1. **Weekly Procedure (3 Marks):**
   - Receive weekly *Admiralty Notices to Mariners* (NtM).
   - Identify charts affected by chart number.
   - For each affected chart, read the full block (preliminary, permanent, or temporary).
   - **Block (a) corrections:** Hand-draw with red ink.
   - **Block (b) corrections:** Cut out pre-printed patches (T&P — Text and Paste) and glue onto chart.
   - Record in the chart's **Correction Record Card** with date, notice number, and signature.
   - For **temporary (T) and preliminary (P)** notices, attach a flag/pencil mark to ensure the latest information is consulted.

2. **Cumulative T&P File (2 Marks):**
   - Maintain a chronological file of all T&P corrections, kept on the bridge.
   - Updates to the T&P file are issued as new block corrections are received.
   - When a chart is reprinted, the cumulative T&P file is incorporated.

3. **Significance of Currency (2 Marks):**
   - **Legal:** Stale charts constitute a maritime offence; PSC detention is a real risk.
   - **Safety:** Newly discovered wrecks, changed buoyage, and uncharted reefs can cause groundings.
   - **Operational:** Insurance, charter-party disputes, and incident investigations depend on chart currency.
   - **Compliance:** Master and Navigating Officer are legally accountable under flag state law.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b)**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Students often confuse **T&P** (Text and Paste) with **temporary/preliminary** notices. T&P is a physical correction method using pre-printed paper patches; T and P are notice categories (Temporary, Preliminary).
> - **Pitfall 2:** Failing to mention the **Master's legal responsibility** leads to loss of 1 mark.
> - **Pitfall 3:** Omitting the importance of the **Correction Record Card** — examiners allocate marks for naming the official record-keeping tool.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Chartwork and Publications**

- [ ] A **nautical chart** is a special-purpose maritime map; a **nautical publication** is a textual/numerical supplement.
- [ ] The **four properties** of map projections are **area, shape, distance, and direction** — no projection preserves all four.
- [ ] **Mercator projection** preserves **angle (direction)**; **rhumb line is straight**; **scale expands with latitude**.
- [ ] **Gnomonic projection** preserves **great-circle distance**; **great circle is straight**; **rhumb line is curved**.
- [ ] **1 Nautical Mile = 1852 metres = 1 minute of latitude** (definition).
- [ ] **1 Knot = 1 NM/hour = 0.5144 m/s**.
- [ ] **Earth's WGS-84 equatorial radius = 6,378,137.0 m**; eccentricity $e = 0.0818191908$.
- [ ] **Meridional Parts (MP)** formula: $7915.7045 \cdot \ln\!\left[\tan(45^\circ + \phi/2) \cdot \left(\frac{1-e\sin\phi}{1+e\sin\phi}\right)^{e/2}\right]$.
- [ ] **Dip of horizon** $D_h = 1.76\sqrt{h_m}$ NM; **Light range** $D_L = 1.17\sqrt{H_m}$ NM; **Geographic range** $D_G = D_h + D_L$.
- [ ] **SOLAS Chapter V Reg 19.2.1** mandates carriage of: **charts, Sailing Directions, Light Lists, Tide Tables, NtM file, Tidal Stream Atlases**.
- [ ] **Master** is legally responsible for chart currency.
- [ ] **NtM** is issued weekly; corrections are applied via **hand-drawing (Block a)** or **T&P patches (Block b)**.
- [ ] **T and P notices** are Temporary and Preliminary respectively; must be **flagged prominently** on charts.
- [ ] **Chart correction record card** must be maintained with date, notice number, and signature.
- [ ] **Squat** and **UKC** calculations are critical in shallow water — never ignore.
- [ ] **Position fixing** uses **cross-bearings** (≥2 objects, ≥30° apart), **running fix**, **GPS fix**.
- [ ] **Course to Steer (CTS)** = True course + Variation + Deviation ± Lee-way.
- [ ] **IHO Chart 5011** is the universal symbol reference.
- [ ] **IHO chart numbering** uses 1xx-7xx by ocean region; 8xx for special-purpose.
- [ ] **Rhumb line distance** $D = \frac{\Delta\phi \cdot 60}{\cos\theta}$ NM.
- [ ] **Great circle distance** uses $\cos(D/R) = \sin\phi_1\sin\phi_2 + \cos\phi_1\cos\phi_2\cos(\Delta\lambda)$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (COLREGs)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (COLREGs)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **COLREGs (International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972)** are a set of **38 internationally binding rules** adopted by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** on **20 October 1972** and entered into force on **15 July 1977**. They constitute the universal "rules of the road" for vessels on all waters — **internal waters, territorial seas, and the high seas**.

These regulations govern the conduct of vessels, the navigation lights, shapes, and sound/light signals that must be displayed in restricted visibility and clear weather, and prescribe the responsibilities of masters, officers, and pilots in avoiding collisions.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Statutory Reference:** COLREGs are formally titled the *International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972*, often abbreviated as **72 COLREGS** or **COLREGS 1972**. They supersede the earlier 1960 Rules.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two cars approaching an intersection with **no traffic lights, no signs, and no road markings** — but the drivers come from different countries and speak different languages. Without an agreed-upon rulebook, chaos ensues. **COLREGs is exactly that rulebook for the world's oceans**, where ships of every flag, language, and size share the same watery "road."

- **Think of Rule 5 (Lookout)** as keeping your eyes on the road.
- **Think of Rule 6 (Safe Speed)** as obeying the speed limit.
- **Think of Rules 13–18 (Sailing Rules)** as the right-of-way protocol at intersections.
- **Think of Rules 20–28 (Lights & Shapes)** as the car headlights and brake lights that tell others what you are doing.

In short: **COLREGs translate the universal need for safe, predictable conduct into binding maritime law.**

### 1.3 Scope and Applicability

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rule 1 – Application:** The Rules apply to **all vessels upon the high seas and all waters connected therewith navigable by seagoing vessels**. They do **not** relieve any vessel, owner, master, or crew of consequences arising from negligence (e.g., customary law, statutory regulations, or special rules imposed by a flag State).

### 1.4 Hierarchy of Authority

According to **Rule 2 – Responsibility**:

$$R_{\text{total}} = R_{\text{COLREGs}} + R_{\text{negligence-defense}} \neq \text{wave off} \cdot R_{\text{duty}}$$

> [!WARNING]  
> **No exemption clause exists.** Neither the size, purpose, nor exceptional circumstances of a vessel relieve it of complying with COLREGs. A warship is *also* bound, though its enforcement against sovereign vessels is by diplomatic channels.

### 1.5 The Five-Part Structure (High-Yield Architecture)

The 38 rules are organized into **five distinct parts**, which form the backbone of every KTU exam question on this topic:

| Part | Rules Covered | Subject Matter |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| **Part A – General** | Rules 1–3 | Application, Responsibility, General Definitions |
| **Part B – Steering & Sailing Rules** | Rules 4–10 | Conduct of vessels in any visibility (lookout, safe speed, TSS, narrow channels) |
| **Part C – Lights & Shapes** | Rules 20–31 | Equipment requirements day and night |
| **Part D – Sound & Light Signals** | Rules 32–37 | Maneuvering and warning signals |
| **Part E – Exemptions** | Rules 38 | Distress signals (Rule 38 is technically the concluding rule) |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Memory Aid for Students:** "**G**eneral, **S**teering, **L**ights, **S**ignals, **E**xemptions" — **GSLSE**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Analytical Breakdown of Each Major Rule

#### **Part A – General Rules**

**Rule 3 — General Definitions (high-yield for definitions)**

This rule defines key terms used throughout the regulations:
- **Vessel**: Any watercraft including non-displacement craft and seaplanes.
- **Power-driven vessel**: A vessel propelled by machinery.
- **Sailing vessel**: A vessel under sail only (machinery off).
- **Vessel engaged in fishing**: A vessel catching fish by nets/lines/trawls, *not* a sport fisher.
- **Vessel not under command (NUC)**: Unable to maneuver due to exceptional circumstances.
- **Vessel restricted in her ability to maneuver (RAM)**: Restricted by work — cable laying, dredging, towing, refueling, etc.
- **Vessel constrained by her draught**: A power-driven vessel severely restricted by draft in a narrow channel.
- **Underway**: Not at anchor, made fast to shore, or aground.
- **Masthead light**: A **white** light, 225° arc, fixed at fore-and-aft centerline.
- **Sidelights**: **Green** (starboard, 112.5°) and **Red** (port, 112.5°).
- **Sternlight**: A **white** light visible over an arc of **135°**.

#### **Part B – Steering and Sailing Rules**

**Rule 4 — Application (Any Visibility):** Rules 4–10 apply in **all visibility conditions**.

**Rule 5 — Lookout:** Every vessel must maintain a proper lookout at **all times** — by sight, hearing, radar, and any other means.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Stop your ship, not your eyes.** A proper lookout is a continuous duty — it cannot be delegated entirely to a single instrument.

**Rule 6 — Safe Speed:** Every vessel must proceed at a safe speed so that:
1. Proper and effective action can be taken to avoid collision.
2. The vessel can be stopped within a distance appropriate to prevailing conditions.

$$S_{\text{safe}} = f(V_{\text{rel}}, D_{\text{radar}}, T_{\text{stop}}, \rho_{\text{traffic}}, C_{\text{bg}})$$

The safe speed is a function of:
- Visibility conditions (fog, rain, darkness)
- Traffic density (including fishing vessels, concentrations)
- Vessel's **manoeuvrability** (stopping distance, turning ability)
- Background lights at night (shore lights confusing visibility)
- Draft in relation to available water depth
- Radar range, accuracy, and limitations (e.g., sea clutter)

**Rule 7 — Risk of Collision:**

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The risk of collision **shall be deemed to exist** if the compass bearing of an approaching vessel **does not appreciably change**. This is the standard **"steady-bearing, decreasing-range"** principle.

**Rule 8 — Action to Avoid Collision:**

> [!NOTE]  
> Any action to avoid collision must be:
> 1. **Positive** — made in ample time.
> 2. **Unambiguous** — alterations of course and/or speed must be large enough to be readily apparent.
> 3. **In accordance with good seamanship** — not a succession of small alterations.

**Rule 9 — Narrow Channels:**
- A vessel proceeding along the **starboard side** of a narrow channel or fairway.
- A vessel of **less than 20 m** or a **sailing vessel** shall *not* impede the passage of a vessel that can safely navigate only within the narrow channel.
- A vessel engaged in fishing or crossing a channel shall not impede transit.

**Rule 10 — Traffic Separation Schemes (TSS):**
- Vessels must use the appropriate traffic lane, follow the general direction of traffic flow.
- Cross traffic lanes **at right angles** when feasible.
- A vessel engaged in fishing, NUC, RAM, or constrained by draught is exempt from lane compliance.

#### **Part B Section II — Conduct of Vessels in Sight of One Another (Rules 11–18)**

**Rule 13 — Overtaking:** The overtaking vessel keeps clear. A vessel is deemed overtaking when coming up with another from a direction **more than 22.5° abaft her beam**.

$$\theta_{\text{overtake}} \geq 22.5^\circ \text{ abaft the beam}$$

**Rule 14 — Head-on Situation:** Vessels meeting end-on or nearly end-on (within ~6°) alter course to **starboard**, passing port-to-port.

$$\theta_{\text{head-on}} \leq 6^\circ \text{ from reciprocal heading}$$

**Rule 15 — Crossing Situation:** When two power-driven vessels are crossing, the vessel which has the other on her **starboard side** is the **give-way vessel** and must keep out of the way.

**Rule 16 — Action by Give-way Vessel:** Keep well clear, cross ahead, or slow down/stop.

**Rule 17 — Action by Stand-on Vessel:** Maintain course and speed. May take action to avoid collision if:
1. The give-way vessel is not taking appropriate action.
2. The stand-on vessel determines the give-way vessel is too close.

**Rule 18 — Responsibilities Between Vessels (Priority Hierarchy):**

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Hierarchy (highest priority first):**
> 1. Vessel not under command (NUC)
> 2. Vessel restricted in ability to maneuver (RAM)
> 3. Vessel engaged in fishing
> 4. Sailing vessel
> 5. Power-driven vessel
> 6. Seaplane on water

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Rule | Key Equation / Threshold | Unit / Value | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|------|------------------------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Lookout duty | R5 | Continuous (24/7) | N/A | Avoidance of negligence claims |
| 2 | Safe speed factor | R6 | $S_{\text{safe}} = f(\text{multi-param})$ | Knots (kn) | Radar plotting, CPA calc |
| 3 | Risk of collision | R7 | $\Delta\phi_{\text{bearing}} \approx 0$ | Degrees (°) | Steady-bearing principle |
| 4 | Avoidance action | R8 | $\Delta\text{Course} \geq \text{large/unambiguous}$ | Degrees (°) | Good seamanship |
| 5 | Narrow channel side | R9 | Starboard side convention | N/A | Channel transit |
| 6 | TSS lane flow | R10 | $90^\circ$ crossing | Degrees (°) | TSS compliance |
| 7 | Overtaking angle | R13 | $\theta \geq 22.5^\circ$ abaft beam | Degrees (°) | Determines stand-on/give-way |
| 8 | Head-on arc | R14 | $\theta \leq 6^\circ$ from reciprocal | Degrees (°) | Starboard alteration |
| 9 | Crossing (starboard) | R15 | Other vessel on starboard | N/A | Give-way trigger |
| 10 | Stand-on action | R17 | Maintain course/speed; may maneuver | N/A | Collision avoidance |
| 11 | Hierarchy | R18 | NUC > RAM > Fishing > Sail > Power | N/A | Priority ranking |
| 12 | Masthead arc | R3, R21 | $\phi = 225^\circ$ | Degrees (°) | Light configuration |
| 13 | Sidelight arc | R3, R21 | $\phi_{\text{port}} = \phi_{\text{stbd}} = 112.5^\circ$ | Degrees (°) | Light configuration |
| 14 | Sternlight arc | R3, R21 | $\phi = 135^\circ$ | Degrees (°) | Light configuration |
| 15 | Visibility ranges (lights) | R22–R28 | Depends on vessel length (m) | Nautical miles (nm) | Visibility spec |
| 16 | Maneuvering signals | R34 | 1 short = stbd; 2 short = port | Sound units | Whistle signals |
| 17 | Distress signals | R37, R38 | Continuous or repeated 5+ times | N/A | Mayday protocol |
| 18 | Speed estimation (Restricted Visibility, R19) | R19 | $S_{\text{moderate}}$; stop if heard forward | Knots (kn) | Fog procedures |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Critical Constant for KTU Numerical:** The arcs of navigation lights always sum to **360°** per side of a vessel — masthead (225°) + sternlight (135°) = 360° per side. Similarly, port sidelight (112.5°) + starboard sidelight (112.5°) = 225° combined visible at the bow.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Naval Architecture:** Determining navigation-light mounting heights and arc positions for new vessels.
2. **Marine Insurance:** COLREGs compliance is central to collision liability claims and apportionment of fault.
3. **Radar/ECDIS Design:** Auto-detection systems rely on Rule 5 (lookout) and Rule 6 (safe speed) algorithms.
4. **Port State Control:** Inspectors verify compliance with Parts C and D (lights, shapes, signals).
5. **Bridge Resource Management (BRM):** STCW-mandated training uses Rule 8 (action to avoid) as the keystone of decision-making.
6. **AIS Integration:** Modern VHF-AIS systems encode vessel type (NUC, RAM, etc.) consistent with Rule 18 hierarchy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of Navigation Light Arc Geometry

The standard 360° visibility requirement around a vessel is partitioned based on the vessel's centerline.

**Step 1: Establish the reference frame.**
Take the vessel's heading (forward direction) as the 0° reference. The horizon around the vessel is a full circle of **360°**.

**Step 2: Define the forward arc.**
A masthead light must be visible to vessels approaching from ahead and from the sides, but not from astern. By international convention, this arc is fixed at:

$$\phi_{\text{masthead}} = 225^\circ$$

This arc extends **22.5° abaft each beam** symmetrically about the vessel's heading.

**Step 3: Define the stern arc.**
The sternlight covers the remaining arc astern:

$$\phi_{\text{stern}} = 360^\circ - 225^\circ = 135^\circ$$

**Step 4: Define the sidelights.**
The green (starboard) and red (port) sidelights each cover an arc of:

$$\phi_{\text{sidelight}} = 112.5^\circ$$

These arcs meet at the bow (fore-and-aft line):

$$\phi_{\text{stbd}} + \phi_{\text{port}} = 225^\circ \text{ (visible from forward of the beam)}$$

The remaining 135° (each) is covered by the masthead light forward and the sternlight aft.

**Step 5: Verification — total forward arc from the bow.**

$$\phi_{\text{visible, fwd}} = \phi_{\text{stbd}} + \phi_{\text{port}} = 112.5^\circ + 112.5^\circ = 225^\circ$$

This **matches the masthead arc** exactly — a designed symmetry to ensure consistent visibility from any forward bearing.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Engineering Insight:** This geometry ensures that at *any* aspect angle, a vessel displays at least one navigation light visible to another vessel, providing unambiguous information about heading and aspect.

### 3.2 Decision Matrix — Determining Responsibility in a Crossing Situation

When two vessels are in sight of one another and not in an overtaking or head-on scenario, apply the following logical decision tree:

| Step | Question | Rule Reference | Outcome |
|------|----------|---------------|---------|
| 1 | Is one of the vessels NUC, RAM, fishing, or sailing? | R18 | If yes, that vessel has priority and the other keeps clear. |
| 2 | Are both power-driven vessels? | R15 | Proceed to step 3. |
| 3 | Which vessel has the other on its **starboard** side? | R15 | That vessel is the **give-way vessel** (KAV — Keep Out of the Way). |
| 4 | Has the give-way vessel taken **positive, early action**? | R16 | If yes, proceed. If no, go to step 5. |
| 5 | Has the stand-on vessel noticed the give-way is not acting appropriately? | R17 | The stand-on vessel may maneuver to avoid collision. |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rule 17 Exception (the "Stand-on Failsafe"):**  
> If the give-way vessel fails to act, the stand-on vessel is **not** absolved of responsibility. It must act to avoid collision itself. This is a critical point frequently tested.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Visibility Conditions vs. Applicable Rules

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Rules Applied} &= R_{\text{any-visibility}} \cup R_{\text{sight-of-one-another}} \cup R_{\text{restricted-visibility}}
\end{aligned}$$

| Visibility Condition | Rules Applied | Special Emphasis |
|---------------------|---------------|------------------|
| **Clear weather, in sight** | R4–R10 + R11–R18 (all of Part B) | Right-of-way rules fully active |
| **Clear weather, not in sight** | R4–R10 only | No lights or shapes required for navigation per se |
| **Restricted visibility (fog)** | R4–R10 + R19 | Proceed at **safe speed**; navigate by sound signals |
| **Any visibility, all conditions** | R5 + R6 + R7 + R8 (overarching) | Continuous lookout, safe speed, risk assessment, action |

### 3.4 Worked Analytical Example — Rule 7 Risk of Collision

A vessel A observes vessel B on radar. The bearing of B from A is measured every minute.

**Given:**
- t = 0 min: Bearing = 045.0°
- t = 1 min: Bearing = 045.2°
- t = 2 min: Bearing = 045.1°
- t = 3 min: Bearing = 045.3°
- Range decreasing from 8.0 nm to 6.5 nm over the same period.

**Step 1:** Calculate the bearing change:

$$\Delta\phi = \phi_{\text{max}} - \phi_{\text{min}} = 045.3^\circ - 045.0^\circ = 0.3^\circ$$

**Step 2:** Apply Rule 7 criterion:

$$0.3^\circ \ll 5^\circ \text{ (threshold of "appreciable change")}$$

**Step 3:** Conclusion:

$$\Delta\phi \approx 0 \implies \text{Risk of collision exists}$$

**Step 4:** Action per Rule 8 — alter course by at least 30° (typically) to make the bearing change **appreciably**:

$$\Delta C \geq 30^\circ \text{ in the direction that increases CPA}$$

> [!WARNING]  
> A common KTU pitfall: students often state "risk of collision exists" without quantifying the bearing change. **Always show the calculation.**

### 3.5 Visibility Range Table (Common KTU Question)

For a power-driven vessel of length $\geq 50$ m:

| Light | Visibility Range |
|-------|------------------|
| Masthead light | 6 nm |
| Sidelights | 3 nm |
| Sternlight | 3 nm |
| Towing light | 3 nm |
| All-round white light (e.g., NUC) | 3 nm |

For a power-driven vessel of length 12 m but < 50 m:

| Light | Visibility Range |
|-------|------------------|
| Masthead light | 5 nm (or 3 nm if vessel < 12 m) |
| Sidelights | 2 nm |
| Sternlight | 2 nm |

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Masthead Range} &= \begin{cases} 6 \text{ nm}, & L \geq 50 \text{ m} \\ 5 \text{ nm}, & 12 \leq L < 50 \text{ m} \\ 3 \text{ nm}, & L < 12 \text{ m} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Master Architecture of COLREGs (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    blockA["PART A: GENERAL RULES (1-3)"]
    blockB["PART B: STEERING AND SAILING RULES (4-19)"]
    blockC["PART C: LIGHTS AND SHAPES (20-31)"]
    blockD["PART D: SOUND AND LIGHT SIGNALS (32-37)"]
    blockE["PART E: EXEMPTIONS AND DISTRESS (38)"]

    blockA --> blockA1["Rule 1: Application"]
    blockA --> blockA2["Rule 2: Responsibility"]
    blockA --> blockA3["Rule 3: General Definitions"]

    blockB --> blockB1["Section I: Any Visibility (4-10)"]
    blockB --> blockB2["Section II: Sight of One Another (11-18)"]
    blockB --> blockB3["Section III: Restricted Visibility (19)"]

    blockB1 --> blockB1a["Rule 5: Lookout"]
    blockB1 --> blockB1b["Rule 6: Safe Speed"]
    blockB1 --> blockB1c["Rule 7: Risk of Collision"]
    blockB1 --> blockB1d["Rule 8: Action to Avoid"]
    blockB1 --> blockB1e["Rule 9: Narrow Channels"]
    blockB1 --> blockB1f["Rule 10: TSS"]

    blockB2 --> blockB2a["Rule 13: Overtaking"]
    blockB2 --> blockB2b["Rule 14: Head-On"]
    blockB2 --> blockB2c["Rule 15: Crossing"]
    blockB2 --> blockB2d["Rule 16: Give-Way Action"]
    blockB2 --> blockB2e["Rule 17: Stand-On Action"]
    blockB2 --> blockB2f["Rule 18: Hierarchy"]

    blockC --> blockC1["Lights by Vessel Type"]
    blockC --> blockC2["Day Shapes"]

    blockD --> blockD1["Rule 34: Maneuvering Signals"]
    blockD --> blockD2["Rule 35: Sound in Restricted Visibility"]
    blockD --> blockD3["Rule 37: Distress Signals"]

    blockE --> blockE1["Rule 38: Distress Signals Summary"]
```

### 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Identifying Responsibility in a Crossing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Two Vessels in Sight of One Another"]
    nodeQ1{"Is one vessel NUC, RAM, fishing, or sailing?"}
    nodeYes1["Apply Rule 18: Priority vessel keeps course/speed"]
    nodeNo1["Both power-driven"]
    nodeQ2{"Vessel A has Vessel B on starboard side?"}
    nodeYes2["Vessel A is GIVE-WAY (Rule 15)"]
    nodeNo2["Vessel B is GIVE-WAY"]
    nodeQ3{"Has give-way vessel acted positively?"}
    nodeYes3["Stand-on vessel maintains course/speed"]
    nodeNo3["Stand-on vessel MUST maneuver (Rule 17)"]
    nodeAction["Avoid collision by clear, early, unambiguous action (Rule 8)"]

    nodeStart --> nodeQ1
    nodeQ1 -- YES --> nodeYes1
    nodeQ1 -- NO --> nodeNo1
    nodeNo1 --> nodeQ2
    nodeQ2 -- YES --> nodeYes2
    nodeQ2 -- NO --> nodeNo2
    nodeYes2 --> nodeQ3
    nodeNo2 --> nodeQ3
    nodeQ3 -- YES --> nodeYes3
    nodeQ3 -- NO --> nodeNo3
    nodeYes1 --> nodeAction
    nodeYes3 --> nodeAction
    nodeNo3 --> nodeAction
```

### 4.3 Navigation Light Arc Geometry (Schematic Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeBow["BOW 0 DEG"]
    nodeStbd22["STARBOARD BEAM 90 DEG"]
    nodeStern["STERN 180 DEG"]
    nodePort22["PORT BEAM 270 DEG"]

    nodeBow -- "MASTHEAD 225 DEG ARC" --> nodeStern
    nodeStern -- "STERNLIGHT 135 DEG ARC" --> nodeBow
    nodeBow -- "RED PORT LIGHT 112.5 DEG" --> nodePort22
    nodeBow -- "GREEN STARBOARD LIGHT 112.5 DEG" --> nodeStbd22
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Sound and Light Signaling System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeSys["COLREGS SIGNALING SUBSYSTEM"]
    nodeWhistle["MANEUVERING SIGNALS - RULE 34"]
    nodeFog["RESTRICTED VISIBILITY SIGNALS - RULE 35"]
    nodeWarn["WARNING SIGNALS - RULE 36"]
    nodeDist["DISTRESS SIGNALS - RULE 37 AND 38"]

    nodeWhistle --> nodeW1["1 SHORT BLAST: Alter Starboard"]
    nodeWhistle --> nodeW2["2 SHORT BLASTS: Alter Port"]
    nodeWhistle --> nodeW3["3 SHORT BLASTS: Astern Propulsion"]
    nodeWhistle --> nodeW4["5 SHORT BLASTS: Uncertain of Intent"]

    nodeFog --> nodeF1["Power-Driven Making Way: 1 Prolonged Every 2 Min"]
    nodeFog --> nodeF2["Power-Driven Stopped: 2 Prolonged Every 2 Min"]
    nodeFog --> nodeF3["Sailing or NUC: 1 Prolonged and 2 Short Every 2 Min"]

    nodeWarn --> nodeWa1["Light Signal: At Least 5 Short Flashes Every Minute"]
    nodeWarn --> nodeWa2["Sound Signal: 5 Short Blasts"]

    nodeDist --> nodeD1["Continuous Sound: Aircraft or Ship Horn"]
    nodeDist --> nodeD2["Intermittent: Any Signal Repeated Frequently"]
    nodeDist --> nodeD3["Visual: Flames, Rockets, Smoke, Dye Marker"]
```
