## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**  
**State the cardinal responsibilities under Rules 5 and 6 of COLREGs. Why are these rules considered "overarching" duties?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Rule 5 (Lookout):** Every vessel shall at all times maintain a proper lookout by sight, hearing, radar, and all other available means so as to make a full appraisal of the situation and of the risk of collision.

**Rule 6 (Safe Speed):** Every vessel shall at all times proceed at a safe speed so that proper and effective action can be taken to avoid collision, and the vessel can be stopped within a distance appropriate to the prevailing circumstances and conditions.

**Why overarching:** These rules apply in **all conditions of visibility** — clear, restricted, day, or night. They are the foundational duties that support all subsequent navigation and collision-avoidance rules (1.5 Marks for each rule, 1 Mark for the explanation).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**  
**Define the following terms under Rule 3 of COLREGs: (i) Vessel Not Under Command (NUC), (ii) Vessel Restricted in Her Ability to Maneuver (RAM), (iii) Vessel Engaged in Fishing.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**(i) Vessel Not Under Command (NUC):** A vessel which through some exceptional circumstance is unable to maneuver as required by these Rules and is therefore unable to keep out of the way of another vessel (1 Mark).

**(ii) Vessel Restricted in Her Ability to Maneuver (RAM):** A vessel restricted in her ability to maneuver when engaged in an operation which severely limits her maneuverability — e.g., cable laying, dredging, replenishment at sea, launching of aircraft (1 Mark).

**(iii) Vessel Engaged in Fishing:** Any vessel fishing with nets, lines, trawls, or other fishing apparatus which restricts maneuverability — but does not include a vessel fishing with trolling lines or other apparatus which do not restrict maneuverability (1 Mark).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 MARKS**  
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **"Hierarchy of Priority"** as defined under **Rule 18** of the International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea. Draw a clear distinction between power-driven vessels, sailing vessels, fishing vessels, RAM, and NUC vessels in terms of priority. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two power-driven vessels are crossing in open sea in clear weather. Vessel A is sighted from Vessel B at 3 nautical miles on B's starboard bow. Vessel A's course is steady. Describe step-by-step the actions required of both vessels under Rules 5, 7, 8, 15, 16, and 17 of COLREGs. **(7 Marks)**

---

**SOLUTION TO QUESTION A:**

#### Part (a) — Rule 18 Hierarchy of Priority (7 Marks)

Rule 18 establishes a **fixed hierarchy of priority** among vessels in sight of one another. The vessel with the **higher** priority in the hierarchy has the right of way, and the vessel lower in the priority must keep out of its way.

**Priority Order (highest to lowest):**

| Priority Rank | Vessel Type | Key Indicator | Required Action |
|---------------|-------------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 (Highest) | Vessel Not Under Command (NUC) | Two all-round red lights at night; two black balls day | Other vessels keep clear |
| 2 | Vessel Restricted in Ability to Maneuver (RAM) | White-red-white vertical lights; ball-diamond-ball shapes | Other vessels keep clear |
| 3 | Vessel Engaged in Fishing | Green-white vertical lights; two cones | Other vessels keep clear |
| 4 | Sailing Vessel | Side lights + sternlight; sail up | Power vessels keep clear |
| 5 | Power-Driven Vessel | Masthead + side + stern | Standard right-of-way rules |
| 6 (Lowest) | Seaplane on Water | As power-driven vessel | Similar to power-driven |

**Distinctions:**
- A **power-driven vessel** is propelled by machinery (1 Mark).
- A **sailing vessel** is under sail only; if machinery is being used, it is treated as power-driven (1 Mark).
- A **fishing vessel** is one whose gear (nets, lines, trawls) restricts maneuverability; a sport fisher is not "engaged in fishing" (1 Mark).
- **RAM** is restricted by *work* (dredging, towing, cable-laying); NUC is unable due to *exceptional circumstances* (breakdown, steering failure) (1 Mark).
- A vessel constrained by her **draught** may exhibit special cylindrical day shape (1 Mark).
- A **seaplane** is treated as a power-driven vessel (1 Mark).
- Concluding summary statement (1 Mark).

---

#### Part (b) — Crossing Situation Decision Walkthrough (7 Marks)

**Given Scenario:** Vessel A is on the starboard bow of Vessel B, 3 nm range, clear weather, in sight.

**Step 1 — Apply Rule 5 (Lookout) — 1 Mark:**
Both vessels must maintain continuous lookout. Vessel B has sighted Vessel A at 3 nm.

**Step 2 — Apply Rule 7 (Risk of Collision) — 1 Mark:**
Continuous observation of Vessel A's bearing is required. If the bearing is **steady** (does not appreciably change) while range decreases, risk of collision exists.

**Step 3 — Apply Rule 15 (Crossing Situation) — 1 Mark:**
Since Vessel A is on Vessel B's **starboard** side, **Vessel B is the give-way vessel** (KAV).

**Step 4 — Apply Rule 16 (Action by Give-Way Vessel) — 1.5 Marks:**
Vessel B must **keep out of the way** of Vessel A. B should:
- Alter course to starboard (passing astern of A), or
- Reduce speed, or
- A combination of both.

The action must be **early, positive, and unambiguous** (Rule 8).

**Step 5 — Apply Rule 17 (Action by Stand-on Vessel) — 1 Mark:**
Vessel A (the stand-on vessel) must **maintain course and speed** initially.

**Step 6 — Rule 17 Stand-on Failsafe — 1 Mark:**
If Vessel B fails to take appropriate action and the range closes dangerously, **Vessel A must maneuver** to avoid collision (typically by turning to starboard or reducing speed).

**Step 7 — Rule 8 Compliance — 0.5 Marks:**
All alterations must be large enough to be readily apparent to the other vessel — small, sequential alterations are prohibited.

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 MARKS (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)**  
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **navigation lights** that must be exhibited by a power-driven vessel of length 50 m or more underway at night. Mention the arc of visibility, color, and minimum range of each light as per Rules 20, 21, and 22 of COLREGs. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A vessel is proceeding along a narrow channel when it meets a tanker constrained by her draught proceeding in the opposite direction. State the responsibilities under Rule 9 and Rule 18. Also describe the sound signals that would be exchanged under Rule 34. **(7 Marks)**

---

**SOLUTION TO QUESTION B:**

#### Part (a) — Navigation Lights for Power-Driven Vessel ≥ 50 m (7 Marks)

Per **Rule 21** (Lights for power-driven vessels underway) and **Rule 22** (Visibility of lights):

| Light | Color | Arc | Minimum Visibility Range |
|-------|-------|-----|--------------------------|
| **Masthead light** (fore) | White | 225° | **6 nm** (Rule 22) |
| **Masthead light** (aft, second, if ≥ 50 m) | White | 225° | 6 nm |
| **Port sidelight** | Red | 112.5° | 3 nm |
| **Starboard sidelight** | Green | 112.5° | 3 nm |
| **Sternlight** | White | 135° | 3 nm |

**Mark Breakdown:**
- Listing all 5 lights with color (2 Marks)
- Correct arcs (2 Marks)
- Correct visibility ranges (2 Marks)
- Reference to Rule 22 for visibility (1 Mark)

> [!NOTE]  
> **Important:** A vessel ≥ 50 m must exhibit a **second masthead light** abaft of and higher than the forward one. The combined arcs ensure visibility over 360°.

---

#### Part (b) — Narrow Channel & Sound Signals (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Apply Rule 9 (Narrow Channels) — 2 Marks:**
- A vessel proceeding along the narrow channel must keep to the **starboard side**.
- An overtaking vessel may signal for passing using the appropriate whistle signal.
- A vessel engaged in fishing or crossing the channel shall **not impede** the transit of a vessel safely navigating in the channel.

**Step 2 — Apply Rule 18 (Priority) — 2 Marks:**
- A vessel **constrained by her draught** has special status; a power-driven vessel navigating in a narrow channel should give way to such a vessel when crossing.
- However, under **Rule 9**, both vessels must facilitate safe passage in the narrow channel.

**Step 3 — Sound Signals under Rule 34 — 3 Marks:**

| Signal | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| One short blast | "I am altering my course to starboard" |
| Two short blasts | "I am altering my course to port" |
| Three short blasts | "I am operating astern propulsion" |
| Four short blasts | (Reserved — not standard COLREGs) |
| Five short blasts | "I am uncertain of your intentions" — a warning signal |

In a narrow channel, vessels meeting head-on may exchange:
- **One short blast** = "I am keeping to starboard" (signaling starboard-side passage).
- **Two short blasts** = agreement on passing arrangement.

When overtaking in a narrow channel, the overtaking vessel typically signals:
- **Two prolonged blasts followed by one short blast** = "I intend to overtake on your starboard side."
- **Two prolonged blasts followed by two short blasts** = "I intend to overtake on your port side."

The vessel being overtaken responds with the same signal as an agreement.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often confuse Rule 13 (Overtaking) angle of 22.5° with a Rule 14 (Head-on) angle of 6°. These are different rules with different thresholds — misremembering costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting that the stand-on vessel (Rule 17) must act when the give-way vessel fails — Rule 17 is **not** "maintain course at all costs." It is a *failsafe* obligation.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing Rule 18 hierarchy — RAM vs. Fishing: RAM has **higher priority** than fishing. The order is NUC → RAM → Fishing → Sailing → Power.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing navigation-light arcs. **Masthead = 225°**, **Sternlight = 135°**, **Sidelights = 112.5° each**. Memorize these as the most-tested triplet.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Forgetting that COLREGs apply to **all** waters navigable by seagoing vessels, not just the high seas — Rule 1(a) clarifies this.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ **COLREGs = 38 rules**; **adopted 20 Oct 1972**, **in force 15 July 1977**; administered by **IMO**.
- ☐ Structure: **5 Parts (A-E)** — General, Steering/Sailing, Lights/Shapes, Sound/Light Signals, Distress.
- ☐ **Rule 1 — Application:** All waters navigable by seagoing vessels.
- ☐ **Rule 2 — Responsibility:** No exemption for negligence; COLREGs do not replace other statutory duties.
- ☐ **Rule 3 — Definitions:** NUC, RAM, Fishing, Sailing, Power-driven, Constrained-by-draught, Underway, etc.
- ☐ **Rule 5 — Lookout:** Always — sight, hearing, radar, all means.
- ☐ **Rule 6 — Safe Speed:** A function of visibility, traffic, manoeuvrability, background lights, draft, radar accuracy.
- ☐ **Rule 7 — Risk of Collision:** Steady bearing + decreasing range = risk exists.
- ☐ **Rule 8 — Action to Avoid:** Positive, early, unambiguous, in accordance with good seamanship.
- ☐ **Rule 9 — Narrow Channels:** Starboard side convention; RAM and constrained vessels get priority.
- ☐ **Rule 10 — TSS:** Follow traffic flow; cross at right angles; cross at right angles when entering/exiting.
- ☐ **Rule 13 — Overtaking:** From ≥ **22.5° abaft the beam**; overtaking vessel keeps clear.
- ☐ **Rule 14 — Head-On:** Within **~6°** of reciprocal heading; alter to **starboard**, pass port-to-port.
- ☐ **Rule 15 — Crossing:** Vessel with other on **starboard side** is the **give-way** vessel.
- ☐ **Rule 16 — Give-Way Action:** Keep well clear; cross ahead; slow/stop if necessary.
- ☐ **Rule 17 — Stand-On Action:** Maintain course/speed; **may maneuver** if give-way fails (failsafe).
- ☐ **Rule 18 — Priority Hierarchy:** **NUC > RAM > Fishing > Sail > Power > Seaplane**.
- ☐ **Rule 19 — Restricted Visibility:** Safe speed; navigate by sound; stop if heard forward of beam.
- ☐ **Navigation Light Arcs:** Masthead **225°**, Sternlight **135°**, Sidelights **112.5° each** (sum 360°).
- ☐ **Light Visibility Ranges (≥ 50 m vessel):** Masthead **6 nm**; sidelights/sternlight **3 nm**.
- ☐ **Sound Signals:** 1 short = stbd; 2 short = port; 3 short = astern; 5 short = warning.
- ☐ **Distress Signals (Rule 37/38):** Continuous horn, repeated rockets, flares, dye marker, square flag with ball above.
- ☐ **Mnemonic for Hierarchy:** "**N**ever **R**efuse **F**ishing **S**upport **P**lease" → **NUC, RAM, Fishing, Sailing, Power**.
- ☐ **Mnemonic for Crossing:** "**R**ed on **R**ight — **K**eep **A**ll **V**isible" → vessel with the other on starboard is KAV (Keep out of the way).

---

**END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — TOPIC: INTERNATIONAL REGULATIONS FOR PREVENTING COLLISIONS AT SEA (COLREGS)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maneuvering Characteristics of Ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Maneuvering Characteristics of Ships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Maneuvering Characteristics of Ships

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Ship Maneuverability** is defined as the ability of a vessel to alter its course and speed in response to helm (rudder) and engine commands, while maintaining the desired trajectory safely under prevailing environmental conditions (wind, wave, current, and shallow water effects). It is fundamentally governed by the interaction between the **hydrodynamic forces** acting on the hull, the **rudder force**, and the **propulsion–resistance balance**.

According to the **IMO Resolution MSC.137(76)** — *Standards for Ship Maneuverability* — a vessel must demonstrate predictable and repeatable turning, course-keeping, and stopping behavior. The two governing non-dimensional indices used by IMO are:

- **Course-keeping index** $K = \dfrac{\text{Initial turning radius}}{\text{Steady turning radius}}$
- **Yaw-checking index** $Y = \dfrac{(v'_1 + v'_2) / 2}{v'_0}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-3 Anchor Statement**
> Maneuvering characteristics describe **how a ship turns, stops, and stays on course** — quantified through standardized geometric parameters (advance, transfer, tactical diameter, track reach) and stability criteria (course-keeping & yaw-checking indices).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Car in a Parking Lot

Imagine driving a **rear-wheel-drive car with the steering wheel only at the back** (a long trailer with rear steering). The ship's rudder behaves exactly like that:

- The hull is a **very long, very heavy object** (~200 m long, ~50,000 tonnes).
- The pivot point of rotation is **NOT at the bow or stern**, but somewhere around **one-quarter to one-third of the ship's length from the bow** — this is the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** or **pivot point**.
- When you turn the rudder, the ship does **NOT pivot around its own center** — it swings like a slow pendulum, with the **bow** moving inward but the **stern swinging outward** in a wide arc.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the stern swing outward?**
> Because the pivot point is forward of midships, when the rudder deflects water, the **bow goes one way and the stern kicks the opposite way**. This is the same physics as oversteer in a car — but at 300,000 tonnes, the consequences (e.g., striking a quay during berthing) are catastrophic.

### 1.3 Key Maneuvering Parameters at a Glance

| Notation | Parameter | Typical Magnitude (VLCC) |
|---|---|---|
| $L_{OA}$ | Length overall | **~330 m** |
| $D_T$ | Tactical diameter | **5–6 ship lengths** |
| $A_d$ | Advance | **~3–4 ship lengths** |
| $T_r$ | Transfer | **~2–3 ship lengths** |
| $V_{90}$ | Speed at 90° heading change | **~50 % of initial** |
| $t_{90}$ | Time to alter course 90° | **~60–90 s** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All geometric maneuverability dimensions are expressed as **multiples of the ship's length between perpendiculars ($L_{pp}$)** to allow comparison across vessels of different sizes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Standard Turning Circle (Turning Trial)

The **turning circle** is the path described by the **Center of Gravity (CG)** of a ship when a **35° rudder angle** is applied at a **steady speed** (typically 90 % of maximum continuous rating). IMO MSC.137(76) mandates this trial on **deep, calm, open water** with no wind, wave, or current.

### 2.2 The Five Canonical Maneuvering Distances

1. **Advance ($A_d$)** — Distance traveled **along the original course** from the point of putting the rudder over to the point where the ship has turned through **90°**.
2. **Transfer ($T_r$)** — Distance traveled **perpendicular to the original course** from the original baseline to the ship's CG at the 90° heading change.
3. **Tactical Diameter ($D_T$)** — Distance traveled **perpendicular to the original course** at the **180° heading change** point. IMO requires $D_T \le 5\,L_{pp}$.
4. **Track Reach ($T_R$)** — Distance traveled along the **original course** from the point of putting the rudder over to the **180° heading change**.
5. **Final Diameter ($D_F$)** — The steady-state turning circle diameter after the ship has settled into its constant yaw-rate regime.

### 2.3 Speed Reduction During the Turn

A ship **loses speed** during a turn because:
- The **rudder deflects the slipstream**, generating less thrust along the ship's longitudinal axis.
- The **transverse drift angle** ($\beta$) increases the angle of attack of the hull to the water, raising resistance.
- Typically, speed at 90° ($V_{90}$) drops to **40–60 %** of straight-line speed, and steady turning speed drops to **~50 %**.

### 2.4 Course-Keeping and Yaw-Checking (IMO Standard)

After a turn, when the rudder is returned to amidships, the ship should:
- **Not overshoot** the new heading by more than a defined amount → quantified by **Course-keeping index $K$**.
- **Not yaw back and forth excessively** → quantified by **Yaw-checking index $Y$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Yaw** is the oscillatory side-to-side rotation of a ship about its vertical axis. A ship with poor yaw-checking oscillates like a fish's tail — dangerous in congested waters.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{D_T}{L_{pp}} \le 5$ | IMO tactical diameter limit | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\dfrac{A_d}{L_{pp}} \le 4.5$ | IMO advance limit | dimensionless |
| 3 | $K = \dfrac{R_0}{R_\infty}$ | Course-keeping index (IMO: $K \ge 1$) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $Y = \dfrac{v'_1 + v'_2}{2\,v'_0}$ | Yaw-checking index (IMO: $Y \ge 1$) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $T = \dfrac{L_{pp}}{V_{ship}}$ | Characteristic time scale | s |
| 6 | $\beta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{v}{u}\right)$ | Drift angle (sideslip) | rad or deg |
| 7 | $\omega = \dfrac{V_0}{R_\infty}$ | Yaw rate at steady turning | rad/s |
| 8 | $F_r = \dfrac{V_0}{\sqrt{g\,L_{pp}}}$ | Froude number (maneuvering regime $<0.30$) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $D_{stop} = f(V_0^2, m, R_T, F_{brake})$ | Stopping distance | m |
| 10 | $P_{rudder} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A_R\,V^2\,F_{rud}$ | Rudder side force | N |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility of these equations:**
> These formulas are used daily by:
> - **Harbour pilots** during berthing/unberthing,
> - **VTS operators** (Vessel Traffic Service) for collision-avoidance geometry,
> - **Naval architects** during sea-trial acceptance,
> - **Ship designers** validating CFD/hydrodynamic codes against IMO standards.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Drift-Angle Relation (Sideslip Geometry)

Consider a ship moving with **forward speed $u$** (longitudinal) and **lateral speed $v$** (transverse) in the body-fixed frame. The **sideslip angle (drift angle) $\beta$** is the angle between the **centerline** and the **velocity vector**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{lateral velocity component}}{\text{longitudinal velocity component}}\right) \\[6pt]
\beta &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{v}{u}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** By definition of the tangent in a right triangle where the opposite side is the lateral velocity $v$ and the adjacent side is the longitudinal velocity $u$, the angle is the inverse-tangent of their ratio.

For small drift angles ($\beta < 10°$), $\tan\beta \approx \beta$, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &\approx \dfrac{v}{u}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Yaw-Rate / Steady-Turning-Radius Relation

At **steady turning**, the ship moves in a circle of constant radius $R_\infty$ with constant yaw rate $\omega$ and constant speed $V_0$. The centripetal acceleration must be balanced by the **net transverse hydrodynamic force**.

The arc-length velocity equals the ship speed, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_0 &= \omega \cdot R_\infty
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for the yaw rate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \dfrac{V_0}{R_\infty}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** A particle moving in a circle of radius $R_\infty$ at constant angular velocity $\omega$ has tangential speed equal to the product $\omega \cdot R_\infty$. Setting this equal to the ship's total speed $V_0$ gives the steady-state yaw rate.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Course-Keeping Index $K$

Let $R_0$ be the **initial turning radius** measured 60 s after the rudder is returned to amidships after a 10°/10° zig-zag maneuver, and $R_\infty$ be the **steady turning radius** measured before the counter-rudder was applied.

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \dfrac{R_0}{R_\infty}
\end{aligned}
$$

IMO requirement: $K \ge 1$ (initial radius must be **no smaller** than steady radius — i.e., no tight looping).

## 3.4 Derivation of the Yaw-Checking Index $Y$

In the **10°/10° zig-zag maneuver**, after the rudder is reversed when the ship has reached 10° overshoot, measure:
- $v'_1$ = first overshoot angle on the **opposite side**.
- $v'_2$ = second overshoot angle on the **same original side** (after the next 10° reversal).
- $v'_0$ = applied rudder angle (10°).

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= \dfrac{(v'_1 + v'_2)/2}{v'_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

IMO requirement: $Y \ge 1$ — meaning the average overshoot must be **at least equal to** the applied rudder deflection, ensuring the rudder has enough authority to control the swing.

## 3.5 Derivation of Stopping Distance from Newton's Second Law

A ship of mass $m$ moving at velocity $V_0$ decelerates under a net retarding force $F_{net}$ (sum of hydrodynamic drag, rudder-induced drag, and brake/thrust-reversal force). Newton's second law:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m\,\dfrac{dV}{dt} &= -F_{net}(V)
\end{aligned}
$$

For simplicity, assume $F_{net}$ is approximately proportional to $V^2$ (drag-dominated regime). Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m\,\dfrac{dV}{dt} &= -k\,V^2
\end{aligned}
$$

where $k$ is a drag coefficient including frontal area, drag coefficient, and water density.

Separating variables and integrating from $V_0$ to $0$ gives the **time to stop**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{V_0}^{0} \dfrac{dV}{V^2} &= -\dfrac{k}{m}\int_{0}^{t_s} dt \\[6pt]
-\dfrac{1}{V}\Big\vert_{V_0}^{0} &= -\dfrac{k}{m}\,t_s \\[6pt]
\dfrac{1}{V_0} &= \dfrac{k\,t_s}{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $t_s$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_s &= \dfrac{m}{k\,V_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Integrating both sides with the limits on velocity (from $V_0$ to $0$) and time (from $0$ to $t_s$) yields the relationship. The mass decelerates faster if $V_0$ is large because the high-$V$ drag dominates.

The **stopping distance** is obtained by integrating velocity over time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{stop} &= \int_{0}^{t_s} V(t)\,dt
\end{aligned}
$$

With $V(t) = \dfrac{V_0}{1 + (k\,V_0\,t/m)}$, and after substitution $x = k\,V_0\,t/m$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{stop} &= \dfrac{m}{k}\int_{0}^{1} \dfrac{dx}{x+1}\cdot\dfrac{1}{1} \quad\text{(after change of variables)} \\[6pt]
d_{stop} &= \dfrac{m}{k}\,\ln(2)
\end{aligned}
$$

For a typical VLCC with $m \approx 3\times10^8$ kg, $k \approx 5\times10^5$ kg/m at full astern crash stop:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{stop} &\approx \dfrac{3\times10^8}{5\times10^5}\cdot(0.693) \approx 415\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the empirical rule of thumb: **stopping distance ≈ 8–10 ship lengths for a crash stop**.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Geometric Relationships

| Maneuvering Phase | Dominant Forces | Key Non-Dimensional Number |
|---|---|---|
| Straight running | Resistance vs. thrust | Froude number $F_r$ |
| Rudder application | Rudder lift + hull sway | Drift angle $\beta$ |
| Steady turning | Centripetal balance | Yaw rate $\omega$ |
| Course transition | Rudder recovery | Course-keeping $K$ |
| Yaw oscillation | Damping vs. inertia | Yaw-checking $Y$ |
| Crash stop | Reverse thrust + drag | Deceleration $\dot V$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Turning Circle Geometry — Mermaid Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Ship on straight course at V0]) --> RudderApply[Rudder put to 35 deg port]
    RudderApply --> BowSwing[Bow swings to port]
    RudderApply --> SternSwing[Stern kicks to starboard]
    BowSwing --> Hdg90[Heading change reaches 90 deg]
    Hdg90 --> PtAdv[Advance Ad measured here along original course]
    Hdg90 --> PtTr[Transfer Tr measured here perpendicular to original course]
    Hdg90 --> ContinueTurn[Continue turning past 90 deg]
    ContinueTurn --> Hdg180[Heading change reaches 180 deg]
    Hdg180 --> PtTact[Tactical Diameter DT measured here]
    Hdg180 --> PtReach[Track Reach TR measured along original course]
    Hdg180 --> Steady[Steady turning circle reached]
    Steady --> DF[Final Diameter DF]
    DF --> Stop([Steady turning continues until counter-rudder])
```

## 4.2 IMO Maneuverability Test Sequence — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1 ["Phase 1 Turning Ability"]
        A1[Straight run at 90 percent MCR] --> A2[Rudder 35 deg]
        A2 --> A3[Measure Ad Tr DT TR]
    end
    subgraph S2 ["Phase 2 Course Keeping"]
        B1[Steady turn] --> B2[Rudder to amidships]
        B2 --> B3[Measure initial radius R0 at 60 s]
        B3 --> B4[Compute Course keeping Index K]
    end
    subgraph S3 ["Phase 3 Yaw Checking"]
        C1[10 deg by 10 deg zig zag] --> C2[Measure first overshoot v1]
        C2 --> C3[Measure second overshoot v2]
        C3 --> C4[Compute Yaw checking Index Y]
    end
    subgraph S4 ["Phase 4 Stopping"]
        D1[Full ahead steady] --> D2[Full astern crash stop]
        D2 --> D3[Measure track reach and stopping distance]
    end
    A3 --> B1
    B4 --> C1
    C4 --> D1
```

## 4.3 Force-Balance During a Steady Turn — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BodyForces ["Forces on Hull"]
        F1[Rudder Side Force F_R] --> Net
        F2[Hull Lift Force F_H] --> Net
        F3[Resistive Drag F_D] --> Net
        F4[Centripetal Reaction F_C] --> Net
    end
    Net[Net Transverse Force = 0 at Steady Turn]
    subgraph Moments ["Moments about CG"]
        M1[Rudder Moment M_R]
        M2[Hull Sway Moment M_H]
        M3[Couple from Drift Angle M_B]
    end
    M1 --> MomBal[Sum of Moments = I times yaw rate dot]
    M2 --> MomBal
    M3 --> MomBal
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above map the **interaction between hydrodynamic forces and moments** during ship maneuvering. They serve as the engineering basis for both **CFD validation** and **real-time maneuvering simulation** used in onboard training simulators (per STCW 2010 Manila Amendments).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
Define *Tactical Diameter* and *Track Reach*. State the IMO limit for tactical diameter in terms of ship length.

**Model Answer:**
The **Tactical Diameter ($D_T$)** is the distance, measured perpendicular to the original course, traveled by the ship's CG between the moment of putting the rudder hard over and the moment the ship has altered its heading by **180°**. The **Track Reach ($T_R$)** is the corresponding distance measured **along the original course** from the point of rudder application to the 180° heading change point.

According to **IMO MSC.137(76)**, the tactical diameter must satisfy:
$$\dfrac{D_T}{L_{pp}} \le 5$$
For a vessel of length $L_{pp}=200$ m, this gives $D_T \le 1000$ m.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
What is *drift angle*? Why does it develop during a turn?

**Model Answer:**
The **drift angle ($\beta$)** is the angle between the **ship's centerline** and the **direction of motion of the CG** through the water. It is given by:
$$\beta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{v}{u}\right)$$
It develops during a turn because the **centripetal acceleration** required for circular motion requires a **lateral velocity component** $v$. Since the hull is symmetric fore-aft but the water flow on the outside of the turn is faster than on the inside, an asymmetric pressure field builds up, generating a **sideslip** that the hull must compensate through **rudder action**.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the aid of a labeled turning-circle diagram, explain **Advance, Transfer, Tactical Diameter, and Track Reach**. State the IMO acceptance limits for each in terms of $L_{pp}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A VLCC with $L_{pp}=320$ m is steaming at 15 knots on course 090°. The Master orders **35° starboard rudder**. After the turn is completed, the ship is on course 270°.
- (i) Calculate the maximum permissible tactical diameter per IMO standards.
- (ii) If the tactical diameter is measured to be **1.55 km**, determine whether the ship complies with IMO standards.
- (iii) If the rudder angle were reduced to 20°, explain qualitatively how $D_T$, $A_d$, $T_r$, and $V_{90}$ would change. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Turning Circle Diagram Explanation** — As described in §2.2 above, with reference to the Mermaid diagram in §4.1.
- IMO limits: $D_T/L_{pp} \le 5$; $A_d/L_{pp} \le 4.5$; recommended $T_r/L_{pp} \le 2.5$.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i)** Maximum tactical diameter:
$$\begin{aligned}
D_T^{max} &= 5 \times L_{pp} \\
&= 5 \times 320 \\
&= 1600\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii)** Given $D_T = 1550\ \text{m} = 1.55\ \text{km}$:
$$\begin{aligned}
D_T / L_{pp} &= 1550 / 320 = 4.84 \le 5
\end{aligned}$$
**Result:** ✅ The ship **complies** with IMO MSC.137(76) (margin of 50 m = 16 % safety margin).

**(iii) Qualitative Effect of Reducing Rudder to 20°:**
- $D_T$ → **increases** (larger turning radius).
- $A_d$ → **increases** (ship travels further along original course before turning 90°).
- $T_r$ → **decreases slightly** then **increases** (less perpendicular deflection per unit advance).
- $V_{90}$ → **closer to $V_0$** (less rudder-induced drag, so speed loss is reduced).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **course-keeping index $K$** and **yaw-checking index $Y$** as defined by IMO. Describe the **10°/10° zig-zag maneuver** used to measure $Y$, and the IMO acceptance criterion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A container ship with $L_{pp}=200$ m completes a 10°/10° zig-zag test. The measured first overshoot is $v'_1 = 12°$ and second overshoot is $v'_2 = 11°$. The rudder was applied at $v'_0 = 10°$.
- (i) Calculate the yaw-checking index $Y$.
- (ii) Does the ship comply with IMO standards?
- (iii) The vessel's course-keeping index is measured as $K = 1.20$. Comment on this value. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theory:** As described in §3.3 and §3.4 above. The 10°/10° zig-zag maneuver involves alternating 10° port and starboard rudder applied every time the heading deviates by 10° from the original course. The overshoots $v'_1$ and $v'_2$ are measured after the first and second rudder reversals.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i)** Compute $Y$:
$$\begin{aligned}
Y &= \dfrac{(v'_1 + v'_2)/2}{v'_0} \\
&= \dfrac{(12 + 11)/2}{10} \\
&= \dfrac{11.5}{10} \\
&= 1.15
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii)** IMO criterion: $Y \ge 1$. Here $Y = 1.15 \ge 1$. ✅ **The ship complies** with yaw-checking standards.

**(iii)** Course-keeping index $K = 1.20$. IMO criterion: $K \ge 1$.
$$\begin{aligned}
K = 1.20 \ge 1.00 \quad ✅
\end{aligned}$$
The ship **complies** with course-keeping standards. The value 1.20 indicates that the **initial turning radius is 20 % larger** than the steady turning radius — a healthy margin indicating good directional stability with no tendency to "loop tight" immediately after counter-rudder.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Points**
> 1. **Mixing up Advance and Transfer**: Many students swap $A_d$ and $T_r$. Remember: $A_d$ is **along** the original course, $T_r$ is **perpendicular** to it. (–1 mark each instance)
> 2. **Forgetting the $L_{pp}$ unit**: When stating IMO limits, ALWAYS express the ratios ($D_T/L_{pp}$, etc.) as **dimensionless multiples of $L_{pp}$**, not in absolute meters. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Confusing Tactical Diameter with Final Diameter**: $D_T$ is at the **180° turn point**; $D_F$ is the **steady-state** (typically reached after several ship lengths of further turning). (–1 mark)
> 4. **Skipping the compliance comparison**: Whenever IMO numbers are given, ALWAYS state the **comparison operator** and a clear **Yes/No** verdict. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Zig-zag overshoot signs**: $v'_1$ and $v'_2$ are **absolute angles** on opposite sides of the baseline; do not assign opposite signs. (–1 mark)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Maneuverability = turnability + course-keeping + yaw-checking + stopping**.
- **Five key distances** of the turning circle: $A_d$, $T_r$, $D_T$, $T_R$, $D_F$ — all measured from the **CG**, not the bow.
- **IMO MSC.137(76)** mandates: $D_T/L_{pp}\le 5$, $A_d/L_{pp}\le 4.5$, $K\ge 1$, $Y\ge 1$, and documented **crash-stop trial**.
- **Drift angle** $\beta$ arises because the CG must trace a circular path while the hull is symmetric — the rudder and asymmetric pressure field supply the lateral force.
- **Speed loss in a turn** is 40–60 % for hard-over rudder; less for smaller rudder angles.
- **Pivot point** lies at **~1/4 to 1/3 $L_{pp}$ from the bow** for a fully loaded ship — this is why the **stern kicks outward** during berthing.
- **10°/10° zig-zag** is the **standard test** for yaw-checking; **35°/35° turn** is the standard for tactical diameter.
- **Crash stop** typically requires **8–10 ship lengths** of track reach at full astern from full ahead.
- **Course-keeping and yaw-checking** are the two **dynamic stability** criteria for directional control — they prevent the ship from becoming "unstable in yaw".
- **Froude number** in maneuvering tests is kept **$<0.30$** to remain in the low-speed, displacement regime.
- Always **label diagrams** with all five parameters ($A_d$, $T_r$, $D_T$, $T_R$, $D_F$) — a complete diagram fetches **full marks** in KTU Part-B questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Collision Avoidance Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Collision Avoidance Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Collision Avoidance Techniques** in maritime navigation refer to the systematic set of international rules, helm maneuvers, signaling procedures, and electronic decision-making protocols employed by vessels to prevent physical contact with other ships, floating objects, or fixed navigational hazards while underway, at anchor, or in restricted waters.

The globally accepted legal and operational framework is the **International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (COLREGs)**, commonly called the **"Rules of the Road at Sea."** These were adopted by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** in **1972** and entered into force on **July 15, 1977**. They are formally known as **IMO Resolution A.464(XII)** and have been amended multiple times (most recently with amendments adopted in 2021).

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — COLREGs (RPNGE 72 / International Rules of the Road):**
> A codified set of 38 Rules (originally 37 in 1972, expanded to 38 with Rule 39 added in 1981; some texts count 41 rules when Annexes are split), divided into **five parts** (A–E) and **four annexes**, governing the conduct of all vessels on the high seas and in connected navigable waters.

### The Five Parts of COLREGs

| Part | Title | Rules Covered | Purpose |
|------|-------|---------------|---------|
| **A** | General | Rules 1–3 | Scope, responsibility, general definitions |
| **B** | Steering and Sailing Rules | Rules 4–19 | Conduct of vessels in any condition of visibility |
| **C** | Lights and Shapes | Rules 20–31 | Required navigation lights for day/night signaling |
| **D** | Sound and Light Signals | Rules 32–37 | Maneuvering, warning, and distress signals |
| **E** | Exemptions | Rule 38 | Historical/temporary exemption for vessels built before rules |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sea Highway" Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Intuition (For first-time learners):**
> Imagine the ocean as a vast, unmarked, foggy **highway without lane markings, traffic lights, or pavements**. Every vehicle (ship) is shaped differently, moves at different speeds, has no brakes, and cannot stop in less than a mile. **COLREGs is the universally agreed "driving code"** that all mariners must follow — just like highway driving rules, but far more strictly enforced by international maritime law.

To make this analogy sharper:
- **"Right of way"** at sea = **"Stand-on vessel"** (the vessel that must *maintain* course and speed).
- **"Yielding"** at sea = **"Give-way vessel"** (the vessel that must alter course, slow down, or stop).
- **"Headlights at night"** = **Navigation lights** (port-red, starboard-green, stern-white, masthead-white).
- **"Car horn"** = **Ship's whistle signals** (one short blast = altering to starboard).
- **"Roundabout etiquette"** = **Crossing / overtaking / head-on encounter rules**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Foundational Legal Principles** (from Rule 2 — Responsibility):
> 1. **Nothing in these Rules exonerates any vessel, or the owner, master, or crew thereof, from the consequences of any neglect** to comply with the Rules or of any neglect of any precaution which may be required by the ordinary practice of seamen or by the special circumstances of the case.
> 2. In construing and complying with the Rules, **due regard shall be had to all dangers of navigation and collision** and to any special circumstances which may render a departure from the Rules necessary to avoid immediate danger.
> 3. (Rule 18 hierarchy) — when vessels of *different priorities* meet, the lower-priority vessel must give way; the higher-priority vessel maintains course and speed.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Maritime Metrics

The following universal maritime units and reference values are essential for collision avoidance calculations:

- **Nautical Mile (NM):** $\mathbf{1\ \text{NM} = 1852\ \text{meters}}$ (exactly, by international agreement since 1929).
- **Knot (kn):** $\mathbf{1\ \text{kn} = 1\ \text{NM/hour}}$.
- **Standard Sound Signal Whistle Range:** $\mathbf{\geq 0.5\ \text{NM}}$ for vessels $\geq 12$ m; $\mathbf{\geq 2\ \text{NM}}$ for vessels $\geq 100$ m.
- **Restricted Visibility Definition:** Visibility $\mathbf{\leq 1\ \text{NM}}$ (Rule 3(k)).
- **Standard Vessel Turn Diameter:** Approximately $\mathbf{5 \times \text{LWL}}$ (Length at Waterline) for a 90° turn at full rudder.
- **COLREGs "Safe Distance" Crossing Rule 15:** Cross at **not less than a wide angle** (typically interpreted as $\geq \mathbf{70^\circ}$ from the other vessel's course line).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Vessel Encounters (Rule 11–18)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Canonical Encounter Types** (must be memorized for KTU 2024 ESE):

### 2.1.1 Head-On Situation (Rule 14)

- **Definition:** Two vessels meeting on **reciprocal or nearly reciprocal courses**, such that the **compass bearing of one from the other does not appreciably change** (i.e., the relative bearing remains close to $000^\circ$ or $180^\circ$).
- **Interpretation Threshold:** Relative bearing is **dead ahead (within $\pm 6^\circ$)** when sighted *mug-to-mug* (both masthead lights visible).
- **Required Action:** **Each vessel must alter course to STARBOARD** so that each passes on the PORT side of the other.
- **Sound Signal:** **Two short blasts** on the whistle before altering course.

### 2.1.2 Crossing Situation (Rule 15)

- **Definition:** Vessels on courses that **cross**, where the vessel having the other on her **OWN STARBOARD side** is the *give-way* vessel.
- **Action:** The vessel which has the other on her **starboard** must **keep out of the way** (give way). The other vessel (with target on her port) is the *stand-on* vessel.
- **Crossing Distance:** Must cross at **not less than a wide angle** — i.e., the course line of the give-way vessel should approach the other at $\geq 70^\circ$ to minimize CPA.

### 2.1.3 Overtaking Situation (Rule 13)

- **Definition:** A vessel approaching another from **more than 22.5° abaft her beam** (i.e., relative bearing is in the sector from $112.5^\circ$ to $247.5^\circ$ relative to the overtaking vessel's heading).
- **At Night Diagnostic:** Only the **stern light** (white) but neither the side light (red/green) of the overtaken vessel is visible.
- **Action:** The overtaking vessel **must keep out of the way**. The overtaken vessel maintains course and speed.
- **Note:** Overtaking rules apply **regardless of which side** the overtaking occurs.

---

## 2.2 Hierarchy of Priority (Rule 18 — Responsibilities Between Vessels)

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule 18 — Order of Priority (Highest Priority First):**
> 1. **Vessel not under command (NUC)**
> 2. **Vessel restricted in her ability to maneuver (RAM)**
> 3. **Vessel constrained by her draft (CBD)**
> 4. **Vessel engaged in fishing** (with nets/lines/trawls — NOT trolling lines)
> 5. **Sailing vessel**
> 6. **Vessel propelled by machinery** (power-driven vessel) — *lowest priority*

Within the same priority class, additional sub-rules apply (e.g., between two sailing vessels, the one with the wind on the **port side** keeps clear; if both have wind on the same side, the **windward** vessel keeps clear).

---

## 2.3 Restricted Visibility (Rule 19)

> [!WARNING]
> **Rule 19** applies whenever visibility is **$\leq 1$ NM** (fog, heavy rain, snow, smoke). In such conditions:
> - **The "stand-on / give-way" framework is SUSPENDED.**
> - **Every vessel must proceed at a SAFE SPEED** (Rule 6) so she can take proper and effective action to avoid collision.
> - Vessels with **operational radar** must use it at **proper scale**, with **radar plotting or equivalent systematic observation**.
> - **Power-driven vessels** hearing a fog signal apparently forward of the beam must **reduce to bare steerageway** and, if necessary, **stop** (Rule 19(c)).
> - **Sound signal in fog:** Power-driven vessel underway makes **ONE prolonged blast ($\geq 4$ seconds) every $\leq 2$ minutes**.

---

## 2.4 Sound and Light Signals (Rules 32–37)

### Maneuvering & Warning Signals (Rule 34) — Day/Night

| Signal | Meaning | When Used |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| **1 short blast** | "I am altering my course to **STARBOARD**" | Day/Night |
| **2 short blasts** | "I am altering my course to **PORT**" | Day/Night |
| **3 short blasts** | "I am operating **astern propulsion**" | Day/Night |
| **5 short blasts** | Danger/doubt signal (unclear intentions) | Day/Night |
| **2 long + 1 short** | "I **agree** to be overtaken" (overtaken vessel) | Day/Night |
| **1 long, 1 short, 1 long, 1 short** (Morse "K") | "I wish to **communicate** with you" | Day/Night |

### Restricted Visibility (Fog) Signals (Rule 35)

| Vessel Type | Signal | Interval |
|-------------|--------|----------|
| Power-driven underway | **1 prolonged blast (≥ 4 s)** | Every $\leq 2$ min |
| Power-driven stopped/restricted | **2 prolonged blasts** | Every $\leq 2$ min |
| Sailing vessel | **1 prolonged + 2 short** | Every $\leq 2$ min |
| Vessel at anchor (≥ 100 m) | Rapid ringing of bell for 5 s | Every 1 min |
| Vessel at anchor (also) | **3 strokes** of bell before & after rapid ringing | At front + 3 min later at rear |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and align with KTU 2024 ESE pattern problems. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\text{abs}(x)$ to denote absolute value (NOT the literal pipe character inside table cells).

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Standard Units | Notes / Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Speed conversion** | $v_{kn} = \dfrac{v_{m/s}}{0.5144}$ | knots (kn) | $1\ \text{kn} = 0.5144\ \text{m/s}$ exactly |
| 2 | **Distance conversion** | $D_{NM} = \dfrac{D_{m}}{1852}$ | nautical miles | $1\ \text{NM} = 1852\ \text{m}$ |
| 3 | **True Bearing from Own to Target** | $BRG_t = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta E}{\Delta N}\right)$ corrected to quadrant | degrees (°) | $\Delta N$ = North displacement; $\Delta E$ = East displacement |
| 4 | **Relative Bearing** | $RB = (BRG_t - SH + 360^\circ) \bmod 360^\circ$ | degrees | $SH$ = own ship's heading; $0^\circ$ = dead ahead |
| 5 | **True Course of Target** | $TC_t = (RB_{\text{new}} - RB_{\text{old}} + SH_{\text{new}} - SH_{\text{old}} + 360^\circ) \bmod 360^\circ$ | degrees | Derived from multiple bearing observations |
| 6 | **Distance to Target** | $D = \sqrt{\Delta N^2 + \Delta E^2}$ | meters (or NM) | Pitagorean distance |
| 7 | **Closest Point of Approach (CPA)** | $CPA = \dfrac{\text{Relative Range} \times \sin(\phi)}{\sin(\theta_R)}$ | NM | $\phi$ = angle between target course and line of sight |
| 8 | **Time to CPA (TCPA)** | $TCPA = \dfrac{\text{Relative Range} \times \cos(\phi)}{V_{rel}}$ | hours | $V_{rel}$ = relative speed of target |
| 9 | **Relative Speed** | $V_{rel} = \sqrt{V_o^2 + V_t^2 - 2 V_o V_t \cos(\alpha)}$ | knots | $\alpha$ = angle between own course and target course |
| 10 | **Course Alteration Vector Sum** | $V_R = \vec{V}_o - \vec{V}_t$ | knots (vector) | Vector difference = Relative Motion |
| 11 | **Lead Distance (Parallel Index)** | $L = D_{\text{track-perp}} \times \tan(\theta_{\text{index}})$ | NM | $L$ = along-track offset for parallel-index steering |
| 12 | **Stopping Distance (Rule 6 Safe Speed)** | $d_{stop} = \dfrac{V^2}{2a}$ | meters | $a$ = deceleration in m/s² |
| 13 | **Turning Circle Diameter (rule of thumb)** | $D_t \approx 5 \times L_{WL}$ | meters | Approximation for planning maneuvers |
| 14 | **Beam Bearing Change (per min)** | $\text{BBR/min} = \dfrac{180^\circ \times V_{rel}\times 60}{R \times \pi}$ | °/min | When BBR/min $\geq 6°$ → closing |

---

## 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in actual ship operations:**
> 1. **Legal Compliance:** Violation of COLREGs is a **criminal offense** under most maritime flag states (e.g., UK MSA 1894 §419; US 33 USC §2032). A master can lose the certificate of competency and face imprisonment.
> 2. **Insurance:** Underwriters (P&I Clubs) require COLREGs compliance for **cargo and hull claims**; non-compliance voids coverage.
> 3. **ECDIS/ARPA Integration:** Modern Integrated Bridge Systems (IBS) overlay CPA/TCPA data from **ARPA (Automatic Radar Plotting Aid)** and **AIS (Automatic Identification System)** to warn the watchkeeper.
> 4. **VTS (Vessel Traffic Services):** Major ports operate radar-based traffic separation schemes that *enforce* Rule 10 (Traffic Separation Schemes).
> 5. **STCW Convention:** Every officer-in-charge of a navigational watch (OICNW) must demonstrate Rule 18 hierarchy and CPA/TCPA computation at operational level (STCW 2010 Manila Amendments, Table A-II/1).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Closest Point of Approach (CPA) and Time to CPA (TCPA)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context (typical KTU 2024 ESE Part-B style):**
> *Own Ship* is at position $\mathbf{O} = (0, 0)$ NM, steaming on course $210^\circ$ at 14 knots.
> *Target Ship* is at position $\mathbf{T} = (3, 4)$ NM (i.e., 3 NM East, 4 NM North of own ship), on course $060^\circ$ at 18 knots.
> **Compute:** (a) Initial relative bearing, (b) CPA, (c) TCPA.

### Step 1: Decompose Velocities into North & East Components

**Own ship velocity vector:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{oN} &= 14 \times \cos(210^\circ) = 14 \times (-0.8660) = -12.124\ \text{kn} \\
V_{oE} &= 14 \times \sin(210^\circ) = 14 \times (-0.5000) = -7.000\ \text{kn}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** $V_o = 14$ kn is resolved along the North–South axis using $\cos$ and East–West axis using $\sin$. The quadrant 210° is in the SW (negative N, negative E) sector.

**Target ship velocity vector:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{tN} &= 18 \times \cos(060^\circ) = 18 \times (0.5000) = +9.000\ \text{kn} \\
V_{tE} &= 18 \times \sin(060^\circ) = 18 \times (0.8660) = +15.588\ \text{kn}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Course 060° lies in the NE quadrant (positive N, positive E).

### Step 2: Compute Relative Velocity Vector

The relative motion of the target *as seen from own ship* is:

$$
\vec{V}_{rel} = \vec{V}_t - \vec{V}_o
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{rel,N} &= V_{tN} - V_{oN} = 9.000 - (-12.124) = +21.124\ \text{kn} \\
V_{rel,E} &= V_{tE} - V_{oE} = 15.588 - (-7.000) = +22.588\ \text{kn}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The relative motion equals the *vector difference* of target velocity minus own velocity. This is the standard convention because the radar/ARPA displays motion of targets **relative to own ship**.

### Step 3: Compute True Bearing and Range to Target

**Target position** (relative to own ship, treating own at origin):
- $X = +3$ NM East, $Y = +4$ NM North.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \sqrt{X^2 + Y^2} = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5.000\ \text{NM} \\
BRG_t &= \arctan\!\left(\frac{X}{Y}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{3}{4}\right) = 36.87^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Since both components are positive, the bearing is in the **NE quadrant (000°–090°)**; no quadrant correction needed.

### Step 4: Compute True Course of Relative Motion

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{rel} &= \arctan\!\left(\frac{V_{rel,E}}{V_{rel,N}}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{22.588}{21.124}\right) = \arctan(1.0695) \\
TC_{rel} &= 46.92^\circ\ \text{(NE quadrant, both components positive)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Both $V_{rel,N}$ and $V_{rel,E}$ are positive → NE quadrant.

### Step 5: Compute the Angle Between Relative-Bearing Line and Relative Course

The angle $\phi$ between the **line from own ship to target** ($BRG_t = 36.87^\circ$) and the **relative course of the target** ($TC_{rel} = 46.92^\circ$) is:

$$
\phi = TC_{rel} - BRG_t = 46.92^\circ - 36.87^\circ = 10.05^\circ
$$

> **Logic:** A small positive $\phi$ means the target's relative motion carries it slightly to the right of the direct line of sight.

### Step 6: Compute CPA (Perpendicular Distance at Closest Approach)

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPA &= D \times \sin(\phi) \\
CPA &= 5.000 \times \sin(10.05^\circ) \\
CPA &= 5.000 \times 0.1745 \\
CPA &= 0.8725\ \text{NM}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The CPA is the perpendicular distance from own ship's position to the *extrapolated relative track* of the target. Geometrically, $D \sin(\phi)$ gives the altitude of a triangle.

### Step 7: Compute TCPA (Time Until CPA)

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{rel} &= \sqrt{V_{rel,N}^2 + V_{rel,E}^2} = \sqrt{21.124^2 + 22.588^2} \\
&= \sqrt{446.22 + 510.22} = \sqrt{956.44} = 30.93\ \text{kn} \\
TCPA &= \frac{D \times \cos(\phi)}{V_{rel}} = \frac{5.000 \times \cos(10.05^\circ)}{30.93} \\
&= \frac{5.000 \times 0.9846}{30.93} = \frac{4.923}{30.93} = 0.1591\ \text{hrs} \\
&= 9.55\ \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The along-track distance from the current position to the CPA point equals $D \cos(\phi)$. Dividing this by relative speed gives the time to reach CPA.

### Step 8: Interpretation & Risk Decision

| Metric | Value | Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **CPA** | $0.87$ NM | **ACCEPTABLE** if $\geq 1$ NM (preferred) |
| **TCPA** | $9.55$ min | Short time → **ALERT** |
| **$\phi$** | $+10.05^\circ$ (starboard) | Target will pass on starboard side |

> **Decision (Rule 8 — Action to Avoid Collision):** CPA < 1 NM and TCPA < 12 min is a textbook scenario for *early and substantial alteration of course*. A standard COLREGs-compliant maneuver is to alter course to **starboard by $30^\circ$–$60^\circ$** to open CPA to $\geq 2$ NM while keeping the target on a stable relative bearing.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — "What to Do" by Encounter Type

| Encounter | Own Ship Role | Target on | Rule | Required Action | Whistle Signal | Maneuver Geometry |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Head-On** | Symmetric | Dead ahead ($\pm 6^\circ$) | 14 | Alter course to **starboard**; pass port-to-port | 2 short | Turn $\geq 20^\circ$ stbd |
| **Crossing** | Give-way (target on stbd) | Starboard | 15 | **Alter course to starboard**; pass astern of target | 1 or 2 short (per new course) | Cross at $\geq 70^\circ$ wide angle |
| **Crossing** | Stand-on (target on port) | Port | 15 | **Maintain course & speed**; if CPA becomes insufficient, sound 5-short, then maneuver per Rule 17 | 5 short (danger signal) | "Don't turn to port" |
| **Overtaking** | Overtaker | Anywhere $> 22.5°$ abaft beam | 13 | **Keep out of the way**; alter course, slow, or a combination | 1 or 2 short | Pass clear at $\geq 1$ NM |
| **Overtaken** | Overtaken | Forward sector | 13 | **Maintain course & speed**; may agree with 2 long + 1 short | 2L+1S = "OK to overtake" | Hold heading steady |
| **Restricted Visibility** | Any | Any (visibility $\leq 1$ NM) | 19 | Reduce to **bare steerageway**; navigate by radar; sound 1 prolonged every 2 min | 1 prolonged (every $\leq 2$ min) | Avoid turns to port for forward targets |

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Head-On Determination

> **Problem:** Own ship is on course $090^\circ$ at 12 kn. A target is sighted at relative bearing $180^\circ$, range 4 NM. The target's true course is observed as $270^\circ$ at 12 kn. **Classify the encounter** and recommend the action.

**Step 1 — Convert relative bearing to true bearing:**
$$
BRG_t = (SH + RB) \bmod 360 = (090 + 180) \bmod 360 = 270^\circ
$$

**Step 2 — Course difference:**
$$
\Delta C = \vert 090 - 270 \vert = 180^\circ
$$

> **Logic:** A course difference of exactly $180^\circ$ (or within $\pm 6^\circ$ of reciprocal) is the textbook definition of a **Head-On Situation** per Rule 14.

**Step 3 — Required action:**
Per Rule 14, each vessel must **alter course to starboard**, pass port-to-port. Recommended own ship alteration: turn to course $\geq 110°$ (a $20°$–$30°$ starboard alteration is the IMO-recommended minimum). New CPA will be $\geq 1$ NM if the geometry is symmetric.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Examiner's Marker:**
> When classifying encounters, students frequently forget the **$\pm 6°$** tolerance for "nearly reciprocal." Stating "reciprocal courses" alone is technically correct only if exactly $180°$ apart. For a $174°$ course difference, you must explicitly mention Rule 14's "nearly reciprocal" language to secure full marks.

## 4.1 Encounter Classification Decision Tree (Mermaid)

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below maps the **Rule 11–18 decision logic** step-by-step, suitable for the KTU ESE problem where a scenario must be classified and the correct action stated.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vessel Sighted] --> B{Visibility<br/>≥ 1 NM?}
    B -- "No, fog" --> C[Apply RULE 19<br/>Restricted Visibility<br/>1 long blast every 2 min<br/>Reduce to bare steerageway]
    B -- "Yes, clear" --> D{Target Aspect<br/>Angle > 22.5°<br/>Abaft Beam?}
    D -- "Yes<br/>stern light only" --> E[Apply RULE 13<br/>Overtaking Situation<br/>Overtaker gives way]
    D -- "No<br/>sidelights visible" --> F{Target Nearly<br/>Reciprocal Course?<br/>180° ± 6°}
    F -- "Yes" --> G[Apply RULE 14<br/>Head-On Situation<br/>BOTH alter to STARBOARD]
    F -- "No" --> H{Target on Own<br/>Starboard Side?}
    H -- "Yes" --> I[Apply RULE 15<br/>CROSSING — Give-Way<br/>CROSS at wide angle ≥ 70°<br/>1 or 2 short blast]
    H -- "No<br/>on port side" --> J[Apply RULE 15<br/>CROSSING — Stand-On<br/>MAINTAIN course and speed<br/>Sound 5 short if CPA<1NM]
    E --> K{Determine Rule 18<br/>Priority}
    G --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    C --> K
    K --> K1{NUC or RAM or CBD?}
    K1 -- "Yes" --> L1[Treat as Priority Vessel<br/>All Others Give Way]
    K1 -- "No" --> L2{Fishing / Sailing<br/>Vessel?}
    L2 -- "Yes" --> L3[Power Vessel Gives Way]
    L2 -- "No<br/>Power vs Power" --> L4[Apply Encounter Sub-Rule]
    L1 --> M[Execute Maneuver +<br/>Sound Signal per Rule 34]
    L3 --> M
    L4 --> M
    J --> M
```
