topics.title: Maritime Search Patterns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Maritime Search Patterns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Maritime Search Pattern** is a pre-planned, systematic, and geometrically defined course of travel executed by a **Surface Rescue Unit (SRU)**, **aircraft (Fixed-Wing or Rotary-Wing)**, or **ship** in order to cover a defined search area with maximum probability of locating a distressed vessel, person, or object at sea. These patterns are codified under the **International Aeronautical and Maritime Search and Rescue Manual (IAMSAR Manual, Volume III)** published jointly by the **International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO)** and the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition:** A Search Pattern is a coordinated, geometric flight/sail route designed to ensure systematic coverage of a search area such that the **Cumulative Probability of Detection (POD)** over the area approaches unity, given operational constraints.

### 1.2 Essential Terminology (KTU Board Frequently Tested)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Datum** | The most probable location of the search object, calculated from the original distress position, drift, and current. |
| **Sweep Width (W)** | The total width on either side of the search unit's track within which the object has a high probability of detection. |
| **Track Spacing (S)** | The perpendicular distance between successive parallel search legs. |
| **Coverage Factor (C)** | Ratio of Sweep Width to Track Spacing (C = W/S). |
| **Probability of Detection (POD)** | The likelihood that the search unit will detect the object during a single pass. |
| **Search Action Plan (SAP)** | The complete plan detailing the pattern, units, duration, and coordination. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you drop your car keys in a dark field at night. You don't randomly walk — instead, you walk in straight lines back-and-forth (parallel sweep), or in an outward spiral (expanding square) to ensure every patch of grass is lit by your torchlight. Maritime search patterns work the **exact same way**, but the "torchlight" is the radar, visual scan, sonar, or FLIR camera, and the "grass" is millions of square nautical miles of ocean.

The selection of which pattern to use depends on:
1. **Accuracy of the Datum** (How sure we are of the object's location).
2. **Type of Search Object** (Liferaft, person-in-water, shipwreck).
3. **Search Platform** (Ship, helicopter, fixed-wing aircraft).
4. **Environmental Conditions** (Sea state, visibility, wind).

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Maritime Search Patterns

The IAMSAR Manual classifies search patterns into two broad families:

### A. Patterns for **Localized Search (Datum Known/Accurate)**
These are used when the search object's position is known with reasonable accuracy.

1. **Expanding Square Search (SS)**
2. **Sector Search (VS)** — also called "Victor Search"
3. **Parallel Sweep Search (PS)**

### B. Patterns for **Wide Area / Track Line Search**
These are used when the object's position is uncertain or it is moving along a predicted route.

1. **Creeping Line Search (Co-ordinated)**
2. **Track Line Search**
3. **Contour Search (parallel to coastline)**

## 2.2 Pattern-by-Pattern Operational Logic

### 2.2.1 Expanding Square Search (SS Pattern)
- **Geometry:** The search unit starts at the datum and moves outward in a series of square legs.
- **Leg Progression:** The length of each successive leg increases by the **track spacing distance**.
- **Logic:** Concentrates search effort near the datum where the probability of object location is highest, then spirals outward.
- **Best Use:** When datum is well-established, single search unit, both day and night, calm-to-moderate seas.

### 2.2.2 Sector Search (VS Pattern)
- **Geometry:** Three radial legs extending outward at **120°** from the datum, then three return legs, forming a 6-leg pinwheel.
- **Logic:** The unit returns to datum after every leg, providing repeated coverage of the most probable position.
- **Best Use:** Single fast rescue unit (helicopter/FPB), good visibility, well-defined datum.

### 2.2.3 Parallel Sweep Search (PS Pattern)
- **Geometry:** Parallel straight legs across the search area.
- **Logic:** Maximizes coverage rate; requires multiple units or repeated passes.
- **Best Use:** Large area, multiple units, electronic search sensors.

### 2.2.4 Creeping Line (Co-ordinated) Search
- **Geometry:** Parallel legs running perpendicular to the search object's expected drift track.
- **Logic:** Catches a drifting object by sweeping across its path.
- **Best Use:** When the datum has drifted significantly along a known axis (downwind/down-current).

### 2.2.5 Track Line Search
- **Geometry:** Single leg along the vessel's intended route.
- **Logic:** Used for missing vessels — sweep along their planned track.
- **Best Use:** Overdue vessels on known routes.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations are HIGH-YIELD for KTU University Examinations.**

| S.No | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Track Spacing | $S = W \times C$ | Nautical Miles (NM) | $W$ = Sweep Width, $C$ = Coverage Factor |
| 2 | Coverage Factor | $C = W/S$ | Dimensionless | Usually $C = 0.5$ to $1.0$ |
| 3 | Number of Parallel Legs | $N = \text{Width} / S$ | Count | Round **UP** to next whole number |
| 4 | Search Area (Rectangle) | $A = L \times W_{\text{area}}$ | NM² | Used for parallel sweep patterns |
| 5 | Search Duration (Parallel) | $T = (N \times L)/V + (N \times t_t)$ | Hours | $V$ = speed, $t_t$ = turn time at end of leg |
| 6 | Expanding Square Leg Length | $L_n = L_1 + (n-1) \times S$ | NM | $n$ = leg number |
| 7 | Sector Search Total Legs | 6 legs (3 outward + 3 return) | – | Each at 120° from previous |
| 8 | Drift Correction | $D = V_{\text{drift}} \times T_{\text{elapsed}}$ | NM | Updated datum position |
| 9 | Probability of Detection (POD) | $POD = 1 - e^{-k \cdot W \cdot N / A}$ | Fraction | $k$ = sweep rate constant |
| 10 | Search Area Per Hour | $\text{SPH} = V \times W \times C$ | NM²/hr | Performance metric of search platform |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant:** For visual searches, $W$ typically ranges from **0.5 NM** (person in water) to **2.0 NM** (raft/large object). For radar searches, $W$ depends on target radar cross-section (RCS).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Coast Guard Operations:** Indian Coast Guard (ICG), USCG, and RNLI use these patterns daily.
2. **Automated SAR Algorithms:** Modern GMDSS and AIS-linked SAR software (e.g., SARIS, CASP) auto-generate these patterns.
3. **Drone / UAV Maritime Surveillance:** Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) are now programmed with these exact geometric patterns.
4. **Offshore Oil & Gas:** Mandatory in HSE (Health, Safety, Environment) contingency plans for MOPU/FPSO operations.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Number of Legs and Search Duration for Parallel Sweep

**Given:**
- Search Area: $A = L \times W_{\text{area}}$ where $L = 30$ NM (length), $W_{\text{area}} = 20$ NM (width).
- Sweep Width: $W = 2.0$ NM
- Desired Coverage Factor: $C = 0.8$
- Search Speed: $V = 10$ knots
- Turn Time at end of leg: $t_t = 0.1$ hours (6 minutes)

### Step 1: Calculate Track Spacing (S)

$$
S = W \times C
$$

$$
S = 2.0 \text{ NM} \times 0.8 = 1.6 \text{ NM}
$$

*Explanation:* The track spacing is set so that successive legs overlap by 20% to compensate for navigation errors and missed detections.

### Step 2: Calculate Number of Legs (N)

$$
N = \frac{W_{\text{area}}}{S} = \frac{20 \text{ NM}}{1.6 \text{ NM}} = 12.5
$$

Since we cannot fly half a leg, we **round up**:

$$
N = 13 \text{ legs}
$$

### Step 3: Calculate Total Distance Traveled

$$
D_{\text{total}} = N \times L = 13 \times 30 = 390 \text{ NM}
$$

### Step 4: Calculate Search Duration

$$
T = \frac{D_{\text{total}}}{V} + (N \times t_t)
$$

$$
T = \frac{390}{10} + (13 \times 0.1)
$$

$$
T = 39 + 1.3 = 40.3 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A single search unit at 10 knots requires approximately 40.3 hours to cover this 600 NM² area with 80% coverage. This is why multiple SRUs are deployed for large-area searches.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Expanding Square Pattern Geometry

**Given:** First leg length $L_1 = 2$ NM, Track spacing $S = 1.5$ NM, Total legs planned $n = 5$.

The general formula for the $n$-th leg length in an Expanding Square search is:

$$
L_n = L_1 + (n-1) \times S
$$

### Step-by-Step Calculation:

**Leg 1:**
$$
L_1 = 2.0 + (1-1) \times 1.5 = 2.0 \text{ NM}
$$

**Leg 2:**
$$
L_2 = 2.0 + (2-1) \times 1.5 = 3.5 \text{ NM}
$$

**Leg 3:**
$$
L_3 = 2.0 + (3-1) \times 1.5 = 5.0 \text{ NM}
$$

**Leg 4:**
$$
L_4 = 2.0 + (4-1) \times 1.5 = 6.5 \text{ NM}
$$

**Leg 5:**
$$
L_5 = 2.0 + (5-1) \times 1.5 = 8.0 \text{ NM}
$$

### Total Distance for 5-leg Expanding Square:

$$
D_{\text{total}} = \sum_{n=1}^{5} L_n = 2.0 + 3.5 + 5.0 + 6.5 + 8.0 = 25.0 \text{ NM}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sector Search Maximum Radius

**Given:** First leg length $r_1 = 3$ NM, Number of outward legs $n = 3$, Successive legs increase by track spacing $S = 1$ NM.

The outward legs form a 3-sided polygon. The maximum radius (datum to outermost point) is:

$$
R_{\max} = r_n = r_1 + (n-1) \times S
$$

$$
R_{\max} = 3 + (3-1) \times 1 = 5 \text{ NM}
$$

The total search area covered (as a hexagonal pinwheel) approximates:

$$
A_{\text{sector}} \approx \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \times R_{\max}^2
$$

$$
A_{\text{sector}} \approx \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \times 5^2 \approx 64.95 \text{ NM}^2
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Board Favorite Table)

| Parameter | Expanding Square | Sector Search | Parallel Sweep | Creeping Line |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Datum Accuracy Required | High | Very High | Moderate | Low-Moderate |
| Number of SRUs | 1 (or more) | 1 (best) | 1 or many | 1 or many |
| Returns to Datum | Yes | Yes (every leg) | No | No |
| Coverage Pattern | Outward Spiral | Radial Pinwheel | Parallel Tracks | Parallel to drift |
| Best Weather | Moderate | Good visibility | Any | Any |
| Time Efficiency | Moderate | Low (high return) | High | High |
| Night Use | Yes | Difficult | Yes (radar) | Yes |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Search Pattern Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Distress Signal Received]) --> datumQ{Is Datum Accurate?}
    datumQ -->|Yes, High Accuracy| singleUnit{Single SRU Available?}
    datumQ -->|No, Drifted or Unknown| wideArea[Use Creeping Line / Parallel Sweep / Track Line]
    singleUnit -->|Yes| visibilityQ{Visibility Conditions?}
    singleUnit -->|No| multiUse[Use Parallel Sweep Multi-Unit]
    visibilityQ -->|Good| sectorChoice[Sector Search Pattern VS]
    visibilityQ -->|Poor or Night| squareChoice[Expanding Square Search SS]
    sectorChoice --> execute1[Execute 6-Leg Pinwheel at 120 deg]
    squareChoice --> execute2[Execute Square Spiral from Datum]
    multiUse --> execute3[Execute Parallel Tracks with Spacing S]
    wideArea --> driftDir{Object Drifting?}
    driftDir -->|Yes, Known Axis| creeping[Creeping Line Perpendicular to Drift]
    driftDir -->|No, but Route Known| trackline[Track Line Along Route]
    driftDir -->|Unknown| parallel2[Parallel Sweep Wide Area]
    execute1 --> endNode([Search Action Plan Activated])
    execute2 --> endNode
    execute3 --> endNode
    creeping --> endNode
    trackline --> endNode
    parallel2 --> endNode
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Search Action Plan

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Distress Beacon Signal] --> procA[Datum Calculation Module]
    procA --> driftCalc[Drift and Current Correction]
    driftCalc --> areaDef[Define Search Area Boundaries]
    areaDef --> patternSel[Pattern Selection Engine]
    patternSel --> ssGen[Expanding Square Generator]
    patternSel --> vsGen[Sector Search Generator]
    patternSel --> psGen[Parallel Sweep Generator]
    ssGen --> coordOut[Coordinate Output to SRU]
    vsGen --> coordOut
    psGen --> coordOut
    coordOut --> execUnit[Surface Rescue Unit Executes Pattern]
    execUnit --> feedback[Sighting or Detection Feedback]
    feedback --> podCalc[POD Accumulator]
    podCalc --> decision{Continue or Suspend?}
    decision -->|POD less than 0.8| patternSel
    decision -->|POD greater than 0.95 or Object Found| suspend[Suspend or Rescue]
```

## 4.3 ASCII Schematic: Expanding Square Pattern Geometry

```
                  LEG 3 (L3 = 5.0 NM)
                  ◄───────────────────────────────►
                  ▲                                │
                  │                                │
        DATUM ●   │ LEG 2 (L2 = 3.5 NM)            │
                  ◄──────────────►                 │
                  │              │                 │
                  │              │                 │
       LEG 1 ───► │              │                 │
       (L1 = 2.0 NM)              │                 │
                  │              │                 │
                  │              │  LEG 4 (L4 = 6.5 NM)
                  │              │ ◄───────────────────────►
                  │              │                 │
                  │              │                 │
                  │              ▼                 ▼
                  │      ◄────────────────────────────────►
                  │         LEG 5 (L5 = 8.0 NM)
                  │
                  └──── Each successive leg = L1 + (n-1) × S
```

## 4.4 ASCII Schematic: Sector Search (Victor Pattern)

```
                        LEG 1
                       ╱
                      ╱   120°
                     ╱ ────────────
                    ╱              ╲
                   ● ───── 120° ────► LEG 2
                  DATUM
                   ╲                 ╱
                    ╲               ╱
                     ╲             ╱
                      ╲   120°   ╱
                       ╲         ╱
                        ▼ LEG 3
                        (Return legs retrace at 120° offset)
```

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Define a "Datum" in maritime search and rescue operations. List the three main factors used to compute it.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Datum** is the most probable location of the search object at a given time, derived from the last known position, the effects of drift (due to wind and current), and the elapsed time since the distress incident.

The three factors used to compute datum are:
1. **Last known position** of the distressed vessel or person.
2. **Total drift** = Leeway (wind-driven) + Set & Drift (current-driven).
3. **Elapsed time** since the last known position was recorded.

The datum position is given by:

$$
D = D_{\text{initial}} + V_{\text{total drift}} \times T_{\text{elapsed}}
$$

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the three factors)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between "Sweep Width" and "Track Spacing" in maritime search patterns.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Sweep Width (W) | Track Spacing (S) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | The width on either side of the search track within which the object can be detected. | The perpendicular distance between successive parallel search legs. |
| **Determined by** | Sensor capability, visibility, target size. | Sweep width and desired coverage factor. |
| **Typical Value** | 0.5 to 4.0 NM | Calculated: S = W × C |
| **Direction** | Perpendicular to track | Perpendicular to track |

(3 marks: full table with relation between W and S)

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a) Explain the "Sector Search Pattern" with a neat sketch. Under what conditions is it the preferred pattern, and what are its limitations? [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The **Sector Search Pattern (also called Victor Search)** is a localized search pattern in which a single search unit executes **six legs radiating outward from the datum**, with each leg offset by **120 degrees** from the previous.

**Geometry:**
- Three outward legs from the datum at angles 0°, 120°, and 240° (or 60°, 180°, 300° depending on reference).
- Three return legs parallel to the outward legs at a perpendicular distance equal to the **track spacing S**.
- Each successive outward leg increases in length by the track spacing.

**Step-by-step procedure:**
1. Position the SRU directly over the datum.
2. Execute Leg 1 of length $r_1$ along bearing 000°.
3. Turn 120° and execute return Leg 2 (parallel, offset by S).
4. Continue alternating outward and return legs until 6 legs are completed.

**Preferred Conditions:**
- Datum is known with **high accuracy**.
- Only **one fast SRU** is available (helicopter or fast patrol boat).
- Good visibility conditions.
- Search object is stationary or has limited drift.

**Limitations:**
- Inefficient for large search areas.
- Requires repeated return to datum (time-consuming).
- Not suitable for low visibility / night operations.
- Single unit vulnerability — no redundancy.

**Sketch:** (Refer to Section 4.4 ASCII diagram)

**(7 marks: 2 for explanation, 2 for geometry, 2 for conditions, 1 for sketch)**

---

**(b) A search area of 25 NM × 16 NM is to be covered using a Parallel Sweep Search. The sweep width is 2.5 NM, desired coverage factor is 0.75, and search speed is 12 knots with a turn time of 5 minutes per leg. Calculate: (i) Track Spacing, (ii) Number of Legs, (iii) Total Search Time. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Length $L = 25$ NM
- Width $W_{\text{area}} = 16$ NM
- Sweep Width $W = 2.5$ NM
- Coverage Factor $C = 0.75$
- Search Speed $V = 12$ knots
- Turn Time $t_t = 5/60 = 0.0833$ hours

**Step (i): Track Spacing**

$$
S = W \times C = 2.5 \times 0.75 = 1.875 \text{ NM}
$$

**Step (ii): Number of Legs**

$$
N = \frac{W_{\text{area}}}{S} = \frac{16}{1.875} = 8.533
$$

Round up:

$$
N = 9 \text{ legs}
$$

**Step (iii): Total Search Time**

Total distance:

$$
D = N \times L = 9 \times 25 = 225 \text{ NM}
$$

Travel time:

$$
T_{\text{travel}} = \frac{225}{12} = 18.75 \text{ hours}
$$

Total turn time:

$$
T_{\text{turn}} = 9 \times 0.0833 = 0.75 \text{ hours}
$$

Total search time:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 18.75 + 0.75 = 19.5 \text{ hours}
$$

**(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 3 distributed across the three sub-parts)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2023]

**(a) With the help of a diagram, explain the "Expanding Square Search Pattern." Derive the formula for the length of the n-th leg. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The **Expanding Square Search (SS)** is a localized search pattern executed from a known datum. The search unit moves outward in a series of **right-angle legs**, with each successive leg being **longer than the previous by the track spacing S**.

**Derivation of the n-th leg formula:**

Let $L_1$ = length of first leg, $S$ = track spacing.

From the geometry of the pattern, the side of each subsequent square increases by the track spacing:

**Leg 1:** $L_1$
**Leg 2:** $L_1 + S$
**Leg 3:** $L_1 + 2S$
**Leg 4:** $L_1 + 3S$
...
**Leg n:** $L_1 + (n-1)S$

Therefore, in compact form:

$$
L_n = L_1 + (n-1)S
$$

**Total distance for n legs:**

$$
D_{\text{total}} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} L_k = n \cdot L_1 + S \cdot \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
$$

**Sketch:** (Refer to Section 4.3 ASCII diagram)

**Advantages:**
- Highest probability of detecting object near datum.
- Easy to execute with single SRU.
- Suitable for both day and night operations.

**Limitations:**
- Inefficient for distant objects (long legs waste time).
- Requires accurate navigation.

**(7 marks: 2 for explanation, 2 for derivation, 2 for sketch, 1 for merits/demerits)**

---

**(b) A maritime search operation requires an Expanding Square Search starting with a first leg of 4 NM and track spacing of 2 NM. If 6 legs are to be executed at a search speed of 8 knots, calculate: (i) The length of each leg, (ii) The total distance covered, (iii) The total search time. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $L_1 = 4$ NM, $S = 2$ NM, $n = 6$, $V = 8$ knots.

**(i) Length of each leg using $L_n = L_1 + (n-1)S$:**

$$
L_1 = 4 + 0 = 4 \text{ NM}
$$

$$
L_2 = 4 + 2 = 6 \text{ NM}
$$

$$
L_3 = 4 + 4 = 8 \text{ NM}
$$

$$
L_4 = 4 + 6 = 10 \text{ NM}
$$

$$
L_5 = 4 + 8 = 12 \text{ NM}
$$

$$
L_6 = 4 + 10 = 14 \text{ NM}
$$

**(ii) Total distance:**

$$
D_{\text{total}} = 4 + 6 + 8 + 10 + 12 + 14 = 54 \text{ NM}
$$

Verification using formula:

$$
D_{\text{total}} = n \cdot L_1 + S \cdot \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
$$

$$
D_{\text{total}} = 6 \times 4 + 2 \times \frac{6 \times 5}{2} = 24 + 30 = 54 \text{ NM} \checkmark
$$

**(iii) Total search time (neglecting turn time):**

$$
T = \frac{D_{\text{total}}}{V} = \frac{54}{8} = 6.75 \text{ hours}
$$

If turn time of 0.05 hr per leg is included:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 6.75 + (6 \times 0.05) = 6.75 + 0.30 = 7.05 \text{ hours}
$$

**(7 marks: 3 + 2 + 2 distributed across sub-parts)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Error Trap:** Always use **Nautical Miles (NM)** for distance and **knots** for speed. Mixing kilometers and knots loses 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Rounding Direction:** Number of legs must be **rounded UP** (ceiling function), never rounded down. 8.53 becomes 9, not 8.
> 3. **Track Spacing Formula:** Students often write $S = W/C$ instead of $S = W \times C$. Memorize: **Spacing = Width × Coverage**.
> 4. **Sector Search Legs:** Forgetting that sector has **6 legs total** (3 outward + 3 return) loses a mark.
> 5. **Expanding Square Direction:** Each leg turns **90°** (not 120° as in sector). Confusing these patterns is a common error.
> 6. **Drift Sign Convention:** Always state whether drift is **upwind/downwind** or **upcurrent/downcurrent**. Marks are awarded for direction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must memorize before KTU exam):**

- ✅ Search patterns are classified into **Localized (SS, VS, PS)** and **Wide-area (Creeping, Track Line, Contour)**.
- ✅ **Expanding Square** uses legs increasing by track spacing $S$ at 90° turns.
- ✅ **Sector Search** has 6 legs at 120° angular separation, returning to datum each time.
- ✅ **Parallel Sweep** is the most time-efficient for large areas and multi-unit operations.
- ✅ Track Spacing $S = W \times C$, where $C$ is typically **0.5 to 1.0**.
- ✅ Number of legs $N = \lceil W_{\text{area}} / S \rceil$ — **always round UP**.
- ✅ Datum position drifts with **leeway + current**; recalculate every hour during long SAR.
- ✅ Total distance for Expanding Square with $n$ legs:

$$
D = n \cdot L_1 + S \cdot \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
$$

- ✅ Search Action Plan (SAP) is the **legal and operational document** that records the chosen pattern.
- ✅ **IAMSAR Manual Volumes I, II, III** are the international reference (ICAO + IMO joint publication).
- ✅ Indian regulations follow the **Indian Search and Rescue Manual** aligned with IAMSAR.
- ✅ For visual search of a **person in water**, $W \approx 0.5$ NM; for **life raft**, $W \approx 1.5$–2.0 NM.
- ✅ **Creeping Line Search** is chosen when the object drifts along a predictable axis (downwind).
- ✅ **Track Line Search** is reserved for vessels with a known planned route.
- ✅ POD increases logarithmically with **N** (number of legs) and **W** (sweep width).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU typically asks one 7-mark question from search pattern geometry and one 7-mark numerical. Practice both the derivation of $L_n$ and the parallel sweep time calculation — these are **guaranteed high-yield topics.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SAR Equipment and Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SAR Equipment and Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is SAR (Search and Rescue) Equipment?

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
Search and Rescue (SAR) Equipment and Technology refers to the integrated suite of electronic, radio-communication, optical, mechanical, and satellite-based systems carried onboard vessels — and deployed at shore stations, RCCs (Rescue Coordination Centres), and in survival craft — to detect, locate, communicate with, and recover persons in distress at sea, in compliance with the **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS)** and the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** performance standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Syllabus Mandate (KTU 2024):** The student must be able to identify, describe the operating principle of, and explain the operational use of each piece of distress, locating, and communication equipment that forms part of the **Global Maritime Distress and Safety System (GMDSS)**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Car Crash Airbag Network for Ships"

Imagine driving a car equipped with a network of smart sensors. The instant the airbag deploys, three things happen *simultaneously*:
1. The car **automatically dials 911** (Emergency Communication — DSC/VHF).
2. A **small beacon pops out of the dashboard and starts transmitting your exact GPS coordinates** to satellites (EPIRB).
3. **Nearby cars receive an alert** on their radar screens, guiding them straight to you (SART response on radar).

SAR Equipment works on **exactly the same principle** for ships. A vessel in distress triggers a chain reaction: satellites detect the beacon, RCCs are alerted, nearby vessels receive a homing signal, and rescue units are vectored to the precise location. Without this equipment, a sinking ship in the open ocean is essentially invisible to the rest of the world.

### 1.3 The Four Functional Pillars of SAR Equipment

| Pillar | Function | Primary Equipment |
|--------|----------|-------------------|
| **1. Distress Alerting** | Send a "Mayday" signal globally, automatically | EPIRB, DSC, INMARSAT |
| **2. Locating / Homing** | Guide rescuers to the exact position of survivors | SART, AIS-SART, Radar, RDF |
| **3. Communication** | Two-way voice/text with rescue units | VHF Radio, GMDSS consoles, INMARSAT-C |
| **4. Survival Support** | Keep survivors alive & visible until rescue | Lifeboat radio, thermal suits, flares, lights |

> [!NOTE]
> The **GMDSS** (Global Maritime Distress and Safety System) is the umbrella framework. All SAR equipment discussed in this module is a *subsystem* of GMDSS, which became mandatory for SOLAS vessels under **Chapter IV** of SOLAS, effective from **1 February 1992** and fully enforced from **1 February 1999**.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics & Standards Bodies

- **406 MHz** — International distress frequency for satellite EPIRBs (Cospas-Sarsat standard).
- **121.5 MHz** — Homing frequency used by overflying aircraft and rescue craft.
- **9 GHz band (X-band)** — Radar frequency interrogated by SARTs (9.3 – 9.5 GHz).
- **156.525 MHz (Channel 70)** — DSC dedicated VHF channel for digital alerting.
- **Standard Bodies:** IMO, ITU (International Telecommunication Union), IEC, IMSO, Cospas-Sarsat.

### 1.5 Quick Topological Map of the GMDSS Sea Areas

| Sea Area | Coverage | Equipment Required |
|----------|----------|--------------------|
| **A1** | Within VHF DSC range of a coast station (~20–30 NM) | VHF DSC |
| **A2** | Within MF DSC range (~100–150 NM) | VHF + MF DSC |
| **A3** | Within INMARSAT coverage (roughly 70°N to 70°S) | VHF/MF + INMARSAT or HF |
| **A4** | Polar regions (outside A3) | VHF/MF + HF DSC + COSPAS-SARSAT EPIRB |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Anatomy of Each Major SAR Equipment

### 2.1.1 EPIRB — Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacon

**Operating Principle (Step-by-Step Logic):**
- A float-free EPIRB is housed in a **hydrostatic release unit (HRU)** that automatically deploys the beacon when the vessel sinks to a depth of **1.5 – 4.5 m** (typically set at 2.5 m).
- On water contact, seawater activates the beacon, which transmits on **406 MHz** (via Cospas-Sarsat satellites) and on the **121.5 MHz** homing frequency.
- A built-in **GPS receiver** encodes the precise latitude/longitude into the 406 MHz burst message.
- The message is relayed to a **Local User Terminal (LUT)** → **Mission Control Centre (MCC)** → the responsible **RCC (Rescue Coordination Centre)**.

**Key Technical Specs (Board Exam Favourites):**
- 406 MHz transmission power: **5 W** (nominal).
- Message repetition rate: every **50 seconds** (approx).
- Battery life: **48 hours** minimum at –20°C.
- Unique 15-hexadecimal-digit ID identifies the vessel.
- Operational after a **10 m drop** into water (rigorous IMO test).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **406 MHz** frequency is reserved **exclusively** for distress beacons worldwide. Routine transmission on this frequency is a punishable offence under international radio regulations.

### 2.1.2 SART — Search and Rescue Radar Transponder

**Operating Principle:**
- A SART is a passive receiver that, when interrogated by a **9 GHz (X-band, 3 cm wavelength) radar pulse** from a searching ship or aircraft, immediately transmits a response signal back.
- The response appears as a **line of 12 dots** on the rescue vessel's radar screen, stretching from the SART's position towards the edge of the radar sweep along the bearing line.
- As the rescue craft closes to within **1 nautical mile**, the dots change to arcs, and finally to a **solid circle** when within ~200 metres, providing unambiguous homing.

**Critical Frequencies:**
- Receive band: **9.3 – 9.5 GHz**.
- Reply band: **9.3 – 9.5 GHz** (12 sweep responses per interrogation).
- Battery endurance: **96 hours** in standby + **8 hours** continuous operation.

### 2.1.3 AIS-SART (Automatic Identification System — Search and Rescue Transmitter)

- A newer alternative to the radar SART, mandated from **1 January 2010** for SOLAS vessels.
- Instead of responding to radar, the AIS-SART transmits **AIS messages (on 161.975 MHz and 162.025 MHz – VHF AIS channels)** containing GPS coordinates, MMSI of the parent ship, and a "SART ACTIVE" status.
- Displays on any **AIS-equipped ship or shore station** within VHF range.
- Operates for at least **96 hours**.

> [!NOTE]
> AIS-SART and Radar-SART are **not interchangeable in all cases**. Aircraft typically carry 9 GHz radar, not AIS receivers, so radar-SART remains the primary choice for aerial SAR.

### 2.1.4 VHF DSC — Digital Selective Calling

- A digital calling system operating on **Channel 70 (156.525 MHz)** for alerting, and voice on **Channel 16 (156.800 MHz)** for subsequent communication.
- A DSC distress alert transmits a **pre-formatted digital burst** containing the vessel's MMSI (9-digit identity), position (if GPS-coupled), nature of distress, and time.
- The dedicated "**Distress Button**" under a red spring-loaded cover must be pressed for **at least 5 seconds** to activate a distress alert.

### 2.1.5 INMARSAT — Satellite Communication System

- Uses **geostationary satellites** at four ocean regions: AOR-E, AOR-W, IOR, POR.
- Services include voice, telex, fax, and data.
- **INMARSAT-C** is the GMDSS workhorse for store-and-forward text distress messaging.
- Standard C is mandated for ships operating in **Sea Areas A3**.

### 2.1.6 NAVTEX — Navigational Telex

- A system for automatically broadcasting maritime safety information (MSI) on **518 kHz** (English) and **490 kHz** (local language).
- Coastal transmitters broadcast up to **400 NM** range.
- Receivers print navigational warnings, weather forecasts, and SAR alerts.

### 2.1.7 Personal Locator Beacon (PLB)

- A smaller, person-carried version of the EPIRB, also operating on **406 MHz**.
- Not mandated by SOLAS, but widely used by personnel on offshore platforms, yachtsmen, and aircraft crew.
- Battery life: typically **24 hours** at –20°C.
- Must be registered with the national authority for the assigned country code.

### 2.1.8 Hand-Held VHF (GMDSS Survival Craft Radio)

- A waterproof VHF transceiver carried in lifeboats and life rafts.
- Operates on **Channel 16** and at least one other channel (often Channel 13 for bridge-to-bridge).
- Power: at least **0.25 W** on low power to conserve battery, **1 W or higher** on high power.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Standard | Units / Notes |
|---------|--------------------|---------------|
| Free-space path loss (FSPL) of distress beacon | $FSPL \;=\; 20\log_{10}(d) \;+\; 20\log_{10}(f) \;+\; 32.45$ | dB ; d in km, f in MHz |
| Cosine-rule range between two GPS points | $d \;=\; R \cdot \arccos\!\bigl[\sin\varphi_1 \sin\varphi_2 \;+\; \cos\varphi_1 \cos\varphi_2 \cos(\Delta\lambda)\bigr]$ | R = 3440 NM (Earth's radius in NM) |
| Great-circle distance (Haversine form) | $d \;=\; 2R \cdot \arcsin\!\Bigl(\sqrt{\sin^2\!\tfrac{\Delta\varphi}{2} \;+\; \cos\varphi_1\cos\varphi_2 \sin^2\!\tfrac{\Delta\lambda}{2}}\Bigr)$ | Avoids floating-point errors near antipodes |
| Doppler shift on 406 MHz beacon | $\Delta f \;=\; f_0 \cdot \dfrac{v_{rel}}{c}$ | f₀ = 406 MHz, v_rel relative velocity, c = 3×10⁸ m/s |
| Radar SART sweep dot count | **12 dots** per interrogation response | Becomes arcs below 1 NM, circle below 200 m |
| EPIRB battery endurance (406 MHz) | ≥ **48 hours** at –20°C | SOLAS/IMO performance standard |
| SART battery endurance (standby) | ≥ **96 hours** | + 8 hours continuous transmission |
| Hydrostatic Release Unit activation depth | **1.5 m to 4.5 m** | Typically set at 2.5 m |
| Radar SART operating band | **9.3 – 9.5 GHz** | X-band, 3 cm wavelength |
| EPIRB distress frequency (satellite) | **406 MHz** | Cospas-Sarsat |
| EPIRB homing frequency | **121.5 MHz** | Also 243 MHz military (older) |
| DSC alerting channel (VHF) | **Channel 70**, 156.525 MHz | Voice follow-up on Ch. 16, 156.800 MHz |
| AIS-SART operating frequencies | **161.975 MHz & 162.025 MHz** | VHF AIS channels |

> [!NOTE]
> The path-loss equation $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.45$ assumes free-space propagation with isotropic antennas. Real-world propagation introduces multi-path, sea-surface reflections, and atmospheric ducting — these are exam-level "real-world" qualifiers expected in KTU 14-mark questions.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The integration of SAR equipment with **AIS**, **ECDIS**, **GMDSS consoles**, and **LRIT (Long-Range Identification and Tracking)** creates a fully redundant, layered safety net. Modern SAR operations depend on:
- **Geostationary satellites (INMARSAT)** for tropical/temperate latitudes.
- **Polar-orbiting Cospas-Sarsat satellites** for full global coverage including poles.
- **Homing receivers on helicopters** (121.5 MHz and 9 GHz radar).
- **Drone-based thermal imaging** as an emerging SAR supplement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Great-Circle Distance between Distress Vessel and Rescue Craft

**Scenario:** An RCC receives a distress alert from vessel A at coordinates $(\varphi_1, \lambda_1)$ and dispatches rescue vessel B at $(\varphi_2, \lambda_2)$. Determine the surface distance (NM) to be covered.

### Step 1: Define the Spherical Triangle

Treat the Earth as a sphere of radius $R = 3440$ NM (mean nautical radius). The two points and the Earth's pole form a spherical triangle.

### Step 2: Compute the Latitude Difference and Longitude Difference

$$
\Delta\varphi \;=\; \varphi_2 - \varphi_1
$$
$$
\Delta\lambda \;=\; \lambda_2 - \lambda_1
$$

Both quantities must be in **radians** before further computation.

### Step 3: Apply the Haversine Formula

$$
d \;=\; 2R \cdot \arcsin\!\Bigl(\sqrt{\sin^2\!\tfrac{\Delta\varphi}{2} \;+\; \cos\varphi_1 \cos\varphi_2 \sin^2\!\tfrac{\Delta\lambda}{2}}\Bigr)
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The haversine is preferred over the cosine rule because it remains numerically stable for very small angular separations, which is common in maritime GPS coordinates.

### Step 4: Worked Numerical Example

**Given:** Distress vessel A at $\varphi_1 = 19.0°\text{N},\; \lambda_1 = 72.8°\text{E}$ (off Mumbai).  
Rescue craft B at $\varphi_2 = 18.5°\text{N},\; \lambda_2 = 72.4°\text{E}$ (closer to coast).

**Convert to radians** (multiply by $\pi/180$):
$$
\varphi_1 = 0.3316 \text{ rad}, \quad \varphi_2 = 0.3229 \text{ rad}, \quad \lambda_1 = 1.2707 \text{ rad}, \quad \lambda_2 = 1.2634 \text{ rad}
$$

**Compute differences:**
$$
\Delta\varphi = -0.00873 \text{ rad}, \quad \Delta\lambda = -0.00733 \text{ rad}
$$

**Half-angles:**
$$
\frac{\Delta\varphi}{2} = -0.00436, \quad \frac{\Delta\lambda}{2} = -0.00366
$$

**Sine squares:**
$$
\sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\Delta\varphi}{2}\right) \approx 1.905 \times 10^{-5}
$$
$$
\cos\varphi_1 \cos\varphi_2 = (0.9455)(0.9486) = 0.8970
$$
$$
\sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right) \approx 1.343 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Inner bracket:**
$$
\sin^2\!\tfrac{\Delta\varphi}{2} + \cos\varphi_1\cos\varphi_2 \sin^2\!\tfrac{\Delta\lambda}{2} = 1.905 \times 10^{-5} + (0.8970)(1.343 \times 10^{-5})
$$
$$
= 1.905 \times 10^{-5} + 1.205 \times 10^{-5} = 3.110 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Square root & arcsine:**
$$
\sqrt{3.110 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.005577
$$
$$
\arcsin(0.005577) = 0.005577 \text{ rad (small-angle approximation valid)}
$$

**Final distance:**
$$
d = 2 \times 3440 \times 0.005577 = 38.37 \text{ NM}
$$

**Interpretation:** The rescue craft must cover approximately **38.4 NM** to reach the distressed vessel. At a typical SAR speed of 15 knots, this translates to roughly **2.56 hours** of steaming.

## 3.2 Derivation: Free-Space Path Loss for a 406 MHz EPIRB Signal to a LEO Satellite

**Given:** A 406 MHz EPIRB transmits at 5 W EIRP toward a Cospas-Sarsat LEO satellite at orbital altitude of **850 km**.

### Step 1: Isotropic Radiated Power & Wavelength
$$
\lambda \;=\; \frac{c}{f} \;=\; \frac{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}{406 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}} \;=\; 0.739 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 2: Free-Space Path Loss
$$
FSPL \;=\; 20\log_{10}\!\bigl(850 \times 10^3 \text{ m}\bigr) + 20\log_{10}\!\bigl(406 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}\bigr) + 20\log_{10}\!\bigl(\tfrac{4\pi}{c}\bigr)
$$

$$
= 20\log_{10}(8.5 \times 10^5) + 20\log_{10}(4.06 \times 10^8) - 147.55
$$

$$
= 20(5.929) + 20(8.609) - 147.55
$$

$$
= 118.58 + 172.17 - 147.55 = 143.2 \text{ dB}
$$

### Step 3: Received Power at Satellite (Assuming 5 W EIRP)

$$
P_{rx} \;=\; P_{tx}(\text{dBW}) - FSPL
$$
$$
P_{tx} = 10 \log_{10}(5) = 6.99 \text{ dBW}
$$
$$
P_{rx} = 6.99 - 143.2 = -136.2 \text{ dBW}
$$

### Step 4: Convert to dBm for Intuition
$$
P_{rx} = -136.2 + 30 = -106.2 \text{ dBm}
$$

**Interpretation:** Despite the enormous path loss, modern Cospas-Sarsat receivers achieve sensitivities near **–134 dBm**, allowing the distress signal to be reliably decoded and triangulated.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choosing the Right Beacon

| Parameter | EPIRB (406 MHz) | SART (9 GHz) | AIS-SART | PLB | VHF DSC |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------|----------|-----|---------|
| **Primary Range** | Global (satellite) | ~5 NM radar | ~4–7 NM VHF | Global (satellite) | ~20–30 NM |
| **Time to Alert RCC** | < 5 min via LUT | Indirect (requires radar-equipped vessel) | Seconds to all AIS ships | < 5 min | Seconds |
| **Position Accuracy** | < 5 km (LEO) / 100 m (with GPS) | 1–2 NM radar accuracy | < 10 m (GPS) | < 5 km / 100 m | 0.5–2 NM (DF) |
| **Battery Endurance** | 48 h | 96 h standby + 8 h | 96 h | 24 h | N/A (ship power) |
| **Aircraft Detection** | Limited (121.5 MHz homing) | Excellent (X-band radar) | Limited | Limited | No |
| **Cost (approx.)** | ₹80k–₹1.5 L | ₹60k–₹1 L | ₹1.2 L–₹2.5 L | ₹20k–₹60k | Shipboard GMDSS console |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Logic for KTU Answers:** A SOLAS vessel on an international voyage must carry both an **EPIRB** (satellite alerting) **and** a **SART** or **AIS-SART** (homing), irrespective of sea area. VHF DSC is mandatory in **all four sea areas** as the primary short-range alert.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — End-to-End Distress Alert & Rescue Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Distressed Vessel] --> node2[EPIRB Activation]
    node2 --> node3[406 MHz Burst with GPS]
    node3 --> node4[LEO / GEO Satellite]
    node4 --> node5[Local User Terminal LUT]
    node5 --> node6[Mission Control Centre MCC]
    node6 --> node7[Rescue Coordination Centre RCC]
    node7 --> node8{SAR Asset Selection}
    node8 --> node9[Rescue Ship]
    node8 --> node10[Rescue Aircraft]
    node8 --> node11[Helicopter]
    node9 --> node12[9 GHz Radar Interrogation]
    node10 --> node12
    node12 --> node13[SART Response 12 Dots]
    node13 --> node14[Radar Display Homing]
    node14 --> node15[Recovery and Survivor Pickup]
    node7 -.DSC VHF Channel 70.-> node9
    node7 -.INMARSAT Voice or Data.-> node10
    node1 -.Voice Channel 16.-> node9
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — GMDSS Equipment Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[GMDSS Master] --> nodeB[Distress Alerting Subsystem]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Locating Subsystem]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Communication Subsystem]
    nodeA --> nodeE[MSI Reception Subsystem]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[EPIRB 406 MHz]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[VHF DSC Ch70]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[INMARSAT C]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Radar SART 9 GHz]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[AIS SART]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[121.5 MHz Homing]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[VHF Radiotelephone]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[MF HF Radiotelephone]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[INMARSAT Voice and Data]
    nodeE --> nodeE1[NAVTEX 518 kHz]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[EGC Safety NET]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — EPIRB Internal Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeX1[HRU Hydrostatic Release] --> nodeX2[Float Free Bracket]
    nodeX2 --> nodeX3[Seawater Switch]
    nodeX3 --> nodeX4[Main Processor and Encoder]
    nodeX4 --> nodeX5[GPS Receiver Module]
    nodeX4 --> nodeX6[406 MHz Transmitter]
    nodeX4 --> nodeX7[121.5 MHz Homing Beacon]
    nodeX5 --> nodeX4
    nodeX6 --> nodeX8[Cospas Sarsat Satellite Uplink]
    nodeX7 --> nodeX9[Overflying Aircraft Receiver]
    nodeX8 --> nodeX10[LUT and MCC Network]
    nodeX10 --> nodeX11[RCC Alert]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagrams are **functional block architectures** rather than physical schematics. They map the signal and data flow between physical devices, which is the standard diagram type expected in KTU board answers (1 mark is usually reserved for neat block diagrams in 14-mark questions).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define an EPIRB. State the operating frequency and the satellite system used for distress alerting.**

**Model Answer:**
An **EPIRB (Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacon)** is a battery-operated radio transmitter that, when activated (manually or automatically upon vessel sinking), broadcasts a distress signal to alert search-and-rescue services.

- **Operating frequencies:** 406 MHz (satellite alerting via Cospas-Sarsat) and 121.5 MHz (homing by aircraft).
- **Satellite system:** **Cospas-Sarsat** (a constellation of LEO and GEO satellites).
- A unique 15-character hexadecimal ID is encoded in every transmission to identify the vessel.
- Modern EPIRBs integrate a **GPS receiver** for position accuracy better than 100 m.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is a SART? Explain the meaning of "12 dots" on a rescue vessel's radar screen.**

**Model Answer:**
A **SART (Search and Rescue Radar Transponder)** is a portable device carried in survival craft or mounted on the parent vessel, which responds to 9 GHz (X-band) radar interrogations from searching units.

- Upon interrogation, the SART transmits a series of **12 response signals**, appearing on the radar PPI as a **line of 12 equally-spaced dots**.
- The dots extend from the SART's position along the bearing line.
- **Within 1 NM:** dots change to **arcs**.
- **Within ~200 m:** arcs merge into a **solid circle**, indicating close-range homing.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A. [KTU University Exam - Jul 2024] — Choice A

**(a) Describe in detail the operating principle, frequencies, and performance standards of a 406 MHz EPIRB. Highlight how the Cospas-Sarsat system processes the distress alert. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition & Purpose (1 mark):** A 406 MHz EPIRB is a satellite-linked distress beacon that transmits a unique identification and position data to the Cospas-Sarsat satellite system on the internationally reserved 406 MHz frequency.

**2. Activation Mechanism (1 mark):** Two modes:
- *Manual* activation via a clearly marked push-button.
- *Automatic* via a **Hydrostatic Release Unit (HRU)** that releases the beacon from its bracket when the vessel sinks to a depth of 1.5 – 4.5 m, with subsequent **seawater switch** activation.

**3. Frequencies Used (1 mark):**
- **406 MHz** — satellite uplink (Cospas-Sarsat, 5 W EIRP).
- **121.5 MHz** — homing transmission for aircraft and rescue craft.

**4. Message Structure (1 mark):** The 406 MHz burst contains:
- 15-character hexadecimal ID (country code + vessel ID).
- Position (if GPS-integrated).
- Nature of distress (when manually selected).
- Battery and beacon status.

**5. Cospas-Sarsat Processing Chain (2 marks):**
$$
\text{Beacon} \;\to\; \text{Satellite (LEO/GEO)} \;\to\; \text{LUT} \;\to\; \text{MCC} \;\to\; \text{RCC}
$$
- **LEO satellites** use **Doppler shift** for triangulation (less accurate, but global).
- **GEO satellites** provide immediate alerting using encoded position (no triangulation).
- LUTs forward data to MCCs, which route alerts to the appropriate RCC.

**6. Performance Standards (1 mark):**
- Battery life ≥ **48 hours** at –20°C.
- Transmission burst every **50 seconds**.
- Must survive a **10 m drop** and 5 minutes at 10 m submersion.
- Operates in temperatures from –20°C to +55°C.

---

**(b) Compare the working principles and operational use of Radar SART and AIS-SART. Under what conditions is each preferred? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Radar SART | AIS-SART |
|--------|------------|----------|
| **Operating Principle** | Responds to 9 GHz X-band radar interrogation by emitting a coded reply | Broadcasts AIS messages (161.975/162.025 MHz) with GPS position |
| **Display on Search Unit** | 12 dots → arcs → circle on radar PPI | Symbol "SART ACTIVE" on AIS plotter/ECDIS |
| **Detection Range** | ~5 NM by ship radar; up to 30 NM by aircraft | ~4–7 NM VHF range |
| **Aircraft Compatibility** | Excellent (aircraft carry 9 GHz radar) | Limited (aircraft may not have AIS receivers) |
| **Position Accuracy** | Radar bearing accuracy (1–2 NM) | GPS-based (better than 10 m) |
| **Battery Endurance** | 96 h standby + 8 h transmit | 96 h continuous |

**Conditions of Preference (1 mark each):**

1. **Radar SART preferred** when:
   - Rescue is being performed by an **aircraft or helicopter** (which carries 9 GHz weather/mapping radar).
   - Operating in areas with **dense radar coverage** but limited AIS.
   - Older rescue infrastructure with conventional X-band radar.

2. **AIS-SART preferred** when:
   - Rescue is by **AIS-equipped surface vessels** (most modern SOLAS ships).
   - **High positional accuracy** is critical (within 10 m).
   - The search pattern involves multiple vessels receiving alerts simultaneously via AIS broadcast.

3. **Mandatory coexistence:** Under SOLAS Chapter III, vessels may carry **either** a radar SART **or** an AIS-SART, but not both, *unless required by flag state or route*. The choice is typically based on the trade area and the predominant SAR assets of the region.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**
> Students frequently confuse **406 MHz** (satellite) with **121.5 MHz** (homing). The 406 MHz band is for *alerting*, not homing. Similarly, the **9 dots / 12 dots** confusion is common — the correct SART response count is **12 dots**. Writing "9 dots" costs 1 full mark in 14-mark questions.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024] — Choice B

**(a) With the help of a block diagram, explain the GMDSS communication architecture and the four sea areas (A1, A2, A3, A4). (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. GMDSS Definition (1 mark):** The Global Maritime Distress and Safety System is an integrated communication network using satellite and terrestrial radio systems to ensure that any vessel in distress can be detected, located, and rescued promptly, regardless of its position at sea.

**2. Functional Architecture (3 marks):**
The GMDSS comprises four functional sub-systems:
- **Distress Alerting** (EPIRB, DSC, INMARSAT).
- **Search and Rescue Co-ordination** (via RCCs).
- **Maritime Safety Information (MSI)** dissemination (NAVTEX, SafetyNET).
- **General Radio Communications** (VHF/MF/HF).

The block diagram (refer to Section 4.2 above) shows the flow from these subsystems to the satellite and shore-based infrastructure.

**3. Sea Areas (3 marks):**

| Sea Area | Coverage Definition | Required Equipment |
|----------|---------------------|--------------------|
| **A1** | Within range of at least one VHF coast station with continuous DSC alerting (~20–30 NM) | VHF DSC |
| **A2** | Within range of at least one MF coast station with continuous DSC alerting (excluding A1, ~100–150 NM) | VHF + MF DSC |
| **A3** | Within INMARSAT geostationary satellite coverage (~70°N to 70°S, excluding A1 & A2) | INMARSAT or HF DSC + EPIRB |
| **A4** | Polar regions outside A3 | HF DSC + 406 MHz EPIRB |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often forget that **Sea Area A4 is a special polar coverage zone** beyond INMARSAT, requiring HF communication as the only satellite-free option for A3-style alerting. This distinction is a **favourite 2-mark follow-up** in KTU exams.

---

**(b) A 406 MHz EPIRB is activated at sea. Calculate the free-space path loss (FSPL) to a Cospas-Sarsat LEO satellite at an orbital altitude of 850 km. Assume an EIRP of 5 W and a satellite receiver sensitivity of –134 dBm. Is the alert decodable? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Free-Space Path Loss formula (1 mark):**
$$
FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.45 \quad \text{(d in km, f in MHz)}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values (2 marks):**
- $d = 850$ km
- $f = 406$ MHz

$$
FSPL = 20\log_{10}(850) + 20\log_{10}(406) + 32.45
$$

$$
= 20(2.929) + 20(2.609) + 32.45
$$

$$
= 58.58 + 52.18 + 32.45 = 143.21 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3: EIRP in dBW (1 mark):**
$$
P_{tx} = 10\log_{10}(5) = 6.99 \text{ dBW}
$$

**Step 4: Received power at satellite (1 mark):**
$$
P_{rx} = 6.99 - 143.21 = -136.22 \text{ dBW} = -106.22 \text{ dBm}
$$

**Step 5: Comparison with receiver sensitivity (1 mark):**
- $P_{rx} = -106.22$ dBm
- Receiver sensitivity = $-134$ dBm
- Since $-106.22 > -134$, the received power is **above** the sensitivity threshold by:
$$
\text{Margin} = -106.22 - (-134) = 27.78 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 6: Conclusion (1 mark):**
**Yes, the alert is decodable.** The link budget provides a healthy **27.8 dB margin**, which more than compensates for real-world factors such as polarisation mismatch, atmospheric losses, and antenna gain roll-off near the horizon.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — SAR Equipment and Technology**

- ⭐ **GMDSS** is the master framework; mandated by **SOLAS Chapter IV**.
- ⭐ **EPIRB:** 406 MHz (satellite) + 121.5 MHz (homing); battery ≥ 48 h; auto-deploys at **1.5–4.5 m depth** via HRU.
- ⭐ **SART (Radar):** 9 GHz X-band; 12 dots → arcs → solid circle during approach.
- ⭐ **AIS-SART:** VHF AIS channels 161.975 / 162.025 MHz; GPS position; 96 h endurance.
- ⭐ **PLB:** Personal version of EPIRB, 406 MHz, 24 h battery.
- ⭐ **DSC:** Channel 70 (156.525 MHz) for alerting; Channel 16 (156.800 MHz) for voice.
- ⭐ **INMARSAT:** Geostationary satellites for **A3** coverage; INMARSAT-C is the GMDSS workhorse.
- ⭐ **NAVTEX:** 518 kHz (English), 490 kHz (local), 4209.5 kHz; up to 400 NM range.
- ⭐ **Sea Area A4** = polar regions → mandatory HF DSC + 406 MHz EPIRB.
- ⭐ **Path-loss formula:** $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.45$ (d in km, f in MHz).
- ⭐ **Great-circle distance:** Use the **Haversine formula** for stability with small angles.
- ⭐ **Doppler shift** is the key LEO-satellite position-fixing method.
- ⭐ **SART** is detected by **radar**, **AIS-SART** by **AIS receivers** — choose based on rescue asset type.
- ⭐ **Hydrostatic Release** depth: **2.5 m** is industry standard.
- ⭐ **COSPAS = COsmicheskaya Sistyema Poiska Avariynich Sudov** (Russian acronym for "Space System for the Search of Vessels in Distress"); **SARSAT = Search and Rescue Satellite-Aided Tracking**.
- ⭐ **HRU** is replaced **every 2 years** (paint-marked date) — exam favourite.
- ⭐ **EPIRB registration** with national RCC is mandatory for 406 MHz IDs to be meaningful.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Marine Structures and Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Marine Structures and Stability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Marine Structures — What Are They?

A **marine structure** in the context of shipbuilding refers to the load-bearing steel skeleton and shell of a vessel that resists hydrostatic, hydrodynamic, dynamic, and inertial loads during a ship's service life. The structure must safely transfer all acting loads (still-water, wave, slamming, cargo, ballast, and thermal) to the supporting water via the hull envelope without permanent deformation or collapse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Aligned with Classification Society SOLAS/IMO):**
> A marine (ship) structure is a welded, compartmented steel assembly comprising the **shell plating**, **deck plating**, **transverse and longitudinal framing**, **bulkheads**, **double bottom**, **superstructures**, and **pillars/stiffeners**, designed to withstand combined bending, shear, torsion, and local patch loading under the worst expected seagoing condition over a 25-year fatigue life.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Floating Skyscraper

Imagine a 300-metre long building floating sideways on water. The ship's hull is like a **sealed horizontal skyscraper** whose "walls" (shell plating) must resist water pressure from outside and cargo pressure from inside. Inside this skyscraper, you insert **vertical walls (bulkheads)** to divide it into watertight rooms — this prevents one big leak from sinking the whole ship. Now add a **backbone (keel and longitudinal girders)** along its bottom, a **spine (deck stringer)** along the top, and **ribs (frames and beams)** every metre. Together, these keep the long, hollow box from snapping in two under wave-induced bending. Stability, then, is whether this floating building returns upright when tilted by wind/waves — like a tumbler toy that rights itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Synergy:** Strength (resistance to breaking) and Stability (resistance to capsizing) are **independent** engineering requirements. A ship can be structurally strong but unstable (rolls over), or stable but weak (breaks apart). Both must be satisfied simultaneously — this is the central theme of Module 3.

---

## 1.2 Ship Stability — The Equilibrium Concept

**Ship stability** is the ability of a floating vessel to return to its original upright position when disturbed by external heeling moments (wind, wave, turning, cargo shift). It is a *rotational equilibrium* problem governed by the relative positions of three points: **Centre of Gravity (G)**, **Centre of Buoyancy (B)**, and **Metacentre (M)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *Transverse stability* is the ability of a ship to resist heeling in the transverse (athwartships) plane and to develop a **righting moment** when inclined to small angles of heel. The fundamental measure is the **Metacentric Height (GM)** — the distance from the centre of gravity to the transverse metacentre, measured in **metres**.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Tumbler & The Broomstick

Take a heavy-bottomed tumbler glass and push it sideways — it pops back up because its **Centre of Gravity is below the pivot**. This is **positive stability** (G below M). Now take a broomstick and try balancing it on your palm — its G is way above the pivot, so the slightest push causes it to topple. That is **negative or neutral stability**. A ship must always behave like the tumbler, not the broomstick — and the taller the "metacentric height", the more aggressively it rights itself (but also, the more uncomfortable the roll!).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of Stability:**
> 1. **Initial Stability** (small angles, φ < 10°–15°) — governed by GM.
> 2. **Large Angle Stability** (φ up to 30°–40°) — governed by GZ curve.
> 3. **Ultimate / Range of Stability** — governed by angle of vanishing stability (φᵥ) and area under the GZ curve (IMO Weather Criterion).
</br>

---

## 1.3 Marine Structural Components — The Vocabulary

| Component | Function | Location |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| **Shell Plating** | Watertight outer skin | Hull exterior |
| **Keel** | Longitudinal backbone | Bottom centreline |
| **Frames** | Transverse ribs | Every 0.5–1.0 m |
| **Longitudinals** | Longitudinal stiffeners | Run fore-aft |
| **Decks** | Horizontal watertight platforms | Across hull |
| **Bulkheads** | Vertical watertight walls | Transverse or longitudinal |
| **Double Bottom** | Tank + structural redundancy | Below inner bottom |
| **Stringers / Girders** | Longitudinal stiffening decks/sides | Topside and bilge |
| **Pillars / Stanchions** | Vertical supports under decks | Between deck and tank top |
| **Superstructure** | Deckhouses above freeboard deck | Upper decks |

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic is High-Yield for KTU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Module 3 carries **direct 14-mark questions** in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) under Course Outcome **CO3**: *"Apply principles of marine structural design and ship stability to evaluate seaworthiness and survivability."* A typical question combines a stability numerical with a structural description, worth **14 marks** with internal choice.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Ship Structural Design Philosophy — Three-Tier Load Path

The structural hierarchy of a ship follows a **hierarchical load transfer path**:

**Tier 1 — Local Loads → Tier 2 — Global Loads → Tier 3 — Hull Girder Loads**

1. **Local Loads** (Hydrostatic pressure, cargo pressure, tank pressure)
   - Resisted by **plating panels** spanning between stiffeners.
2. **Secondary Loads** (Still-water bending, wave bending)
   - Resisted by **transverse frames + longitudinal stiffeners**, then transferred to **transverse webs / girders**.
3. **Hull Girder Loads** (Overall bending moment & shear force)
   - Resisted by the **hull girder** as a hollow box beam, with deck plating/side shell/bottom shell as flanges and webs.

### Why Three Tiers?

- Optimisation: Local plates need thickness ~10 mm; main hull flange plates need thickness ~30–40 mm. Designing every plate to hull-girder strength would be wasteful.
- **Stiffener spacing** (s) is chosen such that plate slenderness $\beta = \dfrac{s}{t}\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}} \le 2.0$ (for yielding) and $\le 3.0$ (for buckling), where $\sigma_y$ = yield stress (**typically 235 MPa for mild steel, 315 MPa for HTS**).

---

## 2.2 Transverse Stability — Theoretical Foundation

When a ship heels by angle $\phi$:

- The **centre of buoyancy (B) shifts** to B' because the submerged volume wedge on one side increases.
- The line of action of buoyancy (upward through B') intersects the original vertical centreline at a fixed point **M** (metacentre) for *small angles*.
- The horizontal distance between B and the vertical through B' is called **BM (metacentric radius)**.
- **GM = KB + BM − KG** (where KG is height of G above keel, KB is height of B above keel).

### The Righting Couple

For an inclining moment (e.g., wind heeling moment), the ship heels until:
$$\text{Heeling Moment} = \text{Righting Moment}$$

$$\text{Righting Lever (GZ)} = \text{GM} \cdot \sin\phi \quad (\text{for small } \phi)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GZ is the heart of KTU stability problems.** A KTU question typically asks: *"Calculate the GZ at 30° heel for a vessel with KG = 5.5 m, KM = 7.2 m, and displacement = 12,000 tonnes."* You compute GZ = (KM − KG) sin φ = 1.7 × sin 30° = **0.85 m**, then check against IMO minimum standards.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> **This table is the single most important revision tool for Module 3.** All numerical questions in the ESE will use these formulas. Memorise the units and the assumptions.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Application |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$ | Displacement = density × volume displaced | Finding Δ from V |
| 2 | $\text{Waterplane Area } A_W = L \times B \times C_W$ | Area of waterplane | For BM calculation |
| 3 | $BM_T = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla} = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12 \nabla}$ | Transverse BM (rectangular) | Initial stability |
| 4 | $BM_L = \dfrac{I_L}{\nabla} = \dfrac{B \cdot L^3}{12 \nabla}$ | Longitudinal BM (roll period) | Rare in ESE |
| 5 | $KM_T = KB + BM_T$ | Height of metacentre | Combined stability |
| 6 | $GM_T = KM_T - KG$ | **Metacentric Height (KEY)** | Righting lever |
| 7 | $GZ = GM_T \cdot \sin\phi$ | Righting lever at small φ | φ < 10° |
| 8 | $GZ = \text{Method of wedges (large angles)}$ | $GZ = \sin\phi \left(KB + \dfrac{v \cdot OB_1 - v' \cdot OB_2}{\nabla}\right)$ | φ > 10° |
| 9 | $\text{Righting Moment} = W \cdot GZ$ | Restoring couple (W = Δ·g) | Equilibrium with heeling |
| 10 | $\text{Free Surface Correction: } GG' = \dfrac{i \cdot \rho_{ll}}{\Delta}$ | Virtual rise of G due to slack tanks | Always subtract |
| 11 | $\text{Period of Roll: } T_R = \dfrac{2 \pi \cdot k}{\sqrt{GM}}$ | Natural roll period (sec) | Comfort check |
| 12 | $l_{sway} = \dfrac{\sum i_x}{\Delta}$ | Free surface moment of inertia | Tank slack effect |
| 13 | $M_{heel} = F \cdot l_{lever}$ | Heeling moment (wind, turn) | Stability check |
| 14 | $\text{Wind Heeling Lever: } l_w = \dfrac{P \cdot A \cdot L_{lever}}{\Delta \cdot g}$ | IMO weather criterion | SOLAS Ch. II-1 |
| 15 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Hull girder bending stress | Structural strength |

### Key Engineering Constants for Numerical Problems

| Quantity | Value (Default) | Unit |
|----------|-----------------|------|
| Density of seawater ($\rho_{sw}$) | **1.025** | t/m³ |
| Density of freshwater ($\rho_{fw}$) | **1.000** | t/m³ |
| Acceleration due to gravity ($g$) | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Specific weight of seawater ($\gamma$) | **10.05** | kN/m³ |
| Minimum GM (passenger ship) | **0.15** | m |
| Minimum GM (cargo ship, IMO intact) | **0.15** | m |
| Minimum area under GZ to 30° | **0.055** | m·rad |
| Minimum area from 30° to 40° | **0.030** | m·rad |
| Max GZ angle | **≥ 25°** | degrees |
| Wind heeling lever $l_w$ (typical) | **0.05–0.10** | m |
| Bilge keel lever arm | **0.30–0.55** | m |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Using $\rho = 1.0$ instead of $1.025$ for seawater. This gives ~2.5% error in displacement. KTU expects you to use **1.025 t/m³** unless freshwater is explicitly stated.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Ship Design Office** | Setting KG limits, GM range criteria (0.15 m ≤ GM ≤ 0.30 m typical) |
| **Loading Computer** | Real-time GM calculation for Master to ensure safety |
| **Classification Society (IRS, DNV, LR, BV)** | Approval of Stability Booklet |
| **Port State Control** | Verifying intact stability compliance |
| **Accident Investigation** | Determining cause of capsize (e.g., Prestige, MOL Comfort) |
| **Warship Design** | Damage stability post-missile hit (flooded compartments) |

---

## 2.5 Free Surface Effect — The Silent Killer

When a partially-filled tank contains liquid that can shift freely (slack tank), the liquid surface remains horizontal as the ship heels. This causes an **apparent shift of G upward** — effectively *reducing GM*. The correction is:

$$GM_{eff} = GM - GG' = GM - \dfrac{i_{free}}{\Delta}$$

where $i_{free}$ = free surface moment of inertia of the slack tank (about its longitudinal centreline for transverse stability).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** A ship with adequate GM can become **negatively stable** if several slack tanks are present. The KTU 2024 syllabus places Free Surface Effect as a **3-mark short-answer topic** within Module 3.

---

## 2.6 IMO Intact Stability Code (IS Code) — The Benchmark

The **International Maritime Organization (IMO) Code on Intact Stability for All Types of Ships Covered by IMO Instruments (Resolution A.749(18))** sets five mandatory criteria:

1. **Area Criterion**: Area under GZ curve from 0° to 30° ≥ **0.055 m·rad**
2. **Area Criterion**: Area from 30° to 40° (or φᵥ if < 40°) ≥ **0.030 m·rad**
3. **Area Criterion**: Total area from 0° to 40° (or φᵥ) ≥ **0.090 m·rad**
4. **Righting Lever Criterion**: GZ max ≥ **0.20 m** at angle ≥ **30°**
5. **Angle Criterion**: φᵥ (angle of vanishing stability) ≥ **30°** (passenger ships) or **≥ 25°** (depending on ship type)
6. **Weather Criterion**: Area b / Area a ≥ 1.0 (see detailed IMO graph)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU questions often quote one criterion and ask: *"Does the ship comply?"* You must check the relevant criterion against the GZ curve values provided in the question data table.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Metacentric Radius (BM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **fundamental derivation** of Module 3. Master this — KTU repeats similar questions in every ESE cycle with different numerical values.

### Starting Principle: Archimedes & Small Angle Geometry

Consider a floating ship in still water with submerged volume $\nabla$ and waterplane area $A_W$. When the ship heels by a small angle $\phi$:

**Step 1:** Identify the wedge mechanics.  
The change in buoyancy is the difference between the volume of the *emerged wedge* and the *immersed wedge*. For small $\phi$, the emerged wedge has volume $v$ and the immersed wedge has volume $v'$, both located at distances $OB_1$ and $OB_2$ from the ship's centreline.

**Step 2:** Apply the principle of moments.  
Taking moments about the original centreline at the original waterline:
- Moment of lost buoyancy on emerged wedge: $-v \cdot OB_1$ (counter-clockwise, negative)
- Moment of gained buoyancy on immersed wedge: $+v' \cdot OB_2$ (clockwise, positive)

The **new centre of buoyancy** B' lies on the line of action of total buoyancy, intersecting the original vertical through B at point M. The transverse shift $BB_1 = BM \cdot \sin\phi$.

**Step 3:** Equate the moments for equilibrium.  
By the principle of buoyancy (the centre of buoyancy must be such that the moment of displaced volume equals the moment of the moment-arm shift):

$$\nabla \cdot BM \sin\phi = v \cdot OB_1 + v' \cdot OB_2$$

Wait — the standard derivation uses:

$$\nabla \cdot BM \sin\phi = \text{(moment of shift of buoyancy)} = v \cdot \dfrac{L \cdot B}{12 \cdot \text{stuff}}$$

Let us do it cleanly for a rectangular waterplane of length L and breadth B.

**Step 4:** Wedge volume for rectangular waterplane.

For a ship with rectangular waterplane of length $L$ and beam $B$, when heeled by $\phi$:

The emerged wedge is a triangular prism of base $OB_1 = \dfrac{B}{2}$ and height $OB_1 \tan\phi \approx OB_1 \cdot \phi$:

$$v = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{B}{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{B}{2}\right)\phi \cdot L = \dfrac{L B^2 \phi}{8}$$

The immersed wedge (opposite side) is identical by symmetry:

$$v' = v = \dfrac{L B^2 \phi}{8}$$

The centroid of each wedge lies at distance $\dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{B}{2} = \dfrac{B}{3}$ from the centreline.

**Step 5:** Moment about the original centreline.

Total moment of buoyancy transfer:
$$\nabla \cdot BM \sin\phi = v \cdot \dfrac{B}{3} + v' \cdot \dfrac{B}{3} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{L B^2 \phi}{8} \cdot \dfrac{B}{3} = \dfrac{L B^3 \phi}{12}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for BM.

Since $\sin\phi \approx \phi$ for small angles:

$$\nabla \cdot BM = \dfrac{L B^3}{12}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{BM = \dfrac{L B^3}{12 \nabla} = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}}$$

where $I_T = \dfrac{L B^3}{12}$ is the second moment of area of the waterplane about the longitudinal centreline.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Variant:** *"A ship has a waterplane moment of inertia $I_T = 4.5 \times 10^5$ m⁴ and a displacement volume of 45,000 m³. Find BM."* → BM = 4.5 × 10⁵ / 45,000 = **10 m**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Righting Lever (GZ) for Small Angles

**Given:** A ship with known KG, KB, and BM is heeled through angle $\phi$.

**Geometry:**

In the upright position, B is directly below G. When the ship heels, the new buoyancy line passes through B' and M (metacentre). The triangle formed by G, B' and the original centreline gives:

The horizontal shift of B from the vertical through G is:
$$GG \cdot \sin\phi = GM \cdot \sin\phi \approx GZ$$

For the righting moment to balance the heeling moment:

$$\text{Righting Lever: } GZ = GM \sin\phi$$

This holds for **small angles only** (φ < 10°–15°), where the metacentre M can be assumed fixed.

For **large angles**, the metacentre moves, and we must use the **Wall-sided formula** or the **direct integration method** (method of wedges).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — KTU Standard Style Problem

### Problem Statement (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

> A box-shaped vessel has the following particulars:
> - Length between perpendiculars, $L = 120$ m
> - Breadth, $B = 20$ m
> - Mean draft, $T = 8$ m$
> - Block coefficient, $C_B = 0.65$
> - KG (height of G above keel) = 6.0 m
> - A slack freshwater tank has free surface moment of inertia $i = 12,000$ m⁴ and is 80% filled.
> - Density of seawater = 1.025 t/m³
>
> **Calculate:**
> (a) Initial metacentric height GM (with free surface correction).
> (b) Righting moment when the ship heels to 10°.
> (c) Comment on compliance with IMO intact stability criterion (GZ max ≥ 0.20 m).

### Part (a) — Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the displaced volume.

$$\nabla = L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B = 120 \times 20 \times 8 \times 0.65$$

$$\nabla = 12,480 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Compute displacement.

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \times 12,480 = 12,792 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 3:** Compute BM using the rectangular approximation (acceptable for box-shaped ship).

For a ship with rectangular waterplane:
$$BM_T = \dfrac{L B^3}{12 \nabla}$$

$$BM_T = \dfrac{120 \times (20)^3}{12 \times 12,480} = \dfrac{120 \times 8000}{149,760}$$

$$BM_T = \dfrac{960,000}{149,760} = 6.410 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Compute KB for a box-shaped ship (centre of buoyancy at half-draft).

$$KB = \dfrac{T}{2} = \dfrac{8}{2} = 4.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** Compute KM.

$$KM = KB + BM = 4.0 + 6.410 = 10.410 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6:** Compute uncorrected GM.

$$GM = KM - KG = 10.410 - 6.0 = 4.410 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7:** Apply free surface correction GG'.

Since the tank is 80% filled, it is a slack tank and the free surface effect applies. The transverse moment of inertia of the free surface about the longitudinal centreline is given directly as $i = 12,000 \text{ m}^4$ (note: this would normally need to be summed across all slack tanks; here only one is given).

$$GG' = \dfrac{i}{\nabla} = \dfrac{12,000}{12,480} = 0.9615 \text{ m}$$

**Step 8:** Corrected GM.

$$GM_{corrected} = GM - GG' = 4.410 - 0.9615 = 3.449 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{GM_{corrected} \approx 3.45 \text{ m}}$$

### Part (b) — Righting Moment at 10° Heel

**Step 9:** Apply the small-angle formula.

$$GZ = GM \cdot \sin\phi = 3.449 \times \sin(10°)$$

$$GZ = 3.449 \times 0.17365 = 0.5988 \text{ m}$$

**Step 10:** Compute righting moment.

$$RM = W \cdot GZ = (\Delta \cdot g) \cdot GZ = 12,792 \times 9.81 \times 0.5988$$

$$RM = 75,167 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$\boxed{RM \approx 75.17 \text{ MN·m}}$$

**Alternative form (more common in KTU):**

If the question asks for the righting lever only (which is more typical):

$$\boxed{GZ_{10°} \approx 0.60 \text{ m}}$$

### Part (c) — IMO Compliance Check

At 10° heel, $GZ_{max} = 0.60 \text{ m}$. This satisfies $GZ_{max} \geq 0.20 \text{ m}$ — **PASS** the GZ max criterion.

However, this is only the 10° value; the IMO criterion requires $GZ_{max} \geq 0.20 \text{ m}$ at angle ≥ 30°. At small angles we use the small-angle approximation, which over-predicts GZ. For full IMO verification, we need the complete GZ curve up to 40° — typically provided in the question data table for higher marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification Insight:** A GM of 3.45 m is **very stiff** — the ship will be uncomfortable to crew, with rapid roll. Typical cargo ships operate with GM = 0.15 to 1.5 m. A box-shaped vessel calculation often overestimates GM; in reality the form coefficient and waterplane shape reduce BM by 5–15%.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Stability Concepts

| Stability Type | Definition | Governing Parameter | Formula | Practical Range |
|---------------|-----------|---------------------|---------|-----------------|
| **Initial (φ < 10°)** | Ship returns upright | GM | $GZ = GM\sin\phi$ | GM = 0.15–1.5 m |
| **Large Angle (φ = 10°–40°)** | Maximum GZ developed | Righting arm curve | Method of wedges | GZ_max = 0.2–1.0 m |
| **Ultimate (φᵥ)** | Stability vanishes | Angle of vanishing | Wall-sided formula | φᵥ = 60°–90° |
| **Dynamic (Area under GZ)** | Energy to capsize | Area in m·rad | Numerical integration | ≥ 0.090 m·rad (IMO) |
| **Free Surface** | Effect of slack tanks | GG' shift | $GG' = i/\Delta$ | GG' < 0.05 m typical |
| **Longitudinal** | Trim stability | GML | $GML = \dfrac{I_L}{\nabla} - KG$ | GML = 100–300 m |

---

## 3.5 Hull Girder Bending Stress — Brief Structural Derivation

The ship's hull is modelled as a **box girder** with:
- **Top flange**: Strength deck plating + upper side shell
- **Bottom flange**: Bottom shell plating
- **Web**: Side shell plating

The bending stress at any vertical level $y$ from the neutral axis is:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I_{girder}}$$

where:
- $M$ = bending moment (still-water + wave-induced)
- $I_{girder}$ = second moment of area of hull cross-section about neutral axis
- $y$ = distance from neutral axis

The maximum allowable bending stress is typically $\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{1.6}$ for combined loading (per IACS Common Structural Rules).

---

## 3.6 Detailed Step-by-Step — Computing Large-Angle GZ (Method of Wedges)

This is a **frequently asked 14-mark problem** in KTU. Be ready to demonstrate.

**Step 1:** For heel angle φ, divide the underwater hull into **N transverse strips** of width $\delta x$.

**Step 2:** For each strip, calculate the original submerged area $A_i$ at the upright condition.

**Step 3:** After heeling by φ, each strip's submerged area changes:
- Emerged wedge of strip $i$: $\Delta A_i = \dfrac{1}{2} y_i^2 \tan\phi$ (subtract)
- Immersed wedge of strip $i$: $\Delta A_i = \dfrac{1}{2} y_i^2 \tan\phi$ (add)

where $y_i$ is the half-beam at that station.

**Step 4:** New B' is located by:
- $\overline{x_{B'}} = \dfrac{\sum (A_i + \Delta A_i) \cdot x_i}{\sum (A_i + \Delta A_i)}$
- $\overline{z_{B'}} = \dfrac{\sum (A_i + \Delta A_i) \cdot z_i}{\sum (A_i + \Delta A_i)}$

**Step 5:** The righting lever is:

$$GZ = KB' \sin\phi - KG \sin\phi + \text{(lateral shift correction)}$$

Simplified:
$$GZ = (\overline{KB'} - KG)\sin\phi + \text{lateral shift}$$

**Step 6:** Plot GZ vs φ to obtain the **GZ curve** and compute areas under it (Simpson's rule or trapezoidal integration).

> [!TIP]
> **Simpson's 1/3 Rule for area under GZ curve:**
> $$\text{Area} = \dfrac{h}{3}\left[y_0 + y_n + 4(y_1 + y_3 + ...) + 2(y_2 + y_4 + ...)\right]$$
> where $h$ = angle interval and $y_i$ = GZ values at stations.

---

## 3.7 Free Surface Effect — Detailed Correction Workflow

**Case A — Tank less than 98% full or with air pocket:** Slack → Apply correction.
**Case B — Tank 98%–100% full:** Nominally full → Marginal correction.
**Case C — Tank 100% full (pressed up):** No correction.

The free surface moment of inertia $i$ about the tank's longitudinal centreline is calculated assuming a rectangular free surface of length $l$ and breadth $b$:

$$i = \dfrac{l \cdot b^3}{12}$$

For non-rectangular tanks, $i$ is given in the stability booklet. Sum across all slack tanks:

$$GG'_{total} = \dfrac{\sum i_j}{\nabla}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trick:** If a question lists 4 tanks and 3 are slack, you must sum the $i$ values of only the 3 slack tanks. **Common mistake:** Including the pressed-up tank in the sum.
