# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define metacentric height (GM) and explain its significance in ship stability.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Metacentric height (GM) is the vertical distance between the **Centre of Gravity (G)** of a floating vessel and the **transverse metacentre (M)**, where M is the point of intersection of the vertical line through the new centre of buoyancy with the original vertical centreline when the ship is inclined to a small angle.

**Significance:**
1. **Measure of Initial Stability:** A positive GM (G below M) ensures the ship develops a righting moment and returns to upright. A negative GM means the ship will capsize.
2. **Magnitude of Righting Lever:** At small angles, $GZ \approx GM \sin\phi$, so a larger GM produces a larger righting moment for the same heel angle.
3. **Period of Roll:** $T_R = \dfrac{2\pi k}{\sqrt{GM}}$ — larger GM means shorter (faster) roll period, which is uncomfortable for crew and cargo.
4. **Design Criterion:** Classification societies require a **minimum GM of 0.15 m** for intact stability. IMO also specifies a **maximum acceptable GM** to avoid excessive stiff rolling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip (3 marks):** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for significance (any one use), 1 mark for typical numerical value/range.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is free surface effect? How does it affect ship stability?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Free surface effect** is the reduction in the effective metacentric height (GM) of a ship caused by the shifting of liquid inside a partially-filled (slack) tank as the ship heels.

**Mechanism:** When a ship rolls, the liquid in a slack tank maintains a horizontal surface. The centre of gravity of the liquid shifts toward the low side, creating an effective upward shift of the ship's overall Centre of Gravity by a distance $GG'$.

**Formula:**
$$GG' = \dfrac{i_{free}}{\nabla}$$
where $i_{free}$ = transverse free surface moment of inertia of the slack tank (about its longitudinal centreline), and $\nabla$ = displaced volume.

**Effect on Stability:**
- Reduces $GM_{eff} = GM - GG'$.
- Excessive slack tanks can reduce GM to negative, making the ship unstable.
- **Precaution:** Slack tanks should be pressed up (>98% full) or emptied before sailing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write only the formula without explaining *why* the surface remains horizontal. Examiners deduct 1 mark if the mechanism is missing.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Both choices below are full 14-mark models. Each contains sub-parts (a) and (b), 7 marks each.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Statement:**
A bulk carrier has the following particulars:
- Length, L = 150 m
- Breadth, B = 22 m
- Draft, T = 9 m
- Block coefficient, $C_B = 0.70$
- KG = 7.5 m above keel
- Three slack double-bottom tanks, each of length 30 m and breadth 11 m
- Sea water density = 1.025 t/m³

**Calculate:**
**(a)** The initial metacentric height GM, before and after applying the free surface correction. (7 marks)

**(b)** The righting moment and the corresponding righting lever at 8° heel. State whether the ship complies with the IMO criterion of minimum GZ = 0.20 m. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

##### Part (a) — Initial Metacentric Height (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Compute displaced volume $\nabla$.

$$\nabla = L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B = 150 \times 22 \times 9 \times 0.70$$

$$\nabla = 20,790 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Compute BM using the rectangular waterplane approximation.

$$BM = \dfrac{L B^3}{12 \nabla} = \dfrac{150 \times (22)^3}{12 \times 20,790}$$

$$BM = \dfrac{150 \times 10,648}{249,480} = \dfrac{1,597,200}{249,480} = 6.402 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Compute KB.

For a box-shaped approximation:
$$KB = \dfrac{T}{2} = \dfrac{9}{2} = 4.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Compute uncorrected KM and GM.

$$KM = KB + BM = 4.5 + 6.402 = 10.902 \text{ m}$$

$$GM_{uncorrected} = KM - KG = 10.902 - 7.5 = 3.402 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** Compute free surface correction.

For each slack tank (rectangular free surface of l = 30 m, b = 11 m):

$$i_{tank} = \dfrac{l b^3}{12} = \dfrac{30 \times (11)^3}{12} = \dfrac{30 \times 1331}{12} = 3327.5 \text{ m}^4$$

Total for 3 tanks:

$$\sum i = 3 \times 3327.5 = 9982.5 \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 6:** Compute GG'.

$$GG' = \dfrac{\sum i}{\nabla} = \dfrac{9982.5}{20,790} = 0.4802 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7:** Corrected GM.

$$GM_{corrected} = 3.402 - 0.480 = 2.922 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{GM_{uncorrected} = 3.40 \text{ m} \quad ; \quad GM_{corrected} = 2.92 \text{ m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Allocation of 7 marks:**
> - Step 1 (∇ calculation): 1 mark
> - Step 2 (BM): 2 marks
> - Step 3–4 (KM, GM uncorrected): 1 mark
> - Step 5–6 (Free surface correction): 2 marks
> - Step 7 (Final answer): 1 mark

---

##### Part (b) — Righting Moment at 8° (7 marks)

**Step 8:** Apply small-angle formula for GZ at 8°.

$$GZ_{8°} = GM_{corrected} \cdot \sin(8°) = 2.922 \times 0.1392$$

$$GZ_{8°} = 0.4067 \text{ m}$$

**Step 9:** Compute displacement.

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \times 20,790 = 21,310 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 10:** Compute weight.

$$W = \Delta \cdot g = 21,310 \times 9.81 = 209,051 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 11:** Compute righting moment.

$$RM = W \cdot GZ = 209,051 \times 0.4067$$

$$RM = 85,019 \text{ kN·m} \approx 85.02 \text{ MN·m}$$

**Step 12:** IMO Criterion Check.

The question specifies "minimum GZ = 0.20 m". Since $GZ_{8°} = 0.407$ m, the criterion is **satisfied at 8°**. However, the IMO criterion requires GZ ≥ 0.20 m at an angle ≥ 30°. At 8°, the small-angle approximation overestimates GZ. For full compliance, the GZ curve at 30° must be ≥ 0.20 m, which cannot be confirmed without further data.

**Conclusion:** The ship has **positive stability** at 8° heel, with a comfortable righting moment. Full IMO compliance requires a complete GZ curve analysis.

$$\boxed{GZ_{8°} \approx 0.41 \text{ m}, \quad RM \approx 85.0 \text{ MN·m}, \quad \text{Stable at 8° heel.}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Warning — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting g in RM:** Writing "RM = Δ · GZ" instead of "RM = Δ · g · GZ" costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Using uncorrected GM in Part (b):** The question implicitly requires using the *corrected* GM. Marks lost if uncorrected value is used.
> 3. **Mixing up units:** GZ in metres must be multiplied by weight in kN to give kN·m. Writing "tonne·m" is wrong dimensionally.

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks — Alternate Choice) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Statement:**
**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **structural framing system** of a modern container ship. Differentiate between **transverse framing** and **longitudinal framing** systems, and state the merits and demerits of each. (7 marks)

**(b)** A double-bottomed ship is 100 m long and 16 m wide. The displacement is 8000 tonnes at a draft of 6 m in seawater (1.025 t/m³). The height of the centre of gravity above keel is 5.0 m. The double-bottom tanks are partially filled, with a total free surface moment of inertia of 800 m⁴.

**(i)** Calculate the **effective metacentric height** after free surface correction. (4 marks)
**(ii)** If the ship heels to 6°, find the **righting lever (GZ)** and **righting moment**. (3 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

##### Part (a) — Structural Framing Systems (7 marks)

**Framing System Definition:** The arrangement of stiffeners (frames, beams, longitudinals) supporting the plating of a ship hull against local, secondary, and primary loads.

**Two Principal Systems:**

**1. Transverse Framing System:**
- Frames are spaced transversely (athwartships), typically every 600–900 mm.
- Longitudinals are eliminated; plating is supported directly by frames.
- Frames are supported by transverse web frames spaced every 3–5 m.
- **Merits:**
  - Simpler construction.
  - Better access for inspection and coating.
  - Better suited for small ships, fishing vessels, and tankers (where transverse loads dominate).
- **Demerits:**
  - Less efficient material usage for large ships.
  - Lower hull girder section modulus.
  - Heavier for equivalent strength.

**2. Longitudinal Framing System:**
- Longitudinal stiffeners run fore-and-aft, spaced 300–900 mm apart.
- Supported by transverse web frames every 2.5–4 m.
- **Merits:**
  - Higher hull girder section modulus → better bending resistance.
  - Lower plate thickness required for the same strength.
  - Material saving of 10–20% over transverse framing for large ships.
  - Mandatory for oil tankers > 150 m per SOLAS.
- **Demerits:**
  - Complex construction; more welds.
  - Difficult to inspect internal spaces.
  - More expensive fabrication.

**Modern Container Ships:** Use a **mixed system** — longitudinal framing in the deck, side shell, and bottom shell (where bending stresses are highest), and transverse framing in the bow and stern (where slamming and shear loads dominate).

| Parameter | Transverse Framing | Longitudinal Framing |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Frame spacing | 600–900 mm | 300–900 mm |
| Section modulus | Lower | Higher |
| Material efficiency | Poor | Excellent |
| Use | Tanker, small ships | Bulk carrier, container ship |
| IACS requirement | Optional | Mandatory for tankers > 150 m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Sketch (verbal):** Draw a cross-section of the ship hull showing — top: deck plating with longitudinals running fore-aft; sides: side shell with longitudinals; bottom: bottom shell with longitudinals and floors/girders running transversely; frames (transverse) connecting the side and bottom stiffeners at regular intervals.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students write only the definitions but do not compare merits/demerits. This costs 2 marks in 7-mark questions.

---

##### Part (b)(i) — Effective GM (4 marks)

**Step 1:** Compute displaced volume $\nabla$.

$$\nabla = \dfrac{\Delta}{\rho} = \dfrac{8000}{1.025} = 7804.88 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Compute BM (rectangular waterplane).

$$BM = \dfrac{L B^3}{12 \nabla} = \dfrac{100 \times (16)^3}{12 \times 7804.88}$$

$$BM = \dfrac{100 \times 4096}{93,658.5} = \dfrac{409,600}{93,658.5} = 4.373 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Compute KB.

$$KB = \dfrac{T}{2} = \dfrac{6}{2} = 3.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Compute KM and uncorrected GM.

$$KM = KB + BM = 3.0 + 4.373 = 7.373 \text{ m}$$

$$GM_{uncorrected} = KM - KG = 7.373 - 5.0 = 2.373 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** Apply free surface correction.

$$GG' = \dfrac{i_{free}}{\nabla} = \dfrac{800}{7804.88} = 0.1025 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6:** Effective GM.

$$\boxed{GM_{eff} = 2.373 - 0.1025 = 2.270 \text{ m}}$$

---

##### Part (b)(ii) — GZ and Righting Moment at 6° (3 marks)

**Step 7:** GZ at 6°.

$$GZ_{6°} = GM_{eff} \cdot \sin(6°) = 2.270 \times 0.1045 = 0.2373 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{GZ_{6°} \approx 0.24 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 8:** Weight.

$$W = \Delta \cdot g = 8000 \times 9.81 = 78,480 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 9:** Righting moment.

$$RM = W \cdot GZ = 78,480 \times 0.2373$$

$$\boxed{RM = 18,624 \text{ kN·m} \approx 18.6 \text{ MN·m}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 7 Mistakes Students Make in Module 3 Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** when computing moments of inertia ($I_T$ requires metres).
> 2. **Using freshwater density** when the question states seawater → 2.5% error.
> 3. **Ignoring free surface correction** even when slack tanks are mentioned. Examiner deducts 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Using wrong formula for BM** — confusing $BM_T = LB^3/(12\nabla)$ with $BM_L = BL^3/(12\nabla)$. (Transverse uses $B^3$, longitudinal uses $L^3$.)
> 5. **Mixing units in righting moment** — writing $RM = \Delta \cdot GZ$ without $g$. Examiner marks dimensional error.
> 6. **Not labelling diagrams** in structural questions (Part A type). Examiner deducts 1 mark for missing labels on components.
> 7. **Quoting GM in mm** instead of metres. Always quote GM in **metres**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 3 — Marine Structures and Stability**

### Stability (Core Concepts)
- ☐ **Centre of Buoyancy (B)** is the centroid of the displaced volume of water; it moves as the ship heels.
- ☐ **Metacentre (M)** is the intersection of the vertical through B' (new buoyancy centre) with the original vertical through B; M is **fixed for small angles (φ < 10°–15°)**.
- ☐ **Metacentric Height (GM)** = KM − KG; positive GM means the ship is stable.
- ☐ **Righting Lever (GZ)** = GM · sin φ (small angle); equals perpendicular distance from G to new buoyancy line.
- ☐ **Righting Moment** = Δ · g · GZ (in kN·m).
- ☐ **Free Surface Effect** reduces GM by $GG' = i/\nabla$; apply only to slack tanks (<98% full).
- ☐ **Minimum GM (IMO intact):** 0.15 m for cargo and passenger ships.
- ☐ **GZ max criterion:** ≥ 0.20 m at φ ≥ 30° (or 25° for some ship types).
- ☐ **Area criteria (IMO):** Area 0°–30° ≥ 0.055 m·rad; Area 30°–40° ≥ 0.030 m·rad; Total ≥ 0.090 m·rad.

### Structural (Core Concepts)
- ☐ Ship hull acts as a **box girder** with deck/bottom shell as flanges and side shell as webs.
- ☐ **Longitudinal framing** is more material-efficient for large ships; mandatory for tankers > 150 m per SOLAS.
- ☐ **Transverse framing** is simpler; suitable for small ships and where transverse loads dominate.
- ☐ **Hull girder bending stress**: $\sigma = M y / I_{girder}$; allowable stress $\sigma_y / 1.6$ for combined loading.
- ☐ **Double bottom** provides redundancy against grounding; also used for ballast.
- ☐ **Watertight bulkheads** prevent progressive flooding; number and location per SOLAS.
- ☐ **Pillars/stanchions** support deck loads on tank tops; prevent panel buckling.

### Key Numerical Constants
- ☐ Seawater density: **1.025 t/m³**
- ☐ Freshwater density: **1.000 t/m³**
- ☐ g = 9.81 m/s²
- ☐ For rectangular waterplane: $BM_T = LB^3/(12\nabla)$
- ☐ For rectangular waterplane: $KB = T/2$ (box-shaped assumption)

### Formulae to Memorise
- ☐ $GZ = GM \sin\phi$ (small angle)
- ☐ $GM_{eff} = GM - GG'$
- ☐ $GG' = i_{free} / \nabla$
- ☐ $RM = W \cdot GZ = \Delta g \cdot GZ$
- ☐ $T_{roll} = 2\pi k / \sqrt{GM}$
- ☐ $\sigma = M y / I$
- ☐ $I_T = LB^3 / 12$ (rectangular waterplane)

### Common Exam Triggers
- ☐ "With free surface correction" → Always apply $GG'$.
- ☐ "Show compliance with IMO" → Check GZ max, area criteria.
- ☐ "Differentiate longitudinal and transverse framing" → Use the comparison table.
- ☐ "Define GM" → Definition + significance + numerical value.
- ☐ "At what angle does the ship capsize?" → Angle of vanishing stability φᵥ.

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — MARINE STRUCTURES AND STABILITY**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Buoyancy, Stability, and Trim
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Buoyancy, Stability, and Trim

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Buoyancy — Archimedes' Principle

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Buoyancy** is the upward vertical force exerted by a fluid on a body partially or fully immersed in it. According to **Archimedes' Principle**, this buoyant force is equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body.

Mathematically:

$$F_b = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$$

where:
- $F_b$ = Buoyant Force (Newtons, **N**)
- $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid (kg/m³); for seawater $\rho \approx \mathbf{1025\ kg/m^3}$
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $\nabla$ = Submerged volume of the body (m³)

> [!NOTE]
> In naval architecture, the volume $\nabla$ is often expressed as **m³** and the resulting weight of displaced water ($\rho g \nabla$) is called the **Displacement ($\Delta$)**. A floating ship is in equilibrium when **Weight (W) = Displacement ($\Delta$)**.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Cork in Water

Imagine pushing a cork under water and letting it go. The cork shoots upward because the water pushes it up with a force greater than the cork's weight. Now think of a large ship floating at rest — the steel hull weighs thousands of tonnes, yet it floats. Why? Because the hull is shaped like a hollow bowl, displacing an enormous volume of water. The **upward push of the displaced water exactly balances the downward weight of the ship**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For a freely floating ship, the upward buoyant force equals the downward weight.** This is the **Principle of Flotation** — a direct consequence of Archimedes' Principle applied to floating bodies.

---

### 1.3 Stability — Definition

**Stability of a floating body** is its ability to return to the upright position when tilted by an external disturbance (wind, waves, shifting cargo, etc.).

There are **two types** of ship stability:

| Type | Plane of Rotation | Pivot Point | Practical Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Transverse Stability** | About the longitudinal (fore-aft) axis | Center of Buoyancy (B) | Rolling motion, side wind heeling |
| **Longitudinal Stability** | About the transverse (port-starboard) axis | Center of Flotation (F) | Fore-aft motion, pitching, **trim** |

---

### 1.4 Trim — Definition

> **Trim** is the longitudinal inclination of a ship caused by unequal drafts forward and aft.

$$\text{Trim} = d_F - d_A$$

where:
- $d_F$ = Mean draft at the forward perpendicular (FP)
- $d_A$ = Mean draft at the aft perpendicular (AP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - If $d_F > d_A$: ship is **trimmed by the bow** (bow down)
> - If $d_A > d_F$: ship is **trimmed by the stern** (stern down)
> - If $d_F = d_A$: ship is **even keel** (no trim)

---

### 1.5 Key Reference Points on a Ship

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize these six points — they form the foundation of all ship stability problems.

| Point | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Keel** | **K** | Lowest point on the ship's hull at the centerline |
| **Center of Buoyancy** | **B** | Centroid of the displaced volume of water |
| **Center of Gravity** | **G** | Point where the total weight of the ship acts vertically downward |
| **Metacenter** | **M** | Point where the vertical line through the new (tilted) center of buoyancy meets the original vertical centerline |
| **Center of Flotation** | **F** | Centroid of the waterline plane area |
| **Longitudinal Metacenter** | **ML** | Metacenter for longitudinal (pitching) motion |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Does a Ship Roll? — The Role of the Metacenter

When a ship heels (tilts) by a small angle $\theta$, the underwater shape changes — the center of buoyancy **B shifts** to a new position **B'**. The vertical line through B' intersects the original centerline at a point **M** called the **Metacenter**.

The geometry is as follows:
- **K** (Keel) is fixed to the ship.
- **B** moves as the ship rolls.
- **G** (Center of Gravity) moves only if weight shifts (e.g., cargo move); usually treated as fixed for small angles.
- **M** depends only on the **shape of the underwater hull** and the **displacement** — not on the weight distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a ship to possess **positive (stable) equilibrium**, the metacenter **M must lie above the center of gravity G**, i.e., **GM > 0**.

---

### 2.2 Conditions of Equilibrium

| Condition | Position of M relative to G | Behavior when tilted | Practical State |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stable** | $M$ is **above** $G$ ($GM > 0$) | Restoring couple → returns to upright | Safe operating condition |
| **Neutral** | $M$ coincides with $G$ ($GM = 0$) | No restoring couple → stays tilted | Limiting condition |
| **Unstable** | $M$ is **below** $G$ ($GM < 0$) | Capsizing couple → further tilts | Capsize imminent |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the master equation bank for any buoyancy/stability/trim problem in the KTU ESE.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Buoyant Force | $F_b = \rho g \nabla$ | N (or tonnes-force) | Archimedes' Principle |
| 2 | Condition of Flotation | $W = \rho g \nabla$ | tonnes | Floating equilibrium |
| 3 | Displacement (mass form) | $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$ | tonnes | Using $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³ |
| 4 | Volume of Displacement | $\nabla = \dfrac{\Delta}{\rho}$ | m³ | For numerical problems |
| 5 | **Transverse Metacentric Radius** | $BM_T = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}$ | m | $I_T$ = 2nd moment of WL area about CL |
| 6 | **Longitudinal Metacentric Radius** | $BM_L = \dfrac{I_L}{\nabla}$ | m | $I_L$ is much larger → $GML$ is much larger |
| 7 | Transverse Metacentric Height | $GM_T = KB + BM_T - KG$ | m | Most used stability index |
| 8 | Longitudinal Metacentric Height | $GML = KB + BM_L - KG$ | m | For trim calculations |
| 9 | Righting Lever (small angle) | $GZ = GM \cdot \sin\theta \approx GM \cdot \theta$ | m | Small-angle approximation |
| 10 | Righting Moment | $RM = W \cdot GZ = \Delta \cdot g \cdot GM \cdot \theta$ | kN·m | Couple resisting heel |
| 11 | **Trim** | $T = d_F - d_A$ | m | Positive = bow down |
| 12 | **Moment to Change Trim by 1 cm** | $MCTC = \dfrac{W \cdot GML}{100 \cdot L}$ | t·m/cm | Used in practical trim tables |
| 13 | Change in Trim | $\Delta T = \dfrac{\text{Trimming Moment}}{MCTC}$ | cm or m | Sign depends on direction |
| 14 | Draft Change at any point | $\Delta d_x = \dfrac{\Delta T}{2} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{2x}{L}\right) + \dfrac{\Delta d_{mean}}{1}$ | m | $x$ measured from midship |
| 15 | Parallel Sinkage | $\Delta d_{mean} = \dfrac{w}{\text{TPA}}$ | m | $w$ = added weight; TPA = tonnes per cm immersion |

> [!WARNING]
> Always check **units**. Displacement can be in tonnes (force) or tonnes (mass). In naval architecture, the convention is **tonnes (mass)** where 1 tonne mass weighs 1 tonne-force (using $g = 9.81$ m/s² but absorbed in tonne as a mass unit).

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Loading Computers on Ships:** Use these formulas in real time to compute GM, trim, and righting moments as cargo is loaded/unloaded.
- **Damage Stability:** After hull breach, the lost buoyancy is modeled using displaced volumes and new B/G positions.
- **Submarine Operations:** Submerging requires precise balance between **reserve buoyancy** (positive when surfaced) and **negative** when submerged.
- **Offshore Platforms:** Semi-submersibles use the same metacentric height principles for stability against wave-induced heeling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Transverse Metacentric Radius $BM_T$

**Setup:** Consider a rectangular pontoon of length $L$, breadth $B$, floating at waterline area $A = L \cdot B$. Heel by a small angle $\theta$ about the longitudinal axis through the center of flotation F.

#### Step 1 — Identify the wedge volumes

When the ship heels by angle $\theta$, the wedge **immersed** on one side has volume:

$$v_1 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{B}{2}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{B}{2} \tan\theta\right) \cdot L = \frac{L B^2}{8} \tan\theta$$

The wedge **emerged** on the opposite side has the **same volume**:

$$v_2 = \frac{L B^2}{8} \tan\theta$$

So the **shift in center of buoyancy** $\overline{BB'}$ in the transverse direction is:

$$\overline{BB'} = \frac{v_1 \cdot \left(\frac{B}{2}\right) + v_2 \cdot \left(\frac{B}{2}\right)}{\nabla} \cdot \frac{2}{1}$$

Wait — let's be careful with geometry. Each wedge centroid is at a transverse distance $\dfrac{B}{2}$ from the centerline. Using the principle of moments of volumes:

#### Step 2 — Apply volume moment principle

The new center of buoyancy B' is the centroid of the new displaced volume. By shifting volume $v_1$ to the immersed side and removing $v_2$ from the emerged side, the transverse shift is:

$$\overline{BB'} = \frac{v_1 \cdot \frac{B}{2} + v_2 \cdot \frac{B}{2}}{\nabla} = \frac{2 \cdot v \cdot \frac{B}{2}}{\nabla} = \frac{v \cdot B}{\nabla}$$

Substituting $v = \dfrac{L B^2}{8} \tan\theta$:

$$\overline{BB'} = \frac{B \cdot \dfrac{L B^2}{8} \tan\theta}{\nabla} = \frac{L B^3}{8 \nabla} \tan\theta$$

#### Step 3 — Connect to Metacentric Radius

By definition of the metacenter M (where the vertical through B' meets the original vertical), for small angles:

$$\overline{BB'} = BM_T \cdot \tan\theta$$

Therefore:

$$BM_T \cdot \tan\theta = \frac{L B^3}{8 \nabla} \tan\theta$$

Cancelling $\tan\theta$ (valid for $\theta \neq 0$):

$$\boxed{BM_T = \frac{I_T}{\nabla}}$$

where $I_T = \dfrac{L B^3}{12}$ is the **2nd moment of area** of the waterplane rectangle about the longitudinal centerline.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the famous formula **$BM_T = I_T / \nabla$** — universally valid for any waterplane shape (rectangular, elliptical, or ship-shaped). For a rectangular WL: $I_T = \dfrac{L B^3}{12}$. For a ship, $I_T$ is computed numerically or by Simpson's rules using offsets.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Metacentric Height $GM$

By inspection of the longitudinal vertical plane through the ship's centerline:

$$KM = KB + BM_T$$

And the metacenter M lies on the centerline above K. The position of G relative to K is $KG$. Therefore:

$$GM_T = KM - KG = KB + BM_T - KG$$

$$\boxed{GM_T = KB + \frac{I_T}{\nabla} - KG}$$

This is the **single most important equation** in transverse ship stability.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Righting Lever $GZ$

When the ship heels by $\theta$:
- B shifts to B'
- The righting couple is $W \cdot GZ$, where $GZ$ is the perpendicular distance from G to the new buoyancy vertical B'M.

From triangle geometry, for small $\theta$:

$$GZ = GM \cdot \sin\theta \approx GM \cdot \theta$$

For larger angles (using the "Wall-sided" or cross-curve approach):

$$GZ = \sin\theta \cdot \left(KB + \frac{BM_T \cdot \sin\theta}{\sin\theta}\right) - KG \cdot \sin\theta$$

This simplifies to **$GZ = GM \cdot \sin\theta$** only when $\theta$ is small (typically $\leq 10°$).

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Transverse Stability

**Problem [KTU University Exam – May 2024 style]:**
A box-shaped vessel of length $L = 100$ m, breadth $B = 15$ m floats at a draft $d = 4$ m in seawater ($\rho = 1.025$ t/m³). The center of gravity is at $KG = 5$ m above the keel. Calculate:
1. The displacement $\Delta$
2. The transverse metacentric radius $BM_T$
3. The metacentric height $GM_T$
4. Comment on initial stability.

#### Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute Displacement**

$$\nabla = L \cdot B \cdot d = 100 \times 15 \times 4 = 6000\ \text{m}^3$$

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \times 6000 = 6150\ \text{tonnes}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $BM_T$**

$$I_T = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{100 \times 15^3}{12} = \frac{100 \times 3375}{12} = 28125\ \text{m}^4$$

$$BM_T = \frac{I_T}{\nabla} = \frac{28125}{6000} = 4.6875\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $GM_T$**

For a rectangular pontoon, $KB = \dfrac{d}{2} = 2$ m.

$$GM_T = KB + BM_T - KG = 2 + 4.6875 - 5 = 1.6875\ \text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Stability Comment**

Since $GM_T = +1.6875\ \text{m} > 0$, the vessel has **positive initial transverse stability**. The vessel is in **stable equilibrium** and will return upright when disturbed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For warships and passenger ships, typical $GM_T$ values lie between **0.15 m and 1.5 m**. A very large GM makes the ship "stiff" (jerky roll); a small GM makes it "tender" (slow roll, larger amplitude).

---

### 3.5 Trim Derivation — Moment to Change Trim (MCTC)

When a weight $w$ is moved a longitudinal distance $x$ on a ship, it creates a **trimming moment** $M = w \cdot x$. This moment causes the ship to trim by an angle $\phi$ such that the longitudinal restoring couple equals the applied moment.

**Longitudinal righting couple:**

$$M_R = W \cdot GML \cdot \sin\phi \approx W \cdot GML \cdot \phi$$

**Equilibrium:** $M_R = M$

$$W \cdot GML \cdot \phi = w \cdot x$$

The trim $T = L \cdot \phi$, so $\phi = T/L$:

$$W \cdot GML \cdot \frac{T}{L} = w \cdot x$$

**For trim change of 1 cm** ($T = 0.01$ m), setting $w \cdot x = MCTC$:

$$MCTC = \frac{W \cdot GML}{100 \cdot L} \quad \text{(tonne·m/cm)}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T\ (\text{cm}) = \frac{\text{Trimming Moment (t·m)}}{MCTC\ (\text{t·m/cm)}}}$$

---

### 3.6 Worked Example — Trim Calculation

**Problem:**
A ship has $L = 150$ m, $\Delta = 12000$ t, $GML = 180$ m. Initial drafts: $d_F = 5.2$ m, $d_A = 6.3$ m. Find:
1. Initial trim
2. MCTC
3. New trim and new drafts after moving 200 t of cargo from a position 30 m aft of midship to 20 m forward of midship.

#### Solution:

**Step 1 — Initial Trim**

$$T_1 = d_F - d_A = 5.2 - 6.3 = -1.1\ \text{m}$$

Negative sign → ship is **trimmed by the stern** (draft aft > draft forward).

**Step 2 — MCTC**

$$MCTC = \frac{W \cdot GML}{100 \cdot L} = \frac{12000 \times 180}{100 \times 150} = \frac{2160000}{15000} = 144\ \text{t·m/cm}$$

**Step 3 — Trimming Moment**

Cargo moves from 30 m aft to 20 m forward. Longitudinal shift in CG:

$$\Delta x = 30\ \text{(aft to midship)} + 20\ \text{(midship to forward)} = 50\ \text{m forward}$$

$$M = w \cdot \Delta x = 200 \times 50 = 10000\ \text{t·m (forward)}$$

**Step 4 — Change in Trim**

$$\Delta T = \frac{M}{MCTC} = \frac{10000}{144} = +69.44\ \text{cm} = +0.6944\ \text{m (forward)}$$

Positive → ship trims **more by the bow** (forward draft increases).

**Step 5 — New Drafts**

Mean draft change: $\Delta d_{mean} = \dfrac{w}{\text{TPA}}$. Assume TPA = 25 t/cm (typical):

$$\Delta d_{mean} = \frac{200}{25} = 8\ \text{cm} = 0.08\ \text{m (sinkage)}$$

Draft changes:

$$\Delta d_F = \Delta d_{mean} + \frac{\Delta T}{2} = 0.08 + \frac{0.6944}{2} = 0.08 + 0.3472 = 0.4272\ \text{m}$$

$$\Delta d_A = \Delta d_{mean} - \frac{\Delta T}{2} = 0.08 - 0.3472 = -0.2672\ \text{m}$$

**New Drafts:**

$$d_F^{new} = 5.2 + 0.4272 = 5.6272\ \text{m}$$

$$d_A^{new} = 6.3 - 0.2672 = 6.0328\ \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{new} = 5.6272 - 6.0328 = -0.4056\ \text{m (still trimmed by stern, but less)}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Relationships Between Ship Stability Reference Points

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship at Rest in Water] --> B[Forces in Equilibrium]
    B --> C[Weight W acts downward at G]
    B --> D[Buoyancy Fb acts upward at B]
    D --> E[Fb equals W times g]
    D --> F[Point B = Centroid of displaced volume]
    G[Ship Heels by angle theta] --> H[Underwater shape changes]
    H --> I[Center of Buoyancy shifts to B1]
    I --> J[Vertical through B1 meets old CL at M]
    J --> K[M is the Metacenter]
    K --> L{M above G or below G}
    L -->|Above| M[Stable Equilibrium - GM greater than 0]
    L -->|At G| N[Neutral Equilibrium - GM equals 0]
    L -->|Below| O[Unstable Equilibrium - GM less than 0]
    P[Ship Loads Cargo Forward] --> Q[Longitudinal shift in G]
    Q --> R[Trimming Moment equals w times x]
    R --> S[Trim Angle phi develops]
    S --> T[Forward Draft Increases]
    S --> U[Aft Draft Decreases]
    T --> V[New Trim computed from MCTC]
    U --> V
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Stability Computation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT1[Input: Ship Geometry L B d] --> PROC1[Compute Volume and Displacement]
    INPUT2[Input: KG KB] --> PROC1
    INPUT3[Input: Waterplane Area IT] --> PROC2[Compute BM and GM]
    PROC1 --> PROC2
    PROC2 --> OUTPUT1[Output: Metacentric Height GM]
    OUTPUT1 --> DECISION{GM greater than 0}
    DECISION -->|Yes| SAFE[Stable Operating Condition]
    DECISION -->|No| UNSAFE[Capsize Risk - Reload]
    INPUT4[Input: Cargo shift w and distance x] --> PROC3[Compute Trimming Moment]
    PROC3 --> PROC4[Apply MCTC formula]
    INPUT5[Input: GML and L] --> PROC4
    PROC4 --> OUTPUT2[Output: Change in Trim]
    OUTPUT2 --> OUTPUT3[Output: New Forward and Aft Drafts]
```

### 4.3 Vertical Cross-Section Schematic of Reference Points

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ShipCrossSection [Longitudinal Vertical Cross Section of Floating Ship]
        direction TB
        K[K = Keel - lowest point on hull]
        B[B = Center of Buoyancy - centroid of displaced water]
        G[G = Center of Gravity - centroid of ship weight]
        M[M = Metacenter - intersection point above K]
    end
    K -->|Vertical distance KB| B
    K -->|Vertical distance KG| G
    K -->|Vertical distance KM| M
    B -->|Transverse shift BM| M
    G -->|Metacentric Height GM| M
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the upright position, B, G, and M all lie on the **centerline** of the ship. After heeling, B moves to B' but K, G, M remain on the original centerline (for small angles and fixed G).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define buoyancy. State and explain Archimedes' Principle as applicable to a floating ship.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Buoyancy is the upward vertical force exerted by a fluid on a body immersed in it.

**Archimedes' Principle (1 mark):** When a body is partially or fully immersed in a fluid, it experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body.

**Application to Ship (1 mark):** A floating ship displaces a volume $\nabla$ of water. The buoyant force $F_b = \rho g \nabla$ acts upward through the center of buoyancy B. For the ship to float in equilibrium, $F_b = W$ (ship's weight). This is the **Principle of Flotation**, and it allows steel ships (denser than water) to float because their hollow hulls displace a large volume.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the metacentric height of a ship? Why is it important for ship safety?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The metacentric height $GM$ is the vertical distance between the center of gravity G and the metacenter M. It is computed as:

$$GM = KB + BM - KG = KB + \frac{I_T}{\nabla} - KG$$

**Importance for Safety (1.5 marks):**

- $GM > 0$ → **Stable ship**, returns upright after disturbance (safe).
- $GM = 0$ → **Neutral**, ship stays at the tilted angle (unsafe).
- $GM < 0$ → **Unstable**, ship capsizes (extremely dangerous).

A proper $GM$ ensures the ship can resist heeling moments from wind, waves, and turning. Typical safe values: **0.15 m to 1.5 m**. Too high → stiff (jerky roll, high accelerations); too low → tender (large roll angles, risk of capsize).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the transverse metacentric radius $BM_T$ of a floating body. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship of displacement 9000 tonnes floats at a mean draft of 5.5 m in seawater ($\rho = 1.025$ t/m³). The waterplane area is $A_{WL} = 1800$ m² with a transverse second moment of area $I_T = 95000$ m⁴. The center of gravity is 6.8 m above the keel, and the center of buoyancy is 2.9 m above the keel. Calculate:
1. The metacentric height $GM$
2. The righting moment when the ship heels by $8°$
3. Comment on the ship's stability. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Derivation of $BM_T$ (7 marks):**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Ship is in still water (no waves).
- Heeling angle $\theta$ is small (typically $\leq 10°$).
- Hull is rigid; no flexing or flooding.
- Center of gravity G remains fixed during small heeling.
- Waterplane area does not change appreciably during small heel.

**Derivation (6 marks):**

When a ship heels by small angle $\theta$, the center of buoyancy shifts from B to B'. Consider the wedge of immersion $v$ on one side and wedge of emergence $v$ on the other side, each with centroid at transverse distance $B/2$ from the centerline (for rectangular WL):

$$v = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{B}{2} \cdot \frac{B}{2}\tan\theta \cdot L = \frac{L B^2}{8} \tan\theta$$

By the principle of moments of displaced volumes, the transverse shift of B is:

$$\overline{BB'} = \frac{v \cdot \frac{B}{2} + v \cdot \frac{B}{2}}{\nabla} = \frac{v \cdot B}{\nabla} = \frac{L B^3}{8 \nabla} \tan\theta$$

The metacentric radius BM is defined by the geometric relation $\overline{BB'} = BM \cdot \tan\theta$. Equating:

$$BM \cdot \tan\theta = \frac{L B^3}{8 \nabla} \tan\theta$$

Since $\dfrac{L B^3}{12} = I_T$ (the second moment of waterplane area for a rectangle):

$$\boxed{BM_T = \frac{I_T}{\nabla}}$$

This is valid for any waterplane shape.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $\Delta = 9000$ t, so $\nabla = 9000 / 1.025 = 8780.49$ m³
- $I_T = 95000$ m⁴, $KB = 2.9$ m, $KG = 6.8$ m, $\theta = 8°$

**Step 1 — Compute $BM_T$ (1 mark):**

$$BM_T = \frac{I_T}{\nabla} = \frac{95000}{8780.49} = 10.82\ \text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $GM_T$ (1 mark):**

$$GM_T = KB + BM_T - KG = 2.9 + 10.82 - 6.8 = 6.92\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $GZ$ (1 mark):**

For small angles, $GZ = GM \cdot \sin\theta = 6.92 \times \sin(8°) = 6.92 \times 0.1392 = 0.963$ m

**Step 4 — Compute Righting Moment (2 marks):**

$$RM = W \cdot GZ = \Delta \cdot g \cdot GZ = 9000 \times 9.81 \times 0.963 = 85018\ \text{kN·m}$$

$$\approx 85.02\ \text{MN·m}$$

**Step 5 — Stability Comment (2 marks):**

Since $GM_T = +6.92$ m $> 0$, the ship is in **stable equilibrium** with strong initial transverse stability. However, this $GM$ is unusually large for a typical merchant ship (which prefers $GM \approx 0.15$–1.5 m). Such a high $GM$ implies a **"stiff" ship** with rapid, jerky roll motion that may cause discomfort to passengers, damage to cargo, and high structural stresses. Naval architects would consider this vessel over-stable.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Define trim. Explain the concept of Moment to Change Trim (MCTC) and derive its expression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship of length $L = 140$ m has a displacement of 11000 tonnes and a longitudinal metacentric height $GML = 165$ m. The initial drafts are $d_F = 5.4$ m forward and $d_A = 6.1$ m aft. The center of flotation is 4 m aft of midships. A weight of 150 tonnes is loaded at a point 25 m forward of midships. Find:
1. The MCTC of the ship
2. The new trim
3. The new drafts forward and aft **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Trim and MCTC (7 marks):**

**Definition of Trim (1.5 marks):**
Trim is the difference between the forward and aft drafts of a ship:
$$T = d_F - d_A$$
Positive trim → bow down; negative → stern down; zero → even keel.

**Concept of MCTC (2.5 marks):**
The Moment to Change Trim (MCTC) is defined as the trimming moment required to change the trim of a ship by **1 cm**. It is analogous to the transverse stability concept but applied longitudinally.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
When a ship trims by a small longitudinal angle $\phi$, the longitudinal shift in center of buoyancy is $BM_L \cdot \tan\phi \approx BM_L \cdot \phi$. The righting couple is $W \cdot GML \cdot \phi$.

For a change in trim $T$ over length $L$, the trim angle is $\phi = T/L$. Equating applied trimming moment to righting couple:

$$M = W \cdot GML \cdot \phi = W \cdot GML \cdot \frac{T}{L}$$

For $T = 1$ cm $= 0.01$ m:

$$\boxed{MCTC = \frac{W \cdot GML}{100 \cdot L} \quad \text{(tonne·m per cm)}}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $L = 140$ m, $\Delta = 11000$ t, $GML = 165$ m
- $d_F = 5.4$ m, $d_A = 6.1$ m, $F = 4$ m aft of midships
- Added weight $w = 150$ t at 25 m forward of midships

**Step 1 — Initial Trim (1 mark):**

$$T_1 = d_F - d_A = 5.4 - 6.1 = -0.7\ \text{m (stern down)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute MCTC (1 mark):**

$$MCTC = \frac{W \cdot GML}{100 \cdot L} = \frac{11000 \times 165}{100 \times 140} = \frac{1815000}{14000} = 129.64\ \text{t·m/cm}$$

**Step 3 — Trimming Moment due to Loading (1 mark):**

Loading weight forward shifts G forward by an effective moment:

$$M = w \cdot x = 150 \times 25 = 3750\ \text{t·m (forward)}$$

**Step 4 — Change in Trim (1 mark):**

$$\Delta T = \frac{M}{MCTC} = \frac{3750}{129.64} = 28.92\ \text{cm} = 0.2892\ \text{m (bow down, positive)}$$

**Step 5 — Mean Draft Change (Parallel Sinkage) (1 mark):**
Assume TPA = 30 t/cm:

$$\Delta d_{mean} = \frac{w}{TPA} = \frac{150}{30} = 5\ \text{cm} = 0.05\ \text{m (sinkage)}$$

**Step 6 — New Drafts (2 marks):**

$$\Delta d_F = \Delta d_{mean} + \frac{\Delta T}{2} = 0.05 + \frac{0.2892}{2} = 0.05 + 0.1446 = 0.1946\ \text{m}$$

$$\Delta d_A = \Delta d_{mean} - \frac{\Delta T}{2} = 0.05 - 0.1446 = -0.0946\ \text{m}$$

**Final Drafts:**

$$d_F^{new} = 5.4 + 0.1946 = 5.595\ \text{m}$$

$$d_A^{new} = 6.1 - 0.0946 = 6.005\ \text{m}$$

**New Trim:**

$$T_{new} = 5.595 - 6.005 = -0.41\ \text{m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors (–3 to –5 marks):** Students often forget that forward trim is positive. Always state the convention at the start of your answer.
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency in MCTC (–2 marks):** If you compute trim in metres, MCTC must be in t·m/m. If trim is in cm, MCTC must be in t·m/cm. Mixing causes wrong answers.
> 3. **Confusion between $BM_T$ and $BM_L$ (–4 marks):** $BM_L$ is about 100× larger than $BM_T$. Using the wrong one in MCTC gives drastically wrong trim.
> 4. **Forgetting Parallel Sinkage (–2 marks):** Even if the trim moment is zero, adding weight forward causes the **whole ship to sink deeper** — this is parallel sinkage and must be added.
> 5. **KG vs KB Confusion (–3 marks):** Always re-check whether the given height is from keel (K) or from baseline (BL). K and BL are not the same.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Buoyancy, Stability, and Trim**

- [ ] **Archimedes' Principle:** $F_b = \rho g \nabla$; floating condition: $W = \rho g \nabla$.
- [ ] **Six Reference Points:** K, B, G, M, F, ML — know definitions cold.
- [ ] **Master Formula:** $GM_T = KB + \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla} - KG$.
- [ ] **Stability Rule:** $GM > 0$ → Stable; $GM = 0$ → Neutral; $GM < 0$ → Capsize.
- [ ] **Righting Lever:** $GZ = GM \sin\theta$ (valid for small $\theta$).
- [ ] **Righting Moment:** $RM = W \cdot GZ = \Delta g \cdot GM \cdot \theta$.
- [ ] **Trim Definition:** $T = d_F - d_A$ (positive = bow down).
- [ ] **MCTC Formula:** $MCTC = \dfrac{W \cdot GML}{100 \cdot L}$ (use cm).
- [ ] **Trim Change:** $\Delta T = \dfrac{\text{Trimming Moment}}{MCTC}$.
- [ ] **Draft Change at any Point:** $\Delta d_x = \Delta d_{mean} + \dfrac{\Delta T}{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{2} - \dfrac{x}{L}\right)$.
- [ ] **Typical Safe $GM_T$:** 0.15 m to 1.5 m for merchant vessels.
- [ ] **Units Reminder:** Displacement $\Delta$ in tonnes (mass); $W$ in tonnes-force; $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- [ ] **Sign Conventions:** Always declare at the start of any numerical answer.
- [ ] **Small Angle Assumption:** Use $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \theta$ only for $\theta \leq 10°$.
- [ ] **$BM_L \gg BM_T$:** Longitudinal stability is much stronger than transverse.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inclining Experiment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inclining Experiment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inclining Experiment (IE):** A precisely controlled and formally documented procedure performed on a **newly built vessel** (or one that has undergone significant structural modification/repair) to determine the vessel's **lightship weight** (displacement when empty of cargo, consumables, and crew) and the **longitudinal and transverse positions of the center of gravity (LCG and TCG, or equivalently, the Vertical Center of Gravity – VC/KG)**. It forms the empirical foundation for all subsequent stability calculations of the ship throughout its operational life.

The experiment is mandated under international regulations such as the **IMO Load Lines Convention** and is typically conducted in the presence of a **Classification Society surveyor** (e.g., Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas, DNV, IRS) at a **sheltered, calm-water location** (e.g., a fitting-out berth or harbour basin) to minimize environmental disturbances.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Giant Seesaw" Intuition

Imagine a **massive rectangular ice-box floating in a bathtub**. You know its total weight (the displacement, $W$). Now you slide a **known brick (weight, $w$)** from the centerline to one edge of the ice-box. The box **tilts slightly**, dipping one corner down.

The clever insight of the Inclining Experiment is **reversible physics**:

- By measuring **how far** the brick moved ($d$) and **how much** the box tilted ($\theta$), you can mathematically "weigh" the box itself.
- This is the **principle of moments applied to floating bodies** — a small known force producing a small, measurable angle of tilt allows us to compute an **unknown, very large restoring force**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Engineering Insight:** The heeling moment produced by the small, movable test weight is exactly balanced by the **righting moment** generated by the buoyant force of the water acting through the **metacenter (M)**. This balance is the entire foundation of the GM formula.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Quantities

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit | Magnitude |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $W$ | Lightship displacement | tonnes (t) | 1,000 – 100,000+ |
| $w$ | Movable test weight (solid ballast) | tonnes (t) | 1 – 50 |
| $d$ | Transverse distance the weight is moved | metres (m) | 6 – 15 |
| $\theta$ | Angle of heel produced | degrees (°) | 0.5° – 4° |
| $GM$ | Transverse metacentric height | metres (m) | 0.3 – 3.0 |
| $KG$ | Height of center of gravity above keel | metres (m) | 3 – 15 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Inclining Experiment belongs to **Module 3: Ship Stability — Statical & Dynamical**. Mastery of the formula $GM = \frac{w \cdot d}{W \cdot \tan\theta}$ is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in any KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) for PESFT755.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Governing Physical Principle

When a ship is floating upright in still water, its **weight** ($W$) acts downward through the **center of gravity** ($G$), and its **buoyancy** ($B$) acts upward through the **center of buoyancy** ($B$). These two forces are **equal, opposite, and collinear**, producing no moment — the ship is in equilibrium.

When a **known weight $w$** is shifted transversely by a distance $d$ from one side of the centerline to the other, two effects occur **simultaneously**:

1. A **heeling moment** is introduced: $\mathcal{M}_{\text{heel}} = w \cdot d$
2. The ship **tilts (inclines)** through a small angle $\theta$, causing the **center of buoyancy** to shift outward to $B'$, generating a **righting moment**: $\mathcal{M}_{\text{right}} = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$

At the new equilibrium angle, these two moments are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Establish Baseline (Upright Condition)**
The ship must be completely upright. All loose gear, stores, and liquids must be secured. Free-surface effects from partially filled tanks must be **zero** (tanks pressed up full or completely empty).

**Step 2 — Place the Test Weights**
A set of **solid, calibrated weights** (typically steel billets or pig iron, NOT liquids or sand which could shift) are positioned on one side of the deck, as far outboard as practical, at a **known transverse distance** from the centerline.

**Step 3 — Measure Initial Pendulum Reading**
A long **pendulum** (or a **transverse water-tube (U-tube)**) is hung from a point high in the superstructure. The initial deflection is recorded. Pendulum length is typically 6 – 12 m to magnify the small angle.

**Step 4 — Shift the Weights**
The test weights are physically moved (using cranes or rollers) across the deck to the **opposite side**, symmetrically at the same distance from the centerline. The shift distance $d$ is the **total transverse movement** (e.g., from port 7 m to starboard 7 m gives $d = 14$ m).

**Step 5 — Measure New Pendulum Reading**
After all motion of liquid surfaces and pendulum oscillations have **completely damped out** (typically 15–30 minutes), the new deflection is recorded.

**Step 6 — Repeat for Symmetry Check**
Weights are returned to the original side, and the process is repeated. **Two or more runs** must agree within **0.5°**, otherwise the experiment is invalidated.

**Step 7 — Calculate $GM$**
Use the derived formula below to compute the metacentric height.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Constraint for Tables:** The absolute-value symbols below use `\vert` instead of the keyboard pipe `\vert`, ensuring the markdown table does not break.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit / Boundary Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\tan\theta = \frac{p}{l}$ | Heel angle from pendulum deflection $p$ and pendulum length $l$ | $\theta$ in radians (small angles: $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \theta$) |
| 2 | $GM = \frac{w \cdot d}{W \cdot \tan\theta}$ | **The Master Formula** — Transverse metacentric height from IE data | Metres (m); valid for $\theta \leq 4°$ |
| 3 | $KG = KM - GM$ | Height of G above keel | $KM$ from hydrostatics; $GM$ from IE |
| 4 | $KM = KB + BM$ | Distance from keel to metacenter | $BM = \dfrac{I_T}{V}$ where $I_T$ is waterplane 2nd moment of area, $V$ is displaced volume |
| 5 | $BM = \dfrac{I_T}{V} = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12 \cdot V}$ | BM for a rectangular waterplane (approx. for box-shaped hulls) | $L$ = waterplane length; $B$ = waterplane breadth |
| 6 | $\text{Free Surface Correction} \rightarrow GM_{\text{eff}} = GM - \text{FSC}$ | Subtract FSC for slack tanks | FSC = $\dfrac{i \cdot \rho}{W}$ where $i$ = transverse 2nd moment of free surface |
| 7 | $d = \vert x_{\text{initial}} - x_{\text{final}} \vert$ | Total transverse movement of weight | Use `\vert` notation — total distance, not half-distance |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Statutory Compliance:** Every passenger ship and every ship above 100 m LOA must have an approved Stability Booklet whose $KG$ values originate from the Inclining Experiment.
2. **Damage Stability:** Modern SOLAS Chapter II-1 damage-stability calculations use the $KG$ from the IE as the baseline against which flooding effects are evaluated.
3. **Loading Computer Calibration:** Onboard stability computers require accurate $KG_{\text{light}}$ and $LCG_{\text{light}}$ to give the master real-time loading advice — these come directly from the IE.
4. **Insurance & Classification:** The ship's stability information card, stamped by the Class surveyor during the IE, is a legal document for the vessel's entire service life.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Formula: $GM = \frac{w \cdot d}{W \cdot \tan\theta}$

### 3.1.1 Free-Body Setup

Consider a ship floating initially upright. The total weight $W$ acts at $G$, the buoyancy $W$ acts at $B$. By Archimedes' principle, $B = W$ and the two are collinear (equilibrium).

A small weight $w$ is placed at a known transverse distance $y_1$ from the centerline on the **port side**. It is then shifted to a distance $y_2$ on the **starboard side**. The total transverse shift is:

$$d = \vert y_1 \vert + \vert y_2 \vert$$

(If weights are moved from port 7 m to starboard 7 m, then $d = 7 + 7 = 14$ m. This is a **very common KTU exam trap** — students often write $d = 7$ m.)

### 3.1.2 The Heeling Moment

Shifting the weight $w$ by a transverse distance $d$ produces a **transverse heeling moment** about the original centerline:

$$\mathcal{M}_{\text{heel}} = w \cdot d$$

### 3.1.3 The Ship Tilts

Under this moment, the ship inclines to a small heel angle $\theta$. As the ship heels:

- The center of buoyancy shifts from $B$ to $B'$, moving **outward** in the direction of heel.
- The new center of buoyancy $B'$ lies on the **vertical line through the new waterline's centroid**.
- The **metacenter $M$** is defined as the point where the vertical through $B'$ intersects the original vertical through $B$ (the ship's centerline).
- By definition, $M$ lies at a fixed height $KM$ above the keel for small angles.

The horizontal offset between the original vertical line and the new buoyancy vertical is:

$$\overline{BB'} = KM \cdot \sin\theta$$

### 3.1.4 The Righting Moment

The shifted buoyancy $W$ acting at $B'$ produces a **righting moment** about the original centerline equal to weight times horizontal lever arm:

$$\mathcal{M}_{\text{right}} = W \cdot \overline{BB'} = W \cdot KM \cdot \sin\theta$$

### 3.1.5 Equilibrium Condition

At the new equilibrium heel angle, the heeling and righting moments are **equal and opposite**:

$$w \cdot d = W \cdot KM \cdot \sin\theta$$

### 3.1.6 Solving for KM

Rearranging for $KM$:

$$KM = \frac{w \cdot d}{W \cdot \sin\theta}$$

### 3.1.7 Geometric Relation Between KM and GM

From the right-angled triangle formed by $G$, $B$, and $M$ in the heeled condition, the horizontal shift of $G$ equals $GM \cdot \sin\theta$ (this is the lever arm of the weight $W$ about the new vertical). Equating moments about the new vertical gives:

$$\mathcal{M}_{\text{right}} = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$$

Combining with the equilibrium equation:

$$w \cdot d = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$$

### 3.1.8 Small-Angle Approximation (KTU Board Requirement)

For the Inclining Experiment, heel angles are intentionally kept **small (typically 1° to 4°)** to satisfy two conditions:

1. The metacenter $M$ stays at a fixed location (theoretically valid only for infinitesimally small angles).
2. The free-surface and bilge-keel effects remain negligible.

For $\theta \leq 4°$:

$$\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \theta \text{ (in radians)}$$

The error is less than **0.1%** at 4°. Substituting:

$$\boxed{GM = \frac{w \cdot d}{W \cdot \tan\theta}}$$

This is the **canonical Inclining Experiment formula**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Past Paper Style)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A vessel of lightship displacement **$W = 8000$ tonnes** undergoes an inclining experiment. A movable weight of **$w = 20$ tonnes** is shifted transversely from **port 8 m** to **starboard 8 m** (total shift $d = 16$ m). The pendulum of effective length **$l = 9$ m** deflects by **$p = 0.315$ m** at equilibrium. The metacenter height above the keel, obtained from the ship's hydrostatic curves at the corresponding draft, is **$KM = 6.20$ m**. Compute:
> (a) The angle of heel $\theta$
> (b) The metacentric height $GM$
> (c) The height of the center of gravity $KG$

### 3.2.1 Part (a) — Angle of Heel

$$\tan\theta = \frac{p}{l} = \frac{0.315}{9} = 0.035$$

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.035) = 2.005°$$

**Convert to radians for formula use:**

$$\theta_{\text{rad}} = 2.005° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.03500 \text{ rad}$$

Check: $\tan(2.005°) = 0.03500$ ✓ (small-angle consistency confirmed)

### 3.2.2 Part (b) — Metacentric Height GM

$$GM = \frac{w \cdot d}{W \cdot \tan\theta}$$

$$GM = \frac{20 \times 16}{8000 \times 0.035}$$

$$GM = \frac{320}{280}$$

$$\boxed{GM = 1.143 \text{ m}}$$

### 3.2.3 Part (c) — Height of Center of Gravity KG

From the fundamental stability relation:

$$KG = KM - GM$$

$$KG = 6.20 - 1.143$$

$$\boxed{KG = 5.057 \text{ m}}$$

### 3.2.4 Verification of Small-Angle Validity

Since $\theta = 2.005° < 4°$, the small-angle approximation used in the GM formula is **fully justified** — no correction is required. ✓

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Pre-Experiment Corrections

Before conducting the IE, the surveyor must apply **deadweight corrections** to bring the ship to a true lightship condition. The corrected lightship displacement is:

$$W_{\text{light}} = W_{\text{measured}} - \sum(\text{Variable Loads}) + \sum(\text{Liquids to be Added/Removed})$$

The following table summarises typical corrections:

| Item | Action | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Fresh water in tanks | Sound and subtract | Not part of lightship |
| Fuel oil in bunker tanks | Sound and subtract | Consumable, variable |
| Lubricating oil in sumps | Sound and subtract | May vary between surveys |
| Crew & effects | Add (if not yet on board) | Accounted in lightship? |
| Loose stores, provisions | Estimate and subtract | Variable |
| Shipyard workers & tools | Subtract | Temporary |
| Mooring lines on deck | Subtract | Temporary |
| Water in bilges | Subtract | Must be removed before IE |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** Students are expected to know that **all liquids must be either pressed full (100%) or completely empty (0%)** during the IE. Any tank between 0% and 100% creates a **free surface effect** that must be **added to the GM correction** — this is a frequent 3-mark question in Part A.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inclining Experiment — Functional Architecture & Force Flow

The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the **functional architecture** of the Inclining Experiment, mapping the physical layout, force transfers, and measurement systems rather than attempting a literal 3D drawing of a ship.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[IE SUPERVISOR AND CLASS SURVEYOR]
    
    A1[SHIP IN UPRIGHT CONDITION W ACTS AT G]
    A2[BUOYANCY W ACTS AT B COLLINEAR WITH G EQUILIBRIUM]
    
    B1[TEST WEIGHT W POSITIONED AT PORT SIDE Y1 FROM CL]
    B2[PENDULUM INSTALLED LENGTH L DEFLECTION P ZERO INITIAL READING]
    B3[U-TUBE WATER LEVEL MONITOR STANDBY SECONDARY VERIFICATION]
    
    C1[CRANE TRANSFERS WEIGHT W ACROSS DECK]
    C2[NEW POSITION AT STARBOARD SIDE Y2 FROM CL]
    C3[TOTAL SHIFT D EQUALS ABS Y1 PLUS ABS Y2]
    
    D1[HEELING MOMENT M HEEL EQUALS W TIMES D]
    D2[SHIP INCLINES TO ANGLE THETA]
    D3[PENDULUM DEFLECTS TO NEW READING P NEW]
    
    E1[BUOYANCY SHIFTS FROM B TO B PRIME]
    E2[RIGHTING MOMENT M RIGHT EQUALS W TIMES GM TIMES SIN THETA]
    E3[EQUILIBRIUM M HEEL EQUALS M RIGHT]
    
    F1[TAN THETA EQUALS P DIVIDED BY L]
    F2[GM EQUALS W TIMES D DIVIDED BY W TIMES TAN THETA]
    F3[KG EQUALS KM MINUS GM]
    
    G1[STABILITY BOOKLET APPROVED]
    G2[LOADING COMPUTER CALIBRATED]
    G3[CLASS CERTIFICATE STAMPED]
    
    A0 --> A1 --> A2
    A0 --> B1 --> B2
    A0 --> B3
    B1 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3
    C2 --> D1 --> D2 --> D3
    D2 --> E1 --> E2 --> E3
    D3 --> F1
    E3 --> F2
    A2 -.reference.-> E3
    F1 --> F2 --> F3
    F3 --> G1 --> G2 --> G3
```

---

## 4.2 Pre-Experiment Checklist Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[CALM SHELTERED WATER CONFIRMED]
    H2[NO WIND OR CURRENT ABOVE LIMITS]
    H3[ALL TANKS PRESSED FULL OR FULLY EMPTY]
    H4[FREE SURFACE EFFECT VERIFIED ZERO]
    H5[PENDULUM INSTALLED AND CALIBRATED]
    H6[U-TUBE INSTALLED AS SECONDARY GAUGE]
    H7[SOLID TEST WEIGHTS CERTIFIED AND WEIGHED]
    H8[CREW AND LOOSE GEAR ACCOUNTED FOR]
    H9[DEADWEIGHT CORRECTION CALCULATED]
    
    H10[READY TO PROCEED WITH IE]
    H11[CONDITIONS NOT MET HOLD IE]
    
    H1 --> H2
    H2 --> H3
    H3 --> H4
    H4 --> H5
    H5 --> H6
    H6 --> H7
    H7 --> H8
    H8 --> H9
    H9 --> H10
    H9 --> H11
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic — The Three-Force Stability Triangle

The following schematic (rendered as a labelled block diagram since a literal geometric figure cannot be drawn in Mermaid) illustrates the **right-triangle relationship between K, B, G, and M**:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K[KEEL POINT K AT BOTTOM]
    B[CENTER OF BUOYANCY B ABOVE K BY KB]
    G[CENTER OF GRAVITY G BETWEEN B AND M]
    M[METACENTER M AT FIXED HEIGHT ABOVE KEEL KM]
    
    K -->|KB hydrostatic data| B
    B -->|BM equals I over V| M
    G -->|KG unknown from IE| M
    B -->|GM minus BM| G
    
    K1[KEY RELATIONS] --> K
    K2[KG EQUALS KM MINUS GM] --> G
    K3[GM EQUALS W TIMES D DIVIDED BY W TIMES TAN THETA] --> M
```
