# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *Define the Inclining Experiment and state its primary objectives.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Inclining Experiment is a **controlled shipboard procedure** carried out on a newly built vessel (or one after major modification) in calm, sheltered water, in which **known solid weights** are shifted transversely across the deck and the resulting **angle of heel is measured** using a long pendulum or U-tube.

**Primary objectives:**
1. To determine the vessel's **lightship displacement ($W$)**.
2. To determine the **height of the center of gravity ($KG$)** and the **transverse metacentric height ($GM$)**.
3. To provide empirical data for the **Stability Booklet**, the **loading computer**, and the **Classification Society records**.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three objectives — any two objectives sufficient for full marks.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> *Why must all tanks be either full or completely empty during the Inclining Experiment?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Partially filled tanks contain a **free liquid surface**, which produces a **free-surface effect (FSE)**. The liquid in the tank shifts toward the low side as the ship heels, raising the vessel's effective center of gravity by an amount equal to $\dfrac{i \cdot \rho}{W}$ (where $i$ is the transverse second moment of the free surface area).

If this is not eliminated, the measured $GM$ during the IE will be **artificially low** — and the resulting $KG$ will be **unreliable** for all future stability calculations. Filling the tanks completely (or emptying them entirely) **destroys the free surface**, ensuring $i = 0$ and FSE = 0 during the critical measurements.

*(2 marks for the free-surface mechanism, 1 mark for the consequence — must mention that measured GM becomes unreliable.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A — Choice 1

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | Internal Choice Q1A]**
> (a) Derive the formula for determining the **metacentric height ($GM$)** from the Inclining Experiment data, clearly stating the assumptions involved. **(7 marks)**
>
> (b) A ship of lightship displacement **$W = 12000$ tonnes** undergoes an Inclining Experiment. A test weight of **$w = 30$ tonnes** is shifted transversely through a total distance of **$d = 18$ m**. The pendulum, of effective length **$l = 10$ m**, shows a mean deflection of **$p = 0.40$ m**. Given $KM = 7.50$ m, determine the **angle of heel**, the **metacentric height**, and the **height of the center of gravity**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the setup. A ship of displacement $W$ floats upright; weight $W$ acts at $G$ and buoyancy $W$ acts at $B$, collinear.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Introduce the heeling moment. Shifting a known weight $w$ through a transverse distance $d$ creates a heeling moment:
$$\mathcal{M}_{\text{heel}} = w \cdot d$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Describe the righting response. The ship heels to angle $\theta$; buoyancy shifts from $B$ to $B'$, producing a righting moment about the original centerline:
$$\mathcal{M}_{\text{right}} = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Equilibrium:
$$w \cdot d = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Small-angle approximation ($\theta < 4°$): $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta$:
$$GM = \frac{w \cdot d}{W \cdot \tan\theta}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Pendulum relation: $\tan\theta = \dfrac{p}{l}$, hence:
$$\boxed{GM = \frac{w \cdot d \cdot l}{W \cdot p}}$$

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Heel angle $\theta \leq 4°$ so $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta$.
- Free-surface effect is zero (all tanks full or empty).
- No wind, current, or mooring forces during the experiment.
- Pendulum and weights are at the same transverse level (i.e., no significant trim effect on heel).

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Angle of heel (2 marks):**
$$\tan\theta = \frac{p}{l} = \frac{0.40}{10} = 0.040$$

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.040) = 2.291° \quad (\text{or } 0.04000 \text{ rad})$$

**Step 2 — Metacentric height (3 marks):**
$$GM = \frac{w \cdot d}{W \cdot \tan\theta} = \frac{30 \times 18}{12000 \times 0.040}$$

$$GM = \frac{540}{480}$$

$$\boxed{GM = 1.125 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3 — Height of center of gravity (2 marks):**
$$KG = KM - GM = 7.50 - 1.125$$

$$\boxed{KG = 6.375 \text{ m}}$$

**Validity check:** $\theta = 2.291° < 4°$ — small-angle approximation fully justified. ✓

---

### QUESTION B — Choice 2

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 | Internal Choice Q1B]**
> (a) Explain the **procedure of conducting an Inclining Experiment** on a newly built vessel, listing the safety and measurement precautions. **(7 marks)**
>
> (b) A vessel of displacement **$8000$ tonnes** has $KG = 5.40$ m and $KB = 2.20$ m. The waterplane area is **$1200$ m²** with second moment of area $I_T = 96000$ m$^4$. Calculate the **metacentric radius $BM$**, the **height of metacenter $KM$**, and the **metacentric height $GM$**. If during loading the FSC (free surface correction) is **$0.15$ m**, find the **effective metacentric height $GM_{\text{eff}}$**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Pre-conditions (2 marks):**
- Calm, sheltered water; wind speed < 5 knots; no current.
- All tanks **pressed full or fully empty** to eliminate free-surface effect.
- All loose gear, tools, provisions, and personnel accounted for or removed.
- Class surveyor and shipyard representatives present.

**Step 2 — Measurement Setup (2 marks):**
- A long pendulum (6–12 m) hung from a high, structurally sound point on the superstructure.
- A U-tube water level gauge as a secondary verification.
- Solid test weights (certified steel billets) pre-weighed and pre-positioned.

**Step 3 — Experimental Procedure (2 marks):**
- Record initial pendulum reading with weights at one side.
- Move weights transversely to the opposite side at a known total distance $d$.
- Wait 15–30 minutes for oscillations and free-surface sloshing to damp out completely.
- Record the new pendulum deflection $p$.
- Repeat the experiment by returning the weights to the original side — **two runs must agree within 0.5°**.

**Step 4 — Calculation & Documentation (1 mark):**
- Compute $GM = \dfrac{w \cdot d}{W \cdot \tan\theta}$ where $\tan\theta = \dfrac{p}{l}$.
- Compute $KG = KM - GM$.
- Sign the Stability Information Booklet and submit to Classification Society.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Waterplane area and displaced volume (1 mark):**
Given waterplane area $A_W = 1200 \text{ m}^2$.
Displaced volume $V = \dfrac{W}{\rho} = \dfrac{8000}{1.025} = 7804.88 \text{ m}^3$ (using seawater density $\rho = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$).

**Step 2 — Metacentric radius BM (2 marks):**
$$BM = \frac{I_T}{V} = \frac{96000}{7804.88}$$

$$\boxed{BM = 12.300 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3 — Height of metacenter KM (2 marks):**
$$KM = KB + BM = 2.20 + 12.300$$

$$\boxed{KM = 14.500 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 4 — Metacentric height GM (1 mark):**
$$GM = KM - KG = 14.500 - 5.40$$

$$\boxed{GM = 9.100 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 5 — Effective GM after FSC (1 mark):**
$$GM_{\text{eff}} = GM - \text{FSC} = 9.100 - 0.15$$

$$\boxed{GM_{\text{eff}} = 8.950 \text{ m}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks — Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **TOTAL shift vs HALF shift:** The most common error is using $d = 8$ m when weights moved from port 8 m to starboard 8 m (correct $d = 16$ m). Always use **total transverse travel**. KTU examiners specifically test this — losing **3 marks** in one go.
>
> 2. **Radians vs Degrees:** When using $\tan\theta$ in the formula, $\theta$ must be in **radians** (or you use the direct ratio $\tan\theta = p/l$ which is dimensionless). Mixing degrees into the formula is a silent killer.
>
> 3. **$KM$ must be obtained from the hydrostatic curves** at the *actual* draft during the experiment. Do not use a generic $KM$ value — the surveyor cross-checks this with the ship's curves.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the small-angle check:** Always verify $\theta \leq 4°$. If $\theta > 4°$, the IE is **invalid** and must be repeated with smaller weights or larger shift.
>
> 5. **Free-surface effect:** A 0-mark answer is given if the student does not mention FSE = 0 as a precondition. Mentioning "all tanks full or empty" fetches **1 mark** by itself.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** The Inclining Experiment is a controlled procedure to find $W$, $KG$, and $GM$ of a newly built or modified ship.
- **Master Formula:** $GM = \dfrac{w \cdot d}{W \cdot \tan\theta}$ — memorize verbatim.
- **Pendulum Relation:** $\tan\theta = \dfrac{p}{l}$, where $p$ = pendulum deflection, $l$ = pendulum length.
- **Total Shift:** $d = \vert y_1 \vert + \vert y_2 \vert$ — port to starboard, NOT half-distance.
- **$KG$ Relation:** $KG = KM - GM$ — always subtract, never add.
- **Small-Angle Rule:** $\theta \leq 4°$ is mandatory for the linear formula to be valid.
- **Pre-conditions:** Calm water, no wind/current, all tanks full or empty (FSE = 0), weights are **solid** (not liquid/sand).
- **Free-Surface Correction:** $GM_{\text{eff}} = GM - \text{FSC}$ where $\text{FSC} = \dfrac{i \cdot \rho}{W}$.
- **Validation:** At least two runs must agree within **0.5°** of each other.
- **Documentation:** Results are recorded in the **Stability Information Booklet**, signed by the **Class Surveyor**, and used for the ship's entire service life.
- **Regulatory Basis:** IMO Load Lines Convention; SOLAS Chapter II-1; Classification Society rules (Lloyd's, BV, DNV, IRS).
- **Longitudinal IE:** A similar experiment shifting weights **fore-and-aft** determines the longitudinal center of gravity ($LCG$) and the longitudinal metacentric height ($GMT$ or $BM_L$).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Damage Stability and Survivability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Damage Stability and Survivability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Damage Stability** is the inherent capability of a vessel to sustain flooding of one or more compartments due to hull breach or structural failure while maintaining **sufficient residual buoyancy and transverse stability** to proceed safely to port, or to survive the casualty phase, without capsizing or sinking below the **margin line**.

**Survivability** is the broader regulatory and design concept that defines the probability and conditions under which a ship and its occupants can survive a defined casualty scenario, expressed in terms of subdivision standards, the **factor of subdivision (f)**, the **permeability (μ)** of compartments, and the residual **GZ curve** after flooding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Damage stability is governed globally by **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Part B-1, B-2, B-3, and B-4**, supplemented by **IMO Resolution A.265(VIII)** and the **Probabilistic Damage Stability Regulations** (Resolution MSC.421(98) for new builds). For KTU examinations, the deterministic 1-compartment and 2-compartment subdivision rules (legacy SOLAS) remain the most frequently tested framework.

## 1.2 Key Governing Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Margin Line**: A line drawn **76 mm (3 inches)** below the **bulkhead deck** at the ship's side. Floodwater must never reach above this line in the final equilibrium condition.
- **Permeability (μ)**: The ratio of the volume within a compartment that can be occupied by water to the total volume of the compartment. Values typically range from **0.60 to 0.95** depending on compartment use.
- **Factor of Subdivision (f)**: A numerical factor (between **0.33 and 1.00**) representing the effectiveness of subdivision arrangements.
- **Permissible Length of Compartment (L)**: Computed as **f × floodable length**, representing the maximum length of a compartment that may be flooded without submerging the margin line.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Understanding)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Watertight Honeycomb Beehive":**
> Imagine a cruise ship as a giant floating **beehive** made of millions of honeycomb cells (compartments). Each cell wall is a **watertight bulkhead**. Now imagine the beehive cracks on one side and water rushes into a few cells. Because the queen bee (the captain) divided the hive into many sealed cells, only a small portion floods — the rest of the hive stays dry and keeps floating. The smaller and tighter the cells (greater subdivision), the more likely the beehive survives even if a whole row of cells is destroyed. The **factor of subdivision (f)** is essentially a measure of how "tightly sealed" this beehive is.

## 1.4 The Four Pillars of Damage Stability Assessment

1. **Buoyancy Retention** — Volume of intact watertight volume remaining.
2. **Residual Righting Lever (GZ)** — Transverse stability after flooding.
3. **Trim & Heel Control** — Final waterline angles within acceptable limits.
4. **Survivability Subdivision** — Probability of avoiding casualty based on number of flooded compartments.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: Establish the Watertight Subdivision
The ship's hull is divided by **transverse watertight bulkheads** into compartments. SOLAS mandates a minimum standard:
- **Cargo ships ≥ 100 GT but < 100 m**: 1-compartment standard (worst-case flooding of 1 adjacent compartment).
- **Passenger ships**: 1-compartment for smaller vessels, **2-compartment standard** for larger vessels (worst-case flooding of any 2 adjacent compartments).

### Step 2: Calculate the Floodable Length
The **floodable length** at any point is the maximum length of the ship, centered at that point, that can be flooded without submerging the margin line.

$$\text{Floodable Length} = \sum \text{(Length segments derived from lost buoyancy below margin line)}$$

### Step 3: Apply the Permeability Factor (μ)
Each compartment has a different fill ratio based on its contents:

| Compartment Type | Permeability μ |
|------------------|----------------|
| Machinery spaces | **0.85** |
| Cargo holds (general) | **0.70** |
| Baggage stores | **0.60** |
| Tanks (intended for liquid) | **0 or 1.00** (full or empty) |

### Step 4: Compute the Permissible Compartment Length
$$\text{Permissible Length (L)} = f \times \text{Floodable Length}$$

If the actual compartment length exceeds the permissible length, the ship fails subdivision compliance.

### Step 5: Apply the Required Area Under the GZ Curve
After flooding, the residual stability must meet:
- **Range of positive GZ ≥ 20°** (for cargo ships)
- **Maximum GZ ≥ 0.10 m** within 20° of heel
- **Area under GZ curve ≥ 0.0175 m·rad** up to angle of maximum GZ
- **Area under GZ curve ≥ 0.0283 m·rad** up to 40° heel
- **Residual GM ≥ 0.05 m**

### Step 6: Heel Angle Verification
The final angle of equilibrium after flooding must **not exceed 15°** for passenger ships and **not exceed 20°** for cargo ships (in survivable conditions).

## 2.2 Why These Rules Exist — The Engineering "Why"

| Engineering Driver | Real-World Justification |
|--------------------|---------------------------|
| Margin Line (76 mm) | Allows for **wave action** and dynamic pressure rise |
| Permeability (μ) | Accounts for **absorbed water in cargo, machinery, insulation** |
| GZ area criteria | Provides **kinetic energy absorption** during roll motion after flooding |
| Range of positive GZ | Prevents **capsize due to progressive roll-over** under wave action |
| Permissible length | Ensures **redundancy of buoyancy** if a single bulkhead fails |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Permeability | $\mu = \dfrac{V_{\text{floodable}}}{V_{\text{compartment}}}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le \mu \le 1.00$ |
| 2 | Permissible length | $L_{\text{perm}} = f \times L_{\text{floodable}}$ | meters | $f$ = factor of subdivision |
| 3 | Factor of subdivision | $f = \dfrac{N + 1 - P}{N + 1}$ | dimensionless | Where $P$ = probability of survival, $N$ = number of compartments |
| 4 | Probability of survival (R) | $R = \sum p_i \cdot s_i$ | dimensionless | $p_i$ = flooding probability, $s_i$ = survival factor |
| 5 | Subdivision index (A) | $A = \dfrac{\sum L_i \cdot p_i \cdot s_i}{L_{\text{ship}}}$ | dimensionless | Must be ≥ required $R$ |
| 6 | Lever arm lever GZ | $GZ = K\sin\phi - KG\sin\phi$ | meters | From cross-curve of stability |
| 7 | Virtual rise in KG | $KG_{\text{new}} = KG + \dfrac{\mu V d}{V_{\text{ship}}}$ | meters | $d$ = vertical centroid of lost buoyancy |
| 8 | Loss of GM | $\Delta GM = \dfrac{\mu V \cdot \overline{KB}}{V_{\text{disp}}}$ | meters | Subtract from intact GM |
| 9 | Margin line submergence check | $h_{\text{final}} < 76 \text{ mm}$ below bulkhead deck | mm | Mandatory regulatory limit |
| 10 | Vanishing angle | $\phi_v$ where $GZ = 0$ | degrees | Determines range of stability |

> **Convention:** Use `\vert` notation in equations (e.g., $\vert GZ \vert$) to avoid table syntax breakage.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Damage Stability Analysis

- **Ship Design Phase**: Determines optimal positioning of bulkheads and tank arrangements.
- **Classification Society Approval**: Required for every merchant vessel > 100 GT by **IACS members (LR, BV, DNV, ABS, etc.)**.
- **Accident Investigation**: Post-casualty analysis to determine if vessel met design survivability standards.
- **Insurance & Risk Premiums**: Directly affects **H&M insurance** premiums (e.g., London market P&I clubs).
- **Naval Architecture Education**: Foundational subject for MEO Class 4 & Class 2 competency examinations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Factor of Subdivision (f) — The Classical SOLAS Approach

### Given:
- A ship is divided into **N watertight compartments**.
- The ship must survive flooding of any single compartment (cargo ship) or any 2 adjacent compartments (passenger).

### To Prove / Derive:
The permissible length of any compartment should not exceed a fraction **f** of its floodable length.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

Let the **floodable length** at a point be $L_f$. This represents the maximum contiguous length (centered on the point) that can be flooded without violating the margin line.

If a compartment of actual length $L_c$ is flooded, the **loss of buoyancy** is proportional to $L_c$.

For survival, we require:

$$\frac{L_c}{L_f} \le f$$

where $f$ is the **factor of subdivision**. Rearranging:

$$L_c \le f \cdot L_f$$

This is the **SOLAS Permissible Length Equation**.

For modern probabilistic standards (Resolution MSC.421(98)), the **Attained Subdivision Index $A$** is derived as:

$$A = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \cdot s_i \cdot L_i}{L_{\text{ship}}}$$

where:
- $p_i$ = probability of flooding case $i$ (based on compartment length)
- $s_i$ = probability of survival after flooding case $i$ (based on residual GZ criteria)
- $L_i$ = length of compartment $i$
- $L_{\text{ship}}$ = total rule length

The required index $R$ is calculated using:

$$R = 0.002 \cdot L_{\text{ship}}$$

For compliance: $A \ge R$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Reduction in GM After Flooding

### Given:
- Original $GM$ (metacentric height) of intact ship.
- A compartment of volume $V_c$ is flooded with permeability $\mu$.
- The vertical centroid of the lost buoyancy is at height $d$ above keel.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** The lost buoyancy volume is:

$$V_{\text{lost}} = \mu \cdot V_c$$

**Step 2:** The new displacement is reduced:

$$\Delta_{\text{new}} = \Delta_{\text{intact}} - \rho g V_{\text{lost}}$$

**Step 3:** The center of gravity rises by the ratio of (lost buoyancy × distance) to the remaining displacement:

$$KG_{\text{new}} = \frac{KG_{\text{intact}} \cdot \Delta_{\text{intact}} - \rho g V_{\text{lost}} \cdot d}{\Delta_{\text{new}}}$$

**Step 4:** The metacentric height becomes:

$$\begin{aligned}
GM_{\text{new}} &= KM_{\text{new}} - KG_{\text{new}} \\
&= \left(KB_{\text{new}} + BM_{\text{new}}\right) - KG_{\text{new}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting the standard small-angle simplification, the loss of GM can be approximated as:

$$\Delta GM \approx \frac{\mu \cdot V_c}{V_{\text{disp}}} \cdot \overline{KB}$$

where $\overline{KB}$ is the vertical center of buoyancy of the lost volume.

**Step 6:** Therefore:

$$GM_{\text{new}} = GM_{\text{intact}} - \Delta GM$$

This is the most frequently tested derivation in KTU ESE.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Equilibrium Heel Angle After Asymmetric Flooding

### Given:
- A compartment on the starboard side is flooded.
- Final waterline crosses the margin line.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** The flood creates a **virtual loss of buoyancy** on the starboard side.

**Step 2:** At the angle of equilibrium $\phi$, the righting lever $GZ$ must equal the heeling lever from the off-center weight.

**Step 3:** The condition for equilibrium:

$$GZ_{\text{righting}}(\phi) = GZ_{\text{heeling}}(\phi)$$

**Step 4:** For a small angle approximation:

$$\tan \phi \approx \phi = \frac{\mu V_c \cdot y}{V_{\text{disp}} \cdot GM_{\text{new}}}$$

where $y$ is the transverse distance of the flooded compartment center from the centerline.

**Step 5:** The SOLAS check requires:

$$\phi_{\text{equilibrium}} \le \phi_{\text{max}}$$

where $\phi_{\text{max}} = 15°$ (passenger) or $20°$ (cargo ship) in survivable conditions.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Damage Stability Compliance Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Always show all three steps — (1) buoyancy check, (2) GZ check, (3) heel check.

| Check # | Parameter | SOLAS Requirement | Verification Method |
|---------|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Margin Line Submersion | Final waterline ≤ 76 mm below bulkhead deck | Bonjean / lost buoyancy integration |
| 2 | Range of Positive GZ | ≥ 20° (cargo) | Cross-curve of stability × sin φ |
| 3 | Max GZ within 20° | ≥ 0.10 m | Read from residual GZ curve |
| 4 | Area A (0°–φ_max GZ) | ≥ 0.0175 m·rad | Simpson's rule integration |
| 5 | Area B (0°–40°) | ≥ 0.0283 m·rad | Simpson's rule integration |
| 6 | A + B Combined | ≥ 0.0559 m·rad | Cumulative integration |
| 7 | Residual GM | ≥ 0.05 m | KM − KG calculation |
| 8 | Equilibrium Heel | ≤ 15° / 20° | GZ curve intersection |

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Demonstrative)

**Problem:** A bulk carrier has intact $GM = 1.20$ m. A cargo hold (volume $V_c = 5000 \text{ m}^3$, $\mu = 0.70$, center of buoyancy of hold $d = 8$ m, displacement $V_{\text{disp}} = 50{,}000 \text{ m}^3$) is flooded.

**Find:** New GM after flooding.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{lost}} &= \mu \cdot V_c = 0.70 \times 5000 = 3500 \text{ m}^3 \\
\Delta GM &= \frac{V_{\text{lost}} \cdot d}{V_{\text{disp}}} = \frac{3500 \times 8}{50{,}000} \\
\Delta GM &= \frac{28{,}000}{50{,}000} = 0.56 \text{ m} \\
GM_{\text{new}} &= 1.20 - 0.56 = 0.64 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $GM_{\text{new}} = 0.64$ m ≥ 0.05 m ✅ **PASS**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Damage Stability Assessment Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Define Ship Subdivision Layout] --> B0[Compute Intact GM, KG, KM]
    B0 --> C0[Identify All Flooding Cases per SOLAS]
    C0 --> D0{For Each Case}
    D0 --> E0[Compute Lost Buoyancy and Permeability]
    E0 --> F0[Derive New KG and KM]
    F0 --> G0[Generate Cross-Curve of Stability]
    G0 --> H0[Compute Residual GZ Curve via Kn or Cross-Curve Method]
    H0 --> I0[Check Margin Line Submersion]
    I0 --> J0[Check Range of Positive GZ]
    J0 --> K0[Check Area Under GZ Curve]
    K0 --> L0[Check Equilibrium Heel Angle]
    L0 --> M0{All Criteria Met?}
    M0 -- Yes --> N0[Pass: Compliance Achieved]
    M0 -- No --> O0[Fail: Modify Bulkhead Position]
    O0 --> A0
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Damage Stability Design Interactions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Hull Geometry Inputs] --> B1[Hydrostatic Database]
    B1 --> C1[Intact Stability Module]
    C1 --> D1[Damage Stability Solver]
    D1 --> D2[Permeability Database]
    D2 --> D1
    D1 --> D3[Flooding Case Generator]
    D3 --> D4[Cross-Curve of Stability Generator]
    D4 --> D5[Residual GZ Calculator]
    D5 --> E1[SOLAS Compliance Engine]
    E1 --> E2[Margin Line Checker]
    E1 --> E3[GZ Area Checker]
    E1 --> E4[Heel Angle Checker]
    E1 --> E5[GM Threshold Checker]
    E2 --> F1[Compliance Report Output]
    E3 --> F1
    E4 --> F1
    E5 --> F1
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Visual — Margin Line & Subdivision

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SHIP["Damage Stability Concept Diagram"]
        MLINE[Margin Line 76 mm below bulkhead deck]
        BD[Bulkhead Deck]
        WL1[Intact Waterline]
        WL2[Flooded Equilibrium Waterline]
        C1[Compartment 1 Intact]
        C2[Compartment 2 Intact]
        C3F[Compartment 3 FLOODED]
        BH1[Watertight Bulkhead 1]
        BH2[Watertight Bulkhead 2]
        BH3[Watertight Bulkhead 3]
    end
    BD --- MLINE
    WL1 --- WL2
    C1 --- BH1
    BH1 --- C2
    C2 --- BH2
    BH2 --- C3F
    BH2 --- BH3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The flooded compartment (C3F) loses buoyancy, causing the waterline to rise (WL1 → WL2). However, due to subdivision, the margin line is not submerged, and the ship remains stable. If the flooded length had exceeded the permissible length, WL2 would cross the margin line — resulting in progressive flooding and loss of the vessel.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Define the term "Margin Line" as used in damage stability regulations. State its regulatory significance.**

**Model Answer (KTU Board Pattern):**
The **Margin Line** is a reference line drawn on the shell plating of a ship, located **76 mm (3 inches) below the bulkhead deck** at the ship's side.

**Regulatory Significance:**
1. It serves as the **upper limit of permissible flooding** — under any damage scenario, the final waterline must not submerge this line.
2. It accounts for **dynamic effects** such as wave action, rolling, and dynamic pressure.
3. It is the **cornerstone criterion** for all SOLAS damage stability compliance.
4. Any loss of stability that causes the margin line to be submerged is considered a **non-survivable casualty**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "1 meter below" or "as per master's discretion." The exact value is **76 mm** as per SOLAS II-1. Writing the wrong numerical value will fetch **zero marks** for the regulatory part.

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Equivalent]**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Q: Differentiate between "Permeability" and "Factor of Subdivision" with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Permeability (μ) | Factor of Subdivision (f) |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------------|
| Definition | Ratio of volume occupied by water to total compartment volume | Ratio of permissible length to floodable length |
| Range | 0 to 1.00 | 0.33 to 1.00 |
| Use | Determines buoyancy loss per compartment | Limits maximum compartment length |
| Example | Machinery space: μ = 0.85 | Cargo ship 100 m: f = 1.00 (1-compartment) |
| Reference | SOLAS Reg. II-1/4.4.2 | SOLAS Reg. II-1/4.4.3 |

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Equivalent]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the expression for the reduction in metacentric height (ΔGM) when a single compartment of volume $V_c$ and permeability $\mu$ is flooded. State the assumptions used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A container ship has an intact metacentric height $GM = 1.50$ m. A cargo hold (volume $V_c = 8000$ m³, permeability $\mu = 0.70$, vertical center of buoyancy of lost volume $\overline{KB} = 7.5$ m, ship displacement volume $V_{\text{disp}} = 60{,}000$ m³) is flooded symmetrically. Determine the new metacentric height and comment on SOLAS compliance. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)**

The derivation proceeds in the following steps:

**Step 1:** Define lost buoyancy volume due to flooding:
$$V_{\text{lost}} = \mu \cdot V_c$$

**Step 2:** The center of gravity effectively rises due to loss of buoyancy below:

$$\begin{aligned}
KG_{\text{new}} &= \frac{KG_{\text{intact}} \cdot \Delta_{\text{intact}} - \rho g V_{\text{lost}} \cdot \overline{KB}}{\Delta_{\text{new}}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Metacentric height after flooding:
$$\begin{aligned}
GM_{\text{new}} &= KM_{\text{new}} - KG_{\text{new}} \\
&= \left(KB_{\text{new}} + BM_{\text{new}}\right) - KG_{\text{new}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Taking the difference and simplifying for small angles:
$$\Delta GM = \frac{V_{\text{lost}} \cdot \overline{KB}}{V_{\text{disp}}} = \frac{\mu \cdot V_c \cdot \overline{KB}}{V_{\text{disp}}}$$

**Step 5:** Final result:
$$GM_{\text{new}} = GM_{\text{intact}} - \Delta GM$$

**Assumptions Used:**
1. Small angle of heel (≤ 15°).
2. Bilge radius is negligible compared to beam.
3. Cross-section of damaged compartment is approximately rectangular.
4. Permeability is uniformly distributed.
5. Free surface effect is negligible or separately accounted for.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)**

Given:
- $GM_{\text{intact}} = 1.50$ m
- $V_c = 8000$ m³
- $\mu = 0.70$
- $\overline{KB} = 7.5$ m
- $V_{\text{disp}} = 60{,}000$ m³

**Step 1:** Calculate lost buoyancy:
$$V_{\text{lost}} = 0.70 \times 8000 = 5600 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Calculate ΔGM:
$$\Delta GM = \frac{5600 \times 7.5}{60{,}000} = \frac{42{,}000}{60{,}000} = 0.70 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate new GM:
$$GM_{\text{new}} = 1.50 - 0.70 = 0.80 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Check SOLAS compliance:
- SOLAS requires $GM_{\text{new}} \geq 0.05$ m.
- Here $GM_{\text{new}} = 0.80$ m ≥ 0.05 m ✅

**Conclusion:** The vessel satisfies SOLAS residual GM requirements for the given flooding case.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the SOLAS probabilistic damage stability index method. Define the attained subdivision index $A$ and required subdivision index $R$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A passenger ship of rule length $L = 200$ m is divided into 8 main watertight compartments. The following data is provided:

| Case | Length (m) | $p_i$ | $s_i$ |
|------|------------|--------|--------|
| 1 | 30 | 0.04 | 1.00 |
| 2 | 35 | 0.05 | 0.95 |
| 3 | 25 | 0.03 | 0.80 |
| 4 | 40 | 0.06 | 0.70 |

Compute the attained subdivision index $A$ and required index $R = 0.002 \cdot L$, and comment on compliance. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Theory (7 Marks)**

The SOLAS Probabilistic Damage Stability method (Resolution MSC.421(98)) replaced the deterministic 1- and 2-compartment rules for new passenger ships ≥ 120 m and new cargo ships ≥ 150 m.

**Attained Subdivision Index $A$:**
$$A = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \cdot s_i \cdot L_i}{L_{\text{ship}}}$$

where:
- $p_i$ = probability of flooding case $i$ (function of compartment length and ship geometry).
- $s_i$ = probability of survival (function of residual GZ curve satisfying SOLAS criteria).
- $L_i$ = length of compartment $i$.
- $N$ = number of flooding cases considered.

**Required Subdivision Index $R$:**
$$R = 0.002 \cdot L_{\text{ship}}$$

**Compliance Criterion:** $A \ge R$

**Step-by-Step Process:**
1. Identify all possible flooding cases (typically from 1-compartment to N/2 adjacent compartments).
2. Compute $p_i$ for each case based on statistical accident data.
3. Compute $s_i$ by evaluating residual GZ against 7 SOLAS criteria.
4. Multiply $p_i \cdot s_i$ to obtain contribution of each case.
5. Sum and normalize to obtain $A$.
6. Compare $A$ with $R$ to verify compliance.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)**

Given: $L_{\text{ship}} = 200$ m

**Step 1:** Compute numerator:
$$\sum p_i \cdot s_i \cdot L_i$$

For each case:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Case 1: } & 0.04 \times 1.00 \times 30 = 1.200 \\
\text{Case 2: } & 0.05 \times 0.95 \times 35 = 1.6625 \\
\text{Case 3: } & 0.03 \times 0.80 \times 25 = 0.600 \\
\text{Case 4: } & 0.06 \times 0.70 \times 40 = 1.680 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Sum:
$$\sum = 1.200 + 1.6625 + 0.600 + 1.680 = 5.1425$$

**Step 3:** Compute $A$:
$$A = \frac{5.1425}{200} = 0.02571$$

**Step 4:** Compute required $R$:
$$R = 0.002 \times 200 = 0.400$$

**Step 5:** Compare:
$$A = 0.02571 \ll R = 0.400$$

**Conclusion:** The vessel **FAILS** the SOLAS probabilistic damage stability requirement. The bulkhead arrangement must be revised — either by adding more subdivision or by repositioning bulkheads to reduce the lengths of critical compartments.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Unit Errors**: Mixing length units (m and mm) — full 7 marks can be forfeited.
> 2. **Forgetting to normalize** the attained index by $L_{\text{ship}}$ results in zero on numerical part.
> 3. **Omitting assumptions** in derivation loses 2 marks immediately.
> 4. **Conclusion without numbers** (e.g., "ship is safe" without computing) gets zero for the compliance check.
> 5. **Using μ = 1.00** universally for all compartments instead of selecting appropriate table value loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-FREQUENCY KTU 2024 ESE REVISION CHECKLIST**

### Quick-Recall Bullets:

- 📌 **Margin Line** = 76 mm below bulkhead deck (NOT 100 mm, NOT 1 m).
- 📌 **Permeability (μ)**: Machinery = 0.85, Cargo = 0.70, Stores = 0.60.
- 📌 **Permissible Length** = $f \times \text{Floodable Length}$.
- 📌 **Factor of Subdivision (f)** for 1-compartment standard = **1.00** (cargo ship, general).
- 📌 **Factor of Subdivision (f)** for 2-compartment standard = **0.50** (passenger ship, general).
- 📌 **SOLAS GZ area** requirements: A ≥ 0.0175 m·rad; A+B ≥ 0.0559 m·rad.
- 📌 **Range of positive GZ** ≥ 20° (cargo) and ≥ 20° (passenger).
- 📌 **Residual GM** ≥ 0.05 m.
- 📌 **Equilibrium heel** ≤ 15° (passenger) / 20° (cargo).
- 📌 **Loss of GM formula**: $\Delta GM = \dfrac{\mu \cdot V_c \cdot \overline{KB}}{V_{\text{disp}}}$.
- 📌 **Attained Subdivision Index** $A$ ≥ **Required Index** $R = 0.002L$.
- 📌 **Governing Code**: SOLAS Chapter II-1, Part B-1 to B-4.
- 📌 **Authority**: Classification Societies (IACS members) enforce compliance.
- 📌 **Free Surface Effect**: Always reduces residual GM — account for it in flooded compartments.
- 📌 **Watertight doors** must remain closed at sea; can be opened only for transit.

### Three Golden Rules for KTU Exam Success:
1. **Always quote the regulation** (e.g., "As per SOLAS Reg. II-1/4.4.2...").
2. **Show units in every numerical step**.
3. **End with a compliance statement** ("Hence, the ship complies / does not comply with SOLAS damage stability requirements").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Marine Materials and Corrosion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Marine Materials and Corrosion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Marine Materials — Formal Academic Definition

**Marine materials** are the specialized engineering alloys, metals, polymers, composites, and protective coating systems specifically engineered or selected to withstand the aggressive **marine (sea-water) environment**. This environment is characterized by high **salinity (~3.5 wt% NaCl equivalent)**, persistent moisture, biological fouling, oxygen saturation, hydrodynamic loading, and cathodic/anodic electrochemical gradients that accelerate material degradation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Marine materials are structural and functional substances whose mechanical, chemical, and electrochemical properties are optimized to resist degradation under prolonged exposure to seawater, marine atmosphere, and splash-zone conditions, while simultaneously fulfilling structural load-bearing and buoyancy requirements.*

The primary categories of marine materials that form the syllabus core are:

| Category | Examples | Typical Marine Use |
|----------|----------|--------------------|
| Carbon & Low-Alloy Steels | ASTM A36, AH36, DH36 | Hull plates, bulkheads |
| Stainless Steels | 304, 316, 904L, Duplex 2205 | Propeller shafts, fasteners |
| Aluminium Alloys | 5083, 5086, 5456, 6061 | Superstructure, fast craft hulls |
| Copper-Nickel Alloys | 90/10 Cu-Ni, 70/30 Cu-Ni | Seawater piping, condensers |
| Nickel Alloys | Monel 400, Inconel 625 | Pump impellers, valve trim |
| Titanium Alloys | Grade 2, Grade 5 (Ti-6Al-4V) | Submarine pressure hulls |
| FRP / Composites | GRP, CFRP | Boat hulls, radomes |
| Marine Coatings | Epoxy, polyurethane, zinc-rich primers | Hull protection |

## 1.2 Corrosion — Formal Academic Definition

**Corrosion** is the irreversible deterioration of a metal (or alloy) caused by an **electrochemical reaction with its surrounding environment**, typically converting the refined metallic state into a more stable oxide, hydroxide, or sulphide compound.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Definition (ISO 8044:2020 / NACE):** *Corrosion is the physico-chemical interaction between a metal and its environment, resulting in changes in the properties of the metal, and which may lead to significant impairment of the function of the metal, the environment, or the technical system of which they form a part.*

The fundamental electrochemical reaction in aqueous (seawater) corrosion is:
- **Anode (Oxidation):** $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$
- **Cathode (Reduction — Oxygen in neutral/alkaline seawater):** $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuitive View

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you leave an iron nail on a wet beach. Within days, a reddish-brown crust (rust) appears. The iron is essentially "trying to return" to the geological state it was mined from — iron oxide ore. **Corrosion is nature's way of reclaiming refined metals back to their original mineral form** through an electrochemical "battery" that forms on the wet surface.

**A corrosion cell behaves exactly like a flashlight battery:**
- **Anode** = the corroding metal (negative terminal, oxidation)
- **Cathode** = the protected/no-sacrificial area (positive terminal, reduction)
- **Electrolyte** = seawater (ionic conductor, like battery paste)
- **External metallic path** = the metal structure itself (electron conductor, like wire)

In a marine vessel, if you bolt a **copper alloy propeller** directly to a **steel hull**, the propeller (more noble) becomes the cathode and the steel hull becomes the anode — and the steel hull will corrode rapidly. This is the **galvanic corrosion** pitfall that sank the Titanic's rivets and ruins countless ship hulls yearly.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Marine Corrosion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants and Metrics to Memorize:**
> - **Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol} \approx 26.8 \text{ A·h}$
> - **Standard Seawater Salinity:** **3.5 wt%** (≈ 35 ppt)
> - **Seawater Resistivity:** ≈ **20–25 Ω·cm** (vs. pure water 18 MΩ·cm)
> - **Dissolved Oxygen:** 5–8 mL/L (saturated surface seawater)
> - **Seawater pH:** **8.1–8.3** (slightly alkaline)
> - **Galvanic Series of Metals in Seawater** (must be memorized, not confused with the standard EMF series)
> - **Corrosion Rate Unit:** **mpy** (mils per year, 1 mil = 0.001 inch) or **mm/yr**

**Conversion:** $1 \text{ mpy} = 0.0254 \text{ mm/year}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Electrochemical Basis of Marine Corrosion

Corrosion in seawater is fundamentally an **electrochemical process** driven by the thermodynamic tendency of metals to lower their free energy (Gibbs free energy) by oxidizing. For the process to be sustained, **four components** must be present simultaneously — this is the **Corrosion Cell Tetrahedron**:

1. **Anode** — site of metal dissolution (oxidation)
2. **Cathode** — site of reduction reaction
3. **Electrolyte** — ionic conductor (seawater)
4. **Metallic Path** — electronic conductor (the metal structure itself)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "corrosion requires water and oxygen" — this is INCOMPLETE. All four elements of the tetrahedron are mandatory. Removing ANY ONE component (e.g., applying an isolating coating to the cathode) halts the corrosion process.

## 2.2 The Eight Primary Forms of Marine Corrosion

### 2.2.1 Uniform (General) Corrosion
- Even material loss across the entire exposed surface.
- **Mechanism:** Micro-galvanic cells distributed uniformly.
- **Marine example:** Atmospheric rusting of unpainted deck plates.
- **Severity:** Predictable, designable via corrosion allowance.

### 2.2.2 Galvanic (Bimetallic) Corrosion
- **Definition:** Accelerated corrosion of the **anodic (less noble)** metal when two dissimilar metals are electrically coupled in a common electrolyte.
- **Driver:** Potential difference $\Delta E$ between metals in the **galvanic series in seawater**.
- **Severity function:** $I_{corr} \propto \dfrac{\Delta E}{R_{electrolyte} + R_{metal}}$
- **Marine example:** Bronze propeller on steel shaft; aluminium superstructure with steel fasteners.

### 2.2.3 Pitting Corrosion
- **Definition:** Highly localized, narrow-depth attack producing cavities ("pits").
- **Mechanism:** Autocatalytic — the pit bottom becomes anodic; the pit mouth becomes cathodic due to oxygen depletion inside.
- **Most dangerous form** — small mass loss, deep penetration, leading to perforation.
- **Marine example:** Stainless steel 304 in chloride-rich ballast tanks.

### 2.2.4 Crevice Corrosion
- **Definition:** Localized attack within shielded volumes (gaskets, lap joints, under deposits, bolt heads).
- **Mechanism:** Oxygen is consumed inside the crevice, then differential aeration cell forms — the oxygen-starved interior becomes the anode.
- **Marine example:** Under rubber gasket of seacock; beneath marine growth (fouling).

### 2.2.5 Intergranular Corrosion
- **Definition:** Preferential attack along grain boundaries.
- **Mechanism:** Precipitation of chromium-depleted zones at grain boundaries (sensitization) in austenitic stainless steels.
- **Marine example:** Un-stabilized 304 stainless after welding.

### 2.2.6 Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)
- **Definition:** Brittle cracking under simultaneous tensile stress and corrosive environment.
- **Marine example:** Caustic embrittlement of boiler tubes; chloride SCC of 316 stainless.

### 2.2.7 Erosion-Corrosion & Cavitation
- **Definition:** Mechanical removal of protective films by high-velocity fluid, exposing fresh metal.
- **Marine example:** Copper alloy condenser tubes at high seawater flow.

### 2.2.8 Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion (MIC)
- **Definition:** Accelerated corrosion driven by microbial activity (sulfate-reducing bacteria produce $H_2S$).
- **Marine example:** Ballast tank bottom plates; pipe interiors.

## 2.3 The Galvanic Series of Metals in Seawater (Descending Nobility)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this hierarchy — it is the single most-tested KTU 2024 concept in this module.**

**Most Noble (Cathodic, Protected) ↓**
1. Graphite / Carbon
2. Platinum
3. Titanium
4. 70/30 Cu-Ni (passive)
5. Monel 400 (Ni-Cu alloy)
6. 90/10 Cu-Ni
7. Bronze, Naval brass
8. Copper (cathodic in clean seawater)
9. Lead
10. **Hydrogen line (reference)**
11. Mild steel
12. Cast iron
13. Aluminium alloys (5052, 5083)
14. Zinc
15. Magnesium

**Most Active (Anodic, Sacrificial) ↑**

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical distinction:** The *Galvanic Series in Seawater* ≠ the *Standard EMF Series*. The seawater series ranks metals by **practical measured potential** in actual marine electrolyte, including oxide film effects. For example, Aluminium is MORE anodic in the seawater series than zinc-based on EMF calculations would suggest.

## 2.4 Fundamental Equations — KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` (not bare `|`) in any column entries to avoid table breakage.**

| Equation / Concept | Symbolic Form | Use / Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Faraday's First Law (Mass Loss)** | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Predict metal loss in corrosion cell; $m$ = mass dissolved, $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $M$ = atomic mass, $n$ = valency, $F$ = 96,485 C/mol |
| **Faraday's Second Law (Equivalent Weight)** | $m = \dfrac{E_w \cdot I \cdot t}{F}$ where $E_w = M/n$ | Electrochemical equivalent mass |
| **Corrosion Current Density** | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{I_{corr}}{A}$ | $i_{corr}$ in $\text{A/m}^2$ or $\mu\text{A/cm}^2$ |
| **Corrosion Rate (Penetration Depth)** | $CR = \dfrac{K \cdot i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}$ | $K = 0.00327$ for mm/yr, $\rho$ = density g/cm³ |
| **Corrosion Rate in mpy** | $mpy = \dfrac{534 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}$ | $i_{corr}$ in $\mu\text{A/cm}^2$, $\rho$ in g/cm³ |
| **Nernst Equation (Single Electrode)** | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \dfrac{[Red]}{[Ox]}$ | Electrode potential under non-standard conditions |
| **Nernst Equation (Concentration Cell)** | $E = \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \dfrac{C_1}{C_2}$ | Differential aeration, salt-concentration cells |
| **Ohm's Law for Corrosion Cell** | $I_{corr} = \dfrac{E_{cathode} - E_{anode}}{R_{total}}$ | Predict galvanic corrosion current |
| **Tafel Equation (Overpotential)** | $\eta = a + b \log \vert i \vert$ | Butler-Volmer approximation, polarization studies |
| **Mixed Potential Theory** | $i_a = i_c = i_{corr}$ at $E_{corr}$ | Evans diagram intersection point |
| **Stern-Geary Equation** | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303 \cdot R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ | LPR (Linear Polarization Resistance) measurement |
| **Cathodic Protection (Impressed Current)** | $W = \dfrac{I \cdot V \cdot t}{\eta_{system}}$ | Power required for ICCP anode |
| **Sacrificial Anode Mass** | $M_{anode} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot u}$ | $u$ = utilization factor (0.85–0.95 for Zn) |

**Standard Constants in Formula Use:**

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Faraday Constant | $F$ | **96,485** | C/mol |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) |
| RT/F at 25 °C | $RT/F$ | **0.02569** | V |
| 2.303 RT/F at 25 °C | — | **0.05916** | V |
| Seawater Density | $\rho_{sw}$ | **1.025** | g/cm³ |
| Seawater Resistivity | — | **20–25** | Ω·cm |

## 2.5 Corrosion Control Methods in Marine Engineering

### 2.5.1 Cathodic Protection (CP) — Sacrificial Anode Method
- **Concept:** A more anodic metal (Zn, Al, or Mg alloy) is electrically connected to the protected structure. The sacrificial anode corrodes preferentially, protecting the steel.
- **Marine application:** Ship hulls, ballast tanks, offshore platforms, port structures.
- **Anode selection criteria:** Must be more active in the galvanic series; have predictable consumption; not passivate.

### 2.5.2 Cathodic Protection — Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)
- **Concept:** An external DC power source forces current from inert anodes (Pt-coated Ti, mixed-metal oxide) through seawater to the protected structure.
- **Protection criterion:** Polarize the structure to **−0.85 V vs Cu/CuSO₄ reference electrode** (or **−0.80 V vs Ag/AgCl/seawater**).

### 2.5.3 Protective Coatings
- **Barrier coatings:** Epoxy, polyurethane — physically isolate metal from electrolyte.
- **Sacrificial coatings:** Zinc-rich primers (galvanizing) — provide cathodic protection if scratched.
- **Marine paint systems:** Primer + tie coat + anti-fouling topcoat.

### 2.5.4 Material Selection & Corrosion Allowance
- Select alloys with inherent seawater resistance (Cu-Ni, Monel, super-duplex, titanium).
- Add **corrosion allowance** of 1–3 mm in hull plate thickness.

### 2.5.5 Design Modifications
- Avoid crevices, dissimilar metal contact, and stagnant water zones.
- Use proper drainage and ventilation.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Corrosion Rate from Faraday's Law

**Problem Setup:** A steel plate of area $A = 100 \text{ cm}^2$ corrodes uniformly in seawater. The measured corrosion current density is $i_{corr} = 10 \, \mu\text{A/cm}^2$. Compute the corrosion rate in **(a)** mpy and **(b)** mm/yr.

**Given Data for Mild Steel (Fe):**
- Atomic mass of Fe: $M = 55.85 \text{ g/mol}$
- Valency (Fe $\rightarrow$ Fe²⁺): $n = 2$
- Density: $\rho = 7.87 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- $i_{corr} = 10 \, \mu\text{A/cm}^2 = 10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A/cm}^2$

### Step 1 — Total Corrosion Current
Calculate the total anodic current $I$ flowing from the plate.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= i_{corr} \times A \\
  &= \left(10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A/cm}^2\right) \times \left(100 \text{ cm}^2\right) \\
  &= 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} \\
  &= 1.0 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The current density multiplied by the active area gives the total electron flow rate — this is the rate of anodic dissolution.

### Step 2 — Mass Loss in One Year Using Faraday's Law
Apply $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ for $t = 1 \text{ year} = 365 \times 24 \times 3600 \text{ s} = 3.1536 \times 10^7 \text{ s}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \dfrac{(1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times (3.1536 \times 10^7) \times 55.85}{2 \times 96{,}485} \\
  &= \dfrac{1.762 \times 10^{6}}{192{,}970} \\
  &= 9.13 \text{ g/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Faraday's law converts coulombs of charge into grams of metal lost, given the molar mass and number of electrons released per atom.

### Step 3 — Convert Mass Loss to Penetration Depth (mm/yr)
Penetration depth $d = \dfrac{m}{A \cdot \rho}$ where $A = 100 \text{ cm}^2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \dfrac{9.13 \text{ g}}{100 \text{ cm}^2 \times 7.87 \text{ g/cm}^3} \\
  &= \dfrac{9.13}{787} \\
  &= 0.01160 \text{ cm} \\
  &= 0.1160 \text{ mm/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Volume loss = mass ÷ density; depth = volume ÷ area. The conversion from cm to mm gives the final answer.

### Step 4 — Convert to mpy
Since $1 \text{ mm} = 39.37 \text{ mils}$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
mpy &= 0.1160 \text{ mm/yr} \times 39.37 \text{ mils/mm} \\
    &= 4.57 \text{ mpy}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $CR = 0.116 \text{ mm/yr} = 4.57 \text{ mpy}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This is a **moderate** corrosion rate for unprotected mild steel in seawater. Naval classification societies typically require hull plates to withstand ≤ 0.10 mm/yr with coating, justifying a 1–2 mm corrosion allowance over a 10-year drydock cycle.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Nernst Equation for Seawater Concentration Cell

**Problem Setup:** Two copper electrodes are immersed in seawater of different salinities. Compute the cell EMF at 25 °C.

**Reactions (Cu/Cu²⁺ couple, $n = 2$):**
- Anode (high concentration): $Cu \rightarrow Cu^{2+} + 2e^-$ (oxidation)
- Cathode (low concentration): $Cu^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Cu$ (reduction)

**Given:**
- $C_1 = 0.05 \text{ M}$ (high concentration half-cell)
- $C_2 = 0.005 \text{ M}$ (low concentration half-cell)
- Temperature: $T = 298 \text{ K}$

### Step 1 — Standard Cell EMF
Since both electrodes are identical (Cu/Cu²⁺), the standard EMF $E^\circ_{cell} = 0 \text{ V}$.

### Step 2 — Nernst Equation Application
The general Nernst equation for a concentration cell:

$$
E = -\dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \dfrac{C_{anode}}{C_{cathode}}
$$

Since current flows from high to low concentration (high acts as anode, low as cathode), the magnitude becomes:

$$
E = \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \dfrac{C_1}{C_2}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \dfrac{(8.314)(298)}{(2)(96{,}485)} \ln \left(\dfrac{0.05}{0.005}\right) \\
  &= \dfrac{2477.572}{192{,}970} \times \ln(10) \\
  &= 0.01284 \times 2.3026 \\
  &= 0.0296 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Logarithmic Form
Using the $2.303 \dfrac{RT}{F} = 0.05916$ shortcut at 25 °C:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \dfrac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} \dfrac{C_1}{C_2} \\
  &= \dfrac{0.05916}{2} \log_{10} (10) \\
  &= 0.0296 \text{ V} \\
  &= 29.6 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $E_{cell} = 29.6 \text{ mV}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** Even a 10:1 concentration difference in dissolved copper creates a measurable corrosion-driving EMF. This is why **differential aeration cells** (e.g., under a barnacle vs. open seawater) drive localized pitting — the same Nernst principle applies to dissolved $O_2$ concentration differences.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Galvanic Corrosion Between Steel Hull and Bronze Propeller

**Problem Setup:** Estimate the galvanic corrosion current when a steel hull ($E_{Fe} = -0.61 \text{ V vs SCE}$) is coupled to a bronze propeller ($E_{bronze} = -0.20 \text{ V vs SCE}$) in seawater. Total circuit resistance $R = 0.5 \, \Omega$.

### Step 1 — Identify the Anode and Cathode
From the galvanic series, bronze is **more noble** (less negative) than steel, so:
- **Anode (corrodes):** Steel hull
- **Cathode (protected):** Bronze propeller

### Step 2 — Driving Potential
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E &= E_{cathode} - E_{anode} \\
        &= (-0.20) - (-0.61) \\
        &= 0.41 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Ohm's Law to the Corrosion Cell
$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{corr} &= \dfrac{\Delta E}{R} \\
         &= \dfrac{0.41 \text{ V}}{0.5 \, \Omega} \\
         &= 0.82 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Translate to Steel Mass Loss
For Fe with $n = 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} \\
  &= \dfrac{0.82 \times 3.1536 \times 10^7 \times 55.85}{2 \times 96{,}485} \\
  &= \dfrac{1.443 \times 10^{9}}{192{,}970} \\
  &= 7478 \text{ g/year} \\
  &= 7.48 \text{ kg/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $7.48 \text{ kg/year of steel loss}$ from a single bimetallic contact.

> [!WARNING]
> **Design Lesson:** Real ship hulls lose far more than this. The remedy is **insulating the bronze propeller from the steel shaft** with a rubber bushing, a non-conductive coupling, or a **sacrificial zinc anode** bolted nearby to divert the current path. Modern vessels install 4–12 zinc anodes per side of the hull for exactly this reason.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: The Four-Element Corrosion Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["ANODE_SITE (Metal Dissolution)"]
        A1["M --> M^n+ + n e^-"]
        A2["Electrons released into metal"]
    end
    subgraph B["METALLIC PATH (Electronic Conductor)"]
        B1["Steel Hull / Wire"]
        B2["e- flow: Anode --> Cathode"]
    end
    subgraph C["CATHODE_SITE (Reduction)"]
        C1["O2 + 2H2O + 4e^- --> 4OH^-"]
        C2["Protected from oxidation"]
    end
    subgraph D["ELECTROLYTE (Ionic Conductor)"]
        D1["Seawater 3.5% NaCl"]
        D2["OH- ions migrate to anode"]
        D3["M^n+ ions migrate to cathode"]
    end
    A -->|oxidation| B
    B -->|electron flow| C
    C -->|consumes O2| D
    D -->|ionic path closes circuit| A
```

**Reading the diagram:** Removing ANY one of the four blocks halts the corrosion reaction. This is the **foundation principle of every corrosion control method** — coatings interrupt the electrolyte path; cathodic protection forces the entire structure to become a cathode; insulation breaks the metallic path.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Galvanic Series Hierarchy in Seawater (Functional Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    NOBLE["MOST NOBLE - Cathodic - Protected"]
    N1["Platinum / Graphite"]
    N2["Titanium / 70-30 Cu-Ni"]
    N3["Monel / 90-10 Cu-Ni"]
    N4["Bronze / Naval Brass"]
    N5["Copper / Lead"]
    HYDRO["Hydrogen Reference Line 0.000 V"]
    A1["Mild Steel / Cast Iron"]
    A2["Aluminium Alloys 5083, 5086"]
    A3["Zinc"]
    A4["Magnesium"]
    ACTIVE["MOST ACTIVE - Anodic - Sacrificial"]
    NOBLE --> N1 --> N2 --> N3 --> N4 --> N5 --> HYDRO --> A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> ACTIVE
```

**Reading the diagram:** When any two metals are coupled in seawater, the **higher** one becomes the cathode (protected) and the **lower** one becomes the anode (corrodes). Sacrificial anodes (Zn, Al, Mg) are placed **below** the structure in the series so that the structure is protected.

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Cathodic Protection System Architecture (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DC["DC Power Source"] -->|positive cable| ANO["ICCP Anode Pt-Ir or MMO coated Ti"]
    ANO -->|current through seawater| HULL["Protected Steel Hull"]
    HULL -->|return cable to negative terminal| DC
    REF["Reference Electrode Cu-CuSO4 or Ag-AgCl"] -->|monitors potential| MON["Controller Auto-adjusts DC output"]
    MON -->|feedback signal| DC
    SAC["Sacrificial Zinc Anode Backup"] -.->|passive galvanic protection| HULL
```

**Reading the diagram:** Two complementary CP systems are typically installed: an **active** ICCP loop with feedback control, and **passive** sacrificial anodes for redundancy. The reference electrode continuously measures hull-to-seawater potential; the controller modulates DC output to maintain the target potential of **−0.85 V vs Cu/CuSO₄**.

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Pitting & Crevice Corrosion Mechanism (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SURF["Seawater Bulk O2 = 5 mL per L"]
    PIT["PIT BOTTOM O2 depleted Chloride rich pH lowered"]
    PITWALL["PIT WALL Active metal dissolution Fe to Fe2+"]
    PITMOUTH["PIT MOUTH Aerated cathode O2 reduction to OH-"]
    SURF -->|O2 access| PITMOUTH
    PITMOUTH -->|OH- production| PITWALL
    PITWALL -->|Fe2+ hydrolysis acidifies| PIT
    PIT -->|anode concentrates chloride Cl- migrates in| PIT
    PITMOUTH -->|cathode reaction site| PITWALL
```

**Reading the diagram:** Pitting is **autocatalytic** — once started, the chemistry of the pit interior reinforces itself. This is why a single small pit can perforate a hull plate in months even when the average corrosion rate appears negligible. **Design remedy:** use smooth surfaces, avoid stagnant zones, and apply cathodic protection to keep the entire structure below the pitting potential $E_{pit}$.

## 4.5 Diagram 5: Marine Coating Layer Architecture (Block Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    STEEL["Substrate: Mild Steel Hull Plate"]
    PREP["Surface Preparation: Grit Blast to SA 2.5"]
    PRIMER["Zinc-Rich Primer 75 microns - Sacrificial"]
    TIECOAT["Epoxy Tie Coat 125 microns - Barrier"]
    ANTIFOUL["Anti-Fouling Topcoat 100 microns - Biocide release"]
    STEEL --> PREP --> PRIMER --> TIECOAT --> ANTIFOUL
    PROT["TOTAL DRY FILM THICKNESS ~ 300 microns"]
    PRIMER -.-> PROT
    TIECOAT -.-> PROT
    ANTIFOUL -.-> PROT
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each layer has a distinct electrochemical or chemical role. Surface preparation ensures adhesion; the zinc-rich primer offers galvanic protection even if scratched; the epoxy barrier blocks electrolyte ingress; the anti-fouling layer prevents biological attachment that would otherwise create differential aeration cells.
