# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define galvanic corrosion. Why is it particularly critical in marine engineering applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition (1 mark):** Galvanic corrosion is the accelerated corrosion of a metal when it is placed in electrical contact with a more noble metal in a common electrolyte, where the less noble (anodic) metal preferentially corrodes.

**Why critical in marine engineering (2 marks):**
- Seawater is a highly conductive electrolyte (~0.04 S/cm) that maximizes galvanic current.
- Marine vessels inherently couple many dissimilar metals — steel hulls, bronze propellers, Cu-Ni piping, aluminium superstructures, Monel valves.
- Even small potential differences (~0.2–0.5 V) drive large corrosion currents due to low seawater resistance.
- Consequences include rapid hull plate thinning, propeller shaft wear, and heat-exchanger tube failure, with major economic and safety implications.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List and briefly explain the four essential elements required for a corrosion cell to operate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 each element):**
1. **Anode** — site of metal oxidation, where $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$.
2. **Cathode** — site of reduction reaction, typically $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$ in aerated seawater.
3. **Electrolyte** — ionic conductor (seawater) that completes the ionic circuit by carrying ions between anode and cathode.
4. **Metallic path** — electronic conductor (the metal structure itself) that allows electrons to flow from anode to cathode.

> [!NOTE]
> Removing any ONE of the four elements arrests the corrosion process — this is the foundation of every corrosion prevention strategy.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q. (a)** Explain the electrochemical mechanism of corrosion in seawater with a suitable cell diagram. Discuss the role of dissolved oxygen and salinity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A carbon steel plate of area $0.5 \text{ m}^2$ suffers uniform corrosion in seawater. The measured corrosion current density is $25 \, \mu\text{A/cm}^2$. Calculate:
1. The total corrosion current
2. The mass loss per year
3. The corrosion rate in mm/year and mpy

Given: $M_{Fe} = 55.85 \text{ g/mol}$, $n = 2$, $\rho_{steel} = 7.87 \text{ g/cm}^3$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q. A (a) — 7 marks

**Electrochemical Mechanism (3 marks):**
- Corrosion of iron in seawater proceeds via a **galvanic cell** with microscopic anodes and cathodes distributed on the surface.
- **Anodic half-reaction:** $Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$ (metal dissolution; site of material loss).
- **Cathodic half-reaction (in aerated neutral seawater):** $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$.
- The overall reaction: $2Fe + O_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2Fe(OH)_2 \rightarrow Fe_2O_3 \cdot H_2O$ (rust).
- Electrons flow through the metal from anode to cathode; ions flow through the seawater closing the circuit.

**Role of Dissolved Oxygen (2 marks):**
- Oxygen is the **primary cathodic depolarizer** in seawater corrosion.
- Higher dissolved oxygen → higher cathodic reaction rate → higher overall corrosion rate.
- Aerated, turbulent zones (splash zone) corrode faster than stagnant, oxygen-poor zones.
- Differential aeration creates localized corrosion cells (crevice and pitting).

**Role of Salinity (2 marks):**
- Higher salinity (3.5% in open ocean) increases seawater **conductivity**, lowering the resistance of the electrolyte.
- Lower resistance → higher galvanic current for the same driving EMF → accelerated corrosion.
- However, beyond ~3.5% NaCl, oxygen solubility drops (salting-out), and corrosion rate peaks around seawater concentration.
- Chloride ions also penetrate passive films (e.g., on stainless steel), initiating pitting.

---

#### Model Solution for Q. A (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $A = 0.5 \text{ m}^2 = 5000 \text{ cm}^2$; $i_{corr} = 25 \, \mu\text{A/cm}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A/cm}^2$

**1) Total corrosion current (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{corr} &= i_{corr} \times A \\
         &= (25 \times 10^{-6}) \times 5000 \\
         &= 0.125 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**2) Mass loss per year (3 marks):**
For one year, $t = 365 \times 24 \times 3600 = 3.1536 \times 10^7 \text{ s}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} \\
  &= \dfrac{0.125 \times 3.1536 \times 10^7 \times 55.85}{2 \times 96{,}485} \\
  &= \dfrac{2.202 \times 10^{8}}{192{,}970} \\
  &= 1141 \text{ g/year} \\
  &\approx 1.14 \text{ kg/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**3) Corrosion rate in mm/yr and mpy (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \dfrac{m}{A \cdot \rho} = \dfrac{1141 \text{ g}}{5000 \text{ cm}^2 \times 7.87 \text{ g/cm}^3} \\
  &= 0.0290 \text{ cm/yr} = 0.290 \text{ mm/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
mpy &= 0.290 \text{ mm/yr} \times 39.37 \text{ mils/mm} \\
    &= 11.41 \text{ mpy}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $I = 0.125 \text{ A}$, $m = 1.14 \text{ kg/yr}$, $CR = 0.290 \text{ mm/yr} = 11.41 \text{ mpy}$

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** With neat sketches, explain the principle, types, and design criteria of **cathodic protection** for ship hulls. Compare sacrificial anode and impressed current systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 100 m² steel hull is to be protected by an impressed current cathodic protection (ICCP) system. If the required protective current density is $150 \text{ mA/m}^2$ and the system operates at 12 V with 70% overall efficiency, calculate:
1. The total protective current
2. The DC power input required
3. The annual electrical energy consumption in kWh. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q. B (a) — 7 marks

**Principle (2 marks):** Cathodic protection (CP) suppresses corrosion by forcing the entire protected structure to act as the cathode of an electrochemical cell, eliminating anodic dissolution. This is achieved by supplying electrons from an external source (either a sacrificial anode or an impressed DC current).

**Types (3 marks):**
1. **Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP):** Uses a more electrochemically active metal (Zn, Al, or Mg alloy) connected to the hull. The sacrificial anode corrodes preferentially. No external power required. Typical current: 10–50 mA/m².
2. **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP):** External DC rectifier forces current from inert anodes (Pt/Ti, MMO/Ti) through seawater to the hull. Higher currents possible (50–500 mA/m²); preferred for large vessels.

**Design Criteria (2 marks):**
- **Protection potential:** Hull must be polarized to **−0.85 V vs Cu/CuSO₄** reference electrode (or −0.80 V vs Ag/AgCl).
- **Current density:** Sufficient to overcome the natural corrosion current of the bare or coated surface.
- **Anode placement:** Distributed to achieve uniform current distribution; avoid "shielding" by close-fitting structures.
- **Anode lifetime:** Sacrificial anodes sized for 2–5 year replacement; ICCP anodes for 10–20+ years.

**Comparison Table (built into above points):**
| Parameter | SACP | ICCP |
|---|---|---|
| Power source | None | DC rectifier required |
| Anode material | Zn, Al, Mg | Pt/Ti, MMO/Ti |
| Current output | Limited by anode potential | Adjustable |
| Maintenance | Periodic anode replacement | Anode long-life; monitor controller |
| Best for | Small vessels, offshore rigs (initial) | Large ocean-going vessels |

---

#### Model Solution for Q. B (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $A = 100 \text{ m}^2$, $i_{prot} = 150 \text{ mA/m}^2 = 0.150 \text{ A/m}^2$, $V = 12 \text{ V}$, $\eta = 0.70$, $t = 1 \text{ year} = 8760 \text{ hr}$.

**1) Total protective current (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= i_{prot} \times A \\
  &= 0.150 \text{ A/m}^2 \times 100 \text{ m}^2 \\
  &= 15 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**2) DC power input required (2 marks):**
Electrical input power must compensate for system inefficiency:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{in} &= \dfrac{V \cdot I}{\eta} \\
       &= \dfrac{12 \text{ V} \times 15 \text{ A}}{0.70} \\
       &= \dfrac{180}{0.70} \\
       &= 257.14 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**3) Annual energy consumption (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= P_{in} \times t \\
  &= 257.14 \text{ W} \times 8760 \text{ hr} \\
  &= 2.252 \times 10^6 \text{ Wh} \\
  &= 2252 \text{ kWh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $I = 15 \text{ A}$, $P_{in} = 257.14 \text{ W}$, $E = 2252 \text{ kWh/year}$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that COST MARKS in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing the Standard EMF Series with the Galvanic Series in Seawater.** The galvanic series is empirical, environment-specific, and EXAM-RELEVANT. Aluminium is more anodic in seawater than the EMF series suggests.
> 2. **Forgetting units in Faraday's Law calculations.** Always convert time to **seconds**, area to **cm² or m²** consistently, and check whether the answer is in grams/year or kg/year.
> 3. **Mixing up anode and cathode in galvanic corrosion questions.** The MORE NOBLE metal is the cathode (protected); the LESS NOBLE metal is the anode (corrodes). Memorize the keyword: "**N**oble = **N**ot attacked."
> 4. **Stating "oxygen causes corrosion"** — incomplete. State that oxygen is the **cathodic depolarizer** in neutral/alkaline seawater.
> 5. **Not stating the protection potential value** (−0.85 V vs Cu/CuSO₄) — examiners specifically allocate 1–2 marks for this in CP questions.
> 6. **Skipping diagrams.** Even rough sketches of a corrosion cell or CP loop fetch easy 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Writing formulae without substituting values** — partial marks are forfeited unless the numerical substitution step is shown explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — Marine Materials and Corrosion**

**A. Core Definitions**
- ☐ Corrosion = irreversible electrochemical degradation of a metal back to its oxide/ore state.
- ☐ Marine materials = alloys and coatings engineered for seawater, splash-zone, and atmospheric-marine resistance.
- ☐ Galvanic corrosion = accelerated attack of anodic metal in a dissimilar-metal couple sharing a common electrolyte.

**B. Electrochemistry Essentials**
- ☐ Anodic reaction: $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$ (oxidation, material loss).
- ☐ Cathodic reaction in aerated seawater: $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$.
- ☐ Four elements of corrosion cell: Anode + Cathode + Electrolyte + Metallic path.
- ☐ Seawater: 3.5% salinity, ρ = 1.025 g/cm³, pH 8.1–8.3, conductivity ~0.04 S/cm.

**C. Eight Forms of Corrosion — Memorize Each**
- ☐ Uniform, Galvanic, Pitting, Crevice, Intergranular, SCC, Erosion-Cavitation, MIC.

**D. Galvanic Series in Seawater — Descending Nobility**
- ☐ Pt / C → Ti → Cu-Ni (70/30) → Monel → Cu-Ni (90/10) → Bronze → Cu → Pb → **Hydrogen line** → Steel → Cast Iron → Al alloys → Zn → Mg.

**E. Key Formulae — Must Memorize**
- ☐ $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ — Faraday's mass-loss law.
- ☐ $E = \dfrac{0.05916}{n} \log \dfrac{C_1}{C_2}$ — Nernst at 25 °C.
- ☐ $mpy = \dfrac{534 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}$ — corrosion rate in mpy.
- ☐ $I_{corr} = \dfrac{\Delta E}{R_{total}}$ — Ohm's law for corrosion cell.
- ☐ Protection criterion: **−0.85 V vs Cu/CuSO₄** for steel in seawater.

**F. Corrosion Control Methods**
- ☐ Cathodic protection (SACP — sacrificial; ICCP — impressed current).
- ☐ Protective coatings (barrier + sacrificial + anti-fouling).
- ☐ Material selection (Cu-Ni, Monel, super-duplex, titanium, GRP).
- ☐ Corrosion allowance (1–3 mm typical for marine steel).
- ☐ Design modifications (avoid crevices, dissimilar metals, stagnant zones).

**G. Conversion Factors**
- ☐ 1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/yr = 1 mil/yr.
- ☐ 1 year = 3.1536 × 10⁷ s = 8760 hr.
- ☐ Faraday constant F = 96,485 C/mol ≈ 26.8 A·hr/mol.

**H. Standard Examination Triggers (High-Frequency KTU Topics)**
- ☐ Calculate corrosion rate from $i_{corr}$ (must memorize the mpy formula).
- ☐ Galvanic corrosion with EMF/galvanic-series reasoning.
- ☐ Cathodic protection system design (current, power, energy).
- ☐ Comparison tables (SACP vs ICCP; corrosion forms; material selection).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties and Selection of Marine Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Properties and Selection of Marine Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marine Materials** are engineered metallic alloys, polymers, ceramics, and composite substances specifically formulated, processed, and qualified to withstand the extreme operational demands of the oceanic environment, including **seawater corrosion**, **hydrostatic pressure**, **cyclic wave loading**, **temperature gradients**, **biofouling**, and **galvanic interactions**, while maintaining structural integrity and seaworthiness over a design service life of typically **25–30 years**.

The **Selection of Marine Materials** is a multidisciplinary engineering decision-making process that integrates **mechanical performance** (strength, toughness, fatigue endurance), **chemical compatibility** (corrosion resistance in chloride-rich media), **fabricability** (weldability, formability), **economic viability**, and **regulatory compliance** with classification society rules (e.g., **DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, IRS, Bureau Veritas, ClassNK**) to identify the optimal material for a given marine component.

> [!NOTE]
> The single most defining characteristic of the marine environment is the presence of **~3.5% NaCl seawater**, which is approximately **500 times more corrosive** than fresh water. This makes corrosion the **dominant selection criterion**, far ahead of raw strength.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of marine materials like **clothing you wear on different expeditions**:

- A **cotton t-shirt** is fine for the beach — soft, breathable, cheap.
- A **wetsuit** is needed for diving — keeps you warm, resists salt, survives pressure.
- A **military-grade diving suit** is needed for deep underwater welding — extreme pressure resistance, fireproof, abrasion-resistant.

Similarly, ship hulls, propellers, engine components, and offshore platforms each need their "suit." A **mild steel hull plate** is the cotton t-shirt (cheap, strong, but rusts fast if unprotected). A **stainless steel propeller shaft** is the wetsuit (moderate cost, good salt resistance). A **titanium alloy deep-submersible pressure hull** is the military suit (expensive, extreme performance).

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb for Marine Engineers:** *The ocean is not just "wet air" — it is a complex electrochemical, biological, and mechanical loading environment. Never select a material for marine service based on strength alone.*

---

## 1.3 The Marine Environment — Demands Imposed on Materials

| Environmental Factor | Effect on Material | Critical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| **Seawater (3.5% NaCl)** | Electrochemical corrosion | **Cl⁻ concentration ≈ 19,000 ppm** |
| **Hydrostatic Pressure** | Compressive collapse | **~0.1 MPa per 10 m depth** |
| **Wave-induced Cyclic Loading** | Fatigue cracking | **10⁸ – 10⁹ cycles** in service life |
| **Temperature Variation** | Thermal stress, embrittlement | **−30 °C (Arctic) to +50 °C (tropical)** |
| **Marine Organisms (biofouling)** | Localized corrosion, pitting | Up to **10 mm/yr** wastage in some alloys |
| **Galvanic Coupling** | Accelerated dissimilar-metal attack | Voltage difference drives current |
| **UV Radiation** | Polymer degradation | Surface cracking, chalking |

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Density of seawater:** **ρ_sw ≈ 1025 kg/m³**
- **Density of mild steel:** **ρ_steel ≈ 7850 kg/m³**
- **Density of seawater (fresh water):** **ρ_fw = 1000 kg/m³**
- **Universal corrosion rate unit:** **mm/year (mmpy)** or **mpy (mils per year)**
- **Standard marine service temperature:** **−10 °C to +40 °C** (surface ships), **−2 °C to +30 °C** (subsea)
- **Design fatigue life for hull:** **≥ 25 years ≈ 10⁸ cycles** for wave loading
- **Young's Modulus of marine steel:** **E ≈ 200 GPa**
- **Service Life Design Target:** **≥ 25–30 years** for commercial vessels

---

## 1.5 Classification of Marine Materials (High-Level Overview)

```text
Marine Materials
├── 1. Metallic Materials
│   ├── Carbon & Mild Steels (ASTM A36, A131)
│   ├── High-Strength Low-Alloy (HSLA) Steels
│   ├── Stainless Steels (304, 316, Duplex, Super-duplex)
│   ├── Aluminum Alloys (5083, 5086, 5456, 6061)
│   ├── Copper Alloys (Naval brass, Cu-Ni 90/10, 70/30)
│   ├── Titanium Alloys (Grade 2, Ti-6Al-4V)
│   └── Nickel-Copper Alloys (Monel 400, K-500)
├── 2. Non-Metallic Materials
│   ├── FRP / GRP Composites (Fibreglass, Carbon-fibre)
│   ├── Marine-grade polymers (HDPE, UHMWPE)
│   ├── Marine plywood & timber
│   └── Rubber/elastomers (neoprene, EPDM)
└── 3. Hybrid / Sandwich Structures
    ├── Steel-Aluminum transition joints
    └── GRP-wood sandwich (small craft)
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Mechanical Properties Critical to Marine Selection

The four pillars of mechanical performance in marine applications are:

### (a) **Yield Strength (σ_y)** and **Ultimate Tensile Strength (σ_UTS)**
- **σ_y** determines the elastic limit beyond which permanent (plastic) deformation begins.
- For ship hull plates, **σ_y ≥ 235 MPa** is mandatory (Class A grade per Lloyd's Register, equivalent to mild steel).
- High-tensile grades (e.g., **AH36, DH36, EH36**) require **σ_y ≥ 355 MPa**.

### (b) **Fracture Toughness (K_IC)**
- Resistance to brittle crack propagation — critical for Arctic operations.
- Measured in **MPa·√m**.
- Minimum acceptable for marine steel: **K_IC ≥ 60 MPa·√m** at service temperature.

### (c) **Fatigue Strength / Endurance Limit (σ_e)**
- Many marine steels exhibit an endurance limit at **~10⁶ cycles**.
- For long-life hull structures, design stress must be **< σ_e / Safety Factor**.
- In corrosive seawater, fatigue strength drops by **30–50%** (corrosion-fatigue interaction).

### (d) **Impact Toughness (Charpy V-notch)**
- Measured in **Joules (J)**.
- Required minimum for hull steel at 0 °C: **27 J** (per IACS UR S6 requirements).

---

## 2.2 Chemical Properties — Corrosion Behavior

Corrosion is the **single largest cause of marine structural failure**, accounting for **~30% of all ship casualties** involving structural degradation.

**Key chemical metrics:**
- **Corrosion Rate (CR)** measured in **mm/year** or **mpy**.
- **Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN)** for stainless steels:
  $$\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}$$
- **Galvanic Series Position**: Predicts which metal becomes the anode (corrodes) when coupled with another.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitting is more dangerous than uniform corrosion** because penetration occurs locally and is often invisible until catastrophic failure.

---

## 2.3 Physical & Environmental Properties

| Property | Significance | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Density (ρ)** | Weight, buoyancy, stability | Steel 7850 kg/m³; Al 2700 kg/m³ |
| **Thermal Expansion (α)** | Thermal stress in mixed-metal joints | Steel ~12×10⁻⁶ /K |
| **Thermal Conductivity (k)** | Heat exchangers, engine parts | Al 205 W/m·K; Steel 50 W/m·K |
| **Electrical Resistivity** | Cathodic protection design | Steel ~1.5×10⁻⁷ Ω·m |
| **Magnetic Permeability** | Magnetic signature (warships) | Austenitic SS ≈ 1.005 (non-magnetic) |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **F1** | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Engineering stress | Pa (N/m²) |
| **F2** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Engineering strain | dimensionless |
| **F3** | $\sigma_y = E \cdot \varepsilon$ (Hooke's Law) | Linear elastic region | Pa |
| **F4** | $\sigma_e = \dfrac{\Delta \sigma}{2} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min}}{2}$ | Stress amplitude | Pa |
| **F5** | $\sigma_a = \sigma_e \cdot (2N_f)^b$ (Basquin) | S-N curve in finite life region | Pa |
| **F6** | $N_f = \left(\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_f'}\right)^{1/b}$ | Cycles to failure | cycles |
| **F7** | $D = \sum \limits_{i=1}^{k} \dfrac{n_i}{N_i}$ (Miner's Rule) | Cumulative fatigue damage | dimensionless |
| **F8** | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{87600 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Corrosion rate (weight loss method) | mm/yr |
| **F9** | $\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \cdot \%\text{Mo} + 16 \cdot \%\text{N}$ | Pitting resistance | dimensionless |
| **F10** | $\text{SF} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{allowable}}}{\sigma_{\text{working}}}$ | Factor of Safety | dimensionless |
| **F11** | $K_{IC} = Y \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Fracture toughness | MPa·√m |
| **F12** | $\text{FS} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{sw}} - \rho_{\text{material}}}{\rho_{\text{sw}}}$ (submerged) | Specific gravity ratio for buoyancy | dimensionless |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **F5 (Basquin's Law)** is used directly by classification societies (DNV GL, Lloyd's) for fatigue assessment of ship hulls under spectral wave loading.
- **F7 (Miner's Rule)** enables cumulative damage calculations for vessels transiting multiple wave climates.
- **F8 (Corrosion Rate)** is the foundation of **corrosion allowance** in hull plate thickness design.
- **F9 (PREN)** is the universal ranking metric for stainless steel selection in ballast tanks, seawater piping, and offshore.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU numericals**, the three most-tested formulas are **F1, F5/F6 (S-N curve), and F8 (corrosion rate).**

---

## 2.6 The Material Selection Logic Flow

The selection follows a **hierarchical exclusion process**:

1. **Regulatory filter** → Must satisfy class rules (e.g., ABS, DNV).
2. **Strength filter** → σ_y ≥ required design stress.
3. **Corrosion filter** → Corrosion rate ≤ acceptable limit (typically **0.1–0.5 mm/yr**).
4. **Fabrication filter** → Weldable, formable, machinable.
5. **Cost filter** → Within project budget.
6. **Weight optimization** (for high-performance vessels).
7. **Final selection** based on lowest life-cycle cost.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Corrosion Rate from Weight-Loss Method

The standard weight-loss corrosion rate equation used universally in marine engineering is derived from Faraday's law combined with mass-to-penetration conversion.

**Step 1 — Experimental measurement:**
A coupon of mass $W$ grams is exposed to seawater for time $t$ hours. The mass loss is $\Delta m$ grams.

$$
\Delta m = W_{\text{initial}} - W_{\text{final}}
$$

**Step 2 — Corrosion current concept:**
Mass loss is proportional to the total electric charge passed during corrosion (Faraday's Law):

$$
\Delta m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t_{\text{sec}}}{n \cdot F}
$$

where $M$ = molar mass of metal (g/mol), $I$ = corrosion current (A), $n$ = valency, $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**.

**Step 3 — Convert mass loss to uniform penetration depth:**
If $\rho$ is the density of the metal and $A$ is the exposed surface area:

$$
\text{Thickness loss } (\text{mm}) = \dfrac{\Delta m \cdot 1000}{\rho \cdot A \cdot 1000}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \dfrac{\Delta m \times 8760}{\rho \times A \times t \times 1000} \times 1000
$$

which reduces to the standard form:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{CR} = \dfrac{87600 \cdot \Delta m}{\rho \cdot A \cdot t}\;}
$$

**Units check:**
- $\Delta m$ in grams
- $\rho$ in g/cm³
- $A$ in cm²
- $t$ in hours

The factor **87,600** converts hours → year (24 × 365 × 10).

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation assumes **uniform corrosion**. For pitting, a **pitting factor** (ratio of deepest pit to average penetration) is applied, typically **4 to 8**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Fatigue Life via Basquin's Equation

**Step 1 — Stress amplitude definition:**
Under cyclic loading with maximum $\sigma_{\max}$ and minimum $\sigma_{\min}$:

$$
\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min}}{2}, \qquad \sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{\max} + \sigma_{\min}}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Empirical S-N curve (high-cycle regime):**
For steels, when $N > 10^3$ cycles, the Basquin relation holds:

$$
\sigma_a = \sigma_f' \cdot (2N_f)^b
$$

where $\sigma_f'$ is the **fatigue strength coefficient** (≈ σ_UTS for many steels) and $b$ is the **fatigue strength exponent** (typically **−0.05 to −0.12** for steels).

**Step 3 — Solve for cycles to failure:**

$$
\boxed{\;N_f = \dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_f'} \right)^{1/b}\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Incorporate corrosion-fatigue correction:**
In seawater, the effective fatigue strength is reduced:

$$
\sigma_{a,\text{eff}} = \sigma_a \cdot C_{\text{CF}}
$$

where $C_{\text{CF}} \approx 0.5$–$0.7$ for unprotected steel in aerated seawater.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Examples

### **Example 1 — Corrosion Rate Calculation**

**Problem:** A steel hull plate (ρ = 7.85 g/cm³) with exposed area **A = 200 cm²** is exposed to seawater for **6 months (4380 hours)**. The mass loss is **Δm = 18.4 g**. Calculate the corrosion rate in mm/yr.

**Solution:**

$$
\text{CR} = \dfrac{87600 \times 18.4}{7.85 \times 200 \times 4380}
$$

Step-by-step evaluation:

$$
\text{Numerator} = 87600 \times 18.4 = 1{,}611{,}840
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 7.85 \times 200 \times 4380 = 6{,}876{,}600
$$

$$
\text{CR} = \dfrac{1{,}611{,}840}{6{,}876{,}600} = 0.2344 \;\text{mm/yr}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{CR} \approx 0.234 \;\text{mm/yr}\;}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is **moderate corrosion**, typical for unprotected mild steel in tidal/splash zone. A **2 mm corrosion allowance** would last ~8.5 years.

---

### **Example 2 — Fatigue Life via S-N Curve**

**Problem:** A marine component is subjected to fully reversed loading with $\sigma_{\max} = +120$ MPa, $\sigma_{\min} = -120$ MPa. Material: steel with $\sigma_f' = 600$ MPa, $b = -0.10$. Determine cycles to failure.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute stress amplitude:

$$
\sigma_a = \dfrac{120 - (-120)}{2} = 120 \;\text{MPa}
$$

Step 2 — Apply Basquin's equation:

$$
\sigma_a = \sigma_f' \cdot (2N_f)^b
$$

$$
120 = 600 \cdot (2N_f)^{-0.10}
$$

Step 3 — Rearrange:

$$
(2N_f)^{-0.10} = \dfrac{120}{600} = 0.2
$$

Step 4 — Take reciprocal and apply logs:

$$
(2N_f)^{0.10} = 5
$$

$$
2N_f = 5^{10}
$$

$$
5^{10} = 9{,}765{,}625
$$

$$
2N_f = 9{,}765{,}625
$$

$$
N_f = 4{,}882{,}812.5 \;\text{cycles}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;N_f \approx 4.88 \times 10^6 \;\text{cycles}\;}
$$

**In seawater (with C_CF = 0.6):**

$$
\sigma_{a,\text{eff}} = \dfrac{120}{0.6} = 200 \;\text{MPa}
$$

$$
N_{f,\text{wet}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \left(\dfrac{200}{600}\right)^{1/(-0.10)} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{3}\right)^{-10}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 3^{10} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 59{,}049 = 29{,}524.5 \;\text{cycles}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;N_{f,\text{wet}} \approx 2.95 \times 10^4 \;\text{cycles}\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical insight:** Fatigue life drops from ~5 million cycles in air to ~30,000 cycles in seawater — a **165× reduction**. This is why corrosion-fatigue is the dominant failure mode in marine engineering, not pure fatigue.

---

### **Example 3 — PREN-Based Stainless Steel Selection**

**Problem:** Compare PREN for the following stainless steels and identify which is suitable for hot seawater piping (need PREN ≥ 40):

| Steel | Cr (%) | Mo (%) | N (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 304 | 18 | 0 | 0 |
| 316 | 17 | 2.5 | 0 |
| Duplex | 22 | 3.0 | 0.15 |
| Super-duplex | 25 | 4.0 | 0.30 |

**Solution:**

Steel 304:
$$
\text{PREN} = 18 + 3.3 \times 0 + 16 \times 0 = 18
$$

Steel 316:
$$
\text{PREN} = 17 + 3.3 \times 2.5 + 16 \times 0 = 17 + 8.25 = 25.25
$$

Duplex:
$$
\text{PREN} = 22 + 3.3 \times 3.0 + 16 \times 0.15 = 22 + 9.9 + 2.4 = 34.3
$$

Super-duplex:
$$
\text{PREN} = 25 + 3.3 \times 4.0 + 16 \times 0.30 = 25 + 13.2 + 4.8 = 43.0
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Only Super-duplex qualifies with PREN} = 43.0 \geq 40\;}
$$

---

### **Example 4 — Cumulative Fatigue Damage (Miner's Rule)**

**Problem:** A propeller blade experiences three load blocks:
- Block 1: $\sigma_1 = 200$ MPa for $n_1 = 10{,}000$ cycles (gives $N_1 = 50{,}000$ cycles to failure)
- Block 2: $\sigma_2 = 150$ MPa for $n_2 = 30{,}000$ cycles (gives $N_2 = 200{,}000$ cycles)
- Block 3: $\sigma_3 = 120$ MPa for $n_3 = 100{,}000$ cycles (gives $N_3 = 800{,}000$ cycles)

**Solution:**

$$
D = \sum \dfrac{n_i}{N_i} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{50{,}000} + \dfrac{30{,}000}{200{,}000} + \dfrac{100{,}000}{800{,}000}
$$

$$
D = 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.125 = 0.475
$$

$$
\boxed{\;D = 0.475 < 1.0 \implies \text{SAFE}\;}
$$

Since $D < 1$, the structure has accumulated only **47.5% of allowable damage**, with remaining life = **(1 − 0.475) / 0.475 ≈ 110% more damage budget** before reaching failure threshold.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Marine Material Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Component] --> B{Service Environment}
    B -->|Submerged| C[Check Corrosion Resistance]
    B -->|Atmospheric| D[Check Weathering]
    B -->|Internal Dry| E[Check Strength Only]

    C --> F{Required PREN}
    F -->|Less than 25| G[Use 304 SS or Mild Steel + Coating]
    F -->|25 to 35| H[Use 316 SS or Duplex]
    F -->|Greater than 35| I[Use Super-duplex or Titanium]

    G --> J[Cost Filter]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    D --> J
    E --> J

    J --> K{Weight Critical}
    K -->|Yes| L[Choose Aluminum 5083 or Composite]
    K -->|No| M[Choose Steel]

    L --> N{Weldability Required}
    M --> N

    N -->|Yes| O[Verify Welding Procedure Qualification]
    N -->|No| P[Use Adhesive Bonded Composite]

    O --> Q[Final Selection]
    P --> Q
```

---

## 4.2 Marine Environment Zones and Material Demand Map

```mermaid
    subgraph Atmospheric Zone
        A1[Splash Zone - Most Severe]

    end

        B3[Deep Submerged - More than 200 m]
    end

        C1[Ballast Tanks - Alternate Wet Dry]
        C2[Cargo Holds - Variable]
        C3[Engine Rooms - Heat and Oil]
    end

    A1 --> D1[Demands: Highest Corrosion Resistance and Coating]
    A2 --> D2[Demands: UV Resistance for Polymers]
    B1 --> D3[Demands: Cathodic Protection Compatible]
    B2 --> D4[Demands: Biofouling Resistant Alloys]
    B3 --> D5[Demands: Pressure Resistant and Tough]
    C1 --> D6[Demands: Coating and Corrosion Allowance]
    C2 --> D7[Demands: Abrasive Resistant Linings]
    C3 --> D8[Demands: Heat Resistant and Fire Safe]
```

## 4.3 Properties Hierarchy for Marine Material Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    P1 --> P1a[Yield Strength]

    P2 --> P2a[Corrosion Rate]

    P3 --> P3a[Density]

    P3 --> P3d[Magnetic Permeability]

    P4 --> P4a[Weldability]

    P5 --> P5a[Initial Cost]

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any three key mechanical properties required for a marine hull steel and state the minimum acceptable value for each as per IACS classification society rules.**

**Model Answer:**

The three critical mechanical properties are:

1. **Yield Strength (σ_y):** Minimum **235 MPa** for normal-strength hull steel (Class A per Lloyd's Register). For high-tensile grades (AH36), minimum is **355 MPa**.

2. **Impact Toughness (Charpy V-notch):** Minimum **27 J at 0 °C** as per IACS UR S6 (Unified Requirements for Hull Structural Steel). This ensures resistance to brittle fracture in cold seas.

3. **Ultimate Tensile Strength (σ_UTS):** For Grade A steel, must lie in the range **400–520 MPa**, providing adequate ductility reserve.

> [!NOTE]
> All values must be backed by **IACS UR S6 / S7 / S8** referenced standards.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is the Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN)? Calculate the PREN of a duplex stainless steel containing 22% Cr, 5% Mo, and 0.15% N. Is it suitable for seawater service?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
PREN is an empirical ranking metric that predicts the resistance of a stainless steel to **pitting corrosion** in chloride-rich environments. Higher PREN indicates greater pitting resistance.

**Formula:**
$$
\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}
$$

**Calculation:**
$$
\text{PREN} = 22 + 3.3 \times 5 + 16 \times 0.15
$$

$$
= 22 + 16.5 + 2.4 = 40.9
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{PREN} = 40.9\;}
$$

**Suitability:** **YES.** A PREN ≥ 40 qualifies for **seawater service**, including hot chloride environments. This alloy qualifies as a **super-duplex stainless steel** and is acceptable for ballast piping and offshore components.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B-A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — **14 Marks**

**Q: A ship hull plate made of mild steel (density = 7.85 g/cm³) with exposed surface area 500 cm² was subjected to seawater immersion for one year. After retrieval, the mass loss was found to be 92 g.**

**(a)** Calculate the corrosion rate in mm/year. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Determine the corrosion allowance required for a 25-year design life, and recommend a suitable corrosion protection strategy. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Corrosion Rate Calculation

**Given:**
- Density, ρ = 7.85 g/cm³
- Surface area, A = 500 cm²
- Exposure time, t = 8760 hours (1 year)
- Mass loss, Δm = 92 g

**Formula (derived in Section 3.1):**

$$
\text{CR} = \dfrac{87{,}600 \cdot \Delta m}{\rho \cdot A \cdot t}
$$

**Substituting:**

$$
\text{CR} = \dfrac{87{,}600 \times 92}{7.85 \times 500 \times 8760}
$$

**Step 1 — Numerator:**

$$
87{,}600 \times 92 = 8{,}059{,}200
$$

**Step 2 — Denominator:**

$$
7.85 \times 500 = 3925
$$

$$
3925 \times 8760 = 34{,}383{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Final division:**

$$
\text{CR} = \dfrac{8{,}059{,}200}{34{,}383{,}000} = 0.2344 \;\text{mm/yr}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{CR} \approx 0.234 \;\text{mm/yr}\;}
$$

**Interpretation:** This corrosion rate is **moderate**, typical for unprotected mild steel in fully submerged aerated seawater (literature values range from 0.1 to 0.5 mm/yr).

---

### Part (b) — Corrosion Allowance & Protection Strategy

**Step 1 — Corrosion allowance for 25 years:**

$$
\text{CA} = \text{CR} \times \text{Design Life}
$$

$$
\text{CA} = 0.234 \times 25 = 5.86 \;\text{mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{CA} \approx 6 \;\text{mm (rounded up for safety)}\;}
$$

**Step 2 — Recommended protection strategy (multi-layered):**

1. **Coating System (Primary Defense):**
   - Coal-tar epoxy or glass-flake epoxy coating on hull plate
   - Coating thickness: **250–500 μm**
   - Expected service life of coating: **5–10 years** before recoating

2. **Cathodic Protection (Secondary Defense):**
   - **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)** with reference electrode for large vessels
   - **Sacrificial Zinc or Aluminum Anodes** for ballast tanks
   - Maintains hull at **−0.85 V vs. Ag/AgCl** reference

3. **Corrosion Allowance (Tertiary Defense):**
   - Built into plate thickness: **6 mm extra** above structural requirement

4. **Operational Measures:**
   - Regular in-service hull inspections (annual, plus 5-year docking)
   - Avoid prolonged idling in harbors (stagnant water increases attack)

**Justification:** A single protection method is insufficient; the **defense-in-depth approach** ensures reliability over 25 years.

---

### **Question B-B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — **14 Marks** (Alternative Choice)

**Q: With a neat sketch, describe the fatigue failure mechanism in marine steels. A marine component is subjected to fully reversed axial loading with σ_max = 200 MPa. The material has fatigue strength coefficient σ_f' = 800 MPa and exponent b = −0.09.**

**(a)** Determine the cycles to failure in air using Basquin's equation. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** If the component operates in seawater with corrosion-fatigue factor C_CF = 0.6, calculate the new fatigue life and discuss the protective measures to mitigate corrosion-fatigue. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Fatigue Life in Air

**Step 1 — Stress amplitude:**

Fully reversed loading ⇒ $\sigma_{\min} = -200$ MPa

$$
\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min}}{2} = \dfrac{200 - (-200)}{2} = 200 \;\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Basquin's equation:**

$$
\sigma_a = \sigma_f' \cdot (2N_f)^b
$$

$$
200 = 800 \cdot (2N_f)^{-0.09}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange:**

$$
(2N_f)^{-0.09} = \dfrac{200}{800} = 0.25
$$

**Step 4 — Take both sides to power 1/−0.09:**

$$
(2N_f) = (0.25)^{-1/0.09} = (0.25)^{-11.111}
$$

Using logarithms:

$$
\log_{10}(0.25) = -0.6021
$$

$$
-0.6021 \times (-11.111) = 6.690
$$

$$
2N_f = 10^{6.690} = 4.90 \times 10^6
$$

$$
N_f = 2.45 \times 10^6 \;\text{cycles}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;N_{f,\text{air}} \approx 2.45 \times 10^6 \;\text{cycles}\;}
$$

---

### Part (b) — Fatigue Life in Seawater

**Step 1 — Effective stress in seawater:**

$$
\sigma_{a,\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\sigma_a}{C_{CF}} = \dfrac{200}{0.6} = 333.33 \;\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Basquin's equation with effective stress:**

$$
333.33 = 800 \cdot (2N_f)^{-0.09}
$$

$$
(2N_f)^{-0.09} = \dfrac{333.33}{800} = 0.4167
$$

$$
\log_{10}(0.4167) = -0.3802
$$

$$
-0.3802 \times (-11.111) = 4.224
$$

$$
2N_f = 10^{4.224} = 1.67 \times 10^4
$$

$$
N_f = 8.36 \times 10^3 \;\text{cycles}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;N_{f,\text{wet}} \approx 8.36 \times 10^3 \;\text{cycles}\;}
$$

**Step 3 — Life reduction ratio:**

$$
\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{2.45 \times 10^6}{8.36 \times 10^3} \approx 293
$$

**Fatigue life reduces by ~293 times** in seawater compared to air.

**Step 4 — Protective measures to mitigate corrosion-fatigue:**

1. **Cathodic Protection:** Maintain electrochemical potential at **−0.85 V vs Ag/AgCl** to suppress anodic dissolution at crack tips.

2. **Surface Treatments:** Shot peening to introduce **compressive residual stresses** at the surface, retarding crack initiation.

3. **Protective Coatings:** Epoxy or polyurethane coatings to isolate the metal from seawater. Apply with **NACE Level 2 surface preparation minimum**.

4. **Use of Corrosion-Fatigue-Resistant Alloys:** Replace mild steel with **austenitic stainless steel (e.g., 316 LN)** or **nickel-aluminum bronze** for critical components.

5. **Design Modification:** Reduce stress concentration by increasing fillet radii and avoiding sharp transitions.

6. **Environmental Control:** Avoid stagnant water zones; ensure drainage in ballast tanks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Material Selection Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 87,600** in the corrosion rate formula — examiners strictly deduct **1 mark** for incorrect unit conversion.
> 2. **Confusing σ_a (stress amplitude) with σ_max (maximum stress)** — this leads to errors of order-of-magnitude in $N_f$.
> 3. **Using σ_UTS instead of σ_f' in Basquin's equation** — although often numerically similar, the **fatigue strength coefficient** is technically distinct.
> 4. **Neglecting the corrosion-fatigue correction factor** in seawater fatigue problems — this costs full marks on part (b) type questions.
> 5. **Stating PREN without specifying the formula** — definition alone is incomplete; the examiner expects the **empirical equation**.
> 6. **Recommending "use stainless steel"** as a protection strategy without specifying grade and PREN — this is considered **superficial** and loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ Marine materials must resist **3.5% NaCl corrosion**, **cyclic fatigue**, **hydrostatic pressure**, and **biofouling** simultaneously.
- ✅ **Carbon/Mild Steel (ASTM A131)** is the workhorse of ship hulls due to **low cost and high weldability**, but requires coating + cathodic protection.
- ✅ **High-tensile steel (AH36/DH36/EH36)** offers σ_y ≥ 355 MPa for high-stress regions.
- ✅ **Aluminum alloys (5083-H116, 5086, 5456)** are used for **superstructures and fast ferries** to reduce top weight (≈ 65% weight saving vs steel).
- ✅ **Stainless steels** are ranked using **PREN**; minimum for seawater = **40**.
- ✅ **Copper-nickel alloys (90/10 and 70/30)** dominate seawater piping due to **excellent biofouling resistance**.
- ✅ **Titanium (Grade 2)** is used in submarine pressure hulls and heat exchangers — **zero corrosion rate** in seawater.
- ✅ **Corrosion rate formula** uses the constant **87,600** to convert hourly data to annual penetration.
- ✅ **Basquin's law** governs high-cycle fatigue; **Coffin-Manson** governs low-cycle (plastic) fatigue.
- ✅ **Miner's rule**: cumulative damage $D < 1$ ⇒ safe; $D \geq 1$ ⇒ failure predicted.
- ✅ Class society rules (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) are the **legal binding standard** for marine material selection — academic choice alone is insufficient.
- ✅ For **warships**, **non-magnetic austenitic stainless steel** (e.g., Nitronic 50) is used to minimize magnetic signature.
- ✅ **Defense-in-depth strategy** = coating + cathodic protection + corrosion allowance + inspection.

### **Key Numbers to Memorize**

| Constant / Value | Number |
|---|---|
| Seawater density | **1025 kg/m³** |
| Steel density | **7850 kg/m³** |
| Aluminum density | **2700 kg/m³** |
| Mild steel hull σ_y (min) | **235 MPa** |
| AH36 σ_y (min) | **355 MPa** |
| Charpy impact (Class A, 0 °C) | **27 J** |
| Hours per year (corrosion factor) | **8760** |
| Conversion factor in CR formula | **87,600** |
| PREN threshold for seawater SS | **≥ 40** |
| Design fatigue life of hull | **≥ 25 years / 10⁸ cycles** |
| Corrosion-fatigue reduction factor | **0.5 – 0.7** |

### **Three Golden Rules of Marine Material Selection**

1. **Corrosion First, Strength Second.** A strong material that corrodes is a liability; a moderately strong, corrosion-resistant material is an asset.
2. **Class Society Compliance is Mandatory.** Selection without DNV/LR/ABS approval is non-compliant for commercial vessels.
3. **Life-Cycle Cost > Initial Cost.** A cheap material requiring frequent maintenance often costs **2–3× more** over 25 years than a premium, low-maintenance alloy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrosion Mechanisms and Prevention
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Corrosion Mechanisms and Prevention

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Corrosion?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Corrosion** is the *physico-chemical interaction* (typically electrochemical in nature) between a metal and its environment, resulting in the *progressive deterioration* and degradation of the metal's mechanical, chemical, and structural properties. The most common form in maritime engineering is the **oxidation of metals** in the presence of water and oxygen, forming stable compounds such as oxides, hydroxides, or salts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (KTU Board Expected):**
> The standard reference electrode potential for the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** is defined as **E° = 0.000 V at 25 °C (298.15 K)**. All other standard reduction potentials are referenced against this value.
> Faraday's Constant **F = 96,485 C/mol** (≈ 96,500 C/mol).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy: "The Rusty Ship's Wounds"**
> Imagine a ship's steel hull as a *healthy human body* immersed continuously in salty seawater. The salt water acts like a slow-acting *acidic bath*. Just as a cut on the skin bleeds and gets infected if untreated, the steel hull develops tiny *electrical wounds* (microscopic galvanic cells) where iron atoms get "eaten" by oxygen. The result is **rust** (Fe₂O₃·nH₂O) — essentially the steel is "bleeding out" its iron atoms into the seawater. Corrosion prevention methods (paint, sacrificial zincs, impressed current) act as *bandages, vaccines, and protective shields* for the hull.

## 1.3 The Electrochemical Nature of Corrosion

Corrosion in marine environments is fundamentally an **electrochemical process**, which means it involves the simultaneous occurrence of two half-reactions at different sites on the metal surface:

| Half-Reaction | Location | Process |
|---|---|---|
| **Anodic Reaction** (Oxidation) | Anode region | Metal loses electrons: $\text{M} \rightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + ne^{-}$ |
| **Cathodic Reaction** (Reduction) | Cathode region | Electrons consumed by species in electrolyte |

The three essential conditions for corrosion (the **Corrosion Triangle**) are:

1. **Anode** (site of metal dissolution / oxidation)
2. **Cathode** (site of reduction reaction)
3. **Electrolyte** (conductive medium — e.g., seawater)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *direction of electron flow*. Electrons travel from **anode to cathode** through the metal, while **ions move through the electrolyte**. Conventional current flows opposite to electrons.

---

## 1.4 Galvanic Series vs. Electrochemical Series

In maritime engineering, the **Galvanic Series in Seawater** is more practical than the standard electrochemical series because it accounts for *real marine conditions* (biofouling, passivity, aeration).

**Key Marine Metals (Anodic → Cathodic):**

$$\text{Magnesium} \rightarrow \text{Zinc} \rightarrow \text{Aluminum} \rightarrow \text{Steel} \rightarrow \text{Brass} \rightarrow \text{Copper} \rightarrow \text{Monel} \rightarrow \text{Titanium} \rightarrow \text{Graphite}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** When two dissimilar metals are coupled in seawater, the one *higher (more anodic)* in the galvanic series corrodes preferentially — this is the principle behind **sacrificial anode cathodic protection**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Components of a Corrosion Cell

A functioning corrosion cell requires four components operating simultaneously:

1. **Anode** — region where metal atoms lose electrons (oxidized) and dissolve into the electrolyte as ions.
2. **Cathode** — region where the electrons liberated at the anode are consumed by reduction of species from the electrolyte (typically $\text{O}_2$ or $\text{H}^+$).
3. **Metallic Path (Electron Conductor)** — internal electrical connection allowing electron flow from anode to cathode.
4. **Electrolyte** — ionic conductor (seawater) that completes the circuit by ion migration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters in Maritime Safety:** Seawater is a *near-perfect electrolyte* (conductivity ≈ **50,000 μS/cm**), making ship hulls and offshore structures especially vulnerable. The dissolved oxygen content (typically **6–8 mg/L**) drives the cathodic reaction continuously.

## 2.2 Primary Cathodic Reactions in Marine Corrosion

Depending on dissolved oxygen availability and pH, the cathodic reaction varies:

$$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-} \quad \text{(neutral/alkaline, aerated seawater)}$$

$$\text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^{+} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \text{(acidic conditions)}$$

$$2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + 2\text{OH}^{-} \quad \text{(oxygen-depleted / anaerobic)}$$

## 2.3 Common Forms of Marine Corrosion

| Type | Description | Maritime Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Uniform (General) Corrosion** | Even metal loss across entire surface | Hull plates exposed to seawater |
| **Galvanic (Bimetallic) Corrosion** | Accelerated attack on anodic metal when coupled to a more cathodic metal | Bronze propeller on steel hull |
| **Pitting Corrosion** | Localized deep cavities; penetrates rapidly with little surface indication | Stainless steel propeller shaft |
| **Crevice Corrosion** | Accelerated attack in stagnant gaps | Plate rivets, gasket surfaces |
| **Intergranular Corrosion** | Attack along grain boundaries | Welded stainless steel (sensitization) |
| **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** | Combined effect of tensile stress and corrosive medium | Brass condenser tubes (ammonia SCC) |
| **Erosion-Corrosion** | Mechanical wear + chemical attack | Pump impellers, propeller tips |
| **Cavitation Corrosion** | Vapor bubble collapse damages passive film | High-speed propellers |
| **Microbial (MIC)** | Bacteria-induced corrosion | Ballast tanks, submerged hulls |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All equations below are exam-critical.** Memorize the form, units, and physical meaning.

| # | Formula / Law | Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nernst Equation** | $E = E° + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \dfrac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]}$ | $E$ = electrode potential (V), $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T$ = temperature (K), $n$ = electrons transferred, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol | Predicts electrode potential under non-standard conditions |
| 2 | **Nernst (Base-10 form)** | $E = E° + \dfrac{0.0592}{n} \log \dfrac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]}$ at 25 °C | Uses log₁₀, factor 0.0592 V | KTU-typical calculation form |
| 3 | **Faraday's First Law** | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | $m$ = mass dissolved (g), $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $M$ = molar mass (g/mol) | Mass of metal lost in corrosion |
| 4 | **Faraday's Second Law** | $\dfrac{m_1}{m_2} = \dfrac{M_1 / n_1}{M_2 / n_2} = \dfrac{EW_1}{EW_2}$ | $EW$ = equivalent weight (g/eq) | Compare metal losses at same charge |
| 5 | **Equivalent Weight** | $EW = \dfrac{M}{n}$ | $M$ = molar mass, $n$ = valency | Used in Faraday's law |
| 6 | **Corrosion Current Density** | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{I_{corr}}{A}$ | $i_{corr}$ = A/m², $A$ = area | Rate of metal loss per unit area |
| 7 | **Corrosion Penetration Rate** | $CPR = \dfrac{K \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ (mpy) | $K = 0.129$ (constant for mpy), $\rho$ = density (g/cm³) | Engineering corrosion rate (mils per year) |
| 8 | **Cell EMF (Galvanic)** | $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ | Both as reduction potentials | Driving force for galvanic corrosion |
| 9 | **Stern-Geary Equation** | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{R_p}$ | $\beta_a, \beta_c$ = Tafel slopes, $R_p$ = polarization resistance (Ω) | Electrochemical corrosion rate measurement |
| 10 | **Pitting Resistance Number (PREN)** | $PREN = \%Cr + 3.3 \times \%Mo + 16 \times \%N$ | Composition in wt % | Stainless steel selection |

## 2.5 Why These Equations Matter in Maritime Engineering

- **Nernst Equation** is used to predict *whether* corrosion will occur in a specific seawater composition (e.g., pH 8.2 typical, temperature 15–25 °C).
- **Faraday's Law** allows ship operators to *quantify* hull thickness loss over a voyage and schedule dry-dock maintenance.
- **Stern-Geary Equation** is the foundation of *linear polarization resistance (LPR) probes* installed on offshore platforms to monitor corrosion in real time.
- **PREN** guides material selection for components like *propellers and seawater piping*.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** A typical oil tanker hull made of mild steel can lose **0.1–0.3 mm/year** in seawater without protection. Over a 25-year service life, this represents **2.5–7.5 mm** of steel loss — a critical structural concern that justifies the multi-million-dollar cathodic protection systems fitted to every commercial vessel.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Starting Point:** The change in Gibbs free energy $\Delta G$ is related to cell potential $E$ by:
> $$\Delta G = -nFE$$
> The free energy change for a reaction at non-standard conditions is given by the thermodynamic relation:
> $$\Delta G = \Delta G° + RT \ln Q$$
> where $Q$ is the reaction quotient.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Combine the two expressions for $\Delta G$:

$$
-nFE = -nFE° + RT \ln Q
$$

**Step 2:** Divide both sides by $-nF$ (note the sign reversal):

$$
E = E° - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q
$$

**Step 3:** For the convention $Q = \dfrac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]}$, and converting $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$ using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$
E = E° - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log_{10} \frac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]}
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the constant at standard temperature $T = 298.15$ K:

$$
\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96{,}485} = 0.05916 \, \text{V}
$$

**Step 5:** Therefore, at 25 °C, the **practical Nernst equation** is:

$$
\boxed{E = E° - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log_{10} \frac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The minus sign appears when we use the **log of [Ox]/[Red]** form. Some textbooks write it with a plus sign by inverting the ratio to [Red]/[Ox]. Both are equivalent.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Nernst Equation in Seawater

> **Problem (KTU Typical):** A copper-zinc galvanic couple is immersed in seawater at 25 °C. The copper half-cell has $[\text{Cu}^{2+}] = 0.01$ M. Given $E°(\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}) = +0.34$ V, calculate the electrode potential. Predict the direction of corrosion.

**Step 1:** Write the half-reaction:

$$
\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{Cu}, \quad n = 2
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the Nernst equation:

$$
E = E° - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log \frac{1}{[\text{Cu}^{2+}]}
$$

**Step 3:** Plug in the values:

$$
E = 0.34 - \frac{0.0592}{2} \log \frac{1}{0.01}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the logarithm term:

$$
\log \frac{1}{0.01} = \log 100 = 2
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the final potential:

$$
E = 0.34 - \frac{0.0592 \times 2}{2} = 0.34 - 0.0592 = 0.2808 \, \text{V}
$$

**Step 6:** Compare with the standard value and conclude:

- The actual reduction potential ($0.2808$ V) is *less positive* than the standard value ($0.34$ V).
- **Direction of corrosion:** Zinc (anode) will preferentially corrode because it is more anodic.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Faraday's First Law

> **Problem (KTU Typical):** A steel hull area of 5 m² is undergoing uniform corrosion with a current density of 0.05 mA/cm². Calculate the mass of iron lost over 1 year. Given: $M(\text{Fe}) = 55.85$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.

**Step 1:** Convert the current density to SI units:

$$
i_{corr} = 0.05 \, \text{mA/cm}^2 = 0.05 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^4 \, \text{A/m}^2 = 0.5 \, \text{A/m}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the total corrosion current:

$$
I = i_{corr} \times A = 0.5 \times 5 = 2.5 \, \text{A}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the time in seconds:

$$
t = 1 \, \text{year} = 365 \times 24 \times 3600 = 31{,}536{,}000 \, \text{s}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply Faraday's First Law:

$$
m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{2.5 \times 31{,}536{,}000 \times 55.85}{2 \times 96{,}485}
$$

**Step 5:** Numerator evaluation:

$$
2.5 \times 31{,}536{,}000 = 78{,}840{,}000
$$

$$
78{,}840{,}000 \times 55.85 = 4.403 \times 10^9
$$

**Step 6:** Denominator evaluation:

$$
2 \times 96{,}485 = 192{,}970
$$

**Step 7:** Final computation:

$$
m = \frac{4.403 \times 10^9}{1.9297 \times 10^5} \approx 22{,}820 \, \text{g} \approx 22.82 \, \text{kg}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A 5 m² area of a ship's hull can lose **22.8 kg of iron per year** under these conditions. This justifies why cathodic protection and anti-corrosive coatings are non-negotiable for commercial shipping.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Corrosion Prevention Methods

| Method | Mechanism | Advantages | Limitations | Maritime Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Protective Coatings (Paint)** | Physical barrier between metal and electrolyte | Low cost, easy to apply, aesthetic | Needs surface prep, periodic reapplication | Hull, ballast tanks, superstructure |
| **Sacrificial Anode CP** | Anode (Zn, Al, Mg) preferentially corrodes | No external power, simple, reliable | Limited lifespan, low current output | Ship hulls, offshore platforms |
| **Impressed Current CP (ICCP)** | External DC power makes structure cathodic | High current, adjustable, long life | Requires power, monitoring, skilled maintenance | Large tankers, submarines, jetties |
| **Alloying / Material Selection** | Use corrosion-resistant alloys (e.g., Cu-Ni, SS 316) | Passive protection, no maintenance | High initial cost, still prone to localized attack | Piping, valves, propeller shafts |
| **Corrosion Inhibitors** | Chemicals that slow anodic/cathodic reactions | Easy dosing, retrofittable | Periodic replenishment, environmental concerns | Ballast water, cooling systems |
| **Design Modifications** | Eliminate crevices, ensure drainage | Permanent, low lifecycle cost | May complicate fabrication | Hull geometry, weld designs |
| **Cladding / Lining** | Bonded corrosion-resistant layer | Excellent barrier, dual protection | Expensive, repair difficulty | Tank linings, pipe interiors |

---

## 3.5 Galvanic Series Order in Seawater — Block Reference

The Galvanic Series in Seawater (from **most anodic / active** to **most cathodic / noble**):

1. Magnesium (most active — sacrificial)
2. Zinc
3. Aluminum alloys
4. Mild Steel / Carbon Steel
5. Cast Iron
6. 304 Stainless Steel (active state)
7. Lead
8. Tin
9. Manganese Bronze
10. Admiralty Brass
11. Nickel
12. 70-30 Copper-Nickel
13. Nickel-Copper Alloy (Monel)
14. 316 Stainless Steel (passive)
15. Titanium
16. Graphite (most noble)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** "Stainless steel doesn't corrode" is a *dangerous myth*. Stainless steel is only corrosion-resistant when its **passive Cr₂O₃ film is intact**. In low-oxygen, chloride-rich environments (e.g., crevices, biofouled hulls), it suffers **crevice** and **pitting** corrosion aggressively.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Electrochemical Corrosion Cell — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["ANODE REGION"]
        A1[Metal Atoms M]
        A2[Oxidation Reaction]
        A3[Electrons Released]
    end

    subgraph E["ELECTROLYTE - Seawater"]
        E1[Ionic Conduction]
        E2[Dissolved O2 and H2O]
    end

    subgraph C["CATHODE REGION"]
        C1[Reduction Reaction]
        C2[Consumes Electrons]
        C3[Forms OH minus ions]
    end

    MP["METALLIC PATH - Electron Flow"]

    A1 --> A2 --> A3
    A3 -- Electrons travel through metal --> MP
    MP --> C1 --> C2 --> C3
    C2 -- Ions migrate through seawater --> E1
    E1 -- Closes the circuit --> A2

    style A fill:#fdd
    style C fill:#ddf
    style E fill:#ffd
    style MP fill:#dfd
```

```mermaid
    SW -- Ions migrate --> PR["Bronze Propeller - CATHODE"]

    PR -->|O2 Reduction| OH["OH minus Forms on Bronze"]
    SH -->|Fe to Fe2 plus| FE["Fe2 plus Enters Seawater"]

```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define corrosion. List the four essential components of an electrochemical corrosion cell.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Corrosion is the deterioration of a metal due to an electrochemical reaction with its environment, typically forming oxides, hydroxides, or other compounds.

**Four Components (2 Marks):**

1. **Anode** — site of oxidation (metal dissolves)
2. **Cathode** — site of reduction (e.g., O₂ reduction)
3. **Metallic conductor** — path for electron flow
4. **Electrolyte** — ionic conductor (e.g., seawater)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between sacrificial anode and impressed current cathodic protection methods.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Sacrificial Anode (SACP) | Impressed Current (ICCP) |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Source** | None (galvanic) | External DC rectifier required |
| **Anode Material** | Zn, Al, Mg (active metals) | Inert anodes (Pt, MMO, graphite) |
| **Current Capacity** | Limited (self-driven) | High and adjustable |
| **Application** | Small hulls, short-term protection | Large vessels, offshore platforms |
| **Maintenance** | Replace anodes periodically | Monitor rectifier output, reference electrodes |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to state that in **SACP**, the anode is *consumed* (sacrificed), while in **ICCP**, the anode is *inert* and the power source does the "work" of driving protective current.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### Question A — 14 Marks

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain in detail the various forms of corrosion encountered in marine environments with suitable examples.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Uniform (General) Corrosion (1.5 Marks):**
Even, continuous metal loss across the entire exposed surface. Driven by uniform electrochemical activity. Example: Atmospheric corrosion of ship superstructure; seawater-induced wastage of unprotected hull plates. Rate typically **0.1–0.3 mm/year** for mild steel.

**2. Galvanic (Bimetallic) Corrosion (1.5 Marks):**
Occurs when two dissimilar metals are electrically coupled in an electrolyte. The more anodic metal (higher in galvanic series) corrodes preferentially. Example: Steel hull coupled with a **Monel** propeller shaft; the steel is attacked.

**3. Pitting Corrosion (1 Mark):**
Highly localized, produces deep cavities with most of the surface unattacked. Driven by breakdown of passive film (e.g., Cl⁻ ions on stainless steel). Example: Stainless steel pump impellers; localized penetration can cause catastrophic failure.

**4. Crevice Corrosion (1 Mark):**
Intensified attack within stagnant gaps where oxygen is depleted. Example: Plate lap joints, gaskets, bolt threads. Differential aeration cell develops.

**5. Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC) (1 Mark):**
Cracking under combined tensile stress and corrosive environment. Example: **Brass condenser tubes** failing in ammonia-rich bilge environments.

**6. Erosion and Cavitation Corrosion (1 Mark):**
Mechanical removal of protective film combined with chemical attack. Example: Propeller blade tips (cavitation); pump impeller casings (erosion).

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Derive the Nernst equation and apply it to calculate the electrode potential of a copper electrode in seawater with $[\text{Cu}^{2+}] = 0.005$ M at 25 °C. Given: $E°(\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}) = +0.34$ V.

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (3.5 Marks):**

The relationship between Gibbs free energy and cell potential:

$$
\Delta G = -nFE
$$

Under non-standard conditions:

$$
\Delta G = \Delta G° + RT \ln Q
$$

Equating:

$$
-nFE = -nFE° + RT \ln Q
$$

Dividing by $-nF$:

$$
E = E° - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q
$$

Converting $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$:

$$
E = E° - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log_{10} Q
$$

At $T = 298.15$ K, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol:

$$
\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96{,}485} = 0.0592
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{E = E° - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log_{10} \frac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]}}
$$

**Application (3.5 Marks):**

For the half-reaction $\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{Cu}$, $n = 2$, $E° = +0.34$ V:

$$
E = 0.34 - \frac{0.0592}{2} \log_{10} \frac{1}{0.005}
$$

$$
E = 0.34 - 0.0296 \times \log_{10}(200)
$$

$$
E = 0.34 - 0.0296 \times 2.3010
$$

$$
E = 0.34 - 0.0681 = 0.2719 \, \text{V}
$$

**Conclusion:** The actual reduction potential is **+0.272 V**, less positive than the standard value. The lower $[\text{Cu}^{2+}]$ reduces the driving force for copper deposition, making the metal *more anodic* and more prone to corrosion in dilute conditions.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)

#### (a) [7 Marks] With a neat sketch, explain the principle and components of an Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP) system used for ship hulls. State the standard protective potential for marine applications.

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (2 Marks):** ICCP uses an **external DC power source** (rectifier) to drive current from an inert anode, through the seawater, onto the steel hull. The hull is forced to become a cathode, suppressing metal dissolution (oxidation) at the anode.

**Components (3 Marks):**

1. **DC Power Source (Rectifier)** — converts AC mains to controlled DC output (typically 0–50 V, 0–100 A).
2. **Inert Anodes** — made of platinized titanium, mixed metal oxide (MMO), or lead-silver alloy; mounted on the hull or on the seabed.
3. **Reference Electrode** — Ag/AgCl or zinc; measures the hull-to-seawater potential continuously.
4. **Steel Hull (Cathode)** — the structure being protected.
5. **Control System** — automatic potential monitoring and current adjustment.

**Protective Potential (1 Mark):** The standard protective potential for steel in seawater (measured against Ag/AgCl reference) is **−0.80 V to −1.05 V** (typically about **−0.85 V**).

**Sketch (1 Mark):** Diagram showing rectifier → anode → seawater → hull → back to rectifier, with reference electrode.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] A steel plate of area 10 m² is losing mass at a corrosion current of 4 A. Calculate the mass of iron lost in (i) 1 hour, and (ii) 30 days. Given: $M(\text{Fe}) = 55.85$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.

**Model Solution:**

**Formula:** From Faraday's First Law:

$$
m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}
$$

**Part (i): For 1 hour (3.5 Marks)**

$$
t = 3600 \, \text{s}
$$

$$
m = \frac{4 \times 3600 \times 55.85}{2 \times 96{,}485}
$$

Numerator: $4 \times 3600 \times 55.85 = 804{,}240$ g·C/mol

Denominator: $2 \times 96{,}485 = 192{,}970$

$$
m = \frac{804{,}240}{192{,}970} \approx 4.17 \, \text{g}
$$

**Part (ii): For 30 days (3.5 Marks)**

$$
t = 30 \times 24 \times 3600 = 2{,}592{,}000 \, \text{s}
$$

$$
m = \frac{4 \times 2{,}592{,}000 \times 55.85}{192{,}970}
$$

Numerator: $4 \times 2{,}592{,}000 \times 55.85 = 5.79 \times 10^8$

$$
m = \frac{5.79 \times 10^8}{1.93 \times 10^5} \approx 3000 \, \text{g} = 3.0 \, \text{kg}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Without protection, a 10 m² hull section loses about **3 kg of iron every month** — equivalent to a uniform thickness loss of:

$$
\text{Thickness} = \frac{m}{\rho \cdot A} = \frac{3{,}000}{7850 \times 10} = 0.038 \, \text{mm/month} = 0.46 \, \text{mm/year}
$$

This justifies mandatory **5-year dry-dock inspections** under IACS regulations.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (Read Carefully):**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** In Nernst equation, students frequently write a **plus** sign instead of **minus**. Examiners deduct up to **1 mark** for this.
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency:** Faraday's law requires time in **seconds** (not hours or days). Always convert before substituting.
> 3. **Confusing Electrochemical and Galvanic Series:** The Electrochemical Series uses *pure* standard potentials; the **Galvanic Series** reflects *real marine* behavior. For shipboard calculations, use the **Galvanic Series**.
> 4. **Skipping Conversion Steps:** Examiners reward **explicit intermediate steps**. A direct jump from problem statement to final answer loses **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Forgetting Reference Electrode Specification:** When stating protective potentials, always specify *against which reference* (e.g., "−0.85 V vs. Ag/AgCl").

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- ⚓ **Corrosion is electrochemical** — always needs anode, cathode, electrolyte, and metallic path.

- ⚓ **Galvanic series in seawater** is the practical reference for marine applications; remember: **Mg → Zn → Al → Steel → Brass → Cu → Ti → Graphite**.

- ⚓ **Anode always loses mass** (oxidation); **cathode is protected** (reduction).

- ⚓ **Electrons flow anode → cathode through metal**; **ions flow in the electrolyte** to complete the circuit.

- ⚓ **Nernst equation:** $E = E° - \dfrac{0.0592}{n} \log \dfrac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]}$ at 25 °C.

- ⚓ **Faraday's First Law:** $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ — use **time in seconds**.

- ⚓ **Sacrificial Anode CP:** No external power; uses Zn/Al/Mg; consumed over time.

- ⚓ **ICCP:** Uses rectifier, inert anodes, reference electrode; standard protection potential for steel ≈ **−0.85 V vs. Ag/AgCl**.

- ⚓ **Most dangerous marine corrosion types:** Pitting, SCC, and MIC — because they cause sudden failure with little warning.

- ⚓ **Prevention hierarchy:** Design → Material selection → Coatings → Cathodic protection → Inhibitors.

- ⚓ **Faraday's constant:** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol (≈ 96,500).

- ⚓ **Standard reference:** SHE at **0.000 V, 25 °C, 1 M, 1 atm**.

- ⚓ **Mild steel loss rate in seawater:** ~**0.1–0.3 mm/year unprotected**; ICCP can reduce this to <**0.02 mm/year**.

- ⚓ **Class society rules (IACS, DNV, ABS)** mandate ICCP/SACP for sea-going vessels; examinee should mention these in design questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
