topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Offshore Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Offshore Structure:** A self-contained, engineered marine installation sited in open sea (typically beyond the continental shelf break or in water depths **> 30 m**) designed to support exploration, drilling, production, storage, or accommodation functions associated with the hydrocarbon (oil & gas) and renewable energy industries, while safely withstanding combined environmental loading from wind, waves, currents, and seabed geotechnical conditions.

These structures are classified by the **American Petroleum Institute (API RP 2A-WSD)** and the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** codes as *permanent* (production) or *mobile* (drilling) units. The design must satisfy **ALARP** (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) principles under the Safety Case regime (UK HSE / OSPAR framework).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> Think of an offshore platform as a **"city on stilts in the middle of the ocean."** Just as a multi-story apartment building must safely support its own weight, resist wind gusts, and provide escape routes during a fire, an offshore platform must:
> 1. Hold up tons of drilling equipment, crew quarters, and storage tanks.
> 2. Resist hurricane-force waves, ocean currents, and corrosive salt spray.
> 3. Provide lifeboats, helidecks, and safe evacuation routes during an emergency.
> 4. "Stay put" using either legs driven into the seabed (fixed) or massive anchor chains (floating).

A simple analogy: a **fixed jacket platform** behaves like a *table standing on four legs pushed deep into sand*, while a **semi-submersible** behaves like a *buoy anchored by heavy chains*, floating but held in place. An **FPSO** is essentially a *giant floating oil tank* that processes and stores crude oil directly above the well.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Reference)

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sea water density, $\rho$ | **1025** | kg/m³ | Used in buoyancy & wave-load calcs |
| Acceleration due to gravity, $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² | Standard gravitational reference |
| Kinematic viscosity of seawater, $\nu$ | **1.05 × 10⁻⁶** | m²/s | Reynolds number for Morison eqn. |
| Standard air density at 15 °C | **1.225** | kg/m³ | Wind force calculation |
| Design wave return period | **100-year** | years | ULS structural design criterion |
| Significant wave height ($H_s$) | 1–15 | m | Typical North Sea 100-yr storm |
| Typical water depth boundary (fixed) | **≤ 500** | m | Beyond this, floating systems used |
| Typical water depth (floating) | **500 – 3000+** | m | Deepwater & ultra-deepwater |

### 1.4 Topic Scope for KTU Module 3

The following sub-topics are mandated by the **KTU 2024 PESFT755 syllabus, Module 3**:
1. Classification of offshore structures.
2. Fixed platforms: Jacket, Gravity Base, Jack-up.
3. Floating platforms: Semi-submersible, FPSO, Drillship, SPAR, TLP.
4. Mooring and station-keeping systems.
5. Hydrodynamic forces and structural response.
6. Safety systems on offshore installations (BOP, ESD, F&G, lifeboats).
7. Evacuation, escape, and rescue (EER) philosophy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Offshore Structures (Operational Logic)

Offshore structures are broadly split into two master categories based on how they maintain position in the ocean:

### A. Fixed (Bottom-Supported) Structures
Rigidly attached to the seabed; the structure's mass and pile/footing fixity resist all environmental loads.

1. **Jacket (Steel Template) Platform** – A 4- or 8-legged steel space-frame (a "lattice") driven into the seabed with large-diameter piles. Used in **30 – 500 m** water depths. Most common type worldwide (e.g., North Sea Brent, Mumbai High).
2. **Gravity Base Structure (GBS)** – A massive concrete or steel caisson (often a *Condeep* design) that sits on the seabed by its own weight. Used where seabed soil is competent (e.g., Troll, Statfjord in the North Sea). Water depth: **100 – 350 m**.
3. **Jack-up Rig (Mobile)** – A barge hull with 3 or 4 lattice legs that can be "jacked up" to lift the hull clear of the waves. Used for exploratory drilling in **up to ~170 m** depths.

### B. Floating Structures
Maintained on station by mooring lines, dynamic positioning, or a combination; they have 6 degrees of freedom (surge, sway, heave, roll, pitch, yaw).

1. **Semi-Submersible** – A pontoon-and-column hull submerged to a draft that provides exceptional stability (large metacentric height). Used for drilling, production, and as a tender-assist vessel. Examples: *Deepwater Horizon* (pre-2010), *Polycrest*.
2. **FPSO (Floating Production Storage & Offloading)** – A ship-shaped or circular vessel that receives, processes, and stores oil before offloading to shuttle tankers. Dominant in **deepwater & harsh environments** (e.g., Espírito Santo basin).
3. **Drillship** – A self-propelled, ship-shaped vessel with a central moonpool and drilling derrick. Used in **ultra-deepwater (> 3000 m)** such as the Gulf of Mexico.
4. **SPAR Platform** – A very large vertical cylinder (buoyancy tank + hull + keel) moored vertically. Excellent heave performance. Used up to **3000+ m** (e.g., *Perdido Spar*).
5. **Tension-Leg Platform (TLP)** – A semi-submersible held in place by *vertical, pre-tensioned tendons* that almost eliminate vertical heave motion. Used in **500 – 1500 m** (e.g., *Heidrun TLP*).

## 2.2 Why and How – Logic Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> **WHY each type exists:** The deeper the water, the more impractical and uneconomic it becomes to build a fixed structure that reaches the seabed. Floating units trade structural connection to the seabed for *station-keeping systems* (mooring + DP).

> [!NOTE]
> **HOW they work:**
> - *Fixed* → Pile-soil interaction transfers wave, wind, and current loads to the seabed.
> - *Floating* → Mooring lines resist horizontal drift; buoyancy + ballast resist vertical motion; DP thrusters actively counter wind/wave drift.

## 2.3 The Six Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF) Concept

Every floating offshore structure can move in six independent ways — critical for stability and mooring design:

| DOF | Translation / Rotation | Axis | Typical Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Surge | Translation | x (horizontal) | Mooring lines |
| Sway | Translation | y (horizontal) | Mooring lines |
| Heave | Translation | z (vertical) | Buoyancy + tendons (TLP) |
| Roll | Rotation | about x-axis | Waterplane area / ballast |
| Pitch | Rotation | about y-axis | Waterplane area / ballast |
| Yaw | Rotation | about z-axis | Mooring + thrusters |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-tested** and align with API RP 2A-WSD, DNV-OS-E301, and ABS rules — the three codes most often cited in KTU 2024 question papers.

| # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ | Sea water density | kg/m³ |
| 2 | $F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot V$ | Buoyancy (Archimedes) | N |
| 3 | $W = \rho \cdot g \cdot V_{sub}$ | Submerged weight of structure | N |
| 4 | $SF = F_B / W$ | Reserve buoyancy / safety factor | — |
| 5 | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | Transverse metacentric height (stability) | m |
| 6 | $BM = \dfrac{I_T}{V_{disp}}$ | BM where $I_T$ is waterplane 2nd moment | m |
| 7 | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Natural heave period (mass-spring) | s |
| 8 | $L_0 = \dfrac{g \cdot T^2}{2\pi}$ | Deep-water wavelength (Airy theory) | m |
| 9 | $F_{wave} = F_D + F_I$ | Morison total force (drag + inertia) | N/m |
| 10 | $F_D = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho \cdot C_D \cdot D \cdot u \cdot \vert u \vert$ | Drag force per unit length | N/m |
| 11 | $F_I = \rho \cdot C_M \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{du}{dt}$ | Inertia force per unit length | N/m |
| 12 | $C_F = C_D + C_M$ | Total force coefficient | — |
| 13 | $u = \dfrac{\pi H}{T} \cdot \dfrac{\cosh(k(z+d))}{\sinh(kd)} \cdot \cos(\theta)$ | Airy horizontal particle velocity | m/s |
| 14 | $\dot{u} = \dfrac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2} \cdot \dfrac{\cosh(k(z+d))}{\sinh(kd)} \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | Horizontal particle acceleration | m/s² |
| 15 | $R_{chain} = W_{chain} \cdot h_{cat}$ | Restoring force from catenary mooring | N |
| 16 | $F_{wind} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho_a \cdot C_S \cdot A \cdot V^2$ | Wind force on superstructure | N |
| 17 | $H_{s} = 4 \sigma$ | Significant wave height from spectral std. dev. | m |
| 18 | $H_{max} \approx 1.86 H_{s}$ | Maximum wave in 3-hr sea state | m |
| 19 | $SF_{mooring} = \dfrac{T_{break}}{T_{max}}$ | Mooring line safety factor (DNV ≥ 1.67) | — |
| 20 | $Re = \dfrac{u \cdot D}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number (for $C_D$ selection) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Note for KTU Exams:** Use SI units throughout. Express accelerations in **m/s²**, forces in **N** or **kN**, moments in **kN·m**, and lengths in **m**. Always state assumptions (e.g., "Assume Airy/linear wave theory is valid, $d/L > 0.05$").

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wave Force (Morison) equation** is the *single most used* equation in offshore jacket design worldwide.
- **Metacentric Height (GM)** determines whether a semi-submersible will capsize or right itself after a heeling moment.
- **Catenary mooring** equations ensure that a floating platform does not drift more than ~5–10 % of water depth under 100-year storm conditions.
- **API & DNV codes** are legally binding for offshore installations in Indian waters (under the **Oilfields (Regulation and Development) Act**, 1948, and **OISD guidelines**).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Morison Equation (Drag + Inertia Force)

This is the *foundation* for hydrodynamic loading on slender offshore members (trusses, braces, risers).

### Starting Point (Physical Reasoning)

When ocean water moves past a vertical cylinder of diameter $D$, the total horizontal force per unit length is the sum of:
- **Drag force ($F_D$)** — proportional to $u \cdot \vert u \vert$ (pressure differential).
- **Inertia force ($F_I$)** — proportional to fluid acceleration $\dot u$ (added mass effect).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Drag Component (Empirical, Buckingham Pi):**
Dimensionless analysis gives $F_D \propto \rho \cdot D \cdot u^2$. Introducing coefficient of drag $C_D$:

$$
F_D = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_D\,D\,u\,\vert u \vert
$$

*Logic:* Factor $1/2$ comes from kinetic energy per unit volume $= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho u^2$; $u \cdot \vert u \vert$ preserves sign of force.

**Step 2 — Inertia Component (Potential Flow + Added Mass):**
From potential flow around a cylinder, an oscillating fluid exerts force equivalent to accelerating an "added mass" of fluid equal to the displaced cylinder mass.

Added mass per unit length $= \rho \cdot C_M \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$

Therefore:

$$
F_I = \rho\,C_M\,\frac{\pi D^2}{4}\,\frac{du}{dt}
$$

*Logic:* $C_M = C_a + 1$ (added mass coefficient + 1 for displaced fluid). For a circular cylinder in oscillatory flow, $C_M \approx 2.0$ (theoretical, $C_a = 1$).

**Step 3 — Total In-Line Force per Unit Length:**

$$
F_{wave} = F_D + F_I = \frac{1}{2}\rho C_D D\,u\,\vert u \vert \;+\; \rho\,C_M\,\frac{\pi D^2}{4}\,\frac{du}{dt}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Force on Member of Length $L$ (Integration):**

$$
F_{total} = \int_{0}^{L} F_{wave}(z)\,dz
$$

**Step 5 — Validity Criterion (KTU exam favourite):**
Morison equation is valid only when $D/L < 0.2$ (slender member). For large-diameter cylinders (e.g., GBS caisson), use **diffraction theory** instead.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Airy Linear Wave Kinematics (Particle Velocity & Acceleration)

**Step 1 — Velocity potential for a monochromatic wave in depth $d$:**

$$
\phi = \frac{gH}{2\omega}\cdot\frac{\cosh(k(z+d))}{\cosh(kd)}\sin(kx-\omega t)
$$

**Step 2 — Horizontal velocity $u = \partial \phi / \partial x$:**

$$
u = \frac{\pi H}{T}\cdot\frac{\cosh(k(z+d))}{\sinh(kd)}\cos(kx-\omega t)
$$

*Logic:* $\omega = 2\pi/T$, $k = 2\pi/L$, and $\partial/\partial x$ brings down a factor of $k$, so $k \cdot \frac{gH}{2\omega} = \frac{\pi H}{T}$.

**Step 3 — Horizontal acceleration $\dot u = \partial u / \partial t$:**

$$
\dot u = \frac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2}\cdot\frac{\cosh(k(z+d))}{\sinh(kd)}\sin(kx-\omega t)
$$

*Logic:* $\partial/\partial t$ of $\cos(kx-\omega t)$ brings down a factor of $\omega$, giving $k\omega = (2\pi/T)(2\pi/L) = 4\pi^2/(LT)$; combined with the $g/\omega$ prefactor this yields the $2\pi^2 H/T^2$ form.

**Step 4 — Substitution into Morison equation gives total inline force at any elevation $z$.**

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Transverse Metacentric Height ($GM$)

**Step 1 — Centre of Buoyancy (B) shift on heeling by small angle $\phi$:**

The buoyancy force tilts about the *new* centre B, and the metacentre M is the intersection of the vertical through the new B with the original vertical centreline.

**Step 2 — Geometry of the small wedge:**

For a waterplane area $A_W$ with second moment $I_T$, the virtual shift in B is:

$$
BM = \frac{I_T}{V_{disp}}
$$

**Step 3 — Final stability formula:**

$$
GM = KB + BM - KG
$$

*Where:*
- $KB$ = vertical distance from keel K to centre of buoyancy B.
- $KG$ = vertical distance from keel K to centre of gravity G.
- $V_{disp}$ = displaced volume = $W / (\rho g)$.

**Step 4 — Stability criterion:** For positive (stable) righting, $GM > 0$. For offshore units, API recommends $GM \geq 0.15$ m for semi-submersibles in survival condition.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Deep-Water Wavelength (Airy)

From dispersion relation of linear waves:

$$
\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kd)
$$

For deep water ($kd \to \infty$), $\tanh(kd) \to 1$, hence:

$$
\omega^2 = gk \quad\Rightarrow\quad k = \frac{\omega^2}{g}
$$

Substituting $L = 2\pi/k$ and $\omega = 2\pi/T$:

$$
L_0 = \frac{g\,T^2}{2\pi}
$$

For a wave of period $T = 10$ s:

$$
L_0 = \frac{9.81 \times 10^2}{2\pi} = \frac{981}{6.283} \approx 156.1 \text{ m}
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Structure

| Water Depth | Soil / Seabed | Function | Recommended Structure | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 – 30 m | Soft | Drilling | Jack-up | Cheapest, mobile |
| 30 – 200 m | Any | Production | Jacket (fixed) | Mature, low OPEX |
| 100 – 350 m | Competent | Production | Gravity Base | Uses own weight, no piling |
| 200 – 500 m | Any | Drilling | Semi-submersible | Mobile, stable |
| 500 – 1500 m | Deep | Production | TLP | Near-rigid heave response |
| 500 – 3000+ m | Deep | Drilling | Drillship / Semi | Mobility + DP |
| 1000 – 3000+ m | Deep | Production | SPAR | Excellent heave response |
| 500 – 2000+ m | Deep | Production | FPSO | Storage + offloading flexibility |

### 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A vertical circular pile of diameter $D = 1.5$ m is exposed to a 100-year design wave with $H = 12$ m and $T = 10$ s in water depth $d = 50$ m. Compute the maximum inline wave force per unit length at the still-water level (SWL, $z=0$). Use $C_D = 1.2$, $C_M = 2.0$, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Deep-water wavelength:**
$$
L_0 = \frac{g T^2}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 100}{6.283} = 156.1 \text{ m}
$$
Since $d/L_0 = 50/156.1 = 0.32$, dispersion relation must be solved iteratively. For first trial assume $L \approx 110$ m. (Use $L \approx 105$ m for $d/L \approx 0.48$.)

**Step 2 — At SWL ($z = 0$), hyperbolic factor:**
$$
\frac{\cosh(kd)}{\sinh(kd)} = \coth(kd) = \coth\!\left(\frac{2\pi \times 50}{105}\right) = \coth(2.99) \approx 1.005
$$

**Step 3 — Max velocity occurs at $\cos = 1$:**
$$
u_{max} = \frac{\pi H}{T} \times 1.005 = \frac{\pi \times 12}{10} \times 1.005 = 3.79 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Max acceleration ($\sin = 1$):**
$$
\dot u_{max} = \frac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2} \times 1.005 = \frac{2\pi^2 \times 12}{100} \times 1.005 = 2.38 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Drag force per unit length:**
$$
F_D = \frac{1}{2}(1025)(1.2)(1.5)(3.79)(3.79) \approx 13.3 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 6 — Inertia force per unit length:**
$$
F_I = (1025)(2.0)\frac{\pi(1.5)^2}{4}(2.38) \approx 8.62 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 7 — Total inline force per unit length:**
$$
F_{wave} = 13.3 + 8.62 = 21.9 \text{ kN/m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever asked for the *total force on a member*, integrate $F_{wave}(z)$ over the wetted length. The result is approximately $\approx F_{wave,SWL} \times (d + \eta_{max})$ where $\eta_{max} = H/2 = 6$ m. So the *integrated* force $\approx 21.9 \times (50 + 6) \approx 1.23$ MN.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a fixed offshore platform and a floating offshore platform. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Fixed Platform | Floating Platform |
|---|---|---|
| Position-keeping | Rigidly fixed to seabed via piles or gravity | Held by mooring + DP or tendons |
| Water depth | 30 – 500 m | 500 – 3000+ m |
| Heave response | None (rigid) | Significant (6-DOF motion) |
| Cost trend | High CAPEX, low OPEX | Lower CAPEX, higher OPEX |
| Examples | Jacket, GBS | FPSO, SPAR, TLP |

*Examples (0.5 mark each, any two pairs = 2 marks; tabular comparison = 1 mark.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the Morison equation and explain when it is applicable.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
F = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho\,C_D D\,u\,\vert u \vert \;+\; \rho\,C_M\,\tfrac{\pi D^2}{4}\,\dfrac{du}{dt}
$$

- **First term** = drag force per unit length.
- **Second term** = inertia force per unit length.
- **Applicability:** Slender members where $D/L_w < 0.2$ (wave length) and the structure does not significantly disturb the wave field (no diffraction). Typically used for jacket braces, risers, and small-diameter piles.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the components and operational principle of a **Tension Leg Platform (TLP)**. Mention its typical water-depth range and one advantage and one disadvantage.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 4-legged steel jacket is to be installed in $d = 80$ m water depth. Each leg pile has diameter $D = 2.0$ m. Design wave: $H = 10$ m, $T = 9$ s. Use $C_D = 1.1$, $C_M = 2.0$. Determine the *maximum inline wave force per unit length on a single leg at SWL*. Comment on whether diffraction theory might be needed.

#### Model Solution

**(a) TLP Description [7 marks]**

- **Sketch (3 marks)** — Floating hull (pontoon + columns), vertical **tendons** (pre-tensioned) anchored to seabed templates; top tension ~5–10 % of displacement.
- **Principle (2 marks)** — Tendons are pre-tensioned so they never go slack; vertical heave is essentially suppressed, giving near-rigid motion response; horizontal excursions are controlled by the tendons' geometric stiffness.
- **Depth range (1 mark):** 500 – 1500 m.
- **Advantage (0.5):** Minimal heave → easier top-side processing.
- **Disadvantage (0.5):** Tendons are fatigue-critical; complex installation.

**(b) Numerical [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Deep-water wavelength:**
$$
L_0 = \frac{gT^2}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 81}{6.283} = 126.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve dispersion relation for $L$ in 80 m water depth.**
Iterate: assume $L = 100$ m, $kd = 2\pi \times 80/100 = 5.03$, $\tanh(5.03) \approx 0.9999$.
$\omega^2/(gk) = 1/0.9999 \approx 1$ → converges. Use $L \approx 100$ m.

**Step 3 — Hyperbolic factor at SWL ($z = 0$):**
$$
\frac{\cosh(kd)}{\sinh(kd)} = \coth(5.03) \approx 1.0
$$

**Step 4 — Max velocity and acceleration at SWL:**
$$
u_{max} = \frac{\pi H}{T} \times 1.0 = \frac{\pi \times 10}{9} = 3.49 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\dot u_{max} = \frac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2} \times 1.0 = \frac{2\pi^2 \times 10}{81} = 2.44 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Drag + Inertia force per unit length on one leg:**
$$
F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(1.1)(2.0)(3.49)(3.49) = 13{,}730 \text{ N/m} = 13.73 \text{ kN/m}
$$

$$
F_I = (1025)(2.0)\frac{\pi (2.0)^2}{4}(2.44) = 15{,}710 \text{ N/m} = 15.71 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 6 — Total inline force per unit length:**
$$
F_{wave} = 13.73 + 15.71 = 29.44 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 7 — Diffraction check:** $D/L = 2.0/100 = 0.02 < 0.2$ → Morison equation is **valid**; diffraction theory not required for this slender leg. (1 mark)

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain with a block diagram the **six degrees of freedom** of a floating offshore structure and describe the role of **mooring systems** in station-keeping.

**(b) [7 marks]** An FPSO of displacement $W = 250{,}000$ tonnes is held by **12 catenary mooring lines**, each of breaking strength $T_b = 12{,}000$ kN. The maximum expected line tension under a 100-year storm is $T_{max} = 4{,}500$ kN. Compute the **mooring safety factor** as per DNV-OS-E301. Comment on whether the design passes DNV acceptance.

#### Model Solution

**(a) 6-DOF + Mooring (7 marks)**

- **Diagram (3 marks):** Show translational (surge, sway, heave) along x, y, z axes and rotational (roll, pitch, yaw) about those axes. (Use SECTION 4.1 Mermaid as the schematic reference.)
- **Mooring function (3 marks):** Restoring horizontal offset; resists environmental drift from wind/wave/current; pretension keeps lines taut at all times. Spread mooring = 8–16 lines radiating symmetrically; tension-leg = vertical pretensioned tendons.
- **DP backup (1 mark):** For station-keeping accuracy < 5 m, DP thrusters supplement mooring.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Mooring safety factor formula (DNV-OS-E301):**
$$
SF = \frac{T_b}{T_{max}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitution:**
$$
SF = \frac{12{,}000}{4{,}500} = 2.667
$$

**Step 3 — DNV requirement for permanent mooring of FPSO in ULS:** $SF \geq 1.67$ (DNV-OS-E301 Sec. 6.5).

**Step 4 — Comparison:** $2.667 > 1.67$ → **PASSES** DNV acceptance. (1 mark)
(Note: Include 1 mark for quoting the code clause; 1 mark for conclusion; 1 mark for proper rounding/precision.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Force per unit length is in N/m or kN/m — *not* just N.
> 2. **Using $u^2$ instead of $u \cdot \vert u \vert$:** This loses the directional sense of drag and is marked down.
> 3. **Applying Morison to large GBS caissons:** Morison is *invalid* for $D/L > 0.2$ — examiners look for this caveat.
> 4. **Skipping the dispersion iteration:** Markers often set 2 marks aside for correct $L$ in finite depth.
> 5. **Confusing FPSO with FSO:** FSO = *storage only*; FPSO = *production + storage + offloading*.
> 6. **Not stating $C_M = C_a + 1$:** The "1" comes from the displaced water mass itself.
> 7. **Missing the safety-factor code clause:** Always cite API/DNV/ABS clause number when judging acceptance.

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] Offshore structures fall into **Fixed (Jacket, GBS, Jack-up)** and **Floating (Semi, FPSO, Drillship, SPAR, TLP)**.
- [ ] Jacket platforms dominate shallow-to-medium water; floating units dominate deepwater.
- [ ] Every floating structure has **6 DOF** — surge, sway, heave, roll, pitch, yaw.
- [ ] **Mooring systems** (catenary, taut, TLP tendons) provide station-keeping.
- [ ] **Dynamic Positioning (DP)** uses thrusters + GPS + acoustic sensors.
- [ ] **Morison equation** applies to slender members ($D/L < 0.2$); for larger members use **diffraction theory**.
- [ ] **Airy/linear wave theory** gives particle velocity and acceleration used in Morison; valid for $H/d < 0.78$ and $d/L > 0.05$.
- [ ] **Stability** of a floating unit depends on **$GM = KB + BM - KG > 0$**.
- [ ] **Safety systems** include **BOP** (subsea), **ESD** (1/2/3 levels), **F&G detection**, **TEMPSC lifeboats**, and **muster points**.
- [ ] **EER philosophy** = Detect → Control → Escape → Evacuate → Rescue.
- [ ] **API RP 2A-WSD, DNV-OS-E301, ABS rules** are the binding design codes cited in KTU answers.
- [ ] **100-year return period** is the standard ULS design storm criterion.
- [ ] **Significant wave height $H_s = 4\sigma$**; **$H_{max} \approx 1.86 H_s$** in a 3-hr sea state.
- [ ] **Mooring safety factor** per DNV ≥ **1.67** for ULS; ≥ **2.0** for ALS (accidental limit state).
- [ ] **Deep-water wavelength** $L_0 = gT^2 / 2\pi$ (Airy).
- [ ] **Sea water density** is taken as **1025 kg/m³** in all standard offshore calculations.
- [ ] Always state **assumptions, code clauses, and units** for full marks in KTU ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Offshore Structures (Fixed, Floating, and Subsea)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Offshore Structures (Fixed, Floating, and Subsea)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Offshore Structures** are engineered marine installations designed to operate in ocean environments for the exploration, drilling, production, storage, and transportation of hydrocarbons (oil and natural gas), as well as for renewable energy harvesting (offshore wind, tidal, wave). They are classified into three principal categories based on their structural response mechanism and mode of load transfer to the seabed:

1. **Fixed (Bottom-Supported) Structures** – Rigidly anchored to the seabed, transferring vertical and horizontal loads directly through their foundations.
2. **Floating Structures** – Buoyancy-stabilized structures moored to the seabed via chains, wires, or synthetic tethers, transferring loads through mooring systems and station-keeping equipment.
3. **Subsea Structures** – Seafloor-mounted equipment and infrastructure used for wellhead completion, flow assurance, and product transport without a surface-visible host platform.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The classification is fundamentally governed by the **water depth of operation** (typically cited in meters), **environmental load regime** (waves, currents, wind, ice), and **functional requirement** (drilling, production, storage, or processing).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **multi-storey apartment building** floating in the middle of a lake:

- If you **build the building directly on a foundation driven into the lakebed**, even when the water level rises, the building does not move. This is analogous to a **Fixed Offshore Structure** (e.g., a Jacket Platform). The structure uses massive steel legs (piles) to "nail" itself to the seabed.

- If the lake is too deep to build a foundation, you could make the building **float like a giant pontoon**, tethering it to the lakebed with strong anchor cables so it doesn't drift away. This represents a **Floating Offshore Structure** (e.g., an FPSO or Semi-submersible). Buoyancy keeps it up; mooring keeps it in place.

- Finally, imagine that instead of building on the surface, you simply **lay pipes and equipment on the lakebed** connecting the underwater wellhead to the shore. These are **Subsea Structures** (e.g., subsea manifolds, pipelines, PLETs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Selection Metric:** Water depth is the **single most decisive parameter** in offshore structural selection. The rule-of-thumb boundaries in industry practice are:
> - **0 – 500 m:** Fixed platforms (Jacket) are economical.
> - **500 m – 1500 m:** Compliant Towers, Semisubmersibles, and FPSOs dominate.
> - **1500 m and beyond:** SPARs, TLPs, and ultra-deepwater floating systems are mandatory; fixed platforms are economically unfeasible.

---

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics & Physical Constants

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Standard Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | Used in all hydrostatic and dynamic force calculations |
| Sea water density | $\rho_{sw}$ | **1025 kg/m³** | Standard for offshore hydrostatics |
| Gravitational acceleration × density | $\rho g$ | **10055 N/m³** | Specific weight for buoyancy computations |
| Atmospheric pressure (sea level) | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** | Reference for pressure-depth integration |
| Hydrostatic pressure gradient | $\nabla P$ | **0.10055 atm/m** (≈ 10.055 kPa/m) | Pressure increase per metre depth |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU numerical problems, always state the assumed seawater density ($\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$) explicitly to earn full marks. Examiners penalize assumptions that are not declared.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Offshore Structures – Operational Logic

The selection of an offshore structure type is not arbitrary; it follows a hierarchy governed by **physics, economics, and operational risk**.

#### 2.1.1 Fixed (Bottom-Supported) Structures

Fixed structures resist environmental loads (waves, wind, current) through their **own structural rigidity**. They behave as a cantilever from the seabed.

**Sub-Types:**

- **Steel Jacket Platforms:** A space-frame truss of tubular steel members, supported by driven piles. The most common fixed platform worldwide. Typical water depth: **up to 400–500 m**.
- **Gravity-Based Structures (GBS):** Massive reinforced concrete caissons that rest on the seabed using their own weight (gravity). Typical depth: **up to 300 m**.
- **Compliant Towers:** A slender, flexible tower that resists lateral loads by **allowing small rotations** (compliance), with the restoring force provided by the buoyancy of the structure itself. Typical depth: **400 m – 900 m**.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. Seabed geotechnical survey determines pile-driving feasibility.
2. Jacket is fabricated onshore and transported by barge.
3. Piles are driven through the jacket legs into the seabed.
4. Topside (drilling/processing facilities) is lifted by a heavy-lift crane onto the jacket.

---

#### 2.1.2 Floating Structures

Floating structures rely on **buoyancy** for vertical support and **mooring systems** for horizontal station-keeping. They have **six degrees of freedom**: surge, sway, heave, roll, pitch, yaw.

**Sub-Types:**

- **Semisubmersibles:** Multi-column, partially submerged pontoon structures with large water-plane area. Used for drilling and production.
- **FPSO (Floating Production, Storage, and Offloading):** A ship-shaped or circular vessel that processes, stores, and offloads oil directly to shuttle tankers.
- **SPAR Platforms:** A single, large-diameter vertical cylinder, deeply ballasted, with exceptional stability in deep water (heave response). Typical depth: **600 m – 3000 m**.
- **Tension Leg Platforms (TLP):** A semi-submersible held in place by **vertical, pre-tensioned tendons** anchored to the seabed. The tendons effectively eliminate vertical heave motion.
- **Drillships:** A mobile floating vessel with a moonpool for drilling operations, dynamically positioned.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. Hull is designed with sufficient buoyancy to support topside payload and provide adequate metacentric height.
2. Mooring system (chains, wire, polyester) is laid in a **3×3 or 4×4 symmetric pattern** for station-keeping.
3. For TLPs, vertical tendons are tensioned to maintain pretension ≥ maximum wave-induced tension loss.
4. Risers connect the floating vessel to the subsea wellhead.

---

#### 2.1.3 Subsea Structures

Subsea systems are deployed **on the seabed** and are interconnected to floating or fixed host facilities via **flexible or rigid pipelines, umbilicals, and risers**.

**Sub-Types:**

- **Subsea Trees (Christmas Trees):** Valves and choke assemblies installed on the wellhead to control flow.
- **Manifolds:** Gathering points where flowlines from multiple wells converge.
- **PLETs / PLEMs (Pipeline End Terminations / Manifolds):** Connectors at the end of pipelines.
- **Subsea Pipelines:** Rigid (steel) or flexible (composite) flowlines transporting oil/gas.
- **Subsea Umbilicals:** Bundled cables carrying hydraulic fluid, electrical power, and chemical injection lines.
- **ROVs (Remotely Operated Vehicles):** Robotic submersibles used for installation, inspection, and intervention.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. Seabed is prepared (leveling, trenching if required).
2. Templates and manifolds are lowered from a construction vessel.
3. Pipelines are laid using S-lay, J-lay, or reel-lay methods.
4. Umbilicals and flying leads are connected via ROV.
5. System is commissioned and monitored continuously.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Domain | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hydrostatic pressure | $P = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \, g \, h$ | Pressure at depth $h$ | Pa |
| Buoyancy force (Archimedes) | $F_b = \rho_{sw} \, g \, V_{disp}$ | Vertical uplift on submerged volume | N |
| Wave particle velocity (linear) | $u = \dfrac{\pi H}{T} \cdot \dfrac{\cosh(kz)}{\sinh(kd)} \cos(\omega t - kx)$ | Horizontal water velocity | m/s |
| Morison inertia + drag force | $F = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D D u \vert u \vert + C_M \rho \tfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \dot{u}$ | Per-unit-length force on slender member | N/m |
| Metacentric height (stability) | $GM = BM - BG$ | Transverse stability criterion | m |
| BM formula (rectangular) | $BM = \dfrac{I}{V_{disp}}$ | $I$ = 2nd moment of water-plane area | m |
| Heave natural period (SPAR) | $T_n = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m + a_{33}}{k_{33}}}$ | Vertical oscillation period | s |
| Pretension in TLP tendon | $T_p = (W_{sub} - F_b) \, / \, n_{tendons}$ | Initial tension per tendon | N |
| Mooring line catenary tension (horizontal) | $T_H = \dfrac{W \, L}{2}$ (deep water approx.) | Approx. horizontal restoring force | N |
| Air-gap criterion | $h_{air} \geq S_{swl} + R_{wave} + C_{freeboard}$ | Wave clearance for fixed decks | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** Whenever the Morison equation is asked, ALWAYS define $C_D$ (drag coefficient, typically **0.6 – 1.2** for smooth cylinders) and $C_M$ (inertia coefficient, typically **1.5 – 2.0**). KTU examiners frequently deduct 1 mark for not specifying these constants.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fixed Platforms:** Used for the majority of shallow-water oil and gas production globally (e.g., North Sea, Persian Gulf, Bombay High).
- **Floating Structures:** Dominate deepwater production (Gulf of Mexico, offshore Brazil, West Africa).
- **Subsea Structures:** Form the **backbone of deepwater field architecture**, enabling tiebacks of distant wells to a central host platform, dramatically reducing capital expenditure.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU Part B answers, always justify the choice of structure with a **depth-versus-cost curve**. This shows examiner-level analytical depth and scores higher marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Hydrostatic Pressure at the Seafloor

We derive the pressure experienced by a subsea structure at depth $h$ below the mean sea level.

**Starting Point:** The fundamental relationship between pressure and depth in an incompressible, static fluid.

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho_{sw} \, g$$

**Step 1:** Integrate both sides from the sea surface ($z = 0$, where $P = P_{atm}$) to depth $h$.

$$\int_{P_{atm}}^{P(h)} dP = -\rho_{sw} \, g \int_{0}^{h} dz$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the definite integrals.

$$P(h) - P_{atm} = -\rho_{sw} \, g \, h$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to express absolute pressure at depth $h$.

$$P(h) = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \, g \, h$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the numerical constants $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

$$P(h) = 101325 + (1025)(9.81) \, h \quad \text{(in Pascals)}$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the specific weight $\rho g$ for engineering convenience.

$$\rho_{sw} \, g = 1025 \times 9.81 \approx 10055 \text{ N/m}^3$$

**Step 6:** Final compact form.

$$P(h) = 101.325 + 0.10055 \, h \quad \text{(kPa, where } h \text{ is in metres)}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Morison Equation (Force on a Slender Vertical Cylinder)

The Morison equation computes the horizontal force per unit length on a slender vertical pile due to wave action.

**Assumption:** The pile diameter $D$ is much smaller than the wavelength $\lambda$, so the wave passes the pile without significant diffraction.

**Step 1:** The total force has two components: a **drag force** (proportional to $u \vert u \vert$) and an **inertia force** (proportional to $\dot{u}$, the water particle acceleration).

$$f(z, t) = f_D(z,t) + f_I(z,t)$$

**Step 2:** Express the drag term using a drag coefficient $C_D$.

$$f_D(z,t) = \frac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} C_D D \, u(z,t) \, \vert u(z,t) \vert$$

**Step 3:** Express the inertia term using an inertia coefficient $C_M$.

$$f_I(z,t) = C_M \rho_{sw} \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \, \dot{u}(z,t)$$

**Step 4:** Combine into the complete Morison equation.

$$\boxed{f(z,t) = \frac{1}{2}\rho_{sw} C_D D \, u \vert u \vert + C_M \rho_{sw} \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \dot{u}}$$

**Step 5:** Total horizontal force on the cylinder is obtained by integrating along the submerged length $L$.

$$F_{total}(t) = \int_{-d}^{\eta(t)} f(z,t) \, dz$$

where $d$ is the still-water depth and $\eta(t)$ is the instantaneous free-surface elevation.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Transverse Stability – Metacentric Height for a Floating Structure

For a floating semi-submersible or FPSO, the metacentric height $GM$ must be positive for stability.

**Step 1:** Define the key geometric points.
- **G** = Centre of Gravity (where weight $W$ acts).
- **B** = Centre of Buoyancy (where buoyancy $F_b$ acts).
- **M** = Metacentre (intersection of the new buoyancy vector with the original vertical axis after a small tilt $\phi$).

**Step 2:** The metacentric radius $BM$ depends on the second moment of area $I$ of the waterplane about the tilting axis, and the displaced volume $\nabla$ (or $V_{disp}$).

$$BM = \frac{I}{V_{disp}}$$

**Step 3:** The vertical distance $BG$ is computed from the geometry of the vessel.

$$BG = z_B - z_G$$

where $z_B$ is the depth of $B$ below the waterline and $z_G$ is the depth of $G$ below the waterline.

**Step 4:** Metacentric height is the difference.

$$GM = BM - BG = \frac{I}{V_{disp}} - BG$$

**Step 5:** Stability criterion (positive GM means stable equilibrium).

$$\boxed{GM > 0 \quad \text{(mandatory for safe operation)}}$$

**Step 6:** Typical KTU-required range: For a semi-submersible, $GM$ is typically in the range **3 m to 20 m**, with values above ~20 m implying an excessively stiff (and uncomfortable) vessel.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix – Selecting the Right Structure

| Criterion | Fixed (Jacket) | Semisubmersible | SPAR | TLP | FPSO | Subsea Template |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Typical Depth Range (m) | 0 – 500 | 200 – 3000 | 600 – 3000 | 300 – 2500 | 200 – 3000+ | 100 – 3000+ |
| Heave Response | None (rigid) | Moderate | Excellent | Near-zero | Moderate | None |
| Station-Keeping | Fixed piles | Mooring | Mooring | Tendons | Mooring/DP | None (small) |
| Storage Capacity | Limited | Limited | Limited | Limited | Large (1–2 Mbbl) | None |
| Capital Cost | High (shallow) | Moderate-High | High | Very High | Moderate | Low–High |
| Primary Application | Shallow production | Deep drilling | Deep production | Deep production | Deep storage + production | Well tieback |
| Decommissioning Cost | Moderate-High | Moderate | High | High | Moderate | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Gold Rule:** Never write "**SPAR is best for deep water**" without qualifying it. Always say "**SPAR is preferred for depths >1500 m due to superior heave response and payload capacity**." Specificity earns marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[OFFSHORE STRUCTURES]:::root --> B[FIXED BOTTOM SUPPORTED]
    A --> C[FLOATING]
    A --> D[SUBSEA]

    B --> B1[STEEL JACKET]
    B --> B2[GRAVITY BASED GBS]
    B --> B3[COMPLIANT TOWER]

    C --> C1[SEMISUBMERSIBLE]
    C --> C2[FPSO]
    C --> C3[SPAR]
    C --> C4[TLP]
    C --> C5[DRILLSHIP]

    D --> D1[SUBSEA TREE]
    D --> D2[MANIFOLD]
    D --> D3[PLET PLEM]
    D --> D4[PIPELINE UMBILICAL]

    B1 -.supports.-> E[TOPSIDE FACILITIES]
    B2 -.supports.-> E
    B3 -.supports.-> E

    C1 -.moored.-> F[MOORING SYSTEM]
    C2 -.moored.-> F
    C3 -.moored.-> F
    C4 -.tensioned.-> G[TLP TENDONS]
    C5 -.dynamic.-> H[DP THRUSTERS]

    D1 -.connected.-> I[HOST PLATFORM]
    D2 -.connected.-> I
    D3 -.connected.-> I
    D4 -.connected.-> I

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef default fill:#dbe5f1,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Schematic – Fixed Jacket Platform (Block-Level Cross Section)

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, May Attempt]`

**Q: Differentiate between a Compliant Tower and a Tension Leg Platform with respect to their load-resisting mechanism.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Compliant Tower | TLP |
|---|---|---|
| **Load-Resisting Mechanism** | A slender, flexible tower that resists lateral loads by allowing a small rotational deflection; restoring force is generated by the buoyancy of the structure itself, combined with the elastic bending stiffness. | A semi-submersible platform moored by **vertical, pre-tensioned tendons** to the seabed. Vertical loads are resisted by tendon pretension, which eliminates heave motion. |
| **Water Depth Range** | ~400 m to 900 m | ~300 m to 2500 m |
| **Natural Period** | Long (>25 s, intentionally) | Short (~2–4 s) |

**Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for each distinct comparison row + 1 mark for depth specification.

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Pattern]`

**Q: Define "Air-Gap" for a fixed offshore platform. Why is it critical for maritime safety?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Air-Gap** is the vertical distance between the **maximum wave crest elevation** (including crest elevation + storm surge + tide) and the **underside of the lowest deck** of a fixed offshore platform.

$$h_{air} = C_{freeboard} - (S_{swl} + R_{wave})$$

**Critical for maritime safety because:**
1. Prevents **wave-in-deck loading**, which can produce impact forces exceeding the platform's design capacity and cause catastrophic failure.
2. Protects personnel and equipment on the lowest deck from wave slam.
3. Required by **API RP 2A** and **ISO 19902** design codes.

**Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for formula + 1 mark for code reference.

---

### 5.2 Part B – Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Module-level internal choice. Students must answer **ONE** of the two questions provided. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024, Model Pattern]`

**(a) Classify the major types of fixed offshore structures. With neat sketches, explain the construction and load transfer mechanism of a Steel Jacket Platform. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification of Fixed Offshore Structures:**
1. Steel Jacket Platforms
2. Gravity-Based Structures (GBS)
3. Compliant Towers

**Steel Jacket Platform – Construction Sequence:**
1. **Fabrication:** The jacket is fabricated horizontally onshore in a fabrication yard. Tubular steel members are welded into a 3D space frame.
2. **Transportation:** The jacket is transported offshore on a heavy-lift barge.
3. **Launching:** The jacket is launched from the barge; ballasting the launch barge tips the jacket into the sea.
4. **Up-ending:** Once floating, the jacket is upended by controlled flooding of its legs.
5. **Pile Installation:** Steel piles (typically 1.5 m – 3 m diameter) are driven through the jacket legs into the seabed using an offshore pile-driving hammer.
6. **Topside Installation:** The topside (drilling/processing modules) is lifted by a heavy-lift crane vessel and set onto the jacket.

**Load Transfer Mechanism:**
- **Vertical Loads:** Topside weight $\rightarrow$ jacket deck $\rightarrow$ jacket legs $\rightarrow$ piles $\rightarrow$ seabed.
- **Lateral Loads (Wave/Wind/Current):** Distributed along the submerged jacket via Morison equation forces $\rightarrow$ transferred to the seabed through bending of the piles and the soil's passive resistance.

**Sketches:** Refer to Section 4.3 above.

**Mark Distribution:** 2 marks classification + 3 marks construction steps + 2 marks load transfer.

---

**(b) Derive the Morison Equation for the wave force on a slender vertical cylinder. State the assumptions clearly. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

(See Section 3.2 above for full derivation. Below is the KTU-style structured answer.)

**Assumptions:**
1. The cylinder diameter $D$ is much smaller than the wavelength $\lambda$, so wave diffraction effects are negligible (i.e., $D/\lambda < 0.2$).
2. Linear (Airy) wave theory is applicable.
3. The drag force is proportional to the square of the velocity, and the inertia force is proportional to the acceleration.
4. The flow is normal to the cylinder axis.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Decompose the total force into drag and inertia components.

$$f(z, t) = f_D(z, t) + f_I(z, t)$$

**Step 2:** Express the drag component using the drag coefficient $C_D$.

$$f_D(z, t) = \frac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} C_D D \, u(z, t) \, \vert u(z, t) \vert$$

**Step 3:** Express the inertia component using the inertia coefficient $C_M$.

$$f_I(z, t) = C_M \rho_{sw} \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \, \dot{u}(z, t)$$

**Step 4:** Combine.

$$f(z, t) = \frac{1}{2}\rho_{sw} C_D D \, u \vert u \vert + C_M \rho_{sw} \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \dot{u}$$

**Step 5:** Total integrated force.

$$F(t) = \int_{-d}^{\eta(t)} f(z, t) \, dz$$

**Numerical Example (for 1 mark):**
For a pile of $D = 1.5$ m, $u = 2$ m/s, $\dot{u} = 0.5$ m/s², $C_D = 1.0$, $C_M = 2.0$:

$$f_D = \frac{1}{2}(1025)(1.0)(1.5)(2)(2) = 3075 \text{ N/m}$$

$$f_I = (2.0)(1025)\frac{\pi (1.5)^2}{4}(0.5) = 1810 \text{ N/m}$$

$$f_{total} = 3075 + 1810 = 4885 \text{ N/m}$$

**Mark Distribution:** 2 marks assumptions + 3 marks derivation + 2 marks numerical application.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024, Alternative Choice]`

**(a) Compare Floating Production Storage and Offloading (FPSO) systems with Semisubmersibles for deepwater oil production. Discuss their respective advantages and limitations. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | FPSO | Semisubmersible |
|---|---|---|
| **Hull Shape** | Ship-shaped (monohull) or cylindrical (SPAR-like) | Multi-column, multi-pontoon |
| **Storage** | Large on-board storage (1–2 Mbbls of oil) | Minimal or none |
| **Vessel Motion** | Larger in waves; weather-vaning allowed | Lower motion due to multi-column form |
| **Station-Keeping** | Turret mooring with disconnectable buoy, or spread mooring | Spread mooring (8–12 lines) |
| **Hydrodynamic Response** | Higher heave and roll in extreme seas | Superior motion behaviour |
| **Cost** | Moderate CAPEX; eliminates shuttle tanker logistics in some cases | High CAPEX; lower OPEX in benign seas |
| **Application** | Deepwater and ultra-deepwater production; remote fields | Drilling, completion, production in harsh environments |

**Advantages of FPSO:**
1. **Storage capacity** eliminates dependence on dedicated shuttle tankers in many cases.
2. **Mobility** – can be disconnected and moved to a new field.
3. **Lower CAPEX** for the hull compared with a semi-submersible of equivalent payload.

**Limitations of FPSO:**
1. Larger vessel motions in extreme weather; sensitive equipment may require motion compensation.
2. Offloading operations in high seas are challenging.

**Advantages of Semisubmersible:**
1. Superior **motion response** in harsh environments (North Sea, Barents Sea).
2. Higher deck payload capacity for processing equipment.

**Limitations of Semisubmersible:**
1. No on-board storage.
2. Higher fabrication complexity and cost.

**Mark Distribution:** 3 marks tabular comparison + 2 marks advantages + 2 marks limitations.

---

**(b) Explain the components and function of a typical subsea production system used for deepwater oil extraction. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **Subsea Production System (SPS)** is a network of seafloor-installed equipment that gathers, controls, and transports hydrocarbons from subsea wells to a host facility.

**Major Components:**

1. **Subsea Wellhead and Christmas Tree:** The wellhead is the structural foundation installed at the top of the wellbore. The Christmas Tree (so named for its valve arrangement resembling a decorated tree) contains valves, chokes, and sensors to control production flow, pressure, and emergency shut-down.

2. **Manifolds:** Steel structures that gather flowlines from multiple wells, providing a common header for production, gas-lift, or water injection. They reduce the number of risers required to reach the host.

3. **PLETs and PLEMs (Pipeline End Terminations / Manifolds):** Connectors and structural supports that terminate pipelines and connect them to subsea equipment.

4. **Flowlines and Pipelines:** Rigid steel or flexible composite pipes that transport hydrocarbons from the wellhead/manifold to the host. S-lay, J-lay, or reel-lay methods are used for installation.

5. **Umbilicals:** Bundled conduits carrying hydraulic fluid (for valve actuation), electrical power, control signals, and chemical injection (methanol, scale inhibitor, corrosion inhibitor).

6. **Subsea Control Modules (SCM):** Electronic pods containing sensors, actuators, and communication interfaces for remote control from the host.

7. **ROVs (Remotely Operated Vehicles):** Tethered robotic vehicles used for installation, inspection, and intervention without divers.

**Functional Significance:**
- Enables **tieback of distant wells** to a single host platform, reducing CAPEX.
- Allows production from fields too deep for fixed platforms.
- Provides **redundancy and modularity** in field architecture.

**Mark Distribution:** 4 marks components + 2 marks function + 1 mark sketch / schematic reference.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not** write "**Compliant Tower is fixed**" – it is fixed but relies on **compliance**, not rigidity.
> 2. **Do not** confuse **FPSO** with **FSO** – FPSO has on-board **processing**, FSO only **storage**.
> 3. **Do not** forget units in numerical problems (pressure in **Pa or kPa**, force in **N or kN**).
> 4. **Do not** skip stating **$C_D$ and $C_M$ values** in the Morison equation – KTU examiners deduct marks silently.
> 5. **Do not** omit the **GMT > 0 stability criterion** when discussing floating structures.
> 6. **Do not** write vague phrases like "subsea structures include pipes" – be specific: **"rigid steel pipelines, flexible composite flowlines, umbilicals, and PLETs"**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary):**

- **Fixed Platforms:** Rely on **rigidity + pile foundation**. Best for shallow water (0 – 500 m). Examples: Jacket, GBS, Compliant Tower.
- **Floating Structures:** Rely on **buoyancy + mooring/tendons**. Best for deepwater. Examples: Semi-submersible, FPSO, SPAR, TLP, Drillship.
- **Subsea Structures:** Rest **on the seabed**, connected to host via pipelines/umbilicals. Examples: Trees, manifolds, PLETs, pipelines.
- **Selection Criterion:** Water depth is the primary decider; then payload, storage, motion sensitivity, and CAPEX.
- **Wave Loads on Slender Members:** Use the **Morison Equation** ($C_D$ and $C_M$ formulation).
- **Hydrostatic Pressure:** $P(h) = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \, g \, h$.
- **Buoyancy:** $F_b = \rho_{sw} \, g \, V_{disp}$.
- **Floating Stability:** Positive **$GM = BM - BG > 0$** is mandatory.
- **TLP:** Heave eliminated by **pre-tensioned vertical tendons**.
- **SPAR:** Heave minimized by **deep draft (~200 m)** and large mass.
- **FPSO:** **Storage + production + offloading** in one vessel.
- **Air-Gap:** Vertical clearance between wave crest and lowest deck; critical for fixed-platform safety.
- **Codes:** API RP 2A, API RP 2T, ISO 19902, ISO 19901 (KTU expects at least one reference).
- **Numerical Constants to Memorise:** $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³; $g = 9.81$ m/s²; $\rho g \approx 10055$ N/m³; $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa.
- **Degrees of Freedom of a Floating Vessel:** Surge, sway, heave, roll, pitch, yaw (Total: 6).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environmental Loads and Design Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Environmental Loads and Design Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Environmental Loads** are the external forces, moments, and kinematic actions imposed on a marine vessel or offshore structure by the surrounding natural environment. These loads arise from the interaction between the structure and the ambient physical conditions it operates within. In the context of maritime safety engineering, they constitute the **primary design-driving actions** that a ship, platform, or coastal structure must safely resist throughout its operational life.

According to **classification society rules** (DNV, ABS, Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas) and **ISO 19900-series standards**, environmental loads are categorized into the following principal families:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PESFT755 / Module 3):**
> Environmental loads constitute the deterministic and stochastic actions of wind, waves, ocean currents, tides, ice, temperature gradients, and seismic events acting upon a maritime structure. Design considerations refer to the codified procedure of combining these loads with appropriate **partial safety factors (γ)**, **return periods (T_R)**, and **limit state criteria (ULS / SLS / ALS)** to ensure structural integrity, stability, and human safety.

The principal families of environmental loading are:

- **Aerodynamic Loads** — wind pressure and gust loading on superstructures, masts, and exposed hull surfaces.
- **Hydrodynamic Loads** — wave slamming, diffraction, radiation, and buoyancy-driven actions.
- **Oceanographic Loads** — steady and tidal currents, hydrostatic pressure variation with depth.
- **Climatic Loads** — snow, ice accretion, and extreme temperature differentials.
- **Geophysical Loads** — seismic and seabed-soil interaction effects.
- **Biological / Chemical Loads** — marine growth drag, corrosion-induced section loss.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The Cyclist in a Storm**
> Imagine you are cycling back home when a sudden thunderstorm hits. Three things push you sideways: (1) the **wind** pushing your jacket and body, (2) the **rain** hitting your face like thousands of tiny hammer blows, and (3) the **mud-splash from a passing truck** acting like a sudden wave. To design a safe bicycle, the engineer must calculate exactly how hard the wind will push (drag), how heavy the rain impact feels per square metre (dynamic pressure), and how to keep the rider upright against all of these combined forces. Now scale this cyclist up to a 300-metre ship or an offshore oil rig — and replace the rain with ocean waves, the mud with currents, and add icebergs, earthquakes, and freezing temperatures. **That is precisely the job of an environmental loads engineer.**

## 1.3 Governing Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sea-water density | ρ_sw | **1025** | kg/m³ |
| Air density (at 15 °C, 1 atm) | ρ_air | **1.225** | kg/m³ |
| Gravitational acceleration | g | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Kinematic viscosity of seawater | ν | **1.0 × 10⁻⁶** | m²/s |
| Design return period (typical offshore) | T_R | **100** | years |
| Drag coefficient (bluff body) | C_d | **0.6 – 1.2** | — |
| Inertia coefficient (circular cylinder) | C_m | **1.5 – 2.0** | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always quote **ρ = 1025 kg/m³ for seawater** and **1.225 kg/m³ for air** unless a question explicitly specifies a different value. Marks are routinely lost for using ρ = 1000 kg/m³ (fresh water) in maritime problems.

## 1.4 The Design Philosophy — Why Environmental Loads Matter

The fundamental design philosophy rests on the **Load–Resistance Inequality**:

$$R_d \geq S_d$$

where **R_d** is the design resistance (capacity) and **S_d** is the design load effect (demand). In KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) examinations, students are expected to:

- Identify the **controlling environmental load case** for a given scenario.
- Apply the correct **load combination rule** (e.g., DNV-OS-C101 combinations).
- Justify the **return period selection** based on the **Consequence Class** of the structure.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students frequently treat environmental loads as static forces. In reality, **waves and wind gusts are stochastic processes** characterised by a **power spectral density (PSD)**. For KTU Part B answers, you must always mention the probabilistic nature of environmental loads.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Five Pillars of Environmental Loading

Every maritime environmental load can be decomposed into one (or a combination) of the following five physical mechanisms:

### Pillar 1 — Drag-Type Loading (Quadratic in Velocity)
Occurs when a fluid flows around a bluff body and separates, creating a pressure differential. Force scales with **V²**.

$$F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_d\, A\, V^{2}$$

> **Why V²?** Doubling the wind speed quadruples the drag — this is why **Category 5 cyclones (V > 69 m/s)** cause exponentially greater destruction than tropical storms (V ≈ 20 m/s).

### Pillar 2 — Inertia-Type Loading (Linear in Acceleration)
Dominant when the **Keulegan–Carpenter number** is small (KC < 3), typical for short waves acting on large-diameter cylinders.

$$F_I = \rho\, C_m\, \nabla\cdot \dot{u}$$

For a circular cylinder of diameter **D**:

$$F_I = C_m\, \rho\, \tfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}\, \dot{u}$$

### Pillar 3 — Buoyancy and Hydrostatic Loading
Pressure increases linearly with depth:

$$p(z) = p_{atm} + \rho\, g\, z$$

### Pillar 4 — Slamming and Impact Loading
Impulsive loads from wave impacts on flat or near-horizontal surfaces (e.g., deck wetness, bow flare). Modelled by **Wagner's solution** or empirical **slamming coefficients**.

### Pillar 5 — Stochastic / Spectral Loading
Real ocean waves are **random Gaussian processes** described by a **wave spectrum** (Pierson–Moskowitz, JONSWAP, or Torsethaugen). The design wave is then extracted for a given **return period T_R**.

## 2.2 Wind Load Theory in Depth

### 2.2.1 Mean Wind Profile (Power Law)
Wind speed varies with elevation above sea level following the **power-law (Hellmann) profile**:

$$V(z) = V_{ref} \left( \dfrac{z}{z_{ref}} \right)^{\alpha}$$

where **α** is the **Hellmann exponent** (≈ 0.10 over open sea, 0.14 near coastal areas, 0.40 over urban terrain).

### 2.2.2 Velocity Pressure
$$q(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\, \rho_{air}\, V(z)^{2}$$

### 2.2.3 Gust Effect Factor
To account for dynamic amplification:

$$F_{wind} = G\, C_f\, q(z)\, A_{ref}$$

where **G** = gust effect factor (typically 1.0 – 1.4 for rigid offshore structures).

## 2.3 Wave Load Theory — Morison's Equation (The Heart of Marine Hydrodynamics)

For a **vertical slender cylinder of diameter D** in waves (valid for **D / L < 0.2**, where L is wavelength), the inline horizontal force per unit length is:

$$f(t) = f_D(t) + f_I(t)$$

$$f(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_d\, D\, u \vert u \vert \; + \; C_m\, \rho\, \tfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}\, \dot{u}$$

> **Physical meaning:** The first term is **drag** (resists fluid flow), the second is **inertia** (resists fluid acceleration). Note the **∣u∣** sign convention — drag always opposes the velocity direction.

### 2.3.1 Kinematics of Airy (Linear) Waves
For a wave of height **H** and period **T** in depth **d**:

$$u(z,t) = \dfrac{H\, g\, T}{4 L} \cdot \dfrac{\cosh\!\left[k(z+d)\right]}{\cosh(kd)} \cdot \cos(kx - \omega t)$$

$$\dot{u}(z,t) = \dfrac{H\, g\, T}{4 L} \cdot \dfrac{\cosh\!\left[k(z+d)\right]}{\cosh(kd)} \cdot \omega \cdot \sin(kx - \omega t)$$

with dispersion relation: **ω² = gk tanh(kd)**.

## 2.4 Current Loads

Ocean currents (tidal, wind-driven, loop, density) superimpose on wave particle kinematics:

$$u_{total}(z,t) = u_{wave}(z,t) + U_{current}(z)$$

Current force is purely drag-type:

$$F_{current} = \tfrac{1}{2}\, \rho\, C_d\, A\, U_{current}^{2}$$

## 2.5 Ice Loads

Three failure mechanisms govern ice–structure interaction:

| Mechanism | Governing Formula | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| Crushing | $F = p \cdot D \cdot t$ | Vertical structures |
| Bending | $F = 0.68 \cdot \sigma_f \cdot h^{1.75} \cdot D^{0.25}$ | Sloping structures |
| Buckling | $F = C \cdot h^{1.25} \cdot D^{0.5}$ | Wide structures |

where **p** = ice pressure (≈ 1.5 – 3.0 MPa), **σ_f** = flexural strength (≈ 0.5 – 1.5 MPa), **h** = ice thickness.

## 2.6 Seismic Loads

For offshore structures in seismically active zones (e.g., Arabian Sea, Sea of Japan):

$$F_{seismic} = m \cdot S_a(T_1)$$

where **S_a** is the **spectral acceleration** at the structure's **fundamental period T₁** obtained from the site-specific **response spectrum**.

## 2.7 Load Combination Framework (DNV / ISO Style)

For the **Ultimate Limit State (ULS)** under environmental loading:

$$S_d = \gamma_g\, G_k + \gamma_q\, Q_k + \gamma_E\, E_k$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical γ value |
|---|---|---|
| γ_g | Dead load factor | 1.2 (unfavourable) |
| γ_q | Live load factor | 1.3 |
| γ_E | Environmental load factor | 1.3 (operating), 1.0 (extreme) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Pearl:** The three standard ULS combinations taught in the PESFT755 syllabus are:
> 1. **ELS — Extreme Level Storm** (100-year return): Wind + Wave + Current (aligned).
> 2. **ALS — Accidental Limit State** (e.g., fire, collision, dropped object).
> 3. **SLS — Serviceability Limit State** (1-year return): Deflection, vibration, comfort checks.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Load Type | Formula | Symbol Meanings | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wind drag force | $F_w = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A V^{2}$ | ρ_a = air density, V = wind speed | N |
| 2 | Velocity pressure | $q = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^{2}$ | Pressure exerted at speed V | Pa |
| 3 | Power-law profile | $V(z) = V_{ref}(z/z_{ref})^{\alpha}$ | α ≈ 0.10 open sea | m/s |
| 4 | Morison drag | $f_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d D\, u \vert u \vert$ | u = water particle velocity | N/m |
| 5 | Morison inertia | $f_I = C_m \rho (\pi D^{2}/4)\, \dot{u}$ | $\dot{u}$ = water acceleration | N/m |
| 6 | Airy wave velocity | $u = (HgT / 4L) \cdot \cosh[k(z+d)]/\cosh(kd) \cdot \cos\theta$ | θ = kx − ωt | m/s |
| 7 | Dispersion relation | $\omega^{2} = gk \tanh(kd)$ | ω = 2π/T, k = 2π/L | rad²/s² |
| 8 | Current force | $F_c = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A U^{2}$ | U = current speed | N |
| 9 | Hydrostatic pressure | $p = \rho g z$ | z = depth | Pa |
| 10 | Ice bending | $F_i = 0.68 \sigma_f h^{1.75} D^{0.25}$ | h = ice thickness | N |
| 11 | Significant wave height | $H_{1/3} \approx 4.0 \sigma_{\eta}$ | σ_η = wave elevation std dev | m |
| 12 | Return period | $P_{exceed} = 1/T_R$ | T_R in years | — |
| 13 | Seismic base shear | $V_b = m \cdot S_a(T_1)$ | m = effective mass | N |
| 14 | Load combination ULS | $S_d = \gamma_g G_k + \gamma_q Q_k + \gamma_E E_k$ | γ partial factors | varies |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Reminder:** In every KTU numerical answer, write the **unit** at the end. The board examiner deducts 0.5 mark per missing unit. Always show units in SI (N, Pa, m, s).

## 2.9 Real-World Utility and Industry Application

The above framework is the daily working language of:

- **Classification Societies** — DNV, ABS, Lloyd's, BV, IRS — who publish the design codes.
- **Offshore Oil & Gas** — platforms in the North Sea, Gulf of Mexico, Persian Gulf, Mumbai High.
- **Naval Architecture** — warship survivability against missile shock, green-water loading.
- **Renewable Energy** — offshore wind turbine monopile and jacket foundation design.
- **Coastal Engineering** — breakwaters, jetties, quay walls.
- **Subsea Engineering** — umbilical, riser, and pipeline VIV (vortex-induced vibration) analysis.

> **Career Note:** Environmental loads engineering is one of the **highest-paying specialisations** within naval architecture and offshore engineering. The skills taught in Module 3 directly map to job roles at **TechnipFMC, Aker Solutions, Saipem, Cochin Shipyard, and L&T Shipbuilding**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wind Drag Force on a Ship Superstructure

### Problem Statement
A naval frigate has a **box-shaped superstructure** of projected area **A = 120 m²** facing the wind. The drag coefficient is **C_d = 1.2**. The mean wind speed at deck level (z = 18 m) is **V₁₀ = 28 m/s** measured at the standard 10-m reference height. Using the **power-law profile** with **α = 0.10**, compute the wind drag force on the superstructure.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Identify the relevant formula — wind drag force.

$$F_w = \tfrac{1}{2}\, \rho_a\, C_d\, A\, V(z)^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the wind speed at deck elevation z = 18 m using the power-law profile.

$$V(z) = V_{ref}\left(\dfrac{z}{z_{ref}}\right)^{\alpha}$$

Substituting **V_ref = 28 m/s, z = 18 m, z_ref = 10 m, α = 0.10**:

$$V(18) = 28 \times \left(\dfrac{18}{10}\right)^{0.10}$$

$$\dfrac{18}{10} = 1.8$$

$$1.8^{0.10} = e^{0.10 \cdot \ln(1.8)} = e^{0.10 \cdot 0.5878} = e^{0.05878} = 1.0605$$

$$V(18) = 28 \times 1.0605 = 29.69\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the velocity pressure.

$$q = \tfrac{1}{2}\, \rho_a\, V^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times (29.69)^{2}$$

$$(29.69)^{2} = 881.50\ \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}$$

$$q = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 881.50 = 539.87\ \text{Pa}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the wind drag force.

$$F_w = C_d\, q\, A = 1.2 \times 539.87 \times 120$$

$$F_w = 1.2 \times 64784.4 = 77{,}741.3\ \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{F_w \approx 77.74\ \text{kN}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification by Dimensional Analysis:** [N] = [kg/m³] × [m²/s²] × [m²] = [kg·m/s²] = [N] ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Morison Wave Force on a Vertical Cylindrical Pile

### Problem Statement
A vertical steel pile of diameter **D = 1.5 m** extends from the seabed (depth **d = 25 m**) to above the still water level. An Airy wave of height **H = 4 m** and period **T = 7 s** propagates past the pile. The drag and inertia coefficients are **C_d = 1.0** and **C_m = 2.0**. Compute the **maximum horizontal wave force per unit length** at the still water level (z = 0). Use **ρ = 1025 kg/m³** and **g = 9.81 m/s²**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Compute the wavelength **L** from the dispersion relation.

$$\omega^{2} = g k \tanh(k d)$$

with **ω = 2π/T = 2π/7 = 0.8976 rad/s**.

$$\omega^{2} = (0.8976)^{2} = 0.8057\ \text{rad}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}$$

$$k d = \dfrac{2\pi}{L} \times 25 = \dfrac{157.08}{L}$$

Trial L = 60 m → kd = 2.618 → tanh(2.618) = 0.9906
RHS = 9.81 × (2π/60) × 0.9906 = 9.81 × 0.1047 × 0.9906 = 1.0178
LHS = 0.8057. RHS too high → try larger L.

Trial L = 70 m → kd = 2.244 → tanh(2.244) = 0.9777
RHS = 9.81 × (2π/70) × 0.9777 = 9.81 × 0.08976 × 0.9777 = 0.8609
LHS = 0.8057. RHS still high → try larger L.

Trial L = 73 m → kd = 2.152 → tanh(2.152) = 0.9738
RHS = 9.81 × (2π/73) × 0.9738 = 9.81 × 0.08608 × 0.9738 = 0.8223
Very close to LHS.

Trial L = 73.5 m → kd = 2.137 → tanh(2.137) = 0.9732
RHS = 9.81 × (2π/73.5) × 0.9732 = 9.81 × 0.08549 × 0.9732 = 0.8162
Very close — interpolate.

$$\boxed{L \approx 73.6\ \text{m}}$$

$$k = \dfrac{2\pi}{73.6} = 0.0854\ \text{rad/m}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the velocity amplitude at z = 0.

$$u_{max}(0) = \dfrac{H g T}{4 L} \cdot \dfrac{\cosh(k d)}{\cosh(k d)}$$

(since cosh(0) = cosh(kd)/cosh(kd) = 1 at z = 0)

$$u_{max}(0) = \dfrac{H g T}{4 L} = \dfrac{4 \times 9.81 \times 7}{4 \times 73.6}$$

$$u_{max}(0) = \dfrac{274.68}{294.4} = 0.9330\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the acceleration amplitude at z = 0.

$$\dot{u}_{max}(0) = u_{max}(0) \cdot \omega = 0.9330 \times 0.8976 = 0.8375\ \text{m/s}^{2}$$

**Step 4:** Identify where maximum force occurs.

The drag force is maximum when **u is maximum**; the inertia force is maximum when **|u̇| is maximum** — but **they occur at different phases** (90° apart in Airy waves). Hence the total force is maximum not at a single instant. Conventionally, two estimates are reported:

**(a) Maximum Drag Phase** — drag dominates (KC >> 1, large D or long waves):

$$f_{D,max} = \tfrac{1}{2}\, \rho\, C_d\, D\, u_{max}^{2}$$

$$f_{D,max} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1.0 \times 1.5 \times (0.9330)^{2}$$

$$f_{D,max} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1.5 \times 0.8705 = 669.4\ \text{N/m}$$

**(b) Maximum Inertia Phase** — inertia dominates (KC < 3):

$$f_{I,max} = C_m\, \rho\, \tfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}\, \dot{u}_{max}$$

$$f_{I,max} = 2.0 \times 1025 \times \tfrac{\pi (1.5)^{2}}{4} \times 0.8375$$

$$f_{I,max} = 2.0 \times 1025 \times \tfrac{3.1416 \times 2.25}{4} \times 0.8375$$

$$f_{I,max} = 2.0 \times 1025 \times 1.7671 \times 0.8375 = 3{,}033.0\ \text{N/m}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the **Keulegan–Carpenter check** to determine which dominates.

$$KC = \dfrac{u_{max}\, T}{D} = \dfrac{0.9330 \times 7}{1.5} = 4.35$$

Since **KC > 3**, drag is significant, but inertia also matters. The conservative total maximum is taken as the **vector sum envelope**:

$$f_{total,max} \approx f_{D,max} + f_{I,max} = 669.4 + 3{,}033.0$$

$$\boxed{f_{total,max} \approx 3{,}702\ \text{N/m} \approx 3.70\ \text{kN/m}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Significant Wave Height from Spectral Data

### Problem Statement
Wave measurements yield a wave elevation standard deviation **σ_η = 0.75 m**. Compute the significant wave height **H_{1/3}** and the **1/100-year design wave height H_{100}** assuming a **Rayleigh distribution** and **H_{1/3} = 4σ_η**.

**Step 1:** Significant wave height.

$$H_{1/3} = 4.0\, \sigma_{\eta} = 4.0 \times 0.75 = 3.0\ \text{m}$$

**Step 2:** Probability of exceedance for a given wave height H in a Rayleigh-distributed sea state.

$$P(H > H^{*}) = \exp\!\left[-2\left(\dfrac{H^{*}}{H_{1/3}}\right)^{2}\right]$$

**Step 3:** Number of waves encountered in time T_R (with T_mean ≈ 0.9 T_p and zero-crossing period T_z).

Assuming **T_z = 6 s**, number of waves in 100 years:

$$N_{100} = \dfrac{T_R \times 365.25 \times 24 \times 3600}{T_z}$$

$$N_{100} = \dfrac{100 \times 31{,}557{,}600}{6} = 5.26 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 4:** Probability of exceedance of design wave (Poisson assumption).

$$P_{exceed} = \dfrac{1}{N_{100}} = 1.90 \times 10^{-9}$$

**Step 5:** Invert the Rayleigh distribution.

$$\exp\!\left[-2\left(\dfrac{H_{100}}{H_{1/3}}\right)^{2}\right] = 1.90 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$-2\left(\dfrac{H_{100}}{H_{1/3}}\right)^{2} = \ln(1.90 \times 10^{-9}) = -20.08$$

$$\left(\dfrac{H_{100}}{H_{1/3}}\right)^{2} = 10.04$$

$$\dfrac{H_{100}}{H_{1/3}} = 3.169$$

$$H_{100} = 3.169 \times 3.0 = 9.51\ \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{H_{100} \approx 9.51\ \text{m}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Load Dominance Regimes

| Regime | KC Number | Wave Type | Dominant Force | Design Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Small KC | KC < 3 | Short-period, large D | Inertia | Use **f_I** term only |
| Moderate KC | 3 < KC < 30 | Typical offshore | Both | Use **Morison full** equation |
| Large KC | KC > 30 | Long-period, small D | Drag | Use **f_D** term only |
| Very large D | D/L > 0.2 | Shorelines, SBMs | Diffraction | Use **MacCamy–Fuchs** theory |

| Consequence Class | Return Period T_R | γ_E (ULS) | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| CC1 (low) | 10 yr | 1.0 | Temporary buoys |
| CC2 (normal) | 100 yr | 1.3 | FPSO, semi-sub |
| CC3 (high) | 1000 yr | 1.3 | Nuclear platforms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numericals Strategy:** Always show four things — (1) given data, (2) chosen formula with justification, (3) stepwise substitution, (4) final answer with units. This sequence alone secures 80% of the marks.
