# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**Explain the difference between deterministic and stochastic environmental loads. Give one example of each.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> A **deterministic environmental load** is one whose magnitude can be computed exactly from first principles given the input conditions — e.g., the drag force due to a known steady current of 1.5 m/s on a pile (computed via $F = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V^2$).
> A **stochastic environmental load** is one whose time-history is random and must be described by a probability distribution — e.g., ocean wave elevation, which is modelled by a **wave spectrum** (JONSWAP) and individual wave heights by a **Rayleigh distribution**.
> Deterministic loads yield a single force value; stochastic loads are characterised by **statistical descriptors** (significant value, spectral peak period, return period).
> *(Award full 3 marks for clear distinction + correct example.)*

### Question A2
**What is the Keulegan–Carpenter number? State its significance in selecting the wave force model.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The **Keulegan–Carpenter (KC) number** is a dimensionless parameter defined as:
> $$KC = \dfrac{u_{max}\, T}{D}$$
> where **u_max** is the maximum horizontal water particle velocity, **T** is the wave period, and **D** is the cylinder diameter.
> **Significance:**
> - **KC < 3** → inertia-dominated flow → use only the **inertia term** of Morison's equation.
> - **3 < KC < 30** → both drag and inertia matter → use **full Morison's equation**.
> - **KC > 30** → drag-dominated flow → use only the **drag term**.
> It therefore guides the engineer in choosing the appropriate wave force formulation for a given structural geometry and sea state.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

**Design a mooring dolphin (vertical cylinder) in a harbour for the following data:**
- Pile diameter: D = 2.0 m
- Water depth: d = 12 m
- Design wave height: H = 3.5 m, period T = 8 s
- Drag coefficient: C_d = 1.0
- Inertia coefficient: C_m = 2.0
- ρ = 1025 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s²

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Determine the wavelength L, and the maximum horizontal water particle velocity and acceleration at the still water level using linear Airy wave theory.**

**Step 1: Wavelength from dispersion relation**
$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi}{T} = \dfrac{2\pi}{8} = 0.7854\ \text{rad/s}$$
$$\omega^2 = 0.6169\ \text{rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$
$$\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kd) \Rightarrow 0.6169 = 9.81 \times k \times \tanh(12k)$$

Iterate: **k = 0.0900 rad/m → L = 69.81 m** (satisfies dispersion with error < 1%).

**Step 2: Velocity amplitude at z = 0**
$$u_{max}(0) = \dfrac{H g T}{4 L} = \dfrac{3.5 \times 9.81 \times 8}{4 \times 69.81} = \dfrac{274.68}{279.24} = 0.9837\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3: Acceleration amplitude at z = 0**
$$\dot{u}_{max}(0) = u_{max}(0) \times \omega = 0.9837 \times 0.7854 = 0.7726\ \text{m/s}^{2}$$

> ✅ **Answer:** L ≈ 69.81 m, u_max ≈ 0.984 m/s, u̇_max ≈ 0.773 m/s²

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compute the maximum drag force per unit length and the maximum inertia force per unit length on the pile at z = 0. Hence determine which term dominates and find the total design force per unit length.**

**Step 1: Maximum drag force**
$$f_{D,max} = \tfrac{1}{2}\, \rho\, C_d\, D\, u_{max}^{2}$$
$$f_{D,max} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1.0 \times 2.0 \times (0.9837)^{2}$$
$$f_{D,max} = 1025 \times 0.9677 = 992.4\ \text{N/m} \approx 0.992\ \text{kN/m}$$

**Step 2: Maximum inertia force**
$$f_{I,max} = C_m\, \rho\, \tfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}\, \dot{u}_{max}$$
$$f_{I,max} = 2.0 \times 1025 \times \tfrac{\pi (2.0)^{2}}{4} \times 0.7726$$
$$f_{I,max} = 2.0 \times 1025 \times 3.1416 \times 0.7726$$
$$f_{I,max} = 4977.5\ \text{N/m} \approx 4.98\ \text{kN/m}$$

**Step 3: KC check**
$$KC = \dfrac{u_{max}\, T}{D} = \dfrac{0.9837 \times 8}{2.0} = 3.93$$

KC lies in **3 < KC < 30** → both drag and inertia matter → use the **full Morison sum envelope**.

**Step 4: Total design force per unit length**
$$f_{total,max} = f_{D,max} + f_{I,max} = 0.992 + 4.98$$
$$\boxed{f_{total,max} \approx 5.97\ \text{kN/m}}$$

**Inertia dominates** because the diameter is relatively large (D = 2 m) and the wave period is moderate.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**An offshore wind turbine monopile foundation is to be designed for the following environmental conditions:**
- Monopile diameter: D = 6.0 m
- Water depth: d = 30 m
- Significant wave height: H_{1/3} = 5.0 m, peak period T_p = 10 s
- Mean wind speed at hub height (z = 90 m): V = 35 m/s (open-sea α = 0.10, V_ref at 10 m)
- Surface current speed: U_c = 1.2 m/s
- ρ_air = 1.225 kg/m³, ρ_sw = 1025 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s²

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Estimate the 100-year return period design wave height H_{100} assuming Rayleigh distribution, and compute the corresponding wavelength in 30 m depth.**

**Step 1: Probability of exceedance in 100 years**
With T_z ≈ 0.9 × T_p = 9 s, the number of waves in 100 years:
$$N_{100} = \dfrac{100 \times 31{,}557{,}600}{9} = 3.51 \times 10^{8}$$
$$P_{exceed} = 1/N_{100} = 2.85 \times 10^{-9}$$

**Step 2: Invert Rayleigh distribution**
$$\exp\!\left[-2\left(\dfrac{H_{100}}{H_{1/3}}\right)^{2}\right] = 2.85 \times 10^{-9}$$
$$-2\left(\dfrac{H_{100}}{H_{1/3}}\right)^{2} = -19.67$$
$$\left(\dfrac{H_{100}}{H_{1/3}}\right)^{2} = 9.835$$
$$\dfrac{H_{100}}{H_{1/3}} = 3.136$$
$$H_{100} = 3.136 \times 5.0 = 15.68\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3: Wavelength for H_{100} and T_p = 10 s**
Using dispersion relation in 30 m depth, iterate to find L:
Trial L = 156 m → kd = 1.208, tanh = 0.834, RHS = 9.81 × (2π/156) × 0.834 = 0.330 → too low.
Trial L = 110 m → kd = 1.714, tanh = 0.938, RHS = 9.81 × 0.0571 × 0.938 = 0.525 → still low.
Trial L = 90 m → kd = 2.094, tanh = 0.970, RHS = 9.81 × 0.0698 × 0.970 = 0.664 → higher.
Trial L = 100 m → kd = 1.885, tanh = 0.957, RHS = 9.81 × 0.0628 × 0.957 = 0.590 → between.
Trial L = 95 m → kd = 1.984, tanh = 0.964, RHS = 9.81 × 0.0661 × 0.964 = 0.625 → close to LHS = ω² = 0.3948 rad²/s²... 

Recompute: ω² = (2π/10)² = 0.3948
Trial L = 95 m → RHS = 0.625 → still > LHS. Need larger L.
Trial L = 130 m → kd = 1.450, tanh = 0.896, RHS = 9.81 × 0.0483 × 0.896 = 0.425 → close.
Trial L = 125 m → kd = 1.508, tanh = 0.908, RHS = 9.81 × 0.0503 × 0.908 = 0.448 → higher.
Trial L = 140 m → kd = 1.346, tanh = 0.874, RHS = 9.81 × 0.0449 × 0.874 = 0.385 → lower than LHS.
Linear interpolation: L ≈ 0.3948 lies between L = 130 (0.425) and L = 140 (0.385).
$$L = 130 + 10 \times \dfrac{0.425 - 0.3948}{0.425 - 0.385} = 130 + 7.6 = 137.6\ \text{m}$$

> ✅ **Answer:** H_{100} ≈ 15.68 m, L ≈ 137.6 m

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Estimate the total wind drag force on the tower (cylindrical, diameter 6 m, height 90 m, C_d = 0.7) using the power-law wind profile. Comment on whether a cylindrical tower experiences vortex-induced vibrations and how it is mitigated.**

**Step 1: Wind speed at top (z = 90 m)**
$$V(90) = V_{ref}\left(\dfrac{90}{10}\right)^{0.10} = 35 \times 9^{0.10} = 35 \times 1.245 = 43.57\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2: Wind speed at bottom (z = 0, just above MSL)**
$$V(0) = 35 \times \left(\dfrac{0}{10}\right)^{0.10} = 0$$ 

For numerical integration, use the average of top and 10-m speed, or integrate analytically.

**Average velocity approach** — divide tower into 9 segments of 10 m, compute V at z = 10, 20, ..., 90:

| z (m) | (z/10)^0.10 | V(z) m/s | V(z)² |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10 | 1.000 | 35.0 | 1225 |
| 20 | 1.072 | 37.5 | 1406 |
| 30 | 1.116 | 39.1 | 1528 |
| 40 | 1.149 | 40.2 | 1616 |
| 50 | 1.175 | 41.1 | 1689 |
| 60 | 1.196 | 41.9 | 1756 |
| 70 | 1.215 | 42.5 | 1806 |
| 80 | 1.231 | 43.1 | 1858 |
| 90 | 1.246 | 43.6 | 1901 |

Average V² = (1225 + 1406 + 1528 + 1616 + 1689 + 1756 + 1806 + 1858 + 1901) / 9 = 14885 / 9 = 1653.9 m²/s²

**Step 3: Drag force per unit length at average**
$$f_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\, \rho_a\, C_d\, D\, \langle V^2 \rangle = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.7 \times 6.0 \times 1653.9$$
$$f_D = 4253.8\ \text{N/m} = 4.25\ \text{kN/m}$$

**Step 4: Total wind force over 90 m height**
$$F_{wind} = f_D \times H = 4253.8 \times 90 = 382{,}842\ \text{N}$$
$$\boxed{F_{wind} \approx 382.8\ \text{kN}}$$

**Step 5: Comment on Vortex-Induced Vibration (VIV)**
A **cylindrical tower** in cross-flow regularly sheds **von Kármán vortices** at a frequency:
$$f_v = \dfrac{St \cdot V}{D}$$
where **Strouhal number St ≈ 0.20**.

For V = 35 m/s, D = 6 m: **f_v = 0.20 × 35 / 6 = 1.17 Hz**, which often coincides with the natural frequency of large monopiles (1–3 Hz). This resonance causes **lock-in vibration** that can fatigue the structure.

**Mitigation measures:**
1. **Tuned Mass Dampers (TMD)** inside the tower.
2. **Helical strakes** or **fairings** on the outer surface to break vortex shedding coherence.
3. **Tapered or polygonal tower cross-section** (e.g., octagonal) to alter shedding frequency.
4. **Active flow control** (synthetic jets, plasma actuators — research stage).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PESFT755 Module 3:**
>
> 1. **Using ρ = 1000 kg/m³ instead of 1025 kg/m³ for seawater** — KTU board deducts **0.5 mark** per occurrence. Always default to **ρ_sw = 1025 kg/m³**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the absolute-value sign in drag term** — writing $u^2$ instead of $u \vert u \vert$ in Morison's equation costs **1 mark** because the sign captures the directional nature of drag.
>
> 3. **Linearisation error in wave kinematics** — students often compute $u$ at z = 0 only, forgetting that **u varies with depth**. KTU expects at least a comment on the hyperbolic depth-decay.
>
> 4. **Ignoring KC check** — Morison's equation validity requires **D/L < 0.2**. If violated, the examiner expects you to mention **MacCamy–Fuchs diffraction theory**.
>
> 5. **Return period confusion** — 100-year return period does NOT mean the event happens every 100 years; it means the **annual exceedance probability is 1%**. KTU board deducts marks for this conceptual error.
>
> 6. **Unit omissions** — every numerical answer without a unit loses 0.5 mark.
>
> 7. **Confusing SLS, ULS, ALS** — Serviceability, Ultimate, and Accidental Limit States have different return periods and γ factors. Mixing them up is a **2-mark penalty**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Environmental Loads & Design Considerations**

- [ ] **Environmental loads** are stochastic in nature — always quote a return period.
- [ ] **Standard densities:** ρ_air = 1.225 kg/m³; ρ_seawater = 1025 kg/m³; g = 9.81 m/s².
- [ ] **Wind drag force:** $F_w = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A V^2$ — quadratic in wind speed.
- [ ] **Power-law wind profile:** $V(z) = V_{ref}(z/z_{ref})^{\alpha}$ with α ≈ 0.10 (open sea).
- [ ] **Morison equation** valid only for **D/L < 0.2** — otherwise use **diffraction theory**.
- [ ] **Drag term:** $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d D\, u \vert u \vert$ — note the **absolute value**.
- [ ] **Inertia term:** $C_m \rho (\pi D^2 / 4) \dot{u}$ — depends on **water acceleration**, not velocity.
- [ ] **Airy wave theory** assumes **small wave steepness (H/L < 1/7)** and **constant depth**.
- [ ] **Dispersion relation:** $\omega^2 = g k \tanh(k d)$ — solve iteratively for L.
- [ ] **KC number** determines which Morison term dominates: KC < 3 (inertia); KC > 30 (drag); in between → both.
- [ ] **Significant wave height:** $H_{1/3} \approx 4.0 \sigma_\eta$ — average of the highest 1/3 of waves.
- [ ] **Rayleigh distribution:** $P(H > H^*) = \exp[-2(H^*/H_{1/3})^2]$ for individual wave heights.
- [ ] **Return period link:** $P_{annual} = 1/T_R$; number of waves $N = T_R \times 31.56 \times 10^6 / T_z$.
- [ ] **Ice loads:** three mechanisms — crushing, bending, buckling — depending on structure slope.
- [ ] **Seismic loads:** $V_b = m \cdot S_a(T_1)$ — function of site response spectrum.
- [ ] **Load combinations:** DNV-OS-C101 style: ULS (100 yr), SLS (1 yr), ALS (accidental).
- [ ] **Partial safety factors:** γ_g = 1.2, γ_q = 1.3, γ_E = 1.3 (ULS operating), 1.0 (extreme).
- [ ] **VIV (Vortex-Induced Vibration)** is critical for slender cylinders — mitigate with strakes, TMDs, fairings.
- [ ] **Slamming loads** are impulsive — relevant for bow flare and horizontal members near SWL.
- [ ] **Hydrostatic pressure** $p = \rho g z$ — must be combined with atmospheric head.
- [ ] **Marine growth** adds **0.05 – 0.15 m** effective radius and increases **C_d** by 20 – 40%.
- [ ] **Classification society rules** (DNV, ABS, LR) are the codified source of all design criteria — reference them in design viva.

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *Read the question carefully → Identify the load regime → Pick the correct formula → Compute with units → Comment on limitations and design implications.* This five-step ritual will help you score **90%+ in PESFT755 Module 3**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structural Integrity and Inspection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Structural Integrity and Inspection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Structural Integrity** in maritime engineering refers to the capability of a vessel's hull, superstructure, and load-bearing components to withstand operational, environmental, and accidental loads without undergoing unacceptable deformation, fracture, buckling, or collapse throughout its service life. It is governed by classification society rules (e.g., **DNV**, **Lloyd's Register**, **ABS**, **Bureau Veritas**), SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea) regulations, and the **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)** unified requirements.

Mathematically, structural integrity is satisfied when:

$$\sigma_{applied} \leq \frac{\sigma_{yield}}{\gamma_m \cdot SF}$$

where $\sigma_{applied}$ is the calculated stress, $\sigma_{yield}$ is the material yield stress, $\gamma_m$ is the partial safety factor for material, and $SF$ is the safety factor (typically **1.5 to 2.0** for primary hull members).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Structural integrity is NOT a single check. It is a **multi-criteria design and maintenance philosophy** combining ultimate strength, fatigue life, corrosion allowance, buckling resistance, and inspection-based condition monitoring.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ship as Bone Skeleton"

Imagine a human skeleton. The hull of a ship behaves exactly like your **skeletal system**:
- The **keel** = your spine (the main longitudinal backbone).
- The **frames and ribs** = your ribcage (transverse support).
- The **deck plates and stringers** = your shoulder blades.
- The **bulkheads** = your skull plates (compartmentalisation prevents total collapse).

Just like a doctor performs regular **X-rays, MRIs, and blood tests** to detect fractures or osteoporosis before catastrophic failure, a ship undergoes scheduled **surveys and NDT inspections** to detect corrosion, cracks, or buckling before the hull gives way. The ocean is the "gravity" — it constantly loads and unloads the structure through waves, slamming, and vibration. Structural integrity is the discipline of ensuring this marine "skeleton" remains healthy for 20–25 years of service life.

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Maritime Safety

| Failure Mode | Real-World Consequence | Loss of Life Risk |
|---|---|---|
| Hull girder collapse | Total vessel loss | Very High |
| Local plate buckling | Flooding of compartment | High |
| Fatigue crack propagation | Sudden fracture | Critical |
| Corrosion thinning | Structural collapse under wave load | High |
| Weld defect growth | Brittle fracture in cold seas | Critical |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Always link structural integrity answers to a **classification society rule reference** (IACS UR-S, UR-Z) and mention **SOLAS Chapter II-1** where applicable. Examiners reward regulatory awareness.

---

### 1.4 The Four Pillars of Structural Integrity

1. **Ultimate Strength** — Will the ship survive the worst expected load without collapsing?
2. **Fatigue Strength** — Will cyclic wave loading cause cracks over 25 years?
3. **Buckling Resistance** — Will thin plates wrinkle under compression?
4. **Corrosion & Maintenance** — Will material loss compromise the above three?

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hull Girder Strength — The "Backbone" of Ship Safety

The hull girder is treated as a **box beam** with the following equivalent section properties:

- **Neutral axis position** measured from baseline: $\bar{z}$ (m)
- **Moment of inertia**: $I_{NA}$ (m⁴)
- **Section modulus**: $Z = I_{NA} / y$ (m³), where $y$ is the distance from the neutral axis to the extreme fibre.

Bending stress at any longitudinal position:

$$\sigma_{xl} = \frac{M_{sw} + M_{wv}}{Z} \cdot 10^{-6} \quad \text{(MPa)}$$

where:
- $M_{sw}$ = Still water bending moment (kN·m)
- $M_{wv}$ = Wave-induced bending moment (kN·m)

### 2.2 Critical Failure Modes Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Yielding Check**
The bending stress must remain below the allowable:
$$\sigma_{xl} \leq \sigma_{perm} = \frac{175}{k} \text{ MPa}$$

where $k$ is the material factor (typically **1.0 for mild steel, 0.78 for H32 high-tensile steel**).

**Step 2 — Buckling Check (Stiffened Plate Panel)**
For a plate panel between stiffeners, the critical buckling stress is given by:
$$\sigma_{cr} = k_c \cdot \frac{\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \cdot \left(\frac{t}{s}\right)^2$$

where:
- $k_c$ = buckling coefficient (function of aspect ratio $l/s$ and boundary conditions)
- $E = 2.06 \times 10^5$ **MPa** (Young's modulus for shipbuilding steel)
- $\nu = 0.3$ (Poisson's ratio)
- $t$ = plate thickness (mm)
- $s$ = stiffener spacing (mm)

**Step 3 — Fatigue Check (Palmgren-Miner Rule)**
$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i} \leq D_{crit}$$

where $n_i$ = number of cycles at stress range $\Delta\sigma_i$, $N_i$ = cycles to failure at that range from the S-N curve, and $D_{crit}$ = **1.0** (Miner's sum, dimensionless damage).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why three checks?** Because a plate can yield, buckle, AND fatigue independently. The lowest margin among the three governs the design. KTU examiners often present a scenario asking which mode governs — practice the linkage.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Table Note:** Vertical bars (absolute value) are written as `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table parser.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bending Stress | $\sigma = M / Z$ | MPa | Hull girder check |
| 2 | Shear Stress | $\tau = F / A_{web}$ | MPa | Web panel check |
| 3 | Buckling Stress | $\sigma_{cr} = k_c \cdot \frac{\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \cdot (t/s)^2$ | MPa | Plate panel |
| 4 | Euler Column Buckling | $\sigma_E = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}$ | MPa | Stiffener column |
| 5 | Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda = L_{eff} / r$ | dimensionless | Column check |
| 6 | Miner's Damage Rule | $\sum n_i / N_i \leq 1.0$ | dimensionless | Fatigue life |
| 7 | Basquin's Law (S-N) | $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N)^b$ | MPa | Fatigue curve |
| 8 | Corrosion Allowance | $t_{as-built} = t_{req} + t_{corr}$ | mm | Plate scantling |
| 9 | Net Thickness | $t_{net} = t_{as-built} - t_{corr}$ | mm | Renewal survey |
| 10 | Renewal Criterion | $t_{renewal} = t_{as-built} \cdot 0.75$ | mm | IACS UR-Z |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in Real-World Shipyards

- **Yard Design Phase**: Scantlings (plate thicknesses) are determined by these formulas before steel is cut.
- **Operational Phase**: Plate thickness gauges verify the formulas remain valid.
- **Repair Phase**: Wasted plate regions are cropped based on minimum renewal criteria.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Hull Girder Section Modulus

We derive the section modulus requirement for a bulk carrier hull girder under combined still-water and wave bending.

**Given Data (Standard KTU Problem Setup):**
- Length between perpendiculars: $L_{BP} = 150$ m
- Breadth: $B = 25$ m
- Design draft: $T = 10$ m
- Block coefficient: $C_B = 0.75$
- Wave bending moment coefficient (hogging): $C_w = 0.11$
- Material: Mild steel, $\sigma_{perm} = 175 / 1.0 = 175$ **MPa**
- Rule safety factor for section modulus: **1.0** (CSR-BC)

**Step 1 — Still Water Bending Moment (SWBM) Hogging**

The rule value for hogging SWBM at midships is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{sw,hog} &= C_w \cdot L_{BP}^2 \cdot B \cdot C_B \\
&= 0.11 \times 150^2 \times 25 \times 0.75 \\
&= 0.11 \times 22500 \times 25 \times 0.75 \\
&= 0.11 \times 421875 \\
&= 46406.25 \ \text{kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The wave bending moment coefficient $C_w$ is an empirical IACS factor multiplying the displacement volume to produce the worst-case hogging moment. We square $L_{BP}$ because wave-induced moments grow with the square of length (longer ships bend more).

**Step 2 — Wave Bending Moment Amplitude (Vertical)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{wv} &= 0.11 \cdot L_{BP}^2 \cdot B \cdot C_B \\
&= 46406.25 \ \text{kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

(For the standard IACS wave, hogging and sagging are symmetric in amplitude.)

**Step 3 — Total Design Bending Moment**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{total} &= M_{sw,hog} + M_{wv} \\
&= 46406.25 + 46406.25 \\
&= 92812.5 \ \text{kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Adding the still-water and wave components gives the worst expected moment. Although in reality they are partially phase-shifted, the **rule-based design adds them linearly** as a conservative envelope.

**Step 4 — Required Section Modulus**

The section modulus needed at midships (deck, keel) to keep stress within allowable:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{req} &= \frac{M_{total} \cdot 10^3}{\sigma_{perm}} \\
&= \frac{92812.5 \times 10^3}{175} \\
&= \frac{92812500}{175} \\
&= 530357.14 \ \text{cm}^3 \\
&\approx 5.30 \ \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** We convert kN·m to N·mm by multiplying by $10^6$, divide by MPa (N/mm²), yielding the result in mm³. Converting mm³ to m³: divide by $10^9$.

**Final Result:**
$$
\boxed{Z_{req} \approx 5.30 \ \text{m}^3}
$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Fatigue Damage (Miner's Rule)

A critical detail in the side shell experiences the following stress range spectrum over one year:

| Bin $i$ | $\Delta\sigma_i$ (MPa) | $n_i$ (cycles/yr) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 60 | 5,000 |
| 2 | 80 | 2,000 |
| 3 | 100 | 800 |
| 4 | 120 | 200 |

The S-N curve (in air, Class B weld detail, IACS UR-S Appendix) is:
$$N = 10^{12.67 - 3 \log_{10}(\Delta\sigma)} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \Delta\sigma = (10^{12.67}/N)^{1/3}$$

**Step 1 — Compute $N_i$ for each bin**

For Bin 1: $\Delta\sigma_1 = 60$ MPa
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_1 &= 10^{12.67} / (60)^3 \\
&= 4.68 \times 10^{12} / 216000 \\
&= 2.167 \times 10^7 \ \text{cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

For Bin 2: $\Delta\sigma_2 = 80$ MPa
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_2 &= 4.68 \times 10^{12} / 512000 \\
&= 9.14 \times 10^6 \ \text{cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

For Bin 3: $\Delta\sigma_3 = 100$ MPa
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_3 &= 4.68 \times 10^{12} / 1000000 \\
&= 4.68 \times 10^6 \ \text{cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

For Bin 4: $\Delta\sigma_4 = 120$ MPa
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_4 &= 4.68 \times 10^{12} / 1728000 \\
&= 2.708 \times 10^6 \ \text{cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Damage Per Year**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{year} &= \sum \frac{n_i}{N_i} \\
&= \frac{5000}{2.167 \times 10^7} + \frac{2000}{9.14 \times 10^6} + \frac{800}{4.68 \times 10^6} + \frac{200}{2.708 \times 10^6} \\
&= 0.000231 + 0.000219 + 0.000171 + 0.0000738 \\
&= 0.000695 \ \text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Service Life**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Life} &= \frac{D_{crit}}{D_{year}} = \frac{1.0}{0.000695} \\
&= 1438.8 \ \text{years}
\end{aligned}
$$

This indicates the detail is **safe** for the design service life of 25 years (damage accumulated = **0.0174**, only 1.74% of the critical 1.0 value).

**Final Result:**
$$
\boxed{D_{25 \text{ yr}} = 25 \times 0.000695 = 0.0174 \ \leq 1.0 \quad \text{(Detail is SAFE)}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the 3-slope S-N curve?** Because fatigue resistance at very high cycle counts (low stress) transitions to a different slope ($m = 5$) below the **constant amplitude fatigue limit at $\Delta\sigma_D$** (typically 53 MPa for Class B in air). KTU examiners may include a **knee-point** check.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Corrosion Renewal Threshold

IACS UR-Z10.1 specifies that plate renewal is required when the measured thickness falls below:

$$t_{renewal} = 0.75 \cdot t_{as-built}$$

For a deck plate with:
- $t_{as-built} = 22$ mm
- Corrosion allowance $t_{corr} = 2$ mm
- Measured thickness $t_{meas} = 17$ mm

**Step 1 — Renewal Threshold**
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{renewal} &= 0.75 \times 22 = 16.5 \ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Wastage Fraction**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Wastage} &= \frac{t_{as-built} - t_{meas}}{t_{as-built}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{22 - 17}{22} \times 100\% \\
&= 22.73\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Decision**
Since $t_{meas} = 17$ mm $> t_{renewal} = 16.5$ mm → **Plate ACCEPTABLE** but flagged for closer monitoring.
If $t_{meas}$ had been 16.4 mm → **Plate must be renewed** by inserting a plate insert.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
Define **structural integrity** of a ship hull. List the four primary failure modes that govern its assessment.

**Model Answer:**
Structural integrity is the ability of the hull girder and its components to sustain all expected operational and environmental loads without exceeding allowable limits for yielding, buckling, fatigue, or corrosion-induced loss of material.

The four primary failure modes are:
1. **Yielding** — exceeding material yield stress under bending.
2. **Buckling** — local or overall instability of plates/columns.
3. **Fatigue** — cumulative cyclic damage leading to crack initiation.
4. **Corrosion** — progressive loss of effective thickness reducing load capacity.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
State **Palmgren-Miner's rule** and explain its significance in fatigue assessment of ship structures.

**Model Answer:**
Palmgren-Miner's rule states that fatigue failure occurs when the cumulative damage index reaches unity:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i} \leq 1.0$$

where $n_i$ is the number of stress cycles experienced at a given stress range $\Delta\sigma_i$, and $N_i$ is the number of cycles to failure at that range as determined from the S-N curve.

**Significance:** It allows the conversion of an irregular wave-induced stress spectrum into a single scalar damage value, enabling life prediction over 20–25 years of service. KTU and IACS UR-S13 mandates this check for all critical details like hatch corners, side shell knuckles, and bilge struts.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **classification society survey cycle** for a merchant vessel. Differentiate between Annual Survey, Intermediate Survey, and Special Survey. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Answer:**

The classification society survey cycle is a structured, **age-based inspection regime** prescribed by IACS Unified Requirements (UR-Z series) to verify continuing structural integrity.

**1. Annual Survey (Every Year):**
- Conducted annually between regular surveys.
- Visual examination of hull, machinery, and safety equipment.
- Verification of operating conditions, no-load testing of essential systems.
- Approximately **1–2 days** in port.

**2. Intermediate Survey (At 2.5–3 Years):**
- Replaces one of the Annual Surveys at approximately the 2nd or 3rd year.
- More detailed than Annual — examines compartments, boilers, electrical systems.
- Thickness gauging of **suspect areas** identified during the Annual.
- Approximately **2–4 days** depending on vessel size.

**3. Special Survey (Every 5 Years):**
- The most comprehensive survey, similar to a complete renewal survey.
- Vessel must be **dry-docked** for underwater hull examination.
- Full **thickness measurement (TM)** of all critical structural members using ultrasonic gauges.
- Internal examination of tanks, holds, and peak spaces.
- Substantial steel renewals if wastage exceeds renewal criteria ($t_{meas} < 0.75 \cdot t_{as-built}$).
- Duration: **1–2 weeks** including drydock.

**Comparison Table:**

| Survey | Frequency | Scope | Drydock? | TM? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Annual | Yearly | Visual + operational | No | Spot |
| Intermediate | ~2.5 yr | Detailed + selected tanks | Sometimes | Selected |
| Special | Every 5 yr | Full comprehensive | **Mandatory** | **Full** |

---

**(b)** A bulk carrier has a deck plate of original thickness **22 mm** with corrosion allowance **2 mm**. During a Special Survey, thickness measurement at the worst-corrosion location yields **15.8 mm**. Apply the **IACS renewal criterion** and determine the decision. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Renewal Threshold (IACS UR-Z10.1):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{renewal} &= 0.75 \cdot t_{as-built} \\
&= 0.75 \times 22 \\
&= 16.5 \ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare Measured Thickness:**
$$
t_{meas} = 15.8 \ \text{mm} \quad \text{vs.} \quad t_{renewal} = 16.5 \ \text{mm}
$$

Since $15.8 < 16.5$, the plate **does not satisfy** the renewal criterion.

**Step 3 — Wastage Percentage:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Wastage} &= \frac{22 - 15.8}{22} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{6.2}{22} \times 100\% \\
&= 28.18\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**
The plate **MUST BE RENEWED**. In practice, the surveyor will:
1. Mark the corroded area for cropping.
2. Insert a new plate of 22 mm thickness (or the rule-required minimum 18 mm after considering wastage).
3. Weld using approved WPS (Welding Procedure Specification).
4. Conduct **NDT (typically UT + MT)** on the new weld joint.
5. Update the vessel's **TM records** in the Classification database.

**Final Result:**
$$
\boxed{\text{Plate RENEWAL REQUIRED — insert a new 22 mm plate or minimum 18 mm insert}}
$$

---

#### QUESTION B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Describe the **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) methods** used for ship structural inspection. Compare **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** and **Radiographic Testing (RT)** with respect to defect type, application, and limitations. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Answer:**

NDT methods are techniques used to evaluate material condition **without impairing future usefulness** of the structure. They are mandatory during Special Surveys and after any significant repair work.

**Major NDT Methods:**

| Method | Principle | Detects | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **VT (Visual)** | Direct eye + magnifier | Surface cracks, dents | All surveys |
| **MT (Magnetic Particle)** | Magnetic flux leakage at defects | Surface + near-surface cracks | Welded joints (ferrous only) |
| **PT (Dye Penetrant)** | Capillary action of dye | Surface-breaking cracks | Non-ferrous alloys, welds |
| **UT (Ultrasonic Testing)** | High-freq. sound wave reflection | Subsurface, internal defects | Plate thickness, lamination |
| **RT (Radiographic Testing)** | X-ray / Gamma-ray absorption | Volumetric internal defects | Butt weld integrity |
| **ET (Eddy Current)** | Electromagnetic induction | Surface + sub-surface cracks | Heat exchanger tubes |

**UT vs RT — Detailed Comparison:**

| Parameter | Ultrasonic Testing (UT) | Radiographic Testing (RT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Defect Type Detected** | Subsurface, planar, lamination, delamination | Volumetric (porosity, slag, root gap) |
| **Best For** | Thickness gauging, weld inspection | Butt-weld volumetric integrity |
| **Sensitivity to Crack Orientation** | High (perpendicular to beam) | Independent of orientation |
| **Speed** | Fast, real-time | Slow, requires film processing |
| **Hazard** | None (ultrasonic waves are harmless) | **Radiation hazard — requires controlled zone** |
| **Cost** | Lower (portable, reusable probes) | Higher (film, darkroom, certified operators) |
| **Limitation** | Requires skilled interpreter; coupling medium | Two-sided access; safety protocols |

**Conclusion:** UT is preferred for **routine thickness gauging and crack depth measurement** during surveys. RT is reserved for **critical new-build weld joints** where volumetric integrity must be proven. Both are complementary, not substitutes.

---

**(b)** A side shell plate is subjected to the following annual stress-range spectrum. Using the **S-N curve** $N \cdot \Delta\sigma^3 = 4.68 \times 10^{12}$ (IACS Class B detail in air), determine the **fatigue life** in years.

| Bin | $\Delta\sigma_i$ (MPa) | $n_i$ (cycles/year) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | 10000 |
| 2 | 70 | 5000 |
| 3 | 90 | 1500 |
| 4 | 110 | 400 |

**\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $N_i$ for each bin using $N_i = 4.68 \times 10^{12} / \Delta\sigma^3$**

For Bin 1: $\Delta\sigma = 50$ MPa
$$
N_1 = \frac{4.68 \times 10^{12}}{125000} = 3.744 \times 10^7 \ \text{cycles}
$$

For Bin 2: $\Delta\sigma = 70$ MPa
$$
N_2 = \frac{4.68 \times 10^{12}}{343000} = 1.364 \times 10^7 \ \text{cycles}
$$

For Bin 3: $\Delta\sigma = 90$ MPa
$$
N_3 = \frac{4.68 \times 10^{12}}{729000} = 6.42 \times 10^6 \ \text{cycles}
$$

For Bin 4: $\Delta\sigma = 110$ MPa
$$
N_4 = \frac{4.68 \times 10^{12}}{1331000} = 3.516 \times 10^6 \ \text{cycles}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Damage per Year**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{year} &= \sum \frac{n_i}{N_i} \\
&= \frac{10000}{3.744 \times 10^7} + \frac{5000}{1.364 \times 10^7} + \frac{1500}{6.42 \times 10^6} + \frac{400}{3.516 \times 10^6} \\
&= 0.000267 + 0.000366 + 0.000234 + 0.000114 \\
&= 0.000981 \ \text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Service Life**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Life} &= \frac{D_{crit}}{D_{year}} = \frac{1.0}{0.000981} \\
&= 1019 \ \text{years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Design Verification**

For 25-year design life:
$$
D_{25} = 25 \times 0.000981 = 0.0245 \leq 1.0 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Final Result:**
$$
\boxed{\text{Fatigue life} \approx 1019 \ \text{years} \gg 25 \ \text{years} \rightarrow \text{Detail is SAFE}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "knee point"**: For $\Delta\sigma < 53$ MPa, the S-N curve slope changes from 3 to 5 (or sometimes 22 — infinite life). If Bin 1 stress is below the knee, use $N \to \infty$ and skip that bin from damage summation.
> 2. **Wrong S-N constant**: Don't mix Class B ($4.68 \times 10^{12}$) with Class D ($1.52 \times 10^{12}$) — exam questions specify the class.
> 3. **Unit errors**: The constant $4.68 \times 10^{12}$ is dimensionless for $\Delta\sigma$ in **MPa** when $m=3$. If $\Delta\sigma$ is in kPa or Pa, the constant changes — read the problem carefully.
> 4. **Adding components**: Don't add stresses from different load cases; use **stress ranges** $\Delta\sigma = \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}$ from a single load cycle.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- [ ] Structural integrity = **strength + fatigue + buckling + corrosion**.
- [ ] Hull girder treated as **box beam** with $Z = I_{NA} / y$.
- [ ] Three checks per element: **yielding → buckling → fatigue**.
- [ ] **IACS UR-Z10.1** renewal criterion: $t_{renewal} = 0.75 \cdot t_{as-built}$.
- [ ] **Miner's rule**: $D = \sum (n_i / N_i) \leq 1.0$.
- [ ] **S-N slope**: $m = 3$ for high-cycle, $m = 5$ (or knee) below the constant amplitude fatigue limit.
- [ ] NDT hierarchy: **VT → UT/MT/PT → RT** (escalating cost & complexity).
- [ ] Survey cycle: **Annual → Intermediate (2.5y) → Special (5y)**.
- [ ] Special Survey **requires dry-docking** and full TM.
- [ ] **SOLAS Chapter II-1** governs subdivision and structural strength regulations.
- [ ] **CSR-BC / CSR-OT** (Common Structural Rules) for bulk carriers and oil tankers.
- [ ] Corrosion allowance typically **1–3 mm** per side depending on compartment.
- [ ] **Young's modulus for shipbuilding steel**: $E = 2.06 \times 10^5$ MPa.
- [ ] **Poisson's ratio**: $\nu = 0.3$ for steel.
- [ ] Plate renewal uses **insert plates**, not patch welding over corroded area.
- [ ] All welds undergo **WPS + WPQR** qualification per **AWS D1.1** or **ISO 3834**.
- [ ] **Fatigue hot spots**: hatch corners, bilge struts, side shell knuckles, engine seatings.
- [ ] Renewal thickness decision is **absolute** — no engineering judgement permitted below threshold.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Marine Pollution and Environmental Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Marine Pollution and Environmental Protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MARINE POLLUTION (IMO Definition):** The introduction by humans, directly or indirectly, of substances or energy into the marine environment (including estuaries) resulting in such deleterious effects as harm to living resources, hazards to human health, hindrance to marine activities (including fishing), impairment of the quality for use of sea water, and reduction of amenities.

According to **UNCLOS (United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea), Article 1(1)(4)**, marine pollution is defined as:

> *"The introduction by man, directly or indirectly, of substances or energy into the marine environment resulting in such deleterious effects as harm to living resources and marine life, hazards to human health, hindrance to marine activities, including fishing and other legitimate uses of the sea, impairment of quality for use of sea water and reduction of amenities."*

The principal international instrument regulating marine pollution from ships is the **MARPOL Convention (International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships), 1973/78**, administered by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Ocean as a Giant Bathtub**

Imagine the world's oceans as a **giant bathtub** that is shared by all of humanity. Now think about what happens if everyone starts pouring waste, oil, chemicals, and plastic into that same bathtub without any rules:

- **No Drainage:** The ocean has no "exit valve" — pollutants can remain for decades or even centuries.
- **Shared Resource:** Just like a shared bathroom, one person's carelessness (an oil spill, plastic dumping) affects everyone else's experience.
- **Bioaccumulation:** Imagine food coloring dropped into the bathtub — even a tiny amount spreads everywhere. Heavy metals and microplastics behave the same way, climbing up the food chain.
- **Self-Purification Limit:** Oceans can naturally absorb some pollution (carbon cycle, dilution), but there is a **tipping point** — beyond which recovery becomes impossible.

**Engineering Perspective:** A marine engineer must treat the ocean like a **finite, shared engineering system** — one with limited assimilative capacity, requiring strict input-output regulations, just like a closed-loop cooling system on a ship.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants in Marine Pollution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Quantitative Metrics (Bolded for KTU Reference):**
> - **Source:** Approximately **80%** of marine pollution originates from **land-based activities**, while **20%** comes from sea-based sources (shipping, offshore platforms).
> - **Oil Input:** An estimated **1.3 million tonnes** of oil enters the marine environment annually from shipping-related sources.
> - **Plastic:** About **8 million metric tonnes** of plastic enter the ocean every year (UN Environment, 2021).
> - **Bunker Fuel Sulfur Cap (MARPOL Annex VI):** Reduced globally from **3.50% m/m** to **0.50% m/m** effective **1 January 2020** (IMO 2020).
> - **ECA Sulfur Limit:** **0.10% m/m** in Emission Control Areas.
> - **Plastic Ban:** Single-use plastics banned on board ships under **MARPOL Annex V** (regional/national enforcement varies).

---

## 1.4 Classification of Marine Pollutants

| Category | Examples | Primary Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Petroleum Hydrocarbons** | Crude oil, diesel, lube oil, bilge water | Tankers, machinery spaces |
| **Chemical Pollutants** | Noxious Liquid Substances (NLS), pesticides, heavy metals | Chemical tankers, industrial runoff |
| **Plastic & Solid Waste** | Fishing nets, packaging, microplastics | Ships, coastal population |
| **Sewage & Grey Water** | Human waste, wash water | Crew vessels, cruise ships |
| **Air Pollutants** | SOx, NOx, CO₂, black carbon | Main engines, boilers |
| **Noise Pollution** | Sonar, propeller cavitation, seismic surveys | Naval and commercial vessels |
| **Ballast Water Organisms** | Invasive species, pathogens | Global shipping traffic |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Marine Pollution (Structured Logic)

### 2.1.1 Land-Based Sources (~80%)
1. **Industrial Effluents** — heavy metals (Hg, Pb, Cd), PCBs, dioxins from coastal factories.
2. **Municipal Sewage** — untreated domestic wastewater carrying pathogens and nutrients.
3. **Agricultural Runoff** — fertilizers causing **eutrophication** and pesticides.
4. **Riverine Discharge** — rivers act as natural conduits transporting land pollutants to the sea.
5. **Atmospheric Deposition** — airborne particulates and acid rain precursors.

### 2.1.2 Sea-Based Sources (~20%)
1. **Operational Discharges from Ships:**
   - Bilge water containing oil
   - Ballast water (invasive species transport)
   - Sewage and grey water
   - Garbage and plastic
   - Cargo residues (noxious liquid substances)
2. **Accidental Spills:** Tanker collisions, groundings, blowouts.
3. **Offshore Platforms:** Drilling fluids, produced water.
4. **Dumping:** Dredged material, industrial waste (regulated under **London Convention 1972**).
5. **Vessel Emissions:** SOx, NOx, GHG from propulsion.

---

## 2.2 MARPOL Convention — The Six Annexes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **MARPOL Convention** is the main international convention covering the prevention of pollution of the marine environment by ships from operational or accidental causes. It is a combination of two treaties: the **1973 Convention** and the **1978 Protocol**, updated by amendments.

### Annex-by-Annex Breakdown:

| Annex | Subject | Coverage | Key Requirements |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Annex I** | Oil | Machinery spaces, cargo areas | **Oily Water Separator (15 ppm)**; IOPP Certificate; SOPEP |
| **Annex II** | Noxious Liquid Substances (NLS) in bulk | Chemical tankers | **Categories X, Y, Z, OS**; prewash procedures |
| **Annex III** | Harmful Substances in Packaged Form | IMDG Code integration | Marking, labelling, documentation |
| **Annex IV** | Sewage | Passenger ships, all vessels | **Sewage Treatment Plant (STP)** or holding tank |
| **Annex V** | Garbage | All vessels | **Plastics banned entirely**; sorting & recording |
| **Annex VI** | Air Pollution | Main/auxiliary engines | **Sulfur cap 0.50%**, NOx Tier I/II/III, EEDI |

---

## 2.3 Oil Pollution Physics & Fate of Oil Spill

When oil enters the marine environment, it undergoes a series of physical and chemical changes known as **"weathering"**:

1. **Spreading** — Gravity-driven horizontal expansion.
2. **Evaporation** — Loss of volatile fractions (light ends).
3. **Emulsification** — Water-in-oil mousse formation (up to 80% water content).
4. **Dissolution** — Water-soluble components dissolve.
5. **Oxidation** — Photochemical reaction with oxygen.
6. **Sedimentation** — Oil-coated particles sink.
7. **Biodegradation** — Microbial breakdown into CO₂ and water.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — KTU Board Examination Reference**

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{eff} = \dfrac{V_o}{V_d} \times C_o$ | Bilge OWS efficiency: $C_{eff}$ is effluent conc., $V_o$ oil vol., $V_d$ discharge vol., $C_o$ initial conc. | ppm |
| 2 | $F_{SO_2} = 10 \times S \times m_{fuel}$ | SO₂ emission factor per tonne fuel, where $S$ is sulfur content (% mass). | g SO₂/tonne fuel |
| 3 | $EEDI = \dfrac{CO_2_{ref}}{DWT \times V_{ref}}$ | Energy Efficiency Design Index baseline reference. | g CO₂/tonne·nm |
| 4 | $C_{oil} \leq 15 \, ppm$ | IMO discharge limit from OWS under MARPOL Annex I Reg. 14. | ppm |
| 5 | $BWR = \dfrac{V_{BW}}{DWT}$ | Ballast Water Ratio for management planning. | dimensionless |
| 6 | $S_{cap} = 0.50\% \, m/m$ (Global) | MARPOL Annex VI sulfur cap from 2020. | % m/m |
| 7 | $S_{ECA} = 0.10\% \, m/m$ | ECA sulfur limit (Baltic, North Sea, N. America ECA). | % m/m |
| 8 | $NO_x \, Tier \, III \Rightarrow -80\%$ | NOx reduction vs. Tier I baseline in ECAs. | % reduction |

> **Note on Formula 6 & 7:** The symbol $\vert$ or $\mid$ is used in exam scripts for absolute value; here we use it safely outside the table.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ship Design:** Influences selection of OWS, STP, Incinerators, and Exhaust Gas Cleaning Systems (EGCS / Scrubbers).
- **Port State Control:** Surveyors verify IOPP, ISPP, IAPP certificates.
- **Cargo Operations:** Tank Cleaning and Crude Oil Washing (COW) systems directly affect Annex I/II compliance.
- **Route Planning:** Voyage planning software integrates ECA zones (e.g., North Sea ECA, Baltic Sea ECA, North American ECA, Caribbean ECA — effective 2025).
- **Accident Response:** SOPEP (Shipboard Oil Pollution Emergency Plan) under Annex I Reg. 37 is mandatory for all oil tankers > 150 GT and non-tanker ships > 400 GT.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: SO₂ Emission from Marine Fuel

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A container ship burns **1,200 tonnes** of HFO per voyage in a non-ECA region. Fuel sulfur content is **2.8% m/m**. Calculate the SO₂ emissions generated, given that 1 tonne of sulfur produces **2 tonnes of SO₂** stoichiometrically ($S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2$, molar mass ratio = 64/32 = 2).

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify given parameters:**
- Mass of fuel burnt, $m_f = 1200$ tonnes
- Sulfur content, $S = 2.8\% \, m/m = 0.028$
- Stoichiometric factor, $k = 2$ (mass of SO₂ per unit mass of S)

**Step 2 — Compute mass of sulfur in fuel:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
m_S &= m_f \times S \\
m_S &= 1200 \times 0.028 \\
m_S &= 33.6 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert sulfur mass to SO₂ mass using stoichiometry:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{SO_2} &= m_S \times k \\
m_{SO_2} &= 33.6 \times 2 \\
m_{SO_2} &= 67.2 \text{ tonnes of SO}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verdict against MARPOL Annex VI:**
- Permitted limit = **0.50% m/m** globally.
- Required $m_S = 1200 \times 0.005 = 6$ tonnes → $m_{SO_2} = 12$ tonnes max.
- The ship emits **5.6 times** the permitted SO₂ mass.

**Conclusion:** The vessel is **non-compliant** in non-ECA waters under 2020+ rules and must either:
1. Switch to **VLSFO (0.50% S)**, or
2. Install an **EGCS scrubber** (hybrid/open-loop/closed-loop).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: OWS Discharge Concentration Compliance

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** An OWS processes bilge water at **20 m³/h** with an inlet oil concentration of **500 ppm**. The unit claims a separation efficiency of **99%**. Verify compliance with MARPOL Annex I Reg. 14 (limit = 15 ppm).

**Step 1 — Define efficiency:**
$$
\eta = \left(1 - \dfrac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}\right) \times 100\%
$$

**Step 2 — Express outlet concentration:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{out} &= C_{in} \times (1 - \eta) \\
C_{out} &= 500 \times (1 - 0.99) \\
C_{out} &= 500 \times 0.01 \\
C_{out} &= 5 \text{ ppm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with regulation:**
$$
C_{out} = 5 \text{ ppm} \;<\; 15 \text{ ppm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{COMPLIANT}
$$

**Step 4 — Operational safety margin:**
$$
\text{Margin} = \dfrac{15 - 5}{15} \times 100\% = 66.7\%
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Real OWS performance degrades with emulsified oil, surfactant contamination, and temperature drops. Always maintain a **safety margin** and monitor with the **Oil Content Monitor (OCM)**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: MARPOL Annex V Garbage Categories

| Category | Description | Discharge Rule Outside Special Areas |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Plastics, synthetic ropes, fishing nets, plastic bags | **PROHIBITED ALWAYS** |
| **B** | Dunnage, lining, packing materials | ≥ 25 nautical miles from nearest land |
| **C** | Paper, rags, glass, metal, bottles, crockery | ≥ 12 nautical miles from nearest land |
| **D** | Food waste, comminuted/ground | ≥ 12 nautical miles (≥ 3 nm if comminuted to < 25 mm) |
| **E** | Cargo residues (non-HME) | As per Annex V Reg. 4–6 |

**Special Areas** (stricter rules): Mediterranean, Baltic, Black, Red, Gulf, North Sea, Antarctic, Caribbean (entered into force 2023).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

### **Q1. [3 Marks]** Define Marine Pollution as per UNCLOS Article 1(1)(4).

**Model Answer:**
Marine pollution, according to **UNCLOS Article 1(1)(4)**, is the introduction by humans, directly or indirectly, of substances or energy into the marine environment (including estuaries) which results in deleterious effects such as harm to living resources and marine life, hazards to human health, hindrance to marine activities including fishing, impairment of sea water quality, and reduction of amenities. *(3 marks: 1 mark for "substance/energy input," 1 mark for marine environment, 1 mark for deleterious effects)*

---

### **Q2. [3 Marks]** List any three Annexes of the MARPOL Convention with their subjects.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Annex I** – Prevention of pollution by **oil** from machinery spaces and cargo areas. *(1 mark)*
2. **Annex IV** – Prevention of pollution by **sewage** from ships. *(1 mark)*
3. **Annex VI** – Prevention of **air pollution** from ships (SOx, NOx, CO₂). *(1 mark)*

*(Equivalent valid answers include Annexes II, III, V)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly lose marks by:
> - Writing only the **name of the annex** without explaining the **engineering mechanism** (e.g., OWS, STP, SOPEP).
> - Failing to quote **specific numerical limits** (e.g., 15 ppm, 0.50% S, 12 nm).
> - Ignoring **Special Areas** under Annex V.
> - Not mentioning **certificates** (IOPP, IAPP, ISPP, IEE).
> - Skipping the **CO/Bloom's Level** keywords expected in 14-mark answers (e.g., "analyze," "evaluate," "design").

---

### **Question A [14 Marks]:** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the **MARPOL Convention** and its **six Annexes** in detail, highlighting the pollution category each Annex addresses and the **shipboard equipment/certificates** required for compliance. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **operation of an Oily Water Separator (OWS)**. Explain how the **15 ppm** discharge limit under MARPOL Annex I Reg. 14 is ensured on board. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Answer A:**

**(a) MARPOL Convention & Annexes (7 marks):**

The **International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships (MARPOL)** is the principal IMO treaty regulating operational and accidental pollution from ships. Originally adopted in **1973** and modified by the **1978 Protocol**, it entered into force on **2 November 1983**. *(1 mark)*

- **Annex I – Oil (Reg. 14):** Applies to all ships; limits bilge discharge to **15 ppm** oil-in-water via OWS; requires **IOPP Certificate** and **SOPEP** for tankers > 150 GT and other ships > 400 GT. *(1 mark)*
- **Annex II – Noxious Liquid Substances:** Categorizes bulk chemicals as **X, Y, Z, OS** with progressively relaxed discharge rules; prewash mandatory for Category X and Y. *(1 mark)*
- **Annex III – Packaged Harmful Substances:** Adopts the **IMDG Code**; mandates marking, labelling, and documentation. *(0.5 mark)*
- **Annex IV – Sewage:** Requires **Sewage Treatment Plant (STP)** or holding tank; sets discharge standards in **Special Areas** (Baltic Sea). *(1 mark)*
- **Annex V – Garbage:** **Plastics banned entirely**; food waste and dunnage discharged only beyond prescribed distances; **Garbage Record Book** and **Garbage Management Plan** mandatory. *(1 mark)*
- **Annex VI – Air Pollution:** Sulfur cap **0.50% m/m** (0.10% in ECAs), NOx Tier I/II/III, **EEDI** for new ships, **SEEMP** for operational efficiency. *(1.5 marks)*

**(b) OWS Operation & 15 ppm Compliance (7 marks):**

The **Oily Water Separator** is a gravity-based or coalescing-type separator that exploits the **density difference** between oil (ρ ≈ 850–900 kg/m³) and water (ρ ≈ 1025 kg/m³). *(1 mark)*

**Operating Principle:**
1. **Inlet** – Bilge water enters the first (coarse) chamber where free oil rises via Stokes' law settling. *(1 mark)*
2. **Coalescer Pack** – Fine oil droplets coalesce on plates (e.g., Corrugated Plate Interceptor, CPI). *(1 mark)*
3. **Second Stage** – Further separation occurs in a polishing chamber. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Oil Content Monitor (OCM)** – A continuous in-line analyzer measures oil concentration. If reading **> 15 ppm**, the **3-way valve** automatically diverts discharge back to the bilge holding tank. *(2 marks)*
5. **Sludge Discharge** – Accumulated oil is transferred to a **sludge tank** for shore disposal via **Incinerator** or **Oily Waste Reception Facility**. *(0.5 mark)*
6. **Compliance Documentation** – Entries made in the **Oil Record Book Part I** (machinery space) per Reg. 17. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Question B [14 Marks]:** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Discuss the **causes and consequences of oil pollution in the marine environment**. Describe the **fate of oil (weathering processes)** after a spill with a suitable diagram. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **Ballast Water Management (BWM) Convention** with the need for ballast water exchange and treatment methods. List the **BWM Certificate requirements**. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Answer B:**

**(a) Oil Pollution – Causes, Consequences & Weathering (7 marks):**

**Causes:** *(1 mark)*
- Operational discharge of bilge water, tank cleaning, ballast water contamination.
- Accidental spills due to tanker collisions (e.g., *Exxon Valdez*, 1989; *Prestige*, 2002; *MV Wakashio*, Mauritius 2020).
- Offshore drilling blowouts (e.g., *Deepwater Horizon*, 2010).

**Consequences:** *(2 marks)*
- **Ecological:** Coating of seabirds, mortality of fish and plankton, destruction of mangroves and coral reefs.
- **Economic:** Loss of fisheries, tourism, port closure, heavy cleanup costs (billions of dollars).
- **Human Health:** Contamination of seafood, respiratory issues from volatile hydrocarbons.
- **Long-term:** Chronic bioaccumulation of PAHs in the food chain.

**Weathering Processes:** *(3 marks — 0.5 each)*
1. **Spreading** – Gravity-driven expansion.
2. **Evaporation** – Loss of light volatile fractions (first 24 hours).
3. **Emulsification** – "Chocolate mousse" formation (water-in-oil).
4. **Dissolution** – Water-soluble components dissolve.
5. **Oxidation** – Photochemical breakdown.
6. **Sedimentation** – Oil-coated particles settle.
7. **Biodegradation** – Bacterial breakdown into CO₂ + H₂O.

---

**(b) BWM Convention (7 marks):**

**Need:** Ballast water is taken in by ships at one port and discharged at another to maintain stability. This introduces **invasive aquatic species** (e.g., Zebra mussels in the Great Lakes, *Mnemiopsis leidyi* in the Black Sea) that can devastate local ecosystems. *(1 mark)*

**BWM Convention (2004):** Adopted by IMO; entered into force **8 September 2017**. *(0.5 mark)*

**Management Methods:** *(3 marks)*
1. **Ballast Water Exchange (BWE):** Exchange at sea using the **D-1 Standard** — 95% volumetric exchange. Done at least **200 nm from nearest land** in > 200 m depth.
2. **Ballast Water Treatment (D-2 Standard):** Onboard treatment meeting organism discharge limits:
   - **< 10 viable organisms/m³** for ≥ 50 μm.
   - **< 10 viable organisms/ml** for < 50 μm and ≥ 10 μm.
   - **Indicator microbes** below specified CFU thresholds.
3. **Treatment Technologies:** Filtration, UV irradiation, chemical biocides (e.g., **DBNPA**, chlorine, ozone), deoxygenation.

**BWM Certificate Requirements:** *(2.5 marks)*
- **International Ballast Water Management Certificate** (for ships ≥ 400 GT engaged in international voyages).
- **Ballast Water Management Plan (BWMP)** approved by Administration.
- **Ballast Water Record Book** for every voyage.
- **Type Approval** of BWMS by the Administration (per IMO G8 Guidelines).
- Survey and certification by **RO (Recognized Organization)**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Board Prep**

- 🔹 **MARPOL = 6 Annexes**; remember them in order: **O, N, P, S, G, A** (Oil, Noxious, Packaged, Sewage, Garbage, Air).
- 🔹 **15 ppm** is the magic number for OWS (Annex I, Reg. 14).
- 🔹 **0.50% m/m** global sulfur cap (Annex VI) since **1 Jan 2020**; **0.10%** in ECAs.
- 🔹 **Plastics = NEVER** — banned under Annex V regardless of distance.
- 🔹 **Garbage zones:** ≥ 3, 12, 25 nm depending on category (Annex V).
- 🔹 **80/20 rule:** 80% land-based, 20% sea-based pollution.
- 🔹 **BWM Convention** entered into force **8 Sept 2017** — D-1 (exchange) or D-2 (treatment).
- 🔹 **Certificates:** IOPP (Oil), ISPP (NLS), IAPP (Air), ISPP, IEE, BWM.
- 🔹 **SOPEP** mandatory for all ships > 400 GT (non-tanker) and tankers > 150 GT.
- 🔹 **OPRC Convention (1990)** + **OPRC-HNS Protocol (2000)** for spill response preparedness.
- 🔹 **Special Areas (Annex V):** Mediterranean, Baltic, Black, Red, Gulf, North Sea, Antarctic, Caribbean.
- 🔹 **NOx Tier III** applies only in **ECAs** for ships built on/after **1 Jan 2016**.
- 🔹 **EEDI** for new ships; **SEEMP** for all ships (operational efficiency).
- 🔹 **1 tonne of S → 2 tonnes of SO₂** (stoichiometric — high-yield exam tip).
- 🔹 **Weathering** order: Spreading → Evaporation → Emulsification → Dissolution → Oxidation → Sedimentation → Biodegradation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Marine Pollution Sources and Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Marine Pollution Sources and Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

**Marine Pollution** is defined under **Article 1(1)(4) of the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), 1982** as:

> *"The introduction by man, directly or indirectly, of substances or energy into the marine environment, including estuaries, which results or is likely to result in such deleterious effects as harm to living resources and marine life, hazards to human health, hindrance to marine activities including fishing and other legitimate uses of the sea, impairment of quality for use of sea water and reduction of amenities."*

The **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** further operationalizes this through the **International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships, 1973, as modified by the Protocol of 1978 (MARPOL 73/78)** — the principal international treaty governing ship-source marine pollution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme places specific weight on the **MARPOL Convention Annexes (I–VI)**, **Land-Based Sources**, **Operational vs. Accidental Discharges**, and **Hazardous Noxious Substances (HNS)**. These are guaranteed high-yield topics in ESE.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Ocean as a Giant Bathtub

Imagine Earth's oceans as a **vast, slowly-flushing bathtub**:

- **The faucet** = Land-based industries, rivers, cities, and ships constantly pouring materials in.
- **The drain** = Natural processes like evaporation, sedimentation, and biological recycling — but the drain is **far too slow** compared to the inflow.
- **The bathtub user** = Marine ecosystems (coral reefs, fish, mammals) which are the ones "bathed" in the accumulating waste.

Now, drop in **oil, plastic, heavy metals, sewage, and chemicals**. The water (ocean) changes color, the bathers (marine life) get sick, and the bather downstream (humans) eventually gets affected through the food chain. This is precisely the systemic problem of marine pollution.

### 1.3 Physical & Regulatory Constants

| Constant / Metric | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Earth's Ocean Coverage** | **~71% (≈ 361 million km²)** | Receives the bulk of anthropogenic waste |
| **Annual Plastic Input to Ocean** | **~8–12 million tonnes** | UNEP estimate, growing trend |
| **MARPOL Entry into Force** | **2 November 1983** | Binding treaty for >98% of world tonnage |
| **MARPOL Annexes** | **6 (I–VI)** | Annex VI added via 1997 Protocol |
| **Land-Based Contribution** | **~80%** | UNEP & IMO joint estimate |
| **Operational vs. Accidental Ratio** | **~70:30** | Operational discharges dominate by volume |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology for KTU:**
> - **Pollutant:** The substance causing pollution (e.g., crude oil, mercury, microplastics).
> - **Contaminant:** An agent that exceeds natural background levels.
> - **HNS (Hazardous and Noxious Substances):** Chemicals carried as cargo that, if released, pose hazards (covered by **OPRC-HNS Protocol, 2000**).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Marine Pollution Sources

Marine pollution is broadly classified into **seven (7) major source categories**. The KTU 2024 syllabus tests all of them, often as 3-mark short notes.

#### **A. Land-Based Sources (≈ 80% of total marine pollution)**

- **Rivers and Estuarine Discharge:** Carries agricultural runoff, industrial effluents, municipal sewage, and plastic debris.
- **Coastal Municipal Discharges:** Untreated or partially treated sewage from coastal cities.
- **Industrial Effluents:** Heavy metals (Hg, Pb, Cd), PCBs, dioxins, furans from chemical plants.
- **Agricultural Runoff:** Fertilizers (N, P compounds) → **eutrophication**; pesticides → bioaccumulation.
- **Aquaculture Discharges:** Antibiotics, antifoulants, uneaten feed, fecal matter.
- **Tourism & Recreational Activities:** Littering, sunscreen chemicals (oxybenzone), sewage from cruise ships near coast.
- **Construction & Coastal Development:** Sediment plumes, dredge spoil.

#### **B. Ship-Based Sources (Operational and Accidental)**

- **Operational Discharges (Intentional):**
  - Bilge water containing oil
  - Ballast water (invasive species transfer)
  - Sewage (gray water, black water)
  - Garbage (plastics, food waste)
  - Tank cleaning residues
  - Exhaust gas emissions (SOx, NOx, CO₂)
- **Accidental Discharges (Unintentional):**
  - Collisions (e.g., *Exxon Valdez*, 1989)
  - Groundings (e.g., *MV Rena*, 2011)
  - Fires and explosions
  - Hull failures
  - Loading/unloading spills at ports

#### **C. Offshore Installations**

- **Oil & Gas Platforms:** Produced water (the largest wastewater stream in offshore O&G), drilling muds, cuttings.
- **Subsea Pipelines:** Chronic leakage, catastrophic rupture.
- **FPSOs (Floating Production Storage & Offloading):** Bilge, storage tank releases.

#### **D. Dumping at Sea (Deliberate)**

- **Dredged Material** (~80% of dumped material by volume)
- **Sewage Sludge**
- **Industrial Waste** (now largely banned by the **London Convention 1972 / London Protocol 1996**)
- **Fish Processing Waste**
- **Vessels and Platforms (deliberate sinking)** — now heavily restricted.

#### **E. Atmospheric Deposition**

- **Airborne particulates** from power plants (coal combustion → mercury fallout).
- **Acid rain precursors** (SO₂, NOx).
- **Pesticide drift** (POPs — Persistent Organic Pollutants).
- **Black carbon** (soot from ships).
- **Microplastic fibres** shed from synthetic clothing.

#### **F. Biological / Biological Warfare Agents**

- **Invasive Species** via ballast water and hull fouling (e.g., *Mnemiopsis leidyi* in Black Sea).
- **Genetically Modified Organisms** (theoretical concern).

#### **G. Noise Pollution (Emerging Concern)**

- **Shipping noise** (low-frequency, ~10–1000 Hz).
- **Seismic airguns** for oil exploration.
- **Sonar systems** (military, e.g., LFA — Low Frequency Active).
- **Pile-driving** for offshore wind farms.
- **Effect:** Marine mammal stranding, communication masking, stress physiology.

### 2.2 Types of Marine Pollutants — Detailed Breakdown

| # | Pollutant Type | Examples | Primary Source | Key Environmental Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Petroleum Hydrocarbons** | Crude oil, fuel oil, lube oil | Tankers, rigs, runoff | Smothering, toxicity, long-term ecosystem damage |
| 2 | **Plastics & Microplastics** | Bags, bottles, fishing nets, microbeads | Land runoff, fishing, ships | Ingestion, entanglement, vector for chemical sorption |
| 3 | **Heavy Metals** | Hg, Pb, Cd, As, Cr | Industry, mining, coal plants | Bioaccumulation, biomagnification, neurological damage |
| 4 | **Nutrients (N, P)** | Nitrates, phosphates | Agriculture, sewage | Eutrophication, algal blooms, hypoxic dead zones |
| 5 | **Sewage & Pathogens** | Coliforms, viruses, organic matter | Municipal, ships | Waterborne diseases, oxygen depletion |
| 6 | **Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs)** | PCBs, DDT, dioxins, PFAS | Industry, pesticides | Bioaccumulation, endocrine disruption, Stockholm Convention |
| 7 | **Radioactive Substances** | Cs-137, Sr-90, tritium | Nuclear plants, reprocessing, weapons | Genetic mutation, long half-life persistence |
| 8 | **Hazardous Noxious Substances (HNS)** | Acids, ammonia, chlorine, solvents | Tanker spills | Toxic plumes, fire/explosion, ecological devastation |
| 9 | **Thermal Pollution** | Heated water from power plants | Coastal power/desalination | Coral bleaching, metabolic stress on marine life |
| 10 | **Underwater Noise** | Propeller cavitation, sonar | Shipping, military | Whale strandings, fish stress |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Engineering Equations

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All absolute values and conditional uses are written with `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

#### **Table 2.3.A — Quantitative Equations for Marine Pollution Analysis**

| # | Equation | Symbol Glossary | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | $C$ = concentration (mg/L), $m$ = mass of pollutant (mg), $V$ = volume (L) | Estimating pollutant load in seawater |
| 2 | $C_{mix} = \dfrac{C_1 V_1 + C_2 V_2}{V_1 + V_2}$ | Conservative mixing | Predicting dilution downstream of an outfall |
| 3 | $C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ | First-order decay; $k$ = decay rate (1/day) | Modeling pollutant decay in water column |
| 4 | $t_{\frac{1}{2}} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}$ | Half-life formula | Persistence estimation of pollutants |
| 5 | $A = \pi \cdot k^2 \cdot t^2$ | **Fay's Spreading Equation (Gravity-Inertia Phase)**; $A$ = oil slick area, $k$ = constant ≈ 150 (SI) | Predicting oil slick expansion |
| 6 | $V_{oil} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot k^3 \cdot t^{3/2}}{4}$ | Fay's equation (gravity-inertia) | Volume estimation of oil slicks |
| 7 | $BOD_5 = \dfrac{D_1 - D_2}{P}$ | $BOD_5$ = 5-day Biochemical Oxygen Demand, $D_1$ = initial DO, $D_2$ = 5-day DO, $P$ = dilution factor | Assessing organic pollution load |
| 8 | $BCF = \dfrac{C_{organism}}{C_{water}}$ | Bioconcentration Factor | Quantifying chemical uptake in marine biota |
| 9 | $D_{eff} = D_0 \cdot (1 + K_H)$ | Effective dilution; $K_H$ = Henry's constant | Air-sea exchange of volatile pollutants |
| 10 | $V = A \times h_{eff}$ | $V$ = spill volume, $A$ = area, $h_{eff}$ = effective thickness | Reverse calculation from aerial imagery |

#### **Table 2.3.B — Pollution Source Contribution Estimates (UNEP / GESAMP Data)**

| Source Category | Estimated % Contribution |
|---|---|
| Land-based sources | **80%** |
| Ship-based (operational) | **10%** |
| Dumping at sea | **5%** |
| Offshore installations | **4%** |
| Atmospheric deposition | **1%** |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fay's Oil Slick Spreading Equation (Gravity–Inertia Phase)

This is the **most-tested** derivation in Maritime Safety modules concerning oil spill modeling. We derive the area growth rate for an oil slick immediately after release.

#### **Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1: Setup of the Physical System**

Consider a fixed mass of oil $m$ released instantaneously at time $t = 0$ over calm seawater. The oil forms a circular slick of radius $r(t)$ and thickness $h(t)$ on the water surface.

**Step 2: Mass Conservation**

Since the oil does not leave the surface (initially):

$$m = \rho_{oil} \cdot V = \rho_{oil} \cdot A \cdot h = \rho_{oil} \cdot \pi r^2 h$$

**Step 3: Force Balance (Gravity vs. Inertia)**

In the **gravity–inertia phase**, the driving force is the **gravitational force** due to the density difference between oil and water. The resisting force is the **inertial force** of the oil mass.

The gravitational force driving spreading is proportional to:

$$F_g \propto \Delta \rho \cdot g \cdot h^3$$

where $\Delta \rho = \rho_{water} - \rho_{oil}$.

The inertial resistance per unit mass:

$$F_i \propto \dfrac{d^2 r}{dt^2}$$

**Step 4: Equating the Two Forces (Dimensional Analysis by Fay, 1969)**

By dimensional analysis, Fay balanced the driving and resisting forces:

$$\Delta \rho \cdot g \cdot h^3 \sim \rho_{oil} \cdot h \cdot \dfrac{d^2 r}{dt^2}$$

After simplification, the radius grows with time as:

$$r(t) = k_1 \cdot t^{\frac{1}{2}}$$

**Step 5: Substituting into Mass Conservation**

From mass conservation:

$$A = \pi r^2 = \pi k_1^2 \cdot t$$

Letting $k = \pi^{1/2} \cdot k_1$:

$$A = k^2 \cdot t$$

This gives Fay's classical result in its standard form:

$$\boxed{A = k^2 \cdot t}$$

where the constant $k$ is empirically found to be approximately **150 (SI units)** in field-calibrated studies.

**Step 6: Deriving the Volume Equation**

Using $V = A \cdot h$, and combining with the relation $h \propto t^{-1/2}$:

$$h(t) = \dfrac{V}{\pi r^2} = \dfrac{V}{A}$$

Thus:

$$V = A \cdot h = k^2 t \cdot h$$

Since $h \propto t^{-1/2}$ from the spreading dynamics, we obtain:

$$\boxed{V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot k^3 \cdot t^{3/2}}{4}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The constant $k$ is **NOT** a universal constant — it depends on oil type, temperature, and sea state. Students who quote $k = 150$ without context lose marks. Always mention "**empirically derived constant depending on oil properties and ambient conditions**."

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — First-Order Pollutant Decay in the Water Column

**Problem Setup:** A pollutant is released into a well-mixed marine compartment. Its concentration $C(t)$ follows first-order kinetics.

**Step 1: Governing Differential Equation**

The rate of change of concentration is proportional to the concentration itself:

$$\dfrac{dC}{dt} = -k \cdot C$$

**Step 2: Separation of Variables**

$$\dfrac{dC}{C} = -k \cdot dt$$

**Step 3: Integration**

Integrating both sides from $t = 0$ to $t$:

$$\int_{C_0}^{C} \dfrac{dC}{C} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\ln(C) - \ln(C_0) = -k \cdot t$$

**Step 4: Exponential Form**

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{C}{C_0}\right) = -k \cdot t$$

$$\boxed{C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-k t}}$$

**Step 5: Deriving the Half-Life**

Setting $C(t_{1/2}) = \dfrac{C_0}{2}$:

$$\dfrac{C_0}{2} = C_0 \cdot e^{-k t_{1/2}}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{2} = e^{-k t_{1/2}}$$

$$\ln(2) = k \cdot t_{1/2}$$

$$\boxed{t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}}$$

**Step 6: Real-World Application**

For marine oil spills, the decay rate $k$ is composed of:

$$k_{total} = k_{evap} + k_{dissolution} + k_{photoox} + k_{biodegrad}$$

Each term represents a removal pathway.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Pollutants to MARPOL Annexes

This is a **guaranteed 7-mark sub-question** in KTU exams. The matrix below is your ready reference:

| MARPOL Annex | Entry into Force | Pollutant Covered | KTU-Mandated Coverage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Annex I** | 2 Oct 1983 | **Oil** (including bilge, ballast, cargo residues) | **Mandatory** — most tested |
| **Annex II** | 6 Apr 1987 | **Noxious Liquid Substances (NLS)** in bulk | **Mandatory** — 4-category system |
| **Annex III** | 1 Jul 1992 | **Harmful Substances in Packaged Form** | Sometimes tested |
| **Annex IV** | 27 Sep 2003 | **Sewage** from ships | **Mandatory** |
| **Annex V** | 31 Dec 1988 | **Garbage** (plastics dominant) | **Mandatory** — plastic ban |
| **Annex VI** | 19 May 2005 | **Air Pollution** (SOx, NOx, ODS, CO₂) | **Mandatory** — EEDI, EEXI |

### 3.4 Numerical Example — Conservative Mixing in an Estuary

**Problem:** A coastal power plant discharges **500 m³/day of cooling water** containing **2.5 mg/L of mercury** into an estuary carrying **20,000 m³/day** of river water with a background mercury concentration of **0.05 mg/L**. Calculate the downstream concentration immediately after complete mixing.

**Step 1: Identify Parameters**
- $Q_1 = 500 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$, $C_1 = 2.5 \text{ mg/L}$
- $Q_2 = 20{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$, $C_2 = 0.05 \text{ mg/L}$

**Step 2: Apply the Conservative Mixing Formula**

$$C_{mix} = \dfrac{C_1 Q_1 + C_2 Q_2}{Q_1 + Q_2}$$

$$C_{mix} = \dfrac{(2.5)(500) + (0.05)(20{,}000)}{500 + 20{,}000}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Numerator**

$$C_{mix} = \dfrac{1{,}250 + 1{,}000}{20{,}500}$$

$$C_{mix} = \dfrac{2{,}250}{20{,}500}$$

**Step 4: Final Answer**

$$\boxed{C_{mix} \approx 0.1098 \text{ mg/L}}$$

This represents a **119% increase** over background, indicating significant contamination concern.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The receiving water is far more polluted than the source river. The discharge **dominates the contaminant load**. KTU examiners often ask for a comparison with **WHO/EPA drinking water standards** (Hg: 0.006 mg/L) and **marine aquatic life standards** (Hg: 0.00094 mg/L chronic).

---
