## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### **PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions (Answer in ~50–70 words each)**

---

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define marine pollution as per UNCLOS, 1982. List any four major sources of marine pollution.**

**Model Answer:**

Marine pollution, under **Article 1(1)(4) of UNCLOS 1982**, is the introduction by man, directly or indirectly, of substances or energy into the marine environment resulting in deleterious effects such as harm to living resources, hazards to human health, and hindrance to marine activities.

**Four major sources are:**

1. **Land-based sources** (rivers, industrial effluents, sewage) — ~80% contribution
2. **Ship-based sources** (operational discharges, accidental spills)
3. **Dumping at sea** (regulated under London Convention 1972)
4. **Offshore installations** (oil platforms, produced water, drilling muds)

A fifth, **atmospheric deposition**, is increasingly significant for mercury, POPs, and microplastics.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for listing, 1 for correct classification)*

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Briefly explain the MARPOL Convention and its six annexes.**

**Model Answer:**

The **MARPOL Convention (1973, modified by Protocol 1978 — MARPOL 73/78)** is the primary IMO treaty for preventing pollution from ships. It contains **six technical annexes**:

| Annex | Subject |
|---|---|
| **I** | Oil |
| **II** | Noxious Liquid Substances in bulk |
| **III** | Harmful Substances in packaged form |
| **IV** | Sewage from ships |
| **V** | Garbage from ships (plastics) |
| **VI** | Air pollution (SOx, NOx, CO₂) |

The convention uses a **"tacit acceptance"** amendment procedure, allowing rapid adoption of stricter measures. Annex VI was added via the **1997 Protocol**.

*(3 marks — 1 for intro, 1 for listing all six, 1 for tacit acceptance or amendment procedure)*

---

### **PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)**

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has **two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is mandatory between Question A and Question B (students answer ONE).

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify and explain in detail the various **land-based sources of marine pollution** with relevant examples. Discuss the contribution percentage of each category.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **fate and transport pathways of oil pollution** in the marine environment after a spill event.

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Land-based sources constitute approximately **80%** of all marine pollution, as confirmed by UNEP, IMO, and GESAMP joint studies. Unlike ship-based sources, these are diffuse, often continuous, and difficult to regulate because they originate from sovereign territories.

**Categories with Examples (5 marks):**

1. **Municipal and Domestic Sewage:** Untreated sewage from coastal cities introduces pathogens (coliforms, E. coli, viruses), nutrients (N, P), and suspended solids. Example: Mumbai, coastal Mediterranean cities, Lagos.

2. **Industrial Effluents:** Heavy metals (Hg, Pb, Cd, Cr), PCBs, dioxins, phenols, cyanide. Example: Minamata disaster in Japan (mercury), pulp and paper industries in Indonesia.

3. **Agricultural Runoff:** Fertilizers (urea, DAP) cause **eutrophication** and **harmful algal blooms (HABs)**. Pesticides introduce POPs. Example: Gulf of Mexico dead zone (Mississippi River discharge), Baltic Sea hypoxia.

4. **Plastic Litter and Urban Runoff:** Macro- and microplastics enter via rivers and storm drains. The "Great Pacific Garbage Patch" is largely land-derived.

5. **Aquaculture Discharges:** Antibiotics, uneaten feed, antifoulants. Example: Norwegian salmon farms, Southeast Asian shrimp farms.

6. **Tourism and Recreational:** Sunscreen chemicals (oxybenzone → coral bleaching), sewage from cruise terminals, beach litter.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Because land-based sources are governed by **national jurisdiction**, IMO conventions are insufficient. The **Global Programme of Action for the Protection of the Marine Environment from Land-Based Activities (GPA, 1995)** is the key UN framework for this category.

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

*(Refer to Section 4.3 — Fate Pathways Diagram)*

**Explanation of Each Pathway (4 marks):**

When oil is spilled at sea, it undergoes a sequence of **weathering processes**:

- **Spreading:** Oil forms a thin film on the surface, governed by **Fay's equations**. Initial gravity-inertia phase followed by gravity-viscous phase.

- **Evaporation:** Light fractions evaporate rapidly (within 24–48 hours for gasoline, much longer for heavy crude). Roughly **30–50%** of a typical crude oil evaporates.

- **Emulsification:** Water-in-oil emulsions ("mousse") form under wave action, increasing the oil's volume by 3–4 times and making cleanup extremely difficult.

- **Dissolution:** Water-soluble components (BTEX — benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, xylene) dissolve into the water column, creating acute toxicity.

- **Photo-oxidation:** Sunlight breaks down hydrocarbons into soluble, sometimes more toxic products.

- **Sedimentation:** Oil-coated particles or heavy residues sink to the seabed, creating long-term benthic contamination.

- **Biodegradation:** Marine bacteria (e.g., *Alcanivorax borkumensis*) consume hydrocarbons, though slowly in cold polar waters.

- **Bioaccumulation:** PAHs (Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons) accumulate in fish and shellfish, with carcinogenic implications for human consumers.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023] — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **MARPOL Convention Annexes I, IV, and V**, highlighting key discharge standards and the regulations concerning oil, sewage, and garbage respectively.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the phenomenon of **eutrophication** in marine and coastal waters. Describe its causes, consequences, and control measures.

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Annex I — Oil (3 marks):**

- Applies to all **oil tankers** and ships >400 GT.
- **Discharge standards for oily water:**
  - **15 ppm** of oil in bilge water (via OWS — Oily Water Separator)
  - **Instantaneous rate** must not exceed 30 liters/nautical mile
- **Special Areas:** Mediterranean, Baltic, Black, Red, Gulf, Antarctic — stricter rules.
- **SOPEP** (Shipboard Oil Pollution Emergency Plan) mandatory for all relevant ships.
- **IOPP Certificate** (International Oil Pollution Prevention) required.
- **Double hull requirement** for tankers (post-*Erika* and *Prestige* amendments).

**Annex IV — Sewage (2 marks):**

- Commenced **27 September 2003**.
- Sewage must be discharged:
  - At **>12 nautical miles** from nearest land (untreated), OR
  - At **>3 nautical miles** if discharged at a moderate rate with **approved Sewage Treatment Plant (STP)** and the resulting effluent does not produce visible floating solids or discoloration.
- Ships must have **sewage holding tanks** and either an STP or a **Marine Sanitation Device (MSD)**.

**Annex V — Garbage (2 marks):**

- **Complete ban on plastic disposal at sea** (since 31 Dec 1988, reinforced 2011/2013).
- Garbage types and discharge rules vary by distance from land.
- **Garbage Management Plan** and **Garbage Record Book** mandatory.
- **Placards** notifying crew of disposal rules must be displayed.
- Plastic fishing gear reported as a major contributor to "ghost fishing."

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Eutrophication is the excessive enrichment of a water body with **nutrients (primarily nitrogen and phosphorus)**, leading to stimulated growth of algae and aquatic plants, followed by oxygen depletion when the biomass decomposes.

**Causes (2 marks):**

- **Agricultural runoff** carrying fertilizers (the dominant source globally).
- **Sewage discharge** from coastal municipalities and ships.
- **Aquaculture waste.**
- **Atmospheric deposition** of nitrogen oxides.
- **Mariculture and fish farm effluents.**

**Consequences (2.5 marks):**

- **Algal blooms** (some toxic — "red tides," "brown tides").
- **Hypoxic and anoxic dead zones** (e.g., Gulf of Mexico — >15,000 km²; Baltic Sea).
- **Fish kills** due to suffocation.
- **Loss of biodiversity**, especially seagrass meadows and coral reefs.
- **Human health risks** from biotoxins in seafood (PSP, DSP, ASP toxins).
- **Economic impact** on fisheries and tourism.

**Control Measures (1.5 marks):**

- **Nutrient input reduction** at source (best long-term strategy).
- **Buffer zones** and constructed wetlands to filter runoff.
- **Precision agriculture** to minimize fertilizer use.
- **Advanced wastewater treatment** (tertiary nutrient removal).
- **Marine Protected Areas (MPAs)** with seasonal restrictions.
- **International cooperation:** OSPAR Commission, Helsinki Commission (HELCOM), now under UNEP GPA framework.

---

### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Marine Pollution Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating MARPOL Annex numbers with pollutant names.** Students often write "Annex I = Garbage" — this is **Annex V**. Memorize via the **mnemonic: "I Oil, II Liquid, III Packaged, IV Sewage, V Garbage, VI Air."**
> 2. **Quoting wrong contribution percentages.** Land-based is **80%**, not 50% or 90%. The 80% figure is from **UNEP/IMO joint assessments**.
> 3. **Confusing BOD₅ with COD.** BOD₅ measures **biodegradable** organics in 5 days; COD measures **total oxidizable** organics. COD > BOD₅ always.
> 4. **Forgetting the "Special Areas" for MARPOL Annex I.** The six special areas (Med, Baltic, Black, Red, Gulf, Antarctic) are frequently asked.
> 5. **Writing "oil floats because it is lighter"** without specifying "**less dense than water**." Density, not weight, governs buoyancy. Use numerical values: $\rho_{crude} \approx 0.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$ vs $\rho_{water} = 1.0 \text{ g/cm}^3$.
> 6. **In equations, writing t⁻¹/² without explaining the meaning of the constant k.** Examiners award marks for the **physical interpretation**, not just the formula.
> 7. **Missing UNCLOS 1982** when defining marine pollution. UNCLOS is the **legal backbone**; MARPOL is the **operational treaty**. Both are mandatory citations.
> 8. **Failing to distinguish bioaccumulation vs. biomagnification.** Bioaccumulation = buildup in **one organism** over time. Biomagnification = increasing concentration **up the food chain**.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Marine Pollution Sources and Types**

#### **A. Core Definitions (Memorize Verbatim)**

- **Marine Pollution (UNCLOS Art. 1):** Introduction of substances or energy → deleterious effects.
- **MARPOL 73/78:** IMO's flagship pollution convention.
- **HNS:** Hazardous & Noxious Substances (OPRC-HNS Protocol, 2000).
- **POPs:** Persistent Organic Pollutants (Stockholm Convention, 2001).
- **SOPEP:** Shipboard Oil Pollution Emergency Plan.
- **IOPP:** International Oil Pollution Prevention Certificate.

#### **B. The Big Numbers (Frequently Asked)**

- **80%** = Land-based source share
- **6** = Number of MARPOL Annexes
- **15 ppm** = Max oil in discharged bilge water
- **12 nm** = Min distance for untreated sewage discharge
- **3 nm** = Min distance for treated sewage discharge
- **9 Special Areas** for MARPOL (Annex I + V combined)
- **0.5%** = Threshold for plastic garbage ban
- **360 million km²** = Ocean surface area
- **8–12 million tonnes** = Annual plastic input to oceans

#### **C. The Big Five Conventions (Link Them)**

- **UNCLOS 1982** → Legal framework
- **MARPOL 73/78** → Operational rules for ships
- **London Convention 1972 / London Protocol 1996** → Dumping at sea
- **Stockholm Convention 2001** → POPs
- **Ballast Water Management Convention 2004** → Invasive species

#### **D. Pollutant Type Quick-Match (for MCQs)**

- **Oil slick spreading** → Fay's equation ($A = k^2 t$)
- **Organic load in water** → BOD₅
- **Toxicity over time** → $C(t) = C_0 e^{-kt}$
- **Chemical in organism** → BCF
- **Mixing downstream** → $C_{mix} = \frac{C_1V_1 + C_2V_2}{V_1 + V_2}$
- **Nutrient enrichment effect** → Eutrophication
- **Plastic in gut of fish** → Microplastic ingestion
- **Whale stranding near naval exercise** → Sonar/noise pollution

#### **E. The Four "Don't Forget" Items**

1. **Ballast water** is a *biological pollution* source, not chemical.
2. **Antifouling paints** (TBT) → IMO banned TBT-based paints in 2008.
3. **DESs and PCBs** are POPs covered by **Stockholm Convention**.
4. **Sewage** under MARPOL = black water + gray water (some definitions differ).

#### **F. Examiner's Three-Point Rule**

For any 14-mark question, ensure your answer contains:

1. **Definition + Legal framework** (UNCLOS / IMO citation)
2. **Classification + Examples** (3–5 categories)
3. **Engineering/quantitative analysis** (a formula, calculation, or diagram)

This **TRIAD** (Legal + Classified + Analytical) is the **gold standard** for KTU ESE answers in this topic.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oil Spills, Chemical Pollution, and Solid Waste
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Oil Spills, Chemical Pollution, and Solid Waste

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Oil Spills in the Maritime Context

An **oil spill** in the maritime domain refers to the intentional or accidental release of petroleum hydrocarbons (crude oil, fuel oil, lubricating oil, bilge oil, or refined products) into the marine environment from ships, offshore platforms, or port facilities. The severity of an oil spill is measured in metric tonnes (MT) discharged and classified as minor (<10 MT), medium (10–100 MT), or major (>100 MT) under the International Maritime Organization (IMO) framework.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MARPOL 73/78 Annex I** is the binding international convention that governs the prevention of pollution by oil from operational and accidental sources. Every KTU exam question on oil pollution traces back to MARPOL Annex I.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a small bottle of honey dropped on a kitchen table. The honey spreads out, gets sticky on whatever it touches, and is very hard to clean up completely. An oil spill at sea behaves similarly — except the "table" is the ocean surface, the "honey" is thousands of tonnes of crude oil, and the "sticky residue" can poison marine life, smother seabirds, contaminate coastlines, and shut down fisheries for years. The "cleaning cloth" is a combination of containment booms, skimmers, dispersants, and natural weathering.

## 1.2 Chemical Pollution (Hazardous and Noxious Substances — HNS)

**Chemical pollution** in the maritime context refers to the discharge, release, or loss of **Hazardous and Noxious Substances (HNS)** — including bulk chemicals, liquefied gases, packaged dangerous goods, and industrial chemicals — into the marine environment. These substances are regulated under **MARPOL Annex II** (bulk noxious liquid substances) and the **OPRC-HNS Protocol (2000)**, which extends the Oil Pollution Preparedness, Response and Co-operation Convention to chemical incidents.

### Intuitive Analogy
If oil is the "honey" that spreads and smothers, then a chemical spill is the "invisible poison gas." A tanker carrying sulfuric acid may release a substance that you cannot see, yet it can dissolve fish gills, corrode ship hulls, and render entire bays biologically dead. Unlike oil, many HNS are **water-soluble**, **reactive**, and **toxic at extremely low concentrations (parts per billion, ppb)**.

## 1.3 Solid Waste (Garbage) from Ships

**Solid waste** or **ship-generated garbage** encompasses all kinds of food, domestic, and operational waste generated during the normal service of a ship or during loading, unloading, and cleaning operations. MARPOL **Annex V** (entering into force 31 December 1988, with major amendments adopted by Resolution MEPC.201(62) effective 1 January 2013) is the governing instrument, with the cardinal rule that **discharge of all plastics into the sea is prohibited**.

### Key Definitions for KTU Examinations

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **MARPOL Convention** | The International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships, 1973, as modified by the Protocol of 1978 (MARPOL 73/78). |
| **IOPP Certificate** | International Oil Pollution Prevention Certificate, mandatory for every oil tanker >150 GT and all other ships >400 GT. |
| **SOPEP** | Shipboard Oil Pollution Emergency Plan, mandatory for every oil tanker >150 GT. |
| **Oil Record Book** | A mandatory log of all oil-transfer and disposal operations, kept in a prescribed IMO format. |
| **HNS** | Hazardous and Noxious Substances (chemicals, gases, packaged dangerous goods). |
| **Garbage Management Plan** | Mandatory written plan describing procedures for collecting, storing, processing, and disposing of garbage (MARPOL Annex V). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sewage** is a separate pollution category regulated under **MARPOL Annex IV** and is treated as a distinct stream from solid waste. However, it is conceptually grouped with ship-generated wastes in environmental management frameworks.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **1 barrel (bbl) of crude oil = 0.159 m³ ≈ 136.4 kg** (using a typical crude density of **860 kg/m³**).
- **1 metric tonne (MT) of crude oil ≈ 7.33 bbl** (at 860 kg/m³ density).
- **1 nautical mile = 1.852 km**.
- The **Area of the world's oceans** = **361 million km²** (≈71% of Earth's surface).
- The **average depth of the world ocean** = **3,688 m**.
- A **dispersant-to-oil ratio (DOR)** of **1:20 to 1:30** is typically applied during chemical response.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Oil Pollution from Ships

The principal operational and accidental sources of oil pollution at sea are:

1. **Operational Discharges** (≈70% of total input):
   - Bilge water and oil-residue discharges
   - Sludge and oil-recovered waste from fuel-purifier operations
   - Ballast water from oil tankers
   - Cargo-tank washing residues
   - Leakage from machinery spaces

2. **Accidental Discharges** (≈30% of total input):
   - Collisions and groundings (e.g., *Exxon Valdez*, 1989; *Erika*, 1999; *Prestige*, 2002)
   - Structural failures
   - Fires and explosions
   - Bunker fuel spills during bunkering operations

## 2.2 Behaviour and Fate of Spilled Oil (Weathering Processes)

Once oil is released at sea, it undergoes a series of simultaneous physical, chemical, and biological transformations collectively called **weathering**:

| Process | Mechanism | Typical Timeframe | Effect on Volume |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Spreading** | Gravity-driven horizontal flow | Minutes to hours | Increases surface area |
| **Evaporation** | Volatile fractions evaporate | Hours to days | 20–60% loss (light crude) |
| **Emulsification** | Water-in-oil mousse formation | Hours to weeks | Up to 4× volume increase |
| **Dissolution** | Water-soluble fractions enter water | Days | <5% loss |
| **Dispersion** | Breaking into droplets | Hours to days | Vertical transport |
| **Photo-oxidation** | UV light oxidizes hydrocarbons | Weeks to months | Forms tars |
| **Sedimentation** | Oil-coated particles sink | Days to months | Vertical transport |
| **Biodegradation** | Microbial action | Months to years | Final mass removal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Evaporation** is the single most important natural removal process during the first 24–48 hours for light crude oils. Heavy fuel oils (HFO) and residual fuels lose only **5–15%** by evaporation.

## 2.3 Chemical Pollution Behaviour

HNS spills behave fundamentally differently from oil spills because:

- **Toxicity dominance**: Many HNS are lethal at concentrations orders of magnitude lower than oil (e.g., hydrogen cyanide LC₅₀ for fish < 100 µg/L).
- **Reactivity dominance**: Some HNS react violently with seawater (e.g., sodium, alkali metals, acids, alkalis).
- **Physical state diversity**: HNS can be gases (LNG, LPG), liquids (acids, solvents), or solids (fertilizers, ores).
- **Flammability**: Many HNS are flammable, with risks of fire and explosion (e.g., liquefied petroleum gas — LPG).
- **Persistence**: Some HNS (e.g., PCBs, DDT) persist for decades and bioaccumulate in the food chain (biomagnification factor of **10⁴ to 10⁷**).

The **Emergency Response Procedures for Ships Carrying Dangerous Goods (EmS Guide)** is the IMO's supplementary guide to the IMDG Code, providing Fire Schedules (F) and Spillage Schedules (S) for every UN-numbered substance.

## 2.4 Solid Waste Classification Under MARPOL Annex V

The Annex V categories (A through K, plus operational waste) are:

| Category | Examples | Discharge Restriction |
|---|---|---|
| **A — Plastics** | Synthetic ropes, fishing nets, plastic bags | **Total ban — all sea areas** |
| **B — Food waste** | Spoiled food, food residues | Ground to <25 mm; discharged >12 nm |
| **C — Domestic waste** | Paper, rags, glass, metal | Discharged >12 nm (paper, rags, metal >25 nm) |
| **D — Cooking oil** | Used cooking oil | Discharged >12 nm |
| **E — Incinerator ash** | Ash from shipboard incineration | Discharged >12 nm |
| **F — Operational waste** | Cargo residues, cleaning agents | Special rules in Polar Code areas |
| **G — Cargo residues** | Solid bulk cargo residues | According to MARPOL Annex V Reg. 6 |
| **H — Fishing gear** | Lost or discarded gear | Total ban |
| **I — E-waste** | Electronic waste | Total ban |
| **J — Cargo hold washing water** | Wash water with non-HME substances | Variable |
| **K — Animal carcasses** | Dead livestock | Discharged >100 nm |

> [!NOTE]
> The **"Special Area"** concept under Annex V applies in the Mediterranean, Baltic, Black, Red, and Antarctic seas, where ALL garbage discharge (except comminuted/ground food waste >12 nm) is **strictly prohibited**.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **V = m / ρ** | Volume = Mass ÷ Density | m³ = kg / (kg/m³) | Converting oil mass to volume |
| **Aₑ = 0.5 · (V·g·Δρ / νₑ)^{1/2} · t^{3/4}** | Fay spreading area (Phase 1, gravity-inertial) | m² | Oil slick surface area |
| **dL_max = (V·g·Δρ/νₑ)^{1/4} · t^{1/2}** | Fay max slick length | m | Length of elliptical slick |
| **E% = 0.165 · log₁₀(API) · T** | Percentage evaporation (Stiver-Mackay) | % | Light crude evaporation |
| **t_½ = ln 2 / k** | Half-life of pollutant | days | First-order decay |
| **C(t) = C₀ · e^(-kt)** | First-order decay / biodegradation | mg/L | Pollutant concentration over time |
| **k = ln 2 / t_½** | Decay rate constant | 1/day | Biodegradation kinetics |
| **BCF = C_organism / C_water** | Bioconcentration factor | dimensionless | Bioaccumulation potential |
| **Re = ρ·v·L / μ** | Reynolds number (oil droplet settling) | dimensionless | Flow regime (laminar/turbulent) |
| **F_b = 0.5 · C_D · ρ · A · v²** | Drag force on droplet | N | Droplet settling velocity |
| **v_s = (4·g·d·(ρ_oil - ρ_water)) / (3·C_D·ρ_water)** | Stokes-like settling velocity | m/s | Droplet descent velocity |
| **DOR = V_dispersant / V_oil** | Dispersant-to-oil ratio | dimensionless | Dispersant application rate |
| **Recovery efficiency η = V_recovered / V_spilled** | Skimmer/booming efficiency | dimensionless | Containment effectiveness |

> [!NOTE]
> The Fay spreading model (1971) treats the slick in three sequential phases: **(1) gravity-inertial, (2) gravity-viscous, and (3) surface-tension-viscous**. In Phase 1 the area grows as **t^{3/4}**, in Phase 2 as **t^{1/2}**, and in Phase 3 the slick reaches a maximum size and stabilises.

## 2.6 Response Hierarchy for Oil Spills

The internationally accepted response priority is:

1. **Source control** (patching the leak, transferring cargo)
2. **Containment** (booms)
3. **Recovery** (skimmers, vacuum systems)
4. **Chemical treatment** (dispersants, gelling agents)
5. **In-situ burning** (when safe)
6. **Bioremediation** (fertilizers + microbes)
7. **Natural recovery / monitoring** (passive)
8. **Shoreline cleanup** (manual/mechanical)

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Converting Spill Mass to Spill Volume

A KTU favourite — converting tonnes of oil into barrels or cubic metres.

### Step 1: Recall the standard density of crude oil.
Crude oil has a typical density of **860 kg/m³** (specific gravity 0.86, API gravity ≈ 33°).

### Step 2: Set up the density relation.
Density (ρ) is mass per unit volume.

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V}$$

### Step 3: Solve for volume.

$$V = \frac{m}{\rho}$$

### Step 4: Substitute a typical spill mass, say **m = 1,000 tonnes = 1,000,000 kg**.

$$V = \frac{1{,}000{,}000 \text{ kg}}{860 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

$$V = 1{,}162.79 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 5: Convert to barrels (1 m³ = 6.2898 bbl).

$$V = 1{,}162.79 \times 6.2898 \text{ bbl}$$

$$V \approx 7{,}313 \text{ bbl}$$

### Step 6: Cross-check with the rule of thumb.

$$1 \text{ MT} \approx 7.33 \text{ bbl at } \rho = 860 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$1{,}000 \text{ MT} \approx 7{,}330 \text{ bbl} \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Fay Gravity-Inertial Spreading (Phase 1)

### Step 1: Begin with the conservation of mass.
For an elliptical slick of major axis **L** and minor axis **2·b**:

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot L \cdot 2b \cdot h}{4} = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot L \cdot b \cdot h$$

where **h** is the slick thickness.

### Step 2: Express the slick area.

$$A = \pi \cdot L \cdot b$$

### Step 3: In Phase 1 (gravity-inertial regime), Fay showed by dimensional analysis that the slick radius grows as **t^{3/8}**, and area as **t^{3/4}**:

$$A_e = 0.5 \cdot \left(\frac{V \cdot g \cdot \Delta \rho}{\nu_e}\right)^{1/2} \cdot t^{3/4}$$

where:
- **g** = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- **Δρ** = density difference between water and oil = **(1000 − 860) = 140 kg/m³**
- **νₑ** = kinematic viscosity of oil = **5 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s** (typical)
- **V** = spill volume (m³)
- **t** = time (s)

### Step 4: Substitute numerical values (V = 1,000 m³, t = 3600 s = 1 hour).

$$A_e = 0.5 \cdot \left(\frac{1{,}000 \times 9.81 \times 140}{5 \times 10^{-5}}\right)^{1/2} \cdot (3600)^{3/4}$$

**Compute the bracket term:**

$$\frac{1{,}000 \times 9.81 \times 140}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1{,}373{,}400}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.7468 \times 10^{10}$$

**Take the square root:**

$$\sqrt{2.7468 \times 10^{10}} = 1.6574 \times 10^{5}$$

**Compute t^{3/4}:**

$$3600^{3/4} = \left(3600^{1/4}\right)^{3} = (7.741)^{3} \approx 463.9$$

**Multiply:**

$$A_e = 0.5 \times 1.6574 \times 10^{5} \times 463.9$$

$$A_e \approx 3.84 \times 10^{7} \text{ m}^2 \approx 38.4 \text{ km}^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **≈ 5 times the area of a 5 km × 7.7 km rectangle** — a single tonne of crude oil, after just **1 hour** at sea, can spread over a phenomenal area.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: First-Order Biodegradation of Hydrocarbons

### Step 1: Define the rate equation.
Microbial breakdown of oil hydrocarbons follows first-order kinetics:

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = -k \cdot C$$

where **C** is hydrocarbon concentration (mg/L) and **k** is the first-order rate constant (1/day).

### Step 2: Separate variables and integrate.

$$\int_{C_0}^{C(t)} \frac{dC}{C} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{C(t)}{C_0}\right) = -k \cdot t$$

### Step 3: Exponentiate to obtain the standard decay equation.

$$C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$$

### Step 4: Derive the half-life relationship.
Setting **C(t₁/₂) = C₀/2**:

$$\frac{C_0}{2} = C_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t_{1/2}}$$

$$0.5 = e^{-k \cdot t_{1/2}}$$

$$\ln 0.5 = -k \cdot t_{1/2}$$

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.693}{k}$$

### Step 5: Numerical example.
A heavy crude oil has a measured first-order rate constant of **k = 0.005 day⁻¹** for n-alkane degradation by marine bacteria at 20°C. Calculate the time for **90%** biodegradation.

$$0.1 \cdot C_0 = C_0 \cdot e^{-0.005 \cdot t}$$

$$-0.005 \cdot t = \ln(0.1) = -2.3026$$

$$t = \frac{2.3026}{0.005} = 460.5 \text{ days} \approx 15.2 \text{ months}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **bioremediation** is suitable only for **chronic, low-energy, sheltered spills** — not for high-energy, exposed, large-scale incidents.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Skimmer Recovery Rate

### Step 1: Define the recovery equation.
A weir skimmer's volumetric recovery rate **Q** is:

$$Q = W \cdot v_s \cdot \eta$$

where:
- **W** = weir width (m)
- **v_s** = approach velocity of oil layer (m/s)
- **η** = efficiency factor (0.4–0.85 depending on sea state)

### Step 2: Compute recovery for a typical weir skimmer (W = 5 m, v_s = 0.05 m/s, η = 0.7).

$$Q = 5 \times 0.05 \times 0.7 = 0.175 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

### Step 3: Convert to tonnes/hour (using ρ = 860 kg/m³).

$$Q = 0.175 \times 860 \times 3600 = 541{,}800 \text{ kg/h} \approx 542 \text{ tonnes/h}$$

### Step 4: Calculate total recovery for a 12-hour day.

$$M = 542 \times 12 = 6{,}504 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

### Step 5: Recovery efficiency for a 25,000-tonne spill.

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{6{,}504 \times 5 \text{ days}}{25{,}000} = 1.30 \quad \Rightarrow \text{cap at } 100\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The equation shows that even under ideal conditions, **mechanical recovery alone** can rarely address more than **10–20% of a large offshore spill** within a reasonable timeframe. This is why the response strategy is a **layered approach**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Spill-Response Decision Logic

| Spill Type | Sea State | Volume | Recommended Primary Response | Secondary |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Heavy fuel oil | Beaufort 0–2 | < 5,000 MT | Containment + skimming | Sorbents |
| Light crude | Beaufort 3–4 | > 10,000 MT | Dispersants (aerial) | Skimming |
| Crude in ice | Sea ice present | Any | In-situ burning | Bioremediation |
| Chemical (acid) | Any | < 100 MT | Neutralization (if feasible) | Booming + absorbents |
| Chemical (LNG) | Any | Any | Exclusion zone + firefighting | Water spray curtains |
| Plastic debris | Any | Any | Manual recovery | Onboard shredding + reception facilities |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Define the term "MARPOL Convention" and state the six annexes currently in force.**

**Model Answer:**

The MARPOL Convention is the **International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships, 1973, as modified by the Protocol of 1978 (MARPOL 73/78)**. It is the principal international treaty aimed at preventing pollution of the marine environment by ships from operational or accidental causes.

The six annexes currently in force are:

1. **Annex I** — Prevention of pollution by oil
2. **Annex II** — Control of pollution by noxious liquid substances in bulk
3. **Annex IV** — Prevention of pollution by sewage from ships
4. **Annex V** — Prevention of pollution by garbage from ships
5. **Annex VI** — Prevention of air pollution from ships
6. **Annex III** — Prevention of pollution by harmful substances carried in packaged form

*(Annex III was made mandatory in 1992; Annex IV became mandatory in 2003.)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**List any four categories of ship-generated garbage as per MARPOL Annex V and state the discharge restriction for plastics.**

**Model Answer:**

The four categories of ship-generated garbage under MARPOL Annex V are:

1. **Plastics (Category A)** — including synthetic ropes, fishing nets, and plastic bags.
2. **Food waste (Category B)** — generated from food preparation and service.
3. **Domestic waste (Category C)** — paper, rags, glass, metal.
4. **Operational waste (Category F)** — cargo residues, cleaning agents.

**Discharge restriction for plastics**: The discharge of **all plastics**, including synthetic ropes, fishing nets, and plastic garbage bags, into the sea is **strictly prohibited in all sea areas** (no minimum distance, no exceptions).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Describe in detail the various **weathering processes** that affect spilled oil at sea. Highlight the relative importance of each process during the first 48 hours of a typical light crude spill. (7 marks)

**(b)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the operation of a **Fay spreading model** for an oil slick. Derive the area-time relationship in the gravity-inertial phase. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (a):**

The weathering processes that affect oil spilled at sea are:

1. **Spreading**: Gravity and inertia drive the oil into a thin, lens-shaped slick. Initial spread rate is rapid, slowing with time. For 1,000 m³ of crude, the slick may cover 30–40 km² within one hour.

2. **Evaporation**: The most important natural removal process in the first 24–48 hours for light crudes. Components boiling below ~200°C volatilise. A light crude may lose **30–40% of its mass** in the first 24 hours; heavy fuel oil loses only **5–10%**. Evaporation increases viscosity, raises density, and reduces the slick's flammability.

3. **Emulsification (mous formation)**: Wave action entrains water into the oil, forming a viscous "chocolate mousse" with water content of 50–80% by volume. This increases slick volume by up to **4×**, dramatically increases viscosity (by 100–1000×), and makes the oil extremely difficult to recover. Mousse can persist for **weeks to months**.

4. **Dispersion**: Breaking of the slick into droplets of size 10 µm – 1 mm, which become entrained in the water column. Natural dispersion is enhanced by wave energy (sea state > 3) and by application of chemical dispersants. The dispersed droplets are then subject to dissolution and biodegradation.

5. **Dissolution**: Water-soluble fractions (e.g., low-molecular-weight aromatics like benzene) dissolve into the seawater. This process is rapid (hours) but accounts for less than **5%** of total mass for most crudes, although it is significant for acute toxicity.

6. **Photo-oxidation**: UV radiation from sunlight breaks down hydrocarbons into polar, oxygenated compounds (peroxides, ketones, acids) that contribute to the formation of persistent **tar balls**.

7. **Sedimentation**: Oil-coated mineral particles (sediment) increase in density and sink to the seabed, causing long-term benthic contamination. Particularly important in shallow, turbid coastal waters.

8. **Biodegradation**: The ultimate removal process. Marine bacteria (e.g., *Alcanivorax borkumensis*) metabolise hydrocarbons into CO₂ and water. First-order rate constants range from **0.001 to 0.05 day⁻¹**, depending on oil type, temperature, and nutrient availability.

**Relative importance (first 48 hours) for a light crude:**
- Evaporation: ~35% (dominant)
- Dispersion: ~20%
- Spreading: passive (no mass change)
- Emulsification: ~15%
- Dissolution: ~3%
- Biodegradation: negligible in first 48 h
- Photo-oxidation: ~1%

**Model Solution (b):**

The **Fay spreading model** (Fay, 1971) describes the horizontal spread of an oil slick on a calm sea surface in three sequential phases, governed by different balances of forces:

- **Phase 1 (Gravity-Inertial)**: Gravity force equals inertial force. Spreading is rapid.
- **Phase 2 (Gravity-Viscous)**: Gravity force equals viscous force. Spreading decelerates.
- **Phase 3 (Surface-Tension-Viscous)**: Surface-tension force equals viscous force. Slick reaches a maximum size.

**Derivation of Phase 1 (Gravity-Inertial) area-time relationship:**

Fay performed a **dimensional analysis** of the relevant variables: spill volume V, time t, gravitational acceleration g, density difference Δρ between water and oil, and oil kinematic viscosity ν.

He constructed two dimensionless groups (π-terms) and matched them to experimental observations to obtain:

$$A_e = 0.5 \cdot \left(\frac{V \cdot g \cdot \Delta \rho}{\nu}\right)^{1/2} \cdot t^{3/4}$$

Equivalently, the slick radius (for a circular slick approximation) is:

$$R(t) = k_1 \cdot \left(V \cdot g \cdot \Delta \rho \cdot t^{3/2}\right)^{1/4}$$

Thus, **area grows as t^{3/4}** — a hallmark of gravity-inertial spreading. The slick thickness at any time can be recovered from:

$$h(t) = \frac{V}{A_e(t)}$$

For Phase 2 (gravity-viscous), Fay found:

$$A_e = 0.98 \cdot \left(V^2 \cdot g \cdot \Delta \rho \cdot t^{1/2}\right) / \nu^{1/2}$$

i.e., **area grows as t^{1/2}**.

The transition from Phase 1 to Phase 2 occurs at the crossover time:

$$t_c \sim \left(\frac{V}{\nu \cdot g \cdot \Delta \rho}\right)^{1/3}$$

(typically 10² to 10³ seconds for a 1,000 m³ spill).

---

### Question B (Option 2) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **shipboard oil pollution emergency plan (SOPEP)** required under MARPOL Annex I. State the information to be reported in case of an oil pollution incident. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **various techniques of oil spill response** at sea. Compare mechanical recovery with chemical dispersion as response options. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (a):**

The **SOPEP (Shipboard Oil Pollution Emergency Plan)** is a mandatory document under **MARPOL Annex I, Regulation 37**, applicable to every oil tanker of **150 GT and above**, and to every other ship of **400 GT and above**. Its purpose is to provide the master and crew with a clear, concise, and ready-reference action plan to deal with an oil-spill incident.

**Contents of the SOPEP (as per IMO Resolution MEPC.54(32)):**

1. **Introduction** — purpose, scope, and regulatory basis.
2. **Notification procedures** — whom to contact, when, and how.
3. **Reportable information** (see below).
4. **Steps to control discharge** — patch, transfer, plug.
5. **National and local coordination** — coastal-state authorities.
6. **Annexes** — contact lists, ship-specific data, location diagrams.

**Information to be reported in case of an oil pollution incident (per Article 8 of the OPRC Convention 1990, and Regulation 37 of MARPOL Annex I):**

- Identity of the ship involved
- Type of ship (oil tanker, chemical tanker, dry cargo, etc.)
- Time, type, and location of the incident
- Quantity and type of cargo / bunker oil on board
- Quantity and type of oil spilled
- Weather and sea conditions
- Ship's course and speed
- Damage status and assistance required
- Pollution forecast (likely drift)
- Ship's representative / owner details

This report must be issued **without delay** to the nearest coastal state. The convention also obliges the ship to act under the direction of the coastal state during response operations.

**Model Solution (b):**

The principal oil-spill response techniques are:

1. **Mechanical recovery** (booming and skimming): Use of floating booms to contain oil, then recovery with weir skimmers, oleophilic skimmers, vacuum systems, or sorbents. Effective in **calm-to-moderate seas (sea state < 4)** and for thick slicks. Recovery efficiency ranges from **5–30% of spilled oil** for large offshore spills but can exceed 60% for small, contained nearshore spills.

2. **Chemical dispersion**: Application of surface-active chemical dispersants (typically sprayed from aircraft or vessels) that reduce oil-water interfacial tension, breaking the slick into small droplets (100 µm or less) that entrain into the water column. Effective in **open water, sea state > 3**, with **light-to-medium crudes** (not heavy fuel oil). Dispersant-to-oil ratio of **1:20 to 1:30**. Avoid near shore, in fisheries, and near marine protected areas.

3. **In-situ burning**: Igniting the contained slick using a fire-resistant boom. Highly effective (>90% removal) but produces air pollution, requires minimum oil thickness of **2–5 mm**, and has safety constraints.

4. **Bioremediation**: Application of fertilizers (N and P) to stimulate indigenous hydrocarbon-degrading bacteria. Slow (weeks to months) but low-impact. Effective in sheltered, low-energy environments.

5. **Sorbents**: Natural (straw, peat) or synthetic (polypropylene) materials that absorb oil. Used for final polishing or for small spills.

6. **Manual/mechanical shoreline cleanup**: Required when oil reaches the coast. Methods depend on substrate (sand, rocks, mangroves).

**Comparison: Mechanical vs. Chemical**

| Criterion | Mechanical | Chemical |
|---|---|---|
| Best sea state | 0–3 (calm) | 3–5 (moderate) |
| Oil type | All | Light/medium crudes only |
| Recovery efficiency | 5–30% | Effective dispersion but mass is not removed, just relocated |
| Cost | High (equipment, vessels) | Lower per tonne |
| Logistics | Boom arrays, skimmer ships | Aircraft, dispersant stockpiles |
| Environmental impact | Low | Moderate (dispersed oil is more bioavailable) |
| Time to deploy | Hours (towing booms) | Faster (aerial) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning**: Students frequently confuse **dispersants with detergents**. Dispersants are **toxicity-reducing, surface-active chemicals** designed for marine use; detergents (e.g., household washing liquids) are **prohibited** and cause severe secondary pollution. Avoid this conflation in answers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Oil Spill / Solid Waste Answers:**
>
> 1. **MARPOL Annex numbering**: Students write "Annex III" for oil (it is **Annex I**). The order is **I (oil), II (chemicals bulk), III (packaged), IV (sewage), V (garbage), VI (air)**. Memorise this sequence.
> 2. **Special Areas vs. Pollution Categories**: Special Areas under Annex V are **Mediterranean, Baltic, Black, Red, Antarctic, and North-West European waters** (post-2021). These prohibit all garbage discharge subject to narrow exceptions.
> 3. **SOPEP vs. SOPEP-S**: Oil tankers >150 GT and other ships >400 GT must carry a SOPEP. For chemical carriers, the equivalent plan is the **SOPEP (Chemical)**, often integrated with the SOPEP.
> 4. **In-situ burning threshold**: Students forget that burning requires an oil film thickness of at least **2–3 mm** (typically > 5 mm for sustained burn).
> 5. **Dispersant toxicity**: Always state that dispersants are **toxicity-reducing, not toxicity-free** — they transfer oil from surface to water column, where it is diluted and biodegraded.
> 6. **Units**: KTU strictly requires SI units. Mixing **tonnes, barrels, m³, and gallons** without conversion will lose marks.
> 7. **Annex V Amendments**: The 2013 amendments (MEPC.201(62)) drastically tightened garbage management. Older answers citing pre-2013 rules are now obsolete.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MARPOL 73/78** has six annexes in force: I (Oil), II (Noxious Liquid Substances), III (Packaged Harmful Substances), IV (Sewage), V (Garbage), VI (Air Pollution).
- **Annex I**: SOPEP mandatory for tankers >150 GT and other ships >400 GT. The **Oil Record Book (ORB)** is the legally required log.
- **Annex V**: Discharge of **all plastics is forbidden** in all sea areas. Food waste must be comminuted to <25 mm and discharged beyond 12 nm. Special Areas impose stricter rules.
- **Oil weathering** order of importance (24–48 h, light crude): Evaporation > Dispersion > Emulsification > Photo-oxidation.
- **Fay spreading model** in three phases: gravity-inertial (A ∝ t^{3/4}), gravity-viscous (A ∝ t^{1/2}), surface-tension-viscous (steady state).
- **Response hierarchy**: Source control → Containment → Recovery → Chemical treatment → In-situ burning → Bioremediation → Natural recovery.
- **HNS spills** are governed by the **OPRC-HNS Protocol (2000)** and the **IMDG Code / EmS Guide**.
- **HNS behaviour** differs from oil because of **toxicity, reactivity, flammability, and water solubility**.
- **Critical constants**: 1 MT crude ≈ **7.33 bbl**; sea state >3 favours dispersion; oil film >2 mm for burning.
- **Reception facilities** at ports are **mandatory under MARPOL** — ships must not dump waste at sea when port facilities are available.
- **The 2013 Annex V amendments** introduced categories I–K and the Garbage Record Book.
- **Bunker spills** are now subject to stricter civil liability under the **Bunker Convention (2001)**.
- **Polar Code (2017)**: All discharges of oil, chemicals, and garbage are heavily restricted in Arctic and Antarctic waters.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Marine Pollution Prevention and Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Marine Pollution Prevention and Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Marine Pollution Prevention and Control** refers to the comprehensive set of international, regional, and national legal frameworks, engineering systems, operational procedures, and technological interventions designed to prevent, reduce, and control the discharge of harmful substances and pollutants from ships into the marine environment. The cornerstone international instrument is the **International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships (MARPOL 73/78)**, administered by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**, which represents the most important international treaty aimed at preventing pollution of the marine environment by ships from operational or accidental causes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** MARPOL 73/78 is a combined treaty comprising the 1973 Convention on Prevention of Marine Pollution by Dumping of Wastes and Other Matter (modified by the 1978 Protocol). It is **mandatorily enforceable** under international law and is treated as the single most critical treaty in maritime safety examinations.

The framework operates through **six technical Annexes**, each addressing a specific category of pollution:

| Annex | Subject | Entry into Force |
|-------|---------|------------------|
| Annex I | Oil / Oily Water | 2 October 1983 |
| Annex II | Noxious Liquid Substances (NLS) in bulk | 6 April 1987 |
| Annex III | Harmful Substances in Packaged Form | 1 July 1992 |
| Annex IV | Sewage | 27 September 2003 |
| Annex V | Garbage | 31 December 1988 |
| Annex VI | Air Pollution | 19 May 2005 |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a massive apartment complex (the ocean) where every resident (the ship) cooks, washes, and throws waste. Without a central sanitation code, the building would collapse into a toxic mess. **MARPOL acts as the building's sanitation bylaw**: each Annex is like a specific rule — one for cooking oil disposal (Annex I), one for chemical drain cleaners (Annex II), and one for kitchen trash sorting (Annex V). The **Port State Control (PSC)** officers are the building inspectors, while **Flag State** administrations are the landlords who sign the leases. Just as a building cannot function without enforced sanitation rules, the oceans cannot survive without MARPOL.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Standard Oil-Water Discharge Limit:** **15 ppm (parts per million)** for machinery space bilges (Annex I Regulation 14).
- **Instantaneous Rate of Oil Discharge Limit:** **30 litres/nautical mile** for oil tankers.
- **Sewage Discharge Distance:** **>4 nautical miles** from nearest land (or 12 nm if untreated and rate ≥ 4 knots).
- **SOx Emission Cap (Global, post-2020):** **0.50% m/m (mass by mass)** sulphur content in marine fuel oil.
- **ECA (Emission Control Area) SOx Cap:** **0.10% m/m** sulphur content.
- **NOx Tier III Limit:** **Tier III** standards apply in NOx Emission Control Areas.
- **Garbage Discharge Prohibition:** **Zero plastic discharge** anywhere at sea (Annex V, Reg. 3).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Memorize the numerical thresholds (**15 ppm, 30 L/nm, 4 nm, 0.50% S, 0.10% S**) verbatim — these are recurring "memory-trigger" questions worth 3 marks each in University Examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The MARPOL Framework — Structured Logic

#### Step 1: Why MARPOL was Created

Before MARPOL, ship-source pollution was governed only by weak customary law. Major oil spills (e.g., *Torrey Canyon 1967*) and increasing chemical discharges necessitated a binding international treaty.

#### Step 2: The Six-Annex Architecture

Each Annex follows a common pattern:
1. **General obligations** (duty to prevent pollution)
2. **Categorical discharge standards** (numerical limits)
3. **Construction & equipment requirements** (e.g., OWS, Sewage Treatment Plant)
4. **Documentation & certification** (e.g., IOPP Certificate)
5. **Special areas** with stricter limits
6. **Port State Control enforcement**

#### Step 3: The "Why" of Oil-Water Separation

Oily bilge water is an unavoidable by-product of engine rooms. Because oil and water naturally separate by density ($\rho_{oil} \approx 850\text{ kg/m}^3$ vs. $\rho_{water} \approx 1025\text{ kg/m}^3$), a **gravity separator** can reduce concentrations to **≤15 ppm** before discharge — this is the engineering basis of the **Oily Water Separator (OWS)** with **15 ppm Oil Content Meter (OCM)** compliance.

#### Step 4: The "How" of Segregated Ballast

To prevent oil tankers from needing to carry ballast water in cargo tanks (a major source of pollution during ballast voyages), MARPOL Regulation 18 mandates **Segregated Ballast Tanks (SBT)** — dedicated ballast compartments that never contact oil cargo, reducing pollution by an estimated **>80%** in routine operations.

#### Step 5: Special Areas — Stricter Rules

Special Areas (e.g., the Mediterranean Sea, Baltic Sea, Antarctic, North-West European Waters, the Gulf, Caribbean, Red Sea) impose near-zero discharge rules because of high ecological sensitivity or slow water exchange.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concept Hook:** A ship entering a Special Area must either retain pollutants on board, discharge to a reception facility, or operate below stricter thresholds — non-compliance attracts detention by Port State Control.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical:** Pipes `\vert` have been replaced with `\mid` or `\vert` to avoid breaking table syntax.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Variables & Units | Boundary / Limit |
|---|---------|----------------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Oil content in bilge discharge | $C \leq 15 \text{ ppm}$ | $C$ = oil concentration | Must be met at all times |
| 2 | Instantaneous oil discharge rate (tanker) | $Q \leq 30 \text{ L/nm}$ | $Q$ = discharge rate | Total quantity $\leq 1/15,000$ cargo |
| 3 | Total oil discharge ratio (tanker) | $\sum Q_{oil} \leq \tfrac{1}{30,000} \cdot m_{cargo}$ | $m_{cargo}$ = total cargo (mass) | For existing tankers (pre-2007) |
| 4 | Discharge distance (oil) | $\geq 50 \text{ nm from nearest land}$ | For oily bilge from machinery | Special Areas: **prohibited** |
| 5 | Sewage discharge | $\geq 4 \text{ nm}$ (treated) or $\geq 12 \text{ nm}$ (untreated, $\geq 4$ knots) | Distance from land | Inside 4 nm: STP mandatory |
| 6 | Garbage discharge (food waste) | $\geq 12 \text{ nm}$ from nearest land (comminuted/ground, $\geq 4$ nm) | Communitated particle $\leq 25$ mm | Plastics: **zero discharge** |
| 7 | SOx in fuel (Global Cap) | $S_{\%} \leq 0.50\%$ | $S_{\%}$ = sulphur mass fraction | ECA: $\leq 0.10\%$ |
| 8 | NOx emission index (Tier I baseline) | $EI_{NO_x} \leq 11.5 \cdot n^{0.2}$ g/kWh | $n$ = engine speed (rpm) | Tier III in NECAs applies |
| 9 | Ballast water organism discharge | $\leq 10 \text{ organisms/m}^3$ ($\geq 50 \mu m$) | IMO D-2 Standard | BWMS required |
| 10 | Total oil on board in cargo tanks | $\sum V_{oil} \leq \tfrac{1}{15,000} \cdot V_{cargo}$ | $V$ = volume | Or $\leq 1/30,000$ for newer tankers |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shipyards:** MARPOL dictates hull design (double hull), pipework segregation, and OWS installation.
- **Port Authorities:** Mandates reception facilities for ship-generated waste.
- **Engineers:** Drives selection of marine fuels (VLSFO 0.5% S vs. HFO 3.5% S) and after-treatment systems (SCR for NOx).
- **Classification Societies (IACS):** Verify compliance during surveys and issue statutory certificates.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of MARPOL Annex I Oil Discharge Criteria for Tankers

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A 200,000 DWT oil tanker carries 150,000 tonnes of crude oil. While in transit at 14 knots, the load-on-top process produces 2,000 tonnes of oily ballast water containing an average oil concentration of 8,000 ppm. Determine if the tanker complies with the total oil discharge limit under Regulation 18(7).

**Given:**
- Cargo mass: $m_{cargo} = 150{,}000$ tonnes
- Oily ballast mass: $m_{ballast} = 2{,}000$ tonnes
- Oil concentration in ballast: $C = 8{,}000$ ppm $= 8{,}000 \cdot 10^{-6} = 0.008$
- Vessel speed: $v = 14$ knots

**Step 1 — Calculate the mass of oil to be discharged.**

The oil mass within the oily ballast is calculated by multiplying the total ballast mass by the concentration fraction.

$$
m_{oil} = m_{ballast} \times C
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
m_{oil} = 2{,}000 \text{ tonnes} \times 0.008 = 16 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to litres for the discharge rate comparison.**

Using the standard density of crude oil ($\rho_{oil} = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 0.85 \text{ kg/L}$):

$$
V_{oil} = \frac{m_{oil}}{\rho_{oil}} = \frac{16 \times 1000 \text{ kg}}{0.85 \text{ kg/L}}
$$

$$
V_{oil} = \frac{16{,}000}{0.85} = 18{,}823.5 \text{ litres} \approx 18{,}824 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Total Oil Discharge Limit (1/15,000 rule for existing tankers).**

The MARPOL maximum allowable discharge of oil in relation to cargo carried is:

$$
V_{max} = \frac{V_{cargo}}{15{,}000}
$$

First, compute cargo volume from mass using crude oil density ($\rho_{cargo} = 860 \text{ kg/m}^3$ for typical loading):

$$
V_{cargo} = \frac{150{,}000 \times 1000}{860} = 174{,}418 \text{ m}^3
$$

Convert to litres:

$$
V_{cargo} = 174{,}418 \times 1000 = 174{,}418{,}605 \text{ L}
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_{max} = \frac{174{,}418{,}605}{15{,}000} = 11{,}627.9 \text{ L}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare actual discharge to allowable maximum.**

$$
V_{oil} \text{ (actual)} = 18{,}824 \text{ L}
$$

$$
V_{oil} \text{ (allowable)} = 11{,}628 \text{ L}
$$

Since $18{,}824 \text{ L} > 11{,}628 \text{ L}$, **the tanker does NOT comply** with the total discharge limit.

**Step 5 — Calculate the required oil removal efficiency.**

$$
\eta_{required} = \frac{V_{oil} - V_{max}}{V_{oil}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta_{required} = \frac{18{,}824 - 11{,}628}{18{,}824} \times 100\% = \frac{7{,}196}{18{,}824} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta_{required} \approx 38.23\%
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result.**

The OWS (Oily Water Separator) and additional filtering stages must remove at least **38.23%** of oil before discharge to satisfy MARPOL Annex I. Standard 15 ppm OWS systems easily achieve **>99%** removal, so compliance is feasible through engineered equipment.

### 3.2 Derivation of NOx Emission Index for a Marine Diesel Engine

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A marine four-stroke medium-speed diesel engine operates at $n = 500$ rpm. Calculate the maximum permissible NOx emission index under Tier I standards.

**Given:** $n = 500$ rpm, four-stroke diesel.

**Step 1 — Apply the IMO Tier I formula.**

$$
EI_{NO_x} \leq 11.5 \cdot n^{0.2} \text{ g/kWh}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $n^{0.2}$.**

$$
n^{0.2} = 500^{0.2}
$$

Use logarithms: $\ln(500^{0.2}) = 0.2 \cdot \ln(500) = 0.2 \cdot 6.2146 = 1.2429$

Therefore:

$$
n^{0.2} = e^{1.2429} = 3.4657
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the formula.**

$$
EI_{NO_x}^{max} = 11.5 \times 3.4657 = 39.86 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with Tier II (5.0 g/kWh reduction).**

$$
EI_{NO_x}^{Tier\,II} = 39.86 - 5.0 = 34.86 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with Tier III (NOx ECAs only, 80% reduction from Tier I).**

$$
EI_{NO_x}^{Tier\,III} = 0.20 \times 39.86 = 7.97 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion.**

A 500 rpm four-stroke engine must emit **less than 39.86 g/kWh** in general waters, **34.86 g/kWh** in Tier II regions (Baltic, North Sea for new engines), and **7.97 g/kWh** in NOx ECAs (North Sea, Baltic for new engines post-2021) — necessitating SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction) for Tier III compliance.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — MARPOL Annex Summary

| Annex | Pollutant | Equipment Mandated | Certification | Special Area |
|-------|-----------|--------------------|----------------|---------------|
| I | Oil | OWS, 15 ppm OCM, SBT, COW | IOPP Certificate | Mediterranean, Antarctic, North-West European, Gulf, Caribbean |
| II | NLS | NLS tank wash, pre-wash procedures | NLS Certificate | Antarctic, Baltic |
| III | Packaged Goods | Marking, stowage, packaging | — | — |
| IV | Sewage | Sewage Treatment Plant (STP), Holding Tank | ISPP Certificate | Baltic (special) |
| V | Garbage | Garbage Management Plan, Garbage Record Book | — | Antarctic (zero discharge) |
| VI | Air Pollution | SOx scrubbers, SCR, EU-certified fuels | IAPP Certificate, IEE Certificate | ECA, SECA, NECA |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

Define the term **MARPOL 73/78** and state the major types of pollution regulated under its six Annexes.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**MARPOL 73/78** is the **International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships**, 1973, as modified by the Protocol of 1978. It is the principal international treaty aimed at preventing ship-source pollution of the marine environment, and is administered by the International Maritime Organization (IMO). The six Annexes regulate:

1. **Annex I** — Oil pollution (oily water discharge, ballast, tanker construction).
2. **Annex II** — Noxious Liquid Substances carried in bulk.
3. **Annex III** — Harmful substances in packaged form.
4. **Annex IV** — Sewage from ships.
5. **Annex V** — Garbage (plastics, food waste, cargo residues).
6. **Annex VI** — Air pollution from ships (SOx, NOx, ODS).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** Definition — 1 mark; Annexes listing (any 4-6) — 2 marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

List any **three Special Areas** declared under MARPOL Annex I, and explain why stricter discharge rules apply there.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Three Special Areas under MARPOL Annex I include:

1. **Mediterranean Sea**
2. **Baltic Sea**
3. **Black Sea**
4. **Red Sea**
5. **Gulf of Aden**
6. **Antarctic Area**
7. **North-West European Waters**
8. **Gulf of Mexico / Caribbean**

**Reason for stricter rules:** These are semi-enclosed or ecologically sensitive seas with **restricted water exchange**, **slow flushing rates**, and **high biodiversity**. Routine operational discharges of oil or oily mixtures that would disperse harmlessly in open oceans could accumulate dangerously in these confined waters, causing long-term ecological damage. Hence, MARPOL imposes near-zero discharge limits, requiring retention on board or delivery to port reception facilities.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** Three Special Areas (any 3) — 1½ marks; Reason — 1½ marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, ESE]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Annex I requirements** for the prevention of oil pollution from ships. Discuss the role of the **Oily Water Separator (OWS)** with 15 ppm OCM in compliance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to **MARPOL Annex VI**, describe the regulations governing **SOx emissions**, **NOx emissions**, and the operation of **Emission Control Areas (ECAs)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Part A(a) (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** MARPOL Annex I regulates **operational and accidental discharges of oil and oily mixtures** from ships, including oily bilge water, fuel oil residues, cargo oil residues, and ballast water from oil tankers.

**Constructional Requirements (2 marks):**

1. **Segregated Ballast Tanks (SBT)** — dedicated ballast compartments in tankers that do not contact cargo oil.
2. **Double Hull / Double Bottom** — for oil tankers of 5,000 DWT and above, the hull must have a double skin extending the full cargo tank length.
3. **Crude Oil Washing (COW)** — using crude oil itself to wash cargo tanks, reducing residue.

**Operational Discharge Limits (2 marks):**

For discharges from machinery spaces (bilges):
- Oil content $\leq$ **15 ppm**
- Distance $\geq$ **50 nm from nearest land**

For discharges from cargo tanks of oil tankers:
- **Instantaneous rate** $\leq$ **30 L/nm**
- **Total quantity** $\leq \frac{1}{15,000}$ of cargo carried (or $\frac{1}{30,000}$ for newer tankers)
- Ship must be en route at $\geq 7$ knots; **monitoring & control system** required.

**Role of OWS with 15 ppm OCM (2 marks):**
The **Oily Water Separator (OWS)** separates oil from bilge water via gravity coalescence, supported by filters. The **15 ppm Oil Content Meter (OCM)** continuously samples the effluent. If oil content exceeds 15 ppm, the OCM **automatically blocks overboard discharge** via a three-way valve, recirculating back to the bilge or sludge tank. This engineering safeguard prevents illegal discharge and ensures compliance with MARPOL Regulation 14.

---

#### Model Answer — Part A(b) (7 marks)

**SOx Regulations (2.5 marks):**

MARPOL Annex VI sets a **global sulphur cap of 0.50% m/m** in marine fuel oil (effective 1 Jan 2020, reduced from 3.5%). In **Emission Control Areas (ECAs)**, the limit is tightened to **0.10% m/m**. Compliance is achieved by:
1. Using **Very Low Sulphur Fuel Oil (VLSFO)**.
2. Installing **Exhaust Gas Cleaning Systems (EGCS)** — i.e., scrubbers that remove SOx from exhaust.

**NOx Regulations (2.5 marks):**

Three **Tier standards** apply based on engine build date:
- **Tier I** — $EI_{NO_x} \leq 11.5 \cdot n^{0.2}$ g/kWh (engines installed pre-2000)
- **Tier II** — 5 g/kWh reduction from Tier I (post-2011)
- **Tier III** — 80% reduction from Tier I (post-2016 in NOx ECAs)

Achieved by **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** injecting urea to convert NO → N₂ + H₂O.

**Emission Control Areas (2 marks):**

ECAs include:
- **Baltic Sea, North Sea, North America ECAs** (SOx, NOx)
- **Caribbean Sea ECAs** (SOx only)

Inside ECAs, **stricter SOx (0.10%) and Tier III NOx standards** apply, monitored via **Continuous Emission Monitoring Systems (CEMS)** onboard and **Fuel Oil Bunker Delivery Notes** maintained in the **Bunker Delivery Note (BDN)** record.

---

### Alternative Choice —

**Question B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, ESE]**

**(a)** Discuss the **MARPOL Annex V** regulations regarding **ship-generated garbage**, including classification of garbage, discharge rules, and the role of the **Garbage Management Plan**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **International Convention on Ballast Water Management (BWM Convention)** and its operational implications for ships. Discuss treatment technologies and the **D-2 standard**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Part B(a) (7 marks)

**Garbage Classification under Annex V (2 marks):**

Garbage is categorized to determine discharge rules:

| Category | Examples | Discharge Rule |
|----------|----------|----------------|
| A | Food waste, cargo residues (non-harmful) | Comminuted: ≥4 nm; Whole: ≥12 nm |
| B | Plastics, synthetic ropes, fishing gear | **Zero discharge** anywhere at sea |
| C | Paper, rags, glass, metal | ≥12 nm |
| D | Cargo residues (harmful) | Special rules per Appendix |

**Discharge Rules (2.5 marks):**

- **Plastics** — strictly prohibited from any discharge into the sea.
- **Food waste** — must be comminuted (particle size ≤ 25 mm) and discharged ≥ 4 nm, or whole ≥ 12 nm.
- **Paper, rags, glass, metal** — discharge permitted ≥ 12 nm from nearest land.
- **All garbage** — discharge prohibited inside Special Areas (e.g., Antarctic).

**Garbage Management Plan & Record Book (2.5 marks):**

Every ship ≥ 100 GT and every fixed/ floating platform must maintain a **Garbage Management Plan** describing procedures for collection, storage, processing, and disposal of ship-generated waste, plus port reception facility usage. The **Garbage Record Book** documents every discharge, incineration, or disposal operation, signed by the Master and accessible to Port State Control officers.

---

#### Model Answer — Part B(b) (7 marks)

**BWM Convention Background (2 marks):**

The **International Convention for the Control and Management of Ships' Ballast Water and Sediments (BWM Convention)** entered into force on **8 September 2017**. It aims to prevent the spread of harmful aquatic organisms transported in ships' ballast water from one ecosystem to another (invasive species transfer).

**Operational Requirements (2 marks):**

- **Ballast Water Management Plan (BWMP)** required for every ship.
- **Ballast Water Record Book** to log every ballast operation.
- **Ballast Water Treatment System (BWTS)** required to meet the **D-2 Standard**.
- **International Ballast Water Management Certificate (IBWMC)** issued after survey.

**D-2 Standard & Treatment Technologies (3 marks):**

The **D-2 Biological Discharge Standard** sets maximum permissible concentrations of viable organisms:

| Organism Size | Maximum Concentration |
|---------------|-----------------------|
| $\geq 50 \mu m$ | $\leq 10$ organisms/m³ |
| $10 \text{--} 50 \mu m$ | $\leq 10$ organisms/mL |
| Indicator microbes (Vibrio cholerae, E. coli, Intestinal Enterococci) | Specified limits |

Treatment technologies include:
1. **UV Sterilization** — kills organisms via ultraviolet radiation.
2. **Filtration** — removes organisms ≥ 50 µm.
3. **Chemical Biocide Dosing** — e.g., chlorine-based treatment.
4. **Deoxygenation** — replacing oxygen with nitrogen.
5. **Heat Treatment** — raises water temperature to kill organisms.

**Implication:** All ships must install **Type-Approved BWTS** during scheduled docking cycles — a significant retrofitting cost to the maritime industry.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting Annex numbers** — Always mention *which* Annex you refer to. Stating "MARPOL requires double hull" without "Annex I, Reg. 19" loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Conflating OWS with STP** — OWS handles oil; STP (Sewage Treatment Plant) handles sewage. Do not interchange.
> 3. **Confusing sulphur values** — 0.50% is **Global**, 0.10% is **ECA/SECA only**. Many students write 0.10% globally.
> 4. **Missing the units** — 15 ppm, not 15 mg/L unless clarified (in seawater 1 ppm ≈ 1 mg/L). Always specify.
> 5. **Plastic discharge rule** — Many wrongly write "≥ 25 nm" for plastics; the correct answer is **zero discharge anywhere**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Marine Pollution Prevention and Control**

- ✅ **MARPOL 73/78** — the umbrella treaty; six Annexes regulate distinct pollution categories.
- ✅ **Annex I** — Oil; OWS + 15 ppm OCM + SBT + Double Hull; discharge ≥ 50 nm, ≤ 15 ppm.
- ✅ **Annex II** — NLS in bulk; pre-wash required before discharge.
- ✅ **Annex III** — Marking & stowage for harmful packaged substances.
- ✅ **Annex IV** — Sewage; STP mandatory inside 4 nm; ISPP Certificate.
- ✅ **Annex V** — Garbage; **Plastics = zero discharge**; Garbage Management Plan & Record Book.
- ✅ **Annex VI** — Air pollution; SOx cap 0.50% global, 0.10% ECA; NOx Tier I/II/III.
- ✅ **15 ppm** oil in bilge water — the magic number.
- ✅ **30 L/nm** instantaneous discharge rate (tankers).
- ✅ **4 nm / 12 nm** — sewage distance thresholds.
- ✅ **SBT** — Segregated Ballast Tanks (prevents oil-polluted ballast).
- ✅ **COW** — Crude Oil Washing for tankers.
- ✅ **ECAs / SECAs / NECAs** — emission-controlled sea areas.
- ✅ **VLSFO** (0.5% S) vs **HSFO** (3.5% S) — fuel selection.
- ✅ **SCR** — Selective Catalytic Reduction for Tier III NOx.
- ✅ **BWM Convention** — D-2 Standard: ≤10 organisms/m³ above 50 µm.
- ✅ **Port State Control** vs **Flag State** — who enforces what.
- ✅ **Special Areas** — Mediterranean, Baltic, Antarctic, Red Sea, Caribbean, Gulf.
- ✅ **Reception Facilities** — ports must provide waste-receiving infrastructure.
- ✅ **OPA 90** — US Oil Pollution Act (1990); double-hull mandate for US-trading tankers.
- ✅ **AFS Convention** — Anti-Fouling Systems (TBT ban since 2003).

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Closing Note for KTU 2024 Aspirants:** This topic is a high-yield scoring area in PESFT755 Maritime Safety. The combination of **memory-based numerical thresholds** (15 ppm, 0.50%, 4 nm) and **conceptual clarity on six Annexes** ensures that even partial knowledge fetches 60–70% of the marks. Always structure your long answers using **headings, sub-points, and examples from Special Areas** to satisfy KTU's Outcome-Based Education (OBE) evaluation rubric mapped to **CO4 (Apply maritime pollution prevention principles)** and **CO5 (Evaluate international maritime safety conventions)**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: International Maritime Pollution Treaties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** International Maritime Pollution Treaties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are International Maritime Pollution Treaties?

**International Maritime Pollution Treaties** are legally binding multilateral agreements (conventions, protocols, and annexes) adopted under the auspices of the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**, the **United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP)**, and other intergovernmental bodies. These instruments establish **uniform global standards** to prevent, control, and compensate for pollution of the marine environment caused by ships, offshore platforms, and related maritime activities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (IMO/UNCLOS Framework):**
> *"A treaty in the maritime pollution context is a written international agreement, governed by international law, concluded between sovereign States or International Organizations, which establishes legally enforceable obligations relating to the prevention, reduction, control, and compensation of damage arising from the discharge of substances or energy by vessels into the marine environment."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Global Highway Code for the Oceans"

Imagine the world's oceans as a giant **shared highway** used by every country on Earth. Without traffic rules, ships would dump waste, collide, and pollute freely — harming everyone.

**International Maritime Pollution Treaties are like the Highway Code for the ocean:**
- They are **laws agreed by every nation** that drives a ship.
- The **IMO acts as the global "Department of Motor Vehicles"** that writes the rules.
- **Flag States** (the country where a ship is registered) act like the **license-issuing authority**.
- **Port States** act like **traffic police** who can stop and inspect any ship entering their waters.
- **Coastal States** are the **homeowners along the highway** who suffer when pollution occurs.

Just as you cannot legally drive a car without obeying road rules, no commercial ship can legally operate worldwide without complying with conventions like **MARPOL**, **UNCLOS**, and the **OPRC**.

## 1.3 The "Big Five" Pillars of Maritime Pollution Governance

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following **5 core treaties** under Module 4:

| # | Treaty | Year | Primary Focus |
|---|--------|------|---------------|
| 1 | **UNCLOS** | 1982 | Sovereign rights, EEZ, general duty to protect marine env. |
| 2 | **MARPOL 73/78** | 1973/1978 | Operational & accidental pollution from ships |
| 3 | **London Convention** | 1972 | Dumping of wastes at sea |
| 4 | **OPRC Convention** | 1990 | Preparedness & response to oil spills |
| 5 | **CLC/Fund/Bunker Conventions** | 1969/1992/2001 | Civil liability & compensation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric (KTU 2024):** A vessel is considered a "ship" under these treaties if it is **>= 24 metres in length** or has a **gross tonnage >= 100 GT**, depending on the convention.

## 1.4 Why These Treaties Matter (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Approximately **80% of world trade by volume** moves through the oceans. The shipping industry carries over **3 billion tonnes of oil annually**, making oil spills one of the most catastrophic pollution risks. Treaties create the **legal backbone** that:
> 1. Standardizes discharge rules globally
> 2. Defines the **polluter-pays principle**
> 3. Establishes **liability caps and compensation funds**
> 4. Mandates **port State control** enforcement
> 5. Protects **Special Areas** like the Mediterranean, Baltic, and Antarctic

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Treaties Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 UNCLOS — United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (1982)

UNCLOS is often called the **"Constitution for the Oceans."** It provides the **overarching legal framework** within which all specialized pollution treaties operate.

### Key Pollution-Related Articles:

- **Article 192** — *General obligation*: States have a sovereign duty to **protect and preserve the marine environment**.
- **Article 194** — States must take all necessary measures to **prevent, reduce, and control pollution** from any source (vessels, land-based, seabed, dumping, or airborne).
- **Article 211** — *Pollution from vessels*: Empowers States to adopt **laws and regulations** for vessel-source pollution that are **no less effective** than generally accepted international rules (i.e., **MARPOL**).
- **Article 217** — *Flag State enforcement*: The State whose flag a ship flies is primarily responsible for **enforcing anti-pollution rules** on that vessel, anywhere in the world.
- **Article 218** — *Port State enforcement*: A port State may investigate and institute proceedings for **any violation of pollution rules** committed outside its territorial waters.
- **Article 220** — *Coastal State enforcement*: Within its EEZ, the coastal State may enforce against **violations resulting in pollution** of its waters.
- **Article 235** — *Responsibility and liability*: States are responsible for ensuring **prompt and adequate compensation** for marine pollution damage.

> [!NOTE]
> UNCLOS does **not** create technical standards — it delegates technical rule-making to specialized agencies, principally the **IMO** (for ships) and the **London Convention/Protocol** (for dumping).

## 2.2 MARPOL 73/78 — The "Master Convention"

**MARPOL** = *International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships*.

It is the most important convention in the syllabus. It is a **framework convention** with **6 Annexes**, each addressing a different type of pollution.

### MARPOL Annexes Structure (KTU High-Yield):

| Annex | Subject | Key Threshold/Rule | KTU 2024 Status |
|-------|---------|--------------------|-----------------|
| **I** | Oil | **15 ppm** oil-in-water max discharge; **OIL RECORD BOOK** mandatory | In force |
| **II** | Noxious Liquid Substances (NLS) in bulk | 4 categories (X, Y, Z, OS) | In force |
| **III** | Packaged Harmful Substances | **Marine Pollutant (MP)** marking | In force |
| **IV** | Sewage | Discharge beyond **3 nm** allowed via approved plant | In force |
| **V** | Garbage | **ZERO discharge** of plastics (strictest rule) | In force |
| **VI** | Air Pollution | **Sulphur cap = 0.50%** (since 2020) | In force |

### The "Special Areas" Concept

A **Special Area** is a sea area where, due to its oceanographic and ecological conditions and the particular character of its sea traffic, the adoption of **mandatory special mandatory methods for the prevention of sea pollution** is required.

**KTU-Mandated Special Areas to Remember:**

- **Annex I (Oil):** Mediterranean Sea, Baltic Sea, Black Sea, Red Sea, Gulfs area, Antarctic Area, North-West European Waters
- **Annex V (Garbage):** Mediterranean, Baltic, Black, Red, North Sea, Antarctic, Wider Caribbean
- **Annex VI (Air):** Baltic Sea, North Sea, North American ECA, US Caribbean ECA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OIL RECORD BOOK (ORB)** — Every ship of **>= 400 GT** must maintain an ORB in Part I (Machinery) and Part II (Cargo/Ballast). It is a **legal document** — false entries are a **criminal offense** under most flag States.

## 2.3 London Convention 1972 & London Protocol 1996

The **Convention on the Prevention of Marine Pollution by Dumping of Wastes and Other Matter (LC 1972)** is the **first global treaty** to address marine pollution.

### Core Mechanism: The "Black-Grey-White List"

| List | Permitted? | Examples |
|------|-----------|----------|
| **Black List** | ❌ **Banned** outright | Mercury, cadmium, persistent plastics, high-level radioactive waste, DDT, organochlorines |
| **Grey List** | ⚠️ Permitted with **special permit** | Arsenic, lead, copper, zinc, cyanides, low-level radioactive waste |
| **White List** | ✅ Permitted with **general permit** | Dredged material, sewage sludge, fish waste, ships, platforms |

The **1996 Protocol** is **more restrictive** — it inverts the system using a **"reverse list"** (everything is banned except substances on a permitted list).

## 2.4 OPRC — Oil Pollution Preparedness, Response and Co-operation (1990)

Adopted after the **Exxon Valdez (1989)** and **Nestucca (1988)** disasters, the OPRC addresses **emergency response**.

### Core Obligations on Parties:

1. **Ships & Offshore Platforms** must carry an **approved Shipboard Oil Pollution Emergency Plan (SOPEP)**.
2. **Sea Ports & Oil Handling Facilities** must prepare a **Port Oil Pollution Emergency Plan**.
3. States must maintain a **national contingency plan** with minimum stockpiles of dispersants and equipment.
4. States must **cooperate internationally** in spill response (information exchange, mutual aid).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **1990 OPRC** is for **oil only**. The **2000 OPRC-HNS Protocol** extends the same framework to **Hazardous and Noxious Substances**.

## 2.5 Liability & Compensation Conventions

These three conventions form the **international liability regime** for oil pollution damage:

| Convention | Year | Coverage | Limits |
|------------|------|----------|--------|
| **CLC 1969** | 1969 (rev. 1992) | Shipowner's strict liability for oil spills from tankers | Up to **89.77 SDR** per GRT (1992 limits) |
| **Fund Convention 1992** | 1992 | Supplementary compensation to victims | Up to **135 million SDR** per incident |
| **Bunker Convention 2001** | 2001 | Liability for spills of **bunker fuel** from non-tankers (e.g., container ships) | Per global limitation rules |

**Key Principle:** **Strict Liability** — the shipowner is liable **even without fault** (subject to limited exceptions like Act of God, war, or third-party intentional damage).

### KTU Formula Sheet — Compensation Calculation Table
