The CLC/Fund regimes are the only conventions in this module with **numeric formulae**. The **limitation of liability (L)** for a tanker under the **1992 CLC** is calculated as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 4.51 \times \text{SDR/GRT} \times \text{GRT (up to 5,000)} \\
&\quad + 631 \times \text{SDR/GRT} \times \text{GRT (5,000 to 140,000)} \\
&\quad + 89.77 \times \text{SDR/GRT} \times \text{GRT (above 140,000)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **SDR** = Special Drawing Rights (IMF currency unit). The 1992 CLC also imposes a **maximum ceiling of 89.77 million SDR**.

**Rule on the pipe `|x|`:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` in any future math blocks. Example: The maximum liability $\vert L \vert \le 89.77 \text{ million SDR}$.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- Naval architects design ships to **comply with MARPOL Annex VI** (scrubbers, LNG fuel).
- Port engineers design **reception facilities** per MARPOL Art. 38.
- Marine surveyors audit **SOPEPs** for compliance.
- Offshore engineers apply **London Protocol rules** for decommissioning waste.
- Insurers use **CLC/Fund limits** to calculate P&I premiums.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Treaty Hierarchy (KTU High-Yield)

Below is a structured matrix showing how pollution treaties **interlock**:

| Level | Treaty | Function | Who Enforces? |
|-------|--------|----------|---------------|
| **Constitutional** | **UNCLOS 1982** | Sets sovereign rights & general duty | UN, ITLOS |
| **Technical Operational** | **MARPOL 73/78** | Sets technical discharge standards | Flag State + Port State |
| **Dumping** | **London Convention/Protocol** | Controls waste disposal at sea | National authorities |
| **Emergency** | **OPRC 1990** | Mandates preparedness & response | Coastal + Flag States |
| **Financial** | **CLC 1992, Fund 1992, Bunker 2001** | Compensation to victims | Domestic courts + IOPC Fund |

> [!NOTE]
> This **pyramid of enforcement** is a frequently-asked KTU 2024 question: *"Explain the relationship between UNCLOS, MARPOL, and OPRC."*

## 3.2 Worked Example — CLC Liability Calculation (14-Mark Style)

### **Question:**
A bulk carrier of **150,000 GRT** is involved in an oil spill. Using the 1992 CLC, calculate the **shipowner's maximum liability** and state whether the **1992 Fund Convention** will provide additional compensation if total damage is **150 million SDR**.

### **Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1: Identify the applicable tier.**
Since GRT = 150,000, we use the **"above 140,000 GRT"** tier of the 1992 CLC formula.

**Step 2: Apply the 1992 CLC formula for the first 5,000 GRT.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 &= 4.51 \times 5{,}000 \\
    &= 22{,}550 \text{ SDR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the formula for GRT from 5,000 to 140,000 (range = 135,000 GRT).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_2 &= 631 \times 135{,}000 \\
    &= 85{,}185{,}000 \text{ SDR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the formula for the remaining GRT above 140,000 (range = 10,000 GRT).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_3 &= 89.77 \times 10{,}000 \\
    &= 897{,}700 \text{ SDR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Sum the liabilities.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= L_1 + L_2 + L_3 \\
          &= 22{,}550 + 85{,}185{,}000 + 897{,}700 \\
          &= 86{,}105{,}250 \text{ SDR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Check the statutory ceiling.**

The 1992 CLC caps liability at **89.77 million SDR**. Since $L_{total} = 86{,}105{,}250 \text{ SDR} < 89{,}770{,}000 \text{ SDR}$, the cap does not bind. Liability is **86,105,250 SDR**.

**Step 7: Determine Fund Convention contribution.**

Damage = 150,000,000 SDR; Shipowner pays 86,105,250 SDR; **Uncompensated damage** = 63,894,750 SDR.

The 1992 Fund Convention covers compensation **between shipowner's liability and the Fund's ceiling of 135 million SDR**. Since 86.1 M SDR < 150 M SDR < 86.1 M + 135 M SDR, the **Fund will pay the 63.89 million SDR gap** (subject to admissibility of claims).

### **Final Answer:**

| Item | Amount (SDR) |
|------|--------------|
| Shipowner's liability | 86,105,250 |
| Fund compensation | 63,894,750 |
| Total | 150,000,000 |

## 3.3 Annex VI — Fuel Sulphur Compliance Derivation

MARPOL Annex VI sets a global sulphur cap in marine fuel oil. The current global limit is **0.50% m/m**, with stricter **0.10%** in **Emission Control Areas (ECAs)**.

To estimate the **fuel-switching compliance** cost differential, the effective mass flow is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{SO}_2} &= F \times S \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $F$ = fuel consumption in **tonnes/hour**
- $S$ = sulphur content **fraction** (e.g., 0.005 for 0.50%)
- $2.0 \times 10^{-3}$ = conversion factor ($\text{SO}_2 / \text{S} = 64/32 = 2$)

**Example:** A main engine burns 50 t/h of HFO with 3.5% sulphur vs. ULSFO with 0.10% sulphur.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{HFO}} &= 50 \times 0.035 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \\
              &= 3.50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ t/h SO}_2 \\[4pt]
M_{\text{ULSFO}} &= 50 \times 0.001 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \\
                 &= 1.00 \times 10^{-4} \text{ t/h SO}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reduction in $\text{SO}_2$ emissions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta M &= 3.50 \times 10^{-3} - 1.00 \times 10^{-4} \\
         &= 3.40 \times 10^{-3} \text{ t/h SO}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

This represents a **97% reduction** — the engineering rationale behind the **ECA** concept.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Pyramid of Maritime Pollution Treaties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    unclosA[UNCLOS 1982]:::constitution --> marpolB[MARPOL 73 or 78]:::technical
    unclosA --> londonC[London Convention 1972]:::dumping
    unclosA --> oprcD[OPRC 1990]:::emergency
    marpolB --> annex1[Annex I OIL]:::annex
    marpolB --> annex2[Annex II NLS]:::annex
    marpolB --> annex3[Annex III PACKAGED]:::annex
    marpolB --> annex4[Annex IV SEWAGE]:::annex
    marpolB --> annex5[Annex V GARBAGE]:::annex
    marpolB --> annex6[Annex VI AIR]:::annex
    marpolB --> clcE[CLC 1992]:::liability
    marpolB --> fundF[Fund 1992]:::liability
    marpolB --> bunkG[Bunker 2001]:::liability
    oprcD --> oprcH[HNS Protocol 2000]:::emergency
    londonC --> londonP[London Protocol 1996]:::dumping
    annex5 --> specialAreaX[Special Areas]:::special
    annex6 --> ecaY[ECA Zones]:::special
    clcE --> iopcZ[IOPC Funds Office]:::liability

    classDef constitution fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef technical fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef dumping fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef emergency fill:#c62828,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef annex fill:#558b2f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef liability fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef special fill:#00838f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — MARPOL Annex VI Compliance Flow (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fuelSupplyA[Fuel Oil Supply]:::input --> engineB[Main Engine Combustion]:::process
    scrubberC[EGCS Scrubber Option]:::process -.->|alternative| engineB
    lngD[LNG Fuel Option]:::process -.->|alternative| engineB
    engineB --> exhaustE[Exhaust Gas]:::output
    exhaustE --> ecaCheckF{Within ECA Zone?}:::decision
    ecaCheckF -->|Yes| applyTen[S Limit equals 0.10 percent]:::output
    ecaCheckF -->|No| applyFifty[S Limit equals 0.50 percent]:::output
    applyTen --> monitoringG[Continuous SO2 Monitoring]:::process
    applyFifty --> monitoringG
    monitoringG --> nonComplianceH{Non Compliance?}:::decision
    nonComplianceH -->|Yes| sanctionsI[Detention plus Fine]:::output
    nonComplianceH -->|No| cleanOpsJ[Compliant Operations]:::output

    classDef input fill:#0277bd,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef process fill:#4527a0,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef decision fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000
    classDef output fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — OPRC Emergency Response Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    spillA[Oil Spill Detected]:::start --> notifyB[Master Reports to Flag State and Coastal State]:::process
    notifyB --> sopepC[Activate SOPEP on Ship]:::process
    sopepC --> portPlanD[Activate Port Plan if in Port]:::process
    portPlanD --> nationalE[Activate National Contingency Plan]:::process
    nationalE --> intlF[Notify OPRC Contact Points]:::process
    intlF --> mutualAidG[Request Mutual Aid from Neighbouring States]:::process
    mutualAidG --> containmentH[Containment and Recovery Operations]:::process
    containmentH --> assessI[Damage Assessment]:::process
    assessI --> claimJ[CLC and Fund Claims]:::output
    claimJ --> payoutK[Compensation Disbursed]:::end

    classDef start fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef process fill:#1565c0,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef output fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef end fill:#4a148c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*State any three obligations of a State Party under the MARPOL Convention.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The State must ensure that ships flying its flag are properly constructed, equipped, and operated to **prevent pollution** (MARPOL Art. 1–5).
2. The State must ensure that **adequate reception facilities** for residues and oily mixtures are provided at ports and terminals (Art. 38).
3. The State must **report incidents** involving the discharge of harmful substances to other affected States and the IMO (Art. 8).
*(Award 1 mark per point; full sentence form required.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is a "Special Area" under MARPOL? Give two examples.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Special Area** is a sea area identified by MARPOL Annexes as requiring **more stringent discharge controls** because of its fragile oceanographic, ecological, or traffic characteristics. The discharge of certain pollutants is either **banned outright** or restricted to a higher standard than elsewhere.

*Examples (any two):*
- The **Baltic Sea** (Annex I — Oil)
- The **Mediterranean Sea** (Annex V — Garbage)
- The **Antarctic Area** (Annexes I, IV, V)
*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark per example, 0.5 for each partial credit.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A: MARPOL Convention (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — [7 Marks]**
*Explain the structure of MARPOL 73/78. List all six Annexes and describe the discharge standards for **Annex I (Oil)** and **Annex V (Garbage)**.*

**Model Answer:**

**Structure of MARPOL 73/78 [3 marks]:**
- Adopted in **1973 (Washington)**, modified by the **1978 Protocol (London)**.
- Operates as a **framework convention** — Articles set general obligations, while technical rules are in **Annexes**.
- A State may become a Party to the Articles + only those Annexes it chooses (a unique **"opt-in annex"** structure).
- Currently has **6 Annexes**; a new **Annex VII** (on EEDI) was proposed but not adopted.

**Annex I — Oil [2 marks]:**
- Applies to all **oil tankers** and ships of **>= 400 GT**.
- **Oily water discharge** must pass through an **Oily Water Separator (OWS)** or filtering equipment with **15 ppm** monitor.
- **SOPEP/Oil Record Book** mandatory.
- **Special Areas:** Discharge completely banned in Antarctic; severely restricted in other Special Areas.

**Annex V — Garbage [2 marks]:**
- **Zero discharge of plastics** everywhere — the strictest rule.
- Food waste may be discharged beyond **3 nm**, ground to <25 mm.
- Garbage Record Book on ships of **>= 100 GT** or carrying >15 persons.
- Special Areas further restrict discharge of domestic waste.

---

#### **Part (b) — [7 Marks]**
*Describe the **Sulphur Cap regulations** under MARPOL Annex VI. What is an **Emission Control Area (ECA)**, and list any two existing ECAs.*

**Model Answer:**

**Sulphur Cap under MARPOL Annex VI [3 marks]:**
- Adopted in **1997**, entered into force in **2005**.
- **Global sulphur cap** in marine fuel oil: **0.50% m/m** (from 2020; previously 3.50%).
- **ECA sulphur cap**: **0.10% m/m** (more stringent).
- Applies to all **ships > 400 GT** and platforms.
- Compliance methods: **Low-sulphur fuel**, **LNG**, **scrubbers (EGCS)**, or **dual-fuel** engines.

**Emission Control Area (ECA) [2 marks]:**
An ECA is a sea area designated under MARPOL Annex VI where **more stringent controls** apply to $\text{SO}_x$, $\text{NO}_x$, and **PM (particulate matter)** emissions, due to ecological sensitivity or population exposure.

**Two existing ECAs (any two) [2 marks]:**
- **Baltic Sea ECA** (in force 2006)
- **North Sea ECA** (in force 2007)
- **North American ECA** (US + Canada, in force 2012)
- **US Caribbean ECA** (in force 2014)
- **Mediterranean ECA** (proposed 2024)

---

### **Question B: OPRC & Liability Conventions (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Part (a) — [7 Marks]**
*Explain the key provisions of the **OPRC Convention 1990**. How does the **OPRC-HNS Protocol 2000** extend its scope?*

**Model Answer:**

**OPRC 1990 — Key Provisions [5 marks]:**
1. **SOPEP Requirement:** Every oil tanker of **>= 150 GT** and other ships of **>= 400 GT** must carry a **Shipboard Oil Pollution Emergency Plan** approved by the flag State.
2. **Port & Terminal Plans:** Ports, terminals, and oil-handling facilities must maintain **Oil Pollution Emergency Plans** with response equipment.
3. **National Contingency Plan:** Parties must maintain a **national system** for prompt response to oil spills, including stockpiles of dispersants and booms.
4. **International Co-operation:** Parties must exchange information, promote R&D, and provide **mutual assistance** in major spills.
5. **Reporting:** Masters of vessels must report oil spills **without delay** to nearest coastal State.

**OPRC-HNS Protocol 2000 [2 marks]:**
The 2000 Protocol **extends the OPRC framework** to **Hazardous and Noxious Substances (HNS)** — chemicals, gases, and other non-oil pollutants. It mirrors OPRC obligations for emergency preparedness and response, and is the basis for the **HNS Convention 1996** (liability and compensation for HNS damage).

---

#### **Part (b) — [7 Marks]**
*Discuss the **Civil Liability Convention (CLC) 1992** and the **International Fund Convention 1992** as mechanisms for compensating victims of oil pollution damage.*

**Model Answer:**

**CLC 1992 — Shipowner's Liability [3 marks]:**
- Applies to **oil pollution damage** from **sea-going tankers carrying persistent oil as cargo**.
- Imposes **strict liability** on the registered shipowner.
- Liability is **channelled exclusively** to the shipowner (defendants are limited in number).
- Liability limits based on **GRT** (as derived in Section 3.2).
- Shipowner must maintain **compulsory insurance** (Blue Card) and obtain a **CLC Certificate**.

**Fund Convention 1992 — Supplementary Compensation [3 marks]:**
- Established the **International Oil Pollution Compensation Fund (IOPC Funds)** in London.
- Fund compensates victims who **cannot obtain full compensation** from the shipowner (insolvency, liability cap reached, or damage exceeds cap).
- Funded by **contributions from oil receivers** (oil companies importing crude in each Member State).
- Maximum compensation: **135 million SDR** per incident (1992 Fund); supplemented by the **2003 Supplementary Fund** to **750 million SDR** for parties to it.

**Relationship [1 mark]:**
The two conventions operate as a **two-tier system** — CLC covers the **primary** loss up to shipowner's cap, while the Fund provides **additional** compensation up to its ceiling, ensuring victims are made whole.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating MARPOL Annexes with Conventions** — Always specify "Annex I of MARPOL 73/78," not just "MARPOL." Annex numbering is a **direct exam question** every year.
> 2. **Confusing CLC and Fund** — Many students think both are paid by the shipowner. The **Fund is paid by oil importers**, not the shipowner.
> 3. **Missing the year of amendment** — MARPOL 1973/78, CLC **1992** (revised), UNCLOS **1982** — examiners require **specific years**.
> 4. **Ignoring "Special Areas"** — These appear in **70% of ESE questions** on MARPOL.
> 5. **Not mentioning the **polluter-pays principle**** — It is the **philosophical foundation** of every pollution treaty (UNCLOS Art. 235, OPRC, London Protocol).
> 6. **Forgetting compulsory insurance** — CLC and Bunker Conventions both require **Blue Cards / Certificates**. Examiners award **1 mark** for this routinely.
> 7. **Citing CLC limits incorrectly** — The formula has **three tiers**; mis-stating the ceiling (89.77 million SDR) is a common error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4 (International Maritime Pollution Treaties)**

- ✅ **UNCLOS 1982** = "Constitution of the Oceans"; Art. 192, 211, 217, 220, 235 are key.
- ✅ **MARPOL 73/78** = 6 Annexes; remember **"ONPSGA"** mnemonic (Oil, NLS, Packaged, Sewage, Garbage, Air).
- ✅ **Annex I:** 15 ppm discharge limit; **OIL RECORD BOOK**; tankers must have **SOPEP**.
- ✅ **Annex V:** **ZERO plastics** discharge anywhere — the strictest rule in the convention.
- ✅ **Annex VI:** Global S = **0.50%**; ECA S = **0.10%**; ECAs = Baltic, North Sea, N. America, Caribbean.
- ✅ **London Convention 1972:** Black/Grey/White list system; 1996 Protocol uses **reverse list**.
- ✅ **OPRC 1990:** SOPEP for tankers **>= 150 GT** and ships **>= 400 GT**; OPRC-HNS 2000 for chemicals.
- ✅ **CLC 1992:** Strict liability of shipowner; GRT-based formula; **89.77 million SDR** cap.
- ✅ **Fund 1992:** Paid by oil importers; **135 million SDR** ceiling; 2003 Supplementary Fund adds up to **750 million SDR**.
- ✅ **Bunker 2001:** Liability for **bunker oil** from non-tankers (e.g., container ships).
- ✅ **Polluter-Pays Principle** is the philosophical bedrock of all these treaties.
- ✅ **Flag State vs Port State vs Coastal State** — Know the **enforcement** distinction.
- ✅ **Compulsory Insurance (Blue Card)** required under CLC, Fund, and Bunker Conventions.
- ✅ **Recent updates:** MARPOL Annex VI GHG regulations (2023); Mediterranean ECA proposal; Hong Kong Convention for ship recycling (2009).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MARPOL Convention and Its Annexes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** MARPOL Convention and Its Annexes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **MARPOL Convention** (full title: *International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships*) is the principal international treaty governing the prevention of pollution of the marine environment by ships from operational or accidental causes. It is officially known as **MARPOL 73/78** because it was originally adopted on **2 November 1973** at the International Maritime Organization (IMO) and subsequently modified by the **Protocol of 1978** (relating thereto). The combined instrument, MARPOL 73/78, entered into force on **2 November 1983**.

The Convention is structured into **twenty-eight (28) Articles** and **six (6) Annexes**, each Annex addressing a specific category of ship-source pollution. Of the six Annexes, **five are mandatory** (I, II, IV, V, and VI) and **one is optional** (Annex III).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** MARPOL 73/78 is administered by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**, a specialized agency of the United Nations headquartered in **London, United Kingdom**. It is the most important international treaty concerning maritime environmental protection.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a global city with **millions of floating houses (ships)** sailing through shared streets (oceans). Just as a city has municipal bylaws — *"don't dump trash in the park," "don't release sewage into the river," "don't burn garbage in your backyard"* — the oceans need a universal set of rules so that **no single ship pollutes the water or air that belongs to everyone**.

**MARPOL is the global "rulebook" for ships**, written jointly by nations through the IMO, and enforced by:
- The **Flag State** (the country where the ship is registered — like a ship's home state).
- The **Port State** (the country whose port the ship visits — like a local inspector).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Memory Hook:** Think of MARPOL as the **"MARine POLlution Police."** Each Annex is a different "law" against a different type of pollution (oil, chemicals, garbage, sewage, air emissions).

### 1.3 Historical Evolution

| Year | Event | Significance |
|------|-------|--------------|
| 1973 | MARPOL Convention adopted at IMO | First comprehensive anti-pollution treaty |
| 1978 | Protocol adopted (Tanker Safety and Pollution Prevention) | Addressed 1970s tanker accidents (e.g., *Amoco Cadiz*) |
| 1983 | MARPOL 73/78 entered into force | Became binding international law |
| 1997 | Annex VI (Air Pollution) adopted | Added airborne emissions to scope |
| 2005 | Annex VI entered into force | Sulfur cap, NOx limits introduced |
| 2020 | **IMO 2020 Sulfur Cap** (0.50% m/m) | Reduced sulfur in marine fuel by ~80% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** MARPOL is a **living instrument** — its Annexes are continuously updated through resolutions adopted at **MEPC** (Marine Environment Protection Committee) sessions. Students must remember that **MEPC**, not the IMO Assembly, is the primary amendment body for MARPOL.

---

### 1.4 Key Definitions (High-Yield)

- **"Discharge"** — any release from a ship, however caused, including escape, disposal, spilling, leaking, pumping, or emptying.
- **"Special Area"** — a sea area where, for recognized technical reasons in relation to its oceanographical and ecological condition and the particular character of its sea traffic, the adoption of **mandatory special methods for the prevention of sea pollution** is required (e.g., the Mediterranean Sea, Baltic Sea, Antarctic Area).
- **"Oil Record Book"** — a mandatory log documenting all oil transfer and disposal operations.
- **"IOPP Certificate"** — *International Oil Pollution Prevention Certificate* issued under Annex I.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Pillars — Annex Architecture

The Convention follows a **modular design**: a single "umbrella" treaty (the Articles) with six technical Annexes that may be ratified independently. This allows countries to accede to specific pollution controls according to their fleet composition and economic capacity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Concept Core:** A state becomes a **Party to MARPOL** only by ratifying the main Convention. Once a Party, it is **automatically bound by Annex I and Annex II** (mandatory). It may opt out of Annexes III, IV, V, and VI only at the time of accession or by following the formal amendment procedure.

### 2.2 Structural Logic — The "Why" Behind Each Step

1. **Why modular Annexes?** → Different ship types face different pollution risks. Bulk carriers don't carry oil; tankers don't carry passengers. Tailoring rules to actual operational risks makes the Convention practical.
2. **Why Special Areas?** → Enclosed or semi-enclosed seas (Mediterranean, Baltic) have limited water exchange; pollution accumulates faster, so stricter standards apply.
3. **Why Flag State + Port State enforcement?** → Ships spend most of their time in international waters beyond any single nation's jurisdiction. Dual enforcement closes the loopholes.

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Data Sheet

Since MARPOL is regulatory (not mathematical), the "formulas" are **operational limits, thresholds, and standards**. This is the high-yield data table for exam preparation:

| **Annex** | **Pollution Type** | **Key Standard / Threshold** | **Entered into Force** | **Mandatory?** |
|-----------|--------------------|------------------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| **Annex I** | **Oil / Oily Water** | Discharge limit: oil content $\leq 15$ ppm | 2 Nov 1983 | ✅ Yes |
| **Annex II** | **Noxious Liquid Substances (NLS)** | Categorized: X, Y, Z, OS (Other Substances) | 6 Apr 1987 | ✅ Yes |
| **Annex III** | **Packaged Harmful Substances** | Marking, labelling, stowage, documentation | 1 Jul 1992 | ❌ Optional |
| **Annex IV** | **Sewage** | Discharge $\geq 12$ NM from nearest land (or via comminuting/disinfecting system) | 27 Sep 2003 | ✅ Yes |
| **Annex V** | **Garbage** | Total ban on plastics disposal at sea | 31 Dec 1988 | ✅ Yes |
| **Annex VI** | **Air Pollution** | Sulfur cap: **0.50% m/m** (global), **0.10%** (ECA) | 19 May 2005 | ✅ Yes |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

For a Marine Engineer or Naval Architect, MARPOL compliance dictates:
- **Engine room design** — Oily Water Separators (OWS) with **15 ppm** monitors and Sludge tanks.
- **Sewage systems** — Holding tanks, sewage treatment plants (STPs), comminuters.
- **Fuel systems** — Fuel changeover arrangements for **Emission Control Areas (ECAs)**.
- **Incinerator specifications** — Limited to specific waste categories per Annex V.
- **Operational documentation** — Oil Record Book (Part I & II), Garbage Management Plan, IOPP Certificate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why It Matters in Industry:** A single MARPOL violation can result in fines of **hundreds of thousands of US dollars**, ship detention, and criminal liability of the Master. Compliance is non-negotiable in commercial shipping.

---

### 2.5 Special Areas — Stricter Rules

| Special Area | Annex | Stricter Rule |
|--------------|-------|----------------|
| Mediterranean Sea | I, V | No oil discharge; no garbage |
| Baltic Sea | I, II, IV, V, VI | Comprehensive restrictions |
| Black Sea | I, V | Sewage: holding tank only |
| Antarctic Area | I, IV, V | Total prohibition of oil/sewage/garbage |
| North Sea | V | Garbage restrictions |
| Caribbean Sea (Wider Caribbean) | I, IV, V | Oil/sewage/garbage |
| North West European Waters | VI | SOx Emission Control Area (SECA) |
| Baltic & North Sea | VI | NOx Tier standards |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert:** Students frequently confuse **SOx ECA** with **NOx ECA**. Currently, **NOx Tier III** applies only in the **North American ECA** and **Caribbean Sea (US Caribbean) ECA**, not globally.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Annex-by-Annex Analysis

This section provides the **exhaustive analytical breakdown** of each Annex — the operational logic, compliance pathway, and certification requirements. Since MARPOL is procedural law rather than an equation, the "derivation" here is the **logical progression of regulatory rules**.

---

#### **ANNEX I — OIL POLLUTION PREVENTION (Operational + Accidental)**

**Step 1: Scope Definition**
- Applies to **all oil cargoes** (persistent and non-persistent) and **engine room bilge water / sludge**.

**Step 2: Categorization of Ships**
- **Oil Tanker** = a ship constructed for carriage of oil in bulk, including combination carriers and NLS tankers.
- **Ship other than oil tanker** = all other vessels (bulk carriers, container ships, etc.).

**Step 3: Operational Discharge Standards (from an oil tanker)**

A tanker may discharge oily water **only if ALL the following conditions are met simultaneously**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Condition 1:} \quad & \text{More than 50 nautical miles from the nearest land} \\
\text{Condition 2:} \quad & \text{Instantaneous rate of discharge} \leq 30 \text{ litres per nautical mile} \\
\text{Condition 3:} \quad & \text{Total quantity discharged} \leq \frac{1}{15{,}000} \text{ of the total cargo carried} \\
\text{Condition 4:} \quad & \text{Oil content of effluent} \leq 15 \text{ ppm} \\
\text{Condition 5:} \quad & \text{Tanker has in operation the SBT/CBT/PL piping system (or equivalent)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** These five conditions must be **simultaneously satisfied**. If even one fails, the discharge is illegal.

**Step 4: Accidental Discharge Prevention**
- Double hull / double bottom construction.
- Segregated Ballast Tanks (SBT), Crude Oil Washing (COW), Inert Gas System (IGS).
- The **"Accidental Oil Outflow Performance Standard"** formula — historical, but KTU students should know MARPOL mandated phasing out of single-hull tankers after the *Erika* (1999) and *Prestige* (2002) disasters.

**Step 5: Certification**
- **IOPP Certificate** + **Oil Record Book (Part I — Machinery)** + **Oil Record Book (Part II — Cargo/Ballast)**.

---

#### **ANNEX II — NOXIOUS LIQUID SUBSTANCES IN BULK**

**Step 1: Categorization of NLS**
- **Category X** — Major hazard; prohibited from any discharge into the marine environment.
- **Category Y** — Hazard; must meet strict conditions.
- **Category Z** — Minor hazard; less stringent conditions.
- **Category OS** — Other Substances; lowest priority.

**Step 2: Pre-Wash Requirement**
- For Categories X, Y, Z, the tanks must undergo **Pre-Wash Procedures** in accordance with the **P&A Manual (Procedures and Arrangements Manual)** before the ship sails to the next port.

**Step 3: Operational Discharge**
- Discharge of NLS residues is permitted only via an **underwater discharge outlet** (above the waterline, in some cases) at a controlled rate, distance, and depth.

---

#### **ANNEX III — PACKAGED HARMFUL SUBSTANCES (OPTIONAL)**

- Applies to substances carried in **packaged form** (containers, drums, etc.) — identified by the **IMDG Code** (International Maritime Dangerous Goods Code).
- Requires proper **marking, labelling, and stowage**.
- The **Marine Pollutant Mark** (a triangle with a fish and tree symbol) must be displayed on packages.
- Since optional, India (a major maritime state) has ratified it.

---

#### **ANNEX IV — SEWAGE FROM SHIPS**

**Discharge Standards:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Standard A:} \quad & \text{Discharge $\geq 12$ NM from nearest land} \\
\text{Standard B:} \quad & \text{Discharge at any distance IF using approved comminuting/disinfecting system} \\
\text{Standard C:} \quad & \text{Discharge in Special Areas only via holding tank or treated system} \\
\text{Threshold:} \quad & \text{Holding tank capacity $\geq 38$ litres/person/day (new ships)}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Sewage Treatment Plant (STP)** must be type-approved.
- **Sewage holding tank** required on all ships $\geq 400$ GT or certified to carry $>15$ persons.

---

#### **ANNEX V — GARBAGE FROM SHIPS**

The **most commonly violated Annex** in practice. The discharge rules are summarized in the KTU examination matrix:

| **Garbage Type** | **Outside Special Areas** | **Within Special Areas** |
|------------------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| Plastic (incl. synthetic ropes, fishing nets) | **Total ban** | **Total ban** |
| Food waste (comminuted) | $\geq 3$ NM from land | **Total ban** |
| Food waste (not comminuted) | $\geq 12$ NM from land | **Total ban** |
| Domestic waste (paper, rags, glass, metal) | $\geq 12$ NM from land | **Total ban** |
| Cargo residues (non-harmful) | $\geq 12$ NM | **Total ban** |

- **Garbage Record Book** mandatory.
- **Garbage Management Plan** mandatory.
- **Placards** for crew awareness.

---

#### **ANNEX VI — AIR POLLUTION FROM SHIPS**

**Step 1: Fuel Sulfur Cap**
- Global sulfur content of marine fuel: **$\leq 0.50\%$ m/m** (since 1 March 2020).
- Within ECAs: **$\leq 0.10\%$ m/m**.

**Step 2: NOx Emission Tiers (for diesel engines)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tier I:} \quad & \text{Pre-2000 engines; baseline standards} \\
\text{Tier II:} \quad & \text{Engines installed on or after 1 Jan 2011; ~20\% reduction vs Tier I} \\
\text{Tier III:} \quad & \text{Engines installed on or after 1 Jan 2016 in NOx ECA; ~80\% reduction vs Tier I}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Other Controls**
- **Ozone-Depleting Substances (ODS)** — banned (Halons, CFCs).
- **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOC)** — controls for tankers carrying crude oil.
- **Ships' Fuel Oil Consumption Database** — mandatory reporting since 2019 (Data Collection System - DCS).
- **Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII)** — introduced 2023, rating ships A–E.

**Step 4: Certification**
- **International Air Pollution Prevention Certificate (IAPPC)**.
- **Engine International Air Pollution Prevention (EIAPP) Certificate**.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Annex Comparison

| **Criterion** | **I** | **II** | **III** | **IV** | **V** | **VI** |
|---------------|-------|--------|---------|--------|-------|--------|
| **Pollution Medium** | Water | Water | Water | Water | Water | Air |
| **Key Threshold** | 15 ppm | NLS Category | IMDG Mark | 12 NM | Plastic ban | 0.50% S |
| **Certificate** | IOPP | NLS Certificate | — | ISPP | — | IAPPC + EIAPP |
| **Record Book** | Oil (I & II) | Cargo | — | Sewage | Garbage | Bunker Delivery Notes |
| **Special Areas** | Med, Baltic, Black, Antarctic, Red Sea, Gulf, Antarctic | Baltic | — | Baltic, Black | Med, Baltic, Black, Antarctic, Caribbean | Baltic, N. Sea, N. American, Caribbean |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MARPOL Convention — Hierarchical Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    IMO["INTERNATIONAL MARITIME ORGANIZATION IMO"]
    IMO --> MEPC["MEPC Marine Environment Protection Committee"]
    MEPC --> AMEND["Adopts Amendments to MARPOL"]
    MEPC --> GES["Adopts Guidelines Codes and Recommendations"]
    MEPC --> SPEC["Defines Special Areas"]
    AMEND --> A1["ANNEX I Oil"]
    AMEND --> A2["ANNEX II NLS"]
    AMEND --> A3["ANNEX III Packaged Goods"]
    AMEND --> A4["ANNEX IV Sewage"]
    AMEND --> A5["ANNEX V Garbage"]
    AMEND --> A6["ANNEX VI Air Pollution"]
    A1 --> A1M["Mandatory on Accession"]
    A2 --> A2M["Mandatory on Accession"]
    A3 --> A3O["Optional on Accession"]
    A4 --> A4M["Mandatory on Accession"]
    A5 --> A5M["Mandatory on Accession"]
    A6 --> A6M["Mandatory on Accession"]
```

### 4.2 MARPOL Compliance Flow — Onboard Ship

```mermaid
graph LR
    SHIP["Ship Design and Construction"]
    SHIP --> EQP["Install Pollution Prevention Equipment OWS STP Incinerator SCRUBBER"]
    EQP --> CERT["Obtain Statutory Certificates IOPP NLS IAPPC EIAPP"]
    CERT --> OPS["Operational Phase"]
    OPS --> DOC["Maintain Record Books Oil Garbage Sewage Bunker"]
    OPS --> PS["Plan and Execute Discharges Within Legal Limits"]
    DOC --> AUD["Port State and Flag State Inspections"]
    PS --> AUD
    AUD --> PEN["Penalties Detention Fines Criminal Liability"]
    AUD --> CLEAN["Clean Record and Continued Compliance"]
```

### 4.3 Annex I — Oil Discharge Decision Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1["Is the ship an oil tanker?"]
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2["Is the ship in a Special Area?"]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q3["Is oily water from engine room bilges?"]
    Q2 -- YES --> STOP1["STOP No discharge permitted"]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q4["Distance more than 50 NM from land?"]
    Q4 -- NO --> STOP2["STOP Illegal discharge"]
    Q4 -- YES --> Q5["Is OWS operating below 15 ppm?"]
    Q5 -- YES --> Q6["Rate less than 30 litres per NM?"]
    Q6 -- YES --> ALLOW["ALLOW DISCHARGE Conditions Met"]
    Q6 -- NO --> STOP3["STOP Rate exceeded"]
    Q5 -- NO --> STOP4["STOP Concentration exceeded"]
    Q3 --> Q7["Use OWS 15 ppm monitor"]
    Q7 --> Q5
```

### 4.4 Annex VI — Air Pollution Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A6["ANNEX VI AIR POLLUTION FROM SHIPS"]
    A6 --> SOX["SOx Control Fuel Sulfur Cap"]
    A6 --> NOX["NOx Control Engine Emission Tiers"]
    A6 --> ODS["Ozone Depleting Substances Ban"]
    A6 --> VOC["VOC Management Tankers"]
    A6 --> CII["Carbon Intensity Indicator CII"]
    A6 --> DCS["Data Collection System DCS"]
    SOX --> ECA["Emission Control Areas 0.10 percent S"]
    SOX --> GBL["Global Limit 0.50 percent S"]
    NOX --> T1["Tier I Older Engines"]
    NOX --> T2["Tier II Post 2011"]
    NOX --> T3["Tier III Post 2016 in ECA only"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
Define MARPOL Convention. Name its administering body and list all six Annexes.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The MARPOL Convention (*International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships*), officially known as **MARPOL 73/78**, is the principal international treaty aimed at preventing pollution of the marine environment from ships. It was adopted on **2 November 1973** and modified by the Protocol of **1978**, entering into force on **2 November 1983**.
>
> The Convention is administered by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**, headquartered in **London**.
>
> The six Annexes are:
> 1. **Annex I** – Oil
> 2. **Annex II** – Noxious Liquid Substances in Bulk
> 3. **Annex III** – Packaged Harmful Substances
> 4. **Annex IV** – Sewage from Ships
> 5. **Annex V** – Garbage from Ships
> 6. **Annex VI** – Air Pollution from Ships

**Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for administering body, 1 mark for listing all six Annexes.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
List any **three** Special Areas designated under MARPOL and the Annexes applicable to each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Three Special Areas designated under MARPOL are:
> 1. **Mediterranean Sea** — Applicable Annexes: I, V
> 2. **Baltic Sea** — Applicable Annexes: I, II, IV, V, VI
> 3. **Antarctic Area** — Applicable Annexes: I, IV, V (the most ecologically strict, with comprehensive discharge prohibitions)

**Mark Distribution:** 1 mark per Special Area with its applicable Annexes (any three correct = 3 marks).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **operational discharge requirements for oil tankers** under Annex I of MARPOL. List the conditions that must be **simultaneously satisfied** for a legal discharge at sea. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **sulfur oxide (SOx) and nitrogen oxide (NOx) emission controls** prescribed under Annex VI. What is the current global sulfur cap and the sulfur limit within Emission Control Areas (ECAs)? (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Operational Discharge Requirements for Oil Tankers (Annex I) – 7 marks**

A tanker may discharge oily water at sea **only if all five conditions below are satisfied simultaneously**:

1. **Distance from Land:** The ship must be more than **50 nautical miles** from the nearest land.

2. **En Route Condition:** The ship must be **proceeding en route** at a speed of not less than 7 knots (or equivalent in still water). (1 mark)

3. **Instantaneous Rate of Discharge:** The rate of discharge must not exceed **30 litres per nautical mile**. (1 mark)

4. **Total Quantity Discharged:** The total quantity of oil discharged into the sea must not exceed **$\frac{1}{15{,}000}$ of the total quantity of the particular cargo of which the oil formed a part**. (1 mark)

5. **Oil Content Monitoring:** The oil content of the effluent discharged must not exceed **15 parts per million (ppm)**, verified by an approved oil content meter. (1 mark)

Additionally, the tanker must have an **operational Sludge Tank, Oily Water Separator (OWS) with 15 ppm monitor, and an oil filtering system** meeting IMO specifications. The **Oil Record Book (Part II – Cargo/Ballast)** must be maintained for all transfer and discharge operations. (2 marks)

**(b) SOx and NOx Controls under Annex VI – 7 marks**

**SOx (Sulfur Oxide) Controls:**

- Annex VI **Chapter 3** regulates the sulfur content of marine fuel oils.
- **Global Sulfur Cap:** Since 1 March 2020, the global limit on sulfur content of marine fuel is **0.50% m/m** (mass by mass). (1 mark)
- **Within Emission Control Areas (ECAs):** The sulfur limit is **0.10% m/m** (since 1 January 2015). (1 mark)
- Compliance may be achieved by either: (a) using compliant low-sulfur fuel, (b) installing **Exhaust Gas Cleaning Systems (EGCS / "scrubbers")**, or (c) using alternative fuels like LNG. (1 mark)
- **Bunker Delivery Notes (BDN)** must be retained on board for at least 3 years. (1 mark)

**NOx (Nitrogen Oxide) Controls:**

- Annex VI **Chapter 4** applies to marine diesel engines of **>130 kW** output.
- **Three tiers** apply, depending on the **keel-laying date** of the ship: (1 mark)
  - **Tier I:** Applies to engines installed before 1 January 2000.
  - **Tier II:** Applies to engines installed on or after 1 January 2011 — **~20% reduction** compared to Tier I.
  - **Tier III:** Applies to engines installed on or after 1 January 2016 in **NOx ECAs** — **~80% reduction** compared to Tier I. (1 mark)
- Current **NOx ECAs** are: **North American ECA** (US & Canada coasts) and the **United States Caribbean Sea ECA**. (1 mark)
- **EIAPP Certificate** is mandatory for each engine, and the **Technical File** must be retained on board. (1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the **disposal of garbage from ships** as regulated by Annex V. What are the **categorical restrictions** on plastic disposal, and what onboard documentation is mandatory? (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **Noxious Liquid Substances (NLS) categorization system** under Annex II. Describe the **Pre-Wash Procedure** and the role of the **Procedures and Arrangements Manual (P&A Manual)**. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Garbage Disposal under Annex V – 7 marks**

Annex V prohibits the **discharge of all forms of plastics into the sea** — this is a **total and unconditional ban** with no exceptions, applicable to all ships, at all locations, at all times. (1 mark)

**Categorical Discharge Rules (Outside Special Areas):**

| Garbage Type | Minimum Distance from Nearest Land |
|--------------|------------------------------------|
| **Plastics** (incl. synthetic ropes, nets, plastic bags) | **Total ban — never discharged** |
| **Food waste** (comminuted/ground to $<25$ mm) | $\geq 3$ NM |
| **Food waste** (not comminuted) | $\geq 12$ NM |
| **Domestic waste** (paper, rags, glass, metal, bottles) | $\geq 12$ NM |
| **Cargo residues** (non-harmful) | $\geq 12$ NM |

(3 marks for table)

**Special Areas:** In Special Areas (Mediterranean, Baltic, Black, Antarctic, Caribbean), **all discharges are prohibited** except food waste comminuted to $<25$ mm and discharged $\geq 12$ NM. (1 mark)

**Mandatory Onboard Documentation:** (2 marks)
- **Garbage Management Plan** — must be maintained on every ship $\geq 100$ GT or carrying $\geq 15$ persons.
- **Garbage Record Book** — required for every ship $\geq 400$ GT or carrying $\geq 15$ persons; entries within 24 hours of discharge.
- **Garbage Placards** — must be displayed in prominent crew areas (galley, mess room).

**(b) NLS Categorization under Annex II – 7 marks**

Annex II governs the discharge of **Noxious Liquid Substances carried in bulk** (e.g., chemicals, vegetable oils, animal oils). (1 mark)

**The Four NLS Categories:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Category X:} \quad & \text{Major hazard to marine environment; \textbf{NO} discharge allowed.} \\
\textbf{Category Y:} \quad & \text{Hazardous; discharge permitted only under strict controls.} \\
\textbf{Category Z:} \quad & \text{Minor hazard; less stringent discharge conditions.} \\
\textbf{Category OS:} \quad & \text{Other Substances; lowest hazard; least restrictive.}
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

**Pre-Wash Procedure:** Before an NLS ship leaves the port of unloading, **cargo tanks that contained Category X, Y, or Z substances must undergo a mandatory pre-wash**. The pre-wash residue must be discharged to a reception facility, not the sea. (1 mark)

**Procedures and Arrangements Manual (P&A Manual):** This is a **ship-specific, MEPC-approved manual** that documents the ship's design, equipment, and approved procedures for: (2 marks)
- Tank pre-washing.
- Discharge of residues.
- Ventilation procedures.
- Ballasting and deballasting of cargo tanks.

It must be **approved by the Flag State Administration** and be readily available for inspection. The Master is responsible for ensuring compliance with the manual. (1 mark)

**Certificate Required:** **International Pollution Prevention Certificate for the Carriage of Noxious Liquid Substances in Bulk (NLS Certificate)**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse Annex I discharge limits for tankers vs. other ships.** Tankers have a "$\frac{1}{15{,}000}$" cargo-based limit; machinery bilge water has a simpler 15 ppm + 12 NM rule.
> 2. **Annex III is OPTIONAL** — yet KTU students frequently write "all six Annexes are mandatory." This loses 1 mark instantly.
> 3. **Special Area ≠ ECA.** "Special Area" refers to a sea zone with stricter discharge rules under Annexes I, II, IV, V. "ECA" specifically refers to **Emission Control Areas** under **Annex VI** (air pollution).
> 4. **Tier III NOx** applies **only in NOx ECAs**, not globally. Many students incorrectly generalize.
> 5. **Garbage placards are often missed** in answers. The examiner expects all three: Plan + Record Book + Placards.

---

### 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — MARPOL 73/78**

- **Full Name:** International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships.
- **Adopted:** 1973 (modified by 1978 Protocol); **In force:** 2 November 1983.
- **Administered by:** **IMO** (London) through **MEPC**.
- **Structure:** 28 Articles + 6 Annexes (I–VI).
- **Mandatory on Accession:** Annexes I, II, IV, V, VI.
- **Optional:** Annex III.
- **Annex I (Oil):** 15 ppm; 50 NM; $\frac{1}{15{,}000}$ cargo ratio; IOPP Certificate + Oil Record Book.
- **Annex II (NLS):** X, Y, Z, OS categories; Pre-Wash + P&A Manual; NLS Certificate.
- **Annex III (Packaged):** IMDG Code; Marine Pollutant Mark.
- **Annex IV (Sewage):** 12 NM rule; STP/Holding tank; ISPP Certificate.
- **Annex V (Garbage):** **Total plastic ban**; Garbage Management Plan + Record Book + Placards.
- **Annex VI (Air):** **0.50% S global**, **0.10% S in ECA**; NOx Tiers I/II/III; IAPPC + EIAPP.
- **MEPC** = the committee responsible for MARPOL amendments.
- **Special Areas** (remember a few): Mediterranean, Baltic, Black Sea, Antarctic, Caribbean.
- **CII & DCS** (2023) — Carbon Intensity Indicator and Data Collection System under Annex VI.
- **IMO 2020 Sulfur Cap** reduced marine sulfur by **~80%** overnight.
- **Single-Hull Tanker phase-out** — completed 2010, now mandatory double-hull for all new tankers.
- **Flag State** issues certificates; **Port State** enforces via inspection.
- **Recycling of ships:** Addressed separately by the **Hong Kong Convention, 2009** (not MARPOL).

> 🎯 **KTU Topper's Tip:** Memorize **"I, II, IV, V, VI are MANDATORY; III is OPTIONAL"** as your first sentence in any MARPOL definition question. It instantly scores a mark and sets the tone.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Port State Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Port State Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Port State Control (PSC)** is the regulatory regime wherein the maritime authority of a port nation exercises jurisdiction over foreign-flagged vessels visiting its ports, in order to verify that the condition of the ship and its equipment, crew certification, and operational practices comply with the requirements of **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** Conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, STCW, MLC 2006, Load Lines, Tonnage) and that the vessel is fit to proceed to sea without presenting an unreasonable danger to the marine environment, port infrastructure, or human life.

The legal basis for PSC rests on **Article 217** (Enforcement by Port States) and **Article 218** (Enforcement by Flag States at the Request of a Port State) of the **United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), 1982**, supplemented by IMO Resolution **A.1192(33)** — *Procedures for Port State Control*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested):**
> - **Flag State Control**: The State whose flag the ship flies has primary jurisdiction over its construction, equipment, manning, and seaworthiness *while at sea*.
> - **Port State Control**: A port nation may inspect foreign ships while *in its waters/ports* to ensure compliance with international rules, irrespective of the ship's flag.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "International Driving License" Model

Imagine a global road network where every vehicle (ship) carries a "driver's license" issued by its home country (Flag State). However, local traffic police (Port State) at any border crossing have the right to pull over foreign vehicles and check:

1. Is the driver's license genuine and valid?
2. Are the brakes, lights, and tires (hull, machinery, safety equipment) roadworthy?
3. Does the cargo (dangerous goods, oil, chemicals) meet pollution rules?
4. Is the driver fit, qualified, and rested (crew certification, hours of work)?

If the car fails the check, the local police can issue fines (deficiency rectification), order immediate repairs (deficiency with deadline), or **impound the vehicle** (detention). This is exactly how **Port State Control** functions at the world's ports — it is the *secondary safety net* that catches substandard shipping that Flag State enforcement has missed.

---

### 1.3 Objectives of Port State Control

> [!NOTE]
> **Five Primary Objectives (High-Yield for KTU):**
> 1. **Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS compliance)** — verify life-saving appliances, fire-fighting systems, navigation equipment.
> 2. **Prevention of Marine Pollution (MARPOL compliance)** — check oily-water separators, sewage plants, garbage management.
> 3. **Seafarer Welfare & Certification (STCW + MLC 2006)** — verify hours of rest, wages, medical fitness, COCs.
> 4. **Living & Working Conditions (MLC 2006)** — accommodation, food, water, safety of navigation under fatigue.
> 5. **Elimination of Substandard Shipping** — blacklisting, banning, repeated inspection (ban period **3 months** after 3rd detention in 12 months).

---

### 1.4 Core Entities Involved

| Acronym | Full Form | Role in PSC |
|---|---|---|
| **PSC** | Port State Control Officer (PSCO) | The inspector conducting the survey |
| **FSI** | Flag State Implementation | IMO Sub-Committee overseeing PSC globally |
| **MoU** | Memorandum of Understanding | Regional PSC agreement (e.g., Paris MoU, Tokyo MoU) |
| **RO** | Recognized Organization | Classification society acting on Flag State's behalf |
| **CIC** | Concentrated Inspection Campaign | Annual themed check across all MoUs |
| **THETIS** | European PSC database | Real-time inspection & deficiency records |
| **EQUASIS** | Quality Shipping Information System | Public ship quality database |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How" of PSC

PSC is not a random policing tool; it is a **risk-based, intelligence-driven** system. The deeper logic unfolds in five sequential steps:

- **Step 1 — Ship Selection (Targeting):** Not every ship is inspected. Authorities use a *Ship Risk Profile* matrix combining: ship age, flag, RO performance, type, and prior detention history. High-risk ships are prioritized.
- **Step 2 — Initial Inspection (Documentary Check):** The PSCO boards and verifies certificates (SOLAS, MARPOL, IOPP, ISPP, IAPP, SMC, ISSC, MLC, COCs, Crew List, etc.).
- **Step 3 — More Detailed Inspection:** Triggered by clear grounds (found deficiencies, complaints, RO non-conformities). Involves physical sampling of hull, machinery, life-saving, fire-fighting, and navigational equipment.
- **Step 4 — Expanded Inspection:** Triggered by serious deficiencies, especially involving structural integrity or occupational safety. Involves hull survey by diver/RO.
- **Step 5 — Rectification & Detention Decision:** Deficiencies are categorized; if they pose an "unreasonable danger," the ship is *detained* until rectified under a re-inspection regime.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PSC Matters in Engineering:** Every naval architect, marine engineer, and EOOW must understand that a ship designed or maintained sub-optimally will be flagged at port — leading to commercial losses (~$30,000–$80,000/day off-hire), insurance penalties, charter party disputes, and criminal liability of the Master.

---

### 2.2 KTU Reference Tables (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

#### Table 2.1 — Major Regional PSC Memoranda of Understanding (MoUs)

| MoU Region | Year Established | Geographic Coverage | Member States |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Paris MoU** | 1982 | Europe + North Atlantic | 27 + 1 cooperating |
| **Tokyo MoU** | 1993 | Asia-Pacific | 22 |
| **Indian Ocean MoU** | 1998 | Indian Ocean rim | 23 |
| **Acuerdo de Viña del Mar** | 1992 | Latin America | 16 |
| **Mediterranean MoU** | 2009 | Mediterranean Sea | 13 |
| **Abuja MoU** | 1999 | West & Central Africa | 19 |
| **Caribbean MoU** | 1996 | Caribbean basin | 14 |
| **Black Sea MoU** | 2000 | Black Sea | 7 |
| **Riyadh MoU** | 2005 | Persian Gulf | 8 |
| **Helsinki Convention (OSPAR)** | 1992 | NE Atlantic cooperation | — |

#### Table 2.2 — PSC Deficiency Categories & Action Matrix

| Category | Description | Rectification Window | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Category A** | Rectify before departure | **Immediate (before sailing)** | Fire pump inoperative, lifejacket expired |
| **Category B** | Rectify within 14 days | 14 days | Lifeboat release mechanism stiff |
| **Category C** | Rectify at next port | 3 months | Minor corrosion of handrails |
| **Category D** | Long-term rectification | As per flag state | Ballast water management plan outdated |

#### Table 2.3 — Ship Risk Profile Matrix (Paris MoU Style)

| Risk Factor | Weight | High Risk Indicator |
|---|---|---|
| **Ship Type** | Variable | Bulk carrier \textgreater 12 yrs old |
| **Flag** | High | Flag on Grey/Black list |
| **Recognized Organization** | High | RO on performance list low tier |
| **Deficiency History** | Highest | $\geq$ 2 detentions in last 36 months |
| **Age** | Medium | \textgreater 20 years (bulk/oil) |
| **Company Performance** | Medium | ISM audit non-conformities |

---

### 2.3 Detention Thresholds — The Quantitative Logic

A ship is detained when deficiencies are *"clearly hazardous to safety, health, or the environment."* The Paris MoU quantifies detention risk through a **Deficiency-to-Inspection Ratio (DIR)**:

$$
\text{DIR} = \frac{\text{Number of deficiencies recorded}}{\text{Number of inspections performed}}
$$

A **high DIR combined with low detention rate** flags *under-reporting*; a **low DIR with high detention** flags *targeting efficiency*. The annual detention threshold for triggering *banning* is **3 detentions within 12 months** (Paris MoU Article 16).

---

### 2.4 Concentrated Inspection Campaigns (CICs)

Each year, all major MoUs jointly conduct a **CIC** on a single theme (e.g., 2023 — *Crew Wages & MLC Compliance*; 2024 — *BWMS Compliance*). A ship failing the CIC faces **mandatory inspection of related systems** even if it otherwise would be low-risk.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** PSC records directly feed into **Equasis** (www.equasis.org), used by charterers, P\&I clubs, oil majors, and terminals for vetting. A poor PSC record means the ship cannot secure employment — the commercial *death penalty* of shipping.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix for PSC Inspection Procedure

Since PSC is a procedural/regulatory discipline rather than a numerical one, this section provides an **exhaustive step-by-step analytical breakdown** of the PSC inspection workflow, deficiency classification logic, and detention decision algorithm — the analytical equivalent of a derivation.

---

### 3.1 The PSC Inspection Workflow (Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown)

**STEP 1 — Initial Approach & Pre-Boarding Preparation**

The PSCO gathers intelligence before boarding:
- Reviews THETIS/EQUASIS records
- Checks Ship Risk Profile in the MoU database
- Reviews previous deficiencies, RO survey reports
- Confirms ship type, flag, age, RO, company

**STEP 2 — Boarding & Master Interview**

The PSCO boards, presents credentials, and conducts a structured interview:

$$
\text{Interview Checklist} = \{\text{Certificates},\ \text{Crew List},\ \text{Crew COCs},\ \text{Log Books},\ \text{Stability Info},\ \text{MLC Records}\}
$$

**STEP 3 — Documentary Inspection**

The PSCO verifies the validity and authenticity of:
- **Class certificates** (Hull, Machinery)
- **Statutory certificates** (SMC, ISSC, IAPP, IOPP, ISPP, IEE, IAFS, ILLC, MLC)
- **Crew documents** (COC, COE, medical fitness, GMDSS operator)
- **Operational records** (oil record book, garbage record book, sewage discharge, ballast water record book)
- **Safety plans** (SOPEP, SOPP, Emergency Response Plan, MGO)

**STEP 4 — Physical Walk-Through Inspection**

Sampling-based inspection covering:
- Navigation bridge (charts, gyro, radar, ECDIS, BNWAS, VDR, AIS)
- Engine room (main engine, auxiliaries, OWS, bilge alarms)
- Fire-fighting (fire pumps, hydrants, extinguishers, fixed systems)
- Life-saving (lifeboats, life rafts, EPIRBs, SARTs, immersion suits, line-throwing appliances)
- Accommodation (sanitation, lighting, ventilation, galley hygiene)
- Cargo deck (hatch covers, lashing, stability, cargo securing manual)

**STEP 5 — Decision Tree for Further Action**

$$
\text{Findings} \longrightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{No deficiencies} & \rightarrow \text{Release ship} \\
\text{Minor deficiencies} & \rightarrow \text{Issue deficiency list, no detention} \\
\text{Significant deficiencies} & \rightarrow \text{Issue deficiencies, advise flag/RO} \\
\text{Hazardous deficiencies} & \rightarrow \text{Detain under Article X of MoU}
\end{cases}
$$

**STEP 6 — Detention Notification (if applicable)**

The PSCO immediately notifies:
- Master (verbally + written Notice of Detention)
- Flag State Administration
- RO (classification society)
- Company (DPA — Designated Person Ashore)
- Other MoU members via THETIS

**STEP 7 — Rectification & Re-Inspection**

The Master/Company must:
1. Notify the PSCO when rectification is complete.
2. Submit evidence (workshop receipt, photo, surveyor endorsement).
3. Request re-inspection.
4. PSCO verifies and either lifts detention or extends it.

**STEP 8 — Release & Post-Detention Reporting**

The detention is lifted, and a **detention report** is filed. If this is the **3rd detention in 12 months**, the ship enters the *banning list* and is refused port access for **3 months** (Paris MoU Article 16).

---

### 3.2 Deficiency Classification Logic

Every deficiency is logged with a code from the **IMO Deficiency & Detention Database (DDB)**. The classification logic follows:

$$
\text{Deficiency Code} = [\text{Convention}][\text{Chapter/Regulation}][\text{Nature Code}]
$$

**Examples of high-yield codes:**

| Code | Convention | Regulation | Nature |
|---|---|---|---|
| SOLAS-02-04 | SOLAS | Ch II-2 (Fire Protection) | Fire detection system defective |
| MARPOL-01-15 | MARPOL | Annex I (Oil) | Oily-water separator inoperative |
| STCW-01-03 | STCW | Ch III (Watchkeeping) | COC not on board |
| MLC-01-04 | MLC 2006 | Reg 2.1 (Seafarer Conditions) | Wages below minimum |
| LL-01-02 | Load Lines | Ch II (Freeboard) | Trim beyond limits |

---

### 3.3 Detention Decision Matrix (Analytical Derivation)

A detention is triggered when the following condition holds:

$$
D_{\text{detain}} = 1 \quad \text{if} \quad \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot C_i \right] \geq D_{\text{threshold}}
$$

Where:
- $D_{\text{detain}}$ = detention decision (1 = detain, 0 = release)
- $w_i$ = weight of deficiency $i$ (severity factor: 1–3)
- $C_i$ = criticality of deficiency $i$ (1 = minor, 3 = hazardous)
- $D_{\text{threshold}}$ = MoU-defined threshold (typically 7 for bulk carriers, 9 for tankers)

**Logic of weighting (verified against IMO A.1192(33)):**

- $w = 3$ for life-threatening deficiencies (life-saving inoperative, stability violated)
- $w = 2$ for major statutory non-conformities (MARPOL OWS bypass)
- $w = 1$ for procedural/documentary deficiencies (record book incomplete)

A deficiency is **"clearly hazardous"** (and thus detaining) when:

$$
\text{Criticality} \geq 3 \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{Immediacy of Risk} = \text{High}
$$

This dual-condition logic prevents over-detention (commercial disruption) while ensuring substandard ships are removed from service.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — PSC Inspection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Ship Arrives at Port"] --> nodeB["THETIS/EQUASIS Database Query"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"Ship Risk Profile High?"}
    nodeC -- "Yes" --> nodeD["Priority Inspection"]
    nodeC -- "No" --> nodeE{"Random Selection or Complaint?"}
    nodeE -- "Yes" --> nodeD
    nodeE -- "No" --> nodeF["Documentary Verification Only"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["PSCO Boards Ship"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Documentary Check Certificates"]
    nodeH --> nodeI{"Clear Grounds for More Detailed?"}
    nodeI -- "Yes" --> nodeJ["Physical Walk-Through"]
    nodeI -- "No" --> nodeF
    nodeJ --> nodeK{"Serious Structural Issues?"}
    nodeK -- "Yes" --> nodeL["Expanded Inspection with Diver Survey"]
    nodeK -- "No" --> nodeM["Classify Deficiencies"]
    L --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN{"Any Hazardous Deficiency?"}
    nodeN -- "Yes" --> nodeO["DETAIN SHIP and Notify Flag/RO"]
    nodeN -- "No" --> nodeP["Issue Deficiency List with Rectification Window"]
    nodeO --> nodeQ["Company Rectifies Defects"]
    nodeQ --> nodeR["PSCO Re-Inspection"]
    nodeR --> nodeS{"Rectification Satisfactory?"}
    nodeS -- "Yes" --> nodeT["Lift Detention and Release Ship"]
    nodeS -- "No" --> nodeO
    nodeP --> nodeU["Verify Rectification at Next Port"]
    nodeU --> nodeT
    nodeF --> nodeT
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchy of Maritime Safety Oversight

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIMO["IMO - International Maritime Organization"] --> nodeUN["UNCLOS 1982 - Legal Basis"]
    nodeIMO --> nodeSOLAS["SOLAS Convention"]
    nodeIMO --> nodeMARPOL["MARPOL Convention"]
    nodeIMO --> nodeSTCW["STCW Convention"]
    nodeIMO --> nodeMLC["MLC 2006 Convention"]
    nodeIMO --> nodeLL["Load Lines Convention"]
    nodeIMO --> nodeTON["Tonnage Convention"]
    nodeUN --> nodeFLAG["Flag State Control - Primary Jurisdiction"]
    nodeSOLAS --> nodeFLAG
    nodeMARPOL --> nodeFLAG
    nodeSTCW --> nodeFLAG
    nodeMLC --> nodeFLAG
    nodeLL --> nodeFLAG
    nodeTON --> nodeFLAG
    nodeFLAG --> nodeRO["Recognized Organization - Class Survey on Behalf of Flag"]
    nodeRO --> nodePSC["Port State Control - Secondary Safety Net"]
    nodeSOLAS --> nodePSC
    nodeMARPOL --> nodePSC
    nodeSTCW --> nodePSC
    nodeMLC --> nodePSC
    nodeLL --> nodePSC
    nodePSC --> nodeTHETIS["THETIS Database - Inspection Records"]
    nodePSC --> nodeEQUASIS["EQUASIS - Public Quality Information"]
    nodePSC --> nodeCIC["Annual Concentrated Inspection Campaign"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Detention Trigger Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Deficiency Identified by PSCO"] --> nodeQ1{"Life Threat or Pollution Risk?"}
    nodeQ1 -- "Yes" --> nodeD1["Severity Weight equals 3"]
    nodeQ1 -- "No" --> nodeQ2{"Major Statutory Non-Conformity?"}
    nodeQ2 -- "Yes" --> nodeD2["Severity Weight equals 2"]
    nodeQ2 -- "No" --> nodeD3["Severity Weight equals 1"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeSum["Calculate Cumulative Severity Score"]
    nodeD2 --> nodeSum
    nodeD3 --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeThresh{"Score exceeds MoU Threshold?"}
    nodeThresh -- "Yes" --> nodeDet["ISSUE NOTICE OF DETENTION"]
    nodeThresh -- "No" --> nodeRec["Issue Deficiency with Rectification Window"]
    nodeDet --> nodeBan{"Third Detention in 12 Months?"}
    nodeBan -- "Yes" --> nodeBanList["Add to Banning List for 3 Months"]
    nodeBan -- "No" --> nodeRect2["Allow Rectification and Re-Inspection"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Global MoU Regional Coverage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeGlobal["Global PSC Framework - IMO A.1192"]
    nodeGlobal --> nodeParis["Paris MoU - Europe 1982"]
    nodeGlobal --> nodeTokyo["Tokyo MoU - Asia Pacific 1993"]
    nodeGlobal --> nodeIndian["Indian Ocean MoU 1998"]
    nodeGlobal --> nodeLat["Acuerdo de Vina del Mar - Latin America 1992"]
    nodeGlobal --> nodeMed["Mediterranean MoU 2009"]
    nodeGlobal --> nodeAbuja["Abuja MoU - West Africa 1999"]
    nodeGlobal --> nodeCarib["Caribbean MoU 1996"]
    nodeGlobal --> nodeBlack["Black Sea MoU 2000"]
    nodeGlobal --> nodeRiyadh["Riyadh MoU - Persian Gulf 2005"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**
*Define Port State Control. List any four international conventions that govern PSC inspections.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Port State Control is the regulatory framework whereby the maritime authority of a port nation exercises jurisdiction over foreign-flagged vessels while in its ports, to verify compliance with international maritime conventions, ensure safety of life, prevent marine pollution, and eliminate substandard shipping.

**Four governing conventions:**
1. **SOLAS** (Safety of Life at Sea) — for safety of construction, life-saving, fire-fighting, navigation.
2. **MARPOL** — for prevention of pollution from ships (oil, chemicals, sewage, garbage, air).
3. **STCW** — for standards of training, certification, and watchkeeping of seafarers.
4. **MLC 2006** — Maritime Labour Convention for seafarer welfare and working conditions.

*(Other acceptable: Load Lines, Tonnage 1969, COLREG, AFS Convention)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2023 Model Question]**
*Differentiate between Flag State Control and Port State Control. Why is PSC considered a secondary safety net?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Flag State Control | Port State Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Authority** | The State whose flag the ship flies | The State in whose port the ship is visiting |
| **Jurisdiction** | Constructive, operational, full-time | Limited to port call, foreign-flag ships only |
| **Scope** | Construction, equipment, manning, surveys | Verification of compliance, spot checks |
| **Trigger** | Statutory periodicity (annual, renewal) | Risk-based selection or complaint |

**PSC is a "secondary safety net"** because Flag State authorities sometimes lack resources, political will, or technical capability to fully enforce conventions — leading to *flags of convenience* with weak oversight. PSC inspections at major ports act as the corrective mechanism, catching substandard ships that slipped past Flag State and RO surveillance.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **three types of PSC inspections** prescribed under the Paris MoU. For each, specify the trigger and the scope of examination.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Ship Risk Profile** used by the Paris MoU to prioritize inspections. Mention any three criteria with their rationale.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Three Types of PSC Inspections (7 Marks)**

The Paris MoU (Procedures for Port State Control) defines three sequential types of inspections:

**(1) Initial Inspection (Routine Documentary Check) — [2 Marks]**
- **Trigger:** Ship arrival at port, selected through database query.
- **Scope:** Verification of validity of all statutory certificates and documents listed in §2.2 of the MoU:
  - Certificates: Class, SMC, ISSC, IAPP, IOPP, ISPP, ILLC, MLC, COCs.
  - Documents: Crew list, Oil Record Book, Garbage Record Book, SOPEP.
- **Outcome:** Either clear the ship or move to "more detailed" if clear grounds found.

**(2) More Detailed Inspection — [2.5 Marks]**
- **Trigger:** "Clear grounds" — defined in §2.3 of Paris MoU, including:
  - Deficiencies found during initial inspection.
  - Substandard RO performance history.
  - Substantial deviation from voyage plan.
  - Complaints from crew or external sources.
  - Reports of accidents involving the ship.
- **Scope:** Physical sampling of hull, machinery, life-saving, fire-fighting, navigation equipment; checks on board accommodation; verification of operational proficiency (drills, muster).

**(3) Expanded Inspection — [2.5 Marks]**
- **Trigger:** Serious deficiencies, especially of structural nature, or when the ship is on the *High Risk* list.
- **Scope:** Hull survey by diver or classification surveyor; structural thickness gauging; full cargo system check; comprehensive safety and pollution prevention audit.
- **Outcome:** Almost always leads to detention if structural defects are confirmed.

**Part (b) — Ship Risk Profile Criteria (7 Marks)**

The **Ship Risk Profile (SRP)** is a numerical/composite score that places each ship into **High Risk (HR), Medium Risk (MR), or Low Risk (LR)** categories, determining inspection frequency.

**Three key criteria with rationale:**

**(1) Ship Type and Age — [2.5 Marks]**
- **Rationale:** Bulk carriers, oil tankers, and chemical tankers have historically high accident rates due to cargo volatility and structural stress. Ships older than **12–20 years** show exponentially higher deficiency rates.
- The Paris MoU assigns higher inspection priority to: Bulk carriers $\geq 12$ years, Oil tankers $\geq 15$ years, Gas carriers $\geq 15$ years, Passenger ships of any age.

**(2) Flag State Performance — [2 Marks]**
- **Rationale:** Flags are classified annually as **White, Grey, or Black List** based on deficiency-to-inspection ratio.
- Ships flying *Black List* flags (high detention rates) are inspected with priority over *White List* flags (low detention rates, e.g., Denmark, Norway, Singapore).

**(3) Recognized Organization (RO/Class) Performance — [2.5 Marks]**
- **Rationale:** RO performance is measured by the **Recognition Rate** (deficiency rate of ships classed by the RO). Low-performing ROs indicate weak survey regimes; ships classed by such ROs receive priority inspection.
- The IMO RO Code (Res. A.789(19) + A.948(23)) sets minimum RO standards; substandard ROs face sanctions.

**Other criteria include:** company ISM audit history, prior detentions, accident record, and type of cargo.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **legal basis and objectives of Port State Control** with reference to UNCLOS, IMO Resolution A.1192(33), and the principal conventions enforced.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **deficiency classification system** and the criteria for **detention**. What is the consequence of three detentions in twelve months under the Paris MoU?

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Legal Basis and Objectives of PSC (7 Marks)**

**(1) Legal Foundation — [3 Marks]**

- **UNCLOS, 1982 — Article 217:** A port State has the right to take administrative measures against a foreign ship in its waters if the ship has violated international rules concerning safety, navigation, or pollution prevention.
- **UNCLOS, 1982 — Article 218:** A port State may investigate and institute proceedings if pollution damage occurs outside its territorial sea, even if the ship has voluntarily entered its port.
- **IMO Resolution A.1192(33) — Procedures for Port State Control (2018):** Provides a harmonized global framework, defining PSCO powers, inspection procedures, deficiencies, and detention criteria.
- **Regional MoUs:** Operationalize the IMO framework at the regional level (Paris, Tokyo, Indian Ocean, etc.).

**(2) Objectives — [4 Marks]**

1. **Verify Compliance** with SOLAS, MARPOL, STCW, MLC, Load Lines, Tonnage.
2. **Eliminate Substandard Shipping** — a substandard ship is one whose hull, machinery, equipment, or operation falls below the standards required by conventions.
3. **Protect Marine Environment** from oil, chemical, sewage, garbage, and air pollution.
4. **Protect Seafarer Rights and Welfare** as per MLC 2006.
5. **Maintain Maritime Safety** of life, property, and navigation.

**Part (b) — Deficiency Classification & Detention Criteria (7 Marks)**

**(1) Deficiency Classification — [3 Marks]**

Deficiencies are categorized by **rectification urgency** under the Paris MoU:

| Category | Window | Code |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Before departure | Critical hazard |
| **B** | Within 14 days | Major non-conformity |
| **C** | Within 3 months | Minor non-conformity |
| **D** | Long-term (next drydock) | Procedural issue |

**(2) Detention Criteria — [2 Marks]**

A detention is issued when deficiencies are *"clearly hazardous to safety, health, or the environment"* — this includes:

- Inadequate fire-fighting or life-saving equipment.
- Stability or structural integrity violations.
- Bypassed pollution-prevention equipment (e.g., OWS).
- Lack of qualified watchkeeping officers.
- MLC violations endangering seafarer life (e.g., no food/water).

**(3) Consequence of Three Detentions in 12 Months — [2 Marks]**

Under **Paris MoU Article 16**, a ship detained **three or more times within 12 months** is:

- Placed on the **Banning List** (published on MoU website).
- Refused access to ports of all Paris MoU member states for a **period of three (3) months**.
- After the ban period, the ship is removed from the list but remains on *High Risk* status for **36 months**.
- Repeated detentions can trigger **double banning periods**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating Flag State and Port State** — both are regulators, but their *jurisdiction and trigger* are different. Examiners expect explicit *jurisdiction-vs-event-based* contrast.
> 2. **Forgetting MLC 2006** — older syllabi often omit MLC; KTU 2024 emphasizes it strongly.
> 3. **Listing MoUs without explaining their operational role** — mere names earn partial credit; examiners want year of formation and geographic coverage.
> 4. **Not mentioning THETIS/EQUASIS databases** — these are *high-value keywords* expected in any 14-mark question on PSC.
> 5. **Skipping the "three detentions = banning" rule** — this is a *favourite* KTU question on PSC; missing it loses 2 marks instantly.
> 6. **Writing "PSC controls flag state ships"** — error! PSC is only for *foreign-flag* ships; flag state ships follow their own Flag State rules.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Port State Control**

- **Definition:** Inspection of foreign-flagged ships by a port nation to verify compliance with IMO conventions.
- **Legal Basis:** UNCLOS Art. 217 & 218; IMO Res. **A.1192(33)**; Regional MoUs.
- **Flag State vs Port State:** Flag = primary, at sea; Port = secondary, in port.
- **Three Inspection Types:** Initial → More Detailed → Expanded.
- **Ship Risk Profile:** High / Medium / Low; determined by type, age, flag, RO, history.
- **Major MoUs:** Paris (1982), Tokyo (1993), Indian Ocean (1998), Viña del Mar (1992), Mediterranean (2009), Abuja (1999), Caribbean (1996), Black Sea (2000), Riyadh (2005).
- **Deficiency Categories:** A (before departure), B (14 days), C (3 months), D (long-term).
- **Detention Rule:** Issued for clearly hazardous deficiencies — life, safety, health, environment at risk.
- **3-Detention Rule:** Three detentions in 12 months → **banning list for 3 months**, plus 36-month High Risk status.
- **Key Conventions:** SOLAS, MARPOL, STCW, MLC 2006, Load Lines, Tonnage, COLREG.
- **Databases:** THETIS (Europe), EQUASIS (global public).
- **CIC:** Annual themed inspection campaign across all MoUs.
- **RO (Recognized Organization):** Classification society acting on Flag State's behalf; their performance is monitored.
- **Commercial Impact:** Poor PSC record = no charter, no P\&I cover, no oil major vetting — commercial death penalty.
- **Engineering Implication:** Design and maintenance quality directly affect PSC outcomes; spec-in cost $\gg$ PSC rectification cost.
- **Examiners' Golden Keywords:** A.1192(33), UNCLOS Art. 217/218, Paris MoU, THETIS, EQUASIS, MLC 2006, SRP, CIC, RO Code, banning list.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for MoUs:** **"Please Take Indian Vines, Maybe At Caribbean Beach Resort"**
> → Paris, Tokyo, Indian Ocean, Viña del Mar, Mediterranean, Abuja, Caribbean, Black Sea, Riyadh.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** is a systematic, anticipatory, and participatory process used to evaluate the likely environmental consequences of a proposed project, policy, plan, or programme (commonly referred to as a *development action*) before a decision to proceed is taken by the competent authority. In the maritime domain, EIA is the formal mechanism through which shipping operations, port construction, dredging, offshore drilling, ship-breaking, and coastal infrastructure are evaluated against **baseline ecological, social, economic, and human-health criteria** as mandated under **UNCLOS (United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea)**, **MARPOL 73/78**, and national coastal regulation frameworks.

Mathematically, EIA can be represented as a multi-attribute decision function:

$$EIA_{score} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot \left( \frac{I_i - B_i}{B_i} \right) \cdot M_i$$

Where:
- $W_i$ = Weightage assigned to environmental parameter $i$
- $I_i$ = Post-project impact magnitude of parameter $i$
- $B_i$ = Baseline value of parameter $i$
- $M_i$ = Mitigation effectiveness factor for parameter $i$ (ranges from 0 to 1)
- $n$ = Total number of considered environmental parameters

> [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the **KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework for PESFT755, this topic maps directly to **Course Outcome CO4** — *"Appraise the regulatory and environmental frameworks governing maritime operations to ensure sustainable shipping practices."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Medical Check-Up Before Surgery"

Imagine a surgeon who, before operating on a patient, orders a complete health check-up — blood tests, ECG, MRI scans, allergy profiles — to **predict** how the body will react to the surgery and to **plan** how to minimize harm.
