topics.title: International Maritime Security Conventions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** International Maritime Security Conventions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **International Maritime Security Conventions** are a body of legally binding multilateral treaties, protocols, codes, and frameworks developed under the auspices of bodies like the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**, the **United Nations (UN)**, and the **International Labour Organization (ILO)**. These instruments establish uniform minimum standards, obligations, and procedural norms for States, flag administrations, port authorities, and ship operators to prevent, deter, and respond to security threats in the maritime domain.

The conventions collectively address threats such as **piracy, armed robbery, terrorism, weapons proliferation, illicit trafficking, and unlawful interference** with shipping and port infrastructure. They form the legal backbone that enables cross-border cooperation, prosecution of offenders, and harmonization of shipboard/port security regimes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PESFT755 Module 4):** Students are expected to identify the major IMO security conventions, describe their scope, explain the role of the ISPS Code, and summarize the legal tools available to suppress unlawful acts at sea.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **global apartment complex** where every resident (country) owns a unit (territorial waters + ships flying its flag). Without house rules, anyone could barge in, tamper with mail, or threaten neighbours. **Maritime security conventions are the "society bylaws"** of this complex — agreed upon, written down, and enforceable. For example:
- The **ISPS Code** is like the **CCTV + visitor-pass system** at the lobby.
- The **SUA Convention** is the **rulebook for prosecuting intruders**.
- **UNCLOS** is the **property deed** defining who owns which hallway and shared garden.
- **SOLAS Chapter XI-2** is the **emergency-firewall protocol** between apartments.

> [!TIP]
> For exam purposes, always link a convention to (a) the threat it addresses, (b) the binding body that adopted it, and (c) the duty imposed on flag/port/coastal States. Examiners award marks for this **triplet linkage**.

## 1.3 Key Organizations & Standard Metrics

- **IMO (International Maritime Organization):** UN specialized agency headquartered in **London, UK**, with **175 Member States** (as of the most recent IMO Assembly session). Sets the global regulatory standard.
- **UNODC (UN Office on Drugs and Crime):** Handles criminal-justice aspects of maritime crime.
- **ILO (International Labour Organization):** Addresses seafarer welfare — relevant to the **MLC 2006**.
- **UN Security Council Resolutions (UNSCR):** Authorize naval action against piracy, e.g., **UNSCR 1816 (2008)** for Somalia.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing *Recommendations* with *Mandatory* provisions. Under SOLAS, **Chapters** are mandatory; **Codes** (e.g., ISPS, IMDG) can be made mandatory only by reference within a Chapter.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of the Maritime Security Framework

The international maritime security regime is best understood as a **three-tier pyramid**:

### Tier 1 — Umbrella Legal Framework
- **United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), 1982**
  - Establishes the legal order of the seas.
  - Defines jurisdictional zones: **Internal Waters, Territorial Sea (12 nm), Contiguous Zone (24 nm), EEZ (200 nm), High Seas**.
  - Articles **100–107** address piracy; **108** address illicit drug trafficking; **110** address the right of visit/hot pursuit.
  - Articles **27–28** govern criminal jurisdiction on foreign ships in territorial waters.

### Tier 2 — Sectoral Specialized Conventions
- **SOLAS Convention, 1974 (as amended)** — particularly **Chapter XI-2** ("Special measures to enhance maritime security").
- **ISPS Code (International Ship and Port Facility Security Code), 2002** — adopted as a new Chapter to SOLAS via the **Conference of Contracting Governments, December 2002**.
- **SUA Convention, 1988** + **2005 Protocols** — Suppression of Unlawful Acts against the Safety of Maritime Navigation.
- **MARPOL 73/78** — environmental security (oil spill prevention, Annex VI air pollution).
- **STCW Convention, as amended** — Standards of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping; indirectly governs security-aware seamanship.
- **MLC 2006** (Maritime Labour Convention) — addresses seafarer welfare, a soft-security concern.

### Tier 3 — Soft Law, Resolutions & Industry Standards
- **IMO Circulars**, **UN Security Council Resolutions** (e.g., 1816, 1838, 1846, 1851), **ISF/ICS Guidelines**, **BIMCO Best Management Practices** (BMP 5), and **Regional Cooperation Agreements** (e.g., **ReCAAP ISC** in Asia, **Djibouti Code of Conduct** in the Western Indian Ocean).

## 2.2 The SOLAS Chapter XI-2 + ISPS Code Pair (Highest KTU Yield)

### Why It Was Created
- Triggered by the **September 11, 2001** terrorist attacks.
- Recognition that a container ship can deliver a weapon of mass destruction (WMD) into a port city.
- Adopted in **December 2002**, entered into force on **1 July 2004**.

### Core Obligations

| Stakeholder | Security Duty | Document / Officer |
|-------------|---------------|--------------------|
| **Flag State** | Approve Ship Security Plan (SSP) | Administration |
| **Company** | Appoint Company Security Officer (CSO); implement SSP | Designated Person Ashore (DPA) |
| **Ship** | Appoint Ship Security Officer (SSO); carry SSP | SSO |
| **Port Facility** | Appoint PFSO; conduct Port Facility Security Assessment (PFSA) | PFSO |
| **Contracting Government** | Set security levels; communicate threats | Designated authority |

### The Three Security Levels (ISPS)
- **Security Level 1 — Normal:** Baseline; routine security measures.
- **Security Level 2 — Heightened:** Heightened threat; additional precautions.
- **Security Level 3 — Exceptional:** Imminent or actual security incident; special, possibly full-scale measures for a limited period.

## 2.3 SUA Convention & 2005 Protocols

- **Original SUA (1988):** Criminalizes seizing ships by force, acts of violence against persons on board, and placing devices likely to destroy ships.
- **2005 Protocols:** Updated SUA to:
  - Include the use of ships as weapons (e.g., a ship as a WMD delivery vehicle).
  - Criminalize transporting persons intending to commit terrorist offences.
  - Allow **shiprider** (ship-board law-enforcement) agreements.
  - Permit **boarding of ships** suspected of terrorism offences beyond territorial seas (with flag-State consent or, in urgent cases, via the new expedited procedure).

## 2.4 UNCLOS Articles Critical for Security

| Article | Subject | Security Relevance |
|---------|---------|---------------------|
| 87 | Freedom of the High Seas | Defines area where piracy jurisdiction applies |
| 100 | Duty to cooperate in repression of piracy | Universal jurisdiction principle |
| 101 | Definition of piracy (private ends, two ships, etc.) | Legal threshold for piracy prosecution |
| 105 | Seizure of pirate ships | Right of any State to seize |
| 110 | Right of visit | Reasonable grounds of suspicion |
| 111 | Right of hot pursuit | Continuous pursuit across zones |
| 224–226 | Marine scientific research enforcement | Compliance & non-proliferation link |

> [!NOTE]
> The **two-ship / private-ends rule** of UNCLOS Art. 101 has been criticized in the post-2008 Somali piracy era, since well-organized pirates used motherships. The **IMO and UN General Assembly Resolution A/RES/65/37** have urged States to interpret "private ends" broadly.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Symbol | Application |
|---------|----------------|---------------|-------------|
| Territorial Sea breadth | $T_s = 12$ | nautical miles (nm) | Sovereignty subject to right of innocent passage |
| Contiguous Zone | $C_z = 24$ | nm | Control to prevent/pre-punish breaches |
| EEZ | $E_{ez} = 200$ | nm | Sovereign rights for resource exploration |
| Piracy jurisdiction | Universal | — | Any State may seize (Art. 105) |
| Hot pursuit initiation | Within or on hot pursuit of EEZ | — | Right continues into high seas if uninterrupted |
| ISPS — Ship-tonnage threshold | $\ge 500$ GT | Gross Tonnage (GT) | Applies to cargo + passenger ships |
| ISPS — Mobile Offshore Drilling Units (MODUs) | All | — | Subject to ISPS |
| ISPS — warships / naval auxiliaries | Exempt | — | Operate under flag-State rules |
| Security level escalation | $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3$ | Discrete | Set by Contracting Government |
| Declaration of Security (DoS) | Required at SS Level 2/3 | Form | Ship–port interface agreement |

> [!TIP]
> Examiners love the **500 GT threshold** question. The 500 GT cutoff applies to: cargo ships (international voyage), passenger ships (international voyage), MODUs, and high-speed craft. **Cargo ships below 500 GT on domestic voyages may be exempt** at the discretion of the Administration.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering & Operational Utility

- **Ship designers** must integrate security hardened spaces, restricted areas, and SSAS (Ship Security Alert System) per ISPS.
- **Port planners** integrate PFSA into facility layout, CCTV placement, access-control zoning, and waterside surveillance.
- **Naval architects** and **marine electrical engineers** design segregated redundant power systems for security-critical systems (per ISPS Reg. 5).
- **Communications engineers** maintain **SSAS** (Ship Security Alert System) — a covert button that transmits a ship-security alert to the flag State, with **no alarm on board**, to prevent hijacker retaliation.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: How an ISPS Security Level Decision Propagates

This derivation walks through the **decision-flow** that takes a Contracting-Government threat assessment to onboard security actions. It is the single most exam-relevant logical chain in this module.

**Step 1 — Threat Intelligence Input.**  
The Contracting Government receives intelligence (e.g., from national intelligence agencies, port-State authorities, or via IMO) indicating the threat level.

$$
\text{Threat Intelligence} \rightarrow \text{Government Security Authority (GSA)}
$$

**Step 2 — Risk Classification.**  
The GSA classifies the threat as one of three discrete levels:

$$
\text{Threat} \in \{L_1,\ L_2,\ L_3\}
$$

where
- $L_1$ = Normal conditions,
- $L_2$ = Heightened threat,
- $L_3$ = Imminent / actual incident.

**Step 3 — Notification Cascade.**  
The Contracting Government notifies:
1. Port Facilities under its jurisdiction,
2. Ships in or intending to enter its ports,
3. Other Contracting Governments (via IMO).

$$
\text{GSA} \xrightarrow{\text{Notifies}} \left\{ \text{PFSOs, \quad SSOs (via CSO), \quad IMO} \right\}
$$

**Step 4 — Ship-Side Action.**  
The SSO, in consultation with the CSO and Master, activates the relevant Ship Security Plan (SSP) provisions:

$$
\text{SSP Actions} = f(L_i) =
\begin{cases}
\text{Baseline (Reg. 6)}, & i = 1 \\
\text{Heightened (Reg. 7)}, & i = 2 \\
\text{Special, Time-limited (Reg. 8)}, & i = 3
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 5 — Declaration of Security (DoS) Triggers.**

$$
\text{DoS Required} \iff (L_i = 2)\ \lor\ (L_i = 3)
$$

**Step 6 — Continuous Monitoring & De-escalation.**  
The SSO updates the Master and CSO; de-escalation reverses the cascade.

$$
L_3 \xrightarrow{\text{Mitigate}} L_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Stabilize}} L_1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A common exam question is: *"At which security level is a Declaration of Security mandatory?"* The answer is **Levels 2 and 3** — at Level 1, a DoS is only required at the request of a Contracting Government or when there is a contractual ship/port interface agreement.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Convention → Threat → Mechanism

| Convention | Year | Threat Addressed | Mechanism | KTU Weightage |
|------------|------|------------------|-----------|----------------|
| **UNCLOS Part VII (Arts. 86–120)** | 1982 | Piracy, trafficking | Universal jurisdiction; right of hot pursuit | High |
| **SOLAS Ch. XI-2 + ISPS Code** | 2002/2004 | Maritime terrorism | SSP, PFSO, CSO, SSAS, 3 security levels | Highest |
| **SUA Convention** | 1988 | Unlawful acts vs. ships | Extradition or prosecution | High |
| **SUA Protocols** | 2005 | WMD, bioterrorism, ships as weapons | Shiprider, ship-boarding, expanded offences | Medium |
| **MARPOL Annex VI** | 1997/2005 | Air pollution / SOx (sulfur cap) | ECA zones, scrubbers, low-S fuels | Medium |
| **STCW + Manila Amendments** | 2010 | Human error / training gaps | Security awareness, training certificates | Medium |
| **MLC 2006** | 2006 (in force 2013) | Seafarer welfare → retention → safety | Working conditions, recruitment standards | Low–Medium |
| **BWM Convention** | 2004 (in force 2017) | Invasive species via ballast | D-1 Exchange / D-2 Performance Standard | Low |
| **ReCAAP ISC** | 2006 | Asian armed robbery | Info-sharing centre in Singapore | Low |
| **Djibouti Code of Conduct** | 2009 | W. Indian Ocean piracy | Regional capacity-building | Low |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Calculating the 500 GT Applicability Boundary

A KTU-style numerical question often tests the **threshold logic** rather than the threshold value itself.

**Problem:** A bulk carrier of **499 GT** engaged solely on **domestic voyages** intends to install ISPS-compliant equipment. Determine whether ISPS applies mandatorily.

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Recall the applicability rule.**  
SOLAS Chapter XI-2 applies to:
- Cargo ships of **500 GT and above** engaged on **international voyages**, AND
- Passenger ships (including high-speed craft) engaged on **international voyages**, AND
- MODUs.

**Step 2 — Compare tonnage.**  
$GT_{ship} = 499$ vs. threshold $GT_{th} = 500$

$$
499 \not\ge 500
$$

**Step 3 — Compare voyage type.**  
Voyage = *domestic* (not international).

**Step 4 — Conjunction check.**  
ISPS applies IF:
$$
\left( GT \ge 500 \right) \land \left( \text{Voyage} = \text{International} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the conjunction.**

$$
\left( 499 \ge 500 \right) \land \left( \text{International} \right) = \text{False} \land (\text{any}) = \text{False}
$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion.**  
ISPS **does not mandatorily apply**. The Administration **may**, at its discretion, extend ISPS-like measures to such ships, but it is not obligated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **final answer must explicitly state the threshold value (500 GT) and the voyage-type qualifier (international)**. Losing either component in the answer costs a full mark.

## 3.4 Derivation: The Piracy Jurisdictional Test (UNCLOS Art. 101)

A case-style derivation commonly asked in Part B.

**Given:** Vessel $A$ attacks Vessel $B$ in the high seas. The attackers board $B$ from $A$ (two ships are involved), with no political/religious motive (private ends), and the attackers are not warships of a recognized State.

**Test (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Location test.**  
Was the act on the high seas (or outside the jurisdiction of any State)?

$$
\text{High Seas} = \text{True} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{UNCLOS Applies}
$$

**Step 2 — Two-ship test.**  
Were two distinct ships involved?

$$
N_{ships} \ge 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Pass}
$$

**Step 3 — Private-ends test.**  
Is the motive exclusively for private gain (NOT political)?

$$
\text{Motive} = \text{Private} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Pass}
$$

**Step 4 — Violence / detention test.**  
Was there an act of violence, detention, or depredation?

$$
\text{Violence} = \text{True} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Pass}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**  
All four tests satisfied → the act is **piracy under UNCLOS Art. 101** → universal jurisdiction applies under Art. 105.

$$
\boxed{\text{Piracy confirmed; any State may seize the ship and arrest the persons.}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Armed robbery against ships** (a related but distinct IMO concept) applies when such acts occur **within a State's territorial sea or internal waters** — these fall under the jurisdiction of the coastal State, not universal jurisdiction. The IMO Code of Practice for the Investigation of Crimes of Piracy and Armed Robbery (Res. A.1025(26)) draws this distinction.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2024 Set A]
**"List the three security levels defined under the ISPS Code. Who has the authority to set these levels for a port facility?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Security Level 1 — Normal:** Baseline routine security measures. (1 mark)
2. **Security Level 2 — Heightened:** Heightened threat; additional precautions. (1 mark)
3. **Security Level 3 — Exceptional:** Imminent or actual security incident; special, time-limited measures. (1 mark)

**Authority:** The **Contracting Government** (i.e., the Government of the State in whose territory the port facility is located) sets the security level for the port. The same Government also sets the security level for ships operating in its territory.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2024 Set B]
**"What is the role of the Ship Security Alert System (SSAS) under SOLAS Chapter XI-2?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The SSAS is a **covert shipboard security system** that, when activated, transmits a **ship security alert** to a competent authority designated by the **Administration (flag State)**, identifying the ship, its position, and that the security of the ship is under threat. (2 marks)
- Critically, **the alert is not to be emitted locally (no on-board alarm)** — this is to prevent hijackers from retaliating. The alert may also be transmitted to other ships or the company. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** For every 14-mark question, candidates may attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B (Internal Choice). Both are constructed below as alternates.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2024 Set A]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **ISPS Code** in detail. Explain its **scope, key stakeholders, and the three security levels**. List the documents that a ship must carry to demonstrate ISPS compliance.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **SUA Convention 1988** and the **2005 SUA Protocols**. What new offences and cooperation mechanisms did the 2005 Protocols introduce in response to evolving maritime terrorism threats?

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A (a)

**1. Origin and Legal Basis (2 marks)**
- The ISPS Code was adopted at a **Diplomatic Conference held at IMO Headquarters, London, in December 2002**.
- It was incorporated into the **SOLAS Convention, 1974** as a new Chapter XI-2, with the Code itself annexed.
- Entered into force on **1 July 2004**.

**2. Scope (1 mark)**
- Applies to:
  - Passenger ships (incl. high-speed passenger craft) on international voyages.
  - Cargo ships $\ge 500$ GT on international voyages.
  - MODUs.
- Excludes: warships, naval auxiliaries, and ships below 500 GT on domestic voyages (may be exempted by Administration).

**3. Key Stakeholders (2 marks)**

| Stakeholder | Role |
|-------------|------|
| Contracting Government | Sets security level; designates authorities |
| Company (operator) | Appoints CSO, provides resources |
| Ship Security Officer (SSO) | Onboard security implementation |
| Port Facility Security Officer (PFSO) | Port security implementation |
| Master | Has overriding authority on ship safety/security |

**4. Three Security Levels (1 mark)**
- **Level 1 — Normal**, **Level 2 — Heightened**, **Level 3 — Exceptional.**

**5. Documents to be Carried (1 mark)**
- Continuous Synopsis Record (CSR).
- Ship Security Plan (SSP) (or a verified extract).
- International Ship Security Certificate (ISSC) or Interim ISSC.
- Declaration of Security (DoS), when applicable.
- Records of training, drills, and exercises (last 10 security drills).

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A (b)

**1. SUA Convention 1988 (3 marks)**
- Adopted in **Rome, 10 March 1988**, in response to the **Achille Lauro** hijacking (1985).
- Creates **universal jurisdiction** for certain offences against ships:
  - Seizing or exercising control of a ship by force, threat, or intimidation.
  - Acts of violence against persons on board.
  - Placing devices or substances on a ship likely to destroy or damage it.
- States must either **extradite** or **prosecute** alleged offenders (*aut dedere aut judicare*).

**2. The 2005 Diplomatic Conference and Need for Update (1 mark)**
- After the **9/11 attacks** and the **USS Cole** bombing, the 1988 SUA was deemed inadequate.
- A Diplomatic Conference adopted **two protocols in October 2005**: the *Protocol of 2005 to the SUA Convention* and the *Protocol of 2005 to the SUA Fixed Platforms Protocol*.
- Entry into force required **12 States**; achieved in **2010**.

**3. New Offences Introduced (2 marks)**
- Using a ship to commit a terrorist attack (the ship itself as a weapon).
- Transporting persons intending to commit a terrorist offence.
- Transporting materials related to WMD.
- Acts intended to cause death/serious injury or material damage for the purpose of intimidating a population.

**4. New Cooperation Mechanisms (1 mark)**
- **Shiprider agreements** — flag State may authorize law-enforcement officers of another State to ride on its flagged ship.
- **Expedited ship-boarding procedure** — flag State may pre-authorize boarding in cases of suspected terrorism, with notification within hours.
- A clear framework for **evidence preservation** in support of prosecution in the flag State.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write that SUA 1988 directly creates universal jurisdiction. It does **not** — it creates an *obligation to extradite or prosecute* under the **aut dedere aut judicare** principle. Universal jurisdiction proper is a feature of **UNCLOS Art. 105** for piracy, not of SUA. Writing "SUA creates universal jurisdiction" will cost full marks in the relevant sub-part.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2024 Set B]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **role of UNCLOS (1982) in maritime security**. Explain the **definition of piracy** under Article 101, the **universal jurisdiction** rule under Article 105, and the **right of hot pursuit** under Article 111.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **SOLAS Convention Chapter XI-2**. List the **key obligations** on (i) Contracting Governments, (ii) shipping companies, and (iii) port facilities. How does the **Declaration of Security (DoS)** function at the ship-port interface?

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B (a)

**1. UNCLOS as the Constitutional Charter of the Oceans (2 marks)**
- Adopted in **Montego Bay, Jamaica, 10 December 1982**; entered into force on **16 November 1994** after the 60th ratification.
- Often called the **"Constitution of the Oceans"** because it sets the comprehensive legal framework for all ocean uses, including security.
- Section 2: **Territorial Sea & Contiguous Zone (Arts. 2–33)**; Section 5: **Exclusive Economic Zone (Arts. 55–75)**; Part VII: **High Seas (Arts. 86–120)**; Part XI: **The Area**.

**2. Definition of Piracy — Art. 101 (3 marks)**

Art. 101 contains the legal test of piracy; the act must satisfy **all** the following elements:

$$
\text{Piracy} \iff
\begin{cases}
\text{(i) Any illegal acts of violence, detention, or depredation;} \\
\text{committed (ii) by the crew or passengers of a private ship or aircraft;} \\
\text{(iii) directed against another ship, aircraft, persons, or property thereon;} \\
\text{(iv) on the high seas or outside the jurisdiction of any State;} \\
\text{(v) for private ends.}
\end{cases}
$$

> Key point: The "two-ship" requirement (a private ship attacking another ship) and the "private ends" requirement (no political motive) are the two most-contested elements in modern practice.

**3. Universal Jurisdiction — Art. 105 (1 mark)**
- Any State may **seize a pirate ship, arrest the persons, and seize the property**.
- The courts of the **seizing State** may decide the penalties and decide what to do with the ship, aircraft, and property, subject to the rights of third parties acting in good faith.

**4. Right of Hot Pursuit — Art. 111 (1 mark)**
- A coastal State may pursue a foreign ship **when there are good reasons to believe** it has violated the laws of that State.
- Pursuit **begins** when the foreign ship is within internal waters, territorial sea, or contiguous zone, and may **continue** beyond the territorial sea (or contiguous zone) into the high seas **provided it has not been interrupted**.
- The right **ceases** as soon as the pursued ship enters the territorial sea of its own or another State.
- Carried out by warships or military aircraft, or other clearly marked government ships/aircraft.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B (b)

**1. Why Chapter XI-2 Exists (1 mark)**
- Recognized the **maritime supply chain as a vector for terrorism**, especially through containers.
- Adopted at the **December 2002 Diplomatic Conference**; entered into force **1 July 2004**.

**2. Obligations of Contracting Governments (2 marks)**
- Establish **security levels** and provide guidance.
- Approve **Port Facility Security Plans (PFSPs)** and **Ship Security Plans (SSPs)**.
- Designate and communicate points of contact for SSAS alerts.
- Ensure the Administration issues the **International Ship Security Certificate (ISSC)**.
- Conduct **audits and verification** of compliance.

**3. Obligations of Companies (2 marks)**
- Appoint a **Company Security Officer (CSO)**.
- Ensure the ship carries an approved **SSP** that addresses the three security levels.
- Ensure the **SSO** is appointed and trained.
- Maintain training, drills, and exercise records.

**4. Obligations of Port Facilities (1 mark)**
- Appoint a **Port Facility Security Officer (PFSO)**.
- Conduct a **Port Facility Security Assessment (PFSA)** leading to a **PFSP**.
- Implement access control, surveillance, restricted areas, and handling of cargo/mail.
- Cooperate with ships at the interface (via DoS).

**5. Declaration of Security (DoS) at the Ship-Port Interface (1 mark)**
- The DoS is a **written agreement** between the ship and the port facility (or another ship) confirming mutual security responsibilities.
- **Mandatory at Security Levels 2 and 3** (Reg. 5).
- At Level 1, required only on the request of a Contracting Government, or when there is a contractual or other agreement for its use.
- The DoS captures: security measures already in place, the interface measures, and the period of validity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Loss in Part B(b):** Students often write that the **Master** has the power to refuse entry to a port or to deny berthing if the ship's security cannot be maintained. While the Master has overriding authority for safety, the decision on whether to enter a foreign port is often a **commercial and flag-State decision**, not a unilateral Master one. Examiners penalize sweeping statements like *"Master can refuse any port at any time."*

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> Use this as a **last-day revision checklist** before the KTU exam.

- **ISPS Code is a SOLAS Chapter (XI-2) annex**, not a standalone convention. (1 mark question)
- **Three Security Levels:** 1 (Normal), 2 (Heightened), 3 (Exceptional). Set by **Contracting Government**.
- **500 GT + International voyage** = ISPS threshold.
- **Key ISPS Officers:** CSO, SSO, PFSO. **Key plans:** SSP, PFSP, PFSA. **Key systems:** SSAS (silent alert).
- **SSAS alerts go to the flag State, not as an on-board alarm** — silence is by design.
- **Continuous Synopsis Record (CSR)** is the ISPS logbook — keep it on board.
- **Declaration of Security (DoS):** mandatory at Security Levels 2 and 3.
- **SUA 1988:** "Hijack-the-ship" offences; **extradite-or-prosecute** rule.
- **SUA 2005 Protocols:** ships as weapons, WMD, **shiprider agreements**, **expedited boarding**.
- **UNCLOS piracy = Art. 101; universal jurisdiction = Art. 105; hot pursuit = Art. 111.**
- **Two-ship rule** + **private ends** = piracy under UNCLOS; **armed robbery in territorial waters** is a separate coastal-State offence.
- **SOLAS is a Convention (mandatory chapters)**; its **Codes** (ISPS, IMDG, IS Code) are mandatory only because they are referenced in mandatory chapters.
- **Warships, naval auxiliaries, and government-owned non-commercial ships** are exempt from ISPS applicability.
- **ReCAAP ISC (2006, Singapore)** is the Asian info-sharing body; **Djibouti Code of Conduct (2009)** is the W. Indian Ocean regional anti-piracy instrument.
- **UNSCR 1816 (2008)** authorized naval operations against Somali pirates — read this as a *soft-law* supporting instrument.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner's Mantra:** For any maritime security convention question, structure your answer in **three blocks** — (1) *Threat/Issue*, (2) *Convention/Instrument*, (3) *Specific Duty on Stakeholders*. KTU examiners consistently reward this triplet structure.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ISPS Code and Its Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ISPS Code and Its Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — ISPS CODE AND ITS REQUIREMENTS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISPS Code (International Ship and Port Facility Security Code):** A comprehensive, internationally binding regulatory framework adopted by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** in **December 2002** under the **SOLAS Convention (Chapter XI-2 — "Special Measures to Enhance Maritime Safety and Security")**. It became mandatory for all Contracting Governments, ports, and ships engaged on international voyages on **1 July 2004**. The Code establishes a layered, risk-based, cooperative international regime to detect security threats, define security levels, and ensure the early identification of, and coordinated response to, security incidents affecting ships, port facilities, and the wider maritime transport chain.

The ISPS Code is structured into **three principal components**:

| Component | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Part A** | **Mandatory** | Defines the detailed, enforceable security-related requirements (Sections 1–19) |
| **Part B** | **Guidance / Recommendatory** | Provides illustrative, non-binding guidance on implementation of Part A |
| **SOLAS Chapter XI-2** | **Mandatory Treaty Article** | The umbrella international convention article that gives Part A its binding legal status |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of the ISPS Code as the "maritime equivalent of an airport security system" — but distributed across a global network of moving assets (ships) and fixed assets (ports).**

Imagine that every **ship** is a flying aircraft, and every **port** it docks at is a different airport, potentially in a different country with different languages, rules, and threats. Without a single common rulebook, a terrorist could board a ship in Country A and exploit lax screening in Country B. The ISPS Code acts like a **"universal security passport system"** that:

- Forces every ship to carry a **security passport** (Ship Security Plan + Certificate).
- Forces every port to maintain a **security desk** (Port Facility Security Plan + PFSO).
- Establishes a **common threat alert dial** (Security Levels 1 → 2 → 3) that everyone must read and react to in the same way.
- Mandates a **silent panic button** (Ship Security Alert System) to call for help covertly if the ship is taken over.

Just as ICAO Annex 17 standardised aviation security after the 1970s hijackings, the ISPS Code standardised maritime security after the **9/11 attacks of September 2001**, which exposed that international shipping was a soft, high-volume target.

---

### 1.3 Historical Trigger & Genesis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Triggering Event:** The 9/11 terrorist attacks (2001) revealed the vulnerability of the global maritime supply chain. The IMO accelerated adoption, and the **Diplomatic Conference on Maritime Security** held in London in **December 2002** adopted the ISPS Code along with amendments to the 1974 SOLAS Convention. Entry into force: **1 July 2004** — one of the fastest entry-into-force timelines in IMO history.

### 1.4 Scope of Applicability

The ISPS Code applies to:

1. The following **types of ships** engaged on international voyages:
   - **Passenger ships** (including high-speed craft).
   - **Cargo ships** of **500 GT (Gross Tonnage) and above**.
   - **Mobile Offshore Drilling Units (MODUs)**.
2. The **port facilities** serving such ships.

The Code does **not** apply to warships, naval auxiliaries, or other ships owned/operated by a Contracting Government and used only on government non-commercial service.

---

### 1.5 Why ISPS Code Is Engineering-Relevant (for a Maritime Safety Course)

> [!NOTE]
> Although the ISPS Code is largely a regulatory and management document, it has direct **engineering implications**: it dictates the design and retrofitting of ships with **Ship Security Alert Systems (SSAS)**, **Automatic Identification Systems (AIS)** with security overlays, **CCTV and access control systems**, **lighting and communications redundancy**, and **Citadel/Tempest** design philosophies. Naval architects and marine engineers must therefore embed security from the *design stage*, not as an afterthought.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Security Levels (The "Threat Dial")

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ISPS Code establishes **three Security Levels**, graduated by threat severity. These are set by the **Contracting Government** in whose waters the ship/port is operating.

| Security Level | Description | Trigger Condition | Required Action Intensity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 1 — Normal** | The level at which ships and port facilities normally operate. | Routine peacetime conditions. | Maintain baseline security measures; continuous monitoring. |
| **Level 2 — Heightened** | Level applying for as long as there is a heightened risk of a security incident. | Confirmed intelligence of a possible threat in the region. | Additional preventive measures; increased surveillance; restricted access. |
| **Level 3 — Exceptional** | Level applying for the period of time when there is a probable or imminent risk of a security incident. | Imminent or actual security incident (e.g., active war, specific threat). | Maximum security posture; possible suspension of operations; ship may be denied entry. |

**Critical Point:** When a ship is at **Level 3** in one port but must transit to a port operating at **Level 1**, the ship must **maintain Level 3** measures until the Contracting Government of the next port confirms it is acceptable to reduce. This prevents a "security gap" during transit.

---

### 2.2 Statutory Roles & Responsibilities (The "Who-Does-What" Matrix)

| Role | Acronym | Appointed By | Core Responsibility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Contracting Government (Ship)** | — | Self | Sets security levels for its flagged ships; issues approvals. |
| **Contracting Government (Port)** | — | Self | Sets security levels for its ports; designates Port Facility Security Officers (PFSOs); approves Port Facility Security Plans. |
| **Recognized Security Organization** | RSO | Contracting Government | May be authorised to carry out approval/audit work on the Government's behalf. |
| **Company** | — | Self (in SOLAS context) | Defines the ship's security duties; appoints the CSO. |
| **Company Security Officer** | CSO | Company | Overall ship security; liaises with PFSO; reviews SSP. |
| **Ship Security Officer** | SSO | Company | On-board security implementation; reports to Master and CSO. |
| **Port Facility Security Officer** | PFSO | Port Facility | Port-side security; coordinates with ship and CSO/SSO. |
| **Master** | — | Company | Has **overriding authority** and the **final decision** on ship security. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Master’s Overriding Authority (ISPS Code Part A, Section 5.2):** *"The Master shall not be constrained by the Company, the Charterer, or any other person from taking or executing any decision which, in the professional judgement of the Master, is necessary to maintain the safety and security of the ship."* This is a KTU-favourite point — **memorize it verbatim** for Part A questions.

---

### 2.3 Key Documents & Records (The "Paperwork" of Maritime Security)

| Document | Acronym | Issued/Held By | Validity | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ship Security Plan** | SSP | Shipowner/Company | Continuous (revised as needed) | Internal, confidential, ship-specific security operating manual. |
| **Ship Security Certificate** | SSC | Contracting Government (or RSO) | **5 years** | Statutory proof of compliance; must be carried on board. |
| **International Ship Security Certificate** | ISSC | Contracting Government (or RSO) | **5 years** | For international voyages (the more common variant). |
| **Continuous Synopsis Record** | CSR | Contracting Government | Continuous (updated on every change) | Public-record log of ship's history relevant to security. |
| **Port Facility Security Plan** | PFSP | Port Facility | Continuous | Port-side security operating manual. |
| **Statement of Compliance of a Port Facility** | — | Contracting Government | **5 years** | Statutory proof that the port facility meets ISPS. |
| **Declaration of Security** | DoS | Ship ↔ Port Facility | Per interface | Bilateral security agreement for a specific ship-port interaction. |

---

### 2.4 Core Functional Requirements (Part A Breakdown)

> [!NOTE]
> The 19 sections of Part A may be grouped into **six functional clusters** for easier learning. This is how KTU examiners frame long-answer questions.

1. **Governance Cluster** (Sections 1–4): Definitions, application, obligations of Contracting Governments.
2. **Ship-Level Cluster** (Sections 5–11): SSP, SSO, CSO, training drills, ISSC.
3. **Port-Level Cluster** (Sections 14–18): PFSP, PFSO, port facility security duties.
4. **Interface Cluster** (Section 19): Ship-port information exchange, including the DoS.
5. **Communication & Alerting Cluster** (Section 9 + SOLAS XI-2 Reg. 6): SSAS, CSR, AIS reporting.
6. **Verification & Audit Cluster** (Section 4 + Reg. 4): Approval, audit, control, and compliance measures.

---

### 2.5 The Ship Security Alert System (SSAS) — The "Silent Panic Button"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SSAS Definition (SOLAS Chapter XI-2, Regulation 6):** A system that, when activated, **transmits a covert security alert to a competent authority designated by the Administration**, identifying the ship, its location, and the fact that the security of the ship is under threat. **The alert must NOT be visible or audible on the bridge** (covert requirement) and must NOT be sent to other ships.

**Key Design Requirements:**

- Two-way alerting capability (to a shore-based authority and optionally to other competent authorities).
- Covert activation from at least **two separate locations** on the ship.
- Initiates and updates the ship-to-shore alert without interrupting the ship's normal communications.
- Power supply from **both the main and emergency sources**.
- Tested periodically (minimum annually, per flag-State guidelines).

**Engineering Trade-off:** SSAS is often integrated into the ship's **GMDSS** (Global Maritime Distress and Safety System) terminal to share antennas and power, but the alert path must remain operationally separate from the routine distress path (e.g., DSC) so that pirates who take control of the bridge cannot suppress the SSAS transmission.

---

### 2.6 The Declaration of Security (DoS) — The "Bilateral Handshake"

> [!NOTE]
> The DoS is a **ship-to-port (or ship-to-ship) record** confirming that both parties have agreed on the security measures that will apply during their interface. It is required:
> - When the ship operates at a **higher Security Level** than the port facility.
> - When there is a **known specific security threat** concerning the ship or the port.
> - When the ship is conducting an **interface with another ship** at high risk.
> - At the request of either party.

The DoS is signed by the **SSO and PFSO (or their equivalents)**, exchanged before ship-port operations begin, and is valid for the duration of the interface or until Security Levels change.

---

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Quick-Reference Table

| Element | One-Line Recall (for the exam) |
|---|---|
| **ISPS adopted** | December 2002 (Diplomatic Conference) |
| **ISPS in force** | 1 July 2002 ❌ → **1 July 2004** ✅ |
| **Under** | **SOLAS Chapter XI-2** |
| **Part A** | **Mandatory** |
| **Part B** | **Guidance (recommendatory)** |
| **Applies to** | Passenger ships; cargo ships **≥ 500 GT**; MODUs |
| **Exempt** | Warships, naval auxiliaries, government non-commercial ships |
| **Security Levels** | 1 (Normal), 2 (Heightened), 3 (Exceptional) |
| **Levels set by** | **Contracting Government** |
| **Master's authority** | **Overriding & final** (Section 5.2) |
| **ISSC validity** | **5 years** |
| **Statement of Compliance (Port)** | **5 years** |
| **CSR update trigger** | **Every change** of flag, owner, management, etc. |
| **SSAS transmission target** | Competent authority designated by Administration (NOT visible on bridge) |
| **DoS required when** | Ship's SL > Port's SL; known threat; inter-ship interface at risk; or on request |
| **PFSP** | Stays at the port facility (NOT given to the ship) |
| **SSP** | Confidential; protected from unauthorized disclosure |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Decomposition (Exhaustive)

> [!NOTE]
> The ISPS Code is a **regulatory / management framework**, not a numerical formula. Therefore, the "derivation" below is the **systematic analytical decomposition** that a KTU full-mark answer requires. Each logical step is unpacked in full, with no shortcuts.

### 3.1 Step-by-Step: How a Ship Becomes ISPS-Compliant (The "Compliance Pathway")

**Step 1 — Flag-State Notification**
The Company informs the **Contracting Government (flag State)** that the ship is to be operated on an international voyage to which the Code applies. The Administration assigns the ship to an **ISPS implementation regime** and approves a **Recognized Security Organization (RSO)** if needed.

- **Logic:** Compliance cannot start without the flag State's formal acknowledgement; otherwise, the ISSC has no legal basis.
- **Why it matters:** A ship that operates internationally without the flag State's recognition is in violation of SOLAS XI-2 and may be detained in any port it visits.

**Step 2 — Appointment of the Company Security Officer (CSO)**
The Company appoints a **CSO** (typically a shore-based manager, not the Master). The CSO is the single point of contact for ship security at the corporate level.

- **Logic:** A ship needs both onboard (SSO) and shoreside (CSO) security leadership; separating them prevents a single-point compromise.
- **Engineering parallel:** Analogous to separating the **CISO** (Corporate Information Security Officer) from the on-site **IT Security Admin** in a corporation.

**Step 3 — Ship Security Assessment (SSA)**
A **Ship Security Assessment** is carried out to identify the existing security measures, threats, vulnerabilities, and operational points (e.g., gangways, cargo holds, machinery spaces) that need protection. The SSA is a pre-requisite for writing the SSP.

- **Logic:** You cannot write a security plan without first knowing what you are protecting, against whom, and from where.
- **Outputs:** Identification of the three "critical" areas — **access points, restricted areas, and vital systems**.

**Step 4 — Drafting the Ship Security Plan (SSP)**
The Company drafts the SSP based on the SSA. The SSP must address, **at minimum**, the 14 elements listed in Section 9 of Part A (full list in §3.3 below).

- **Logic:** The SSP is the operational, ship-specific "playbook" for security.
- **Confidentiality:** Access to the SSP is **restricted** to the Master, SSO, CSO, and any authorized authority. The SSP itself is **not audited in detail** by port-State inspectors — they verify the *existence* and *implementation* of the SSP, not its full content (to protect confidentiality).

**Step 5 — Approval of the SSP by the Administration**
The SSP must be **approved** by the Administration (or an RSO acting on its behalf).

**Step 6 — Issuance of the International Ship Security Certificate (ISSC)**
After implementation, an **ISSC is issued**, valid for **5 years**, with **intermediate verifications**.

**Step 7 — Onboard Implementation by the SSO**
The SSO implements the SSP, conducts **drills** (at least once every 3 months — though some flag States set higher frequencies), and maintains the **Continuous Synopsis Record (CSR)**.

**Step 8 — Continuous Maintenance**
- The SSP is reviewed and updated **after any change** affecting security (e.g., change of ownership, change of management, structural modification, change of trade route).
- Drills are conducted and recorded.
- SSAS is tested periodically.

**Step 9 — Renewal before Expiry**
The ISSC is renewed **before the 5-year expiry** through a renewal verification; otherwise, the ship is non-compliant and may be detained.

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step: How a Port Facility Becomes ISPS-Compliant

**Step 1 — Port Facility Security Assessment (PFSA)**
The Contracting Government identifies the boundaries of the port facility, the assets to be protected, the threats, and the vulnerabilities.

**Step 2 — Appointment of the PFSO**
A single PFSO is appointed for each port facility. Where multiple facilities are co-located, separate PFSOs may be required.

**Step 3 — Drafting the Port Facility Security Plan (PFSP)**
Based on the PFSA. The PFSP addresses access control, restricted areas, handling of cargo, monitoring, security incidents, etc.

**Step 4 — Approval of the PFSP**
By the Contracting Government.

**Step 5 — Issuance of Statement of Compliance**
Valid for **5 years**.

**Step 6 — Continuous Operation**
- The PFSO implements the PFSP.
- PFSO must liaise with the SSOs of ships calling at the port.
- When Security Levels change, the PFSO must brief ships and, where necessary, complete a Declaration of Security (DoS).

---

### 3.3 The 14 Mandatory Elements of the SSP (ISPS Part A, Section 9)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **very common KTU Part B sub-question**: *"List the elements to be included in the Ship Security Plan."* Memorize the full list, ideally grouped as below.

1. Measures designed to **prevent weapons, dangerous substances, and devices** intended for use against persons, ships, or ports from being introduced aboard the ship.
2. Identification of the **restricted areas** and measures to control access to them.
3. Measures for the **protection of cargo** and the ship's cargo handling operations.
4. Measures for the **protection of the ship's stores and bunkers**.
5. Measures for the **protection of the crew** and for maintaining a **safe working environment**.
6. Measures for **monitoring** the ship, including the **SSAS**.
7. Procedures for **auditing** the SSP.
8. Procedures for **responding to security threats or breaches**, including provisions for **maintaining critical operations** of the ship or for the safe evacuation of the crew.
9. Procedures for **responding to security instructions** issued by Contracting Governments in Security Level 2 or 3.
10. Procedures for **evacuation** in case of security threats or breaches.
11. Procedures for **exercising and drilling** security routines.
12. Procedures for **interface with port facilities** and other ships (i.e., the DoS).
13. Procedures for **periodic review and update** of the SSP.
14. Identification of the **SSO, CSO, and 24-hour contact** details.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Trigger → Action → Document

| Trigger | Action Required | Document Generated/Updated |
|---|---|---|
| Ship change of flag | Update flag State info | CSR updated |
| Change of ownership | New Company | CSR updated, new ISSC |
| Major structural modification | New SSA | SSP revised, re-approved |
| Increase to Security Level 2 | Heightened measures | SSP "Level 2" procedures invoked |
| Increase to Security Level 3 | Maximum security | May involve ship diversion; DoS reviewed |
| Ship-port interface with risk | Bilateral security agreement | DoS signed |
| 5-year anniversary of ISSC | Renewal verification | New ISSC |
| Periodic (≥ 3 months) | Security drill | Drill record |
| Periodic (≥ annually) | SSAS test | SSAS test record |

---

### 3.5 Worked Analytical Example (KTU-style)

> **Question:** *"A container ship of 35,000 GT is engaged in international trade between Singapore, Dubai, and Hamburg. The Administration of the flag State has set Security Level 2 for the vessel. On arrival at Dubai, the port facility is at Security Level 1. The Master has received intelligence of a possible attempt by a militant group to seize the vessel. Explain, step by step, the actions required under the ISPS Code."*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Authority to set the Security Level:** The **Administration (flag State)** has set Level 2 for the ship, while the **Contracting Government of the United Arab Emirates** has set Level 1 for the port. The **higher of the two applies** to the ship–port interface (ISPS Part A, Section 7).

2. **Master's Overriding Authority:** The Master, having received credible intelligence of a seizure attempt, exercises the **overriding authority** under Section 5.2. The Master may decide to **not berth**, to **divert**, to **raise Security Level to 3**, or to **invoke the SSP's contingency procedures** — without needing Company approval.

3. **SSAS Activation Decision:** The Master may consider **covertly activating the SSAS** to alert the Administration's competent authority of the threat. This is consistent with the covert design of the SSAS.

4. **Declaration of Security (DoS):** Because the ship's Security Level (2) is higher than the port's (1), a **DoS is mandatory** before any ship–port operations (Part A, Section 19). The SSO and the PFSO must jointly complete it.

5. **Restricted Areas & Access Control:** The SSO tightens the **three-tier access regime** (perimeter → ship → restricted areas). Crew and visitor passes are re-verified; all personal bags are searched.

6. **Communication with CSO and PFSO:** The SSO informs the **CSO** at the Company and the **PFSO** at Dubai. The PFSO in turn notifies port-side security.

7. **CCTV, Lighting, Watchmen:** Continuous monitoring is intensified; lookouts are doubled; bridge wings are manned.

8. **Record-Keeping:** All decisions and actions are logged in the ship's logbook; the CSR is reviewed and updated if flag/ownership conditions change.

9. **Audit Trail:** During the next port-State control inspection, the actions taken will be checked against the SSP, the DoS, and the SSAS test log.

**Marking Insight:** Examiners will award 1–2 marks for correctly identifying the **Master's overriding authority**, 1–2 marks for the **higher Security Level rule**, 2 marks for the **DoS trigger**, and the remaining marks for correctly sequencing the operational steps.

---

### 3.6 Pitfall-Spotting: Distinctions the Examiner Will Test

| Confused Pair | Distinction (the Examiner Wants) |
|---|---|
| **ISSC vs. CSR** | ISSC is a **certificate** (5 years); CSR is a **continuous record** updated on every change. |
| **SSP vs. PFSP** | SSP is **ship-side**; PFSP is **port-side**. The ship carries the SSP; the PFSP stays at the port. |
| **CSO vs. SSO** | CSO is **shore-based** (Company level); SSO is **onboard the ship**. |
| **Part A vs. Part B** | Part A is **mandatory**; Part B is **guidance**. |
| **Security Level 1 vs. 2 vs. 3** | Level 1 = normal; Level 2 = heightened; Level 3 = exceptional/imminent. |
| **ISPS Code vs. SOLAS Chapter XI-2** | The ISPS Code is the **detailed security regime**; SOLAS Chapter XI-2 is the **Convention article** that makes it binding. |
| **DoS vs. SSP/PFSP** | DoS is a **per-interface bilateral agreement**; SSP/PFSP are **standing plans**. |
| **ISSC validity vs. DoS validity** | ISSC is **5 years**; DoS is **per interface**. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ISPS Code — Overall Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[SOLAS Convention 1974] --> A1[Chapter XI-2 Special Measures]
    A1 --> A2[ISPS Code Part A MANDATORY]
    A1 --> A3[ISPS Code Part B GUIDANCE]
    A2 --> B1[Governance Cluster Sections 1 to 4]
    A2 --> B2[Ship Level Cluster Sections 5 to 11]
    A2 --> B3[Port Level Cluster Sections 14 to 18]
    A2 --> B4[Interface Cluster Section 19]
    B1 --> C1[Contracting Government Ship]
    B1 --> C2[Contracting Government Port]
    B1 --> C3[Recognised Security Organization RSO]
    B2 --> D1[Ship Security Plan SSP]
    B2 --> D2[Ship Security Officer SSO]
    B2 --> D3[Company Security Officer CSO]
    B2 --> D4[International Ship Security Certificate ISSC]
    B2 --> D5[Continuous Synopsis Record CSR]
    B2 --> D6[Ship Security Alert System SSAS]
    B3 --> E1[Port Facility Security Plan PFSP]
    B3 --> E2[Port Facility Security Officer PFSO]
    B3 --> E3[Statement of Compliance Port]
    B4 --> F1[Declaration of Security DoS]
    B4 --> F2[Security Level Coordination]
    D1 --> F1
    E1 --> F1
    D2 --> F1
    E2 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Security Level 1 Normal]
    F1 --> G2[Security Level 2 Heightened]
    F1 --> G3[Security Level 3 Exceptional]
    G2 --> C1
    G3 --> C1
    G2 --> C2
    G3 --> C2
```

### 4.2 Ship-Side Compliance Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Ship Owner identifies international trade] --> S2[Notify Flag State Administration]
    S2 --> S3[Appoint CSO Company Security Officer]
    S3 --> S4[Conduct Ship Security Assessment SSA]
    S4 --> S5[Draft Ship Security Plan SSP]
    S5 --> S6[Submit SSP for Approval by Administration or RSO]
    S6 --> S7{Approved?}
    S7 -- No --> S5
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Implement SSP Onboard]
    S8 --> S9[Issue International Ship Security Certificate ISSC 5 years]
    S9 --> S10[Continuous Compliance Drills SSAS tests CSR updates]
    S10 --> S11{Renewal Due?}
    S11 -- No --> S10
    S11 -- Yes --> S12[Renewal Verification]
    S12 --> S9
```

### 4.3 Port-Side Compliance Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Contracting Government identifies port facility] --> P2[Conduct Port Facility Security Assessment PFSA]
    P2 --> P3[Appoint Port Facility Security Officer PFSO]
    P3 --> P4[Draft Port Facility Security Plan PFSP]
    P4 --> P5[Submit PFSP to Contracting Government for Approval]
    P5 --> P6{Approved?}
    P6 -- No --> P4
    P6 -- Yes --> P7[Issue Statement of Compliance 5 years]
    P7 --> P8[Continuous Operation Access control CCTV monitoring]
    P8 --> P9[Interface with calling ships via DoS]
    P9 --> P10[Periodic PFSP review and drills]
    P10 --> P8
```

### 4.4 Security Level Escalation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Baseline Operating Condition] --> L1[Security Level 1 NORMAL]
    L1 --> L2{Threat Intelligence Received?}
    L2 -- No --> L1
    L2 -- Yes Heightened Risk --> L3[Security Level 2 HEIGHTENED]
    L3 --> L4{Probable or Imminent Threat?}
    L4 -- No --> L3
    L4 -- Yes --> L5[Security Level 3 EXCEPTIONAL]
    L5 --> L6{All Clear?}
    L6 -- Yes --> L3
    L6 -- Sustained --> L1
    L5 --> L7[Possible Ship Diversion or Refusal of Entry]
    L3 --> L8[Additional Preventive Measures Activated]
    L1 --> L9[Baseline Security Measures Maintained]
```

### 4.5 Ship–Port Interface Handshake (DoS)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Ship as Ship SSO
    participant Port as Port PFSO
    Ship->>Port: Arrival notification and Security Level query
    Port-->>Ship: PFSO confirms Port Security Level
    alt Security Level mismatch or specific threat
        Ship->>Port: Draft Declaration of Security DoS
        Port-->>Ship: Counter sign DoS
        Note over Ship,Port: Both parties implement agreed measures
    else Routine Level 1 interface
        Note over Ship,Port: No DoS required
    end
    Ship->>Port: Operations commence under agreed measures
    Port-->>Ship: Continuous liaison and monitoring
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions (with Model Answers)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model]**
*Define the ISPS Code and state when it entered into force.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - (1 mark) **Definition:** The International Ship and Port Facility Security (ISPS) Code is a comprehensive set of mandatory and recommendatory measures adopted by the International Maritime Organization (IMO) in December 2002 under SOLAS Chapter XI-2, to enhance the security of ships, port facilities, and the wider maritime transport chain.
> - (1 mark) **Adoption:** Adopted at the IMO Diplomatic Conference in London in **December 2002**.
> - (1 mark) **Entry into force:** **1 July 2004** — making it one of the fastest implementations in IMO history.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model]**
*List the three Security Levels defined in the ISPS Code and briefly state when each applies.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - (1 mark) **Level 1 — Normal:** The level at which ships and port facilities operate routinely during peacetime.
> - (1 mark) **Level 2 — Heightened:** Applies when there is a heightened risk of a security incident (e.g., credible threat intelligence in the region).
> - (1 mark) **Level 3 — Exceptional:** Applies when there is a probable or imminent risk of a security incident (e.g., an actual or expected attack).

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**
*Explain in detail the responsibilities of the Ship Security Officer (SSO), the Company Security Officer (CSO), and the Port Facility Security Officer (PFSO) under the ISPS Code. Highlight the relationship among the three.*

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **1. Company Security Officer (CSO) — Shore-based (≈ 2 marks):**
> - Appointed by the **Company** for each ship or group of ships.
> - Acts as the **single point of contact** between the ship and the Company.
> - Reviews the **Ship Security Assessment (SSA)** and submits the **Ship Security Plan (SSP)** to the Administration for approval.
> - Ensures the **SSO is properly trained, equipped, and supported**.
> - Liaises with PFSOs and Contracting Governments on security matters.
> - Maintains the **Continuous Synopsis Record (CSR)** in cooperation with the Master.
>
> **2. Ship Security Officer (SSO) — Onboard (≈ 2 marks):**
> - Appointed by the Company; the **Master may also serve** as the SSO if he/she is suitably qualified.
> - Conducts the **regular security inspections** of the ship.
> - **Implements the SSP** and any amendments after approval.
> - Reports security deficiencies to the CSO and the Master.
> - Ensures **drills and exercises** are conducted at the prescribed frequency.
> - **Liaises with the PFSO** when the ship is in port and participates in the **Declaration of Security (DoS)**.
> - Maintains **24-hour ship-to-shore security communication** capability (often co-located with SSAS monitoring).
>
> **3. Port Facility Security Officer (PFSO) — Port-based (≈ 2 marks):**
> - Appointed by the **port facility** and approved by the Contracting Government.
> - Conducts the **Port Facility Security Assessment (PFSA)** and prepares the **Port Facility Security Plan (PFSP)**.
> - **Implements the PFSP** and ensures compliance with the relevant Security Level.
> - Acts as the **point of contact** for the SSO of any ship calling at the facility.
> - Coordinates **port-side security with ship-side security**, including joint exercises and DoS signings.
> - Notifies the Contracting Government of any **higher security level** declared for the port.
>
> **4. The Tripartite Relationship (≈ 1 mark):**
> The **CSO** provides corporate oversight; the **SSO** executes shipboard security; the **PFSO** controls port-side security. They form a **continuous security chain** that bridges ship and port, and their cooperation is formalized through the **DoS** at the ship–port interface.

**(b) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
*Describe the Ship Security Alert System (SSAS). Explain its purpose, design requirements, and how it differs from routine GMDSS distress alerting.*

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **1. Purpose (≈ 2 marks):**
> - The SSAS is a **covert alerting system** that allows a ship under threat (e.g., piracy, armed robbery, terrorist attack) to silently notify a **competent authority designated by the Administration** that the security of the ship is compromised.
> - The alert **identifies the ship, its position, and the nature of the threat**, and is routed **only to the designated authority** — not broadcast openly, not sent to other ships, and **not made visible on the bridge** (the "covert" requirement).
> - The goal is to enable **rapid covert response** by naval/armed forces while not alerting the attackers.
>
> **2. Design Requirements (≈ 3 marks):**
> - **Covert activation** from at least **two independent locations** on the ship (so that one can be used if the bridge is compromised).
> - **Power supply from both the main and emergency sources** to ensure availability even under partial loss of ship power.
> - **No audible or visual alarm on the bridge** during transmission.
> - Capability to **initiate and update the alert** without interrupting the ship's normal communications.
> - **Periodic testing** (commonly annually) and recorded in the ship's logbook.
> - May be **integrated with GMDSS** equipment (e.g., INMARSAT-C, FleetBroadband) for transmission, but the alert channel is logically separate.
>
> **3. Difference from GMDSS Distress Alerting (≈ 2 marks):**
> | Aspect | GMDSS Distress Alert | SSAS Security Alert |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Trigger** | Maritime distress (sinking, fire, man overboard, medical emergency, etc.) | **Security threat** (piracy, terrorism, boarding) |
> | **Recipients** | All ships in vicinity + shore authorities (DSC, EPIRB, INMARSAT) | **Only the designated competent authority** (Administration) — NOT broadcast |
> | **Covert?** | No — designed to attract maximum attention | **Yes — designed to be invisible to the attacker** |
> | **Bridge visibility** | Audible/visual alarms by design | **No** audible/visual on the bridge |
> | **Regulatory base** | SOLAS Chapter IV | **SOLAS Chapter XI-2, Regulation 6** |
> | **Purpose** | Coordinate rescue | **Covertly request armed response / naval intervention** |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model]**
*List and briefly explain the documents required under the ISPS Code for a ship and for a port facility. Indicate the issuing authority and validity of each.*

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **1. Ship-Side Documents (≈ 3.5 marks):**
> | Document | Issued By | Validity | Purpose |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Ship Security Plan (SSP)** | Company (approved by Administration / RSO) | Continuous (revised as needed) | Internal, confidential, ship-specific security operating manual. |
> | **International Ship Security Certificate (ISSC)** | Administration (or RSO) | **5 years** (with intermediate verification) | Statutory proof of compliance for international voyages. |
> | **Continuous Synopsis Record (CSR)** | Administration | Continuous (updated on every change) | Public record of the ship's security-relevant history. |
> | **Declaration of Security (DoS)** | Ship ↔ Port Facility | **Per interface** | Bilateral security agreement for a specific ship–port interaction. |
>
> **2. Port-Side Documents (≈ 3.5 marks):**
> | Document | Issued By | Validity | Purpose |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Port Facility Security Assessment (PFSA)** | Port Facility | Continuous (reviewed periodically) | Identifies threats, vulnerabilities, and assets to be protected. |
> | **Port Facility Security Plan (PFSP)** | Port Facility (approved by Contracting Government) | Continuous (revised as needed) | Port-side security operating manual. Stays at the port. |
> | **Statement of Compliance (Port Facility)** | Contracting Government | **5 years** | Statutory proof that the port facility meets ISPS. |
> | **Declaration of Security (DoS)** | Ship ↔ Port Facility | **Per interface** | Same DoS, but on the port side, it commits the port's measures. |

**(b) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model]**
*Explain the Master’s Overriding Authority under the ISPS Code. Why is it considered the cornerstone of maritime security governance?*

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **1. The Rule (≈ 2 marks):**
> Under ISPS Code Part A, Section 5.2, the **Master shall not be constrained by the Company, the Charterer, or any other person** from taking or executing any decision that, in the **Master's professional judgement**, is necessary to maintain the safety and security of the ship.
> - This includes decisions to:
>   - **Decline entry** to a high-risk port.
>   - **Divert** to a safer port.
>   - **Raise the Security Level** onboard above that of the port.
>   - **Activate the SSAS** covertly.
>   - **Order evacuation or lockdown** of the ship.
>
> **2. Why It Is the Cornerstone (≈ 3 marks):**
> - **Operational reality:** A ship at sea or approaching a port may face a **time-critical** threat (e.g., a fast boat attack, a stowaway weapon sighting) where waiting for corporate or port-state approval would cost lives.
> - **Information asymmetry:** Only the **Master is on the bridge** and sees the actual situation in real time. Corporate managers may be on a different continent, working from sanitized reports.
> - **Legal insulation:** The rule protects the Master from being **pressured by commercial interests** (e.g., a charterer demanding that a cargo be delivered on time) into skipping security precautions.
> - **International uniformity:** It provides a **globally consistent legal doctrine** so that a Master can invoke the rule anywhere in the world, knowing it will be respected by flag and port states alike.
>
> **3. Limits and Boundaries (≈ 2 marks):**
> - The rule applies **only to safety and security decisions**, not to commercial decisions.
> - The Master must still **report** to the CSO and the Administration after the fact.
> - The Master's judgement must be **reasonable, in good faith, and based on professional competence**; otherwise, the Company may challenge the decision post-incident.
> - The rule does **not** override the port State's right to deny entry if the ship itself is the threat (e.g., a known security breach).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing the entry-into-force date:** Students frequently write *1 July 2002* (the adoption date) instead of **1 July 2004** (entry into force). This alone can cost 1 mark on a 3-mark question.
> 2. **Confusing ISSC and CSR:** The ISSC is a **certificate with a 5-year life**; the CSR is a **continuous, event-driven record**. Examiners will deliberately test this distinction.
> 3. **Forgetting the Master’s Overriding Authority:** This is the single most-tested concept in Part B. Always cite **ISPS Part A, Section 5.2** verbatim or near-verbatim.
> 4. **Mis-classifying Part A and Part B:** Part A is **mandatory**; Part B is **guidance**. Reversing this is a 0.5–1 mark loss.
> 5. **Conflating SOLAS Chapter XI-2 with the ISPS Code:** Chapter XI-2 is the **umbrella convention article**; the ISPS Code is the **operational instrument** that implements it. Examiners want both named.
> 6. **Wrong Security Level triggers:** "Level 2" is *heightened* (not "highest"); "Level 3" is *exceptional/imminent*. Mixing these is a typical trap.
> 7. **DoS validity:** DoS is **per interface**, not 5 years. Students often incorrectly state "5 years" for the DoS as well.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **ISPS Code** adopted **Dec 2002**, in force **1 July 2004**, under **SOLAS Chapter XI-2**.
- [ ] **Part A = Mandatory**, **Part B = Guidance**.
- [ ] Applies to **passenger ships, cargo ships ≥ 500 GT, MODUs**; **not** to warships.
- [ ] **Security Levels:** 1 (Normal), 2 (Heightened), 3 (Exceptional) — set by **Contracting Government**.
- [ ] **Higher Security Level** wins at the ship–port interface.
- [ ] **Master's Overriding Authority (Part A, Section 5.2)** is the cornerstone — protect the Master's right to decide.
- [ ] **CSO** = shore-based (Company); **SSO** = onboard; **PFSO** = port-side.
- [ ] **ISSC** = 5 years (with intermediate verification); **Statement of Compliance (Port)** = 5 years.
- [ ] **CSR** = continuous, updated on every flag/owner/management change.
- [ ] **SSP** is **confidential**; only its existence/implementation is audited, not its contents.
- [ ] **PFSP** stays at the port; the ship does not get a copy.
- [ ] **SSAS** is **covert**, **bridge-invisible**, transmitted **only** to the Administration's designated competent authority.
- [ ] **SSAS trigger:** security threat (piracy, terrorism); **NOT** the same as a GMDSS distress alert.
- [ ] **DoS** is required when ship's Security Level > port's, or on a known threat, or on a high-risk inter-ship interface, or on request.
- [ ] **DoS validity:** per interface, not 5 years.
- [ ] **SSO is the Master** if the Master is suitably qualified.
- [ ] **RSO (Recognized Security Organization)** may act for the Administration in approval and audit.
- [ ] **Drills:** at least quarterly (every 3 months); **SSAS test:** at least annually.
- [ ] **Engineers' role:** design SSAS redundancy, integrate with GMDSS without coupling, plan **citadel/refuge** spaces, route CCTV/access-control cabling, and ensure power is from both **main and emergency** sources.
- [ ] Memorize the **14 mandatory elements of the SSP** (ISPS Part A Section 9) — it is a near-guaranteed 7-mark sub-question.
- [ ] The ISPS Code is **not** a stand-alone treaty; it is **operationally nested inside SOLAS Chapter XI-2** — always name both in answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maritime Security Measures
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT755 - MARITIME SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Maritime Security Measures

# Maritime Security Measures — Module 4

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Maritime Security Measures** refer to the layered, internationally mandated and nationally enforced set of preventive, detective, and responsive protocols, procedures, physical safeguards, and operational controls applied to ships, port facilities, cargo, crew, and the broader maritime domain to deter, detect, and defeat threats such as piracy, armed robbery, terrorism, smuggling, and cyber-attacks. The cornerstone of this framework is the **International Ship and Port Facility Security (ISPS) Code**, adopted under **SOLAS Chapter XI-2** by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** in **2002**, in direct response to the **9/11 terrorist attacks (2001)**.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Maritime Security System like the Security of a Large Apartment Complex:**
> - The **Apartment Building** = The Ship (or Port Facility)
> - The **Residents** = Crew / Personnel
> - The **Locks on the Doors** = Physical Security Barriers (Gates, CCTV, Access Control)
> - The **Security Guard at the Lobby** = Ship Security Officer (SSO) / Port Facility Security Officer (PFSO)
> - The **Intercom System** = Communication & Reporting Systems
> - The **Neighbourhood Watch** = International Maritime Security Cooperation (ReCAAP, MARSIC, etc.)
> - The **Threat Level Indicator** posted at the entrance = The **Ship Security Level (1, 2, or 3)**
>
> Just as a complex has graduated security protocols (normal → heightened → emergency), the ISPS Code mandates **three escalating security levels** based on the assessed threat.

### 1.2 Why Maritime Security Matters — The Strategic Context

The global shipping industry transports roughly **80% of world trade by volume** and **70% by value**. Any disruption — be it piracy, a terrorist incident, or a major cyber-attack on port systems — sends shockwaves through the global supply chain. The **cost of piracy alone** is estimated at billions of dollars annually, including ransoms, insurance premiums, rerouting, and naval deployments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Statistical Anchors (Standard Industry Metrics):**
> - **ISPS Code adoption:** Came into force on **1 July 2004**, mandatory for **>300 SOLAS contracting governments**.
> - **Coverage:** Applies to **passenger ships, cargo ships ≥ 500 GT**, and **mobile offshore drilling units** on international voyages.
> - **Reportable incidents (IMO data):** Hundreds of piracy/armed robbery incidents reported annually, primarily in the **Gulf of Aden, Strait of Malacca, Gulf of Guinea, and South China Sea**.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of Maritime Security

Maritime Security is built upon **three integrated pillars** — a concept examiners frequently test:

1. **Preventive Measures** — Deter threats *before* they occur (e.g., access control, fencing, lighting, pre-employment screening).
2. **Detection Measures** — Identify threats *as they occur* (e.g., CCTV, intrusion alarms, AIS, LRIT — Long Range Identification and Tracking).
3. **Response & Recovery Measures** — Neutralize and recover *after* an incident (e.g., citadels, private armed security teams, emergency communication drills).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The ISPS Code — Architectural Breakdown

The **International Ship and Port Facility Security (ISPS) Code** is a two-part international regulatory framework:

| Part | Title | Scope |
|------|-------|-------|
| **Part A** | Mandatory (Detailed Requirements) | Specifies the *prescriptive* obligations, including the format of the Ship Security Plan (SSP) and Port Facility Security Plan (PFSP). |
| **Part B** | Guidance (Recommendations) | Provides *advisory* guidance on how to implement Part A requirements. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "PAM":**
> **P**art **A** = **M**andatory → **PAM**

### 2.2 Ship Security Levels (SSL) — The Escalation Ladder

The ISPS Code establishes **three Ship Security Levels**. The level is set by the **Contracting Government** (typically the flag state, in consultation with port states) based on the prevailing threat:

| Security Level | Description | Implementation |
|---------------|-------------|----------------|
| **Level 1 — Normal** | Baseline; routine operations | Standard preventive measures continuously maintained |
| **Level 2 — Heightened** | Heightened risk of security incident | Additional measures; may include continuous surveillance, restricted access |
| **Level 3 — Exceptional** | Imminent or actual security incident probable/imminent | Maximum protective measures; possible suspension of vessel operations |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Trap:** Students often confuse *who sets* the security level. The flag-state Contracting Government sets the level, but the **Master has overriding authority** to refuse sailing or take emergency measures if the security of the ship is compromised.

### 2.3 Key Stakeholders in the ISPS Framework

- **CSO (Company Security Officer):** Designated by the Company; responsible for ensuring the SSP is implemented and maintained.
- **SSO (Ship Security Officer):** Designated by the Company, responsible for ship-specific security operations; reports to the Master.
- **PFSO (Port Facility Security Officer):** Designated by the Port Facility; responsible for PFSP implementation.
- **Master:** Has **overriding authority** and final decision-making power on ship security.
- **Recognized Security Organization (RSO):** An organization authorized to act on behalf of the Administration (flag state) for approval of SSPs and security audits.

### 2.4 Ship Security Plan (SSP) — Mandatory Contents

Under ISPS Code Part A, the SSP must address (at minimum):

1. **Measures to prevent** weapons, dangerous substances, and devices intended for use against people, ships, or ports from being carried on board.
2. **Identification of restricted areas** (e.g., bridge, engine room, accommodation).
3. **Access control procedures** for the ship and its restricted areas.
4. **Procedures for monitoring** ship-to-ship and ship-to-shore movements.
5. **Security level escalation procedures.**
6. **Procedures for responding to security threats and breaches.**
7. **Procedures for handling** security-related information.
8. **Training, drills, and exercise requirements.**
9. **Procedures for interfacing** with port facility security.
10. **Procedures for periodic review** and amendment of the SSP.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Since Maritime Security is largely qualitative/regulatory, the formulas below focus on **risk assessment mathematics** and **operational metrics** that frequently appear in KTU numerical/analytical questions.

| # | Concept | Formula / Metric | Description |
|---|---------|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Risk Score (Qualitative Matrix)** | $R = T \times V \times C$ | $R$ = Risk; $T$ = Threat Likelihood; $V$ = Vulnerability; $C$ = Consequence (each rated 1–5) |
| 2 | **Security Coverage Ratio** | $S_{cov} = \dfrac{N_{guarded}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $N_{guarded}$ = number of high-value assets under active security; $N_{total}$ = total assets |
| 3 | **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | $SLE$ = Single Loss Expectancy; $ARO$ = Annualized Rate of Occurrence |
| 4 | **Cost-Benefit of Security Upgrade** | $ROI_{sec} = \dfrac{ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - C_{sec}}{C_{sec}} \times 100\%$ | $C_{sec}$ = annual cost of security upgrade |
| 5 | **AIS Reporting Compliance** | $C_{AIS} = \dfrac{H_{reported}}{H_{scheduled}} \times 100\%$ | $H$ = hours of AIS transmission per IMO LRIT requirements |
| 6 | **Security Level Escalation Index (Conceptual)** | $SL_{index} = w_1 T + w_2 V + w_3 I$ | $w_i$ = weighted factors; $T$ = threat; $V$ = vulnerability; $I$ = intelligence indicator |
| 7 | **Piracy Risk Index (Regional)** | $PRI = \dfrac{I_a \times D_s}{R_t}$ | $I_a$ = number of incidents; $D_s$ = average severity; $R_t$ = number of transit voyages |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of the Risk Equation $R = T \times V \times C$:**
